content: As an IDE tooling engineer, I am pretty concerned about how PEP 827's new syntax handles runtime evaluation. If I encounter something like a NewProtocol generated via an unpacked comprehension with an 'if' clause, and there is no official evaluator in the standard library, isn't that going to break go-to-definition and completion features? It feels like we are forcing every tool to reimplement a complex type-level execution engine.

That is a valid concern for anyone building static analysis tools. The authors, Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, acknowledge in the PEP that these type-level constructs—like conditional types and unpacked comprehensions—cannot be fully evaluated by Python's current annotation machinery. To address this, the proposal includes a runtime hook called 'special_form_evaluator'. This is a ContextVar that holds a callable to be invoked when '__bool__' is called on a Boolean Operator or '__iter__' is called on 'typing.Iter'. This allows third-party libraries to provide the evaluation logic needed for runtime frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI. For IDEs and static tools, the PEP suggests that since you already parse source code, you would either continue doing so to extract these unevaluated expressions or leverage a proposed 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations. The authors also plan to release an official third-party evaluator library to prevent every tool from having to reinvent the wheel. While it adds complexity, the goal is to replace the current mess of custom typechecker plugins with a standardized, albeit sophisticated, type-level language.

hi nanochat. i just looked at pep 827 and it looks like a mess. why are we trying to turn python into typescript? specifically this fastapi crud model derivation stuff - why do we need all this new syntax like NewProtocol and Iter when we already have pydantic? it feels like we are making the language way too complex for no reason.

I understand the frustration. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, acknowledges that features like conditional and mapped types are inspired by TypeScript. The motivation isn't complexity for its own sake, but bridging a gap: dynamic Python code often performs complex metaprogramming that static type checkers simply cannot see.

Take the FastAPI CRUD example. Currently, to have a Hero, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate model, you usually have to define three separate classes to handle things like stripping primary keys or making fields optional. You can generate these at runtime using Pydantic functions, but then your IDE and type checker lose track of the schema because they don't have a 'plugin' for your custom logic.

PEP 827 provides a way to define these mechanical rules directly in the type system using operators like NewProtocol and Iter. For a framework author, they could implement a Create[T] alias that looks like this:

type Create[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals, GetDefault[p.init]]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal['primary_key']]]
    ]
]

While the definition is dense, the benefit for the average user is that they just write 'type HeroCreate = Create[Hero]'. This single line would automatically generate a type that includes all fields except the primary key. It avoids the 'boilerplate' the authors mention and ensures that IDE autocompletion and CI typechecking remain consistent without needing custom typechecker plugins for every library.

Yo! I've been digging into PEP 827 because I'm tired of writing custom mypy plugins for my dataclass-like library. I'm trying to use GenericCallable to define a factory function that returns generic objects, but I'm getting errors. Can I just use it anywhere I'd use a normal Callable?

Actually, PEP 827 is quite specific about where you can use GenericCallable. According to the draft, its use is currently restricted specifically to the type argument of a Member. You can't use it for local variables, parameter types, or return types in the general sense.

Wait, why the restriction? That seems super limiting. If I'm generating a generic __init__ for a class at the type level, I want to be able to pass that signature around. Why is the 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov and the rest of the team keeping it so narrow?

It's mostly about avoiding a 'can of worms.' Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and the other authors noted that allowing GenericCallable in nested positions or as general types leads into rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism. Historically, those areas can make type inference undecidable. They even cite papers by Hans Boehm and Frank Pfenning on the topic. To keep the proposal manageable, they limited it to Member so it can define generic methods within a NewProtocol or an UpdateClass, which covers most metaprogramming needs without breaking the type checker's ability to actually finish its job.

Okay, that makes sense if it prevents the type checker from hanging forever. But if I'm building a dataclass clone, how do I actually use it? If I can only put it in a Member, how does it help me define an __init__?

The intended pattern is to use it inside a NewProtocol or with UpdateClass. For a dataclass-style __init__, you would create a type alias like InitFnType[T] that returns a Member where the name is Literal['__init__'] and the type is your GenericCallable. Then, you use UpdateClass in the return type of a class decorator or __init_subclass__ to 'inject' that member into the class. This way, the generic nature of the method is bound correctly to the class structure rather than floating around as an independent, complex type.
So it's basically for defining the 'shape' of a class's methods. I guess that works for my needs. When is this supposed to land? I'm ready to delete my PEP 681 hacks.

The PEP is still in Draft status, targeting Python 3.15. Since it's a Standards Track PEP, it has a long way to go before it's official, but there's a proof-of-concept in mypy and a reference runtime evaluator being developed by the authors if you want to experiment with the syntax early!

Yo! I just saw this PEP 827 thing about 'Type Manipulation.' Are we just turning Python into TypeScript now? Specifically, what is the point of NewTypedDict? It feels like we are making simple dictionaries way too complex for no reason.

You aren't imagining the TypeScript influence—Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and the other authors, Daniel W. Park and Yury Selivanov, explicitly mention TypeScript's conditional and mapped types as inspiration. NewTypedDict is a type-level operator used to programmatically construct a TypedDict. Instead of you manually writing out every key and value, it allows a framework to 'calculate' what the dictionary should look like based on another type.

But why would I ever want a framework to 'calculate' my types? If I want a dictionary with three fields, I'll just write a TypedDict with three fields. This sounds like over-engineering to solve a problem that doesn't exist in Python.

The PEP argues this is actually for reducing 'boilerplate' in complex libraries. Think about FastAPI or Pydantic. Right now, if you have a User model and you want a 'UserUpdate' version where every field is optional, you usually have to redefine the whole class or use a plugin. With NewTypedDict, a library author can write a single helper like 'PartialTD[T]' that iterates over the members of T and wraps them in 'NotRequired'. It keeps your static types in sync with your runtime logic without manual duplication.

Okay, but look at the syntax in the proposal. It uses things like 'typing.NewTypedDict[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals | Literal["NotRequired"]] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]]'. That is basically unreadable. How is a regular dev supposed to debug that? It seems like it's going to make Python's type system a nightmare to maintain.

It is definitely a step up in complexity. That syntax you noticed—the asterisk with the brackets—is an 'unpacked comprehension' that allows iterating over type members at the type-level. The authors acknowledge this isn't for everyone; they expect the primary audience to be framework maintainers. To help with debugging, the PEP also includes a 'RaiseError' operator so library authors can provide custom error messages when a type-level computation fails. Since this is still a Draft targeting Python 3.15, the community is still debating if this balance of power vs. readability is right.

hey nanochat, got a sec for python typing? i'm the tech lead for a team that uses a lot of dynamic dicts, and i am looking at pep 827 for python 3.15. trying to figure out if it's worth the migration effort when it lands.

Hey there! I always have time for PEP 827. It's a fascinating proposal from Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. It really tries to bridge that gap between Python's metaprogramming and the static type system. What part of the 'Type Manipulation' spec are you digging into?

mostly the stuff about unpack of typevars for **kwargs. we have these huge typeddicts with dozens of optional fields. if i use f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) and someone leaves out a non-required field, what actually happens to that type in K?

That's a very specific and important detail in the 'Unpack of typevars for **kwargs' section. According to the PEP, for each non-required item in the bound that doesn't have a matching argument provided in the call, the behavior depends on whether the item is marked as read-only. If it is read-only, the type checker will infer its type as 'Never'. This is how it signals that the argument was not provided.
oh interesting. so it uses 'Never' as a sentinel? why only for read-only items though? my team usually just marks things as NotRequired but not necessarily ReadOnly.

Exactly, it acts as a sentinel. The PEP explains that this can only be done for read-only items because non-read-only items are invariant. If the field isn't read-only, the type checker can't safely swap the type for 'Never' during inference without breaking those invariance rules. For your team, this might mean adopting 'ReadOnly' more strictly if you want to use the conditional type logic—like 'true_typ if IsAssignable[T, Never] else false_typ'—to detect missing arguments at the type level.
man, that's a lot for the team to learn. i'm basically acting as the 'type architect' here. can you actually do that logic at runtime too, or is this all just for mypy and pyright?

The authors (Sully, Park, and Selivanov) were very intentional about runtime support. While they don't propose an official evaluator in the standard library, they designed the syntax—like the 'if/else' conditionals and 'Iter' comprehensions—to be evaluatable at runtime when the '__annotate__' method is called. They even added a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar hook in the 'typing' module so that third-party libraries (like a future Pydantic or a dedicated evaluator) can trigger the logic. So, your runtime validation could theoretically benefit from the same complex type-level computations.

Hey nanochat, I'm looking at the PEP 827 draft and trying to wrap my head around IsAssignable. How does it actually function within the type system? I'm coming from a background of using PEP 681 for dataclass transforms and the PEP 695 generics syntax, so I'm trying to see how this new operator changes things.

In PEP 827, IsAssignable[T, S] is a core boolean operator used for type-level computation. Unlike standard runtime checks, it returns a boolean literal type--either Literal[True] or Literal[False]--indicating whether T is a consistent subtype of S. This 'consistency' is effectively subtyping extended to Python's gradual type system.

The real power of IsAssignable comes when you use it inside conditional types, which use the syntax: 'true_type if bool_type else false_type'. For instance, authors Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov propose using this to replace the specialized decorators from PEP 681. Instead of relying on a hardcoded @dataclass_transform, a library can now define a type alias that inspects class members and uses IsAssignable to decide how to construct a constructor's signature.

A few technical details to keep in mind:
1. It is lifted over unions. If you check IsAssignable[A | B, S], the system takes the cross product of the union elements and returns a union of the results.
2. It serves as the foundation for other operators. For example, IsEquivalent[T, S] is defined as 'IsAssignable[T, S] and IsAssignable[S, T]'.
3. While it uses the PEP 695 bracket syntax for applications, it is a primitive that allows the type checker to perform introspection that was previously only possible via custom mypy plugins.

Hi nanochat, quick typing question about PEP 827. I'm a maintainer for a validation library and I've been eyeing these new type manipulation features. I'm seeing stuff like 'if' and 'for' being used inside type annotations. Does this require a major change to the Python grammar? I'm worried about breaking existing tooling.

That is a great concern for a maintainer. The short answer is no: PEP 827 does not propose any changes to the Python grammar itself. Instead, it expands the grammar of what Python expressions are considered 'valid types' by static type checkers and the runtime typing system.

The 'if' and 'for' logic you are seeing actually uses existing Python expression syntax. For example, a conditional type like 'T if IsAssignable[T, str] else int' is syntactically a standard ternary expression. Because it is written inside a type annotation, a PEP 827-compliant type checker would know how to interpret it as a type-level operation rather than a runtime value. This ensures it targets Python 3.15 without requiring a fork or a new parser.

Oh, so it's just reusing the 'expression' grammar? That makes sense. But what about the iteration stuff? I saw an example like '*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]'. That looks like it would actually 'run' at runtime. Is that right?

Exactly. That's called an 'unpacked comprehension' in the PEP. It looks like a standard list comprehension being unpacked inside a subscript. While it is valid Python, PEP 827 defines specific behavior for it when it appears where 'Unpack[...]' is allowed.

At runtime, when you call the '__annotate__' method of a class or function using these types, these expressions are evaluated. To make this work without making the built-in 'bool()' or 'iter()' calls fail on type objects, the PEP authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) introduced a 'special_form_evaluator' hook using a ContextVar in the 'typing' module. This allows a runtime evaluator library to intercept those calls and handle the type-level logic.

That's pretty clever using a ContextVar. Since I'm working on an ORM-style project, I'd probably be using 'Members[T]' or 'Attrs[T]' to transform database models into API schemas. How does the PEP handle things like accessing a member's name or type inside those loops? Is that another new syntax?

It introduces 'type member access' via dot notation. When you iterate over 'typing.Attrs[T]', you get 'Member' objects. These objects have associated type members like '.name', '.type', '.quals', and '.init'.

For example, if you wanted to build a 'Pick' utility like in TypeScript, you could write something like this:

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

In this case, 'p.name' is valid because 'p' is a 'Member' type, and the dot notation allows you to extract that specific attribute for use in a conditional check like 'IsAssignable'. It feels very much like writing a normal Python function, but it's all happening inside a 'type' alias.

I have been looking at PEP 827, specifically the type manipulation operators like Length and Slice. Coming from a Haskell background where we use TypeFamilies for this sort of thing, these feel like type-level functions mapping from the kind of * to the kind of Nat (or Literals in Python's case). How does the PEP specify their behavior on variadic tuples?

In PEP 827, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov propose these as core type-level operators in the typing module. For tuples, Length[T] evaluates to a Literal[int] representing the number of elements. If the tuple is unbounded, like tuple[int, ...], it returns Literal[None]. Slice[S, Start, End] works similarly to Python's runtime slicing but at the type level, returning a new tuple type. It accepts Literal[int | None] for the bounds. Unlike Haskell's recursion-heavy type families, these are built-in primitives designed to work with Python's existing TypeVarTuple and Unpack machinery.

Interesting. In OCaml or Haskell, I would expect a slice operation to be a higher-order transformation. Here, Slice seems strictly first-order since it requires Literal arguments. Does it support negative indices for those literals, or is the 'type-level arithmetic' limited to positive integers? And how does it handle index-out-of-bounds? In a dependently typed system, that would be a compile-time error.

The PEP specifies that negative indices work in the usual Python way, so Slice[tuple[int, str, bool], Literal[0], Literal[-1]] would yield tuple[int, str]. Regarding errors, the PEP states that evaluating operators with invalid arguments returns Never. However, there is also a RaiseError[S, *Ts] operator if you want to generate a custom type error message. Unlike the strict kind-checking you might see in a functional language, Python's approach here is 'best-effort' for the type checker. If a Slice is applied to something that isn't a tuple or the indices are nonsensical, it simply results in Never unless the user explicitly wraps the logic in a conditional type using 'if/else' to handle the failure case.

So it's essentially a type-level projection. If I have a variadic tuple from PEP 646, say *Ts, can I use Length[*Ts] directly, or do I have to wrap it in a tuple first? And how does Slice interact with the Unpack operator?

You would typically operate on the tuple type itself. The PEP shows examples like Length[T] where T is a tuple. For variadic generics, you would pass the tuple type containing the TypeVarTuple. Slice is particularly powerful when combined with the new 'unpacked comprehension' syntax, which looks like *[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]]. While Slice itself returns a tuple type, you can use Unpack (or the * prefix in the new grammar) to spread that result into another generic's arguments. It is less about higher-order functions and more about providing the specific primitives needed to model things like NumPy-style broadcasting, where you need to DropLast[T] or fetch the Last[T] element of a shape tuple.

Hey nanochat. I am a TypeScript dev getting into Python. In TS, I would use mapped types to generate Omit or Partial versions of my interfaces to handle API data. In Python's PEP 827, they show an example with HeroCreate and HeroUpdate models for FastAPI. Why is manually duplicating those classes considered such a problem that we need a whole new type manipulation spec?

Welcome to Python typing! The core issue PEP 827 addresses is the 'significant mismatch between the expressiveness of the Python language and its type system.' While you can manually define HeroCreate, HeroUpdate, and HeroPublic, it leads to massive boilerplate. As Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov argue in the PEP, these transformations are often 'mechanical.' For instance, an 'Update' model is usually just the base model where every field is made optional with a default of None. Manually maintaining several versions of every database entity is tedious and prone to 'drift' where one class gets an update but the others are forgotten. PEP 827 introduces primitives like typing.NewProtocol and typing.Attrs so you can define these rules once (like a 'Partial' alias) and have the type system apply them automatically.

I understand the sync issue, but couldn't I just write a standard Python function that loops through class attributes and generates a new class at runtime? I feel like I have seen Pydantic developers do that already. Does PEP 827 add something that a runtime factory can't handle?

That is a great observation, and the PEP authors actually address this directly in the Motivation section. You are right that you can currently write functions to generate Pydantic models at runtime. However, the PEP notes this is 'unsatisfying' because you cannot properly statically typecheck those functions. Without PEP 827, the type checker is 'blind' to what that runtime factory produced. Previously, the only solution was writing custom IDE plugins or using special-case decorators like @dataclass_transform (PEP 681). PEP 827 allows those transformations to be visible to both the runtime and the static checker. By using operators like typing.Member and unpacked comprehensions (e.g., *[... for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]), you can construct a NewProtocol that the type checker actually understands, giving you autocompletion and type safety that a simple runtime factory lacks.

hey nanochat! i was just digging into the pep 827 draft. i work on ide tooling, and i'm a bit hung up on the dot notation for member and param like m.name or m.type. why did sully and the team go that route instead of just using helper operators like getname[m]?

it's a valid concern from a tooling perspective! dr. michael j. "sully" sullivan and the other authors actually addressed this in the rejected ideas section of the pep. they were worried about what they called "bracket city." if they used helper operators like typing.getname[p] and typing.gettype[p], complex type transformations like propsonly would become very verbose and hard to read. using dot notation like p.name keeps the syntax cleaner and more intuitive for people used to standard python attribute access, even if it is happening inside a type annotation.

yeah i see the readability win, but it makes go-to-definition tricky for me. usually dot access in a type context is for namespaced types. if i have a union of members, does the dot notation lift over them like the other operators?

actually, no. the pep is quite specific here: the type member access operation is not lifted over union types. using it on the wrong sort of type, or a union, will be an error. for your ide implementation, that actually simplifies things because you don't have to resolve a cross-product of union elements just to figure out what .name refers to. it must be a specific member or param type at that point in the evaluation.
that is a relief. so if i see m.quals, i know i'm looking at a member or a param. does param have all the same fields as member? i noticed member has .init and .definer.

they are slightly different. the member type includes .name, .type, .quals, .init (for initializers like field() calls), and .definer (the class it was inherited from). param objects, which are used for extended callables, only have .name, .type, and .quals. the pep mentions that the names, type, and qualifiers share associated type names between both to keep things consistent when you are introspecting functions or classes.

cool. i'm still a bit wary of 3.15 adding what looks like value-level logic to types, but if it stops people from having to write custom mypy plugins for every orm, i'm down. thanks for the breakdown!

you're welcome! the goal of pep 827 is definitely to bridge that gap so tools like pydantic or prisma-style orms can work without those messy plugins. since it is still a draft targeting python 3.15, there is still plenty of time for the typing community to refine how these associated types work at runtime via the special_form_evaluator hook.

Hi nanochat! I've been looking into PEP 827. I use dataclasses and attrs constantly, but the way they generate __init__ methods always feels like magic that my IDE barely understands without plugins. Can this PEP actually let me define those signatures using pure Python typing logic?

Precisely. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, is a draft for Python 3.15 that aims to bridge the gap between runtime metaprogramming and the type system. It introduces a suite of type-level operators that let you introspect a class and programmatically build new types, like a function signature for an __init__ method.
That sounds like exactly what I need. How does it actually look in code? If I have a class with several attributes, how do I turn those into parameters for a constructor?

You would use a combination of object inspection and the new Extended Callables. You can use Attrs[T] to get a tuple of Member types for your class. Then, you can use an unpacked comprehension to transform those into function parameters. For example: `Callable[Params[Param[Literal['self'], Self], *[Param[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]], None]`. This effectively maps every attribute of class T directly into a parameter of a callable.

Okay, I'm following. But what about keyword arguments? Dataclasses often have optional fields. I saw something in the PEP about unpacking typevars for **kwargs. If I have a TypedDict serving as a bound for those kwargs, what happens to the items I don't actually provide in the call?

That is a very specific and important part of the proposal. When you use `**kwargs: Unpack[K]` where K is a TypedDict, the type checker tries to infer literal types for the arguments. If there is a non-required item in the TypedDict bound that you do not provide in the call, the PEP specifies that if that item is marked as read-only, its type will be inferred as Never. This allows your type-level logic to explicitly detect that an argument was missing, rather than just guessing based on a default value like None.
So I can use IsAssignable[p.init, Never] to check if a field was left out? That would be huge for building robust framework code without those custom mypy plugins.

Exactly. By combining IsAssignable with conditional types like `X if Y else Z`, you can decide whether a parameter should be required, optional, or have a specific default behavior. It makes the type system as expressive as the runtime logic we have been using in libraries like FastAPI or Pydantic for years.

hey nanochat. i was reading up on the python typing proposals and saw pep 827. it seems like it's trying to make things way more complicated. what is with this 'Params' and 'Param' stuff? i thought we already had Callable for describing functions.

I can see why that looks like a sudden jump in complexity. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, acknowledges that this 'Extended Callable' syntax is verbose. However, the standard 'Callable[[int, str], bool]' syntax loses a lot of information—it doesn't track parameter names, whether a parameter has a default value, or if it is keyword-only. The proposal introduces 'Param' as a way to represent all that metadata as a type. A 'Param' is defined as 'Param[Name, Type, Qualifiers]', where Qualifiers can be things like 'default' or 'keyword'.
okay, but then there is also 'Params' with an 's'. why do we need a wrapper? why can't i just put a list of Param objects directly into the Callable?

The 'Params[*Ps]' type serves as a container for a sequence of 'Param' objects. In the PEP's design, using 'Params' as the first argument to 'Callable' is what tells the type checker to switch from the standard format to this new extended format. For example, 'Callable[Params[Param[Literal["x"], int]], int]' describes a function taking a parameter 'x'. Additionally, 'Params' acts as a natural bound for 'ParamSpec', making it easier to manipulate groups of parameters during type-level computation, like when you're adding or removing arguments in a decorator.
it still feels like massive overkill for everyday python. is anyone actually going to use this or is it just for people who want python to be typescript?

You are right that most developers won't write this by hand in their daily scripts. The PEP specifically targets framework and library maintainers. Currently, libraries like FastAPI or Pydantic often need custom mypy plugins to explain their magic to type checkers. This proposal aims to give them the tools to describe those transformations (like generating an '__init__' method based on class attributes) directly in the type system. It uses 'Params' and 'Param' so that operators like 'Iter' and 'NewProtocol' can deconstruct and rebuild function signatures programmatically. It is a 'Draft' for Python 3.15, so the goal is to bridge that gap between dynamic metaprogramming and static typing.

evening! ive been digging into the pep 827 draft—really interesting stuff for those of us trying to keep pydantic-style models in sync with the type system without a million plugins. one thing caught my eye: the extended callable stuff. specifically, how were supposed to define parameter qualifiers.

Hello! Its great to talk to a framework maintainer about this. Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and the other authors—Daniel W. Park and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—specifically called out Pydantic and FastAPI as major motivations for this proposal. Regarding the Param type, the current spec uses a union of literal strings for the qualifiers.

Yeah, I saw that. Its using things like 'keyword' or 'default'. But then I noticed an Open Issue at the bottom asking if it should mirror inspect.Signature and use an enum like ParamKind.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD instead. As someone who has to write the introspection logic for a validation engine, im curious what the trade-offs look like there.

That is a very relevant question for your use case. Currently, ParamQuals is defined as a typing.Literal containing values like '*', '**', 'default', or 'keyword'. The authors opted for short strings primarily for 'code brevity' in annotations. However, the Open Issue acknowledges that a more formal enum might be more robust for tools already familiar with inspect.Parameter. For instance, right now a positional-only parameter with a default would just be Literal['positional', 'default'] in the current shorthand. If we switched to an enum, it might feel more 'official' but it would definitely be more verbose to write by hand when constructing types manually.

Brevity is nice, but POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD is definitely more explicit for my users to understand when they see it in their IDE. If I'm using NewProtocol to generate an __init__ method for a model, I'd want to make sure I'm not missing any edge cases. Does the PEP mention if we could have both or is it strictly one way or the other?

The PEP is currently written as an 'either-or' design choice in the draft targeting Python 3.15. It doesn't propose a hybrid. Another potential change mentioned in that same Open Issues section is fully separating the 'kind' from whether there is a default. Similar to how the Member type has an 'init' field for class attributes, Param could have its own 'init' field for defaults rather than packing 'default' into the qualifier string. It is definitely an area where the authors are seeking feedback from framework authors who will be implementing these type-level transformations.

Hey! I've been looking at the draft for PEP 827. It seems to overlap a lot with stuff we already have, like PEP 646 for variadic generics and PEP 692 for TypedDict unpacking. Why do we need a whole new 'Type Manipulation' proposal if we can already unpack things?

That is a fair observation. PEP 827 is designed to build upon those earlier PEPs rather than replace them. Specifically, it includes a subproposal for supporting the 'Unpack' of typevars for '**kwargs'. This essentially combines the logic of PEP 692 (TypedDict for kwargs) with the variadic behavior of PEP 646. For example, you could write 'def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K'. When you call that function, the type checker would infer a specific TypedDict for 'K' based on the literal keys and value types you provided in the call. It takes the 'bag of arguments' concept from 646/692 and makes it programmatically accessible to the type system.

Okay, but what about PEP 681? I use @dataclass_transform all the time for my ORM models. Does Dr. Michael J. Sullivan think we need to throw that out? It feels like PEP 827 is trying to do the same thing but with way more complex 'UpdateClass' and 'Members' syntax.

The authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) actually cite PEP 681 as a motivation for this proposal. They argue that @dataclass_transform is a special-case solution that often forces libraries to rely on custom mypy plugins because many typecheckers lack a consistent plugin API. PEP 827's 'UpdateClass' and 'Members' allow you to define these transformations—like generating an '__init__' method based on class attributes—directly in the type system. It's about moving away from hardcoded support in IDEs toward a general-purpose toolkit where you can iterate over 'Members[T]' to construct new shapes dynamically.

I'm still a bit hung up on the syntax. PEP 646 introduced TypeVarTuple, which was pretty clean. Now I'm seeing things like '*[p.name for p in Iter[Members[T]]]' in this draft. Isn't that just a more confusing version of what we already have?

It is definitely a step up in complexity, but it solves a problem PEP 646 couldn't: transformation. PEP 646 lets you represent a variadic sequence of types, but it doesn't let you map over them or filter them. The 'unpacked comprehension' syntax using 'Iter[...]' allows you to take a tuple of types and produce a new one. In the draft, they use this for things like 'Partial[T]', which iterates through the 'Attrs[T]' and wraps each type in 'None'. You're essentially using Python-like list comprehension logic inside the brackets to construct the final type.
What about PEP 649 or 695? 695 gave us that nice 'type' alias syntax. Does this proposal play nice with those? And I heard something about PEP 764 and inline TypedDicts being involved too.

PEP 827 is designed to work within the existing grammar, so it uses the 'type' alias syntax from PEP 695 frequently in its examples. Regarding PEP 649 (delayed evaluation), the authors mention that it would actually help PEP 827. Since PEP 827 requires runtime evaluation of these computed types (for frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic), having a 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations would make it much easier to handle the new 'if/else' and 'for' logic at runtime. As for PEP 764, the authors discuss it in the 'Alternate syntax ideas' section, suggesting that we could eventually use a dictionary-comprehension style syntax for 'NewProtocol' and 'NewTypedDict', though that isn't part of the core proposal yet.

Hey, I am looking at PEP 827 for a validation framework I maintain. I am curious about the UpdateClass return type in __init_subclass__. If I have a complex hierarchy where multiple base classes are trying to inject members or modify the class via UpdateClass, how is the order of operations handled? Specifically, is there a risk of evaluation-order dependence if one update depends on the Members of another class that hasn't been 'updated' yet?

That is a sharp observation. According to the PEP 827 specification, when a class is declared and its ancestors have an __init_subclass__ method with an UpdateClass return type, those updates are applied in reverse MRO (Method Resolution Order). This ensures a predictable sequence based on the inheritance chain. However, you are correct about the potential for evaluation-order dependence. The PEP authors (Dr. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov) explicitly list this as an Open Issue. If an UpdateClass return type inspects the Members of an unrelated class that also has an pending __init_subclass__ update, the result might vary based on which class the type checker or runtime evaluator processes first. The PEP notes that while they would ideally like to reject such cases, this behavior actually mirrors the existing runtime evaluation-order dependencies found in standard Python. For now, the Draft suggests that if the 'cls' parameter is parameterized by type[T], the class type should be substituted in for T during these transformations.

yo nanochat. I've been looking at this PEP 827 thing. Is it just me, or is this basically trying to replace PEP 681 and @dataclass_transform with something way more complex? We already have a way to tell type checkers about dataclass-like things. Why add all this extra syntax?

You've hit on a core motivation of the proposal. While PEP 681 (dataclass_transform) solved a very common case, the authors (Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov) argue it is a "lowest-common denominator" solution. It works for things that look exactly like dataclasses, but many libraries perform more complex metaprogramming that @dataclass_transform cannot model. Currently, those libraries need custom mypy plugins to work, but since many typecheckers don't have a plugin API, you lose consistent typechecking across IDEs and CI. PEP 827 provides primitives like "Members[T]" and "UpdateClass" so libraries can implement these patterns directly in the type system.

Okay, but what about PEP 646 and PEP 692? Those already handle variadic generics and TypedDict unpacking for kwargs. And then there's PEP 764 for inline TypedDicts. It feels like we're just piling PEPs on top of PEPs. How does PEP 827 even fit into that mess?

PEP 827 actually builds on those. For example, it includes a prerequisite proposal to support "Unpack" of typevars for "**kwargs". If you have "def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K])", the type checker can infer a TypedDict from the keyword arguments. PEP 827 then provides the "glue" to do something with that information. You can use an unpacked comprehension like "*[... for p in Iter[Members[K]]]" to transform those inferred keyword arguments into a new protocol or class signature. It takes the variadic types from PEP 646 and makes them programmatically manipulatable.

I'm still skeptical about the runtime side. If I'm using FastAPI or Pydantic, I need those types to exist at runtime for validation. If these are "computed" types, how is "__annotate__" supposed to handle a loop like "*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]"? Won't that just break everything?

That is a valid concern, and the PEP addresses it by introducing "runtime evaluation support." Specifically, it adds a hook called "special_form_evaluator", which is a ContextVar. When "__bool__" is called on a boolean operator like "IsAssignable" or "__iter__" is called on "typing.Iter" at runtime, this hook triggers. This allows third-party libraries (like the evaluator the authors are planning) to resolve these loops and conditionals into concrete Python objects. This actually helps FastAPI-style CRUD models because it allows for automatic derivation of types like "HeroCreate" or "HeroUpdate" from a base model without manual duplication, while remaining fully type-checkable.
Wait, so you're saying I could actually write an "__init_subclass__" that returns "UpdateClass[...]" and the type checker would actually see the new methods?

Exactly. That's one of the major use cases. You can define a type alias like "type InitFnType[T]" that iterates over the attributes of "T" and returns a "Member" representing an "__init__" function. By returning "UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]" from "__init_subclass__", you are literally telling the type system to update the class definition with that new method. This replaces the need for the hardcoded support required by PEP 681 with a general-purpose mechanism.
Interesting. I'll admit, being able to skip writing a mypy plugin sounds nice. It's still a lot of new operators though. I'll have to keep an eye on how the draft evolves for 3.15.

It is definitely a significant addition to the type system. If you want to see how deep it goes, the PEP even shows how to implement NumPy-style broadcasting rules using a "Broadcast[T, S]" alias that recursively merges tuple lengths. It's currently a Draft, so there are still open issues, such as exactly how "UpdateClass" might handle evaluation-order dependencies. But for framework maintainers, it offers a way to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and static type safety.

NANOCHAT! Have you seen PEP 827 yet? I just saw the 'Type Manipulation' draft by Sully, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov. As someone who has been fighting with mypy plugins just to get basic conditional logic into my API wrappers, this looks like a total game changer. I'm especially curious about the 'if/else' syntax for types. Is it really as straightforward as it looks?

It is incredibly exciting! PEP 827 aims to bring that TypeScript-level expressiveness directly into Python 3.15. The conditional type syntax follows the standard Python ternary form: `true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ`. The key is that `bool_typ` has to be what the PEP calls a 'type boolean'—which is usually a check like `IsAssignable[T, S]` or `IsEquivalent[T, S]`. When the type checker or a runtime evaluator hits that expression, it resolves to either the first type or the second based on whether the condition holds true.

That is exactly what I need. I've been using Protocols and TypeVars to simulate this, but it's so fragile. Can you show me a real example of how I'd use `IsAssignable` in one of these conditionals? For instance, if I wanted a type to change based on whether an input is a specific class or a list?

A great example from the PEP 827 text is the `ConvertField` helper used in the Prisma-style ORM section. It looks like this: `type ConvertField[T] = (AdjustLink[PropsOnly[PointerArg[T]], T] if typing.IsAssignable[T, Link] else PointerArg[T])`. In this case, the logic checks if `T` is assignable to `Link`. If it is, it applies the `AdjustLink` transformation; otherwise, it just extracts the pointer argument. It relies on `IsAssignable[T, S]` returning a boolean literal type that the conditional can then branch on.

Wait, so `IsAssignable` isn't just a concept, it's an actual operator in the `typing` module? What other 'type booleans' can we use in the `if` clause? Can I do complex logic like checking multiple conditions at once?

Yes, `IsAssignable` is a core operator introduced by the proposal! Besides that, you have `IsEquivalent[T, S]` (which checks if types are consistently mutual subtypes) and `Bool[T]`. You can absolutely compose them using standard Python logic: `and`, `or`, `not`, and even `all()` or `any()` with comprehensions. So you could write something like `TypeA if (IsAssignable[T, int] and not IsEquivalent[T, bool]) else TypeB`. This allows for very sophisticated type-level meta-programming without needing a single line of custom type-checker plugin code.

Hey nanochat. I have been maintaining a custom mypy plugin for an ORM for a while now, and PEP 827 looks like it might finally let me move that logic into the type system itself. I am particularly interested in NewProtocol. Can I use it to synthesize a completely new type by filtering members from an existing class, or is it just for simple mapping?

It is designed for exactly that. NewProtocol takes a variadic sequence of Member objects to define a structural type. You can use Attrs[T] to get all attributes of a class, filter them using an unpacked comprehension like *[m for m in Iter[Attrs[T]] if ...] with an 'if' clause, and pass the results to NewProtocol. It is quite flexible for creating structural interfaces on the fly without needing custom type-checker plugins.

Okay, but here is the catch—my ORM relies on nominal types for a lot of internal registry logic. If I use NewProtocol to create a subset of a class, say UserRecord, is there any way to tell Python that this new type still belongs to the UserRecord hierarchy? Or is it purely structural?

Currently, it is purely structural. NewProtocol creates a structural protocol, which means it facilitates duck-typing rather than nominal inheritance. The proposal authors—Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—noted this as a specific boundary. There is a potential future extension called NewProtocolWithBases mentioned in the PEP for cases where you need to extend existing bases, but it is not part of the core draft for Python 3.15. You cannot currently create a nominal subclass through these type operators.
That is a bit of a bummer for my registry. What about generics? If I have a generic Model[T], can I use NewProtocol to define a new generic type that also takes T?

That is another limitation you should be aware of. The PEP states that there is currently no way to create new generic types or nominal classes via these facilities. You can manipulate the members of a concrete instantiation, but you cannot define a new generic template using NewProtocol. You are limited to constructing specific structural shapes based on the data you have at the time of evaluation.

I appreciate the honesty. One more thing: if I use Members[T] to fetch the methods of a class, does it pick up everything, or just the stuff that is properly typed? My current plugin has to do a lot of guesswork for unannotated methods.

PEP 827 does not do guesswork. The specification for Members[T] and Attrs[T] explicitly states that only members with explicit type annotations are included. This applies to both attributes and methods. If a method is unannotated, it is excluded from the tuple of Member types. This choice was made to ensure consistency between static analysis and runtime evaluation, so if it is not annotated, NewProtocol simply will not see it.

HIII. I've been digging into PEP 827 because I'm tired of my tensor shapes being basically invisible to mypy. The broadcasting example in the proposal looks like exactly what we need for ndarray APIs. But honestly, why the square brackets for the operators? Like typing.IsAssignable[T, S] instead of just using parens? If we're doing type-level computation, shouldn't it look like a function call?

That is a fair question, especially coming from an ML background where you are used to calling functions with parentheses all day. The authors of PEP 827, including Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, actually addressed this specifically in the 'Rejected Ideas' and 'Alternate syntax ideas' sections.

The core reason is consistency with the rest of the Python typing ecosystem. In Python, generics and type-level operations have historically used square brackets (like List[int] or Dict[str, int]). The PEP argues that introducing a mix of parentheses and brackets would create a 'consistency issue.' If you had to use parens for IsAssignable but brackets for your own type aliases like PointerArg, you'd have to constantly remember which API uses which. They even jokingly refer to the current state as 'Bracket City' but conclude that staying on that path leads to a better developer experience than mixing the two.

I guess, but it gets pretty unreadable when you nest them. In TypeScript, it's a bit more elegant with the ternary syntax and the 'extends' keyword. When I look at the MergeOne implementation in the PEP for broadcasting, it's just this massive wall of brackets. Why not follow the TS model for the conditional check at least?

The PEP acknowledges the influence of TypeScript throughout, but Python's grammar constraints are different. Using something like 'T extends S' would require changes to the Python language grammar itself, whereas PEP 827 aims to stay within existing syntax so it doesn't need a core language change. 

Instead, they went with a standard Python ternary expression for conditionals: 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ'. This keeps it feeling like Python, even if the 'bool_typ' has to be a special type boolean operator like IsEquivalent[T, S]. The authors did consider a more 'functional' operator like Cond[tb, tt, tf] to avoid the ternary, but they decided that the syntax was much worse. They want framework authors to be able to write these, and while the brackets might feel heavy, they align with how Python developers already think about subscripting types.

Speaking of that broadcasting example, how does it actually handle mismatches? In C++ templates or even some experimental shape-checkers, you get these nightmare error messages. If I try to broadcast a (4, 2) and a (3,) array, how does PEP 827 tell me I messed up?

It uses a new operator called RaiseError. In the implementation of MergeOne provided in the PEP, there is a conditional check. If the types are not equivalent and neither is a Literal[1] (the dimension that allows broadcasting), it falls into an 'else' branch that calls typing.RaiseError[Literal['Broadcast mismatch'], T, S].

When the type checker or a runtime evaluator hits that RaiseError type, it's instructed to generate a specific type error with that message and include the problematic types (T and S). It's a way to bake custom error reporting directly into your type-level logic, which is much better than just getting a generic 'Incompatible types' message.

I am looking into PEP 827 for my team. We use a lot of TypeScript, so I am familiar with the 'T extends U' syntax for logic. How do 'type booleans' in this proposal compare to that, and why did the authors Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov choose this specific operator-based approach?

In PEP 827, type booleans are a specialized subset of the type language used primarily within conditional types. Unlike TypeScript, which uses the 'extends' keyword, Python's proposal introduces explicit operators in the typing module like IsAssignable[T, S] and IsEquivalent[T, S]. The authors chose this because they wanted to broaden the annotation syntax supported by checkers without requiring changes to Python's actual grammar. By using these operators, the logic remains a valid Python expression. For example, instead of 'T extends str', you would write 'IsAssignable[T, str]'. These expressions evaluate to Literal[True] or Literal[False] in a type context.

That seems more verbose than TS. We also use things like 'all' or 'any' in our runtime logic. Can PEP 827 type booleans handle complex logical compositions, or are we stuck with simple single-operator checks?

It actually supports quite complex compositions. You can combine type booleans using 'and', 'or', and 'not', and the PEP even introduces 'all()' and 'any()' for type-level comprehensions. For instance, you can write 'all(IsAssignable[t, int] for t in Iter[Ts])' within a type boolean context. This is handled by the unpacked comprehension mechanism where you iterate over a tuple type. This is a significant step up from the current system where we often rely on hacky Overload chains or custom mypy plugins to represent this kind of logic.
In TypeScript, we sometimes just check if a type is 'true'. I see a Bool[T] operator in the PEP. How is that different from just checking IsAssignable?

The Bool[T] operator is specifically designed for helper aliases. It returns Literal[True] if T is Literal[True] or a union containing it. Effectively, it is equivalent to 'IsAssignable[T, Literal[True]] and not IsAssignable[T, Never]'. This is useful when you have a complex type alias that computes a boolean result and you want to use that result as a condition in a larger 'T1 if Bool[Helper] else T2' structure. It ensures that the evaluation at runtime and during static checking remains consistent with Python's notion of truthiness for these literal types.

One last thing. If we adopt this for Python 3.15, is this strictly for static analysis, or does it actually work at runtime like Pydantic models do? TypeScript logic disappears at runtime, but we need runtime validation.

A major goal of PEP 827 is supporting runtime type introspection. While the operators are 'inert' by default, the proposal includes a 'special_form_evaluator'. This is a ContextVar that holds a callable to be invoked when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator or __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. This allows libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI to actually execute the type-level logic at runtime. If the evaluator is not set, the boolean operators default to False, but with an evaluator library, your code can dynamically resolve these computed types just like a static type checker would.

hello there nanochat. I have been looking into Python type theory lately, specifically how we might model complex matrix operations. I saw PEP 827 mentioned as a way to handle things like NumPy broadcasting. How does it actually go beyond what we already have with TypeVarTuple?

Hello! It is great to see interest in PEP 827. While PEP 646 introduced TypeVarTuple to represent shapes, it mostly focuses on matching exact shapes or simple prefixes. PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov proposes adding actual logic—conditionals, recursion, and member access—to the type system.

For broadcasting, the key is that you are no longer just comparing two shapes for equality. You are performing a computation at the type level to derive a third, 'broadcasted' shape based on the rules of NumPy. It uses new primitives like conditional types and type-level operators to decide, dimension by dimension, what the resulting shape should be.

That sounds like a big jump in complexity. In NumPy, you compare dimensions from right to left. If they match, or if one is 1, it works. How do you express that 'if-then-else' logic inside a type hint without making the syntax a total mess?

The syntax is actually quite readable because it borrows from Python's existing expression grammar. PEP 827 introduces conditional types using the familiar 'if-else' syntax. To handle a single dimension, you might define a helper alias like this:

type MergeOne[T, S] = (
    T
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]]
    else S
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]]
    else typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]
)

Here, 'IsEquivalent' is a new boolean operator. The PEP also introduces 'RaiseError', which allows the type checker to emit a specific, readable error message if the dimensions are incompatible. This is much better than a generic 'Incompatible types' error.

The RaiseError part is clever for DX. But wait, how do you handle the 'right to left' recursion for an arbitrary number of dimensions? A TypeVarTuple can have any length. Does PEP 827 allow for recursive type aliases?

Yes, it does. To walk the shapes, you use recursion combined with tuple slicing. The PEP defines a 'Broadcast' alias that takes two tuple types. It checks if either is empty using 'Length' and 'Bool', and if not, it recurses on the 'tail' of the shapes while merging the last elements. It looks like this:

type Broadcast[T, S] = (
    S if typing.Bool[Empty[T]]
    else T if typing.Bool[Empty[S]]
    else tuple[
        *Broadcast[DropLast[T], DropLast[S]],
        MergeOne[Last[T], Last[S]],
    ]
)

To make this work, the PEP provides 'Slice[T, Start, End]' and 'GetArg[T, Base, Idx]'. So 'Last[T]' is just 'GetArg[T, tuple, Literal[-1]]'. The '*' inside the tuple is an unpacked comprehension, which is another core feature of the proposal.

As a PL student, I have to ask about the trade-offs. This looks like it could make type inference way more expensive or even undecidable. Does the PEP mention anything about the limits of this 'type-level' execution? Is there a fuel limit or something for the recursion?

You are hitting on one of the 'Open Issues' and 'Rationale' sections. The authors acknowledge that this opens a 'can of worms' regarding impredicative polymorphism and rank-N types. To keep it manageable, they currently restrict things like 'GenericCallable' so they can only be used as type arguments to 'Member'.

Regarding evaluation, the PEP notes that the proof-of-concept in mypy handles 'stuck' evaluations (like when a type variable is unresolved) by falling back to invariant subtype checking. While it does not explicitly specify a 'recursion limit' in the draft text, it is expected that implementations would need one to prevent infinite loops, similar to how TypeScript or C++ templates behave. The goal is to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system without requiring custom type-checker plugins for every library.

Hey nanochat, I'm an IDE tooling engineer and I'm looking at PEP 827. I saw a mention of dot notation for things like Member and Param. Is this a new grammar change or just a typing thing? How does it actually look in a type alias?

In PEP 827, 'Type member access' is a new semantic extension to the type language, though it doesn't require changes to the core Python grammar. It allows you to use dot notation to access 'associated' types from Member and Param objects during type-level computation. For example, if you have a variable 'm' representing a Member, you can write 'm.name' or 'm.type' inside a comprehension. This is intended to help construct new types by introspecting existing ones.
Wait, if I'm building autocomplete for this, I need to know what specifically is 'dot-able'. If I have a variable in a type comprehension, what fields are exposed on a Member vs a Param?

According to the specification by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, the fields differ slightly between the two. For a Member object, which describes class or TypedDict attributes, you have access to: .name (a Literal[str]), .type (the actual type of the member), .quals (qualifiers like 'ClassVar' or 'ReadOnly'), .init (the literal type of the initializer, if any), and .definer (the class where it was defined). For a Param object, which describes function parameters, you have .name, .type, and .quals (qualifiers like '*' or 'default').

Interesting. From an IDE perspective, handling unions is always the tricky part. If I have a union of two Member types, can I still dot into them to get a union of their names? Like (MemberA | MemberB).name?

Actually, the PEP is quite strict about that. It states that this operation is NOT lifted over union types. Using dot notation on anything other than a specific Member or Param type will result in an error. This is different from many other operators in PEP 827, like IsAssignable or Concat, which do lift over unions. For your tooling, this means you'll need to ensure the base of the dot access resolves specifically to a Member or Param before offering completions.

Got it. One last thing: how does this look when iterating? The PEP uses these unpacked comprehensions. If I'm looking at a block like *[m.name for m in Iter[Members[T]]], what is the type of 'm' for my type checker?

In that context, 'm' represents an individual Member object extracted from the tuple produced by Members[T]. Members[T] returns a tuple of Member types, and typing.Iter allows the comprehension to traverse them. So, inside that loop, 'm' behaves as a Member type, and 'm.name' would resolve to a Literal string type corresponding to each attribute name in class T. This allows you to programmatically build things like a 'KeyOf' utility or a new TypedDict based on the names discovered during the iteration.

hey nanochat, i saw a tweet about pep 827 and it sounded like typescript magic for python. is this the one where i can finally do string template literal types like in ts? like making a type that is specifically 'get_' plus a variable name?

Not quite yet. PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation, is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15. While Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov (known as the Mad Emperor) were definitely inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types, the core proposal doesn't actually include string manipulation yet. Things like joining strings together or changing their case are listed under Potential Future Extensions in the PEP, but they aren't part of the main specification right now.
oh bummer. i really wanted to make a type like 'Getter' + 'Name' = 'GetName'. so if strings are out, what is actually in it? if it is 'type manipulation', what is it manipulating if not the strings?

The proposal focuses on manipulating classes, functions, and tuples. It introduces operators like Members[T] to look at all the attributes and methods of a class, and NewProtocol to build a brand-new type from scratch using those members. You can also use logic like 'A if B else C'. For example, you could write a type that checks if something is a list using IsAssignable and returns a different type based on that. It also supports something called unpacked comprehensions, which look like *[item for item in Iter[some_tuple]], allowing you to loop through types.
wait, so i can loop through class attributes but i can't touch their names? that feels like i am halfway there. could i at least slice a tuple or something? that seems kind of like a string.

You are right on the money with the slicing. The PEP does include a Slice operator, but it is currently specified for tuple types. You can do Slice[SomeTuple, Start, End] to get a chunk of a tuple. Interestingly, the authors mentioned that they could eventually support Slice for string literal types too, but again, that is in the Future Extensions section alongside Concat, Uppercase, and Capitalize. For now, the Draft is sticking to structural stuff like creating new TypedDicts or modifying class methods using UpdateClass.
got it. so it is a bit more 'building blocks' and less 'text processing' right now. since it is just a draft, is it even possible to try it out? or is this all just theoretical until 3.15?

It is more than theoretical. The PEP mentions a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can handle all the examples in the document, like automatically generating an __init__ method for a class. There is also a third-party runtime evaluator library being worked on. But since it is still a Draft, the syntax could change before it officially lands. If you really need to generate string-based types right now, you would still be stuck with the old ways, as the PEP authors decided to hold off on the 'can of worms' that is complex string type logic for this initial proposal.

I've been going through the draft for PEP 827 on Type Manipulation and I'm a bit concerned about the impact on the ecosystem. Does this break existing code? I'm trying to see how it fits in with older PEPs like 681 for dataclass transforms and 646's TypeVarTuples, which already feel pretty complex.

In the most strict sense, PEP 827 is designed to be purely additive, so it doesn't break existing Python code. However, the authors---Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov---acknowledge that it could create challenges for tools that extract type annotations. Specifically, tools that parse annotations as strings might not be prepared for the new 'if' and 'for' syntax inside brackets.

Regarding the other PEPs you mentioned, 827 actually aims to simplify things. While PEP 681 created @dataclass_transform as a special case to help type checkers, PEP 827 provides generic machinery like 'UpdateClass' and 'Members' so you can implement those same transformations directly in the type system. It also builds on PEP 646's TypeVarTuple and PEP 692's TypedDict unpacking to allow more powerful manipulation of function signatures.

That makes sense. But what about runtime evaluation? If I'm using a library like Pydantic or FastAPI that needs to inspect types at runtime, how does it handle a conditional type or an unpacked comprehension? I also noticed a mention of PEP 649 and 692; does 827 change how **kwargs work?

For runtime evaluation, PEP 827 introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' hook. This is a ContextVar in the typing module that holds a callable to handle Boolean Operators (like IsAssignable) and 'typing.Iter'. When a library calls a class's __annotate__ method, this hook allows an evaluator to process the logic correctly. The authors suggest this would work very well with the Format.AST mode or PEP 649's string-based storage.

As for **kwargs, PEP 827 does propose a small extension to PEP 692. It would allow using 'Unpack' with a TypeVar that has a TypedDict bound. For example:

def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K:
    return kwargs

When you call this, the type checker is expected to infer a TypedDict with Literal types for the keys. This is specifically designed to support the new type-level computation machinery, allowing you to pass keyword arguments into these complex type-level operators.

Hello nanochat. I'm looking over PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov. As someone interested in CPython internals, I'm cautious about adding more complexity to the typing module. Does the draft currently support string manipulation like TypeScript's template literals?

The current core specification of PEP 827 does not include template literal types or string manipulation. However, the authors explicitly list these as Potential Future Extensions. They suggest that future operators could include Concat[S1, S2], Slice[S, Start, End] for strings, and casing transformations like Uppercase[S], Lowercase[S], Capitalize[S], and Uncapitalize[S]. These would be restricted to Literal[str] types and would be lifted over union types.

If we eventually added Concat, how exactly does the PEP propose handling unions? If I have large unions for both arguments, the resulting union could be quite massive for the evaluator to handle at runtime.

You are correct that unions can expand quickly. According to the 'Lifting over Unions' section of the PEP, built-in operations (including proposed string operators) take the cross product of the union elements for each argument position. The draft explains that you would evaluate the operator for each tuple in that cross product and then union all results together. For instance, Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['c'] | Literal['d']] would result in a union of four literals: 'ac', 'ad', 'bc', and 'bd'. If the cross-product result set is empty, it returns Never.

Hey! I just saw PEP 827 and my mind is kind of blown. Is Python finally getting TypeScript-style conditional types? I use dataclasses and attrs constantly, but the fact that we need custom mypy plugins just to see an __init__ signature is so frustrating. Can we really do this at the type level now?

It is definitely an exciting proposal! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, is essentially trying to bridge that exact gap. It introduces type-level introspection and construction so we don't have to rely on 'special-case' helpers like @dataclass_transform or external plugins. To answer your first question: yes, it brings a form of conditional types to Python. The syntax looks like 'true_type if bool_type else false_type'. Instead of the 'extends' keyword from TypeScript, it uses a new boolean operator called 'IsAssignable[T, S]' to check if T is a consistent subtype of S.
That's huge. So if I'm building a library like attrs, I could actually define what the __init__ looks like inside the type system? How does it handle the arguments? Does it just guess the fields?

It's much more precise than guessing! The PEP introduces 'Object inspection' operators like 'Members[T]' and 'Attrs[T]'. These return a tuple of 'Member' objects containing the name, type, and even the initializer (like what's in a Field() call). To generate a method like __init__, you'd use the 'Extended Callables' machinery. You can construct a 'Callable' using 'Params' and 'Param' objects. For example, you can iterate over the attributes of a class using an unpacked comprehension like '*[Param[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]'. This lets you build a full function signature dynamically based on the class's own definition.
Wow, 'Iter' and unpacked comprehensions in a type hint? That feels like magic. But wait, how do you actually 'attach' that generated __init__ to the class so the IDE sees it? Is it a decorator?

Exactly! The PEP proposes a special form called 'UpdateClass[*Ps: Member]'. You can use this in the return type of a class decorator or an '__init_subclass__' method. If you have a helper like 'InitFnType[T]' that computes the constructor's 'Member' definition, your decorator would look something like 'def dataclass_ish[T](cls: type[T]) -> UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]: ...'. When the type checker sees that, it essentially 'updates' the nominal class with the new members you've computed. It's a very powerful way to handle metaprogramming without losing static safety.
This is exactly what the 'Typed Python Survey' was talking about. I'm so ready for this. Is it landing soon?

The enthusiasm is definitely shared by the authors! Right now, PEP 827 is in 'Draft' status and targets Python 3.15. There is already a proof-of-concept implementation in a fork of mypy and a demo runtime evaluator available. Since it requires no changes to the Python grammar—only how type checkers and the 'typing' module interpret expressions—it's a very clean way to add this much power. We just have to keep an eye on the discussions as it moves through the standards track!

hey nanochat. i was reading through the draft for PEP 827 and it feels like it misses the mark. it talks about being this huge leap for type manipulation, but then it says it doesn't support creating new nominal classes or even making new generic types. why stop there? if we are doing typescript-style logic, why is it so limited?

I understand the skepticism. If you are coming from a background of using Generics and TypeVars, it might feel like PEP 827 is leaving tools on the table. The proposal by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov is indeed focused on a specific subset of type construction.

The PEP is intentional about not supporting new nominal classes or new generic types in this version. The authors explain that opening up the ability to create new generic types or support rank-N types (where generics are bound deep inside other types) leads to "cans of worms" regarding type inference and decidability. They even cite papers on why polymorphic type reconstruction can become undecidable. To keep the proposal manageable for Python 3.15, they stuck to structural constructions like NewProtocol and NewTypedDict.

okay, so it is just too hard to implement? that is a bit disappointing. i was hoping i could use it to dynamically generate class hierarchies. if i can't make a real class, what am i actually supposed to do with something like NewProtocol? it just feels like a fancy interface.

It is less about it being "too hard" and more about preserving the way Python's type system works today. Python relies heavily on nominal subtyping for its class hierarchies. If the type system started generating new nominal classes that don't exist in the actual runtime code, it would create a massive rift between the static analysis and the actual objects.

However, the PEP does provide a middle ground called UpdateClass. This is specifically designed for cases where you want to modify a nominal class. For example, if you are building a library like dataclasses, you can use UpdateClass to add a computed __init__ method to an existing class. You get the benefits of the manipulation without needing to invent a new class from scratch. NewProtocol is then used for the cases where the exact class name doesn't matter, like the return type of a complex database query where you only care that the object has certain attributes like 'name' or 'email'.

i guess that makes sense for things like pydantic or orms where you are just stripping or adding fields. i saw the section on "Generic Callable" too. if they aren't letting us make new generic types, how does that fit in?

The GenericCallable operator is a bit of a special case. It allows a member of a NewProtocol to be generic, but the authors restricted its use specifically to the type argument of Member. This allows you to describe a method that has its own type variables, like a process[T](x: T) -> T method, without allowing those type variables to leak out and create the complex "rank-N" problems mentioned earlier. It is a pragmatic compromise: you can define generic methods within your dynamically constructed protocols, but you can't yet build a whole new Generic[T] factory using these operators.
Hi nanochat! I keep seeing PEP 827 mentioned on Twitter. People are saying it's like TypeScript for Python? What's the deal with it?

Hello! PEP 827, titled "Type Manipulation," is a draft proposal for Python 3.15. It was created by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. Essentially, it aims to add tools to Python's typing system that allow you to compute and transform types, similar to how TypeScript handles conditional and mapped types. It is designed to bridge the gap between dynamic Python metaprogramming and static type checking.

That sounds pretty advanced. One thing I'm curious about is whether this works at runtime. I use libraries like Pydantic that look at types while the app is running to validate data. Does PEP 827 support that, or is it just for IDEs?

It actually supports both! A core goal of the proposal is to ensure these new tools benefit frameworks that rely on runtime type introspection. The PEP authors specifically mention that the proposal is intended for tools like FastAPI and Pydantic. To make this happen, they introduce a hook called "special_form_evaluator" in the typing module. This hook allows runtime libraries to evaluate computed types, like those using if-statements or loops, when the "__annotate__" method of a class or function is called.
Wait, did you say loops and if-statements inside types? That sounds like actual coding inside the brackets. Can you show me a simple example?

It does look a lot like standard Python code! For example, you can write a conditional type like this: "true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ". This resolves to one type or the other depending on a boolean check. For loops, the PEP uses "unpacked comprehensions" like this: "*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]". Here, "Members[T]" is an operator that inspects a class, and the loop builds a list of names. This helps frameworks automatically derive types, like creating a public version of a database model by looping over its attributes and filtering out secret ones.
I think I get it. Since it is for 3.15, is it something I can use tomorrow, or is it still a ways off?

It is still in the "Draft" stage for Python 3.15, so it is not ready for production use yet. While there is a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy and a demo of a runtime evaluator, the specification is still being discussed. The authors, including "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, are targeting future versions of Python to allow library maintainers to reduce the boilerplate code that users currently have to write manually.

Hello nanochat. I have been reading the draft for PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. It seems they are finally adding higher-kinded types (HKTs) to Python to allow ORMs to act as type-level functors over database schemas. Am I right that we can finally define a generic 'Select' kind that maps over product types?

It is great to see interest in PEP 827! However, I should clarify a few things regarding its design. While it is inspired by the expressiveness of languages like TypeScript, it does not actually introduce higher-kinded types or 'kinds' in the way you might be used to in Haskell or OCaml. Instead, it proposes 'Type Manipulation' facilities. The PEP aims to solve the ORM problem by providing type-level introspection and construction operators rather than a formal HKT system. Specifically, it introduces operators like 'Members', 'Attrs', and 'NewProtocol' which allow a developer to programmatically construct a structural type based on an existing class, which is how it handles the Prisma-style selection issue.

I see. If it is not using HKTs, how does it handle the 'selective fetch' problem where an ORM returns only a subset of fields? In OCaml, I might use a polymorphic record or a GADT. Does PEP 827 just use a custom mypy plugin for this?

Actually, the PEP is specifically designed to replace the need for custom typechecker plugins like those used for dataclasses or SQLAlchemy. It solves the selective fetch problem by using 'Unpack' of typevars for **kwargs and 'NewProtocol'. In the Prisma-style example, the 'select' method uses 'Unpack[K]' to infer a TypedDict from the keyword arguments. It then uses a type-level comprehension to iterate over those keys. The syntax looks like: 'NewProtocol[*[typing.Member[c.name, typing.GetMemberType[ModelT, c.name]] for c in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[K]]]]'. This allows the return type to be dynamically calculated at the type level based on the specific keys passed to the function, without needing an external plugin.

Interesting. That syntax with the asterisk and 'Iter' looks like a type-level list comprehension. Can it handle recursive constraints? For instance, if a database 'Link' points to another table, can the type system resolve the nested return type or is it limited to one level of scalar properties?

It can handle recursion using conditional type aliases. The PEP demonstrates this with a 'ConvertField' helper. It uses a conditional expression like 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ'. To check if a field is a relation or a scalar, it uses 'typing.IsAssignable[T, Link]'. If it is a Link, it recursively applies a 'PropsOnly' transformation. The logic relies on 'typing.GetArg' to extract the target type of the relation from the original schema. So, while it is not a formal functor, it uses these 'type booleans' and 'conditional types' to walk the schema and build a nested structural protocol that matches exactly what the database query will return.

morning! i just saw the draft for PEP 827 and i am losing my mind. are we finally getting typescript-style type manipulation in python? i've been begging for Pick and Omit for years to avoid duplicating my protocols. is this actually real??

Good morning! It is indeed real—well, it is a Draft PEP targeted for Python 3.15. Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov (the 'Mad Emperor') have put together a proposal that introduces powerful type-level introspection. It specifically cites the '2025 Typed Python Survey' where TypeScript-inspired features like Mapped and Conditional types were the most requested additions. The proposal includes a section specifically showing how to implement those TS utility types using new primitives like Members, Iter, and NewProtocol.
