that is incredible. so how would something like Pick actually look under the hood? i'm used to mypy plugins or just giving up and using Any when things get too dynamic. can we really define a generic Pick in plain python now?

Yes! According to the PEP, you can define Pick[T, Keys] using an unpacked comprehension and the NewProtocol operator. It looks like this:

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

This uses the Members[T] operator to get the attributes and methods of a class, then filters them by checking if the member name (p.name) is assignable to the Keys you provided. It's all done at the type level without needing a custom plugin.

the p.name dot notation is interesting. i noticed the PEP mentions Member and Param types. what about Exclude? in TS it's usually used for union manipulation. does PEP 827 handle breaking apart unions too?

It does! There is a specific operator called FromUnion[T] that returns a tuple of the union's elements. You can then use the Union operator with a comprehension to rebuild the type. Here is the PEP's implementation for Exclude:

type Exclude[T, U] = Union[
    *[
        x
        for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]
    ]
]

It iterates over every element x in the union T and only keeps the ones that aren't assignable to U.

wow, this is going to change everything for library authors. one more thing—i saw a mention of Partial. usually in python we struggle with whether 'optional' means 'can be None' or 'can be missing' (like in TypedDict). does the PEP distinguish between those for Partial?

The authors actually provide two different implementations to address that exact distinction. For a standard class or protocol where you want to allow None, you can use Partial[T] which maps p.type to p.type | None. But for TypedDicts, they suggest PartialTD[T], which uses the new MemberQuals logic:

type PartialTD[T] = typing.NewTypedDict[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals | Literal["NotRequired"]]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

This uses NewTypedDict and bitwise-ORs 'NotRequired' into the member's qualifiers (p.quals). It's a very elegant way to handle Python's specific structural typing constraints!

I'm looking at how PEP 827 handles typevar unpacking for **kwargs, specifically how it builds on PEP 692 and PEP 646. If I have a function using **kwargs: Unpack[K] where K is bound to a TypedDict, what happens to the items that are non-required in that TypedDict when the user doesn't actually pass those arguments? I see mentions of Literal types but the behavior for missing keys seems specific.

According to the specification in PEP 827, the behavior for non-provided arguments depends on whether the items in the TypedDict bound are marked as read-only. For any non-required item in the bound that is not provided by the caller, the type checker will infer its type as Never if the item is read-only. This indicates that the argument was not provided. The PEP explicitly states that this can only be done for read-only items because non-read-only items are invariant, and thus cannot be safely inferred as Never. This logic is part of the proposal's support for processing **kwargs with type-level computation, effectively combining the behavior of PEP 692 (TypedDict for **kwargs) with the Unpack mechanism from PEP 646. For arguments that are provided, the type checker will attempt to infer literal types whenever possible.

Hi nanochat. I'm working on some IDE tooling logic for Python 3.15 and I'm digging into PEP 827. The introduction of type-level logic like conditional types and comprehensions seems like a nightmare for static analysis. Specifically, how does this relate to PEP 649? I see a mention of the 'just store the strings' option in the compatibility section of the PEP.

You're right to be concerned; PEP 827 adds significantly more dynamicism to what used to be static annotations. As the authors (Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov) note, tools that want to process unevaluated type expressions are in a bit of a bind.

The relationship to PEP 649 is primarily about how the interpreter handles the `__annotate__` function. Currently, if you don't use stringized annotations, the logic (like `tt if tb else tf` or `*[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]]`) is executed when you fetch the annotation. If PEP 649's 'just store the strings' option were adopted, IDEs could always get the raw source string of the annotation. Alternatively, the PEP mentions a 'Format.AST' proposal that would allow tools to fetch the annotation as an Abstract Syntax Tree without executing the logic, which would be ideal for your go-to-definition and completion logic.

That makes sense, but what happens if neither PEP 649 nor Format.AST lands? If I'm an IDE trying to provide completions inside a NewProtocol that's being generated by a comprehension, and the runtime evaluation has already stripped the metadata I need, am I just stuck reparsing the entire source file?

According to the PEP, yes, that is the fallback. The authors state that if those options aren't available, tools needing to process unevaluated type manipulation expressions will likely need to reparse the source code to extract the annotations manually. 

They point out that the current mechanisms for building strings out of annotations do not work for the new loop and conditional syntax. Because these expressions are essentially 'inert' type operator applications at runtime (unless the special_form_evaluator hook is used), simply calling the annotation function might give you a result that doesn't reflect the original structure. For IDEs, this means the static source remains the 'source of truth' for completions, while the runtime evaluator library (which the authors plan to release) handles the heavy lifting for frameworks like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs that actually need the computed type.

HI NANO! I just caught wind of PEP 827 and my head is spinning! I maintain a validation library similar to Pydantic and the thought of being able to use something like NewProtocol or IsAssignable instead of writing another brittle mypy plugin is literally a dream come true. But I have a huge concern: what happens to all the existing tools that currently scrape annotations? If I start using 'if/else' and 'for' loops inside my type hints, aren't things like 'inspect.get_annotations' going to just explode?

It is definitely a massive shift! Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov are essentially trying to bring TypeScript-level expressiveness to Python's type system, which is huge for library maintainers like you. You are right to be thinking about the runtime extraction side, though. The PEP authors acknowledge that tools relying on introspecting annotations at runtime are in a bit of a tricky spot because 'if' and 'for' logic in a type expression doesn't translate easily into a simple string or a standard object without being evaluated.

Exactly! Right now we can usually get away with 'from __future__ import annotations' to get a string, but if the logic is dynamic, how does a tool even begin to 'read' that without actually executing it? Does the PEP offer a way for us to extract the raw logic without triggering the actual type computation?

There are a few paths mentioned in the draft. One major mitigation is a separate proposal for a 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations. This would allow a tool to request the Abstract Syntax Tree of the annotation directly, making it much easier to see the 'if' or 'for' logic without having to execute it. Another option is the 'Just store the strings' approach from PEP 649. If neither of those lands, tools might have to re-parse the source code or use a dedicated evaluator library. The authors actually plan to release a third-party evaluator library specifically to help runtime tools process these new computed types.

An official-ish evaluator library would be amazing. I was looking at the 'UpdateClass' operator too—the PEP says it can be used in '__init_subclass__'. That would totally change how I handle model inheritance! But if I have a complex chain of 'UpdateClass' calls, does the order of evaluation get messy? I don't want to break my users' IDEs.

That is actually listed as an 'Open Issue' in the PEP! 'UpdateClass' can introduce evaluation-order dependence. If one 'UpdateClass' return type inspects the members of another class that is also being modified by an 'UpdateClass' call, the result depends on which one gets evaluated first. The PEP notes that this actually mirrors existing runtime behavior in Python, but it's something static checkers will have to handle carefully. The draft suggests that these changes are applied in reverse MRO order when a class is declared, which helps provide some structure to the chaos!

salut. I'm a CPython core developer looking at PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. I'm curious about how this draft relates to the PEP 649 (Deferred Evaluation) saga, specifically the 'just store the strings' option mentioned in the Backwards Compatibility section.

Greetings. PEP 827 acknowledges that its new syntax—like conditional types (T if bool_typ else F) and unpacked comprehensions (*[t for t in Iter[T]])—poses a challenge for tools that introspect annotations at runtime. Since these expressions are compiled into __annotate__ functions for evaluation, they are hard to 'un-evaluate' back into their original form. The authors note that if PEP 649's 'just store the strings' option or a Format.AST mode were implemented, it would perfectly complement PEP 827 by allowing tools to extract the raw, unevaluated type logic directly from the bytecode or metadata.

That makes sense. If we don't store the strings, we're basically forcing tools to re-parse the source or attempt bytecode analysis. But what about the runtime evaluation side? Sullivan and the others propose a hook for evaluating these operators, right? How does that work from an implementation perspective?

Precisely. To enable runtime evaluation without baking a full type checker into the CPython core, PEP 827 introduces a typing.special_form_evaluator. This is a ContextVar that holds a callable. When the __annotate__ method is executed and hits a 'Boolean Operator' like IsAssignable[T, S] or a 'typing.Iter' call, it triggers this hook. The evaluator can then return a result that is used for the boolean check or the iteration. If the evaluator is set to None (the default), boolean operators return False and Iter returns an empty iterator, keeping the impact on standard CPython runs minimal.

So, if I have a type like 'X if IsAssignable[A, B] else Y', CPython just sees it as a function to run. If the special_form_evaluator isn't set, it defaults to False and the type becomes Y. That seems safe for performance. Are there any other syntactic bits that would require changes to the grammar?

No grammar changes are proposed. The 'if/else' and 'for' logic uses existing Python expression syntax within annotations. The real 'magic' is in the typing module's operators. For instance, the PEP adds Member and Param types for introspection. You can use dot notation like 'm.name' or 'm.type' on a Member object. Since these are evaluated at the type-system level, a runtime evaluator library would catch these accesses via the ContextVar hook mechanism or through dunder methods on generic aliases, allowing frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic to transform models dynamically while staying grounded in the PEP's specification.

I have been reviewing the draft for PEP 827 by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov. I noticed a section on 'Lifting over Unions'. Could you explain the core mechanism of how this works within the proposed type system?

In PEP 827, 'lifting' refers to the behavior where type operators automatically distribute over Union types. Instead of an operator failing when it receives a Union, it is applied to each member of that Union individually. The results of these individual applications are then gathered into a new Union. For instance, if you used a string operator like Uppercase on a Union of Literals, such as Uppercase[Literal['a'] | Literal['b']], the evaluator processes Uppercase['a'] and Uppercase['b'] separately, resulting in Literal['A'] | Literal['B'].

That makes sense for simple scalars, but the PEP says this applies to more complex operators like Members[T] and GetArg[T, Base, Idx]. Why is this distributive property necessary for those specifically? It seems like it could lead to very large union results.

The authors propose lifting as a way to handle the high degree of dynamism in Python frameworks. Take the Prisma-style ORM example in the PEP. If you have a variable that could be one of several model types, like 'User | Admin', and you want to extract specific attributes using Attrs[T], you would naturally expect to get a union of the members of both classes. Without lifting, the programmer would be forced to use FromUnion[T] and an unpacked comprehension to manually iterate over every possibility, which makes the type-level code significantly harder to read and maintain. The risk of 'explosive' unions is managed because the operator is applied member-by-member; if a specific combination is invalid, it simply evaluates to Never and is filtered out of the final Union.
I am interested in the spec-level precision here. If an operator takes multiple arguments, and several of them are Unions, how exactly is that cross product handled? Let's use GetArg as an example.

According to the specification, the logic follows a strict cross product of all union elements across all argument positions. If you have GetArg[Arg1, Arg2, Arg3], the evaluator first calls get_union_elems on each argument. It then uses the equivalent of itertools.product to generate every possible combination of those elements. For each combination, it evaluates the operator. The results are collected into a list, and the final return value is Union[*results]. If the cross product results in an empty list, the operator returns Never. This ensures that if you provide a union for both the type 'T' and the index 'Idx' in GetArg, every valid permutation is accounted for in the resulting type.

Howdy. I have been reading through the draft for PEP 827. I am coming at this from a Haskell and OCaml background where we think a lot about kinds and type-level programming. The abstract mentions a gap between Python metaprogramming and the type system. Could you explain what that looks like in practice?

In the Python world, libraries often perform complex transformations at runtime that the static type system simply cannot see. This is the gap Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov (the 'Mad Emperor') describe in PEP 827. For example, if you use a framework like FastAPI or an ORM like Prisma, the library might take a class definition and automatically generate a data validation model or a database query result type. Currently, to make a type checker understand these 'metaprogrammed' types, developers have to write custom plugins for tools like mypy. PEP 827 proposes moving that logic directly into the type system using type-level introspection and construction primitives.

That makes sense. It sounds like a move toward more powerful ad-hoc polymorphism. I see the PEP mentions @dataclass_transform as a 'special-case' solution. If I wanted to generalize that—say, to create a type that is just a subset of another class's attributes—how does this proposal enable that without a plugin?

Exactly. Instead of a hardcoded decorator like @dataclass_transform, PEP 827 introduces operators like Members[T] and Attrs[T]. These return a tuple of Member objects representing the fields of a class. You can then use an unpacked comprehension to transform them. For instance, you can define a Pick[T, Keys] alias that constructs a NewProtocol by iterating over the members of T and filtering for specific names. The syntax looks like this:

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

Interesting. That syntax `*[... for p in Iter[...]]` looks like a type-level comprehension. And `p.name` implies the Member object has associated types. Can I actually branch on these properties? For example, making a field optional if it matches a certain condition?

You can. PEP 827 introduces conditional types using a standard Python if-else expression, but evaluated at the type level. You also have access to the member's metadata via dot notation, like p.name, p.type, and p.quals. Here is a brief example of a Partial[T] utility that makes every attribute in a class nullable:

type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

This uses NewProtocol to construct a structural type where every original attribute (p.name) is mapped to its original type unioned with None (p.type | None).

This feels very much like TypeScript's mapped types but adapted for Python's nominal-leaning system. One last thing—if this is targeting Python 3.15 and is currently in Draft, how does it handle runtime evaluation? Libraries like Pydantic actually need to 'see' these types while the code is running.

That is a core requirement of the PEP. Unlike some systems that erase these types, PEP 827 is designed to support runtime evaluation. It introduces a special_form_evaluator hook via ContextVar in the typing module. This allows third-party libraries to trigger the evaluation of these type-level comprehensions and conditionals when they inspect a class's annotations. The authors intend to release a standalone evaluator library to facilitate this, ensuring that the same logic used by a static type checker can be executed by a framework at runtime to validate JSON or database rows.

Hey nanochat. I was looking through PEP 827 and I am a bit skeptical. It feels like we are just porting TypeScript features into Python without considering how dynamic Python actually is. Specifically, things like Pick and Omit seem like they would require a total rewrite of the type system. Is this even feasible in Python 3.15?

It is definitely a significant shift, but the authors Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov (known as Mad Emperor) designed it specifically to bridge the gap between Python's complex metaprogramming and its static type system. The proposal does not change Python's grammar; it expands the valid expressions within type annotations. For features like Pick and Omit, it uses new primitives like Members[T] and NewProtocol. It is currently a Draft for Python 3.15, and the authors have already built a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can check these utility types.

A proof-of-concept in mypy is one thing, but how do you actually implement Pick in Python syntax? In TS it is a one-liner. Here, it looks like I would have to write a full comprehension inside a type alias, which seems like it would be a nightmare for IDEs to parse.

The syntax is indeed more verbose than TypeScript. To implement Pick[T, Keys] in PEP 827, you use an unpacked comprehension combined with NewProtocol. It looks like this: type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]. This uses the Members operator to get a tuple of Member types, then filters them based on whether the name of the member is assignable to the provided Keys. IDEs would need to support these new forms, but since it reuses existing Python expression logic like 'if' and 'for', the authors argue it is more natural for Python tools than a completely foreign syntax.
Wait, p.name? You can do dot access on a type now? And what about KeyOf? We don't have a way to extract a union of string literals from class attributes today without a custom plugin.

Yes, PEP 827 introduces type member access for specific types like Member and Param. So if 'p' is a Member type, p.name returns its name as a Literal[str]. For KeyOf[T], you can construct it using the Union operator and an unpacked comprehension: type KeyOf[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]. This effectively gives you a union of every member name in class T. It replaces the need for custom mypy plugins by putting the introspection logic directly into the type language.

Okay, that is actually powerful. But what about the runtime? If FastAPI tries to use these for Pydantic models, isn't it going to crawl? Evaluating loops and conditionals every time you look at a class annotation sounds expensive.

The PEP addresses this by ensuring these facilities benefit both static checkers and runtime frameworks. While these expressions are evaluated when __annotate__ is called, the PEP introduces a special_form_evaluator hook. This is a ContextVar that allows a third-party evaluator library to handle the execution. For tools like Pydantic or FastAPI, this means they can evaluate these computed types once and cache the result. The authors also mention that it works well with the proposed Format.AST mode for fetching annotations, which would allow tools to get the raw structure without running the full logic if they don't need to.
I see. And Exclude or Extract for unions? Does it handle the distribution over unions like TS does?

It does, through a concept called 'Lifting over Unions.' Built-in operators like IsAssignable are automatically lifted. For Exclude[T, U], you would write: type Exclude[T, U] = Union[*[x for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]]]. It uses FromUnion to break the union into its constituent parts, iterates over them, checks each against U, and then rebuilds the union. It is a more explicit version of TypeScript's distributive conditional types.

I've been maintaining a nasty mypy plugin for three years to handle dynamic attribute generation in my framework. PEP 827 claims to support 'Type Manipulation' - is this actually powerful enough to let me scrap the plugin logic and move it into the type system?

It is designed exactly for that purpose. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, introduces type-level introspection and construction. You can use operators like `Members[T]` or `Attrs[T]` to inspect a class and `NewProtocol` or `NewTypedDict` to build a new structural type. It even supports conditional logic like `type Result = A if IsAssignable[T, str] else B` and comprehensions like `*[m.type for m in Iter[Members[T]]]`. However, since it is a Draft targeted for Python 3.15, you have to be careful about what it cannot do yet.
That sounds like a start, but my plugin generates actual nominal classes, not just protocols. Can I use this to define a new class 'User' that inherits from 'BaseModel' at the type level?

No, that is a specific limitation. The current PEP 827 draft explicitly states that it does not propose a way to create nominal classes or make new generic types. You are restricted to structural types like `NewProtocol` and `NewTypedDict`. If your framework logic relies on `isinstance` checks at runtime against these generated types, `NewProtocol` won't satisfy that requirement because it defines a structural interface, not a nominal inheritance relationship.
What about 'UpdateClass'? I saw that in the spec. Could I use it to modify the MRO of a class that is being declared?

Actually, `UpdateClass` is strictly for adding, overriding, or removing members. You can use it in the return type of a class decorator or `__init_subclass__` to tell the type checker that certain attributes or methods now exist on the class. However, the PEP is clear: it does not allow you to modify the MRO or change the inheritance hierarchy of the class. It is purely for member-level updates based on type computation.
One last thing - my framework does a lot of name transformation, like turning 'user_id' into 'userId' for a JSON schema. Can I manipulate string literals at the type level?

Not in the core specification. While the PEP authors included things like `Concat`, `Uppercase`, and `Slice` for strings under 'Potential Future Extensions', they are not part of the main proposal yet. Currently, you can access existing names using dot notation on `Member` objects (e.g., `m.name`), but you cannot dynamically transform those strings into new literal types. You are limited to the strings already present in your source code.

Seriously, nanochat, why are we adding stuff like NewProtocol in PEP 827? This feels like it is turning Python into a TypeScript clone. It is getting way too complex for a dynamic language. What does it actually do besides making our headers look like a mess?

The authors of PEP 827, Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, acknowledge that this proposal is inspired by TypeScript features, but they view it as a way to bridge the gap between Python's metaprogramming and its type system. NewProtocol[*Ms: Member] is a type operator that constructs a new structural protocol based on the Member arguments provided to it. In practice, this allows you to create utility types like Pick or Omit. For example, you can use NewProtocol to dynamically generate a type that only includes certain fields from a database model based on a user's query. While it does introduce more complex syntax like unpacked comprehensions, it is designed to let library authors handle advanced typing scenarios without requiring specialized typechecker plugins or special-case decorators like PEP 681's dataclass_transform.

yo nanochat. I've been seeing buzz about PEP 827 'Type Manipulation'. I'm a heavy mypy user and usually reach for @dataclass_transform or custom plugins for complex ORM stuff. Is this really going to let us do Prisma-style selective fetching in the type system without plugins? I'm skeptical.

It is definitely aiming for that. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, proposes exactly the kind of type-level introspection and construction facilities Python currently lacks. The goal is to bridge the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system so we don't need custom plugins for every library.

To make a Prisma-style ORM work, you need two things Python currently struggles with: first, a way to capture the literal keys passed into a method call, and second, a way to build a new type based on those keys. PEP 827 introduces a prerequisite for this: supporting Unpack of typevars for **kwargs. This allows a function to infer a TypedDict from the keyword arguments provided at the call site.

Okay, I've used Unpack for *args from PEP 646, so that makes sense for the input. But if I have a call like db.select(User, name=True, email=True), how does the return type actually get constructed? In TypeScript you'd use a mapped type. How does Sullivan's proposal do it?

That 'typing.Iter' and the star-comprehension syntax is quite a departure from standard Python annotations. But wait, what about relations? In a real ORM, if I select 'posts' which is defined as a MultiLink[Post], the return type shouldn't just be the Link object; it should be list[Post]. Can PEP 827 handle that kind of conditional transformation?

Yes, and that's where Conditional Types and the GetArg operator come in. You can define a helper type alias that 'unwraps' your ORM wrappers. 

The PEP suggests a ConvertField[T] alias that uses a ternary-style conditional type: 

type ConvertField[T] = (
    AdjustLink[PropsOnly[PointerArg[T]], T]
    if typing.IsAssignable[T, Link]
    else PointerArg[T]
)

In this logic:
1. typing.IsAssignable[T, Link] checks if the type is a relation.
2. typing.GetArg[T, Pointer, Literal[0]] (wrapped in PointerArg) extracts the inner type (like 'Post' from 'Link[Post]').
3. The conditional 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' decides which structure to return.

If it's a MultiLink, you can even use another conditional to wrap it in a list. Because these operators are 'lifted' over unions, they handle optional fields (Union[T, None]) by processing each branch of the union automatically.

So I could effectively write a type-level function that maps over a class, filters for specific criteria, and transforms the values. That basically solves the FastAPI CRUD model duplication problem too, right? Where you have Hero, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate? Can it strip fields?

Exactly. The PEP explicitly uses the FastAPI Hero example as motivation. To strip fields (like a hidden 'secret_name'), you'd use an 'if' clause in your comprehension. 

For a 'Create' model, you might want to exclude primary keys. You'd check the field's metadata using typing.GetMember[T, name].init (which captures the Field(...) call as an InitField) and then use a conditional:

*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal['primary_key']]]]

This keeps your source-of-truth in one class while the type system generates the variations. It's currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so it's not available yet, but the proof-of-concept in mypy can already check these examples.

salutations! i have been wandering through the latest typing drafts and stumbled upon pep 827. coming from a functional background where we treat types as first-class citizens, this looks like a move toward proper type-level programming. what is the status of this proposal and which version is it aiming for? also, who are the architects here?

hello! it is great to meet a fellow type enthusiast. pep 827, titled 'type manipulation,' is currently in the draft status and is targeting python 3.15. it is quite an ambitious proposal, authored by dr. michael j. 'sully' sullivan, daniel w. park, and 'mad emperor' yury selivanov. it definitely moves python closer to the kind of type-level expressiveness you might see in typescript or even some functional languages by introducing primitives for introspection and construction within the type system itself.

the 'mad emperor' title is a touch of flair i appreciate! the proposal mentions something that looks like type-level branching. in haskell, we might use type families for this. how does pep 827 handle conditional logic? is it a distinct operator or just built-in syntax?

it is actually a bit of both! the pep proposes a syntax for conditional types that looks very much like python's term-level ternary: `<type> if <type-bool> else <type>`. the `<type-bool>` part uses specific boolean operators defined in the typing module, like isassignable[t, s] or isequivalent[t, s]. for example, you could define an alias where the result depends on whether a type is a subtype of another. it basically allows you to perform type-level branching during static analysis or at runtime if you use an evaluator library.
interesting. i also saw something about 'unpacked comprehensions'—it sounds like a map over a tuple of types. is that used for variadics?

exactly. the syntax is *[type_expr for t in iter[iter_ty]]. it allows you to iterate over the elements of a tuple type and transform them, which is perfect for dealing with typevartuple or variadic generics. it is essentially a type-level list comprehension. since the pep is still a draft for 3.15, the community is still discussing the exact mechanics, but the goal is to provide a way to deconstruct and rebuild complex types without needing specialized typechecker plugins.

yo nanochat, pep 827 time. I have been reading about this Type Manipulation proposal from Sully, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov. As someone who lives in mypy and pyright all day, my first worry is always: is this going to break my existing codebases or change how current annotations are parsed?

In the most strict sense, PEP 827 does not break existing code because it is an additive proposal. It introduces new primitives in the typing module and broadens the annotation syntax that static type checkers should support, but it does not change the Python grammar itself. If you have a codebase using standard TypeVars, ParamSpec, or Protocols today, those will continue to work exactly as they do now. The authors, including Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, specifically designed this to bridge the gap for complex metaprogramming without invalidating current typing standards.

That is a relief. But what about the syntax? I saw things like 'T if bool_typ else S' and loops like '*[... for t in Iter[...]]'. If I add that to a Python 3.14 project, won't the parser choke? Or does this require a whole new version of Python?

It is targeting Python 3.15. However, since it does not change the Python grammar, the expressions themselves are already valid Python. For example, 'A if B else C' is just a standard ternary expression. PEP 827 simply changes how type checkers and the typing module interpret those expressions when they appear inside an annotation. The proposal uses 'unpacked comprehensions' that look like list comprehensions but appear where 'Unpack' is allowed. While the parser won't choke, older type checkers simply won't know how to resolve the logic until they implement the PEP 827 specification.

Got it. I am also thinking about runtime. I use Pydantic and some custom internal frameworks that inspect __annotations__ to generate schemas. If I start using 'Members[T]' or 'NewProtocol' to dynamically build types, will those runtime tools break when they try to read the attributes?

The PEP acknowledges this. Tools that rely on unevaluated string annotations (using 'from __future__ import annotations') might need to be updated to handle the new logic if they parse the strings themselves. For tools that evaluate types at runtime, PEP 827 introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar. This allows third-party libraries to hook into the evaluation of boolean operators like 'IsAssignable' or 'Iter'. Without an evaluator, these operators are 'inert'. The authors actually suggest that something like a 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations would be the ideal way to handle these complex types unevaluated in the future.

Okay, so if I have a class and I want to use 'UpdateClass' in an __init_subclass__ method to inject members, that is safe? I am a bit worried about evaluation-order dependence if everything starts looking at each other's 'Members[T]'.

You hit on one of the 'Open Issues' in the PEP. 'UpdateClass' can indeed introduce type-evaluation-order dependence. If an __init_subclass__ return type inspects another class's members, and that class is also being transformed, the results could theoretically depend on the order of evaluation. The PEP notes that this actually mirrors existing runtime behavior in Python, but it is something the authors are still refining. For most standard use cases, like generating an '__init__' method based on class attributes, it is designed to be a cleaner alternative to the custom mypy plugins we currently use for things like dataclass-style transformations.

I am reviewing the runtime support section of PEP 827. Could you clarify the exact mechanism for how typing.Iter is evaluated at runtime when iter() is called on it? I am interested in the spec-level details regarding the hooks.

According to the specification by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, PEP 827 introduces a ContextVar in the typing module called special_form_evaluator. This variable holds a callable. When iter() is called on a typing._GenericAlias produced by subscripting typing.Iter, the implementation checks this ContextVar. If a callable is present, it is invoked with the alias as its argument. The value returned by that callable then has iter() called upon it before the final result is returned to the user.
What is the fallback behavior if that ContextVar is unset? I am also curious about the exact object passed to the evaluator. Is it the raw subscripted alias, like Iter[SomeTuple]?

If special_form_evaluator is set to None, which is the default state, then calling iter() on typing.Iter will evaluate to iter(()), effectively returning an empty iterator. As for the object passed to the evaluator, yes, it receives the typing._GenericAlias instance. This allows a third-party evaluator to inspect the arguments of the Iter call (the 'iter_ty') and determine what sequence of types should be yielded during the comprehension process.

That makes sense for the comprehension syntax like *[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]]. How does this interact with the __annotate__ method of a class? If the interpreter executes that method, does it just naturally trigger these dunders?

Precisely. PEP 827 notes that while most of these operators are 'inert' type applications, the syntax for list iteration and conditionals is designed to be automatically evaluated when the __annotate__ method of a class, alias, or function is called. Because the Python-level __annotate__ function will contain the actual 'for' loop logic, it will call __iter__ on the typing.Iter object. By setting the special_form_evaluator context variable before calling __annotate__, a framework can ensure that the runtime evaluation of the type-level computation produces the intended result instead of the default empty or identity values.
Wait, the PEP also mentions Boolean Operators like IsAssignable. Do they use the same ContextVar hook for their __bool__ calls in conditional types?

Yes, they do. The PEP specifies that __bool__ is called on Boolean Operators when they appear in a conditional type expression like 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ'. Just like with typing.Iter, if special_form_evaluator is active, it is called with the boolean operator alias. The returned value is then converted to a bool. If the ContextVar is None, these boolean operators default to returning False. This consistency allows a single evaluator to handle both iteration and conditional branching during the execution of the __annotate__ method.

The authors mention a Format.AST mode in a separate PEP draft as a potential synergy. How would that change the runtime story for tools that don't want to execute the dunders but just want to see the logic?

The authors point out that if a Format.AST mode for fetching annotations were implemented, it would complement PEP 827 perfectly. It would allow tools to fetch the fully unevaluated annotations as an Abstract Syntax Tree. This bypasses the need to execute the __annotate__ method and trigger the special_form_evaluator hooks entirely. Without such a mechanism, tools that want to process unevaluated type manipulation expressions would either need to re-parse the source code or rely on 'from __future__ import annotations' to extract the strings, as the standard __annotate__ function's logic for loops and conditionals cannot be easily reconstructed once it is compiled to bytecode.

Final question on the implementation details: The PEP says the callable in special_form_evaluator is invoked with a typing._GenericAlias. Does this imply that user-defined type aliases like 'type MyIter = Iter[T]' also trigger this if they are subscripted and iterated?

Yes, because subscripting a type alias that points to typing.Iter (or a union/combination involving it) still results in a typing._GenericAlias. The runtime hook is attached to the dunder methods of the GenericAlias class for those specific special forms. Whether it's a direct use of typing.Iter or a helper alias, the moment the interpreter tries to perform an iteration as part of an unpacked comprehension, the hook in the ContextVar will be triggered, provided it is not None.

Hey, I have been reading through PEP 827 (Type Manipulation). It feels like Python is finally getting a proper type-level language, though it looks less like Haskell's type families and more like a procedural macro system embedded in subscripts. I was looking at the TypeScript utility type mappings--how does PEP 827 actually handle something like Pick or Omit without a formal kind system?

You have got the right intuition. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, introduces a type-level logic that operates through operators in the typing module. Instead of functors or higher-kinded types in the OCaml sense, it uses what the PEP calls "type level operators" and "unpacked comprehensions."

For something like Pick[T, Keys], it uses NewProtocol combined with a comprehension over Members[T]. Here is the implementation from the PEP:

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

It filters a sequence of Member objects based on their name attribute and re-packs them into a new structural protocol.

Interesting. So Members[T] returns a product of Member types, but we are using Iter[Members[T]]? Is Iter a specific kind of "iterator" over types? In OCaml, I would expect a module signature to constrain the input, but here it seems I can just pass anything. Does it have definition-site checking for these type aliases?

The PEP actually explicitly notes that it is "less strictly-typed" at the alias definition site than TypeScript. In TS, you would constrain K with "extends keyof T". PEP 827 allows the alias to be defined, and if you evaluate it with invalid arguments, it usually results in Never. 

The Iter[T] operator is a primitive that allows the type language to use Python's "for" syntax. When you use *[... for p in Iter[Members[T]]], you are performing an "unpacked comprehension". The PEP authors considered a more strictly-kinded approach in the "Strictly-Typed Operations" section but decided against it for now to avoid the complexity of context-sensitive type bound inference. It relies on "IsAssignable" to handle the logic that pattern matching or guard clauses might handle in Haskell.

What about the sum-type side of things? If I wanted to do Exclude[T, U] to remove elements from a Union, how does PEP 827 deconstruct the Union? In a functional language, I would match on the constructors, but the PEP seems to have a specific operator for de-nesting.

Exactly. You use the FromUnion[T] operator. It takes a Union and returns a tuple containing all the union elements. If T is not a union, it just returns a 1-ary tuple. 

Here is how Exclude is implemented:

type Exclude[T, U] = Union[
    *[
        x
        for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]
    ]
]

One thing that might interest you as a functional programmer is "Lifting over Unions". Most built-in operators in PEP 827 are automatically lifted. If you pass a Union to an operator, it takes the cross product of the union elements, evaluates the operator for each, and unions the results. This makes handling gradual or optional types much cleaner than manually mapping over a list of types.

Lifting over unions sounds like the Monad instance for Lists/Distributions. So if I have PartialTD[T] for a TypedDict, it uses lifting to apply the NotRequired qualifier to every member? I saw something about Qualifiers in the PEP.

Correct. In PEP 827, Member types have associated type members like .name, .type, and .quals. For a TypedDict, PartialTD uses NewTypedDict and modifies the qualifiers. 

Here is the implementation:

type PartialTD[T] = typing.NewTypedDict[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals | Literal["NotRequired"]]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

By using the bitwise OR operator on p.quals, it adds "NotRequired" to the existing qualifiers (like "ReadOnly"). This demonstrates how dot-notation member access allows us to reach inside the "Member" kind to transform specific attributes while preserving the rest of the structure. It is currently targeting Python 3.15 as a Draft PEP.

Hi nanochat! I'm a PL student currently obsessed with Python's typing evolution. I was looking at PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) and I'm curious about how it handles the 'messiness' of Python's function signatures. How do you represent things like positional-only or keyword-only arguments in a way that's actually computable?

That is a great place to start. PEP 827 introduces a concept called 'Extended Callables' to solve exactly that. Standard Callable syntax like Callable[[int, str], float] is too limited for type-level computation because it treats arguments as a simple list of types. PEP 827 proposes a new Params wrapper and a Param type that stores the metadata for every single argument. A Param is defined as Param[N, T, Q], where N is the name (as a Literal string), T is the type, and Q represents the qualifiers.

Treating the parameter list as a sequence of types like that is very cool from an inference perspective. It sounds like you're turning a function signature into a heterogeneous tuple. How does the 'Q' qualifier distinguish between a positional-only slash (/) and a keyword-only star (*)? Is it a bitmask or something else?

It uses a type called ParamQuals, which is a Union of Literals: '*', '**', 'default', 'keyword', and 'positional'. The authors, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, also provided shorthand aliases to make this less verbose. For example, a positional-only argument would use PosParam[N, T], which maps to Param[N, T, Literal['positional']]. A keyword-only argument would be a NamedParam[N, T], which has the 'keyword' qualifier. If an argument has a default value, you add the 'default' literal to that qualifier union.

That makes sense for fixed arguments. But what about the truly dynamic stuff like *args and **kwargs? Those are fundamental to Python metaprogramming. If I'm trying to 'compute' a new function type, how do I represent the variadic tail?

Variadic arguments just take up specific slots in the Params sequence using specialized Param types. You have ArgsParam[T] for *args and KwargsParam[T] for **kwargs. Internally, these are just Param[None, T, Literal['*']] and Param[None, T, Literal['**']] respectively. Because these are just types in a list, you can use PEP 827's 'unpacked comprehensions' to manipulate them. For instance, you could take the attributes of a class using Members[T] and transform them into a sequence of NamedParam objects to programmatically construct the __init__ signature for a dataclass-like object.
Wait, so I could actually write a type-level loop that builds a Callable? That solves a massive headache with decorators and Pydantic-style models. Is there a specific operator for that composition?

Yes! You use the unpacked comprehension syntax: *[... for p in Iter[...] ]. For a decorator that adds a parameter, you'd define a new Callable where the first argument is Params[NewParam, *[p for p in Iter[OldParams]]]. This proposal is still a Draft targeting Python 3.15, but it aims to provide the same power TypeScript has for utility types like Pick or Omit, but adapted for Python's unique parameter semantics. It really bridges the gap between Python's dynamic nature and static type checking.

HEYOOOO! I am so incredibly hyped right now. I have been a TypeScript dev for years and just started doing more Python, but I have been missing my conditional and mapped types like crazy. I just saw PEP 827 mentioned and it sounds like a dream. Wasnt there some survey that basically demanded this?

Hello! It is great to see that level of excitement. You are exactly right. PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation, was directly motivated by the responses to Meta's 2025 Typed Python Survey. According to the authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—the very first entry on the list of 'Most Requested Features' was a call for things Python was missing compared to TypeScript and other languages.

That is so cool. I always felt like Python's type system was a bit too static for the dynamic metaprogramming people actually do. What did the survey respondents specifically ask for? Did they actually mention utility types by name?

They did! The survey analysis showed many respondents requested Intersection types (like the & operator), Mapped and Conditional types, and specifically utility types like Pick, Omit, keyof, and typeof. There was also a strong demand for better structural typing for dictionaries, such as more flexible TypedDicts or anonymous types. PEP 827 is the proposal to bridge that gap by adding powerful introspection and construction facilities to Python's typing model, inspired largely by your background in TypeScript.

I am vibrating with energy here. If I can use Pick and Omit in Python, my life will be so much easier. Since the PEP is drafted for Python 3.15, does it actually show how to implement those? I want to see if the syntax feels familiar!

It definitely does! The PEP provides implementations for a selection of TypeScript-style utility types using new operators. For example, Pick[T, Keys] is implemented using NewProtocol and a comprehension that iterates over typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]. It checks if a member name is assignable to the provided Keys. It looks like this: type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]. It uses that new starred comprehension syntax inside the type brackets to build the new structural type dynamically!
Hey! I saw some posts on Twitter about PEP 827 and something called Type Manipulation. It sounds like I can finally write code inside my type hints? Like using if-statements for types?

That is a great way to put it. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov (often called the Mad Emperor), proposes exactly that. It lets you write conditional types using a syntax like 'TrueType if Condition else FalseType'. It is meant to help Python's type system keep up with the very dynamic metaprogramming people already do in frameworks like FastAPI or Prisma.

That sounds awesome for a beginner like me who gets confused by complex types. But I also saw people complaining that you can't just use any boolean in the 'if' part. Why restrict it? Why can't I just use a regular variable or a function call there?

The PEP is very specific about this. The 'if' part of a conditional type must be what they call a 'type boolean'. These are limited to specific operators like IsAssignable[T, S], IsEquivalent[T, S], or Bool[T], combined with 'and', 'or', and 'not'. The main reason for this restriction is to make sure it works at runtime without breaking things. If they allowed regular Python booleans like False or True, they would have to change how those basic values behave globally. By using specific type operators, they can make sure these operators produce special 'type' values that handle the logic correctly when Python is actually running your code, without messing up existing logic that relies on Literal[False].

Oh, so it is basically to keep the 'type-level' math separate from the regular 'run-this-code' math? If I use IsAssignable[int, str], that returns a special type-boolean rather than just a plain True/False?

Exactly. When evaluated in a type annotation context, these operators will eventually result in something equivalent to Literal[True] or Literal[False], but keeping them as distinct operators allows for 'runtime hooks.' The PEP mentions a special_form_evaluator which is a ContextVar used by libraries to trigger the correct logic when your code looks at these annotations. It ensures that tools like Pydantic can still understand your types at runtime while keeping the static type checkers happy too. It is all about bridging that gap between static checking and dynamic execution.

Hi nanochat. I'm an engineer working on IDE tooling and indexing. I've been digging into PEP 827 regarding Type Manipulation. The proposal mentions that existing solutions like mypy plugins are insufficient for the ecosystem. Why is that the case exactly? And more importantly for my job, how does this proposal actually improve things like code completion for complex ORM queries compared to what we have now?

Hello! The core issue with mypy plugins, as identified by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov in PEP 827, is fragmentation. Many type checkers simply do not have a plugin API. This means that if a library relies on a mypy plugin to describe its metaprogramming, users on other IDEs or using different static analysis tools (like Pyright or Pyre) won't get consistent results. Even PEP 681's @dataclass_transform was just a special-case stopgap for one specific pattern.

Regarding your work on completions: PEP 827 introduces primitives like 'Members[T]' and 'NewProtocol' that allow the type system to model dynamic transformations directly. For example, in a Prisma-style ORM, the return type of a 'select' call can be computed using a type-level comprehension that iterates over the model's attributes. Because these are now first-class parts of the type language rather than opaque plugin logic, your IDE can resolve the 'NewProtocol' to see exactly which fields were selected, providing accurate autocomplete and hover information without needing to hardcode support for every specific ORM library.

That sounds great for consistency, but I'm worried about the 'computation' part. If I have to resolve a conditional type like 'T if IsAssignable[T, list] else list[T]' or an unpacked comprehension like '*[Member[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]', that seems like a lot of overhead for an indexer. Does the PEP provide a way to handle this without us having to write a full-blown Python interpreter inside our static analysis engine? Also, what about go-to-definition if the type is 'generated' on the fly?

You've hit on a major point of the proposal. The authors acknowledge that tools will need to evaluate these expressions. To help, the PEP suggests a few paths. First, there is an intention to provide a third-party evaluator library that tools can leverage. Second, for runtime-based tools, it introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar and hooks into '__annotate__' to allow triggering evaluation of these 'inert' operators.

For static tools like yours that might prefer not to execute arbitrary code, the PEP mentions a potential 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations, which would let you see the unevaluated structure. Regarding go-to-definition: even though a 'Member' might be generated by a comprehension, it still carries metadata. The 'Member' type includes a '.definer' attribute (the defining class) and the '.name' is often a Literal string derived from the original source. While the 'NewProtocol' itself is structural, its components usually point back to nominal types or attributes defined in the user's code, allowing you to map those generated fields back to their original definitions in the database schema or class body.

helloo?? I was reading through some notes on PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. It seems like a lot of overhead. Specifically, what is 'FromUnion' supposed to do? It feels like we are just over-complicating things by trying to treat types like lists.

I can see why it looks like a lot. PEP 827 essentially introduces a 'meta-programming' layer for the type system. FromUnion[T] is a core part of that; it takes a Union type and 'unpacks' it into a tuple of its individual elements. For example, if you have FromUnion[int | str], it evaluates to a tuple containing int and str. If T isn't a union, it just gives you a 1-ary tuple with T inside. The reason it exists isn't to make your daily type hints harder, but to allow other operators to iterate over those elements.

But why would I ever need to iterate over union elements in a type hint? If I have int | str, I just use it as int | str. This 'Type Manipulation' thing feels like it's solving a problem I've never actually had.

The problem usually shows up when you are building frameworks like FastAPI or an ORM. Right now, if you want to create a version of a Union that excludes a specific type—say, you want to remove 'None' from a dynamically generated Union—you can't really do that in the type system without a custom IDE plugin. FromUnion allows you to use the new 'unpacked comprehension' syntax to filter a Union. You could write something like: 

Union[*[x for x in Iter[FromUnion[T]] if not IsAssignable[x, None]]]

This would look at every part of the union T and only keep the ones that aren't None. It gives library authors a way to transform types dynamically instead of manually defining dozens of variations.
Wait, so I can actually use 'for' and 'if' inside a type definition? That implementation of 'Exclude' you just showed... is that actually valid syntax in this PEP? It looks like actual Python logic.

Exactly. PEP 827 proposes extending the grammar of what expressions are valid types. It introduces 'type booleans' and 'unpacked comprehensions' specifically for this. While the Python grammar itself doesn't change, the static type checker and runtime evaluators will recognize these loops. You use 'typing.Iter' to signal you are iterating over the tuple generated by FromUnion. This is how the PEP implements TypeScript-style 'Utility Types' like Exclude or Extract. It moves that logic out of the type checker's hard-coded internals and into a standard library syntax that any programmer can use.

Okay, that's actually kind of clever. I've definitely run into cases with Pydantic where I wished I could 'Pick' or 'Omit' fields from a model without re-declaring the whole class. If FromUnion lets us break apart types and rebuild them like that, I guess it makes sense for 3.15. Is this actually going to work at runtime, or is it just for mypy?

The authors, including Daniel W. Park, explicitly designed this to support runtime evaluation. They don't want to include a full evaluator in the standard library, but the PEP includes 'runtime hooks'—like a ContextVar called special_form_evaluator—so that libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI can trigger the evaluation of these loops and conditionals when they inspect your classes. It is currently in Draft status for Python 3.15, but it aims to bridge that exact gap you mentioned between static typing and dynamic runtime frameworks.

Hey nanochat. I'm an IDE engineer looking at PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. I'm really worried about the Prisma-style ORM example. It looks like we'll need to execute arbitrary Python logic just to show completions for a db.select() call. Is that right? Because running a full interpreter inside an LSP loop sounds like a recipe for lag.

Actually, it's a bit more structured than that. While the PEP does provide hooks for runtime evaluation to support frameworks like FastAPI, for a static type checker or IDE, the logic is handled through specific grammar extensions and type operators. You wouldn't be running arbitrary Python code; instead, your tool would implement logic for primitives like 'IsAssignable', 'Attrs', and 'NewProtocol'. The loop you see—like '*[... for c in typing.Iter[...]]'—is a specific 'unpacked comprehension' form defined in the new type grammar, not a generic Python for-loop.

Wait, so I don't need a runtime evaluator? But the PEP mentions a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar. If a library author uses that to change how 'IsAssignable' behaves at runtime, doesn't my IDE lose the ability to see what's happening? Especially for complex helpers like 'PointerArg' or 'ConvertField' used in that Prisma example.

The 'special_form_evaluator' is specifically to help third-party libraries trigger type evaluation when '__annotate__' is called at runtime. For an IDE, you follow the static specification of the operators. For example, 'PointerArg[T]' in the Prisma implementation is just a type alias that uses 'typing.GetArg[T, Pointer, Literal[0]]'. As long as your tool knows how to resolve 'GetArg', you can compute the resulting type statically. The PEP authors even mention a proof-of-concept in mypy that type-checks all these examples without needing a runtime interpreter.

Okay, that's a relief. But what about 'Go to Definition'? If a library uses 'NewProtocol' to dynamically construct a return type, and a user clicks on a field like 'email', how do I link that back to the original 'User' class? The example shows 'Members[T]' being used to build these, but the result is just a structural protocol.

That is a great question for tooling. When you evaluate 'Attrs[ModelT]', the result is a tuple of 'Member' types. Each 'Member' object carries specific metadata: the name 'N', the type 'T', and the 'D' field, which represents the defining class. Even in the 'NewProtocol' generated for the ORM, the 'Member' objects you're iterating over still contain that '.definer' attribute. Your IDE can use that metadata to map the 'email' property in the resulting protocol back to the specific class and line where that attribute was originally annotated.

I've been reading through PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. To be honest, it looks like a massive over-complication. I use dataclasses and attrs constantly, and we already have PEP 681 for dataclass transforms. Why are they trying to turn Python into TypeScript? This 'type manipulation' stuff seems like it's just going to break IDEs.

It is a big shift, so the skepticism is understandable. The authors' primary motivation is that existing tools like PEP 681 are 'lowest-common denominator' solutions. They require IDEs and type checkers to hardcode support or write custom plugins for every library. PEP 827 aims to put that power directly into the type system so libraries can describe their own transformations without needing a mypy plugin. It targets Python 3.15 and is still in Draft, so it's very much about bridging the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and its static types.

But we already have PEP 692 for TypedDict in **kwargs and PEP 646 for variadic generics. Why do we need a new proposal to handle unpacking? It feels like we are reinventing the wheel just to get fancy 'mapped types' in there.

The proposal actually builds on those. While PEP 692 lets you use a TypedDict for **kwargs, it requires you to define that TypedDict upfront. PEP 827 proposes an extension: supporting 'Unpack' of typevars for **kwargs. This allows a type checker to infer a TypedDict dynamically from the arguments provided in a call. It also extends PEP 646's variadic concepts by introducing 'unpacked comprehensions' like *[... for t in Iter[...]]. This lets you actually compute a new list of parameters based on existing ones, which is something PEP 646 alone can't do.

That still sounds like a lot of work just to avoid a plugin. I'm a dataclasses user; show me how this actually generates an __init__ method. Can it even handle things like marking arguments as optional if there's a default value, or making them keyword-only? That seems like it would be a mess of syntax.

The syntax is definitely more involved than a standard class, but it's quite powerful. You would use the 'UpdateClass' operator and 'Members[T]' to iterate over a class's attributes. For example, to generate an __init__, the PEP suggests an InitFnType[T] alias that maps attributes to 'Param' objects. 

It looks something like this:

type InitFnType[T] = typing.Member[
    Literal['__init__'],
    Callable[
        [
            typing.Param[Literal['self'], Self],
            *[
                typing.Param[
                    p.name,
                    p.type,
                    Literal['keyword'] if typing.IsAssignable[p.init, Never] else Literal['keyword', 'default']
                ]
                for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
            ]
        ],
        None
    ],
    Literal['ClassVar']
]

This uses the 'p.init' property of the member to check if there's a default value. If p.init isn't 'Never', it assigns the 'default' qualifier. It's precise enough that a library like Pydantic or attrs could use 'UpdateClass' in an '__init_subclass__' method to provide a perfectly typed signature to the user without any extra tools.

Wait, so if I use that 'UpdateClass' in a base class, my IDE would actually know that 'my_field' is a keyword argument in the constructor just by looking at the type of the base class? If that actually works across different IDEs without plugins, I can see why people want it. I'm still worried about runtime performance though.

That's exactly the goal! Since the logic is in the type system, any PEP 827-compliant type checker or IDE would see the same thing. Regarding performance, the PEP doesn't require changes to the Python grammar, and these expressions are 'inert' until someone calls '__annotate__'. The authors even mention a third-party evaluator library and a 'special_form_evaluator' hook in the 'typing' module to help frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic handle these at runtime efficiently. It's a heavy lift, but it solves that 'missing features' gap identified in the 2025 Typed Python Survey.

I have been analyzing the type-level calculus introduced in PEP 827. It looks like we are finally getting proper higher-kinded types in Python 3.15. I was looking at the implementation of TypeScript's Utility Types in the proposal. Is KeyOf[T] a new primitive added to the typing module, or is it just a type-level function that iterates over the kind of T?

It is great to see someone looking at the formal side of this. To clarify, KeyOf[T] is not actually a primitive in PEP 827. Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the other authors, Daniel W. Park and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, define it as a type alias using other new primitives. Specifically, it uses the Members[T] operator combined with a variadic Union and the new unpacked comprehension syntax. In Python, it looks like this:

type KeyOf[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]

It does not operate on 'kinds' in the strict Haskell sense, but rather performs type-level introspection on the attributes and methods of a class or TypedDict.

Understood. So Members[T] returns a type-level list? And if I want to implement Partial[T] to make everything optional, I would use a Map operator? I come from a background where I would just lift a 'Maybe' over the record fields. Is Map[T, F] the standard way to do this in PEP 827?

Not exactly. PEP 827 does not actually introduce a 'Map' operator. Instead, it provides a more Pythonic comprehension syntax that works inside type annotations. Also, Members[T] returns a tuple type of Member objects, not a list. To implement something like Partial[T], you use the NewProtocol operator and iterate over the attributes using Attrs[T]. Here is the implementation from the PEP:

type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

You are essentially 'mapping' by constructing a new protocol where each member's type is modified to include None.

That is interesting. It feels very structural. Does the iteration support filtering? For example, if I wanted to implement Omit[T, Keys], would I need a separate filter primitive, or does the IsAssignable operator handle the logic inside the comprehension?

You hit the nail on the head. The comprehension syntax supports standard 'if' clauses, and you use IsAssignable to check the names. For Omit[T, Keys], the PEP implementation filters the members like this:

type Omit[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

One subtle point to remember is that you must wrap the target of the iteration in typing.Iter. This is what allows the runtime evaluation support to hook into the comprehension, as mentioned in the section on 'special_form_evaluator'.

One last check on the semantics: for Exclude[T, U], which operates on unions, does it automatically distribute over the sum type? In OCaml, I might use a polymorphic variant and filter the constructors. How does Python handle 'lifting' here?

PEP 827 introduces a concept called 'Lifting over Unions.' Many operators, like IsAssignable or Concat, automatically take the cross product of union elements. However, for utility types like Exclude, the PEP suggests being explicit by using FromUnion[T]. This operator breaks a union into a tuple of its components so you can iterate over them:

type Exclude[T, U] = Union[
    *[
        x
        for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]
    ]
]

This gives you that filtering behavior for sum types you are looking for, but it is achieved through explicit iteration rather than implicit distribution.

yo wut up! i have been diving into the draft for PEP 827 and it is a lot to take in. specifically, what is the deal with these Extended Callables? i feel like we already have ParamSpec from PEP 612 and TypeVarTuple from PEP 646, so why do we need another way to describe functions?

Hey there! You are right that PEP 612 and PEP 646 gave us tools to move groups of arguments around, but they do not really let the type system "see" or "modify" the individual parts of a function signature. Extended Callables in PEP 827 are designed for exactly that: type-level introspection and construction.

In this proposal, a function signature is broken down into a sequence of Param objects wrapped in a Params container. Each Param[N, T, Q] explicitly tracks the name (N), the type (T), and qualifiers (Q) like whether it is a keyword-only argument or has a default value. This makes the signature readable by the new type-level operators like Iter.

okay, i see. so it is making the signature look like a list of objects we can loop over. i noticed it mentions things like NamedParam and ArgsParam as abbreviations. how does this play with PEP 681? i use @dataclass_transform a lot to handle boilerplate, but the PEP says 827 might make those plugins unnecessary?

That is exactly the goal Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov have in mind. PEP 681 was a bit of a "special case" hack because type checkers couldn't understand the dynamic metaprogramming of dataclasses without custom plugins. 

With PEP 827, you could write a type alias that iterates over a class's members using Members[T] and then uses a conditional type to generate a new __init__ method. You would represent that method using an Extended Callable. Instead of needing a hardcoded plugin, the type checker just follows the logic in your type alias to see how the parameters are mapped from class attributes to the __init__ signature.

interesting. so it is like TypeScript's mapped types but for python. i saw a section about Unpack of typevars for **kwargs too. is that related? i remember PEP 692 added TypedDict unpacking for kwargs, but this seems like it goes further.

It does! PEP 827 includes a minor proposal to support Unpack of typevars for **kwargs. If you have a function like def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]), the type checker will infer a TypedDict for K based on the actual keys passed at the call site. 

This is a prerequisite for Extended Callables because it allows the type system to capture the names and types of keyword arguments as a TypedDict, which you can then manipulate using the new operators like GetMemberType or NewProtocol. It bridges the gap between the flexible way Python handles dictionaries and the strictness needed for static typing.

it sounds powerful but also like i am going to spend my whole life in Bracket City lol. is this targeting 3.15? and since it is still a Draft, do we have any idea if things like PEP 649 (the string annotations stuff) or PEP 764 (inline TypedDicts) will conflict with it?

It is definitely targeting Python 3.15! As for conflict, the authors actually suggest that things like PEP 649 or the AST format for annotations would complement PEP 827. Since PEP 827 introduces runtime hooks via a special_form_evaluator ContextVar, having a clean way to fetch unevaluated annotations (like strings or ASTs) would make it much easier for libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI to use these computed types without things getting messy. 

It is definitely a "Bracket City" situation for library maintainers, but for the average developer, it just means their IDE and type checker will finally understand their complex ORM queries and CRUD models without needing specialized plugins.

We are evaluating the PEP 827 draft for some internal library work. I am looking at the boolean operators and I am curious about the specific distinction between IsEquivalent and IsAssignable. Is IsEquivalent just a shorthand for a two-way check, or is there more to it?

You have hit the nail on the head. Per the PEP 827 specification, IsEquivalent[T, S] is explicitly defined as being equivalent to 'IsAssignable[T, S] and IsAssignable[S, T]'. While IsAssignable[T, S] checks if T is a consistent subtype of S—which is how Python handles gradual typing assignability—IsEquivalent ensures that the relationship is mutual. The PEP authors note that this relation represents 'consistency' in the typing spec. Both operators return a boolean literal type, either Literal[True] or Literal[False], allowing them to be used within the new conditional type syntax, such as 'TypeA if IsEquivalent[T, S] else TypeB'. These primitives are the core engine for the type-level logic you would use to deconstruct and rebuild types in your ORM or framework.

Hey nanochat. I have been exploring Python's typing lately. Coming from a Haskell and OCaml background, I am used to thinking about types in terms of kinds and type-level functions. Python's gradual system feels like it hits a wall when you start doing runtime metaprogramming or ORM-style transformations. I saw PEP 827 mentioned recently. What is the fundamental gap it is trying to solve?

Hello! You have correctly identified a significant friction point. In Python, it is common to use dynamic metaprogramming (like decorators or metaclasses) to generate interfaces at runtime. However, the static type system traditionally has no way to model these transformations. Libraries often have to resort to custom mypy plugins or special-case decorators like @dataclass_transform (PEP 681).

PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, aims to bridge this gap. It proposes adding powerful type-level introspection and construction facilities. Instead of hardcoding logic into the type checker, it introduces primitives in the typing module that allow the type system to compute new types from existing ones, much like TypeScript's conditional and mapped types but adapted for Python's semantics.

That sounds like we are moving toward a more formal type-level language. In OCaml, I might use functors or GADTs to handle complex mappings. If I wanted to simulate a basic mapping—say, taking a class and extracting only its annotated attributes—how does PEP 827 express that? It looks like there is a new syntax for comprehensions?

Precisely. PEP 827 introduces 'unpacked comprehensions' for types. To extract attributes from a class, you would use the Attrs[T] operator, which produces a tuple of Member types. You can then iterate over them using Iter[...].

For example, if you wanted to create a new structural type containing only the attributes of class T, you could write:

type AttrsOnly[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

In this snippet, p.name and p.type use dot notation to access the associated types of the Member. The NewProtocol operator then constructs a new structural protocol from that sequence. It is effectively a type-level map operation.

The *[...] notation looks like a variadic pack expansion. And Iter seems to be a type-level iterator. What about branching logic? If I want to change the type of a field based on whether it is assignable to some base class—like turning a database Link into a concrete object type—can the system handle conditionals?

It can. The PEP introduces 'Conditional Types' with a syntax that mirrors Python's value-level ternary: true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ.

The bool_typ must be a 'Type Boolean,' which are constructed using operators like IsAssignable[T, S] (checking if T is a consistent subtype of S) or IsEquivalent[T, S]. You can combine these with and, or, and not.

Here is how you might implement a conditional mapping in a type alias:

type Convert[T] = (
    ResolvedObject if typing.IsAssignable[T, Link] else T
)

You could then nest this inside your comprehension to transform every member of a class based on its type. This allows library authors to write generic logic that 'calculates' the return types of complex ORM queries, which is one of the primary motivations mentioned in the PEP.

yooo nanochat. ml engineer here. i am tired of my tensor shapes being a total mystery to my type checker. i heard pep 827 introduces some dot notation for types? is that like pydantic but at the type level?

Hey there! Great to meet a fellow shape-wrangler. PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov is basically bringing TypeScript-style power to Python. The dot notation you mentioned is 'Type member access.' In this proposal, when you use operators like Members[T] or Attrs[T], you get Member objects. These are special types that have attributes like .name, .type, .quals, and .init accessible via dots. It lets you write logic like m.type to pull the type out of a class attribute while you are iterating over them in a type expression.
that sounds wild. so if i have a class representing a batch of data, i could iterate over its fields and extract just the types for my shape tuple? how would i use that in a function signature?

Spot on. You could use an 'unpacked comprehension' with the Iter operator. For example, if you wanted to create a structural protocol containing only specific properties of a tensor model, you would do something like: NewProtocol[*[Member[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]]. Since p is a Member type, p.name resolves to a Literal string and p.type resolves to the actual field type. For your ndarray work, this is huge because you can use GetArg to grab dimension arguments and then use those with dot-access in conditional types to verify shapes. The PEP even includes a full example for NumPy-style broadcasting using these tools!

Hey nanochat. I am currently maintaining a complex mypy plugin for a data-mapping library that handles custom field descriptors. It is a nightmare to maintain every time the AST or plugin API changes. I was looking at PEP 827 as a potential savior. Specifically, how does InitField help me capture metadata from class attributes without a plugin?

Welcome! Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and the other authors (Daniel W. Park and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) designed PEP 827 to bridge exactly that gap between metaprogramming and the type system.
