InitField[KwargDict] is a new primitive that collects arguments from a field descriptor instantiation in a class body—like Field(default=0)—and stores them in a way the type system can actually see. It uses a TypedDict to represent the keyword arguments passed to the field's constructor, essentially 'lifting' runtime initialization values into the type domain.
Right, but how does that metadata actually persist in the type? If I have 'x: int = Field(default=10)', my plugin usually has to scrape the AST to find that value. How does PEP 827 represent it?

The proposal introduces a Member type to describe class members. When you use operators like Members[T] or Attrs[T] on a class, the checker returns a tuple of these Member objects. Each Member has an .init attribute. For a field defined as 'foo: int = InitField(default=0)', the .init attribute of that Member is automatically inferred as a TypedDict where the 'default' key maps to Literal[0]. This makes the metadata part of the static type information associated with the class member, so you can access it via type-level operators without any AST scraping.
That implies the type checker is doing some heavy lifting on the assignment side. Does it support arbitrary keyword arguments, or is it just for specific things like 'default'?

It is generic. You can define your own descriptor as a subtype of InitField[YourCustomTypedDict]. To make this work, PEP 827 includes a subproposal for 'Unpack of typevars for **kwargs'. When you instantiate your field, the type checker is instructed to infer Literal types whenever possible for arguments that are not in the TypedDict bound or are read-only. So if your TypedDict includes a 'primary_key' bool and you pass 'primary_key=True', the .init field captures Literal[True]. It effectively turns class-level assignments into structured type metadata.

Okay, so I can capture the metadata. But my plugin's main job is generating an __init__ method signature based on those fields. Can PEP 827 actually synthesize a method signature from those Member objects?

Absolutely. That is one of the core use cases. You would use the new 'extended callable' format along with the UpdateClass operator. You can iterate over Attrs[T] using an unpacked comprehension: '*[Param[p.name, p.type, ...] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]'. This allows you to construct a new Callable type that represents your __init__ method. You then use UpdateClass to 'inject' this generated method back into the nominal class, either through a class decorator or via __init_subclass__.

What if I need to transform the types during that generation? For example, if a field has a default value in InitField, it should become an optional parameter in the generated __init__ signature. How do I branch based on the .init metadata?

You use conditional types and the IsAssignable operator. The PEP demonstrates a 'GetDefault[Init]' helper alias that checks 'if IsAssignable[Init, Field]'. In your __init__ generation logic, you would use a conditional: 'Literal["keyword", "default"] if not IsAssignable[GetDefault[p.init], Never] else Literal["keyword"]'. This allows the synthesized Param for each attribute to dynamically decide whether it has a default based on the captured metadata in p.init. It is very similar to how TypeScript handles conditional types but adapted for Python's syntax.

This sounds like it could completely replace my plugin and PEP 681 (dataclass_transform). My last concern is runtime. My library is also an ORM that needs to see these types at runtime to build SQL queries. Does this only work in the type checker?

The authors explicitly want this to support runtime evaluation for frameworks like yours. They have proposed a special_form_evaluator ContextVar in the typing module. This allows a third-party evaluator library to intercept calls to __bool__ on boolean operators or __iter__ on typing.Iter, triggering the actual evaluation of the type-level logic. While it is currently a Draft PEP targeted for Python 3.15, the goal is to ensure that runtime frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI can leverage the same logic the IDE uses.

Hey nanochat. I've been struggling with tensor shape typing in Python for a while. I saw PEP 827 mentions something about inferring Literal types for **kwargs. Why is that specific detail so important for those of us doing ML or data engineering?

It is a crucial bridge for making type-level math actually work. In PEP 827, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov propose that when you use Unpack with a TypeVar for **kwargs, the type checker should infer Literal types whenever possible. For an ML engineer, this is the difference between a type checker knowing a dimension is 'an integer' versus knowing it is 'exactly 1' or 'exactly 480'. Without those specific literals, you cannot perform the logic needed for things like broadcasting or validation of specific axis operations at compile time.
That makes sense. Usually, if I pass axis=1, the type checker just sees 'int'. How does the PEP actually change that? Can you show me the syntax for the function signature?

Sure. The PEP introduces a way to capture those keyword arguments into a TypedDict type variable. It looks like this: 'def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K: return kwargs'. If you call this as 'f(axis=1, keepdims=True)', the type checker doesn't just infer 'K' as a generic dict. It infers 'K' as 'TypedDict({"axis": Literal[1], "keepdims": Literal[True]})'. This is vital because the other operators in PEP 827, like 'IsEquivalent' or conditional types, can then 'see' that 1 and branch logic based on it.
Okay, so if I have those literals captured, how do I use them for something like NumPy broadcasting? I saw a mention of a MergeOne operator in the draft.

Exactly. In the PEP's broadcasting implementation, they define a 'MergeOne[T, S]' helper. It uses the captured literals to decide the resulting dimension. For example, if one dimension is 'Literal[1]' and the other is 'Literal[4]', 'MergeOne' can see 'Literal[1]' is present and return 'Literal[4]'. If both were just 'int', the type system wouldn't know if they are compatible for broadcasting or if it should raise an error. The PEP even suggests using 'RaiseError' with a literal string like 'Broadcast mismatch' if the literals don't align. By inferring literals in **kwargs, you can pass shape parameters directly into these recursive type aliases like 'Broadcast[T, S]' to compute the final array shape dynamically.

hey nanochat. i saw some drama on twitter about pep 827. something about 'type manipulation'. honestly, it looks like a mess. why are we adding all this logic to types? and what is this 'lifting over unions' thing? sounds like over-engineering.

Hello! It is definitely a big shift. PEP 827, proposed by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, is trying to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic 'metaprogramming' and the static type system. Currently, libraries like FastAPI or Prisma have to use complicated plugins because the type system cannot 'calculate' types. 'Lifting over unions' is a core part of making this calculation work. Basically, it means that if you have a union type (like int | str) and you apply a type operator to it, Python will automatically apply that operator to every part of the union for you.
But if I am checking every part of the union, isn't that just more work? I am a beginner, and regular types already feel like a lot to learn. Why would I want the type system to do math or 'lifting'?

I understand the skepticism. It sounds complex because it is designed for people building big libraries. Imagine you have a tool that combines two pieces of data. If the first piece could be 'a' or 'b', and the second piece is 'c', a normal type system might get confused. But with 'lifting', Python says: 'Okay, I will calculate (a + c) and (b + c) and tell you the result is either ac or bc.' This is called the cross product. It allows your code editor to give you accurate autocomplete even when your data is dynamic. You might not write the 'lifting' code yourself, but you benefit because the libraries you use will have much better type hints.
So it is like a for-loop for types? If the PEP says something like Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['c']], it just handles both possibilities automatically?

That is exactly right! In the PEP 827 specification, the authors explain that for every argument position, they take the union elements, evaluate the operator for each combination, and union the results together. So Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['c'] | Literal['d']] becomes Literal['ac'] | Literal['ad'] | Literal['bc'] | Literal['bd']. Without lifting, developers would have to manually write out every single possible combination, which is impossible for large projects. This way, the type system 'keeps up' with the dynamic nature of Python. It is currently a Draft for Python 3.15, so there is still a lot of discussion, but the goal is to make typing as powerful as the language itself.

Hi, I've been looking at PEP 827 (Type Manipulation). As someone who uses mypy and pyright daily, it feels like a massive increase in complexity. I'm already using ParamSpec and Protocols for most of my generic needs. Is there actually demand for this, or are we just trying to turn Python into TypeScript?

It is a significant leap for the type system, but the demand appears quite high based on industry feedback. The authors of the PEP--Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov--explicitly cite the Meta 2025 Typed Python Survey. In that survey, the top requested features were specifically 'Missing Features From TypeScript,' including intersection types, mapped and conditional types, and utility types like Pick and Omit.

While ParamSpec and Protocols handle many cases, they struggle to model the kind of dynamic metaprogramming common in libraries like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs. Currently, those frameworks often require custom typechecker plugins to work properly. PEP 827 aims to provide the primitives—like Members[T], NewProtocol, and conditional if/else types—so these libraries can be typed natively without special-casing every new framework in the IDE.

That makes sense for the framework authors, I guess. But for an average senior dev, am I really going to be writing 'type-level computation'? I'm used to standard generic aliases. If I wanted to implement something simple like TypeScript's Pick, how gross does the Python syntax get?

It's definitely more verbose than TypeScript because it has to fit within Python's existing grammar, but it's consistent. Instead of new syntax, it uses standard expressions like list comprehensions inside the brackets. 

For example, implementing Pick[T, Keys] in PEP 827 looks like this:

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

This uses the new Members[T] operator to get a tuple of Member objects, then filters them by checking if the member's name is assignable to your set of Keys. The asterisk (*) at the start is an 'unpacked comprehension,' which is a core part of this proposal for variadic type arguments. It's meant for library maintainers to provide these as 'Utility Types' so most users can just import them and use them like Pick[User, Literal['id', 'name']].

The IsAssignable operator and Member.name access look interesting. Does this mean the type checker is actually evaluating that 'if' statement at check-time? And what happens if the PEP doesn't land? Is this still targeting 3.15?

Yes, exactly. Type checkers like mypy or pyright would evaluate the conditional type logic—like 'tt if tb else tf'—and the comprehension logic during their analysis. PEP 827 also specifies a 'special_form_evaluator' hook so that runtime libraries like Pydantic can also evaluate these types when you call __annotate__.

As for the timeline, PEP 827 is currently a Draft and it is indeed targeting Python 3.15. There is already a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can handle these examples. Since it's a Draft, the specifics might shift, but the goal is to land a unified way to handle type construction instead of the fragmented plugin approach we have now with things like PEP 681 (dataclass_transform).

hi nanochat! i saw some posts on twitter about pep 827 and something called BaseTypedDict. i am still pretty new to python typing, so is this just another way to make a dictionary? i already use TypedDict for my json data, so i am wondering why i need a base one.

Welcome! PEP 827 is a big proposal for Type Manipulation by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. It is definitely more than just a new way to store data like a regular dictionary.

In standard Python, a TypedDict is something you define ahead of time to say "this dictionary will have these specific keys and types." BaseTypedDict is a new primitive introduced in PEP 827 specifically to act as a "bound." It lets you tell the type checker: "I don't know exactly what keys are coming in, but whatever they are, treat them as a TypedDict."

It looks like this:

class BaseTypedDict(typing.TypedDict):
    pass

You use it mainly with a type variable like [K: BaseTypedDict]. This allows the type checker to look at your code and actually build a custom TypedDict for you on the fly.
okay, so it is like a template? in my code now, i just use **kwargs and everything works fine. how is this different from just letting python handle keyword arguments normally?

The difference is all about precision. Usually, when you use **kwargs, the type checker just sees it as a general dictionary where keys are strings and values could be anything (Any). You lose the specific names and types of those keys.

With PEP 827, you can do something like this:

def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K:
    return kwargs

If you call f(name="nano", version=3.15), the type checker doesn't just see a dict. It actually infers that K is a unique TypedDict with 'name' as a string and 'version' as a float. It even tries to infer Literal types, so it knows 'version' is exactly 3.15. Regular **kwargs can't do that level of "guessing" or inference.
that sounds way more complicated than what i usually do. is this basically just making python act more like typescript? i keep seeing people compare the two.

You hit the nail on the head. The PEP authors explicitly mention that this proposal is inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types. They even cite a 2025 Typed Python Survey where developers specifically asked for TypeScript-like features like Pick, Omit, and better structural typing.

While regular Python is very dynamic and lets you do whatever you want at runtime, the type system has been a bit "stiff." BaseTypedDict and the other tools in PEP 827 are there to bridge that gap. It's less about making your day-to-day beginner code harder and more about helping library authors build tools (like ORMs or FastAPI) that can understand your code as well as a human can, without needing custom plugins.

salut. i've been looking at the type manipulation facilities in pep 827 by dr. michael j. "sully" sullivan, daniel w. park, and "mad emperor" yury selivanov. it seems they're trying to give us a more granular way to decompose callables. can you explain the relationship between the Params and Param types? is Params just a product type over a heterogeneous list of parameters?

exactly. in pep 827, Param is the primitive that describes a single function parameter. its signature is `Param[N, T, Q]`, where N is the name (as a string literal or None), T is the type, and Q is a union of ParamQuals like 'default' or 'keyword'. Params[*Ps] acts as a variadic container for these. when you use Params as the first argument in a Callable, the type checker knows you're using the 'extended callable' format rather than the standard one.

that's interesting. so it's a structural representation that's actually accessible to the type-level operators. if i wanted to model something with mixed semantics--say, a positional-only int and a keyword-only string with a default--how would that look in this encoding?

it would be represented like this: `Callable[Params[Param[Literal['a'], int, Literal['positional']], Param[Literal['b'], str, Literal['keyword', 'default']]], None]`. to make this more readable, the pep authors included abbreviations like `PosParam[Literal['a'], int]` and `NamedDefaultParam[Literal['b'], str]`. the real power comes from the fact that Params is a natural bound for ParamSpec, meaning you can actually perform type-level loops over Params using Iter to transform signatures.

i see. it's about making the domain of the function arrow introspectable. since Params is variadic, i assume we can use the new unpacked comprehensions on it? like, could i construct a new Params by filtering out all parameters that have the 'default' qualifier?

precisely. you could use an unpacked comprehension like `Params[*[p for p in Iter[Ps] if not IsAssignable[p.quals, Literal['default']]]]`. this allows frameworks to derive new function signatures from existing ones at the type level, which is a major goal for the authors to support things like dataclass-style method generation without custom plugins.

I've been looking over the PEP 827 draft by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov. This 'Lifting over Unions' concept for type operators sounds like a performance nightmare for type checkers. Why are we forcing a cross-product evaluation of every union element? If I'm building a complex ORM with dozens of models, won't this just explode?

The skepticism is understandable. In PEP 827, 'lifting' means that if you pass a Union to a type operator, the system essentially maps that operator across every permutation of the union's members. For example, Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['c']] becomes Literal['ac'] | Literal['bc']. While the cross-product logic (itertools.product style) could grow, it's designed to ensure that type manipulation behaves predictably when dealing with Python's gradual types. Without lifting, an operator would have to decide how to handle a Union as a single block, which usually results in just returning 'Any' or failing, making the whole manipulation system useless for real-world dynamic code.

But as a library maintainer, I'm worried about the 'Members[T]' operator. If I have a union of three models, T = User | Admin | Guest, and I call Members[T], I'm getting a union of tuples of Member types. How am I supposed to iterate over that in an unpacked comprehension without the logic becoming completely unreadable?

It definitely adds complexity, but the PEP argues this is a feature, not a bug. If you are iterating over the attributes of 'User | Admin | Guest' using '*[... for p in Iter[Members[T]]]', the lifting ensures that your comprehension logic is applied to each model individually. If your logic produces a result type for User, one for Admin, and one for Guest, the final result is automatically a Union of those results. This is actually what you want for an ORM; if you're transforming a 'Link' to a list of IDs, and the field could be one of three different models, you want the final type to reflect all three possibilities. If the operator didn't lift, you'd be stuck trying to introspect the Union itself, which doesn't have 'Members' in the traditional sense.

I see the point about Concat, but what about 'IsAssignable[T, S]'? If both T and S are unions, that cross-product is going to be massive. Is the 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov really suggesting we check every single combination just to see if a type fits?

The PEP is indeed quite rigorous about it. For IsAssignable[T, S], lifting ensures the boolean result is a Union of Literal[True] and Literal[False] based on every combination. If any branch is assignable and another isn't, you get 'Literal[True, False]' (essentially 'bool'). This allows you to use the 'typing.Bool[T]' helper within a conditional type: 'ResultA if typing.Bool[IsAssignable[T, S]] else ResultB'. If the result is ambiguous because of the union, the conditional type itself evaluates both branches and unions them. It preserves the 'truth' of the type system across all possible runtime paths, which is critical for the FastAPI-style CRUD generation patterns the authors are targeting.

Okay, so it effectively forces the type-level computation to stay 'sound' relative to the union. If I use 'Exclude' or 'Extract' (those TypeScript-style utilities), I'm basically leaning on this lifting behavior to filter the union members one by one, right?

Exactly. The implementation of 'Exclude[T, U]' provided in the PEP uses 'FromUnion[T]' and 'Iter' to go through each element 'x'. Because 'IsAssignable[x, U]' is lifted (or in this case, evaluated per element), the comprehension can filter out specifically the branches that match 'U'. Without lifting, you couldn't reach 'inside' the union to transform it. The authors acknowledge that this requires more work from type checkers, but they believe it's the only way to make type-level programming as expressive as the dynamic metaprogramming we already do in frameworks like Pydantic or SQLAlchemy.

yo, I'm digging into PEP 827 because I'm tired of my tensor shapes being 'Any' or just tuples of ints. I was trying to write a runtime validator for a custom array class that uses the new 'typing.Iter' to loop over dimensions, but when I call 'iter()' on it in my shell, it just gives me an empty iterator. Is the draft broken for 3.15, or am I just using the syntax wrong?

The draft isn't broken, but there is a specific mechanism for how 'typing.Iter' behaves at runtime. By default, calling 'iter()' on 'typing.Iter' returns 'iter(())', which is exactly why you are seeing an empty result. PEP 827 introduces a hook called 'special_form_evaluator' specifically to handle this. It is a 'ContextVar' in the 'typing' module that needs to hold a callable evaluator. If that variable is 'None' (the default), 'Iter' stays inert and empty to avoid side effects during normal execution.

Wait, so I have to manually manage a ContextVar just to make the type-level loops work? That seems super counter-intuitive for a 'Type Manipulation' proposal. I thought the whole point was that the 'for p in Iter[...]' syntax would just work like a normal list comprehension when I inspect the class annotations.

I can see why that's confusing. The logic is that the 'unpacked comprehension' syntax—like '*[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]]'—is meant to be evaluated when the '__annotate__' method of a class or function is called. For libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI to actually execute those loops at runtime, they (or a third-party evaluator library) will set the 'special_form_evaluator'. This evaluator intercepts the '__iter__' call on 'typing.Iter' and the '__bool__' call on boolean operators like 'IsAssignable' to produce the actual results. It's designed this way so that the standard library doesn't have to ship a full-blown type-system solver, but provides the hooks for tools to do it.

Okay, that makes more sense. So if I'm building a NumPy-style broadcasting alias, I shouldn't really be calling 'iter()' myself in my logic. I should be defining a 'type Broadcast[T, S]' alias using the primitives. Is that how the 'MergeOne' and 'DropLast' examples in the PEP work? They don't seem to use 'for' loops; they use recursion.

Exactly. For something like broadcasting, the PEP implementation uses recursion with 'typing.Slice' and 'typing.GetArg'. For example, 'DropLast[T]' is defined as 'typing.Slice[T, Literal[0], Literal[-1]]'. The 'Broadcast' alias then recursively calls itself: 'tuple[*Broadcast[DropLast[T], DropLast[S]], MergeOne[Last[T], Last[S]]]'. You only really use the 'for ... in Iter[...] ' syntax when you are constructing new shapes or protocols from things like 'Attrs[T]' or 'Members[T]', usually within the 'NewProtocol' or 'NewTypedDict' operators.

hey, i'm an engineer working on ide tooling and i've been looking at pep 827. honestly, this looks like a nightmare for completions and go-to-definition. why are we bringing all this typescript-style complexity to python?

I totally hear you. As a toolmaker, seeing things like `*[... for t in Iter[iter_ty]]` in a type hint probably feels like the ground is shifting. But the core reason Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov proposed this is to bridge the gap between Python's highly dynamic metaprogramming and the type system.

Right now, if you use something like a Prisma-style ORM or FastAPI, the library has to rely on custom mypy plugins or a massive amount of boilerplate (like PEP 681's `@dataclass_transform`) just to tell the IDE what's happening. The goal of PEP 827 is to move that logic into the type system itself so you don't need a custom plugin for every single library just to get working completions.

That makes sense in theory, but look at the Prisma example in the PEP. It's using `NewProtocol` and `Iter[Attrs[K]]` to build a return type on the fly based on `**kwargs`. How am I supposed to provide completions for a type that doesn't actually exist until the code is running?

It's definitely a step up in complexity! In that Prisma-style example, the `select` method uses `**kwargs: Unpack[K]` where `K` is a `TypedDict`. The proposal suggests that when inferring types for `**kwargs`, the type checker should infer literal types whenever possible.

Because the return type is defined as a `NewProtocol` wrapping a comprehension over `Attrs[K]`, your engine would evaluate that type-level loop. Since the keys in `K` are literals (like `name=True`), the `NewProtocol` becomes a concrete structural type with a `name` attribute. Instead of guessing, the IDE can look at the evaluated `NewProtocol` and say, 'Okay, the user requested `name` and `email`, so I will only suggest those two on the returned object.' It actually makes completions *more* accurate for these dynamic patterns without you needing to hardcode ORM logic.
So I'd basically need a full type-level evaluator. What about performance? If I'm doing go-to-definition on a `HeroUpdate` model that's computed via `Update[Hero]`, that sounds slow.

The PEP authors acknowledge that. They're actually planning to release a third-party evaluator library to help with this. For `HeroUpdate`, you'd be looking at the `Partial` or `PartialTD` utility implementation, which uses `NewTypedDict` or `NewProtocol` to iterate over `Attrs[T]`. 

From a tooling perspective, the PEP mentions that these expressions are 'inert' until evaluated. It also points to a potential `Format.AST` mode for fetching annotations, which would make it easier to extract these unevaluated structures without running the full Python `__annotate__` function if you just want to do static analysis. It's still in Draft for Python 3.15, so there's room for the tooling community to weigh in on those 'RaiseError' or 'UpdateClass' flows!

I was looking at the PEP 827 draft and I am honestly exhausted. It feels like we are just turning Python into TypeScript with all these 'type manipulation' features. Specifically, what is the point of this 'Unpack of typevars for **kwargs' thing? It looks like boilerplate for the sake of looking like a compiler.

It is true that PEP 827 is inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types, as the authors Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov acknowledge. The 'Unpack' of typevars for **kwargs is a specific prerequisite designed to bridge the gap between dynamic Python code and the static type system.

Currently, you can use a TypedDict for **kwargs (via PEP 692), but that requires you to define a fixed structure. The new proposal allows a function to capture the exact shape of the arguments passed to it into a type variable. For example:

def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K:
    return kwargs

When you call this with f(a=1, b='test'), the type system doesn't just see a generic dict; it infers 'K' as a specific TypedDict containing those exact keys and types.

But why do I care if the type system knows the 'exact shape' of my kwargs inside the function? If I am writing a library, I usually want to constrain what people pass in, not just accept anything and give it a fancy label. This just feels like more complexity for the 'typing-sig' crowd to play with.

The utility comes when you combine this with the other operators in PEP 827. In frameworks like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs, you often have functions that take arbitrary keyword arguments and return an object whose shape depends on those arguments.

If you can capture the kwargs as a type variable 'K', you can then use operators like 'Members[K]' or 'NewProtocol' to programmatically define a return type. It allows you to write one generic 'select' function that returns a type containing only the fields the user actually asked for in the keyword arguments, with full IDE autocompletion for the result.

I also saw some weirdness about 'Never' being used for missing arguments. If I have a bound and I don't provide an argument, it becomes 'Never'? That sounds like it would break every piece of logic I have.

The PEP specifies a very narrow rule for that to avoid exactly the kind of breakage you are worried about. The 'Never' inference only applies to non-required items in the bound that are also marked as 'read-only'.

If an item is in the bound (like a field in your BaseTypedDict) but the caller doesn't provide it, the type checker will infer that specific field as 'Never' to signal to other type-level operators that the field is absent. This is mainly to support type-level computation—it doesn't mean your runtime code suddenly has to deal with 'Never' values, it just tells the static analyzer that a specific key wasn't part of the input 'K'.

And it infers Literal types too? So if I pass x=10, K thinks the type is Literal[10] instead of just int? That seems like it would make type variables extremely brittle if you try to pass them to other functions.

That is intentional. The PEP states that when inferring types for this pattern, the type checker should infer literal types when possible. This is critical for the 'Type Manipulation' goal. If you are building a database query and you pass 'name=True', the type system needs to know it was 'Literal[True]' so a conditional type like 'tt if bool_typ else tf' can decide whether or not to include the 'name' field in the returned object. 

Without literals, the type system would just see 'bool', and it wouldn't be able to perform the 'manipulation' part of PEP 827 because it wouldn't know which branch of the conditional to take.

Hey! I've been digging into the draft for PEP 827. It seems really powerful for things like Prisma-style ORMs, but I'm trying to reconcile it with other recent typing PEPs like 695 and 681. One thing that stands out is that Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the other authors specifically say they aren't proposing a way to create nominal classes or new generic types. Why is that? Is it just too complex for the first version?

You've hit on a key boundary in the proposal. The authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—are very explicit in the 'Object creation' section that they are only focusing on structural types, like 'NewProtocol' and 'NewTypedDict'. They aren't trying to create brand-new nominal identities in the type system yet. Part of the reason, which they touch on in the 'Generic Callable' rationale, is to avoid 'cans of worms' like impredicative polymorphism and Rank-N types. Those can make type inference undecidable, and they want to keep the system manageable for existing type checkers. It's essentially a move to bridge the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and static types without breaking the fundamental rules of Python's nominal hierarchy.

That makes sense, though it's a bit of a bummer. But what about 'UpdateClass'? It feels like a bit of a loophole since it lets you modify a nominal class via '__init_subclass__' or a decorator. Also, how does this interact with PEP 646 (variadic generics) and the ideas in PEP 649 for runtime annotations? I noticed a mention of a Format.AST mode in the draft.

You're right that 'UpdateClass' is the closest thing to nominal manipulation. It doesn't create a *new* nominal type, but it 'updates' an existing one by adding, overriding, or removing members (using 'Never' to hide them). This is specifically designed for 'dataclass_transform' style logic (PEP 681) but more flexible. Regarding PEP 646, PEP 827 leans heavily on it—especially for the NumPy-style broadcasting example where they use 'TypeVarTuple' to handle shapes. As for PEP 649 and the 'Format.AST' draft, the authors mention that having a way to fetch annotations as an AST would 'combine extremely well' with this proposal. Since PEP 827 introduces runtime evaluation hooks like 'special_form_evaluator', being able to fetch unevaluated ASTs would make it much easier for the third-party evaluator library they plan to release to process things like 'tt if tb else tf' without the usual runtime string-parsing headaches.
hey nanochat, i've been reading up on the type manipulation stuff in pep 827. is it really bringing typescript style utility types like pick and omit to python?

it certainly is. pep 827, authored by michael j. sully sullivan, daniel w. park, and yury selivanov, proposes exactly that. it introduces a set of type-level operators and a specific comprehension syntax to allow for the construction of types like pick, omit, and partial that we usually see in typescript.
okay cool. as a heavy mypy user, i'm curious how it handles the introspection. like, how would you even define keyof in python under this pep?

in pep 827, keyof[t] is constructed using the members[t] operator and an unpacked comprehension. it looks like this: type keyof[t] = union[*[p.name for p in typing.iter[typing.members[t]]]]. here, members[t] produces a tuple of member types representing the attributes and methods of t. the iter operator allows the comprehension to walk over that tuple, and p.name accesses the literal string name of each member.
that's wild. so it's basically list comprehension but for types. how does pick[t, keys] work then? do you have to use a protocol?

exactly. you use the newprotocol operator. for pick[t, keys], the implementation is: type pick[t, keys] = typing.newprotocol[*[p for p in typing.iter[typing.members[t]] if typing.isassignable[p.name, keys]]]. it iterates through the members of t and only includes those where the member name is assignable to the keys provided. isassignable is a new boolean operator introduced in the pep for these kinds of checks.
i see. and what about union types specifically? typescript has exclude and extract. does pep 827 handle those too?

it does, via the fromunion[t] operator. fromunion takes a union and turns it into a tuple of its components. so exclude[t, u] is defined as: type exclude[t, u] = union[*[x for x in typing.iter[typing.fromunion[t]] if not typing.isassignable[x, u]]]. this lets you filter out specific parts of a union type statically.
got it. one last thing: partial is huge for api work. making everything optional in a typeddict is a pain today. does this pep simplify that?

it simplifies it significantly. for a regular structural type, you can define partial[t] using typing.newprotocol[*[typing.member[p.name, p.type | none, p.quals] for p in typing.iter[typing.attrs[t]]]]. if you are specifically targetting typeddicts, the pep suggests a partialtd version that uses qualifiers: typing.newtypeddict[*[typing.member[p.name, p.type, p.quals | literal['notrequired']] for p in typing.iter[typing.attrs[t]]]]. it uses the attrs operator instead of members to focus just on the data fields.

I have been looking over the draft for PEP 827 and I am a bit skeptical. As an IDE tooling engineer, my first thought is: why do we need this? We already have mypy plugins to handle things like dataclasses or SQLAlchemy. This looks like a massive amount of complexity to add to the type system when we already have a workaround. Is this going to kill performance for completions and go-to-definition?

That is a fair concern to raise. The authors of the PEP—Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—address this directly in the Motivation section. The core issue is that mypy plugins are not a universal standard. Many type checkers do not have a plugin API, which means you cannot get consistent type checking or IDE features across different environments like VS Code, PyCharm, and CI tools. By moving this into the type system itself using primitives like Members[T] or NewProtocol, the logic becomes transparent to all tools rather than being trapped in a tool-specific plugin.

Okay, but standardizing complexity is still adding complexity. If I have to resolve a type like 'NewProtocol[*[Member[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[K]]]]' just to show a list of attributes in a completion window, that sounds like a lot of work for the language server. Is this actually better for the user experience?

The PEP argues it actually enables a better experience than what is currently possible. For instance, it highlights the Prisma-style ORM example. In that case, an IDE could offer code completion for all arguments of a 'db.select' call matching the actual database column names, and it can do this recursively. Because the PEP provides a formal grammar for conditional types—like 'true_type if bool_type else false_type'—and type member access via dot notation like 'm.name', the engine of your IDE can follow a predictable specification to resolve these types instead of relying on hard-coded magic or opaque plugins.

I see. So instead of me writing a custom provider for every library's metaprogramming, I just implement the PEP 827 evaluator once? I noticed there is a dot notation for 'm.name' and 'm.type' on Member objects. How does that work without breaking the AST?

The PEP specifies that no changes to the Python grammar are required; these are purely changes to what expressions are considered valid types. For the dot notation, the PEP proposes that Member and Param types have associated type members. This allows you to deconstruct types using familiar syntax. For example, you can iterate over Attrs[T] and filter them using typing.IsAssignable[p.type, Property]. This lifting of introspection into the type system is exactly what helps bridge the gap between dynamic Python code and static analysis.

It is starting to make sense why this is being proposed for 3.15. If it truly eliminates the need for tool-specific plugins, it might actually stabilize things for us in the long run. I was worried about inconsistent behavior, but a unified Standards Track PEP for type manipulation might be the right direction if the performance holds up.

Exactly. The goal is to provide a 'fairly conventional' way to model the complex metaprogramming that is already common in Python. While it is still a Draft, it aims to unify how we handle things like FastAPI CRUD models and dataclass-style method generation so that your IDE tooling can provide accurate types without needing a different plugin for every framework.

Hey nanochat! I am a tech lead for a team that deals with a lot of complex ORM-style metaprogramming. I am looking at PEP 827 and wondering: why is the current 'mypy plugin' route considered such a failure that we need this massive change to the type system? Is it just about making things easier for the authors or is there a real benefit for a team like mine?

That is a great question. According to the PEP authors, Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov, the main issue with mypy plugins is the lack of a consistent developer experience across the ecosystem. If your team uses mypy for CI but some developers prefer Pyright or Pyre for their IDEs, you often end up with a situation where the types work in one place but show up as errors in another. Most typecheckers do not even have a plugin API. This proposal aims to make type manipulation a first-class feature so that any PEP 827-compliant tool can understand your metaprogramming without needing a custom extension.

I see. We definitely struggle with the 'red squiggles' in VS Code even when our CI is green. But didn't PEP 681 and dataclass_transform solve the most common cases? Why go as far as adding 'unpacked comprehensions' and 'conditional types'?

The PEP acknowledges that @dataclass_transform was a good start, but it calls it a 'special-case' solution. It only works for a specific subset of dataclass-like behaviors. If you are building something more dynamic, like a Prisma-style ORM where you want to return a type that only includes the specific fields a user selected in a query, PEP 681 can't help you. PEP 827 gives you tools like Members[T] and NewProtocol so you can actually construct those types on the fly. Instead of a plugin that hardcodes your ORM logic, you could write a type alias like: type PickFields[T, K] = NewProtocol[*[p for p in Iter[Members[T]] if IsAssignable[p.name, K]]]. This makes the transformation logic part of your Python code, readable by any compliant IDE.

That is wild. It sounds like we would basically be writing 'mini-programs' inside our type hints. As a lead, I worry about the complexity. Is this actually going to land soon, or is it just a research project?

It is currently a Draft PEP targeting Python 3.15. While the syntax like 'T if bool_typ else false_typ' and 'Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]' looks complex, the authors argue that the audience for this syntax will mostly be framework and library maintainers. Your team members would likely just use the helper aliases you define. The goal is to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic nature and its static type system so you don't have to choose between 'expressive code' and 'proper type checking.' There is already a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can handle these examples.

Hi nanochat, quick typing question. I've been looking at PEP 827 and how it might help me stop manually duplicating my FastAPI models into Public, Create, and Update versions. But honestly, the UpdateClass stuff looks like a recipe for runtime chaos. If I use it in __init_subclass__, aren't we just asking for evaluation-order bugs?

That is a very sharp observation. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, actually explicitly flags this as an 'Open Issue.' When you use UpdateClass in the return type of __init_subclass__, you are essentially telling the type system to modify the class members based on a computation. Because these computations can inspect other classes using the Members[T] operator, you can end up with a situation where the result depends on which class is evaluated first.

Right, so it's as brittle as the dynamic Python code we're trying to tame. If my 'HeroUpdate' model inspects 'Hero' to make fields optional, but 'Hero' is itself being modified by some other base class using UpdateClass, it sounds like the type checker might see two different things depending on the import order. How does the PEP even propose to solve that?

The PEP doesn't have a silver bullet for the general case of cross-class dependencies yet. It notes that this 'mirrors a potential runtime evaluation-order dependence.' However, for the specific case of inheritance, it specifies that if multiple ancestors have an __init_subclass__ with an UpdateClass return type, they are applied in reverse MRO (Method Resolution Order). This provides a predictable sequence for local class hierarchies. But if you are doing cross-class inspection—like HeroUpdate looking at Hero—the authors admit that evaluation-order dependence is a risk that ideally should be rejected or handled by the evaluator.

If it mirrors the runtime mess, I don't see the benefit of adding it to the typing module. Why would I want my static analysis to be as unpredictable as my runtime? It feels like we're sacrificing safety for less boilerplate.

The trade-off the authors propose is bridging the gap between Python's highly dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. Currently, libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI often require custom mypy plugins to understand these transformations. PEP 827 aims to make these transformations part of the standard type language so that any IDE or type checker can understand them without plugins. To make this safer at runtime, the PEP introduces a special_form_evaluator ContextVar hook. This allows frameworks to provide a custom evaluator that can handle the complexity of these 'stuck' type evaluations or raise errors when a circular dependency is detected. It is definitely more complex, but the goal is to standardize the 'magic' that's already happening in your FastAPI apps.

Hey nanochat! I've been digging into PEP 827 lately. I'm a bit of a typing nerd, so I've followed PEP 646 for variadic generics and PEP 695 for the new generics syntax, but 'Extended Callables' in 827 has me a little confused. Is it just a more verbose way to write a Callable, or am I missing the bigger picture?

It definitely looks more verbose at first glance, but there is a specific reason for that. Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov proposed Extended Callables because the standard Callable syntax is too opaque for type-level manipulation. While a regular Callable tells you the input and output types, it doesn't easily let a type checker or a runtime library 'dissect' individual parameters like whether they have defaults or are keyword-only. The Extended Callable format uses the Param and Params types to turn a function signature into a structured list that you can iterate over using the new type-level operators in PEP 827.

So it's like a reflection API but inside the type system? I saw Param takes a few arguments like N and Q. How does that actually look in code? If I have a function with a default value, how does PEP 827 represent that compared to how we might handle it now with something like PEP 681's dataclass_transform?

Exactly. Instead of the hardcoded logic in PEP 681, PEP 827 lets you define these transformations yourself. A parameter is represented as Param[N, T, Q], where N is the name (as a Literal string), T is the type, and Q is the qualifier. For a parameter with a default, you'd use a qualifier like Literal['default']. For example, if you have a param 'c' that is an int with a default, it looks like Param[Literal['c'], int, Literal['default']]. You then wrap these in Params[*Ps], which is the first argument to a Callable. This allows you to programmatically generate an __init__ method for a class by iterating over class Members and converting them into Params. It's much more flexible than dataclass_transform because it isn't limited to a specific decorator's behavior.

That makes sense. It reminds me of PEP 692 where we started using Unpack for TypedDict in **kwargs, but 827 seems to take it further by letting us compute those types. Since this is targeting Python 3.15 and it's still a Draft, how does it handle the runtime side? I know PEP 649 and 649's 'Format.AST' ideas are floating around for getting annotations without executing everything.

The authors are very conscious of the runtime needs, especially for frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic. PEP 827 introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' hook, which is a ContextVar that holds a callable. This allows third-party libraries to trigger the evaluation of these type booleans and comprehensions at runtime when __annotate__ is called. They also mention that if a 'Format.AST' mode is adopted, it would pair perfectly with PEP 827 because you could fetch the fully unevaluated AST of the type manipulation logic. It's a significant bridge between the static world of type checkers and the dynamic meta-programming Python is famous for.

hi! I'm currently looking into PEP 827 because I work on IDE tooling and the new type-level computation stuff is going to be a huge lift for our completions engine. I'm particularly stuck on the **kwargs unpacking with TypedDict bounds. If a user defines a non-required field in the TypedDict but doesn't actually pass it in the function call, how is that reflected in the inferred type?

Hello! I'm nanochat, and I can help you navigate the PEP 827 specification for that. According to the PEP, when you use Unpack on a typevar with a TypedDict bound for **kwargs, the type checker is supposed to infer literal types whenever possible. For non-required items in the bound that aren't provided in the call, if those items are marked as read-only, the type checker will infer their type as Never. This acts as a signal that the argument was explicitly not provided.

Ah, okay. So we'd see Never in the inferred TypedDict for that key. That's actually super helpful for our 'go-to-definition' or hover states because we can show that the value is missing. Why is it limited to read-only items though? Why not just all non-required items?

The PEP authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—specified that this only happens for read-only items because non-read-only items in a TypedDict are invariant. Since Never is a bottom type, using it for non-read-only (invariant) items would cause compatibility issues. By restricting it to read-only items, the system stays type-safe while still allowing type-level functions to branch based on whether a key exists.

Got it. That makes sense from a variance perspective. I noticed you mentioned the authors by their full titles, including 'Mad Emperor'—is that how the PEP actually lists Yury Selivanov? That's quite the identity for a spec author.

It is! That is exactly how he is credited in the Authorship Note of PEP 827. It certainly adds some personality to the Draft. As an assistant, I stick strictly to the text of the PEP, so I make sure to use their preferred titles as listed. From a tooling perspective, do you think representing these missing keys as Never will make it easier to handle conditional types that check for argument presence?

Definitely. If I have a conditional like 'T if IsAssignable[K, ReadOnly[TypedDict(x=Never)]] else S', it gives us a clean way to handle optionality without guessing. It's much better than just having the key disappear from the mapping entirely.

Exactly. The PEP explicitly mentions this behavior was designed to support 'processing **kwargs with type level computation.' It ensures that the 'shape' of the TypedDict remains predictable for operators like IsAssignable or the new conditional 'if/else' syntax, even when the user omits parameters.

So I've been reading through this PEP 827 proposal by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov. Is it just me, or are we just trying to turn Python into TypeScript now? Mapped types, conditional types, 'Mad Emperor' nicknames... it feels like the simplicity of Python is being buried under a mountain of type-level boilerplate.

It is definitely a significant shift toward more complex type-level machinery. The authors, including Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, acknowledge that this is inspired by TypeScript. However, they argue that Python's existing metaprogramming—like what you see in ORMs or NumPy—is already very dynamic and complex. Currently, the type system just can't keep up without custom plugins. PEP 827 is trying to bridge that gap using the language's own syntax rather than hidden compiler magic.

I get the 'gap' argument, but look at the NumPy broadcasting example. Broadcasting is already confusing enough. Does this PEP actually make it better, or just add another layer of stuff that can break? How would you even define a broadcasted shape in this system?

It uses type-level recursion and operators. To handle broadcasting, you'd define a helper like 'MergeOne[T, S]'. It uses a conditional type to check if the dimensions are compatible. The syntax looks like this: 'T if typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]] else S if typing.IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]] else typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]'. It checks if the types match or if one is a 1-dimension. If they don't, it actually triggers a 'RaiseError' with a custom message that shows up in your IDE.
Okay, that 'RaiseError' part is interesting. Better than a generic 'Type mismatch' I guess. But how does it handle whole tuples of shapes? Do I have to manually write out every dimension?

No, you use recursion with the new 'Slice' and 'GetArg' operators. You can define a 'Broadcast[T, S]' alias that walks down the input tuples. It uses 'typing.Slice' to drop the last element and 'typing.GetArg' with index 'Literal[-1]' to get the last dimension. Then it uses an unpacked comprehension to rebuild the shape. It looks like: 'tuple[*Broadcast[DropLast[T], DropLast[S]], MergeOne[Last[T], Last[S]]]'. It keeps going until one of the tuples is empty, which it checks using 'typing.Length[T]' and 'typing.Bool'.

I have to admit, being able to catch a 'Broadcast mismatch: Literal[2], Literal[3]' error at the static analysis stage instead of waiting for a runtime crash is a win. I still think the syntax 'for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]' is a bit much, but if it means I don't need a specialized mypy plugin for every library I use, maybe there's some value in it.

That is exactly the trade-off the PEP proposes. By adding these primitives—like 'IsAssignable', 'Members', and 'NewProtocol'—framework authors can write the logic once in the typing module. It makes the type system more powerful so the user's experience in the IDE is smoother, even if the 'under the hood' type definitions look a bit more like a functional programming language. It is still just a Draft for Python 3.15, so the community is still debating if this is the right level of complexity.

Hey nanochat! I've been reading through the Draft for PEP 827 and my mind is blown. I spend half my life writing dataclasses and custom attrs-style decorators, and seeing 'Type Manipulation' in the title got me hyped. Can we really use dot notation on types now? Like, how does accessing members of a class work inside a type alias?

It is definitely an exciting time for the Python typing world! Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov have proposed something quite powerful. The 'Type Member Access' feature is a huge part of that. In PEP 827, when you use an operator like `Members[T]` or `Attrs[T]`, you get back a tuple of `Member` objects. You can then use dot notation—exactly like regular Python—to access specific metadata about that member. For a `Member` object `m`, you can access `m.name` to get the literal string name, `m.type` for the actual type, and even `m.init` to see the default value or initializer.

That's exactly what I was hoping for! So if I'm trying to generate a constructor—basically a type-level __init__—I could just loop through the attributes and pull their names and types? How would that look if I wanted to distinguish between a regular field and one with a default value?

Spot on! The PEP actually provides a 'dataclasses-style' example for this. You would use an unpacked comprehension to iterate over the attributes. To handle defaults, you would check `m.init`. For example, if you are building a list of `Param` objects for a `Callable`, you can do something like: `typing.Param[p.name, p.type, Literal['keyword', 'default'] if p.init else Literal['keyword']]`. This uses that dot notation to grab the name and type from each attribute `p` in your `Iter[Attrs[T]]`. The `p.init` access is specifically designed to work with a new `InitField` type that captures things like `Field(default=0)` or direct assignments.
Wait, I saw `Param` mentioned too. Does it work the same way as `Member`? If I'm introspecting a function instead of a class, can I use the same dot notation on the parameters?

Yes, the consistency is one of the best parts of the proposal! `Param` objects, which are used inside the new `Params` and `Callable` extended formats, also support dot notation. You can access `.name`, `.type`, and `.quals`. The `.quals` (qualifiers) bit is especially cool because it tells you if a parameter is positional-only, keyword-only, or a variadic `*args`/`**kwargs` via `ParamQuals` like `*` or `**`. It makes it possible to take an existing function's signature and programmatically transform it into a new one, which is a dream for anyone building frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic!

I am so ready for Python 3.15 if this lands. Being able to 'GetMemberType' and then just use 'm.name' inside a 'NewProtocol' definition is going to save me from so many messy mypy plugins. Thanks for the breakdown, nanochat!

You're very welcome! It really does feel like we're getting 'TypeScript-level' powers but adapted for Python's unique style. If you want to dive deeper into the implementation of things like `UpdateClass` or how `IsAssignable` works within those conditionals, just let me know. Happy typing!

Hey nanochat. I've been maintaining a nasty mypy plugin for my ORM's select logic--basically trying to simulate what Prisma does in TypeScript. I saw PEP 827. Is this finally the 'plugin killer' I've been waiting for?

It very well might be! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, specifically targets that 'missing features from TypeScript' gap. The proposal aims to replace the need for custom typechecker plugins with primitives like `Members[T]`, `NewProtocol`, and conditional types (`T if bool_typ else S`) that can model the kind of metaprogramming you're doing in your ORM. It's currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15.

My biggest worry is breaking things. If I start sticking 'if' and 'for' inside my type aliases, will older versions of Python or existing tools just blow up? My users aren't all on 3.15 yet, obviously.

In a strict sense, the PEP only proposes new features and doesn't change the Python grammar itself. However, it does introduce complex syntax for annotations, like `*[... for t in Iter[...]]`. For older Python versions, you'd likely need to wrap these in strings or use `from __future__ import annotations`. The PEP notes that tools relying on runtime introspection might have trouble because the `__annotate__` function will now contain logic. To help with this, they've proposed a `special_form_evaluator` ContextVar hook so framework authors can provide an evaluator for these computed types at runtime.

Okay, let's talk about the 'Update' logic. My plugin currently 'injects' an `__init__` into classes. The PEP mentions `UpdateClass`. How does that work without breaking the nominal class structure? Is it just for structural typing?

Actually, `UpdateClass[*Ps: Member]` is designed to update existing nominal classes. It's a special form used in the return type of a type decorator or `__init_subclass__`. When the type checker sees a class decorated with a function returning `UpdateClass`, it applies those member changes (overriding old ones or adding new ones) to that specific class. This allows you to generate an `__init__` based on class attributes without needing a mypy plugin. For example, you could iterate over `Attrs[T]` and construct a `Member` for `__init__` using the new `Extended Callables` format.

What about union lifting? My ORM fields are often `int | None`. If I run a type-level operator on a union, do I just get back `Any` or some 'Never' error? That's where my current plugin logic gets really messy.

PEP 827 handles this elegantly through 'Lifting over Unions.' Built-in operators like `IsAssignable`, `GetArg`, and `GetMemberType` are automatically lifted. This means the engine takes the cross-product of the union elements, evaluates the operator for each, and then unions all the results together. So if you check `IsAssignable[int | str, float]`, it evaluates for `int` and `str` separately. This keeps your results precise even when dealing with optional or union types.

I'm looking at the `Members[T]` part. My plugin currently looks at unannotated methods sometimes. The PEP says it only includes members with explicit type annotations. Is that a hard rule? It might break my logic if I can't see 'implied' methods.

Currently, yes. The specification for `Members[T]` and `Attrs[T]` states that only members with explicit type annotations are included to ensure consistency between static and runtime evaluation. This is actually listed as an 'Open Issue' in the PEP--the authors are still debating whether to include unannotated methods. If this lands as written, you'd need your users to provide at least a basic annotation for methods they want your ORM logic to 'see' through type-level inspection.

One last deep dive: the `IsAssignable` check. How does that handle Protocols at runtime? That's always been the wall for my plugin--detecting if a type matches a structural interface without a huge performance hit or complex AST walking.

That is a known limitation. The PEP authors admit that while `IsAssignable[T, S]` is powerful for static checkers, runtime evaluators will likely be 'best effort.' Checking against Protocols at runtime is notoriously difficult because class attributes can be inferred or missing from `__dict__`. The PEP doesn't include an official evaluator in the standard library but suggests a third-party library will be provided. Most of the power is intended for static analysis, while runtime tools can use the `special_form_evaluator` hook to decide how strictly they want to enforce those checks.

Hey, I've been maintaining a pretty complex mypy plugin for my ORM to handle dynamic field selection. I keep hearing PEP 827 might let me ditch the plugin entirely. Is that actually the goal here, or is it just for simple stuff?

That is exactly one of the primary goals. The authors, Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, specifically mention in the motivation that relying on custom mypy plugins is a problem because many IDEs and tools don't support them. PEP 827 aims to bridge the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and the type system so you can define those transformations directly in Python code. It's designed to handle complex cases like Prisma-style ORMs or FastAPI models.

My plugin basically looks at a class, sees which fields the user requested in a 'select' call, and then spits out a new anonymous type. How does the PEP handle 'looking at a class' without a plugin hook?

It introduces several new operators in the 'typing' module for object inspection. The heavy lifter for your case would be 'Members[T]' or 'Attrs[T]'. These produce a tuple of 'Member' types that describe the attributes and methods of a class. You can then iterate over these at the type level using a new syntax called an unpacked comprehension. For example: '*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]'.
Wait, that looks like a list comprehension inside a type bracket. Can I actually filter them too? Like, 'if the field name is in my selection'?

Exactly. You can use 'if' clauses in those comprehensions and a 'conditional type' syntax. To check if a field is selected, you might use 'typing.IsAssignable' to compare the member name against a set of keys. If you wanted to build a new type with just those fields, you'd use 'NewProtocol'. Here is a basic look at how you might define a helper to pick specific keys:

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

The '.name' there is a type member access, which is another feature of PEP 827.

That is wild. But in my ORM, I don't just pick the field; I might need to change its type. Like if it is a 'Link' to another table, I want to wrap it in a list if it's a 'MultiLink'. Is that possible?

Yes, you can use conditional types to transform the field types as you iterate. The syntax is 'true_type if bool_type else false_type'. You could define a 'ConvertField' alias that checks the property type:

type ConvertField[T] = (
    list[GetArg[T, Link, Literal[0]]] 
    if typing.IsAssignable[T, MultiLink] 
    else T
)

Then in your 'NewProtocol' block, you'd pass the member type through that helper. It uses 'IsAssignable' to perform the check and 'GetArg' to extract the inner type of the link. This moves all that logic you currently have in your mypy plugin's 'get_method_signature_hook' directly into the type annotations.
I'm worried about the performance or the complexity for my users. Does this require a special runtime flag or something for Python 3.15?

PEP 827 is currently a Draft for Python 3.15. One of the unique aspects is 'Runtime evaluation support'. The PEP doesn't put an evaluator in the standard library, but it adds hooks like 'special_form_evaluator' (a ContextVar) so that third-party libraries like Pydantic can trigger the evaluation of these loops and conditionals at runtime. For the user, it looks like standard Python, but for the framework author, you're essentially writing 'type-level' programs that both the static checker and the runtime can understand.

Hey! I was just getting comfortable with PEP 681 and @dataclass_transform for some of my custom model classes. Then I see PEP 827 'Type Manipulation' popping up. Is this basically a replacement for 681 or something totally different?

You can think of it as a much more powerful, generalized evolution. PEP 681 was a 'lowest-common denominator' specifically created to help type checkers understand libraries that behave like dataclasses. PEP 827, which is currently a Draft for Python 3.15 by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, aims to give you the tools to define those transformations yourself directly in the type system.

While PEP 681 is a special-case decorator, PEP 827 introduces a whole suite of type-level operators like Members[T], IsAssignable, and UpdateClass. It's designed to eliminate the need for custom typechecker plugins or hardcoded support for specific libraries.
So instead of just telling the type checker 'this class acts like a dataclass,' I actually describe how the class is built? Like, I could write logic to generate the __init__ signature?

Exactly. The PEP actually includes an example of 'dataclasses-style method generation.' Instead of a black-box decorator, you could use the UpdateClass operator in an __init_subclass__ or a decorator to add a programmatically generated __init__ method.

You would use Attrs[T] to inspect the attributes of your class and then use a comprehension to build the parameters. It looks something like this:

`*[typing.Param[p.name, p.type] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]`

This constructs an extended callable type that the type checker understands natively, without needing to know anything specifically about dataclasses.
Wait, I'm doing a loop and an 'if' check inside a type hint? That feels wild. I just learned how to use TypeVar and Generics, and now there's basically a mini-language inside the brackets.

It is a big jump! The proposal introduces 'type-level manipulation' inspired by TypeScript. It adds syntactic forms like `true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ` and unpacked comprehensions like `*[... for t in Iter[...]]`.

It's intended for framework and library maintainers who currently have to write complex mypy plugins to get their APIs to typecheck. For example, the PEP shows how you could automatically derive FastAPI CRUD models—like creating an 'Update' version of a class where every field is automatically made optional—using only these new typing primitives.
That Prisma-style ORM example in the PEP looks cool too. But doesn't this make the type annotations super hard to read at runtime? Like, if I call __annotations__, what even comes out?

That's a great point. The PEP authors recognize that runtime evaluation is vital for libraries like Pydantic. They don't propose an official evaluator in the standard library yet, but they suggest a 'special_form_evaluator' hook in the typing module. This would allow third-party libraries to trigger the evaluation of these loops and conditionals when you access __annotate__.

If the evaluator is set to None, these new forms like typing.Iter would just return empty defaults (like an empty iterator) to avoid breaking existing code, but the goal is to make these 'computed types' fully introspectable at runtime.

Okay, so it's more flexible than PEP 681 because I can do things like filter out 'hidden' fields or change types on the fly. Since it's targeting 3.15, I guess I have some time to stick with my current setup?

Definitely. It's still in the Draft stage. PEP 681 isn't going anywhere, and for simple dataclass-like patterns, it's much less verbose than writing a full PEP 827 transformation. But if you've ever felt limited by what @dataclass_transform can do—like not being able to handle complex keyword parameter modifications or recursive type transformations—PEP 827 is the future answer the authors are proposing.

Hey nanochat. I have been reading through PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. While the idea of type-level manipulation is cool, I am worried this is going to make type error messages impossible to debug. We already have specialized PEPs like 681 for dataclass transforms and 646 for variadic generics. If we start nesting conditional types and unpacked comprehensions, aren't we just creating a maintenance nightmare for whoever has to read the CI output?

That is a fair critique of adding this much power to the type system. However, the authors actually address this specifically with the RaiseError operator. Instead of a generic failure, library authors can write something like RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]. This allows a framework to produce a specific, readable error message when a type manipulation fails. It is actually meant to solve the problem where PEP 681 or custom mypy plugins might fail in ways that are hard to diagnose across different IDEs that do not support the same plugin API.

The RaiseError bit makes sense for frameworks, but the syntax still feels heavy. I'm looking at how it compares to PEP 692 or PEP 764. If I use an unpacked comprehension like "*[p.name for p in Iter[Members[T]]]", that seems way more complex than just a simple TypedDict. Also, how does this play with PEP 649? If we are deferring evaluation of these annotations, does a runtime tool like Pydantic just see a giant mess of un-evaluated operators?

It is definitely more complex than a standard TypedDict, but it is meant for cases where one-size-fits-all PEPs like 692 fall short, such as when you need to selectively transform fields for an ORM. Regarding PEP 649, PEP 827 is designed to be compatible. It introduces a special_form_evaluator ContextVar hook. This lets runtime libraries trigger evaluation when they call __bool__ on a boolean operator or __iter__ on typing.Iter. The authors even plan to release a third-party evaluator library for this. So while the syntax is denser, it provides a standardized way to handle the metaprogramming that previously required specialized plugins or lowest-common-denominator tools like @dataclass_transform.

yo, type manipulation! I'm tired of rewriting my Pydantic models for every CRUD action in FastAPI—Hero, HeroCreate, HeroUpdate, it's just so much boilerplate. I saw PEP 827 might let me derive them automatically using things like NewProtocol and Iter. But if I mess up a calculation, like trying to get an attribute that isn't there, does the whole thing just crash with a type error, or does it return Never? The 'Open Issues' section mentioned something about that.

It's a great question, and you're right that PEP 827 aims to solve that exact FastAPI boilerplate problem. According to the current draft spec for Python 3.15, most type operators—like GetArg or GetMemberType—are designed to return Never if they are evaluated with invalid arguments, such as trying to fetch an argument index that doesn't exist. However, the authors (Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov) have officially listed the choice between returning Never and raising a hard type error as an 'Open Issue.' If you specifically want to trigger a failure during your CRUD model derivation to catch logic bugs, the PEP provides a RaiseError operator. This allows you to explicitly generate a type error with a custom message if a condition isn't met. Otherwise, the 'lifting over unions' logic and general operator behavior tend to favor producing Never to avoid crashing the type checker unnecessarily.

hey hey, I've been stuck writing HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate models for every single entity in my FastAPI app. I just stumbled onto PEP 827 and it looks like a lifesaver. But I'm confused about the syntax. Why are operators like IsAssignable and NewProtocol using square brackets instead of just being regular functions with parentheses? It feels like I'm stuck in Bracket City.

It is definitely a change of pace! The authors of PEP 827 -- Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov -- actually addressed this specifically in the 'Alternate syntax ideas' section of the proposal. They considered using parentheses for type operators to make them look more like functions, but they decided against it for consistency.

In Python's typing system, subscripting with square brackets is the established way to specialize generics or call type aliases. If they allowed parentheses for built-in operators like IsAssignable[T, S], you'd end up with a confusing mix of syntax. For example, if you wrote a helper alias like PointerArg[T], you would still have to use brackets for that, even if the built-in operators used parentheses. The PEP argues that keeping everything in brackets leads to a better developer experience because you don't have to remember which APIs use which characters.

That makes some sense, but I thought these were basically just class factories. If I'm building a HeroUpdate model where everything is optional, can't I just write a regular Python function that loops over the fields and returns a new class? Why do I need this NewProtocol[... for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]] stuff?

That's a common misconception with this proposal. While you can write logic to generate Pydantic models at runtime today, static type checkers like mypy or Pyright have no idea what those dynamic classes look like. They usually require custom plugins to understand things like @dataclass_transform.

PEP 827 moves that logic into the type system itself. When you use NewProtocol or NewTypedDict with an unpacked comprehension like *[typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]], you are giving the type checker a blueprint it can evaluate statically. This way, your IDE can actually provide autocomplete for those dynamically derived models without needing a special plugin for every framework.
Wait, I just noticed you used 'if' in one of your examples. Is that a regular Python if-statement? I tried putting a standard if/else inside my type alias and my linter went crazy.

Ah, that's another bit of troubleshooting! It isn't a standard Python statement. PEP 827 introduces 'Conditional types' with a specific syntax: <type> if <type-bool> else <type>. This exists entirely inside the type annotation.

For example, if you were implementing the Create model for FastAPI and wanted to check if a field is a primary key, you would use something like:

GetDefault[p.init] if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal['primary_key']]] else Never

This isn't executed as normal code when your module loads; it's part of the grammar that type checkers (and the proposed runtime evaluator) use to compute the final type. You can't use standard Python loops or blocks here; you have to use the specific forms like typing.Iter for comprehensions and the ternary-style if/else for logic.

yo, i am a typescript dev checking out the python 3.15 draft for pep 827. the conditional types look super familiar to what i use in ts, but i was looking at the future extensions and saw NewProtocolWithBases. what is that for and why is it deferred?

welcome to the python typing world. dr. michael j. sully sullivan, daniel w. park, and the mad emperor yury selivanov explicitly cited typescript as a huge influence for this proposal. NewProtocolWithBases is a specialized form intended to define a structural protocol that also requires a set of nominal base classes. its signature is NewProtocolWithBases[Bases: tuple[type], *Ms: Member]. it is currently deferred because the authors felt that adding base class requirements to protocols was a significant change to the protocol system that they did not want to tangle with yet. they also believe many of those use cases can be simulated using the other tools in the pep for now.

that tracks. it sounds like it would have been perfect for something like pydantic where you want the class to behave like a specific model base but with dynamic fields. when they say it can be simulated, are they talking about using NewProtocol with standard inheritance?

precisely. the pep mentions that NewProtocolWithBases would specifically help when creating things like pydantic models, but the draft is focused on the core primitives first. for now, you would use NewProtocol to define the structural requirements of your members. since this is all still a draft for python 3.15, the authors are prioritizing the main operators like IsAssignable, Members, and the new conditional if-else syntax for types before expanding how protocols themselves function.
yo nanochat, pep 827 time! I saw a thread on Twitter about how Python is finally getting booleans in the type system. Does this mean I can just write x: True or something instead of using bool?

Not quite! PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation, is actually about something much more powerful than just replacing the bool type. It introduces what it calls type booleans to help Python perform logic during type checking.

Think of it as a way for the type checker to ask questions about your code before it even runs. Instead of just saying a variable is a boolean, you use these type booleans to decide what a type should be. For example, you can write a type that changes depending on whether one class is assignable to another.
Oh, so it is just like a regular Python if-statement? Like, I could check if a list is empty using if len(my_list) > 0 inside the type hint?

That is a common misconception! You cannot use regular Python logic like len() or > inside these type hints. PEP 827 defines a very specific set of boolean operators that only work on types.

These special operators include things like IsAssignable[T, S], which checks if type T can fit into type S, or IsEquivalent[T, S]. You can combine them using and, or, and not, but they have to be these specific type-level tools. Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and the other authors, Daniel W.
