Park and Yury Selivanov, designed it this way so that type checkers and runtime libraries can handle the logic without getting confused by normal Python values.

Wait, the PEP says these evaluate to Literal[True] or Literal[False]. If they just turn into Literals anyway, why does the PEP make a big deal about a special category for them? Why can't I just use any Literal[True] in an if-else type?

That is a great catch. While they do evaluate to Literal[True] or Literal[False] in the end, PEP 827 strictly limits what can appear in a conditional type (the true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ syntax).

The reason is technical: the proposal wants to allow libraries to hook into these operators at runtime. If the PEP allowed any old Literal[True] to trigger this logic, it might break existing code or make it impossible for frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI to evaluate the types properly. By restricting the bool_typ to this special subset of operators like IsAssignable or Bool[T], the system stays predictable. It ensures that when you see an if in a type hint, it is performing a specific type-level calculation rather than just checking a static value.
yo nanochat, I have been digging through PEP 827. Is this just a fancy version of the dataclass_transform from PEP 681, or are we finally getting something like TypeScript utility types?

It is definitely closer to the TypeScript approach. While PEP 681 was a specialized fix for dataclass-like patterns, PEP 827 provides a general toolkit for type manipulation. It introduces operators like Members[T] and Attrs[T] that let you introspect a class. These return a tuple of Member types, which are essentially objects that describe the attributes and methods of a class.
Right, I saw that. But how do I actually use those Member objects? I noticed the PEP mentions dot notation like m.name and m.type. Is that allowed in Python type hints now?

In the specific context of PEP 827's type manipulation syntax, yes. This is called Type Member Access. When you are iterating over a tuple of members using the new comprehension syntax, like *[some_transform[m.type] for m in Iter[Attrs[T]]], you use dot notation to pull specific metadata. For a Member object, you can access .name for the literal string name, .type for the field type, .quals for qualifiers like Final or ClassVar, and .init for the initializer type. This allows you to programmatically reconstruct types, like creating a NewProtocol where every attribute name is the same but the types are modified.

That is a big shift from the static generics we have in PEP 695. Does this work for functions too? I am looking at the Param type and it also seems to have dot accessors. Is that related to PEP 692 or PEP 646?

Exactly. PEP 827 introduces Extended Callables to allow introspection of function signatures. Just as classes have Member objects, callables use Params[*Ps] containing Param objects. A Param has dot accessors for .name, .type, and .quals. This goes beyond PEP 692 and PEP 646 by letting you programmatically add, remove, or modify specific parameters. For instance, you could use an unpacked comprehension to iterate over a function's Param list and use dot notation to find all keyword-only parameters and make them optional.
One technical detail: what happens if I try to use dot notation on a Union type? Also, how does this interact with the deferred evaluation ideas in PEP 649?

The PEP is very specific: type member access is NOT lifted over union types. If you try to use m.name on a Union, it is an error at both type-checking and runtime. As for PEP 649, the authors of PEP 827 note that something like the Format.AST mode for fetching annotations would be a perfect companion. Since PEP 827 relies on runtime evaluation of loops and conditionals via a new special_form_evaluator hook, having the raw AST would make it easier for tools to process these complex annotations without full execution.
hi! I am looking into PEP 827 for a validation library I am working on. Can you explain what GetMemberType actually does? I am trying to see if it replaces some of the hacky introspection I do now.

Hello! GetMemberType is one of the core object inspection operators introduced in PEP 827. Essentially, it allows you to extract the type of a specific attribute or method from a class or TypedDict at the type level. The syntax looks like GetMemberType[T, S], where T is your class and S is a Literal string of the member name. If the member exists and has an explicit type annotation, it returns that type; otherwise, it returns Never.

That sounds perfect. So if I have a Model class with 'email: str', I can just do GetMemberType[Model, Literal['email']] and get back str? Does it handle inherited members or only things defined directly on the class?

Yes, that is exactly how it works! Regarding inheritance, the PEP specifies that it produces information for members of the class, which includes inherited ones. One important detail for your library is that it only picks up members with explicit type annotations. It is also 'lifted' over unions, so if you passed it a union of two models, you would get a union of the types of that member from both classes. Just keep in mind this is targeting Python 3.15 and is currently in Draft status.
Draft is fine, I am just planning ahead. What is the difference between GetMemberType and GetMember? I saw both in the draft.

The difference is how much data you get back. GetMemberType[T, S] gives you just the type (the 'T' in 'name: T'). However, GetMember[T, S] returns a full Member object. A Member object is a special type that packages the name, the type, any qualifiers like Final or ClassVar, the defining class, and even the initializer type if there is one. For a validation framework, GetMember is often more useful because you can check m.quals to see if a field is ReadOnly or m.init to see if it has a default value like Field(default=None).

hey nanochat, got a sec for python typing? i've been maintaining a nasty mypy plugin for my orm to handle field extraction, but i'm looking at pep 827 to see if i can finally ditch the plugin logic. specifically, how does GetMemberType work? is it basically a string-based lookup like typescript's indexed access types?

It's very similar to TypeScript's indexed access, but adapted for Python's nominal-leaning system. In PEP 827, GetMemberType[T, S] takes a type T (like a class or TypedDict) and a literal string S. It extracts the type of that specific member. If the member doesn't exist, it returns Never. Unlike a mypy plugin where you have to manually traverse the MRO and handle symbol tables, GetMemberType handles the lookup logic for you within the type system itself.

okay, but in my plugin i can see qualifiers like ClassVar or if it's a Final field. does GetMemberType just give me the raw type, or does it preserve those qualifiers? in ts, T[K] just gives you the value type, usually losing the readonly-ness unless you use other utilities.

Actually, PEP 827 separates the 'raw type' from the 'member metadata'. If you use GetMemberType[T, S], you get just the type. But if you need the metadata like ClassVar, Final, or even the initializer value, you should use GetMember[T, S]. That returns a Member type, which has associated type members you can access via dot notation: .name, .type, .quals, and .init. The .quals member will contain a union of MemberQuals like 'ClassVar' or 'ReadOnly'.

that dot notation m.type is wild. i'm used to mypy's internal API where i'm checking 'is_classvar' flags on a SymbolTableNode. what happens if i try to use GetMemberType on a Union? my plugin currently has to loop over every item in the union. do i have to write a comprehension for that in pep 827?

You don't! PEP 827 defines a concept called 'Lifting over Unions'. Operators in the Object Inspection section, including GetMemberType and GetMember, are automatically lifted. This means if T is 'A | B', GetMemberType[A | B, 'field'] automatically evaluates to 'GetMemberType[A, "field"] | GetMemberType[B, "field"]'. It takes the cross product of the union elements for you, which is a huge step up from the manual iteration you'd do in a mypy plugin or even some TypeScript conditional type gymnastics.

that sounds way cleaner. one more thing—my plugin currently handles some magic where if a field is 'primary_key=True' in a Field() call, i change the type. can GetMemberType see into those Field() assignments?

It can, provided you use the InitField mechanism described by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov. If your field is assigned using something that inherits from InitField, like 'id: int = Field(primary_key=True)', that information is captured in the .init attribute of the Member. You can then use a conditional type with IsAssignable to check if 'primary_key' is Literal[True] inside that init metadata. It allows you to replace that custom plugin 'magic' with a standard 'type NewT = ... if ... else ...' expression.

I am so tired of writing Hero, HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate models in FastAPI. It is constant duplication. I saw PEP 827 by Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov might fix this. How does it handle the boilerplate?

Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov designed PEP 827 specifically to bridge the gap between dynamic Python metaprogramming and the type system. For your FastAPI use case, you could define a single Hero model and then use type aliases to compute the other forms. For example, you could write 'type HeroPublic = Public[Hero]', where 'Public' is a type-level helper that uses the new 'NewProtocol' and 'Iter[Attrs[T]]' features to filter out fields marked as 'hidden' or to make the primary key non-optional automatically.

That sounds great for my IDE, but FastAPI and Pydantic actually need those types at runtime to validate JSON. If I use this new syntax like 'tt if tb else tf' or comprehensions in my annotations, won't Python just crash or treat them as garbage when the framework tries to inspect the class?

The PEP authors recognized that runtime evaluation is critical for frameworks like Pydantic. To support this, PEP 827 introduces a new hook in the 'typing' module: a ContextVar named 'special_form_evaluator'. This variable holds a callable that an evaluator library (which the authors plan to release as a third-party tool) can use to intercept type evaluation. When you call the '__annotate__' method of a class or function, it triggers the evaluation of these computed types.

So it is a ContextVar hook. How does it actually intercept the logic though? If I have an 'if' check like 'IsAssignable[T, list]', what does the evaluator actually do to prevent it from just being a static object?

The 'special_form_evaluator' is invoked in two specific scenarios: when '__bool__' is called on a Boolean Operator (like 'IsAssignable', 'IsEquivalent', or 'Bool') and when '__iter__' is called on 'typing.Iter'. When the evaluator is set, it receives a 'typing._GenericAlias' argument and returns a value that then has 'bool()' or 'iter()' called on it. If 'special_form_evaluator' is 'None' (the default), Boolean operators simply return 'False' and 'Iter' returns an empty iterator. This mechanism allows tools to 'drive' the evaluation of the 'if' and 'for' logic embedded in your type annotations at runtime.

hi nanochat. i am working on an ide plugin and trying to implement support for pep 827. how does this proposal handle function parameter metadata compared to something like typescript's parameter types or python's own inspect.signature? specifically, how do we tell a positional-only arg from a keyword-only one?

hello! pep 827 introduces a much more structured way to represent callables using the 'param' and 'params' types. unlike the simpler 'paramspec' from pep 612, this is very similar to 'inspect.signature' because it explicitly tracks names and qualifiers. to distinguish parameter types, it uses 'param[n, t, q]', where 'q' is a union of 'paramquals' like 'keyword', 'positional', and 'default'. for example, a positional-only parameter is represented as 'posparam[literal["name"], type]', which is an alias for 'param[literal["name"], type, literal["positional"]]'. keyword-only parameters use 'namedparam' or have 'keyword' in their qualifier list.
that is quite a jump from typescript where rest parameters are just an array type. how does pep 827 represent *args and **kwargs then? does it just use the names 'args' and 'kwargs'?

it is a big departure from typescript's approach. in pep 827, '*args' and '**kwargs' are represented by 'argsparam[t]' and 'kwargsparam[t]'. these are actually aliases for 'param[none, t, literal["*"]]' and 'param[none, t, literal["**"]]'. interestingly, the name slot 'n' is set to 'none' for these variadic parameters because, at the type level, the specific identifier used in the source (like 'args' or 'kwargs') does not change how the type system treats the variadic unpacking.

from a tooling perspective, if the name is 'none', how am i supposed to provide go-to-definition or renaming for the '*args' variable in a user's code? 'inspect.signature' actually keeps the string name. is there a reason the type system discards it?

pep 827's 'extended callable' format is optimized for type-level computation and construction, not necessarily as a full replacement for the ast or runtime 'inspect' metadata. because 'callable' types can be constructed dynamically—for example, via 'updateclass' or by iterating over 'members[t]'—the proposal prioritizes the structural properties of the signature. the name is 'none' for variadics because 'argsparam' and 'kwargsparam' are intended to match any variadic name. if you need the actual variable name for 'go-to-definition', you would still look at the 'inspect' metadata or the original 'annotate' function, which the pep author notes will be triggered when tools call it at runtime.
okay, so i should treat 'params' as a structural description. what about defaults? in 'inspect.signature' i have to check for a 'default' attribute. here it is just another literal in the qualifier?

exactly. the 'q' parameter in 'param' uses 'literal["default"]' to indicate a default value exists. there are helper aliases provided for this: 'posdefaultparam' and 'nameddefaultparam'. these use a union of literals, like 'literal["keyword", "default"]'. one unique thing about pep 827 is that while it tracks that a default exists, it does not necessarily store the runtime value of that default inside the type itself, unless it is being derived from an 'initfield' where it might be captured as a 'literal' type.

hello, trying to wrap my head around a new PEP. I've been reading about PEP 827 and it seems like it's trying to replace the magic of @dataclass or attrs. But isn't this just moving the complexity from a custom mypy plugin into a really dense type definition? Like, if I want to generate an __init__ method for my class based on its fields, how does that actually look under this proposal?

You've hit on a central point of the proposal by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. The authors acknowledge that while libraries like dataclasses use @dataclass_transform (PEP 681) or custom mypy plugins, those solutions aren't portable across all IDEs and type checkers. PEP 827 moves that logic into the type system itself so it's standardized.

To generate an `__init__` method, you use a combination of `Members` (to see the class attributes) and `NewProtocol` or `UpdateClass`. For example, a helper alias might look like this:

```python
type InitFnType[T] = typing.Member[
    Literal["__init__"],
    Callable[
        [
            typing.Param[Literal["self"], Self],
            *[
                typing.Param[
                    p.name,
                    p.type,
                    Literal["keyword"]
                ]
                for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
            ],
        ],
        None,
    ],
    Literal["ClassVar"],
]
```

It is definitely more dense than a decorator, but the benefit is that any type checker supporting PEP 827 can 'calculate' the signature without needing a specialized plugin for your specific library.

That InitFnType example looks pretty wild with all the typing.Iter and typing.Attrs. I see UpdateClass mentioned too—how do I actually apply that to a class so it feels like a regular decorator? And what happens if my field has a default value? Does the generated __init__ actually know that the parameter should be optional?

To use it like a decorator, you would define a function where the return type is `UpdateClass`. When the type checker sees this, it 'updates' the nominal class with the members you've computed. For example:

```python
def dataclass_ish[T](cls: type[T]) -> typing.UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]:
    ...
```

Regarding defaults, PEP 827 handles this using conditional types and the `init` field of a `Member`. The PEP includes an `InitField` type that captures initializer information. You can write a helper to check if a field has a default and then adjust the `Param` qualifiers:

```python
# Inside the comprehension for InitFnType
Literal["keyword", "default"] if typing.IsAssignable[GetDefault[p.init], Never] == False else Literal["keyword"]
```

By checking `p.init`, the logic can decide whether to include the `"default"` qualifier in the `Param` definition. If it's there, the type checker treats that argument as optional in the generated `__init__`. This effectively replicates the logic usually hidden inside the mypy source code, but makes it visible and editable in your own Python code.

Hey nanochat. I was looking through the PEP 827 draft by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. I am trying to wrap my head around "InitField". We already have dataclass_transform (PEP 681) to handle some of this. Why is a new primitive necessary here, and how does it actually hook into the class body evaluation?

The core issue PEP 827 addresses is that PEP 681 is a lowest-common denominator solution that often requires hardcoded support or custom plugins in type checkers. InitField[KwargDict] is a more generic tool. It is designed to capture the arguments passed to field descriptors like Pydantic's Field or dataclasses.field at the type level. When you instantiate a subtype of InitField in a class body, the type system captures the keyword arguments and stores them in a TypedDict. This information is then exposed through the ".init" attribute of the Member type, which is produced by the Members[T] or Attrs[T] operators. This allows you to perform type-level computation on things like defaults or metadata to generate new types, like a "Create" model that strips the primary key.

Interesting. But how does the inference of the "KwargDict" work specifically? If I have a line like "foo: int = InitField(default=0, primary_key=True)", how does the type checker know to treat those as Literals rather than just "int" and "bool"? That seems like a significant change from how standard assignment works.

You have identified one of the key prerequisites in the PEP. To make InitField work, PEP 827 proposes supporting "Unpack" of typevars for "**kwargs". The specification states that when inferring types for these unpacked keyword arguments, the type checker should infer literal types whenever possible. In your example, the initializer's type would be inferred as something like InitField[TypedDict({'default': Literal[0], 'primary_key': Literal[True]})]. This literal-first inference is what allows type-level helpers to extract exact configuration values. This data is then stored in the Member.init field, making it available for conditional types and comprehensions like "*[p for p in Iter[Attrs[T]] if not IsAssignable[GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal['primary_key']], Literal[True]]]". This bridges the gap between dynamic framework metadata and the static type system without needing specialized compiler plugins.

Hey. I've been reviewing the PEP 827 draft. As someone who works on CPython internals, I'm naturally cautious about adding more weight to the typing module. We just got PEP 681 (dataclass_transform) stabilized as a 'special-case' solution. Why should we pivot to this much more complex type manipulation machinery instead of just refining the existing special-case decorators?

The authors of PEP 827—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—actually point to the limitations of PEP 681 as a primary motivation. They argue that while dataclass_transform covers a common subset, it requires hardcoded support in every typechecker. Many tools don't have a plugin API, so consistent typechecking across IDEs and CI is hard to maintain. PEP 827 aims to provide generic primitives, like 'Members', 'NewProtocol', and 'UpdateClass', so that framework authors can implement their own logic directly in the type system without needing special-case decorators or external plugins. It's essentially trying to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system's current rigidity.

I'm looking at the 'UpdateClass' operator specifically. It says it can be used in the return type of '__init_subclass__'. From an implementation perspective, that sounds like a headache. The PEP mentions evaluation-order dependence as an open issue. If we're applying these in reverse MRO order, how does the proposal ensure this doesn't create a performance bottleneck during class creation or, worse, inconsistent states if one class inspects another that hasn't finished its own 'UpdateClass' application?

You've hit on a significant point of discussion in the PEP. For nominal classes, when an ancestor has an '__init_subclass__' with an 'UpdateClass' return type, those updates are applied in reverse MRO order. If the 'cls' parameter is parameterized by 'type[T]', then 'T' is substituted. The PEP acknowledges that if an 'UpdateClass' return type inspects an unrelated class's 'Members', and that other class is also undergoing its own '__init_subclass__' evaluation, the results could be order-dependent. This mirrors existing runtime complexity in Python, but it's new for the type system. To mitigate performance concerns, the draft suggests that tools specifically interested in unevaluated annotations could use a 'Format.AST' mode to keep the expressions inert, avoiding full execution during a simple import unless explicitly requested.

That brings me to the 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar. Integrating a hook into typing that triggers during '__bool__' on Boolean Operators or '__iter__' on 'typing.Iter' seems like a huge shift. If 'typing' has to manage a ContextVar just to decide if 'IsAssignable' is True or False at runtime, aren't we basically embedding a mini-type-checker into the runtime? How do we prevent this from bloating the memory footprint of every single typed function?

The 'special_form_evaluator' is indeed the core of the runtime support. By default, it's 'None', and these operators remain 'inert'—'IsAssignable' would just return False and 'Iter' would return an empty iterator. The bloat is minimized because the logic only kicks in when a framework, like Pydantic or FastAPI, explicitly sets that ContextVar and calls the '__annotate__' method of a class or function. The PEP authors don't even propose an official evaluator for the standard library; they intend to release a third-party evaluator library. This way, CPython provides the 'runtime hooks' (the infrastructure) but doesn't necessarily carry the full weight of a type-checking engine in the core 'typing' module itself.

I've been reading through PEP 827 and noticed the UpdateClass operator. As someone who uses dataclasses and attrs heavily, I've always been frustrated that type checkers can't easily see the __init__ methods we generate at runtime. How does UpdateClass solve this, and where exactly am I allowed to use it?

UpdateClass[*Ps: Member] is a special form introduced in PEP 827 specifically to bridge the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. It allows you to 'update' a nominal class by adding, overriding, or even removing members (by setting their type to Never). For your use case, you would define the signature of your generated __init__ as a Member type and then wrap it in UpdateClass. According to the specification, UpdateClass is restricted to two specific contexts: it can be the return type of a class decorator (allowing a @dataclass_ish decorator to tell the type checker about the new method) or the return type of an __init_subclass__ method. This latter case is particularly powerful for library authors, as it allows a base Model class to automatically define the type-level signature of __init__ for every subclass based on its annotated attributes. When used in __init_subclass__, the PEP notes that these updates are applied in reverse MRO order during type evaluation.

yo whats gud. i was reading up on pep 827 and it sounds like it's trying to do what @dataclass_transform does. i just finally figured out Generics, is this basically just a more complex way to handle those library-specific plugins?

Hey! Not quite. PEP 681 (dataclass_transform) was actually created because the type system couldn't model metaprogramming natively, so it provided a special-case flag for type checkers. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, aims to solve this generally. Instead of hardcoded support, it introduces type-level operators like Members[T] and conditional types so libraries can define their own transformations without needing custom mypy plugins.

So it's like I can write logic for my types? How does that work in practice? If I wanted to generate an __init__ method for a class based on its fields, how does PEP 827 even know what fields a class has without running the code?

Exactly. It uses a new primitive called typing.Members[T] (or typing.Attrs[T] for just attributes). When you pass a class to Attrs[T], it produces a tuple of Member objects. Each Member has associated type members you can access with dot notation, like m.name and m.type. Crucially, this happens at the type-system level. According to the PEP, only members with explicit type annotations are included to ensure consistency between static analysis and runtime evaluation.

Wait, if I get a tuple of fields, can I loop over them? I'm used to list comprehensions in Python, but doing that in a type annotation sounds wild. How do you filter out fields that shouldn't be in the constructor, like a primary key?

It actually looks remarkably like standard Python. PEP 827 introduces "unpacked comprehensions" using the *[...] syntax. To filter, you'd write something like: *[Member[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]] if not IsAssignable[p.type, PrimaryKey]]. It uses typing.Iter[iter_ty] to signal iteration over the tuple arguments. You can then use UpdateClass to take those generated Member objects and actually apply them back to a nominal class. It's essentially bringing the power of TypeScript's mapped and conditional types into Python's grammar-compatible syntax.

I was just looking at PEP 827 and it honestly feels like Python is trying way too hard to be TypeScript. We are getting conditional types, mapped types, and now this mess of brackets for 'type manipulation'. Why can't we just use Python for logic and keep types simple? It feels like we are over-engineering everything for static analysis.

It's a valid concern. Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov actually acknowledge the TypeScript influence right in the abstract. The motivation comes from the fact that Python is naturally very dynamic with its metaprogramming, but the current type system can't keep up. Right now, if a library does something clever with attributes, it usually requires a custom mypy plugin just to be understood by IDEs. PEP 827 tries to move that logic directly into the type system so it's consistent across all tools without needing those plugins.

But we already have things like PEP 681 for dataclass_transform. Why do we need this whole new language of 'IsAssignable' and 'UpdateClass' just to handle things like generating an __init__ method? It seems like a lot of complexity just to avoid a few lines of boilerplate.

The PEP authors argue that PEP 681 is a 'lowest-common denominator' solution. It works for standard dataclasses, but it's hardcoded and doesn't scale to more complex ORMs or frameworks like FastAPI. 

With PEP 827, instead of a special-cased decorator, a library author can actually define the __init__ signature using type-level iteration. For example, they can use 'typing.Attrs[T]' to get a tuple of every annotated attribute and then use an unpacked comprehension to turn those into parameters. It looks like this:

`*[typing.Param[p.name, p.type, Literal['keyword']] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]`

This creates a real 'Callable' type for the __init__ method that matches the class attributes exactly, and it can even decide if a parameter should have a default based on the 'init' field of the 'Member' object.
That syntax is exactly what I'm talking about. Iterating over types inside a bracket? It looks like a completely different language. How is a normal developer supposed to maintain that?

The PEP notes that the primary audience is framework and library maintainers rather than every developer. The idea is that a maintainer writes a helper like 'AddInit[T]' once. Then, the end user just writes:

`class Model(BaseModel): ...` 

Under the hood, 'BaseModel' would use '__init_subclass__' returning an 'UpdateClass' type. To the user, it feels like magic, but to the type checker, it's a precisely defined transformation. It moves the complexity away from the user and into the library code where it can be properly specified. It's a Draft for Python 3.15, so the syntax is still being debated, but the goal is to make these 'mechanical' transformations reliable.
And what happens at runtime? Does this slow down every import because Python is now 'evaluating' these loops in every annotation?

That is one of the bigger challenges. The PEP proposes that these operations are largely 'inert' until someone actually calls '__annotate__'. However, they've introduced a 'special_form_evaluator' which is a ContextVar hook. This allows runtime libraries—like a future version of Pydantic—to plug in an evaluator that can resolve these conditionals and loops to produce actual runtime objects for validation. If you aren't using a runtime type-checking library, the default behavior for the boolean operators is just to return False and for Iter to be an empty iterator, keeping the overhead minimal.

Hey! I've been digging into the latest typing proposals. I was looking at PEP 827 and I'm a bit confused. It is understandable to see the overlap! PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov is actually much broader than PEP 681. While PEP 681 provides a special-case decorator to tell type checkers that a function acts like a dataclass, PEP 827 introduces a general engine for type-level computation. It allows you to programmatically construct types using logic like `true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ` and operators like `IsAssignable` or `Members`.

As for PEP 764, PEP 827 does mention it in the context of alternate syntax for `NewProtocol`, but it's a separate proposal. PEP 827's core goal is to bridge the gap between dynamic Python metaprogramming and the static type system, targeting Python 3.15.

Okay, that makes sense. I was looking at the 'Extended Callables' section. It uses this `Param` type, like `Param[Literal["a"], int, Literal["positional"]]`. I noticed the qualifiers like "*" and "keyword" are just short strings. If we're already using literals, why not use an Enum? It feels a bit messy compared to something like `inspect.Parameter.KEYWORD_ONLY` or a proper `ParamKind` enum.

You've hit on a specific 'Open Issue' in the PEP! Currently, the authors define `ParamQuals` as `typing.Literal["*", "**", "default", "keyword"]`. The primary argument for using short strings is code brevity, especially when constructing complex types manually. 

However, the PEP explicitly asks if mirroring `inspect.Signature` more directly with an enum like `ParamKind.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD` would be better. Another potential change being weighed is fully separating the parameter 'kind' from whether it has a default, similar to how the `Member` type handles class initializers via an `init` field. Since it's still a Draft, the exact representation of those qualifiers is still being debated to find the right balance between readability and being spec-compliant.

Right, I see that in the Rationale now. I guess my confusion came from how it interacts with PEP 695 generics. If I use `GenericCallable`, am I basically making a type-level lambda? And if I use those string qualifiers in a loop like `*[typing.Param(p.name, p.type, "keyword") for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]`, does the type checker actually validate that those strings are valid?

A quick correction: in PEP 827, that unpacked comprehension would use square brackets, like `*[... for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]`. And yes, the type checker would validate those strings against the `ParamQuals` definition. 

Regarding `GenericCallable`, you can think of it as a way to delay evaluation. The PEP authors restrict its use to the type argument of `Member` for now to avoid 'cans of worms' like rank-N types or impredicative polymorphism. It uses a lambda syntax, like `GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty]`, specifically so that runtime evaluators can handle generic methods without losing one side of a conditional type immediately during evaluation. It's quite different from the standard PEP 695 `type` aliases because it's designed to be introspected and manipulated by operators like `UpdateClass` or `NewProtocol`.

Hey nanochat! I saw some hype on Twitter about PEP 827 'Type Manipulation' and it sounds like magic. Can you explain what it actually lets us do in plain English? Is it like making new classes on the fly?

Hi there! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, is a big proposal for Python 3.15. In plain English, it lets you 'calculate' types using logic directly in your code annotations. Imagine being able to use 'if' statements or 'for' loops to figure out what a type should be based on another type.

However, it isn't exactly making new classes on the fly in the traditional sense. It introduces ways to create 'structural' types, like NewProtocol or NewTypedDict. For example, you could take a class and 'calculate' a version of it where all the fields are optional. But the PEP specifically notes that it does NOT support building new nominal classes (the kind you define with the 'class' keyword) or creating brand new generic types right now.

Wait, if it's called 'Type Manipulation,' why can't I use it to build a new nominal class or a generic type? That seems like a missing piece if I'm trying to do real metaprogramming. Did the authors say why they left that out?

The authors are being very careful about what they call a 'can of worms.' In the PEP, they explain that things like 'impredicative polymorphism' (where you put generics inside other generics) and complex nested generic types are incredibly hard for type checkers to handle. In fact, they point out that some of this stuff is 'undecidable,' meaning the type checker might get stuck in an infinite loop trying to figure out if your code is valid.

By sticking to protocols and TypedDicts for now, they can provide the 'Utility Types' people want (like Pick, Omit, or Partial from TypeScript) without breaking the way Python's type system works under the hood. It's still a Draft, so they want to solve the most requested features from the 2025 Typed Python Survey first without opening those more dangerous cans of worms.

It is super exciting! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, is a proposal to bring powerful type-level logic to Python. To answer your question: the PEP actually states that they do not propose adding an official evaluator to the Python standard library. Instead, they intend to release a third-party evaluator library for users who need to process these types at runtime.

Exactly! You would use the third-party library to 'evaluate' or resolve the logic. In plain English, these new types can have logic like 'if this then that' or 'for every item in this list.' An evaluator looks at those rules and calculates the final result. For example, if you use a conditional type like 'int if IsAssignable[T, str] else list', the evaluator is the engine that checks if T is a string and then tells Python the answer is 'int' or 'list'. While it is not in the standard library, the PEP adds a hook called 'special_form_evaluator' so that third-party tools can plug into Python's internals and make it all work seamlessly!

You don't have to wait completely! While it is a Draft for Python 3.15, the PEP mentions there is already a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy and a demo of a runtime evaluator available. This lets people test out the new operators like 'Members[T]' or 'NewProtocol' to see how they can automate things like dataclass-style methods or database models before it becomes a full standard.

I'm currently the tech lead for our core frameworks team, and we're evaluating the feasibility of adopting PEP 827 when it hits Python 3.15. One of the more interesting additions is this idea of "Type member access." Could you explain what that actually looks like in practice? Does it allow us to use dot notation directly on types?

Exactly. PEP 827 introduces type member access, which allows you to use dot notation on specific introspection types like Member and Param. Usually, in Python's typing system, you cannot reach into a type to grab its attributes like a regular object. But under this proposal from authors like Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, when you use operators like Members[T] or Attrs[T], you get back a sequence of Member objects. You can then access properties like m.name or m.type within type-level comprehensions or conditionals to build new types dynamically.

That sounds powerful for reducing boilerplate. For our validation logic, we often need to know more than just the name and type. What specific attributes are exposed on a Member? For instance, does it track if something is a ClassVar or if it has a default value?

It does. A Member type provides several associated type members that you can access via dot notation. These include .name (the name as a literal string), .type (the type itself), .quals (a union of MemberQuals like 'ClassVar', 'Final', 'NotRequired', or 'ReadOnly'), and .init (the literal type of the attribute initializer, often an InitField). There is also a .definer attribute which indicates the class in the MRO where the member was actually defined. These properties are what allow frameworks to implement logic like the Create or Update models in the FastAPI-style examples from the PEP.

Okay, let's talk about the constraints. If I am iterating over a union of types, or if a member itself is part of a union, how does the dot notation behave? Is this one of the features that "lifts" over unions automatically, or are there restrictions?

That is a key technical detail. Unlike many other operators in PEP 827, type member access is NOT lifted over union types. The specification states that using it on the wrong sort of type—such as a Union of multiple members—is an error. This is intentional to ensure that static type checking and runtime evaluation remain synchronized. If you need to handle a union, you would typically use the FromUnion[T] operator to get a tuple of the union's elements and then iterate over them using an unpacked comprehension like *[m.name for m in Iter[FromUnion[T]]].

HIII nanochat! I saw some hype about PEP 827 on Twitter. It sounds like we can just use regular Python if-statements inside my type hints now? Like if x > 10: int else str? That would be so cool for my math scripts!

Hello! It is exciting to see more logic coming to the Python type system, but PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, works a bit differently than you might expect. It does not actually let you use runtime variables like 'x' inside your types.

Instead, it introduces conditional types that look like this: `TrueType if BooleanTypeOperator else FalseType`. The 'if' part has to be a special 'type-level' boolean, not a standard Python calculation. It is for checking things about types themselves, like 'is this type a string?' rather than 'is this number greater than 10?'

Oh, I think I get it. So it is like a logic puzzle for the type checker. But wait, why can I only use certain operators in that 'if' part? I read that they restricted it to things like IsAssignable. Why not just let me use any boolean expression I want if it is already in Python?

That is a great question! The authors decided to restrict the operators to a specific set (like `IsAssignable`, `IsEquivalent`, and `Bool`) for a very practical reason involving how Python works under the hood.

Normally, in Python, `True` and `False` are just simple values. If the PEP let you use any regular boolean expression, the runtime would evaluate it immediately, and we would lose the 'type-level' information. By using specific operators like `IsAssignable[T, S]`, the PEP creates special 'type' values. These special values allow libraries (like Pydantic or FastAPI) to use a hook called `special_form_evaluator` to look at the logic later at runtime.

If they didn't restrict it, they would have to change the behavior of every `Literal[False]` in Python, which would cause huge compatibility problems. This way, the type logic stays contained and manageable for both the static type checker and your running code.

So it's basically to keep the 'type logic' from getting mixed up with 'normal logic' so nothing breaks? That makes sense. What kind of things can I actually check then, if I'm stuck with those specific operators?

Exactly! You use those primitives to build complex rules. For example, `IsAssignable[T, str]` returns a type-level boolean telling you if type `T` can be treated as a string. 

You can also combine them using standard logic keywords like `and`, `or`, and `not`, or even `all()` and `any()` for list-like type structures. So you could write something like: `int if IsAssignable[T, int] or IsAssignable[T, float] else str`. It lets you transform types based on what they are, which is super helpful for library authors who want to automate how data is converted or validated!

The proposal aims to bridge the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system so you do not need custom plugins for every library. It introduces tools to inspect classes with "Members", create new types with "NewProtocol", and perform type-level logic. You are correct that "infer" was not adopted. Instead, PEP 827 provides the "GetArg" operator. To extract that inner type, you would write "typing.GetArg[T, Wrapper, Literal[0]]". The PEP authors actually discussed the "infer" keyword in the "Rejected Ideas" section. They acknowledged it is an elegant mechanism, but concluded it would be "very difficult to shoehorn into Python's existing syntax" because of how it binds variables. By using "GetArg", the system avoids the complexity of introducing a new binding syntax like TypeScript's "infer" while still letting you reach into generics.
so it is just "GetArg"? what if the user passes something that is not a "Wrapper"? does it crash or just return something else?

If the type "T" cannot be interpreted as the "Base" (in this case, "Wrapper"), "GetArg" will return "Never". You can use this inside a conditional type to make your logic robust. For example: "type Extract[T] = typing.GetArg[T, Wrapper, Literal[0]] if typing.IsAssignable[T, Wrapper] else Never". This uses the conditional syntax "A if B else C" introduced in the PEP. One major reason the authors preferred this over pattern matching was runtime support; tools like Pydantic or FastAPI need to evaluate these annotations at runtime, and "infer" would have required "gnarly" workarounds to handle unbound variables in a Python environment.

If you have a "Member" from a class, you can check its "init" field. For example, the PEP shows a "Create" helper that filters out primary keys by checking "if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]]]". This allows your type-level code to branch based on the actual values passed to field descriptors like "Field(primary_key=True)".

UpdateClass is related, but it has a very specific scope. You can't use it to conjure a brand new nominal class out of thin air. The draft is currently targeting Python 3.15 and focuses on bridging the gap for patterns like Prisma-style ORMs or FastAPI models where structural typing (via NewProtocol) is usually sufficient to describe the shape of the data being returned or validated.

Hi! I've been following PEP 827 because I'm a heavy user of dataclasses and attrs, and I'm really interested in how it might replace the need for custom mypy plugins when generating __init__ methods. I noticed a section on 'type booleans'—how do these actually differ from a regular type like a Union or a Literal?

Hello! PEP 827 makes a specific distinction between regular types and 'type booleans' to enable conditional logic during type construction. While a regular type describes a value, a type boolean is a restricted subset of the type language used specifically in the 'if' clause of a conditional type or the 'if' clause of an unpacked comprehension. Syntactically, a type-bool consists of specific operators like IsAssignable[T, S], IsEquivalent[T, S], or Bool[T], which can be combined using 'and', 'or', 'not', 'all', and 'any'. When evaluated in an annotation context, they resolve to Literal[True] or Literal[False], but the grammar limits them so that type checkers can reliably compute them at static-time.

That helps. So if I'm trying to filter out ClassVar attributes when generating my __init__ signature, I'd be using an 'if' clause inside a comprehension. Can I just use standard Python boolean logic there? For example, if I'm iterating over Attrs[T], how does the type system know it's a boolean check rather than just another type?

Exactly. In an unpacked comprehension like *[f(p) for p in Iter[Attrs[T]] if <type-bool>], the grammar requires the expression after 'if' to be a valid type-bool. To filter out ClassVar, you would use the IsAssignable operator. For instance, you might check 'if not IsAssignable[p.quals, Literal["ClassVar"]]'. The distinction is important because the type system isn't just looking for any object; it's looking for these specific boolean compositions. This allows the evaluator to trigger the 'special_form_evaluator' hook at runtime or perform the subtype check statically to determine whether to include that specific Member in your NewProtocol or Callable.

I see the vision. Looking at the InitFnType example in the PEP, they use 'p.name' and 'p.type' inside the Param definition. Since I'm used to attrs where we often transform field names or types, how does PEP 827 handle accessing those specific bits of a Member? Is 'p.name' considered a type boolean too, or is that a different mechanism?

Accessing 'p.name' or 'p.type' is actually 'type member access,' which is a separate syntactic feature from type booleans. While type booleans drive the logic of 'if' branches, member access allows you to deconstruct a Member or Param object. In your case, 'p.name' evaluates to a Literal[str] representing the attribute name. This is crucial for your __init__ generation: you can pass 'p.name' into the first argument of a Param[...] to define the keyword name of the constructor argument. This combination—using type booleans to filter fields and member access to project their names and types—is exactly how PEP 827 lets you implement dataclass-style method generation without a single line of plugin code.

Hey nanochat! I've been looking over the draft for PEP 827. I'm a tech lead for a team that does a lot of heavy metaprogramming, and I'm trying to figure out if this is something we should plan for in 3.15. Specifically, could you explain the new Param type? What exactly are those type arguments encoding?

Hello! It's great to meet a tech lead looking ahead to Python 3.15. PEP 827 is quite ambitious, especially in how it handles function introspection. The Param type is a core building block for what the authors (Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov) call "Extended Callables."

In the PEP, a Param is defined like this:

class Param[N: str | None, T, Q: ParamQuals = typing.Never]:
    pass

It encodes three specific pieces of information about a function parameter:
1. N: The name of the parameter, typically as a Literal string or None.
2. T: The actual type of the parameter.
3. Q: The qualifiers, which describe how the parameter is used (like if it is keyword-only or a variadic *args).
Got it. That looks a lot more structured than just getting a list of types. What are the valid values for that third argument, the qualifiers? You mentioned ParamQuals.

Exactly. The Q argument uses a type called ParamQuals, which is a Literal union. According to the PEP, the valid strings are:

- "*" for variadic positional arguments (*args)
- "**" for variadic keyword arguments (**kwargs)
- "default" if the parameter has a default value
- "keyword" for keyword-only parameters
- "positional" for positional-only parameters

The PEP also provides some handy type abbreviations to make this less verbose. For example, a PosParam[N, T] is just a Param where Q is Literal["positional"], and an ArgsParam[T] is a Param where N is None and Q is Literal["*"].

That makes sense. So if I were building a tool to transform a function signature—say, adding a 'self' parameter or making everything keyword-only—I'd be manipulating a sequence of these Param objects inside that new Params container?

You hit the nail on the head. You would use the Params[*Ps] type to wrap a sequence of Param objects. This Params then becomes the first argument to a Callable. 

For a tech lead, the big win here is that because these are structured types, you can use the new unpacked comprehensions to iterate over them. You could write a type alias that takes an existing function's parameters, filters some out using an 'if' clause, and returns a new Callable. It moves logic that used to require a custom mypy plugin directly into the type system using standard Python-like syntax.

hello nanochat :) I'm looking at PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) to see if I can use it for some dynamic model generation in my validation framework. Specifically, I'm trying to figure out how it handles the boolean logic for conditional types. Does it just treat any type that returns Literal[True] as a boolean?

Hello! That is a great question. In PEP 827, 'type booleans' are actually a restricted subset of the type language. While they do evaluate to Literal[True] or Literal[False], the PEP is quite specific about what qualifies as a type boolean to ensure that static checkers and runtime evaluators can handle them without modifying the core Python grammar.

According to the specification, a type boolean must be composed of specific boolean operators like IsAssignable[T, S], IsEquivalent[T, S], or Bool[T]. You can then combine these using standard logical operators: 'and', 'or', 'not', 'all', and 'any'.

You cannot just use any arbitrary type that might resolve to a Literal[True]. The PEP authors (Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov) restricted this so that these operators can produce specialized 'type' values at runtime with custom dunder methods for evaluation hooks.
Wait, so I can't just define my own type alias that results in Literal[True] and use it in an 'if' clause within a type? Like `type MyCheck = Literal[True]` and then `int if MyCheck else str`?

Actually, you can use a helper alias, but you'd need to wrap it in the Bool[] operator to make it a valid 'type boolean' for the conditional syntax. The PEP specifies that the condition in `true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ` must syntactically be a type boolean.

For your example, it would look like this:

```python
type MyCheck = Literal[True]
type Result = int if typing.Bool[MyCheck] else str
```

The Bool[T] operator returns Literal[True] if T is Literal[True] (or a union containing it), satisfying the requirement for the conditional body.

That seems like a bit of a hurdle. Why the restriction? Also, if I'm building a framework, can I use these booleans in regular Python code? Like if I have a Member object from Members[T], can I just write `if m.quals == 'ReadOnly': ...` in my runtime logic?

There are some significant limitations there. First, on the syntax restriction: the authors want to avoid changing Python's grammar. By defining a specific 'type boolean' subset, they can allow these expressions in annotations without requiring the Python interpreter itself to understand type-level logic during initial parsing.

Regarding your second point about runtime logic: PEP 827 types are mostly 'inert' by default. If you try to use a type boolean in a standard Python `if` statement at runtime, it won't work automatically. The PEP says that by default, boolean operators return False and Iter returns an empty iterator.

To make it work, you have to use the `special_form_evaluator`. This is a ContextVar provided by the typing module. You have to set it to a callable that knows how to evaluate these typing._GenericAlias objects. Without an active evaluator library (which the PEP authors plan to release as a third-party tool), the runtime Python objects won't 'know' their own boolean truthiness.

So the PEP doesn't even include a default evaluator in the standard library? That feels like a major missing piece for a framework maintainer. If I want my users to have a seamless experience, I basically have to ship an entire type-system evaluator with my ORM?

You are correct. PEP 827 does not propose adding an official evaluator to the Python standard library. The PEP explicitly states: 'We do not propose to add an official evaluator to the standard library, but intend to release a third-party evaluator library.'

This is a known boundary of the proposal. It provides the hooks (like special_form_evaluator) and the grammar for static checkers to understand the logic, but the heavy lifting of runtime execution is left to the ecosystem. For a framework maintainer, this means you would likely need to depend on that third-party evaluator or implement a subset of it yourself to handle things like resolving `IsAssignable` or `Members[T]` when your users define their models.

Hey, I have been maintaining a custom mypy plugin for my ORM to handle some complex method generation, and PEP 827 looks like exactly what I need to ditch the plugin. I was looking at the GenericCallable spec, though. Why is its use restricted specifically to the type argument of Member? I would love to use it for locals or nested return types, but the draft says that is a 'can of worms'—could you elaborate on the undecidability aspect mentioned there?

As a framework author, you are likely looking for ways to model methods that are generic independently of the class they reside in. PEP 827 introduces GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty] to represent these signatures, but the authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) intentionally restricted it to Member arguments. This was done to avoid opening the 'can of worms' associated with rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism. Rank-N types allow generics to be bound in nested positions deep within function types, while impredicative polymorphism allows instantiating type variables with other generic types. Combining these features with type inference often leads to undecidability, as noted by researchers Hans Boehm and Frank Pfenning in the PEP's footnotes. By limiting GenericCallable to the context of a class Member, the proposal sidesteps these inference complexities for local variables and parameter types. Furthermore, the use of a lambda in the syntax is a practical necessity for runtime evaluation. It delays the evaluation of the function body—which might include conditional types or IsAssignable checks—so that the framework can introspect the full structure when calling Members[T] without losing information to premature simplification.

hey nanochat, i'm digging into pep 827 for some ide tooling work. the type-level manipulation stuff is wild. specifically, how do the Length and Slice operators work on tuple types? i'm trying to figure out if this is going to break my completion engine.

Hey! Glad to help. For an IDE engineer, PEP 827 definitely adds some heavy lifting. The Length and Slice operators are basically the compile-time equivalent of what we do with lists and tuples at runtime. Length[T] takes a tuple type and returns its length as a Literal[int]. If the tuple is unbounded, like tuple[int, ...], it returns Literal[None]. Slice[S, Start, End] works exactly like Python's slicing. It takes a tuple type S and returns a new sliced tuple type based on the Literal start and end indices you provide.

so if i have something like tuple[int, str, float, bool] and i call Slice[T, Literal[1], Literal[3]], the IDE should expect the resulting type to be tuple[str, float]? what about completions? if i have a variable of that sliced type, do i just treat it as a standard tuple?

You got it exactly. The result of that Slice would be tuple[str, float]. For completions, you would treat it as a standard tuple of that specific length and those types. One thing to keep in mind is that these operators are lifted over unions. If your tuple is tuple[int] | tuple[str], Length would return Literal[1] | Literal[1] (which collapses to Literal[1]). If you're doing go-to-definition on the elements, you're just looking at the original types that were packed into the tuple.
gotcha. the lifting over unions sounds like it could get spicy for performance. does Slice support None for the indices like we do in [1:] or [:3]?

Yes, the PEP explicitly allows Literal[int | None] for both Start and End in Slice. So Slice[T, Literal[1], Literal[None]] is perfectly valid and would represent a slice from index 1 to the end. It follows the standard Python slicing semantics. Since this is still a Draft for Python 3.15, the goal is to keep it as intuitive for Python devs as possible, even if it makes our tools work a bit harder!

Regarding PEP 827, I am looking at the specification for InitField[KwargDict]. Could you explain the exact mechanism by which it captures field metadata from class definitions? I am particularly interested in how it avoids the typical limitations of PEP 681's dataclass_transform.

Under PEP 827, InitField is designed to capture the arguments passed to field descriptors like Field() directly into the type system. It works by leveraging a TypedDict bound to a type variable. When you define an attribute like 'foo: int = InitField(default=0)', the type checker is instructed to infer a specific TypedDict for that instance, using Literal types for the arguments whenever possible. This metadata is then stored in the 'Init' field of the Member type produced by the Members[T] or Attrs[T] operators. Unlike PEP 681, which relies on type checkers having hardcoded logic to recognize specific decorator patterns, InitField provides a generic way to pass descriptor metadata into type-level functions like NewProtocol.

That sounds powerful, but I am skeptical about the inference limits. If I have a complex expression or a non-literal default value in my Field call, can InitField still capture it? For example, if I use a constant defined elsewhere, does the 'Init' field of the Member just become 'Never' or does it lose the specific value?

You've identified a significant constraint. PEP 827 leans heavily on Literal types for its computation. The specification for InitField states that the type checker should infer literal types for arguments that do not appear in the bound. However, if an argument is a complex expression that cannot be resolved to a Literal at type-check time, the system will struggle. The PEP is silent on full symbolic execution; it generally expects values to be Literals. If a value cannot be inferred as a Literal, it may fall back to its base type (like 'int' instead of 'Literal[0]'), which might break conditional logic that checks for specific metadata flags using IsAssignable or IsEquivalent.

What about runtime evaluation? If I am building a framework like FastAPI and I want to use these computed types to drive Pydantic validation, how does the library actually extract those 'Init' values without a full mypy-like engine at runtime?

The PEP authors admit that runtime evaluation is a challenge. To solve this, the proposal includes a 'special_form_evaluator' hook, which is a ContextVar in the typing module. This hook allows third-party libraries to register a callable that intercepts boolean operations or Iter calls during the evaluation of the __annotate__ method. However, PEP 827 does NOT propose adding an official, comprehensive evaluator to the Python standard library. Frameworks would need to rely on an external library (which the authors plan to release) to perform the heavy lifting. If that hook isn't set, boolean operators simply return False and Iter returns an empty iterator at runtime.

I see. So it doesn't actually solve the 'introspectability' problem in the core; it just provides a hook for others to solve it. One last thing: can InitField be used to modify the type of the attribute itself, or is it strictly for metadata collection used by operators like NewProtocol?

InitField is strictly for metadata collection and initialization. It does not natively 'wrap' or transform the attribute type in the class where it is declared. To actually transform the types—for instance, to make an attribute optional in a 'Create' model—you must use a type alias that iterates over the Members of the class and constructs a NewProtocol. The only way to modify the original nominal class itself is through the UpdateClass operator, which is restricted to the return types of class decorators or __init_subclass__. So, InitField is the 'input' for the computation, while operators like NewProtocol or UpdateClass are the 'output'.
hiii. i am a typescript dev trying to make sense of pep 827. i use utility types like Pick and Omit constantly. does python have a direct equivalent or am i stuck with basic type hints?

Welcome! PEP 827 actually draws heavy inspiration from TypeScript specifically to support these patterns. While Python does not have built-in keywords like Pick or Omit yet, the PEP provides the primitives to build them yourself. For example, Pick[T, Keys] is implemented using an unpacked comprehension: `typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]`. It is more verbose than TS because Python uses explicit iteration over class members rather than implicit mapped types.
that syntax is definitely wordier. in ts, i rely on keyof T to constrain keys. how do i get a union of keys in this proposal? is there a keyof operator?

There is no single keyof operator, but you can construct it using the same iteration logic. According to the specification by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the other authors, you would define it as `type KeyOf[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]`. This uses the `Members[T]` operator to fetch a tuple of Member objects and then extracts the `.name` attribute from each during the comprehension.

okay i see. what about Partial? in ts it just makes everything optional. python is tricky because we have regular classes but also TypedDict. how does it handle the difference between making a field None vs making it NotRequired?

PEP 827 handles that by providing two different implementations depending on the target. For general structural protocols, `Partial[T]` uses the pipe operator: `typing.NewProtocol[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]]`. However, for TypedDicts, there is a `PartialTD[T]` helper that uses the `NewTypedDict` operator and modifies the qualifiers. It looks like this: `typing.NewTypedDict[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals | Literal['NotRequired']] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]]`. This ensures the fields are marked as NotRequired rather than just allowing None.
got it. and Exclude/Extract? in ts those work on unions. does python's IsAssignable work the same way inside a union loop?

Exactly. To handle unions, the PEP introduces `FromUnion[T]` to deconstruct them. `Exclude[T, U]` is implemented as `Union[*[x for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]]]`. The `IsAssignable[T, S]` operator acts as the core engine for the check, behaving like TypeScript's `extends` in conditional types. It is currently a Draft PEP for Python 3.15, so it is not live yet, but it aims to bridge that gap between Python's dynamic nature and its static type system.

Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—actually talk about exactly why plugins aren't a perfect fix. The biggest issue is portability. If you write a mypy plugin, it only works in mypy. It can be! Instead of manually defining HeroUpdate where every field is optional, you could define a single helper alias. Using the syntax from the PEP, it might look like this:

type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

Then you just write 'type HeroUpdate = Partial[Hero]'. This uses an 'unpacked comprehension' to iterate over the attributes of your original Hero class. The 'p.name' and 'p.type' parts use the new dot-notation for Member and Param types.

The PEP specifically addresses this because things like FastAPI and Pydantic are so popular. It introduces 'runtime evaluation support.' While it doesn't put a full evaluator in the stdlib yet, it adds a 'special_form_evaluator' hook using a ContextVar. This allows frameworks to trigger the evaluation of those 'if/else' and 'for' loops in the annotations. So when FastAPI asks what 'HeroUpdate' is, Pydantic can use a third-party library to compute the actual structure at runtime. It's not just a static-only trick.

Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov introduces some clever logic for this. When you use Unpack on a type variable K bounded by a TypedDict, the type checker tries to be very precise. If the non-required item is not read-only, the PEP says this 'Never' inference cannot be done because non-read-only items are invariant. If you are dealing with an unbounded tuple (like tuple[int, ...]), it returns Literal[None] instead of an integer. sup nanochat. im an ide dev looking at pep 827. specifically trying to wrap my head around this new Param type for the extended callables proposal. what exactly are the three type arguments encoding and how does it look for something like a variadic or keyword-only arg?

In PEP 827, the Param type is the building block for describing function signatures in a way that type-level operators can manipulate. The signature is Param[N, T, Q]. N is the parameter name, which is a Literal string or None. T is the type of the parameter. Q is a union of qualifiers from ParamQuals, which include "*", "**", "default", and "keyword". For a variadic *args, you would use ArgsParam[T], which is shorthand for Param[None, T, Literal["*"]]. For a keyword-only argument, you would include "keyword" in the qualifiers, like NamedParam[Literal["my_arg"], int]. The PEP also suggests abbreviations like PosParam and NamedDefaultParam to make this more readable when you are constructing these inside a Params sequence.

ok so for jump-to-definition or completions, if i have a computed signature using this stuff, can i actually access those fields? like if a library uses a type-level loop to generate an __init__ via NewProtocol, how do i get the name out to show in an autocomplete list?

That is where the type member access feature comes in. PEP 827 allows dot notation directly on types like Member and Param. If you have a parameter p, you can access p.name to get the literal string of the argument name and p.type to get its type. As a tooling engineer, if you see a class using UpdateClass or NewProtocol, you would evaluate those operators to see the resulting Members. Since these members contain Param-based callables, you can iterate through the Params and use the .name attribute to populate your completion list. It effectively replaces the need for custom IDE plugins for things like dataclasses or Pydantic, because the signature logic is moved into the type system itself.

Hey nanochat! I've been maintaining a custom mypy plugin for my framework's ORM layer for years, and it's a nightmare because it doesn't work in VS Code or PyCharm. I saw PEP 827 mentioned as a way to do this logic directly in the type system, inspired by TypeScript. Can you explain the connection to TypeScript's model and what specific 'utility types' this PEP is trying to bring over?

Hello! You've hit on the core motivation behind PEP 827. The authors—Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—explicitly designed this to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system, drawing heavily from TypeScript's conditional and mapped types.

The proposal was actually spurred by the 2025 Typed Python Survey from Meta, where the most requested features were things like Intersection types, Pick, Omit, and keyof. PEP 827 introduces several primitives that mirror these TypeScript utilities using a new Pythonic syntax for type-level computation:

1. Conditional Types: You can now write `true_type if bool_type else false_type`. The `bool_type` uses operators like `IsAssignable[T, S]` to check compatibility at the type level.
2. Mapped Types: Instead of TypeScript's syntax, Python uses 'Unpacked Comprehensions' like `*[f(p) for p in Iter[Members[T]]]`. This allows you to iterate over class members or TypedDict fields to transform them.
3. Utility Types: The PEP demonstrates how to implement TypeScript-style helpers using these primitives. For example, `Pick[T, Keys]` is implemented as a `NewProtocol` that iterates over `Members[T]` and filters for names assignable to `Keys`.

By providing these as first-class type operators in the typing module, the goal is to eliminate the need for those brittle mypy plugins (or the limited `@dataclass_transform`) so that complex transformations—like your ORM's select queries—work consistently across all IDEs and type checkers.

I have been looking at PEP 827 and it feels like we are just trying to turn Python into TypeScript. Why do we need this level of complexity? IsAssignable, conditional types, type-level loops... it seems like a lot of bloat just to satisfy IDE autocomplete.

The authors of PEP 827, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, argue that there is currently a significant mismatch between Python's expressive metaprogramming and its type system. While Python code often performs complex transformations (like in dataclasses or ORMs), the static type system is fairly conventional and cannot model them. This often forces library authors to write custom mypy plugins. PEP 827 aims to provide generic type manipulation primitives so that these patterns can be handled directly in the type system without special-cased plugins.

But adding actual logic like 'if/else' and 'for' loops inside type annotations feels like a maintenance nightmare. If I have to write 'type T = A if IsAssignable[X, Y] else B', I am basically writing a second program inside my types. Is this really better than just using runtime checks?

The challenge is that many modern frameworks, like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs, already use runtime introspection for validation and serialization. For instance, FastAPI uses Pydantic models to strip extra fields for a 'Public' view or make fields optional for an 'Update' view. Currently, you have to manually define HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate classes, which causes duplication. PEP 827 allows you to define a single 'NewSQLModel' and then use operators like 'NewProtocol' and 'Iter[Attrs[T]]' to mechanically derive those variations. This ensures that the runtime behavior and the static type-checking behavior actually match.

Even so, some of the mechanics look incredibly dense. This concept of 'Lifting over Unions' sounds like it would make type resolution take forever. If I pass a Union of five types into one of these operators, does it just explode into a massive cross product?

That is essentially how it works. When an operation is 'lifted' over Union types, the system takes the cross product of the union elements for each argument position. It evaluates the operator for every tuple in that cross product and then applies 'Union[*results]' to the final set. For example, 'Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['c'] | Literal['d']]' would result in 'Literal['ac'] | Literal['ad'] | Literal['bc'] | Literal['bd']'. While it adds work for the type checker, it is necessary to maintain consistency when dealing with the 'gradual' nature of Python's type system.

The PEP actually includes a specific operator called RaiseError[S, *Ts] for exactly that purpose. This uses a conditional type: if the two types are equivalent or if one of them is Literal[1], it returns the appropriate dimension. This allows the static type checker to provide a custom error message that identifies the specific conflicting types. This logic is part of a larger Broadcast[T, S] recursive alias that uses typing.Bool[Empty[T]], typing.Slice, and typing.GetArg to walk through the shape tuples. Hey nanochat! I just spent the morning reading the draft for PEP 827 and I am literally vibrating with excitement. This is exactly what we need for our tensor libraries! I was looking at the section on Object Inspection and saw Members[T] and Attrs[T]. Could you break down the difference? I want to make sure I use the right one for validating ndarray shapes.

It is definitely an exciting proposal! Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov are really pushing the boundaries of Python's type system here. To your question: both operators produce a tuple of Member types, but they filter the results differently.

Members[T] is the comprehensive one. It produces a tuple describing both the attributes and the methods of a class or TypedDict. Attrs[T], on the other hand, is a specific subset—it only returns the attributes and excludes any methods.

One crucial thing to remember for your ML work: both operators only include members that have explicit type annotations. If a member isn't annotated, it won't show up in that tuple!

That makes sense! So if I have a Tensor class with a .shape attribute and a .mean() method, Attrs[Tensor] would skip .mean() entirely? That's perfect because I usually only care about the data properties when I'm doing shape transformations. Since they return a tuple of Member types, can I use the new comprehension syntax to grab just the names?

Spot on! You can absolutely use an unpacked comprehension to extract that data. For example, if you wanted a union of all attribute names, you could write something like:

type AttrNames[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]]

Because the Member type provides dot-notation access to its components, p.name gives you the Literal[str] of the attribute name. You can also access p.type, p.quals, p.init, and p.definer. It makes it incredibly easy to filter for specific properties, like only those annotated with a specific Shape type!

This is going to save us so much boilerplate. I saw that it's targeting Python 3.15—I can't wait to see this land. Being able to programmatically construct these types instead of writing custom mypy plugins for every tensor operation is a dream come true!

It really is a massive shift. The authors specifically noted that they want to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system without requiring specialized plugins for every library. For ML engineers dealing with complex ndarray shapes and broadcasting, being able to use operators like IsEquivalent or Slice inside a conditional type will be much more intuitive than the current workarounds. Just keep in mind that since it's still a Draft, the exact behavior of things like the MemberQuals or the runtime evaluation hooks might shift before 3.15!

Hey nanochat. I was digging through the draft for PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. I saw this new 'GenericCallable' form. It looks a bit like the old mypy_extensions stuff but with a lambda? Can you break down what it is actually doing?

The GenericCallable introduced in PEP 827 is designed to represent callables that have their own type variables, specifically for use when you are constructing types dynamically. The syntax is GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty], where 'Vs' is a tuple of unbound type variables and 'Ty' is the actual Callable or method type (like staticmethod or classmethod). The lambda is there to bind those variables within the body of the type definition. It allows the type system to represent a method like 'def process[T](self, x: T) -> T' within a NewProtocol or other type-level construction without losing the generic nature of the function during evaluation.

That lambda syntax is wild for a type annotation. If I am using NewProtocol to build a mock interface, why is the PEP restricting GenericCallable specifically to the type argument of a Member? As a senior dev, I frequently want rank-N types or generic functions as parameters to other functions. This feels like a major bottleneck if I can only use it inside a class member definition.

The authors acknowledge that rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism (where type variables are instantiated with other generic types) are 'cans of worms' regarding type inference and decidability. By restricting GenericCallable to Member arguments, they are specifically targeting the use case of generating method signatures for protocols or classes without opening up the complexity of allowing generics to be nested deep inside other types or passed as local variables. Dr. Sullivan and the team explicitly cite papers by Hans Boehm and Frank Pfenning on the undecidability of partial polymorphic type reconstruction as the reason for this caution.

So if I have a complex type alias that needs to return a generic factory function, I'm stuck? Is there a reason why the lambda is strictly necessary for runtime evaluation compared to how we currently handle generics with PEP 695?
