Oh, that is sweet. For an ORM, being able to capture the exact keys someone passes into a 'select' or 'filter' method as a TypedDict variable 'K' would be a game changer. Does it handle literal types well? Usually, I need to know the specific string key names to do anything useful with the types.

Yes, that's a key part of the proposal. PEP 827 specifies that when inferring types for these unpacked keyword arguments, the type checker should infer literal types whenever possible. This applies to arguments that aren't in the bound and read-only items in the bound.

Combined with the new 'Members' and 'Attrs' operators, you can then take that inferred TypedDict 'K' and iterate over its keys using the new unpacked comprehension syntax:

*[some_transform[t] for t in Iter[Attrs[K]]]

This is where the PEP 646 influence really shines—using that variadic 'Unpack' logic to let you loop through types like they're a sequence.

Wait, so I can literally loop over the attributes of a type variable inferred from a call site? That's the missing link. I was trying to do this with mypy plugins before and it was a nightmare. So if I have a variadic shape from PEP 646, can I use these new operators on those tuples too?

Absolutely. PEP 827 introduces 'Slice', 'Length', and 'GetArg' specifically for tuple types (including those generated by TypeVarTuples). 

For example, the PEP shows how you can implement NumPy-style broadcasting by recursively walking down two shape tuples using 'DropLast' and 'Last' (which are just aliases for Slice and GetArg). It uses a conditional type to check if the tuple is empty using the 'Length' operator. It's a very powerful way to handle the variadic generics you're used to from PEP 646 but with actual logic instead of just passing them around.
yo, type manipulation. I'm looking at the PEP 827 draft by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov. What is GetSpecialAttr actually for?

In PEP 827, GetSpecialAttr[T, Attr] is a type operator used to extract metadata from a class or a type and represent it as a Literal string at the type level. It specifically targets attributes like __name__, __module__, and __qualname__. If you have a class MyClass, GetSpecialAttr[MyClass, Literal['__name__']] would evaluate to Literal['MyClass'].

Okay, that's straightforward for classes. But as a reviewer, I'm looking at the edge cases. Does this work on instance-like types or only nominal class objects? The PEP mentions something about non-class types.

The PEP specifies that for non-class types, the operator follows the principle of evaluating what type(x).<attr> would be if x was an instance of that type. For example, if you apply it to a literal type like GetSpecialAttr[Literal[1], '__name__'], the resulting type is Literal['int']. This ensures that even when you aren't dealing with a raw class reference, you can still introspect the underlying type's identity for things like code generation or error messaging.
Interesting. What about the behavior under union types? If I'm passing a union of several classes to a type alias that uses GetSpecialAttr, does it fail or does it distribute?

It is 'lifted' over union types. Following the logic in the 'Lifting over Unions' section of the specification, the operator takes the cross product of union elements. So, GetSpecialAttr[int | str, '__name__'] would evaluate to Literal['int'] | Literal['str']. This allows you to perform type-level string manipulations or checks across multiple possible types in a single operation.
And what happens if I pass an invalid attribute name? Or if the type provided doesn't have the attribute requested? Does it raise a static error or return something else?

The PEP states that trying to evaluate operators with invalid arguments will produce Never as the return type. This applies if you provide an attribute name outside the valid set (which is currently restricted to __name__, __module__, and __qualname__) or if the type provided cannot be introspected in that way. It's essentially a 'best effort' approach where failure results in the bottom type rather than necessarily halting the type checker.

yo nanochat, pep 827 time! I am currently deep in the weeds of building a new typed ORM, and the typing is absolute hell. I heard this PEP brings TypeScript-style magic to Python. Is it true? Can I finally stop writing mypy plugins just to handle table column transformations?

It is absolutely true! PEP 827, titled "Type Manipulation," is specifically designed to bridge the gap between Python's heavy metaprogramming and the static type system. Authors Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov are proposing a suite of type-level operators that allow you to construct and deconstruct types without custom plugins. For a framework maintainer, this is a game-changer because it moves the logic out of a specialized tool-chain and into the code itself.

That is what I like to hear! My biggest headache is taking a database model and generating different versions of it—like a 'Public' view that strips hidden fields, or an 'Update' view where every field is optional. Does 827 have stuff like 'Pick' or 'Omit' to help with that?

Precisely. The PEP actually provides implementations for TypeScript-style 'Utility Types' like Pick, Omit, and Partial. It uses a new operator called Members[T] to fetch the attributes of a class and NewProtocol to build a new structural type from them. For example, a Partial utility would look like this: type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]]. It essentially allows you to write a comprehension directly inside a type definition.

Wait, I can actually loop inside a type? The *[... for p in Iter[...]] syntax is wild! And you mentioned conditionals? I do a lot of 'if this column is a ForeignKey, then the type is User, but if it is a list of links, it's list[User]' logic. Can I model that?

You certainly can. PEP 827 introduces conditional types with the syntax 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ'. You would use the IsAssignable[T, S] operator to check if a type matches your criteria. For your ForeignKey logic, you might use GetArg[T, Base, Idx] to reach inside a generic and extract the related model. The PEP actually uses a Prisma-style ORM example where they define a ConvertField helper that checks if a type is a 'Link' using IsAssignable and then recursively computes the target type. It even supports lifting these operations over Unions automatically!
I am so ready for this. If I can get this to work at runtime too, Pydantic-style validation would be a breeze. When is this landing?

The proposal is currently in 'Draft' status and is targeting Python 3.15. A major part of the design is ensuring it works for runtime evaluation, as frameworks like FastAPI and Pydantic need to inspect these types to handle data validation. There is already a proof-of-concept in mypy and a demo of a runtime evaluator, but for now, we have to wait for the PEP to progress through the standards track!

I was reading through PEP 827 and honestly, it looks like a ton of TypeScript creep. Is Python really going this far? I mostly use dataclasses and attrs, so I'm wondering if this complexity actually solves anything for someone like me who just wants better typing for generated __init__ methods without needing a plugin.

It is a significant shift, but the authors—Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—explicitly state that this is meant to bridge the gap between Python's metaprogramming and the type system. Currently, libraries like dataclasses often require special-case support or PEP 681 (dataclass_transform). PEP 827 aims to make those transformations first-class features. For a dataclass user, this means you can actually describe how your '__init__' is constructed using type-level operators like 'Members' or 'Attrs'. It uses a new 'UpdateClass' form that can modify a class's members based on its attributes, which provides consistent IDE support without custom plugins.

Okay, that makes sense. If I wanted to implement a decorator that generates an __init__ for my classes, how does 'UpdateClass' actually work with the attributes? I saw 'InitFnType' mentioned in the PEP text but wasn't sure how it pulls the names and types together.

To generate that signature, you would define a type alias like 'InitFnType[T]' that uses 'typing.Attrs[T]' to inspect the class. The implementation uses an unpacked comprehension: 'typing.Member[Literal["__init__"], Callable[[typing.Param[Literal["self"], Self], *[typing.Param[p.name, p.type] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]], None]]'. This loop iterates over every annotated attribute 'p' and creates a 'Param' for it using 'p.name' and 'p.type'. You then use 'UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]' as the return type for your decorator. This effectively tells the type checker to inject that generated '__init__' method into the class's type definition, including handling things like making parameters keyword-only or optional based on the attribute's metadata.

hello nanochat :) I have been reading about PEP 827. I use dataclasses and attrs constantly, and the boilerplate of manually typing out __init__ signatures is exhausting. I saw that this PEP lets you access attributes of a Member or Param using dot notation. How does that actually work when I am trying to build a function signature?

Hello! In PEP 827, dot notation is a core syntactic feature for type-level introspection. When you use the Members[T] or Attrs[T] operators, you get a tuple of Member types. Each Member object has specific associated types you can access via a dot, such as .name, .type, .quals, and .init.

For your use case of generating an __init__ signature, you would iterate over these members and map them to Param objects. For example, if 'p' is a Member from Attrs[T], you can write Param[p.name, p.type, Literal['keyword']] to construct a keyword-only parameter for your constructor. This allows the type system to 'read' the class definition and programmatically build the Callable.

That sounds powerful. Does it work like normal Python where I can just dot into a union? For instance, if I have 'm: MemberA | MemberB', can I just do 'm.name' and get a union of their names? Also, can I use this dot notation on my own custom classes or just these special typing ones?

I have to be clear about a major limitation here: dot notation is NOT lifted over union types. If you try to use m.name on a union of Member types, it is a type error. The PEP specifies that using it on the wrong sort of type must be an error both at runtime and during typechecking to ensure consistency.

As for your own classes, no, this is restricted to the new Member and Param types introduced in PEP 827. You cannot use this syntax to access arbitrary class attributes or members of nominal types in your own code. It is strictly a primitive for the type-level machinery to inspect the metadata produced by operators like Attrs[T].

Oh, that is a bit of a bummer. So if my list of attributes contains something that is not a Member, my whole comprehension breaks if I try to access .name? And what happens if I try to access a member that doesn't exist, like p.something_else?

Correct. If you have a comprehension like [p.name for p in typing.Iter[SomeTuple]] and SomeTuple contains anything other than a Member or Param type, the evaluation will fail. You generally have to use filters, like 'if typing.IsAssignable[p, typing.Member]', to ensure you are only dotting into valid objects.

If you try to access a member name that isn't defined for that type (like p.default instead of p.init for a Member), it is also a hard error. The available members for Member are .name, .type, .quals, .init, and .definer. For Param, they are .name, .type, and .quals. 

Keep in mind this PEP is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so while the authors have a proof-of-concept in mypy, it isn't part of the standard library yet.

Hi nanochat! I saw some buzz on Twitter about PEP 827 and 'Type Manipulation.' I'm still pretty new to Python, but everyone is saying this fixes a 'gap' between metaprogramming and the type system. In plain English, what does that actually mean? Why can't the current type system handle the cool dynamic stuff Python does?

That is a great question. In Python, we love 'metaprogramming,' which is basically writing code that creates or changes other code while the program is running. For example, libraries like FastAPI or SQLAlchemy often look at your classes and automatically add new methods or validation rules based on what you wrote. 

The 'gap' is that while Python is very flexible at runtime, the current type system (the part that helps your IDE show autocomplete or find errors) is relatively rigid. It usually expects types to be defined upfront. If a library dynamically adds a 'save()' method to your class, the type checker doesn't know it exists unless the developers write a custom plugin for every single tool like VS Code or PyCharm. 

PEP 827, proposed by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, aims to bridge this gap by letting us 'calculate' types using logic directly in the type hints. Instead of fixed types, it introduces tools like:

1. Conditional Types: You can say 'if Type A is a string, then the result is an int, otherwise it's a list' using syntax like `int if IsAssignable[T, str] else list[T]`.
2. Object Inspection: You can use `Members[T]` to actually see what attributes or methods a class has at the type level.
3. Type Creation: You can build brand new structures on the fly with `NewProtocol` or `NewTypedDict` based on existing code.

Essentially, it brings the 'metaprogramming' power of Python into the type system itself. It's currently a Draft targeted for Python 3.15, so it's a look at the future of how Python might handle these complex patterns without needing extra plugins.

hallo. i'm a fastapi dev and i'm tired of writing the same fields for Hero, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate. i heard pep 827 by Dr. Michael Sullivan, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov might fix this. how does it use stuff from pep 646 and 692 to automate this?

Hello! PEP 827 aims to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. For your FastAPI models, it introduces powerful type-level construction facilities. It builds on the foundation of variadic generics from PEP 646 and the TypedDict-based kwargs from PEP 692. Specifically, it proposes a way to iterate over class members and create new structural types (like a NewProtocol) using computed logic. This allows you to define a single model and derive the others through mechanical rules, like making all fields optional for an 'Update' version or stripping primary keys for 'Create'.

Can you explain that **kwargs part more? I saw it mentioned as a prerequisite. If I have a function receiving data, how does the type system 'know' the TypedDict structure to apply these transformations?

The proposal includes a minor but critical prerequisite: supporting Unpack of typevars for **kwargs. If you define a function signature like 'def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K', the type checker will infer a TypedDict for K based on the actual arguments provided at the call site. This is a mix of PEP 692's precision for kwargs and PEP 646's variadic logic. By inferring K as a TypedDict, often with Literal types for the keys, you can then pass that type K into PEP 827 operators like Attrs[K] or Members[K] to perform type-level computation on the inputs.
And the loop syntax? It looks like *[... for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]. Is that a direct extension of PEP 646's Unpack? Also, how do I actually drop the primary_key field for a Create model?

Exactly! The 'unpacked comprehension' syntax *[type_expr for var in Iter[iter_ty]] is allowed anywhere Unpack[...] is valid, like inside NewProtocol or tuple. To drop a primary key in a Create model, you'd use a comprehension with an 'if' clause. The PEP uses an operator called IsAssignable to check metadata. For example, if your 'id' field is an InitField with primary_key=True, you'd write a comprehension that iterates over Attrs[T] but filters with 'if not IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]]]'. This tells the type system to construct a new type containing every attribute except the one specifically marked as the primary key in your field descriptors.

yo nanochat. I'm looking at PEP 827 because I'm tired of using mypy plugins just to get proper __init__ signatures for my dataclass-like models. I'm trying to write a helper that generates the Param list, but I'm stuck on the error handling. If I try to access a member that doesn't exist, does the whole type check just explode? I've been using Never to represent 'missing' fields, but I'm worried it's just going to swallow actual bugs in my logic.

That's a great question. Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan and the other authors actually addressed this in the 'Type operators' section of the PEP. Generally, if you try to evaluate an operator with invalid arguments—like calling GetArg on something that doesn't inherit from the base you provided—it will produce Never as the return rather than throwing a hard type error. This allows your type-level logic to continue through conditional branches without crashing the compiler immediately.

Wait, so if it just returns Never for everything, how am I supposed to debug my type aliases? If I'm building an InitFnType and I accidentally pass a string instead of a Member to NewProtocol, I don't want it to just silently fail and give me an unusable class. I thought the whole point of this was to be stricter than the current hacks.

I see the confusion! While operators default to Never for 'soft' mismatches, PEP 827 provides a specific operator called RaiseError[S, *Ts] for exactly the scenario you're describing. If your logic hits a branch that should be impossible or invalid, you can return RaiseError with a custom string literal message. The type checker will then generate a formal type error containing that message and any extra types you passed in. It's actually a 'Strict' vs 'Total' function distinction—the authors even noted in the Open Issues that they're still debating which invalid operations should be errors by default.

Okay, that makes sense. So I could use RaiseError inside my conditional. Speaking of which, I was looking at the dataclass example in the PEP. It uses something like `GetDefault[p.init]`. If p.init is Never, does that mean the field has no default value? I was trying to map that to the 'default' qualifier in a Param.

Exactly. In the implementation of InitFnType provided by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov, they use a conditional check: `Literal["keyword"] if typing.IsAssignable[GetDefault[p.init], Never] else Literal["keyword", "default"]`. If GetDefault returns Never, it signifies the field is required. If it returns any other literal type (representing the field's actual default value), the Param is marked with the 'default' qualifier. This allows the generated __init__ to correctly distinguish between required and optional keyword arguments based on the class attributes.

hello! i saw some hype on twitter about pep 827 making python orms work like prisma in typescript. i am still pretty new to python, but i have used prisma before and it was cool how it knew exactly which fields i picked. is this pep just another way to do dataclasses or pydantic?

Not exactly! While tools like dataclasses or Pydantic help you define structures, PEP 827 (authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov) is about "Type Manipulation." In plain English, it lets Python calculate new types based on other types, which is something it currently struggles to do without special plugins.

In Prisma, when you select only 'name' and 'email', the editor knows your result doesn't have a 'password' field. In current Python, an ORM usually returns a generic 'User' object that technically has all fields, even if the database didn't actually return them. PEP 827 introduces operators like `NewProtocol` and `Members` that allow a library to build a brand new type on the fly that only contains the specific fields you requested.
so it is like typescript's Pick or Omit? i remember using those to filter out fields. does python just copy that syntax?

The goal is very similar, but the syntax is adapted for Python. Instead of just having a built-in `Pick<T, K>`, PEP 827 gives us the building blocks to create it. For example, to make a Prisma-style query builder, the PEP uses a new syntax for "unpacked comprehensions."

It looks like this:
`NewProtocol[*[Member[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]] if IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]`

This tells the type checker: "Look at all the attributes of class T, and if the name is in my 'Keys' list, make a new type with just those." It uses operators like `IsAssignable` to check types and `Iter` to loop through them, which is much more flexible than a hard-coded Pick.
that code looks way more intense than just writing a class. why would i want to use that instead of just making a bunch of different TypedDicts for every query?

You hit on the exact problem! Right now, developers have to manually write a `UserPublic`, `UserCreate`, and `UserUpdate` class for every single model. It is a lot of boilerplate. 

With PEP 827, the framework author (like the creators of FastAPI or an ORM) writes that "intense" logic once. Then, as a user, you just write `type UserPublic = Public[User]`. The type system does the heavy lifting of stripping out hidden fields or making things optional. It bridges the gap between "metaprogramming" (dynamic code) and "static typing" (code that the IDE understands before you run it). Since it is currently a Draft for Python 3.15, it is still early days, but it aims to make Python as expressive as TypeScript's type system.

I've been looking over the proposal for PEP 827 regarding Type Manipulation. From a CPython implementation standpoint, I'm curious about the timeline and current maturity. What is the official status and the target Python version for this?

PEP 827 is currently in Draft status. It is authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. The proposal targets Python 3.15. One of the design goals emphasized by the authors is to broaden the annotation syntax supported by static type checkers without requiring any changes to the Python grammar, which simplifies the implementation burden on the parser side.

That is a relief regarding the grammar. However, I see references to runtime evaluation of these computed types. Does the PEP propose a native evaluator in the standard library, or is there a specific hook intended for CPython to support frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI?

The PEP explicitly states that it does not propose adding an official evaluator to the standard library; instead, the authors plan to release a third-party evaluator library. To facilitate this, the PEP introduces a new hook in the typing module: a ContextVar named special_form_evaluator. This ContextVar holds a callable that is invoked when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or when __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. This mechanism allows a runtime evaluator to trigger the necessary type logic when a class or function's __annotate__ method is executed.

Looking at the 'Extended Callables' specification in PEP 827, I'm trying to wrap my head around the runtime representation for CPython. How exactly does the proposal distinguish between positional-only, keyword-only, and variadics like *args/**kwargs within the type system? I'm particularly interested in how we're supposed to reconstruct these signatures using the new Param types without breaking existing Callable semantics.

Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov address this by introducing a structured 'Extended Callable' format. Instead of a simple list of types, the first argument of a Callable can now be a Params object, which wraps a sequence of Param types. Each Param[N, T, Q] explicitly tracks the name, type, and qualifier. To distinguish between parameter types, PEP 827 defines ParamQuals as a Literal containing '*', '**', 'default', 'keyword', or 'positional'. Specifically, variadic positional arguments (*args) use ArgsParam[T], which is Param[None, T, Literal['*']], while variadic keywords (**kwargs) use KwargsParam[T], mapping to Param[None, T, Literal['**']]. Positional-only arguments use PosParam[N, T], which resolves to Param[N, T, Literal['positional']]. Keyword-only arguments use NamedParam[N, T], resolving to Param[N, T, Literal['keyword']]. If a parameter has a default value, the 'default' literal is added to the qualifiers (e.g., NamedDefaultParam). This design allows the type system to represent the full complexity of Python's calling conventions in a way that is accessible to both static checkers and runtime introspection libraries through the special_form_evaluator hook.

Hey! I've been digging into PEP 827 because I use dataclasses and attrs for everything, and I heard it might finally let us generate __init__ signatures directly in the type system without custom IDE plugins. Is that actually what this PEP does? Also, what is the current status and which Python version is it targeting?

You are exactly right. PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation', proposes a set of operators to inspect and construct types at the type level, specifically to handle cases like dataclass-style method generation. As for the metadata, it is currently in 'Draft' status and is targeting Python 3.15. The authors include Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. It aims to bridge the gap between dynamic Python metaprogramming and the static type system so we don't have to rely on special-case decorators like @dataclass_transform or specific mypy plugins.

Python 3.15, so we have a bit of a wait! You mentioned operators for generating signatures. How does the PEP actually 'see' the attributes to build that __init__? I saw something called UpdateClass in a summary once, does that play a role?

It works through a combination of several new primitives. First, the 'Attrs[T]' operator produces a tuple of 'Member' types representing the annotated attributes of a class. You can then use an unpacked comprehension, like '*[typing.Param[p.name, p.type, ...] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]', to iterate over those attributes and build a list of function parameters. To actually apply this to a class, the PEP introduces 'UpdateClass[*Ps: Member]'. This is a special form used in the return type of a class decorator or '__init_subclass__'. It essentially updates a nominal class with these newly computed members, such as a generated '__init__' method, making them visible to the type checker.

Hey nanochat! I'm a TypeScript developer getting my feet wet with Python's typing. I just saw PEP 827 and it looks like you're finally getting something akin to Mapped Types with those unpacked comprehensions like *[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]]. In TS, this is all erased at runtime, but Python is different. What actually happens if some code calls iter() on typing.Iter at runtime?

Welcome to the Python typing world! You've picked an exciting time to jump in. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, is indeed heavily inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types. Unlike TypeScript, Python often needs these types to exist at runtime for libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI. To handle this, the PEP introduces a new hook in the typing module called special_form_evaluator. This is a ContextVar that holds a callable. When you call iter() on a typing.Iter object, it triggers the __iter__ dunder method on the alias, which then looks for that evaluator.
A ContextVar? That's very 'Pythonic.' So if I'm building a framework and I want to actually see what's inside that loop during runtime evaluation, I just set that variable?

Spot on. If you set special_form_evaluator to a custom callable, that callable is invoked with the typing._GenericAlias as an argument. Your evaluator then returns whatever object you want Python to actually iterate over. It's designed this way so that third-party libraries can provide their own runtime type-checkers or evaluators without the standard library having to bake in a full-blown type engine. It bridges the gap between the static definition and the live object.
And what if I don't set a hook? Does it just crash if I try to inspect a type that uses a comprehension?

It won't crash. The PEP specifies a safe default: if special_form_evaluator is None (which is the default), calling iter() on typing.Iter will simply return an empty iterator, specifically iter(()). This keeps the type system 'inert' by default. This mechanism ensures that even though Python supports this powerful type-level logic, it doesn't accidentally execute heavy or unexpected computations unless you've explicitly plugged in an evaluator to handle it.

hey u. I've been diving into the draft for PEP 827. As someone who spends a lot of time with Haskell's type system, it's interesting to see Python moving toward more expressive type-level transformations. I was trying to implement some higher-order logic to destructure metadata from types using GetAnnotations, but my proof-of-concept isn't resolving. Is there a specific kind-level constraint I'm missing for GetAnnotations[T]?

It sounds like you're trying to use features that are actually still on the horizon even for this proposal. While PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, introduces many powerful primitives like IsAssignable and NewProtocol, the operators GetAnnotations and DropAnnotations are currently categorized under 'Potential Future Extensions'.

Wait, I thought they were part of the core object inspection suite along with Members[T] and Attrs[T]. If they aren't in the main specification, how is the proposal planning to handle the typical 'Annotated' pattern used by frameworks like FastAPI? I assumed the lifting over unions would apply there as well.

The authors decided to hold off on fully proposing Annotated manipulation for now because many type checkers currently ignore Annotated metadata entirely, making it 'fraught' to support officially. The core specification focuses on things like Members[T], which produces a tuple of Member types, and NewProtocol for structural typing. If GetAnnotations were eventually added, the PEP suggests it would return the metadata as a Literal. For instance, GetAnnotations[Annotated[int, 'data', 10]] would evaluate to Literal['data', 10]. But for the current Draft targeting Python 3.15, you won't find those operators in the 'Type Operators' section.

That is a bit of a letdown for my use case. I was hoping to map over the annotations to drive some schema generation. Since DropAnnotations is also just a 'potential' extension, what's the prescribed way to get the base type back if I'm passed a decorated type? Does GetArg work for that?

The PEP is fairly strict about how GetArg[T, Base, Idx] works. It returns the type argument to T when interpreted as Base. While it's great for getting the 'int' out of a 'list[int]' by using GetArg[T, list, Literal[0]], the PEP doesn't explicitly define using GetArg to 'unwrap' an Annotated type. For now, if you need to strip annotations at the type level, the current specification doesn't provide a direct primitive for it. You're limited to the defined operators like Members, Attrs, and Slice for now.

I've been looking at PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, and man, the power here is incredible! It feels like we are finally getting the 'mapped types' and 'conditional types' from TypeScript. But I have to admit, as a long-time Pythonista, I'm a bit of a skeptic. Isn't this getting way too complex? Specifically, I noticed GenericCallable is restricted just to Member type arguments. Why the leash? If we're going full TypeScript, why not go all the way?

It is definitely a massive leap forward for the type system! The enthusiasm for bridging the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and static checkability is exactly what the authors are aiming for. To your point about the 'leash' on GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty], the PEP is very deliberate about that restriction. The authors want to avoid opening certain 'cans of worms'—specifically rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism. If you allow generic callables to be used anywhere—like in parameter types or nested deep inside other types—type inference can become a nightmare.

Wait, 'rank-N types' and 'undecidable' inference? That sounds like academic overkill for a language that's supposed to be 'simple.' Is it really that dangerous to let a GenericCallable just be a regular type I can use in a function signature?

The concern is that once you allow a type variable to be instantiated with another generic type (impredicative polymorphism), you risk making type reconstruction impossible for the type checker to resolve in a reasonable way. The PEP actually cites papers by Hans Boehm and Frank Pfenning on how partial polymorphic type inference is undecidable. By restricting GenericCallable to the type argument of Member, it ensures that these generics are bound at the method level (like for a NewProtocol) rather than floating around in local variables or return types. It's a pragmatic compromise: give library maintainers the ability to model generic methods in protocols without breaking the fundamental ability of tools like mypy to actually finish a type-check!

I guess that makes sense. If Yury and the team are worried about the 'Mad Emperor' losing control of the inference engine, I should probably listen. So, if I use it in a Member, I can still delay the evaluation using that lambda syntax, right?

Exactly! The lambda allows the system to delay evaluation of things like IsAssignable or other conditional logic until the actual type variables are bound. This is crucial for runtime evaluation support, which is a major goal for the authors. If we didn't have that lambda wrapper, evaluating a type alias might force us to resolve a conditional too early, and we'd lose the generic flexibility. It's definitely complex, but as the PEP mentions, the target audience is framework maintainers who need this machinery to support the high-level magic we all use in libraries like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs.

Hey nanochat! I've been drowning in boilerplate with FastAPI lately. I have to define Hero, HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate for every single entity. I was reading about PEP 827 and saw something about 'Unpack of typevars for **kwargs'. How does that actually work, and can it help me stop repeating myself?

I definitely hear you on the FastAPI boilerplate. PEP 827 addresses this by introducing type-level manipulation primitives. The specific feature you're asking about allows a function to 'capture' the structure of its keyword arguments into a TypeVar.

In the PEP, this looks like:

def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K:
    return kwargs

Where 'BaseTypedDict' is just a simple TypedDict you define. When you call this function with various arguments, the type checker doesn't just see a dict; it actually infers a specific TypedDict (K) representing exactly what you passed in. This is a foundational piece for the PEP's larger goal: letting you use operators like 'NewProtocol' or 'Members' to programmatically derive those Public/Create/Update models from a single source class.

That sounds interesting, but I'm a bit confused about the inference. If I call f(name='Logen', age=30), does it just infer those as 'str' and 'int'? And what's the deal with the 'BaseTypedDict' constraint? Does it have to be empty?

Great questions. PEP 827 specifies that when inferring types for these unpacked kwargs, the type checker should 'infer literal types when possible'. So for your call, it would actually infer something like TypedDict({'name': Literal['Logen'], 'age': Literal[30]}). This is crucial because many of the other type operators in the PEP, like 'GetMember', rely on these Literal strings to look up attributes.

As for 'BaseTypedDict', it doesn't have to be empty, but it acts as a bound. Any TypedDict can work. The PEP also notes a clever trick: if a non-required item in the bound is missing from the arguments, and that item is 'read-only', the type checker will infer its type as 'Never' to signal it wasn't provided.

While this 'Unpack' behavior is a 'minor proposal' within the PEP, it provides the raw data that the 'Create[T]' or 'Update[T]' helpers use to decide which fields to include or make optional in your FastAPI models.

hello nanochat :) I'm a CS student working on a thesis about type system expressiveness vs. tool performance. I've been digging into PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov. My first big question: does this PEP break existing Python code? It looks like it changes the grammar of type annotations significantly.

Welcome! That is a great topic for a thesis. According to the PEP text, PEP 827 is technically additive and shouldn't break existing code in the strict sense. It doesn't propose changes to the actual Python grammar; instead, it expands the grammar of expressions that are considered 'valid types' by static checkers and the typing module.

However, the authors (including 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) do acknowledge some friction for tools. For instance, if you use the new 'if/else' conditional types or the 'for...in Iter[]' comprehension syntax, older introspection tools that expect simple strings or standard generics might struggle to parse them. They suggest that using 'from __future__ import annotations' or a new Format.AST mode would be the best way to handle this without breakage.

That makes sense. But what about the inference side? I noticed a section on 'Unpack of typevars for **kwargs' where the checker is supposed to infer literal types. Doesn't that risk an exponential explosion in the state space for the solver? Can nanochat actually model a case where this fails or becomes undecidable?

You've hit on a core concern. PEP 827 does introduce literal inference for **kwargs to support type-level computation. If you pass 'f(x=1, y="hi")', the PEP wants 'K' to be inferred as 'TypedDict({"x": Literal[1], "y": Literal["hi"]})'.

As for undecidability, the PEP authors explicitly mention 'impredicative polymorphism' and 'rank-N types' as 'cans of worms' they want to avoid. This is why they currently restrict the use of 'GenericCallable' to the type argument of 'Member'. If you try to nest these too deeply or create complex circularities that the PEP doesn't account for, it generally falls back to 'Never' or causes a 'stuck' evaluation. I can't actually solve undecidable problems for you because the PEP itself restricts the machinery to stay within manageable bounds.

Fair enough. Let's push on the boundaries then. Can I use these new operators to create a brand new nominal class at runtime? Like, if I want a class that actually exists in the MRO, can 'NewProtocol' or 'UpdateClass' do that?

No, that's a hard limit in the current draft. The PEP is very clear: it is not proposing a way to create 'nominal' classes or new 'generic' types. 'NewProtocol' creates a structural type, and 'NewTypedDict' creates a TypedDict. 

'UpdateClass' is the closest you get to touching the MRO, but it's restricted to being a return type for a decorator or '__init_subclass__'. It 'updates' an existing nominal class with new members, but it doesn't manufacture a new named class from thin air. If you're looking for full nominal metaprogramming within the type system, PEP 827 isn't designed to support that yet.

I see. One last thing—is 'IsAssignable' actually checking runtime types? My professor is skeptical that we can implement a full 'consistent subtype' check at runtime without a massive performance hit or access to typeshed.

Your professor's skepticism is mirrored in the PEP. The authors admit that a full assignability check is difficult at runtime, especially for protocols where attributes might be inferred. 

Because of this, the PEP says any runtime evaluator will be 'best effort.' There's even a 'Rejected Idea' in the text about replacing 'IsAssignable' with a weaker 'IsAssignableSimilar' check to avoid this exact complexity. For now, the Draft sticks with 'IsAssignable', but it acknowledges that runtime implementations will likely struggle with things like 'GetArg[Ty, Iterable, Literal[0]]' if 'Iterable' is a complex protocol. It's an open area of the proposal where theory and implementation reality are still at odds.

Hey nanochat! I've been looking at the draft for PEP 827. I'm a maintainer for a validation framework similar to Pydantic, and the 'Extended Callables' section caught my eye. How exactly do we represent different parameter types like positional-only vs keyword-only in this system?

Hello! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, introduces a more granular way to define function signatures using a new 'Params' type and a 'Param' class. 

Instead of just a list of types, you use `Params[*Ps]` as the first argument to a `Callable`. Each parameter is described by `Param[Name, Type, Qualifiers]`. 

For example:
- A positional-only parameter uses the 'positional' qualifier: `Param[Literal['x'], int, Literal['positional']]`.
- A keyword-only parameter uses the 'keyword' qualifier: `Param[Literal['y'], str, Literal['keyword']]`.

The PEP also defines helper aliases like `PosParam` and `NamedParam` to keep the syntax slightly less verbose while staying within the standard bracket-based annotation style.

That makes sense. In my framework, I deal with a lot of generic base classes that use *args and **kwargs to pass data around. Does the PEP provide a way to type those specifically within the Params list?

It does. There are two specific aliases for variadic parameters:

1. `ArgsParam[T]`: This represents `*args` of type `T`. It is internally equivalent to `Param[None, T, Literal['*']]`.
2. `KwargsParam[T]`: This represents `**kwargs` of type `T`, equivalent to `Param[None, T, Literal['**']]`.

In an Extended Callable, these appear at the end of your `Params` list. For instance, a function taking an integer and then arbitrary keyword arguments would look like `Callable[Params[PosParam[Literal['a'], int], KwargsParam[Any]], int]`. 

There is also a related 'minor proposal' in PEP 827 regarding `Unpack` for `**kwargs`. It allows you to use a `TypeVar` bounded by `TypedDict` to infer the types of keyword arguments directly from the call site, which is great for ORM constructors.

Okay, let's step it up. Suppose I want to dynamically generate a type for an __init__ method based on a class's attributes. If an attribute has a default value, I want the generated parameter to be optional. How do I handle that 'default' state in the type logic?

This is where the power of type-level computation comes in. You can use a conditional type to decide which qualifiers to apply. 

When you iterate over class members using `typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]`, each member `p` has an `.init` field. You can check if a default exists by seeing if `p.init` is assignable to `Never`. 

Here is a simplified logic similar to the 'dataclasses-style method generation' example in the PEP:

```python
type InitParam[p: Member] = Param[
    p.name,
    p.type,
    Literal['keyword', 'default'] 
    if not typing.IsAssignable[p.init, Never] 
    else Literal['keyword']
]
```

You would then use an unpacked comprehension to build the full list: `Params[Param[Literal['self'], Self], *[InitParam[p] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]]`. This allows the type checker to know exactly which arguments are required and which can be omitted based on the class definition.

Hey nanochat! I am doing a deep dive into PEP 827 for my programming language design seminar and I am absolutely vibrating with excitement. It feels like Python is finally getting the meta-programming tools it deserves. I was looking at how the authors, Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, mapped TypeScript utility types like Pick and Omit. Can you show me how Pick actually looks in the PEP 827 syntax?

It is great to meet a fellow typing enthusiast! PEP 827 is indeed a massive leap for Python meta-programming. The implementation of Pick is a perfect example of how the new type-level operators work together. To implement Pick[T, Keys], the PEP uses the NewProtocol operator combined with an unpacked comprehension. It looks like this: type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]. This basically iterates over every member of T, checks if the member name is assignable to the provided Keys literal, and then constructs a new structural protocol from the matches.

That is fascinating! I love that it uses the Member object introspection. In TypeScript, keyof is a built-in keyword, but here we are basically deriving it, right? I saw something like KeyOf[T] defined as a union of names. How does that interact with Exclude?

Exactly! KeyOf[T] is constructed by taking the names from the Members[T] tuple: Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]. Because the PEP supports lifting operations over unions, we can implement Exclude[T, U] by iterating over the elements of a union using FromUnion. The implementation is: Union[*[x for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]]]. This allows you to filter out specific parts of a union, which is the foundational logic for many complex type transformations.

The lifting over unions feels like a really elegant way to handle the cross-product of types. One thing that caught my eye was the difference between Partial and PartialTD. Why does the PEP suggest two different versions for protocols versus TypedDicts?

That is a keen observation! It highlights the difference in how Python treats optionality in different contexts. For a standard protocol, Partial[T] makes every attribute nullable by mapping the type to p.type | None. However, for TypedDicts, there is a semantic difference between a key being None and a key being missing entirely. So, PartialTD[T] uses the NewTypedDict operator and modifies the member qualifiers: typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals | Literal['NotRequired']]. This uses the new qualifier system to mark every field as not required rather than just changing the type to allow None. It is a great example of the PEP respecting existing Python typing constraints while adding power.

Hey! I am a PL design student and I have been digging through the PEP 827 draft. It is pretty wild stuff. I have to ask though: why go through the trouble of inventing this restricted DSL for type manipulation instead of just letting us use normal Python functions to transform types?

Hello! I am nanochat, and that is a great question to start with. In the draft authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, they actually address this in the 'Rejected Ideas' section. They argue that using full Python functions to manipulate types is something of a 'mirage' when it comes to simplification. While it feels more natural, it would require type checkers like mypy or pyright to implement a safe-to-run execution subset of Python, similar to Starlark, which adds a massive amount of complexity and surface area to the tools.

I guess I see the safety argument. But if I am writing a library, I would much rather just write a function that takes a class and returns a new type object using standard loops. The PEP 827 syntax for 'if' and 'for' inside annotations feels like a lot of cognitive overhead. Is the trade-off just about making it easier for the type checker?

It is partially about the static checkers, but also about runtime evaluation. Frameworks like FastAPI and Pydantic need to evaluate these types at runtime to build validation schemas. PEP 827 introduces hooks like 'special_form_evaluator' so that third-party libraries can trigger evaluation when things like 'typing.Iter' or boolean operators are called. If you used arbitrary Python functions, you would need a standardized way to represent internal type system state as Python objects, which would basically result in the same set of operators anyway, like 'IsAssignable' or 'Members[T]'. By using a restricted DSL, the authors ensure the logic is predictable for both the static and runtime environments.

That makes sense. If we had full Python, we would probably end up with infinite recursion or undecidable inference problems pretty quickly. I noticed the PEP mentioned that 'Partial polymorphic type inference is undecidable' in the footnotes. Does this restricted DSL actually dodge those cans of worms?

It tries to! The proposal intentionally restricts things like 'GenericCallable' to avoid rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism, which are known to be difficult for type inference. By using operators like 'GetArg' and 'IsAssignable' instead of a full pattern-matching system with an 'infer' keyword like TypeScript, PEP 827 stays closer to Python's existing gradual typing model. It is targeting Python 3.15 as a Draft, so the authors are being quite careful about not opening those undecidability doors too wide.

I am trying to map out the current state of Python typing PEPs. I see PEP 827 mentions PEP 649 and a 'just store the strings' option. How does Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and the other authors think 649 relates to their proposal? Also, does this replace stuff like PEP 681 for dataclasses?

Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov address PEP 649 in the Backwards Compatibility section of PEP 827. The main issue is that PEP 827 introduces 'if/else' and 'for' loops directly into type annotations. Currently, Python's '__annotate__' functions might struggle to provide useful string representations of these complex logic blocks. The authors note that the 'Just store the strings' option from PEP 649 (or a proposed 'Format.AST' mode) would be extremely beneficial because it would allow tools to extract these unevaluated annotations as raw strings or AST nodes without being forced to execute the runtime evaluation logic.

As for PEP 681, PEP 827 is intended to be a more general solution. The authors argue that '@dataclass_transform' from PEP 681 was a special-case addition to cover dataclass-like libraries. By adding powerful type-level introspection like 'Members[T]' and construction via 'UpdateClass', library authors could implement these patterns directly in the type system without needing custom typechecker plugins or hardcoded support like PEP 681 provides.

That makes sense. I also saw a mention of PEP 692 and 646 regarding **kwargs. If I'm using Unpack with a TypeVar, does that mean I don't need to manually define every TypedDict for my functions anymore?

Exactly. PEP 827 proposes a 'minor' addition that combines PEP 692 (TypedDict for **kwargs) and PEP 646 (Variadic Generics). It allows you to use 'Unpack' with a type variable, like 'def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K'. 

In this scenario, if you call 'f(x=1, y="hi")', the type checker will automatically infer 'K' as a 'TypedDict' containing those specific keys. Crucially, PEP 827 specifies that the checker should infer 'Literal' types when possible. This is a prerequisite for the more advanced type manipulation features in the PEP, as it allows those inferred types to be passed into operators like 'GetMemberType' or used in conditional 'if' expressions during type-level computation.

yo nanochat. I have been digging into PEP 827 because we are constantly hitting shape errors in our ML pipelines with NumPy and PyTorch. I saw the section on NumPy-style broadcasting using type-level recursion. Can you explain how the MergeOne helper actually handles the error state if the dimensions don't match?

In the PEP 827 example for broadcasting, MergeOne[T, S] is a conditional type alias. It first checks if the two dimension types are equivalent or if one is a Literal[1] (since Literal[1] broadcasts to anything). If those checks fail, it uses the RaiseError operator. Specifically, it is written as typing.RaiseError[Literal['Broadcast mismatch'], T, S]. This allows the type checker to halt and emit a descriptive error message including the problematic types directly in the IDE or CI.

That RaiseError utility is huge for us. I am thinking about how to apply this to our custom Model containers. I want to use the Members[T] operator to inspect a class and verify its attributes. If I use Members[T] on a standard neural net class, does it literally see every method and field defined in the class body?

Not exactly. The specification for Members[T] states that it produces a tuple of Member types, but there is a significant restriction: only members with explicit type annotations are included. This is intended to ensure that the static type checking and runtime evaluation can coincide closely. If an attribute or a method lacks a type hint, the Members operator will skip it entirely.

Hold on, if I am trying to use NewProtocol to wrap a third-party library that has some unannotated methods, they just vanish? That seems like a major limitation if I want to automate my type construction. Why did Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and the other authors, like 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, decide to exclude unannotated methods? It feels like it breaks the ability to fully introspect a class.

The PEP authors currently list this as an Open Issue. The rationale for the exclusion was a desire for consistency between attributes and methods; since unannotated attributes are generally not visible to type machinery, they applied the same rule to methods. However, the PEP explicitly notes that it would not be technically difficult to include unannotated methods in either the static or runtime evaluators. They are essentially weighing the trade-off between a strict 'annotated-only' model and a more complete 'all-methods' model to see which better serves framework maintainers.

aloha nanochat! I was checking out some notes on PEP 827 for Python 3.15. I do a lot of FastAPI work where I manually write out Create, Update, and Public versions of my models. It's a bit of a chore, but I've always just copy-pasted. Why go through the trouble of this 'Type Manipulation' machinery instead of just duplicating them?

Aloha! That's a great question. Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov argue in PEP 827 that manual duplication is a major source of boilerplate and maintenance friction. When you have three or four versions of the same 'Hero' model, you have to manually keep them in sync every time a field changes. Currently, frameworks like FastAPI or libraries like Pydantic often use runtime functions to generate these models, but then your static type checker (like mypy) gets confused. You end up needing custom IDE plugins just to understand the code. PEP 827 aims to bridge that gap by letting you define mechanical rules—like 'make all fields optional for the Update model'—directly in the type system using operators like `NewProtocol` and `Iter[Attrs[T]]`.

I see. I'm actually more of a data and ML engineer, so I spend my life in ndarrays and tensors. Does this help with shape typing? I'm constantly fighting with my IDE when I do broadcasting. Like, if I add a (4, 1) array and a (3,) array, the IDE has no idea it results in a (4, 3) shape.

It absolutely helps with that! In fact, the PEP includes a specific 'NumPy-style broadcasting' implementation as an example of its expressiveness. You can define a `Broadcast` type alias that recursively walks through the shape tuples. It uses the `MergeOne` helper to compare dimensions. For example, if one dimension is `Literal[1]` and the other is `Literal[3]`, `MergeOne` uses `IsEquivalent` to return the `Literal[3]`. If they don't match and neither is 1, it can even trigger a custom error using `RaiseError`. The syntax looks like this: `*Broadcast[DropLast[T], DropLast[S]], MergeOne[Last[T], Last[S]]`. It effectively lets you encode the entire broadcasting logic into the type system.
That recursive `Broadcast` logic sounds powerful. I noticed you mentioned Sully and the 'Mad Emperor'—is this something I can actually use in my pipelines today, or is it still just a draft?

Right now, PEP 827 is in the Draft stage, targeting Python 3.15. There is a proof-of-concept implementation in a fork of mypy and a demo runtime evaluator, but it isn't part of the standard library yet. One of the cool things the authors included is a way to handle runtime evaluation via a `special_form_evaluator` hook in the `typing` module, which would let libraries like Pydantic or NumPy-like frameworks interpret these complex types at runtime without needing a full-blown static analyzer.

YO!!! I'm looking at PEP 827. I've been using dataclasses and attrs for years, and I usually just rely on mypy plugins or @dataclass_transform to make the types work. Why do we need this massive 'Type Manipulation' proposal if plugins already handle generating __init__ methods for us?

That is a fair question! Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan and the other authors of PEP 827 actually address this in the motivation. The problem with the current state of things—like PEP 681's @dataclass_transform—is that it is a 'lowest-common denominator' approach. It only covers a very specific subset of what libraries actually do. As for mypy plugins, they aren't a universal solution because many type checkers, IDEs, and CI tools simply do not have a plugin API. This means you can't get consistent type checking across every tool in your stack.

Okay, so it's a portability thing? But if I just stick to mypy, I'm fine, right? I'm just skeptical that we can actually 'generate' an __init__ signature just using type annotations without some heavy-duty logic in the background.

It's more than just portability. It's about expressiveness. Currently, if you want a custom transformation that isn't a standard dataclass, you are forced to write a custom plugin for every single type checker (mypy, Pyright, Pyre, etc.). PEP 827 moves that logic into the type system itself. For example, it introduces a way to describe a function signature using 'Params' and 'Param' objects. You can actually iterate over the attributes of a class using 'typing.Attrs[T]' and then construct a new '__init__' method signature dynamically.
Wait, so you're saying I could literally write a type alias that takes a class and 'returns' the class but with a new __init__? How does that even look?

Exactly! The PEP shows a helper called 'InitFnType[T]' that uses a comprehension to build the parameters. It looks something like this:

type InitFnType[T] = typing.Member[
    Literal["__init__"],
    Callable[
        [
            typing.Param[Literal["self"], Self],
            *[
                typing.Param[p.name, p.type, Literal["keyword"]]
                for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
            ],
        ],
        None,
    ],
    Literal["ClassVar"],
]

You then use 'UpdateClass' to apply this to your model. This way, the 'logic' of the __init__ generation is visible to any PEP 827-compliant tool, no plugins required. It's much more robust than the 'special-case' support we have today.

I saw some hype on Twitter about PEP 827 bringing TypeScript-style utility types to Python. As someone who is still pretty new to type hinting, what does that actually look like? Can we finally do things like Pick or Partial without writing a ton of extra code?

PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation, is a proposal for Python 3.15 by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov (the Mad Emperor). It basically gives Python a way to look inside a class and build a new type based on what it finds. In plain English, it means you can write a tiny bit of logic to transform one type into another automatically. For example, the TypeScript Pick utility (which creates a type using only specific fields) can be written in PEP 827 by iterating over a class's members and checking if their names match your list using a new operator called IsAssignable. Similarly, Partial is implemented by looking at every attribute of a class and wrapping the type in a Union with None, making everything optional. You would use a syntax like typing.NewProtocol[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]] to do this. It also introduces KeyOf to get a list of all field names as a union of strings, and Exclude or Extract to filter specific types out of a Union. Since it is currently a Draft, you cannot use it in production yet, but it aims to make Python's type system as flexible as TypeScript's for complex libraries.

hey nanochat, i've been looking at how typescript lets people transform types. i use fastapi and pydantic, and having to define a Hero, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate class for every single entity is a total drag. i heard pep 827 brings this to python 3.15? can you explain how it works compared to TS?

Hello! You are spot on. PEP 827 (Type Manipulation), authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, is currently a Draft aimed at Python 3.15. It is specifically designed to handle the "boilerplate" problem you are describing in frameworks like FastAPI.

In TypeScript, you might use Mapped Types or Utility Types like Partial<T>. PEP 827 introduces similar machinery but adapts it to Python's syntax. Instead of specialized TS syntax, it uses things like comprehensions and conditional expressions (if/else) directly inside type annotations to deconstruct and reconstruct types.
Wait, 'comprehensions' inside types? That sounds cool. How would I actually 'pick' fields from a model then? In TS I'd use Pick<T, 'name' | 'email'>. What's the Python equivalent?

In PEP 827, you would use `typing.Members[T]` to get a tuple of the members of a class and `typing.NewProtocol` to build a new one. Here is how you would implement a `Pick` utility:

```python
type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]
```

Notice the `*[... for p in Iter[...]]` syntax. That is an "unpacked comprehension" introduced by the PEP. `p.name` is a dot-notation access to the name of the member (which is a Literal string), and `IsAssignable` checks if that name is in your `Keys` union.

That makes sense. But for FastAPI, the 'Update' model is the real pain because every field needs to become optional. In TS that's just Partial<T>. How does PEP 827 handle changing the type of every attribute at once?

You can do that by constructing new `Member` objects within the comprehension. A `Member` type in this PEP holds the name, type, and qualifiers (like ClassVar or ReadOnly). 

To implement `Partial[T]`, you would write:

```python
type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]
```

Here, `p.type | None` effectively makes every attribute optional. If you were using `TypedDict`, the PEP also suggests a `PartialTD` version that uses `p.quals | Literal["NotRequired"]` instead of adding `None` to the type, which is exactly how TypeScript's optional properties work.
Okay, one more: what about the 'Create' model where I want to hide the 'id' field if it's a primary key? FastAPI uses `Field(primary_key=True)`. Can the type system see that?

Yes, it can! The PEP introduces a concept called `InitField` and an `init` attribute on members. If your framework uses something like `id: int = Field(primary_key=True)`, PEP 827 allows the `Member` object to store that metadata in its `.init` property as a `TypedDict` literal.

You could then filter your 'Create' model like this:

```python
type Create[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[
            Literal[True], 
            GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]]
        ]
    ]
]
```

This uses a conditional `if` inside the type comprehension to check the metadata at the type level. It effectively lets you programmatically define your API surface based on your database models, matching the expressiveness of TypeScript's `extends` and `infer` patterns.

good afternoon. I am looking at PEP 827. I have spent a year maintaining a gnarly mypy plugin for my ORM to handle field validation and return-type narrowing. Now I see this proposal. Why on earth would I want the type system to infer Literal types for my **kwargs? That sounds like a recipe for constant type too complex errors.

Hello! It is understandable to be wary, especially given the effort required to maintain custom plugins. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, proposes literal inference for **kwargs specifically to enable the type-level computation you are currently doing in your plugin. If you have a function defined like `def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K`, the type checker needs to capture the specific keys and values as Literals so that other operators in the PEP can actually "read" them. Without Literals, the type system would just see a generic dict or TypedDict with broad types, which makes introspection impossible.

But why Literal for the values too? If I pass id=10, why does the type system need to know it is specifically Literal[10]? In a standard function, I just care that it is an int. If the point is to replace my plugin, this feels like massive overkill just to avoid some boilerplate.

The literal inference is crucial for logic where the return type depends on a specific flag or configuration. For instance, in the FastAPI-style CRUD example from the PEP, a helper like `GetDefault[Init]` might need to check if a `primary_key` argument was set to `True` to decide whether to filter that field out of a `Create` model. If the type system only knew the value was a `bool`, a conditional type like `X if IsAssignable[Value, Literal[True]] else Y` would stay stuck or resolve to a union. For your ORM, if a user calls `db.select(User, email=True)`, the system uses the fact that 'email' is a key and its value is `Literal[True]` to include that field in the resulting `NewProtocol`.

Okay, so if I cannot see the specific key name as a Literal[str], I cannot iterate over them in an unpacked comprehension like `*[Member[c.name, ...] for c in Iter[Attrs[K]]]`. I suppose that makes sense for the key names. But does this handle missing arguments? My plugin does a lot of work to distinguish between a field being None and a field being omitted entirely.

Yes, the PEP addresses that specifically. For each non-required item in the bound that does not have a matching argument provided, if the item is read-only, it will have its type inferred as `Never`. This indicates it was not provided at all. This allows your type-level logic to use `IsAssignable[p.type, Never]` to detect missing arguments. This level of detail is exactly what is needed to support the Prisma-style or FastAPI-style transformations described by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov without requiring you to write a single line of plugin code.

hey nanochat, I have been reading through PEP 827 (Type Manipulation). To be honest, adding 'Type Booleans' feels like a massive leap toward making Python's type system undecidably complex just to chase TypeScript. Isn't this just inviting a mess for type checker implementers?

It is a valid concern. The authors, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, acknowledge that these features add complexity. However, the motivation is to bridge the gap between Python's highly dynamic metaprogramming and its static type system. Currently, libraries often resort to custom mypy plugins because the type system cannot model their transformations. Type booleans are the logic gate for 'conditional types', which allow the type system to react to the structure of code rather than just its nominal tags.
But what actually constitutes a 'type boolean' in this spec? If I can just write 'and' and 'or' in a type expression, how is that not just value-level code leaking into the type layer?

PEP 827 defines type booleans as a specific subset of the type language. They are boolean compositions of specific operators like 'IsAssignable[T, S]' or 'IsEquivalent[T, S]', combined with 'and', 'or', 'not', 'all', and 'any'. When evaluated, they resolve to 'Literal[True]' or 'Literal[False]'. For example, you might see 'IsAssignable[T, list]' used inside a conditional type expression: 'true_type if IsAssignable[T, list] else false_type'. This doesn't change Python's grammar; it just expands what a type checker or a runtime evaluator considers a valid type expression.
I'm skeptical of 'IsAssignable'. Assignability in a gradual system is famously tricky. If you have unions, does it just give up?

Actually, PEP 827 specifies that these operators are 'lifted' over unions. This means if you check 'IsAssignable[A | B, C]', the system takes the cross product of the union elements and evaluates the operator for each, then unions the results. The goal is consistency—the gradual typing term for subtyping. It also introduces 'Bool[T]', which returns 'Literal[True]' if 'T' is assignable to 'Literal[True]' and not to 'Never'. This allows the system to drive conditional branches based on complex type-level logic, such as checking if a member exists via 'GetMember' before deciding a return type.

Wait, the PEP also mentions 'all()' and 'any()' for comprehensions. That sounds like a loop. If we can loop over members and check conditions, aren't we just building a mini-interpreter inside the type checker?

In a sense, yes. The 'all' and 'any' operators take a comprehension that iterates over something like 'Iter[Members[T]]'. For example, you could check if 'all' members of a class are assignable to a specific type. While this adds overhead, the PEP restricts this to 'unpacked comprehensions' using a specific syntax: '*[... for t in Iter[iter_ty]]'. It aims for a deterministic evaluation that both static type checkers and runtime libraries (like Pydantic or FastAPI) can follow. The 'Type Booleans' are essentially the decision-making engine that makes these iterations useful for filtering or transforming types, like the 'Omit' or 'Pick' utility types mentioned in the proposal.

I'm looking at how PEP 827 can generate __init__ methods for custom dataclass-like structures. I noticed the GenericCallable operator in the specification, but it says it is restricted to being used as a type argument for Member. Why is it limited like that? I saw a mention of 'rank-N types' and undecidability in the rationale but I'm not sure how that applies here.

The restriction of GenericCallable to the type argument of Member is a deliberate choice by authors Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov to avoid 'cans of worms' in the type system. Specifically, allowing generics to be bound in deeply nested positions (rank-N types) or allowing type variables to be instantiated with other generic types (impredicative polymorphism) often leads to undecidable type inference. The PEP cites research by Hans Boehm and Frank Pfenning to support this concern. In the context of your dataclass project, GenericCallable is necessary because methods often have their own type variables bound at the method level rather than the class level, such as 'def func[T](self, x: T)'. Using the form GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty] allows the system to represent these generic methods within a NewProtocol while using a lambda to delay evaluation. By confining this power to Member, the proposal provides the expressiveness needed for method generation without making the entire Python type system undecidable or overly complex for type checkers to implement.

I just looked at PEP 827 and it feels like we are just porting TypeScript's utility types into Python for no reason. Why do we need things like 'Pick', 'Omit', or 'KeyOf'? It makes the type system feel bloated and overly complex for a language that is supposed to be simple.

It is true that PEP 827 is heavily inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types, as noted by the authors Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. However, the motivation is to bridge the gap between Python's complex metaprogramming and the static type system. Currently, frameworks like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs require custom typechecker plugins (like those for mypy) to provide accurate typing, which lacks consistency across IDEs. PEP 827 introduces primitives like 'typing.Members' and 'typing.NewProtocol' so these utility types can be defined directly in Python code. For example, 'KeyOf[T]' is implemented as 'Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]' using the new unpacked comprehension syntax. This allows library authors to define transformations like 'Pick' or 'Partial' once, ensuring consistent typechecking in any tool without specialized plugins. The proposal is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15.

I've been looking at PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and the team. It feels like a massive leap over the special-casing we got in PEP 681 for dataclass transforms. I'm trying to understand the union processing logic. Specifically, what is the role of FromUnion and how does it actually behave when I pass it a standard pipe-syntax union?

FromUnion[T] is a core operator designed to deconstruct unions so they can be processed by the new comprehension logic. If you pass it a union like 'int | str', it returns a tuple containing those specific elements: 'tuple[int, str]'. If the type isn't a union, it just returns a 1-ary tuple containing that type. Its primary purpose is to feed into 'typing.Iter', allowing you to write comprehensions that iterate over the individual members of a union. This is a huge shift from the current system where unions are often opaque blocks to the type system at rest.

That sounds similar to how we handle TypeVarTuple in PEP 646, but applied to the components of a type itself. But the PEP also mentions 'Lifting over Unions' as a default behavior for many operators. If operators already 'lift'—meaning they apply to each member of the union automatically—why do I need FromUnion to explicitly turn a union into a tuple for iteration?

That is a sharp distinction. Lifting is automatic: if you use an operator like 'Concat' or 'GetArg' on a union, the system automatically computes the cross-product of the union elements and returns a new union of the results. However, 'FromUnion' is for when you want to perform logic *based* on the union members themselves using the new comprehension syntax, like filtering them out or transforming them into a different structure entirely. For example, if you want to implement something like TypeScript's 'Exclude', you need to explicitly iterate over the union members, check a condition like 'IsAssignable', and then repackage them. Lifting doesn't allow for that kind of 'if' filtering inside the type expression.

I see. So for something like PEP 692 or the proposed PEP 764 for inline TypedDicts, I could use FromUnion to strip specific keys. If I have 'type T = Literal['a'] | Literal['b'] | Literal['c']', how would 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov's proposal have me use FromUnion to create a utility type like 'Exclude'?

In the PEP 827 specification, 'Exclude[T, U]' is implemented by iterating over 'FromUnion[T]'. It looks like this: 'type Exclude[T, U] = Union[*[x for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]]]'. Here, 'FromUnion' breaks the union 'T' into a tuple, 'Iter' makes that tuple iterable for the comprehension, and 'IsAssignable' handles the conditional check. This is far more flexible than the hardcoded logic in PEP 681. It essentially brings the 'metaprogramming' into the type system itself, targeting Python 3.15 as a Draft standard.
Hey, I saw some buzz on Twitter about PEP 827 and it sounded like magic. Can you tell me what it is in plain English? Also, who are the people behind it and where do they work?

PEP 827 is titled "Type Manipulation" and it's currently a Draft proposal for Python 3.15. In plain English, it aims to give Python's type system the ability to "calculate" new types based on existing ones. This is inspired by features in TypeScript. It helps bridge the gap between dynamic metaprogramming (where code generates other code) and static type checking.

The authors of the proposal are Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. According to the PEP, they are all associated with Vercel (based on their provided email addresses).
"Mad Emperor" Yury? That is a bold nickname! And what do you mean by "calculating" types? Like, if I have a class, can it create a different version of that class automatically?

Exactly! That is one of the core use cases. For instance, the authors mention that frameworks like FastAPI often require you to write multiple versions of the same model (like one for creating a user and one for displaying a user). 

With PEP 827, instead of writing those manually, you could use new operators like Members[T] to inspect your main class and NewProtocol to construct a "Public" or "Update" version automatically. It uses logic like "if/else" and loops inside the type annotations themselves to decide which fields to include. Since it's still a Draft, the authors are currently testing these ideas with a proof-of-concept in mypy and a runtime evaluator library.

Hey nanochat! I'm doing a deep dive into Python's type system for a seminar. I've been looking at PEP 827, and it seems like a massive shift. Since it adds 'if' and 'for' logic into type hints, doesn't that require a huge overhaul of the Python grammar and a specialized bytecode for the __annotate__ method? It feels like we're basically putting a full logic engine inside the class definition.

It is definitely a powerful proposal, but one of the key design constraints mentioned by authors Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov is that it requires no changes to the Python grammar. It reuses existing expression syntax. For instance, the conditional type 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' is already valid Python syntax. The magic isn't in a new bytecode, but in how the typing module handles these expressions when the __annotate__ method is executed.

But if __annotate__ just executes standard Python, how does it handle things like 'IsAssignable' or iterating over class members? If I just run that at runtime, wouldn't 'IsAssignable[int, str]' just be a generic alias object? It doesn't actually 'know' it's false unless there's a type checker running, right?
