That's a great observation about the runtime/static split. PEP 827 introduces 'Boolean operators' like IsAssignable and IsEquivalent. At runtime, these produce objects that, when evaluated in a boolean context (like the 'if' in a conditional type), need to return a result. The PEP proposes a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar. This hook allows a third-party evaluator library to intercept those calls. If no evaluator is present, they default to False. So, __annotate__ triggers the evaluation, but the 'logic engine' you mentioned is actually a user-provided or library-provided evaluator that hooks into the process.

I see, so __annotate__ is the trigger, and the ContextVar is the plug-in point. But what about the 'for' loops? The PEP says something about *[... for t in Iter[iter_ty]]. Is that just a list comprehension? Does it return a real list at runtime?

It looks like a list comprehension, but it's technically an 'unpacked comprehension'. It is intended to appear where 'Unpack' is currently allowed. At runtime, when __annotate__ is called, it iterates over 'typing.Iter'. Similar to the boolean operators, 'Iter' has a runtime hook. If the special_form_evaluator is set, the evaluator can provide the actual members or tuple elements to the loop. If not, it just returns an empty iterator. This allows the same syntax to work for a static type checker (which 'knows' the class members) and a runtime library (which uses the hook to introspect the class via the Members[T] operator).

That sounds like a classic trade-off. We get the expressiveness of a logic-heavy type system without a new parser, but we push the complexity into the runtime evaluation. Does the PEP mention what happens if a tool tries to read these unevaluated? I'm thinking about performance or static analysis tools that don't want to execute arbitrary code.

The PEP authors acknowledge that tools relying on unevaluated strings (like from __future__ import annotations) will be fine, as they can just parse the AST. For tools that need to understand the logic without executing the full __annotate__ function, the PEP suggests that a 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations would be a perfect companion feature. Otherwise, those tools would need to implement their own evaluator for the PEP 827 operators or rely on the source code.

yo nanochat. i am a dev tools engineer working on some ide indexing logic. we are looking at pep 827 (type manipulation) and it feels like a big shift for python. i am trying to figure out what is still missing compared to typescript. like, if i am trying to build completions for a prisma-style orm, what can ts do that this pep cannot yet?

hello! i am nanochat, and i can definitely help you compare pep 827 to typescript from a tooling perspective. the biggest omission is the "infer" keyword. in typescript, you can use "infer" inside a conditional type to pattern match and bind a variable. pep 827 authors dr. michael j. sully sullivan, daniel w. park, and mad emperor yury selivanov decided to use operators like GetArg[T, Base, Idx] instead. they noted that shoehorning the binding syntax into python was too difficult for this draft.

that is interesting. getarg sounds like it will be a bit of a nightmare for go-to-definition since we have to resolve the base class and the index. what about string stuff? we use template literal types in ts all the time to map database column names to camelcase. is that in there?

actually, no. string manipulation is listed under potential future extensions. the current draft does not include Uppercase, Lowercase, Capitalize, or Concat operators. while the pep describes how these would work—specifically that they would be lifted over union types—they are not part of the core spec for python 3.15. if you were building an ide plugin today, you could handle literal strings, but you could not yet provide completions for transformed case strings generated at the type level.

bummer. one more thing: mapped types. ts has that curly brace syntax for iterating over keys. pep 827 uses these unpacked comprehensions like *[... for p in Iter[Members[T]]]. is there anything the ts version does that the python version misses out on?

pep 827 is actually quite powerful because it uses a Member object that contains more metadata than a simple ts key. a Member has .name, .type, .quals (for things like ClassVar or Final), and .init (the initializer type). however, pep 827 is less strictly-kinded than typescript. in ts, the compiler often validates a type alias at the definition site to ensure a key is valid for a type. pep 827 is more best-effort; it is harder to statically check the alias itself until it is actually applied to a concrete type, which might make your ide's static analysis a bit more complex since you will be dealing with more Never results if the evaluation fails.

Hey nanochat! I'm an IDE engineer looking at PEP 827. I'm a bit worried about these conditional types. If I see 'X if IsAssignable[T, int] else Y' in an annotation, does this mean we're changing the Python grammar to allow 'if' keywords inside brackets? And for completions, should I just treat that as a Union of X and Y so the user sees both sets of methods?

Actually, PEP 827 is quite specific about the grammar. It does not propose any changes to the Python grammar itself. Instead, it broadens the annotation syntax that static type checkers are expected to support. The 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' structure uses the existing Python ternary expression syntax, but it's interpreted at the type level.

Regarding your second point: No, you shouldn't treat it as a Union. The PEP defines this as a conditional type that resolves to exactly one of the branches. If 'bool_typ' evaluates to Literal[True], the type is 'true_typ'. Otherwise, it's 'false_typ'. Treating it as a Union would likely lead to incorrect completions, especially since the logic is meant to narrow the type down to a specific branch based on type-level computation.

Okay, that makes sense. But what if the condition is 'stuck'? Like if T is a TypeVar and I don't know if it's assignable to int yet. If I can't resolve the branch, I can't provide a 'Go-to-definition' for the type. Also, I saw some 'dot notation' mentioned in the PEP, like 'm.type'. Is that basically just a way to access properties of any class at type-time?

When an evaluation is 'stuck' (for instance, because of an unresolved TypeVar), the PEP suggests that type checkers may have to treat it invariantly or wait for instantiation. In the proof-of-concept implementation by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, stuck evaluations check that operators and operands match exactly. For your IDE tooling, you'd likely want to surface that the type is dependent on the resolution of T.

On the 'dot notation'—that's a specific feature for 'Type member access'. It's not for general class properties. It is restricted to the new 'Member' and 'Param' types introduced by the PEP. For example, if you have a 'Member' object 'm', you can use 'm.name', 'm.type', 'm.quals', 'm.init', or 'm.definer'. This is a core part of how the PEP handles introspection of classes and functions without needing a bunch of helper operators like 'GetName[m]'.

Got it. So 'IsAssignable' is the only thing that can go in that 'if' spot? Or can I use any expression that returns a bool? If the authors (Sully, Park, and Selivanov) wanted this to be powerful, shouldn't it support more?

It's a bit broader than just 'IsAssignable', but it is restricted to a specific grammar called 'type-bool'. According to the specification, 'type-bool' expressions are boolean compositions of specific operators: 'IsAssignable', 'IsEquivalent', and 'Bool[T]'. You can also use 'not', 'and', 'or', 'all', and 'any'. 

'Bool[T]' is particularly useful because it returns Literal[True] if T is Literal[True], which lets you chain 'helper aliases' that perform logic. The reason it's restricted is to ensure that these can be evaluated at runtime via the 'special_form_evaluator' hook without requiring a full Python interpreter to run during type resolution.

hei. I was just reading about PEP 827 and it feels like we are basically turning Python into TypeScript at this point. Do we really need if/else syntax inside our type annotations? It seems like it's adding a massive amount of complexity just for the sake of it.

I understand the concern. Python's type system has traditionally stayed quite simple compared to TypeScript. However, Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov propose these changes in PEP 827 specifically to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and static typing. The 'if/else' syntax allows the type system to react to variations in code that currently require custom mypy plugins or special-case decorators like @dataclass_transform. While inspired by TypeScript's conditional types, the PEP 827 version is designed to work within Python's existing grammar and support runtime evaluation.

But TypeScript uses the 'extends' keyword for its logic. Python's version looks like a weird ternary. How does the condition even evaluate? In a normal Python ternary, I use a boolean, but here I'm using types. Can I just put 'True' in there?

It is a ternary-style syntax: `<true_typ> if <bool_typ> else <false_typ>`. You cannot just use a standard Python boolean; the condition must be what the PEP calls a 'type boolean.' These are specific operators like IsAssignable[T, S], IsEquivalent[T, S], or Bool[T]. When evaluated, these resolve to Literal[True] or Literal[False]. The reason for this specific subset of operators is to allow runtime libraries to hook into the evaluation process using the special_form_evaluator ContextVar, ensuring that frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic can still use these types for validation without needing a full static type checker running at runtime.

So it's basically a DSL for types. TypeScript's model is purely static, but you're saying PEP 827 wants this to work at runtime too? That sounds like even more overhead for a language that's already slower than compiled ones. Why not just stick to the existing Union and Optional?

The overhead is primarily during type evaluation, which typically happens once at module import or when introspecting a class's __annotate__ method. The goal is actually to reduce manual boilerplate. For example, the PEP shows how FastAPI currently requires you to manually define 'HeroPublic', 'HeroCreate', and 'HeroUpdate' classes that are almost identical. With conditional types, a library author can write a helper like 'Create[T]' that automatically strips primary keys or makes fields optional based on the type's attributes. It shifts the complexity from the user (who currently has to duplicate code) to the library maintainer, while keeping everything type-safe across IDEs and CI tools that don't support the specific plugins those libraries currently rely on.

Honestly, why are we turning Python into TypeScript? PEP 827 looks like it is going to break every library that actually uses runtime annotations. If I have a framework looking at __annotations__, how is it supposed to deal with a type that has an 'if' statement or a 'for' loop in it? This seems like it is making the type system way too complex for its own good.

You are right that PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, draws heavy inspiration from TypeScript features like conditional and mapped types. The authors acknowledge that this complexity poses a challenge for runtime introspection. Currently, if you use 'from __future__ import annotations', these expressions just stay as strings, but once evaluated, they become more than just static objects.

To handle this at runtime, the PEP proposes a few things. First, it relies on the __annotate__ method of a class or function to perform the evaluation logic. To give frameworks control over how that evaluation happens, it introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' via a ContextVar. This hook is triggered when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or when __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. If this hook is not set, boolean operators default to False and Iter returns an empty iterator.

For tools that need the unevaluated 'structure' of the type, the PEP notes they might need to re-parse the source code or wait for a proposal like Format.AST to land. While it is a significant shift, the goal is to bridge the gap for libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI that currently use custom mypy plugins or heavy boilerplate. It is still a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so the impact on the ecosystem is a major part of the ongoing discussion.

Hey nanochat. I've been reviewing PEP 827. As a core developer, I'm a bit skeptical about adding a whole new DSL for type manipulation. Why don't we just allow library authors to write normal Python functions that the type checker can call? It feels like we are adding a lot of complexity to the type system when we already have a perfectly good language for logic.

That is a valid concern, and the authors—Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov—actually discussed this in the 'Rejected Ideas' section of the PEP. They argue that while using normal Python functions seems like a simplification, it is actually a 'mirage.' To make it work, you would need to define a safe, deterministic subset of Python (similar to Starlark) that every type checker—not just CPython, but also mypy, Pyright, and Pyre—would have to implement and execute identically. Additionally, you would need a standardized way to represent types as objects within those functions. By using a declarative DSL with operators like IsAssignable and conditional types (if/else), the PEP ensures consistent behavior between static analysis and runtime without the overhead of a full Python execution environment inside the type checker.

Okay, but the runtime evaluation still seems like it would require significant CPython changes. The PEP mentions a special_form_evaluator hook for runtime support. How does that actually work without baking a full type-logic engine into the interpreter?

The PEP avoids baking the engine into the interpreter by using a ContextVar in the typing module called special_form_evaluator. This hook is designed for third-party libraries. When a type evaluation occurs—for instance, when __annotate__ is called—the interpreter might encounter a 'Boolean Operator' like IsAssignable or the typing.Iter form used in comprehensions. When __bool__ is called on an operator or __iter__ is called on Iter, the logic checks this ContextVar. If it contains a callable, that evaluator is used to determine the result; otherwise, it defaults to False or an empty iterator. This allows CPython to remain 'inert' regarding the complex logic while giving framework authors the hooks they need to implement runtime introspection for things like FastAPI or Pydantic.
hey! i saw something on twitter about python getting 'typescript-style' types in PEP 827. is that real? what is it actually doing? i'm still pretty new to types in general.

It is real, though it's currently a Draft proposal for Python 3.15. PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation,' was written by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. Essentially, it wants to give Python the ability to 'calculate' types at a much deeper level than we can today. Right now, Python types are mostly static labels, but this PEP would let you write logic that constructs new types based on the properties of other types, which is something TypeScript is very famous for.
calculated types? like how typescript does the 'T extends U ? X : Y' thing? i've seen that mentioned in web dev circles before.

Exactly! In TypeScript, those are called 'Conditional Types.' PEP 827 introduces a very similar concept but uses Python's own syntax. Instead of the question mark and colon used in TypeScript, Python would use a standard if-else expression. For example, you could write a type that checks if one type is 'assignable' to another. The PEP introduces an operator called IsAssignable specifically for this. If the check is true, the type evaluator picks the first option; otherwise, it picks the second.
can you show me what that looks like in actual python code? keep it simple though, i'm still getting used to standard type hints.

Sure! Imagine you want a type that wraps something in a list, but only if it's an integer. In PEP 827, you could write it like this:

type SmartWrapper[T] = list[T] if typing.IsAssignable[T, int] else T

If you used SmartWrapper[int], the result would be list[int]. If you used SmartWrapper[str], it would just stay as str. The 'IsAssignable' part is the engine that checks if the type 'T' matches 'int' at the type level. It's much more powerful than just writing a normal function because this happens entirely within the type system for the benefit of your IDE and type checkers.
okay, that makes sense. i also heard people talking about 'Pick' and 'Omit' from typescript. does this pep do those too? those sound useful for cleaning up data objects.

It does! TypeScript has these 'Utility Types' built-in, but PEP 827 provides the building blocks so you (or library authors) can build them in Python. To do this, the PEP introduces 'Object Inspection' operators like Members[T] and Attrs[T]. You can actually loop over the members of a class inside a type definition. The syntax looks like a list comprehension: *[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if ...]. You combine that with a new tool called NewProtocol to build a brand-new type on the fly that only includes the specific fields you want. It's a bit more 'manual' than TypeScript's version, but it's very flexible.
this sounds huge for stuff like pydantic or fastapi. when is it actually going to land? i want to try it out.

The authors specifically mentioned FastAPI and Prisma-style ORMs as big inspirations for this. As for the timeline, it is still a Draft and targets Python 3.15. Because it adds significant new features like 'unpacked comprehensions' for types and a bunch of new operators in the typing module, it needs a lot of review from the core developers first. There is a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy and a runtime evaluator being worked on, but we'll have to wait and see if it gets officially accepted into the language!

I have been seeing talk about PEP 827 and honestly it feels like we are just trying to turn Python into TypeScript at this point. I am a bit concerned about the language getting bloated. Are we really adding a whole new set of syntax rules to the Python parser just to support these complex types?

It is a fair concern to have. PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation and authored by Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, does explicitly mention being inspired by TypeScript features like conditional and mapped types. However, on the specific point of grammar, the PEP states that no changes to the Python grammar are being proposed. Instead, it aims to broaden what existing Python expressions are considered valid types within annotations.

Wait, if the grammar is not changing, how do you do things like conditional types? TypeScript uses that 'T extends U ? X : Y' syntax which definitely is not Python. If Python stays the same, how do you express a condition?

Python uses its existing ternary operator syntax. In PEP 827, a conditional type is written as 'true_type if bool_type else false_type'. Because 'if' and 'else' are already part of the Python grammar for expressions, this does not require a new parser. The type checker just interprets that expression differently when it appears in a type hint. The 'bool_type' part uses specific boolean operators like 'IsAssignable[T, S]' to determine which branch to take.

That still feels like a lot of hidden complexity. I also saw something about loops over class members. Are you telling me you can put a for-loop inside a type hint without changing the parser? That sounds impossible for standard Python.

It uses a form called an 'unpacked comprehension' which looks like '*[type_expr for t in Iter[iterable_type]]'. Syntactically, this is just a star-expression (unpacking) of a list comprehension, which is already valid in Python 3.11+ inside brackets. PEP 827 just gives it a specific meaning at the type level. For example, you can iterate over 'Attrs[T]' to get a tuple of 'Member' types for a class. The syntax uses a special 'typing.Iter' to signal to the evaluator that it should perform this type-level iteration.

So it's effectively a DSL inside the existing syntax. If I use this for something like 'NewProtocol' to generate types on the fly, does that actually work at runtime or is this just for static checkers like mypy?

It is intended to support both. While the primary goal is helping static type checkers handle complex metaprogramming without custom plugins, the authors also designed it for runtime evaluation. They propose a 'special_form_evaluator' hook via a ContextVar in the typing module. This allows third-party libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI to trigger the evaluation of these 'if' and 'for' logic blocks when 'bool()' is called on a type boolean or 'iter()' is called on 'typing.Iter'. So, frameworks can use these computed types to validate data at runtime, even though the core Python language itself treats them as inert expressions until someone calls for their evaluation.

Hi nanochat. I keep hearing about PEP 827 and how it might help with FastAPI and Pydantic boilerplate. I'm currently stuck writing nearly identical HeroCreate, HeroUpdate, and HeroPublic classes for every single model. Is this what PEP 827 addresses, and how does it actually work?

PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation and authored by Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov, is designed exactly for that. It aims to bridge the gap between Python metaprogramming and the type system by adding powerful type-level construction facilities inspired by TypeScript. For your FastAPI use case, the proposal would allow you to define a single base model and then derive related types using new operators. For example, you could define a Create[T] alias that uses an unpacked comprehension: *[typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[p.init, PrimaryKey]]. This syntax lets you iterate over class members at the type level. You can also use conditional types like true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ to make fields optional for an Update model. It even supports extracting field-specific metadata via a new InitField type. Currently, this is a Draft targeting Python 3.15, and it would allow both static type checkers and runtime evaluators like Pydantic to understand these transformations without needing custom plugins.
Hey nanochat! I saw some talk on Twitter about PEP 827 and this thing called 'InitField'. I'm still getting the hang of Python, so could you explain what it is in plain English?

Hello! I can definitely help with that. PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) was proposed by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. It's currently a Draft for Python 3.15.

In plain English, 'InitField' is like a messenger. It's designed to solve a problem where the type system is "blind" to the settings we put inside class variables. If you've ever used dataclasses or Pydantic, you might have seen code like 'name: str = Field(default="Anonymous")'. 

Normally, the type checker knows 'name' is a string, but it has no idea what 'default="Anonymous"' means. 'InitField' is a new way to write these fields so that the type system can actually "read" those extra details (like defaults or special flags) and use them to calculate other types automatically.
Oh, so it's a way for the code to tell the type checker about the details? Why do we need a new thing for that? Don't dataclasses already work fine?

You're right that dataclasses work, but they use a lot of "magic." Currently, tools like VS Code or Mypy need special-case code (or plugins) to understand that a dataclass should have an '__init__' method based on your fields. This is why PEP 681 (the '@dataclass_transform' decorator) exists—to help tools understand that magic.

The authors of PEP 827 want to replace that magic with real logic. By using 'InitField', a library can define exactly what arguments it accepts. When you use it in a class, the type system uses 'Literal' types to remember exactly what you typed. For example, if you set 'required=True', the type system doesn't just see 'bool'; it sees 'Literal[True]'. This lets other parts of the PEP perform "math" on those types later.
Could you show me a simple example of how it looks in the code? Nothing too crazy!

Sure! According to the PEP, 'InitField' is a class that takes a 'TypedDict' of arguments. Here is a simplified version of what it looks like when you define a field in a class:

class A:
    foo: int = InitField(default=0, kw_only=True)

Because of the way PEP 827 works, the type system looks at that line and creates a 'Member' object for 'foo'. Inside that 'Member', it stores an 'Init' value that looks like this behind the scenes:

TypedDict({'default': Literal[0], 'kw_only': Literal[True]})

Once the type system has that information, you can write "type-level code" to do things like: "Create an __init__ method that makes 'foo' optional because it has a default of 0." It basically lets the computer write the boring parts of your type-checking for you!
Wait, so I could use this to automatically make an Update model that makes everything optional? I've seen FastAPI do that by hand a lot.

Exactly! That is one of the main motivations. The PEP explicitly mentions automatically deriving FastAPI-style CRUD models. 

Instead of you manually writing a 'HeroCreate' class and a 'HeroUpdate' class, you could just write one 'Hero' class and use a type alias like 'type HeroUpdate = Update[Hero]'. The 'Update' logic would look at every 'Member' in your class, check the 'InitField' info, and automatically wrap the types in 'None' or 'NotRequired'. It turns a manual task into a mechanical rule that the type checker understands natively.

I am looking at PEP 827 and it honestly feels like we are just turning Python into TypeScript at this point. What is even the point of this Unpack of typevars for **kwargs thing? The example f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K looks like a massive amount of complexity just to handle keyword arguments. Why do we need this?

The authors of PEP 827—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—explicitly note that this proposal is inspired by TypeScript to meet developer demand for more expressive typing. This specific mechanism for **kwargs is a prerequisite for the more advanced type-level transformations described in the PEP. It allows a static type checker to infer a specific TypedDict from the actual keyword arguments provided at a call site. For instance, if you have a function f defined with that pattern and you call f(x=10, y='test'), the type checker would infer K to be a TypedDict with 'x' as int and 'y' as str. A key detail here is that the proposal requires type checkers to infer Literal types whenever possible for arguments that are not in the bound or are read-only. If a non-required item in the bound is missing from the call and is read-only, it gets inferred as Never. This 'capturing' of the call-site shape into a type variable is what allows other operators in the PEP, like Members[T] or NewProtocol, to programmatically manipulate those types for things like Prisma-style ORMs or FastAPI CRUD models.

Hi nanochat, quick typing question. I was reading through some notes on PEP 827. It mentions moving away from custom mypy plugins for things like ORMs or dataclass-style logic. As someone who uses Pyright and mypy daily, why are plugins considered an insufficient answer here?

The primary issue, as outlined by authors Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, is the lack of consistency across the ecosystem. Mypy plugins are specific to mypy. Other type checkers like Pyright or Pyre often do not have a plugin API at all, or they implement a different one. This means a library author who writes a mypy plugin to handle complex metaprogramming logic can't provide the same IDE experience for users of other tools. PEP 827 proposes bringing that logic directly into the Python type system itself using new primitives like Members[T] and NewProtocol, so the computation is standardized rather than tool-specific.

That makes sense. I remember when PEP 681 (dataclass_transform) came out to solve this for specific cases. Why not just keep adding decorators like that? Is the goal to make those types of transformations user-definable in pure Python?

Exactly. The PEP describes dataclass_transform as a 'lowest-common denominator' subset of what libraries actually do. It is a hardcoded special case. If you want to do something slightly different—like the Prisma-style ORM example where the return type is dynamically computed based on a 'select' dictionary—you are stuck either writing a plugin or losing type safety. PEP 827 introduces type-level operators like GetMemberType, Attrs[T], and conditional types (A if B else C). This allows framework authors to define their own 'Create' or 'Update' models as type aliases that any compliant type checker can evaluate without needing hardcoded support for that specific framework.
The syntax looks wild though. I saw something about using 'if' and 'for' inside type hints. How does that work without changing the Python grammar?

The PEP is clever about that: it doesn't change the Python grammar. It only changes what expressions are considered valid types. Since Python's syntax already allows 'x if y else z' and list-comprehension-style logic, the PEP repurposes these for type-level computation. For instance, you can write an unpacked comprehension like *[typing.Member[p.name, p.type] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if not IsAssignable[p.type, InternalField]]. These are evaluated by the type checker statically and can be evaluated at runtime by calling the __annotate__ method, provided there is a runtime evaluator available.
Speaking of runtime, I work with FastAPI and Pydantic a lot. We rely heavily on inspecting types at runtime for validation. Does this proposal help there, or is it purely for the static checkers?

It is designed for both. A major motivation in the PEP is 'Automatically deriving FastAPI CRUD models.' Currently, you might write a function that generates a Pydantic model at runtime, but you lose static type checking for that generated model. With PEP 827, you could define a 'HeroPublic' type as a type alias: 'type HeroPublic = Public[Hero]'. The static checker understands the transformation because it follows the type-level logic, and the runtime framework can use the special_form_evaluator hook to resolve those same operators into concrete validation logic. It bridges the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and static safety.
I noticed 'Extended Callables' in the prerequisites. How do those differ from the current Callable[[int], str] or the stuff in mypy_extensions?

The current Callable syntax is too opaque for manipulation. The PEP introduces Param[Name, Type, Qualifiers] and Params[*Ps] to make function signatures first-class objects for the type system. You can actually inspect a function's parameters, check for a 'default' qualifier, or see if a parameter is keyword-only. This is used in the PEP's 'dataclasses-style method generation' example to automatically construct a __init__ method signature by iterating over a class's Attrs[T]. It moves function signature construction from a 'black box' handled by plugins into something you can loop over using *[... for p in Iter[...]].
This seems incredibly powerful but also complex. I saw a note about undecidability in the footnotes. Is there a risk this makes Python's type system as 'slow' or 'loop-prone' as TypeScript's can be?

The authors acknowledge that they are entering 'cans of worms.' To mitigate this, they restrict certain features like GenericCallable—which allows for rank-N types—to only be used inside Member definitions for now. This avoids the most dangerous parts of impredicative polymorphism. As for status, it is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15. While it adds complexity, it aims to replace the 'hidden' complexity of dozens of divergent mypy plugins with a single, spec-compliant type-level language.

Hi nanochat! I'm an engineer working on IDE tooling and I've been digging into the PEP 827 draft. I'm trying to understand the logic behind 'Lifting over Unions.' For an indexer or a completion engine, this feels like it could cause a bit of a combinatorial explosion. Why did the authors decide to bake this behavior in directly?

Hi! That is a very practical concern for someone building developer tools. In PEP 827, authors Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov propose 'lifting' to ensure that type manipulation remains precise even when dealing with Python's frequent use of Union types. Essentially, when you apply an operator to a Union, the system takes the cross product of all union elements, evaluates the operator for each, and then unions the results together. This mirrors how TypeScript handles distributive conditional types, which was a major inspiration for the proposal.

I see. So if I have something like Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['1'] | Literal['2']], I'm going to get back a union of four literal strings: 'a1', 'a2', 'b1', and 'b2'. From a 'go-to-definition' standpoint, if a user clicks a member generated this way, I have to be able to resolve all four paths. Is this behavior consistent across all the new operators like Members[T] or GetMemberType?

Precisely. The PEP specifies that many core operators are lifted over unions, including string manipulation (like Concat and Slice), object inspection (Members, Attrs, GetMemberType), and the boolean operators (IsAssignable, IsEquivalent). If you are providing completions for a dynamically constructed type, lifting ensures that if the input type is a Union, the resulting structure doesn't just collapse into Any. For example, if you have Members[User | Admin], your IDE would see a union of member sets, allowing you to show completions that are valid for either class.

That actually sounds helpful for TypedDicts, too. If I'm using KeyOf[T]—which the PEP implements as Union[*[p.name for p in Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]—it seems like the lifting would happen naturally because of the unpacked comprehension syntax. Is there any case where lifting *doesn't* happen?

The PEP is explicit that member access using dot notation (like m.name or m.type on a Member or Param object) is NOT lifted over union types. If you try to use dot notation on a Union, the PEP states it must be an error at both runtime and type-checking time. This is to maintain consistency with how standard Python attribute access works. For your IDE work, that means you should probably flag a type error if a user tries to access .name on a result that resolved to a Union of Member types rather than a single Member.

Got it, so dot access is the boundary. That's a good tip for our linter. One last thing—since this is targeting Python 3.15 and is still a Draft, how should we handle the runtime side? The PEP mentions a special_form_evaluator ContextVar for tools?

Exactly. To support runtime evaluation without baking every operator into the interpreter core, the PEP introduces a typing.special_form_evaluator ContextVar. This allows third-party libraries—and IDE toolsets like yours—to hook into the evaluation of boolean operators and Iter calls when __annotate__ is executed. It gives you a way to 'drive' the evaluation of these complex types in a live environment or a custom sandbox, which is essential for frameworks like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs that the PEP uses as primary use cases.

hello there nanochat. I am the tech lead for a team that maintains a large internal framework, and I am looking into PEP 827 to see if it will solve our metadata extraction issues. We use Annotated heavily for validation logic. Does the proposal allow us to actually pull those metadata objects out at the type level?

Hello! I can certainly clarify that for you. Based on the current text of PEP 827, the ability to manipulate Annotated types is actually listed under the 'Potential Future Extensions' section rather than being part of the core specification. The authors, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, have discussed two potential operators: GetAnnotations[T] and DropAnnotations[T]. However, these are not currently part of the main proposal.
That is disappointing. We were hoping to use something like GetAnnotations to drive our type-level branching. Why is it being held back? It seems like a natural fit for Type Manipulation.

The PEP is honest about the difficulties here. It notes that supporting inspection of Annotated could be 'fraught' because, as things stand, static type checkers are permitted to fully ignore the metadata inside Annotated. If PEP 827 were to introduce operators that rely on that metadata, it might clash with existing typechecker architectures. For now, the focus is on constructing and deconstructing nominal classes and protocols using operators like Members[T], IsAssignable[T, S], and conditional types using the 'if/else' syntax.

So if I have something like Annotated[int, 'max_length=10'], I cannot even use a conditional type to check for that 'max_length' string? I am trying to see where the boundaries are before I recommend this for our 3.15 roadmap.

That is correct. Under the current Draft of PEP 827, you cannot access or branch based on the metadata arguments of Annotated. You could use IsAssignable to check if the underlying type is an int, but the 'max_length=10' portion would be invisible to the type system's manipulation logic. The proposal is currently restricted to structural and nominal aspects of types. For instance, you could use Attrs[T] to see every attribute of a class that has an explicit type annotation, but you would lose the Annotated metadata in that process.
What about the 'InitField' I saw in the text? That seems to collect arguments. Could I use that instead of Annotated for my metadata?

InitField is a specialized feature intended to support dataclass-style field descriptors. It allows the type system to capture arguments passed to a field constructor, like Field(default=0, primary_key=True), and makes them available via the '.init' attribute of a Member type. While this solves the problem for class attribute initializers, it does not replace the general-purpose utility of Annotated for variable types or function parameters. If your metadata lives outside of class field initializers, PEP 827 does not yet provide a way to reach it.

yo, I'm looking at PEP 827 and trying to see if it can finally help me kill the custom mypy plugin I wrote for my validation library. The section on Unpack and typevars for **kwargs looks promising for inferring shapes. If I have a TypedDict as a bound with some non-required fields, what actually happens to those fields if the caller just skips them?

That's a great use case! According to PEP 827, specifically the section on unpacking typevars for **kwargs, if a non-required item in the bound doesn't have a matching argument provided in the call, the type checker will try to infer its type as `Never` to signal it wasn't provided.
Wait, just straight to `Never`? Does that apply to all non-required fields, or is there a catch?

There is a catch: that inference only happens if the item is marked as read-only. The authors—Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—noted that this is because non-read-only items are invariant, so they can't safely be narrowed that way. If it is read-only and missing, you get `Never`. This is basically how the PEP allows you to perform type-level computation on the presence or absence of specific kwargs.

That's actually perfect for my 'Update' models where I need to know if a field was explicitly set to None or just omitted entirely. Since this is still a Draft for Python 3.15, how much of this works at runtime? I'm worried about Pydantic-style evaluation of these annotations.

The PEP actually puts a lot of weight on runtime evaluation. While it doesn't add an official evaluator to the stdlib, it introduces a `special_form_evaluator` ContextVar in `typing`. This is a hook that triggers when `__bool__` is called on a Boolean Operator (like `IsAssignable`) or when `__iter__` is called on `typing.Iter`. So your framework can set that context, call `__annotate__`, and the logic (like your loops or if/else type conditionals) will execute using your custom logic.

Hey nanochat! I was just reading through PEP 827 by Dr. Michael Sullivan, Daniel Park, and the 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. Is it just me, or are we basically turning Python into TypeScript now? I am a bit skeptical about how much complexity we are adding to the type system. Specifically, what is the deal with these 'type booleans'?

It is an incredibly ambitious proposal! PEP 827 really does aim to bring TypeScript-style power to Python 3.15. Type booleans are essentially the logic gates of this new system. They are a special subset of types that evaluate to Literal[True] or Literal[False] at type-checking time. The core primitives are operators like IsAssignable[T, S], which checks if T can be assigned to S, and IsEquivalent[T, S]. You can also use Bool[T] to check if a type is equivalent to Literal[True]. They are designed to be used inside conditional types to let the type system make decisions dynamically!

Literal[True] as a type logic gate? That sounds like a lot to wrap my head around. If I want to check multiple conditions, do I have to nest a bunch of these, or is there a cleaner way? I am worried the syntax is going to look like alphabet soup.

I totally hear you on the syntax complexity! To keep it readable, the PEP allows you to compose these booleans using standard Python keywords like 'and', 'or', and 'not'. You can even use all() and any() with type-level comprehensions. For example, you could write something like 'IsAssignable[T, int] or IsAssignable[T, str]'. When this is used in a conditional type, like 'float if IsAssignable[T, int] else str', the type checker resolves the boolean first and then picks the appropriate branch. It is very high-energy stuff for metaprogramming!
Wait, all() and any() work too? How does that even work with types? Do I have to iterate over a list of types manually?

Exactly! You use what the PEP calls 'unpacked comprehensions' combined with the Iter[] operator. If you have a tuple type, you can check every element. The syntax looks like 'all(IsAssignable[t, str] for t in Iter[SomeTuple])'. The authors included a 'special_form_evaluator' hook via ContextVar so that even runtime libraries like Pydantic can trigger this evaluation when they need to inspect annotations. It is a huge shift, but for framework authors, it means they can finally ditch those custom mypy plugins!

Hey! I'm an ML engineer looking at PEP 827. I saw it mentions some Meta survey from 2025. What exactly were people asking for in that survey that led to this proposal? I'm specifically hoping for better ways to handle tensor shapes without custom plugins.

According to the PEP 827 text, Meta's 2025 Typed Python Survey found that the most requested features were capabilities inspired by TypeScript. Specifically, developers wanted Intersection types (the & operator), Mapped and Conditional types, Utility types like Pick and Omit, and more flexible structural typing for dictionaries. For your work with ndarrays and tensors, the PEP authors explicitly highlight 'NumPy-style broadcasting' as a primary use case. By using the new conditional types (if/else) and type operators like Slice, GetArg, and IsEquivalent, you can define a Broadcast[A, B] alias. This allows the type system to compute resulting shapes for array operations directly, avoiding the need for the custom typechecker plugins that many ML libraries currently rely on.

Hey. I am an IDE tooling dev looking at PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. I am trying to figure out how this interacts with the __annotate__ mechanism. Are we talking about a PEP 649-style system where we get a function to execute, or is this more like PEP 695 where it is all built into the AST?

PEP 827 is designed to work with the __annotate__ mechanism introduced in PEP 649. Because this proposal adds conditionals ('if/else') and loops ('*[... for t in Iter[...]]') to the type language, these will be evaluated when the __annotate__ method of a class or function is called. The PEP authors suggest that most of these extensions are 'inert' until evaluated. For runtime tools, they even propose a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar hook that allows a library to intercept calls to boolean operators or 'typing.Iter' to control how the type-level logic unfolds during execution.

That sounds like a nightmare for static analysis. In TypeScript, mapped types are handled by the compiler's type system. Here, you are saying the source of truth for a type could be a loop inside __annotate__. If I'm trying to provide completions for a Prisma-style 'select' call, do I have to actually run the Python bytecode of __annotate__ just to see what fields are available?

Not necessarily. While the runtime uses __annotate__, static type checkers are expected to implement a static evaluator for the new operators like 'Members[T]', 'Attrs[T]', and 'GetMemberType'. The PEP authors have already started a proof-of-concept in mypy. For your IDE, instead of running bytecode, you would implement the logic for the type operators. For example, if you see 'p.name' inside an unpacked comprehension iterating over 'Attrs[T]', you know 'p' is a 'Member' type, and 'p.name' is a literal string of the attribute name. It is very similar to how you currently handle 'ParamSpec' or 'TypeVarTuple' from PEP 612 and 646, just with more powerful primitives like 'IsAssignable'.

What about 'UpdateClass'? If a library uses that in an '__init_subclass__', it literally changes the members of a class. This feels way more dynamic than PEP 681's '@dataclass_transform'. How am I supposed to handle 'go-to-definition' if the '__init__' method doesn't even exist in the source code but was injected by a type-level operator?

You are right that 'UpdateClass' is more powerful than PEP 681. It allows a decorator or '__init_subclass__' to return a form that adds or overrides members. The PEP explains that this allows libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI to define their logic (like generating an '__init__' based on annotated fields) directly in the type system. For your IDE, 'go-to-definition' on an injected '__init__' would ideally point to the type alias that defines it, such as 'InitFnType[T]' from the PEP's dataclass example. It shifts the burden from 'hardcoded tool support' to 'generic type evaluation'. It is definitely a more complex model for tooling, but it aims to provide a consistent experience across IDEs without needing custom plugins for every single framework.

The PEP also mentions 'type member access' using dot notation, like 'm.type'. Is that restricted to these new 'Member' and 'Param' objects, or is Python moving toward a general 'typeof' system like TS?

According to the PEP text, this dot notation is specifically for 'Member' and 'Param' types to access 'associated' type members like '.name', '.type', '.quals', and '.init'. It is not a general 'typeof' operator for any arbitrary object. The authors noted that using this on the wrong sort of type will be an error both at runtime and during type checking. While there is a 'Rejected Idea' section about a more general mechanism for associated types, the current specification limits it to these specific introspection primitives to keep the scope manageable for Python 3.15.
hello! i'm an ml engineer tired of debugging tensor shape mismatches. i heard pep 827 might let us actually type-check broadcasting logic? is that real?

Hello! Yes, that is exactly one of the goals of PEP 827 (Type Manipulation). Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov propose adding operators like Slice, GetArg, and IsEquivalent to the typing module. This allows you to write recursive type aliases that calculate the resulting shape of an operation. For instance, the PEP shows how to define a Broadcast[A, B] type that compares dimensions and handles Literal[1] broadcasting rules just like NumPy. It is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15.

that sounds powerful but also like it might break my current stack. i use a lot of libraries like pydantic that inspect annotations at runtime for validation. if i put a recursive if/else inside a type hint, won't those tools just crash trying to read it?

The PEP authors address this specifically for tools that rely on runtime type introspection. They propose adding a special_form_evaluator hook to typing, which is a ContextVar that holds a callable. When a library calls the __annotate__ method of a class or function, this hook can be used to trigger the evaluation of boolean operators and Iter loops. For static tools or those that want to process the raw, unevaluated structure, the PEP suggests it would work well with a proposed Format.AST mode for fetching annotations, or the tool can simply parse the source code as many already do.
so if i wanted to implement my own broadcasting check, what does the syntax actually look like? i'd need to compare dimensions to see if they are the same or if one is 1, right?

Exactly. You would use IsEquivalent for the checks and a conditional type for the logic. In PEP 827, you could define a helper called MergeOne[T, S] as: T if typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]] else S if typing.IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]] else typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]. This uses the RaiseError operator to provide a specific, readable error message during type checking if the shapes are incompatible, which is much better than a generic failure.

Hey nanochat! I've been looking at PEP 827 lately. Honestly, it feels like we're turning Python into TypeScript with all these conditional types and members. Between PEP 681 (dataclass_transform) and PEP 646 (Variadic Generics), do we really need this much complexity? It seems like a huge jump from PEP 695's clean generics.

It is definitely a significant step in terms of expressiveness! Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov (the authors) acknowledge that this is a complex proposal. However, the motivation stems from the fact that while PEP 681 helped with dataclasses, it's a special-case solution. Many libraries still rely on custom mypy plugins because the type system can't model their metaprogramming. The goal of PEP 827 is to provide generic primitives like IsAssignable and Members so framework authors don't have to keep writing plugins for every new pattern.

I get that, but the syntax is getting wild. I saw something about *[... for t in Iter[...]] in the draft. That's a lot of brackets. Also, how does this play with PEP 649? If we're doing type-level loops at runtime, doesn't that make the 'deferred evaluation' stuff even more complicated?

You're right about 'Bracket City'! The PEP actually discusses using parentheses for operators but sticks with brackets to stay consistent with existing Python typing. Regarding PEP 649, PEP 827 is designed to work with it or a 'Format.AST' approach. The proposal includes a special_form_evaluator ContextVar hook. This allows runtime libraries (like Pydantic or FastAPI) to trigger evaluation of these computed types when __bool__ or __iter__ is called on them. It basically bridges the gap between static analysis and the dynamic type-generation that Python frameworks already do at runtime.

Speaking of TypedDicts, I saw PEP 764 mentioned in the 'Rejected' section for dictionary-based syntax. If PEP 827 lands, does that mean we'll finally have things like 'Pick' or 'Omit' from TypeScript? I saw some examples using NewProtocol and NewTypedDict.

Exactly! PEP 827 can implement those utility types directly. For example, Omit[T, Keys] would be defined using NewProtocol with an unpacked comprehension that filters out members where typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys] is true. It even supports PEP 692-style **kwargs by allowing Unpack on typevars, which helps infer a TypedDict from keyword arguments. It's currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so the 'Mad Emperor' and the team are still refining how these pieces fit together, especially around things like 'stuck' type evaluations in generic functions.

Hey nanochat, I've been digging into the PEP 827 draft by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan and his co-authors. The section on "Lifting over Unions" is interesting from a type theory perspective. Why did the authors decide to make operators like IsAssignable or Concat distributive over union types instead of treating the union as a single opaque block?

That is a sophisticated question. In PEP 827, lifting is a core mechanic designed to ensure that type manipulation remains precise when dealing with branching types. According to the specification, when an operator is lifted, the evaluator takes the cross product of the union elements for each argument position. For example, if you are using an operator like Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['c'] | Literal['d']], the system evaluates every combination—'ac', 'ad', 'bc', and 'bd'—and then joins them into a single Union. The goal is to mirror how dynamic Python code actually behaves; since a variable could be any one of those union members at runtime, the type system needs to account for every possible outcome of the manipulation to maintain soundness.

That makes sense, but as a PL student, I worry about the inference tradeoffs. If I have several nested operators and each argument is a complex Union, wouldn't this cross-product logic lead to a combinatorial explosion during type checking? Did the PEP mention any specific constraints to prevent the checker from getting bogged down?

The PEP acknowledges that these operations are the "core engine" of the proposal and definitely adds complexity to the type-checking process. While the draft doesn't explicitly define a maximum 'depth' or limit for these cross products, it notes that the goal is to bridge the gap for frameworks like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs that require this level of introspection. The trade-off is intentional: the authors favor expressiveness and accuracy for framework maintainers over the simplicity of a system that can't 'see' inside unions. If an evaluation results in an empty set of types, the PEP specifies that it returns Never. Additionally, tools that process these annotations will need to implement an evaluator or use a library, and the authors are already working on a proof-of-concept in mypy to demonstrate that this is computationally feasible for real-world code like the 'NumPy-style broadcasting' or 'Utility Types' examples.

Hey there! I was just reading through some of the latest PEPs and 827 caught my eye. I use FastAPI and Pydantic daily, and I'm constantly rewriting models for 'Public', 'Create', and 'Update' versions of the same entity. Is this PEP really going to let me just derive those automatically?

Hello! It's great to meet a fellow developer. Yes, PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation', is designed specifically to address that kind of boilerplate. The authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—propose adding type-level operators that let you 'calculate' new types from existing ones. In the FastAPI example from the PEP, you could define a single 'Hero' model and then use a type alias like 'type HeroCreate = Create[Hero]'. Behind the scenes, 'Create' would use 'NewProtocol' and a comprehension like '*[p for p in Iter[Attrs[T]] if ...]' to filter out primary keys or make fields optional automatically.

That sounds like magic. But I'm a bit worried about the tools I already use. Pydantic and FastAPI rely heavily on extracting annotations at runtime to build their validation logic. If I start using 'if' and 'for' inside my type hints, won't that just break every tool that expects a simple string or a class?

That is a very sharp observation. The PEP authors actually addressed this in the 'Backwards Compatibility' section. Currently, if you use 'from __future__ import annotations', these expressions stay as strings, which tools can still parse with 'ast.parse'. However, for actual runtime evaluation, the PEP introduces a new hook called 'special_form_evaluator'. It's a ContextVar that holds a callable. When a tool calls '__bool__' on a Boolean Operator like 'IsAssignable' or '__iter__' on 'typing.Iter', this hook triggers the evaluation logic. While there isn't an official evaluator in the standard library yet, the authors plan to release a third-party library to handle this so frameworks like Pydantic can support these computed types without starting from scratch.
Got it, so there is a bit of a transition path. I also saw something about 'Format.AST' in the text. Is that a PEP 827 thing too?

Not exactly—'Format.AST' is mentioned as a separate PEP draft that would complement 827 perfectly. It would allow tools to fetch annotations as an Abstract Syntax Tree directly, making it even easier to handle complex manipulations without manually parsing strings. For now, since PEP 827 is still in 'Draft' status and targeting Python 3.15, the authors are focusing on ensuring the primitives like 'Members[T]', 'GetMemberType', and 'UpdateClass' work well for both static checkers like mypy and runtime frameworks. It's definitely an ambitious shift toward the kind of flexibility you see in TypeScript's Mapped Types!

Hey nanochat. I'm currently maintaining a custom mypy plugin for an ORM to handle query return types, and I've been reading through Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov's draft for PEP 827. It looks like a lifesaver for getting rid of plugin code. However, I noticed the authors rejected the 'infer' keyword from TypeScript's pattern matching. As someone used to TS, using GetArg feels a bit clunky. What was the specific logic for leaving 'infer' out?

The authors of PEP 827, including Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, did acknowledge that TypeScript's 'infer' mechanism is very elegant, but they cited a few major hurdles for Python. First, the binding structure for 'infer' is difficult to shoehorn into Python's grammar without major changes. Since PEP 827 avoids changing the Python grammar itself, we are limited to the existing 'true_type if bool_type else false_type' ternary syntax.

More importantly, PEP 827 places a high priority on runtime evaluation. The PEP notes that supporting something like 'El if IsAssignable[T, list[Infer[El]]] else Never' at runtime would be 'gnarly.' It would require a custom globals environment to catch unbound 'Infer' arguments during evaluation. By sticking to operators like GetArg, the proposal ensures that both static type checkers and runtime libraries (like Pydantic or FastAPI) can reliably evaluate the types using a consistent model.

That 'gnarly' runtime environment part makes a lot of sense, especially since I need these types to work for Pydantic validation. If I'm forced to use GetArg[T, Base, Idx], how does the 'Base' parameter actually behave? For example, if I'm trying to extract a type from a deeply nested structure or a class that inherits from multiple generics, is 'Base' strictly nominal?

Yes, 'Base' helps disambiguate where the argument is coming from in the inheritance hierarchy. The PEP defines GetArg[T, Base, Idx] as returning the type argument at index 'Idx' to 'T' when interpreted as 'Base'. For example, if you have 'class A(B[C]): ...', then 'GetArg[A, B, Literal[0]]' evaluates to 'C'. If 'T' cannot be interpreted as 'Base', it returns 'Never'.

You should be aware of a few nuances mentioned in the specification:
1. For nested structures, you would indeed compose operators. 
2. Negative indexes work for 'Idx', just like standard Python indexing.
3. Runtime evaluators might struggle if 'Base' is a Protocol. The PEP notes that while a static checker might handle 'GetArg[Ty, Iterable, Literal[0]]', a runtime evaluator may fail to resolve that if the attributes aren't explicitly visible.
4. For special forms like 'Callable', the arguments are packed into a tuple, and '...' is treated as ArgsParam/KwargsParam using the new Param types.

While it's more verbose than TS-style pattern matching, this explicit mapping allows the type system to navigate Python's MRO-based inheritance more predictably than a structural 'infer' might.

Hello! I have been reading the draft for PEP 827 and I am absolutely ecstatic. Coming from a background in Haskell and OCaml, seeing Python move toward this level of type-level expressivity is incredible. I was looking at the section on object inspection with Members[T], but I am particularly interested in the 'Potential Future Extensions' regarding metadata. Does the proposal currently outline a way to destructure or manipulate 'Annotated' types? I'm thinking about how we might extract type-level attributes for our own DSLs.

It is definitely an ambitious proposal! Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov have indeed acknowledged the demand for manipulating 'Annotated' types. While it isn't in the core specification for the initial 'Standards Track' push, they included it in the 'Potential Future Extensions' section because frameworks like FastAPI rely so heavily on that metadata.

The PEP suggests two specific operators for this: 'GetAnnotations[T]' and 'DropAnnotations[T]'. 'GetAnnotations' would allow you to fetch the metadata as Literal types, which you could then use inside the other type-level machinery like conditional types or comprehensions.

That is exactly what I was hoping for! In terms of the 'kind' of the result, if I have something like 'Annotated[int, "primary_key", 42]', how would 'GetAnnotations' represent those disparate metadata items? Would it return a tuple or a union? I want to know if I can iterate over them using 'Iter' and an unpacked comprehension.

The draft indicates that 'GetAnnotations[Annotated[int, "xxx", 5]]' would evaluate to 'Literal["xxx", 5]'. If you apply it to a type that has no annotations, like a plain 'int', it would return 'Never'. 

Once you have that 'Literal' of the annotations, you could certainly use it in logic. For instance, you could use 'DropAnnotations[T]' to get the base type ('int') and then use 'GetAnnotations' to decide how to transform that type in a 'NewProtocol'. This would allow for the kind of automated model derivation the authors demonstrate in their FastAPI and Prisma-style ORM examples, but driven by 'Annotated' metadata instead of just class-level Field objects.

Fascinating. So 'DropAnnotations' essentially acts as a way to reach the 'naked' type. I can see how that would be combined with 'NewProtocol[*Ms]'. One more thing—since this is still a 'Draft' for Python 3.15, is there much discussion about how 'GetAnnotations' interacts with union lifting? If I have a Union of Annotated types, does the operator distribute over them?

The PEP is very clear about its 'Lifting over Unions' principle. It states that many built-in operations are 'lifted,' meaning they take the cross product of union elements and evaluate the operator for each. 

While 'GetAnnotations' is in the extensions section, it would likely follow the same rules as 'Members[T]' or 'Attrs[T]'. If you had 'Annotated[int, "A"] | Annotated[str, "B"]', applying a lifted operator would process each constituent part. The authors' goal is to ensure that the new type-level machinery benefits both static type checkers and runtime frameworks, so this consistency across the type system is a core part of the design.

Hey nanochat. I am an IDE tooling engineer looking at PEP 827. The proposal for type-level comprehensions and conditionals like "true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ" looks like a nightmare for static analysis performance. How are we supposed to provide completions when the type of a member is hidden behind a recursive Iter loop or a complex conditional?

I understand the concern from a tooling perspective. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, aims to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and its type system. For IDEs, the core challenge is the evaluation of operators like "Members[T]" or "Attrs[T]" within an unpacked comprehension, such as "*[... for t in Iter[...]]". Since the PEP specifies that only members with explicit type annotations are included in these operations, the search space for your static analysis is at least somewhat bounded compared to full runtime introspection. You would essentially need to implement a type-level evaluator within your indexing engine to resolve these to concrete "Member" types.

Even with explicit annotations, what about "UpdateClass"? If a class decorator or an "__init_subclass__" returns "UpdateClass[*Ps: Member]", it literally modifies a nominal class. If a user performs a "Go to Definition" on a method injected this way, where do we point them? The source code for the original class won't have the method, and the computation might be five layers deep in a type alias.

That is a specific pain point mentioned in the PEP. "UpdateClass" is a special form that updates a nominal class by adding, overriding, or even removing members by setting their type to "Never". In terms of "Go to Definition," the source of truth is the type-level computation itself. For example, if a method is generated via an "InitFnType[T]" helper, the definition effectively points to that alias. The PEP notes this introduces a type-evaluation-order dependence, similar to runtime evaluation-order. Tools will have to handle the fact that the resulting class structure depends on the reverse MRO order of "__init_subclass__" calls and their associated "UpdateClass" returns.

The Prisma-style ORM example is particularly aggressive. It uses "NewProtocol" and "Unpack[K]" to infer a TypedDict from kwargs and then transforms it via "ConvertField" and "PropsOnly". If a schema has many "Link" and "MultiLink" references, I'm worried about the IDE lagging the UI thread while it tries to resolve "IsAssignable" or "GetArg[T, Base, Idx]" recursive checks during every keystroke.

The "db.select" example is indeed a high-bar test for any type checker. It relies on "**kwargs: Unpack[K]" where "K" is a "BaseTypedDict" to infer literal types for column names. The engine then uses "Iter[typing.Attrs[K]]" to build a "NewProtocol". To keep the IDE responsive, you would likely need to cache the results of boolean operators like "IsAssignable" and "IsEquivalent". The PEP authors suggest that since these features are inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types, IDE engineers can look to how TypeScript's language server optimizes "Utility Types" like "Pick", "Omit", and "Partial". Since PEP 827 is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, the proof-of-concept implementation in mypy is probably the best place to look for how to handle these recursive evaluations efficiently.

hello nanochat :) I've been looking at PEP 827 because I do a lot of ML work with tensors. It claims to solve the broadcasting problem, but it looks like a nightmare. Aren't we just moving the complexity of a mypy plugin into the source code? I shouldn't need a PhD in Type Theory just to add two ndarrays.

I understand the concern! Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan and the other authors, Daniel W. Park and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, actually address this in the PEP. You're right that the logic for things like NumPy-style broadcasting is complex, but the idea is that this complexity currently lives in custom mypy plugins that don't work in other IDEs or tools. By moving this into the type system as "Type Manipulation," the behavior becomes consistent across all Python 3.15 compliant tools. As a user, you likely wouldn't write the Broadcast logic yourself; you would just benefit from the work library maintainers put into those type-level helpers.

But wait, if I am already using TypeVarTuple from PEP 646, I can handle shapes, right? Why would I ever need Slice or a recursive Broadcast alias? It feels like we are over-engineering a problem that variadic generics already solved.

That is a common misconception. PEP 646 (Variadic Generics) allows you to label a shape and ensure two arrays have the *exact* same shape, but it cannot perform arithmetic or logic on those shapes. For instance, if you add an array with shape (4, 1) to one with shape (3,), the resulting type should be (4, 3). PEP 646 has no way to compute that. PEP 827 provides the engine to actually calculate it using a type alias like this: "type MergeOne[T, S] = (T if typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]] else S if typing.IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]] else typing.RaiseError[Literal['Broadcast mismatch'], T, S])". It uses IsEquivalent and conditional if/else syntax to simulate the actual broadcasting rules at the type level.

Wait, so I can actually trigger a Type Error with a custom message like 'Broadcast mismatch' just by using a type alias? That is pretty wild. What is that Iter stuff I saw in the proposal then? Is that for shapes too?

Exactly! RaiseError allows for much better feedback than just a generic "Incompatible types." As for the Iter part, it is used in "unpacked comprehensions." In PEP 827, you can write something like "*[some_logic(t) for t in typing.Iter[some_tuple]]". This is essential for shape typing because it lets you iterate over the dimensions in a TypeVarTuple or a tuple type to transform them. For example, if you wanted to create a NewProtocol that only includes certain attributes or dimensions, you would use that Iter syntax to loop through the existing Members[T] or Attrs[T] at the type level.

I was looking at this PEP 827 proposal by Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov. It feels like we are just turning Python into a second-rate TypeScript with all these type-level operators. Now I see something called FromUnion. What is the point of this? Is it just more complexity for the sake of it?

The FromUnion[T] operator is a primitive for deconstructing types. In PEP 827, it takes a Union and turns it into a tuple containing all the individual union elements. If the type isn't a union, it just returns a 1-ary tuple containing T. The authors intended this to allow programmers to iterate over the components of a union using the new unpacked comprehension syntax, like *[x for x in Iter[FromUnion[T]]]. It is definitely a step toward the kind of type-level computation seen in TypeScript, specifically to support things like the Exclude or Extract utility types mentioned in the PEP.

Right, so it's a 'utility type' wrapper. But Python isn't TypeScript. Our types actually exist at runtime. If I use FromUnion, can I actually use that tuple for anything useful at runtime, or is this just another static-only trick? And can it handle nested unions, or does it just break?

The PEP specifies that FromUnion is 'lifted' over unions, but its primary job is to flatten a single level into a tuple for iteration. Regarding runtime, that is one of the main limitations right now. While the proposal includes a special_form_evaluator hook via ContextVar to help libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI evaluate these expressions, there is no official evaluator in the standard library. If you try to use FromUnion at runtime without a third-party evaluator, it remains an 'inert' type operator application. It does not automatically turn into a standard Python tuple you can call .sort() on or manipulate with regular Python code.

That sounds half-baked. So if I want to filter a Union to remove None, I have to learn this weird comprehension syntax instead of just using a list filter? Can FromUnion even guarantee the order of types it returns, or is it non-deterministic like a set? I bet your 'nanochat' logic can't even tell me if it preserves order.

You've hit on a real constraint. The PEP text doesn't explicitly guarantee a specific order for the elements returned by FromUnion, though it notes that operators like Slice work on the resulting tuples. If you wanted to filter out None, you would use the Exclude utility type, which is implemented using FromUnion and a conditional check. As for the logic, the PEP is silent on whether FromUnion[int | str] is guaranteed to be (int, str) versus (str, int). Furthermore, the current implementation in the authors' mypy proof-of-concept is still 'Draft' status for Python 3.15, and many tools that parse Python files today wouldn't even know how to extract these annotations without reparsing the source code.

Hola. I am looking into the roadmap for Python 3.15. I saw PEP 827 mentioned for type manipulation. I am a tech lead for a team that uses a lot of Pydantic and we are always looking for better ways to handle conditional types. One thing I'm confused about: are these 'type booleans' just regular Python bools that we can use inside annotations now?

Hello! I can certainly help clarify that for you. While I can understand your greeting, I work best in English for technical details. Regarding PEP 827, which is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, you have hit on a common point of confusion. Type booleans are not actually standard Python bool values used at the type level. Instead, they are a specific subset of the type language designed for use within conditional types. Syntactically, they look like 'true_type if bool_type else false_type'. However, that 'bool_type' must be a special type boolean operator from the typing module, such as IsAssignable[T, S] or IsEquivalent[T, S]. When these are evaluated, they resolve to Literal[True] or Literal[False] rather than the built-in bool type.
Right, so if I want to check if a type is a string, I can just write 'str if T == str else int'? That seems like it would solve a lot of our boilerplate in our CRUD models.

Not exactly. PEP 827 does not support the '==' operator for type comparison in that way. To achieve what you are describing, you would use the IsEquivalent operator. The syntax would look like: 'str if typing.IsEquivalent[T, str] else int'. The PEP authors, Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, defined these operators specifically so they can produce type values with appropriate behavior at runtime without changing how standard Python literals work. You can also combine these operators using standard logic keywords like 'and', 'or', and 'not', but they must remain within the supported set of boolean operators defined in the specification.
Okay, that makes sense. If we are doing complex logic, can I use regular Python functions to return these type booleans? My team would probably want to wrap some of this logic in helper functions.

The PEP is quite specific about this: you cannot use arbitrary Python functions to compute these booleans at the type level. The grammar for type booleans is restricted to boolean compositions of the provided operators. However, you can create 'helper aliases' using the new Bool[T] operator. For example, if you have a type alias that evaluates to a boolean literal, you can use Bool[MyAlias[T]] inside a conditional. For more complex iteration, the PEP also introduces 'all()' and 'any()' which can take a comprehension, such as 'all(typing.IsAssignable[p.type, str] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]])'. This allows you to perform checks across all members of a class or TypedDict, which should be very useful for the Pydantic-style validation logic your team handles.

aloha! I've been looking at PEP 827. I am a heavy FastAPI and Pydantic user, and the boilerplate for defining HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate models for every single entity is killing me. Can this PEP really generate those dynamically so I don't have to duplicate fields manually?

Hello! Yes, that is exactly one of the core motivations Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov (the 'Mad Emperor') describe in the PEP. Specifically, they propose using NewProtocol and Attrs to programmatically derive these models. For a 'HeroCreate' type, you could use an unpacked comprehension like *[Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals] for p in Iter[Attrs[Hero]] if not IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal['primary_key']]]]. This would allow you to strip the primary key automatically based on the Field metadata.

That sounds like magic, but I am worried about my developer experience. If I define 'type HeroCreate = Create[Hero]', will my IDE actually show me the fields? I don't want to lose autocompletion and go back to guessing strings.

That is a valid concern. The authors argue that while libraries currently use custom mypy plugins or PEP 681's @dataclass_transform, those don't provide consistent support across all IDEs and tools. PEP 827 aims to bridge this by putting the manipulation logic directly in the type system. By using standard operators like GetMemberType and Members, the static type checker (and thus your IDE) can evaluate the resulting structural type. Sullivan and the team have even started a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can already type check these examples.

Okay, so the IDE evaluates it. But what about runtime? FastAPI needs to know the fields at runtime to do Pydantic validation. If the type is 'NewProtocol', does the library see it as a real class with a schema?

The PEP addresses this through 'Runtime evaluation support.' It introduces a hook called special_form_evaluator, which is a ContextVar holding a callable. When a framework like FastAPI calls __annotate__ or checks a boolean type operator at runtime, this hook triggers the evaluation. While the PEP doesn't include an official evaluator in the stdlib, the authors intend to provide a third-party library so that tools like Pydantic can resolve these computed types into concrete schemas for validation.
That makes sense. I also saw something about 'UpdateClass'. If I want to automatically add a generated __init__ method to a class based on its attributes, is that where that comes in?

Exactly. UpdateClass is a special form intended for the return type of class decorators or __init_subclass__. You can define an 'InitFnType[T]' that iterates over Attrs[T] and constructs a Param sequence for a Callable. Then, your base class __init_subclass__ returns UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]. This formally tells the type system that the class now possesses a specific __init__ signature without you having to write it out. It uses the new 'Extended Callables' format, which allows Param[Literal['name'], Type, Quals] to represent every aspect of the function signature.
