This is a ContextVar that holds a callable to handle the evaluation when something like __bool__ is called on a boolean operator like IsAssignable. While the PEP doesn't put an evaluator in the standard library, the authors plan to release a third-party library so that tools like Pydantic can resolve these computed types at runtime. 

So, when FastAPI looks at your type HeroCreate = Create[Hero], the evaluator would run, see the IsAssignable checks, and produce a concrete type that Pydantic can use for validation. It's a Draft for Python 3.15, so it's not here yet, but the goal is specifically to bridge that gap between 'static magic' and 'runtime reality'.

Hey. I am looking at PEP 827 for a validation library I'm working on. I noticed the spec for Type Booleans is quite specific about which operators can be used in the body of a conditional. Why are we restricted to specific operators like IsAssignable or IsEquivalent instead of just using any expression that evaluates to a literal boolean?

According to the PEP 827 text, the restriction on which operators can appear in a type-boolean expression (like IsAssignable, IsEquivalent, and Bool[T]) exists primarily to support runtime evaluation without breaking standard Python semantics. 

By limiting these to specific typing-module operators, the proposal allows runtime classes to produce 'type' values that have special behavior. Specifically, it introduces a ContextVar hook called 'special_form_evaluator'. When a runtime evaluator calls __bool__ on one of these boolean operator aliases, the hook is triggered to compute the result. If we allowed arbitrary Literal[True] or Literal[False] values to drive these conditionals, the runtime wouldn't be able to distinguish between a static literal and a computed type-level boolean that needs this special evaluation logic. This design ensures the type-level machinery can function at runtime without requiring any changes to the core Python grammar or the existing behavior of literal types.

Hey nanochat! I am the tech lead for a team heavily invested in Pydantic and FastAPI. We are looking at PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) for Python 3.15. My main concern is CI performance. This proposal basically adds a computation engine to the type system. Is this going to make mypy or pyright runs take ten times longer?

That is a valid concern for any tech lead. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, definitely introduces significant complexity. It is inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types, which are known to add overhead. However, the PEP argues that this actually improves consistency. Currently, many libraries use custom typechecker plugins (like for PEP 681 dataclass transforms) which are often opaque and can be slow themselves. By moving this into the core typing module with standardized operators like IsAssignable and Members, typecheckers can implement highly optimized, native paths for these computations rather than relying on external Python-based plugins.

I noticed the 'Lifting over Unions' section in the spec. It says for every operator, we take the cross product of union elements. If I have nested unions being passed into something like Concat or a custom logic helper, doesn't that lead to a combinatorial explosion during the type-checking phase?

You've spotted a potential bottleneck. The PEP specifies that for an operator applied to unions, the evaluator takes the cross product of the elements, evaluates the operator for each tuple, and then unions the results. For example, Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['c'] | Literal['d']] results in four distinct strings. While this provides the expressiveness needed for complex APIs like NumPy-style broadcasting, the PEP authors acknowledge that 'stuck' type evaluations (where an operator is applied to an unresolved TypeVar) are still an area where evaluation rules are somewhat unclear. In the reference implementation in mypy, they currently handle these by checking operands invariantly to prevent infinite recursion or excessive branching.

The PEP also mentions runtime evaluation, specifically for FastAPI CRUD models using NewProtocol and Attrs[T]. If my runtime framework calls __annotate__ to generate a schema, is it going to trigger this whole computation on every import or execution? That sounds like it could hurt startup times or request latency if not cached properly.

PEP 827 addresses this via 'Runtime evaluation support'. It does not include an official evaluator in the standard library but proposes a hook called 'special_form_evaluator'. This is a ContextVar that holds a callable invoked when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. Because the results of these manipulations (like NewProtocol or NewTypedDict) produce standard typing._GenericAlias objects, frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic would likely cache the resulting types after the first evaluation. The PEP also notes that the proposed Format.AST mode for fetching annotations would allow tools to get the raw expression without executing the logic, which could help tools that want to avoid the computation cost entirely.

merhaba. I have been reading through the draft for PEP 827 (Type Manipulation). It seems like a massive jump in complexity for Python's type system. I am a student of PL design, and I am curious about the actual mandate here. Is there documented demand for this, or are we just trying to mirror TypeScript because it is popular?

Hello! It is a fair question. While Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov definitely took inspiration from TypeScript, the mandate is rooted in the current limitations of Python metaprogramming. The PEP specifically cites Meta's 2025 Typed Python Survey. The top request from respondents was for features found in other languages: specifically Intersection types, Mapped and Conditional types, and better utility types like Pick, Omit, and keyof. Currently, libraries like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs have to rely on custom mypy plugins or special-case decorators like PEP 681's dataclass_transform because the core type system cannot model their dynamic transformations. This proposal aims to provide a generic way to bridge that gap.

The survey results are one thing, but from an inference perspective, TypeScript is structural, while Python is primarily nominal. PEP 827 introduces IsAssignable[T, S], which it defines as a 'consistent subtype' check. How does this operator handle the 'gradual' part of Python's typing without making the checker's job computationally intractable, especially when dealing with protocols at runtime?

The PEP acknowledges that full assignability checking is difficult, especially for protocols where attributes might be inferred. At runtime, the authors suggest the evaluator should be 'best effort.' To manage complexity, the PEP introduces 'Lifting over Unions.' When an operator like IsAssignable is applied to a Union, the checker takes the cross product of the union elements and evaluates the operator for each tuple. For example, IsAssignable[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], T] is checked by evaluating both elements. This prevents the logic from getting 'stuck' on a complex union. However, the authors admit that if the check involves unresolved type variables, the evaluation might remain 'stuck' and requires invariant checking of operands to maintain soundness.

The 'stuck' evaluation is exactly what I was worried about. Looking at the GenericCallable specification, it uses a lambda-based syntax like GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty]. The PEP mentions this is to 'delay evaluation' to avoid the 'can of worms' of impredicative polymorphism and rank-N types. As a student of type theory, this feels like a significant constraint. If we can only use these generic callables inside a Member type argument, does that mean we are giving up on truly first-class generic function manipulation?

In this draft, yes. The authors are quite explicit about avoiding the 'can of worms' involving undecidable type reconstruction, citing Hans Boehm and Frank Pfenning. By restricting GenericCallable to the type argument of a Member (which represents class attributes or methods), they prevent these complex types from appearing in locals or return types where they would complicate general type inference. The goal isn't necessarily to solve every problem in higher-order logic, but to allow framework authors to generate class-level signatures—like a dataclass's __init__ or a Prisma query result—which currently requires out-of-band tooling. It is a trade-off: limiting the scope of generic callables makes the implementation in tools like mypy (where a proof-of-concept already exists) much more feasible.

Hey nanochat. I'm digging into PEP 827 and trying to figure out how to handle methods with multiple signatures when building a dynamic protocol. I assume I just use `@typing.overload` inside the `NewProtocol` block, or is there a new decorator for the type-level functions?

Actually, PEP 827 handles this differently. When you are constructing a type using the new manipulation facilities, you don't use the `@overload` decorator. Instead, the PEP introduces a specific type operator called `Overloaded[*Callables]`.

Wait, an operator? So it's not a decorator? I'm used to how we do it in standard class bodies. If I'm using `NewProtocol` to generate a structural type, how does `Overloaded` actually look in the code? Does it just take a list of functions?

It takes a variadic sequence of callable types. In the specification authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, `Overloaded[*Callables]` represents an overloaded function type where the underlying types are provided in order. 

For example, if you were building a member for a protocol that needs to handle both `int` and `str` inputs, it might look like this:

```python
typing.Member[
    Literal["process"],
    typing.Overloaded[
        Callable[[int], int],
        Callable[[str], str]
    ]
]
```

This allows the static type checker to treat the member as having multiple signatures without needing the imperative decorator syntax.

Ah, okay, I see. So it's a way to 'pack' multiple `Callable` definitions into a single type for a `Member`. But what if I'm inspecting an existing overloaded function? Does `Members[T]` just give me an `Overloaded` object automatically?

Exactly. The PEP specifies that `Overloaded` is used to represent overloaded function types with the underlying callables in order. When you use `Members[T]` or `Attrs[T]` on a class that has methods defined with `@overload`, the type system will present that member using the `Overloaded` operator. This makes it possible to iterate over or deconstruct those signatures using other operators like `Iter` or `FromUnion` if needed.

Got it. One more thing—since this is targeting Python 3.15 and it's still a Draft, is `Overloaded` strictly for `NewProtocol`, or can I use it as a standalone type hint in my regular code to replace the old `@overload` boilerplate?

The PEP primarily introduces `Overloaded` (and the extended `Params`/`Param` machinery) to provide a way of constructing and introspecting types that is 'amenable to creating and introspecting callable types' via type-level computation. While it is a valid type form, its main purpose is to bridge the gap for libraries that need to generate complex signatures—like a Prisma-style ORM or FastAPI CRUD models—where the standard `@overload` decorator wouldn't work because the signatures are being computed dynamically.

Hey nanochat! I am super hyped about PEP 827. Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan and the team are really cooking with these type manipulation primitives. I was diving into the spec for runtime evaluation support -- specifically, how does typing.Iter actually behave when you call iter() on it during a runtime check?

It is a massive proposal! To make those unpacked comprehensions work at runtime without changing the Python grammar, PEP 827 introduces a specific hook. When you call iter() on a typing.Iter object, it triggers its __iter__ method, which looks for a ContextVar named typing.special_form_evaluator. If that ContextVar holds a callable, Python invokes it with the typing._GenericAlias as the argument. The result of that call then has iter() called on it. This allows third-party libraries to intercept the type-level loop and provide actual data for the iteration.

That is a clever way to handle it without a heavy-handed standard library evaluator. So if I am writing a tool like Pydantic and want to support these types, I just need to set that ContextVar? What happens if the ContextVar is empty or set to None?

Exactly! You would set the ContextVar during your evaluation phase. If special_form_evaluator is None (the default), the PEP specifies that typing.Iter will simply return an empty iterator: iter(()). This ensures that these advanced annotations don't break existing tools that might accidentally trigger an iteration; they just see an empty result. The same logic applies to the boolean operators like IsAssignable or IsEquivalent—if the hook is None, calling bool() on them just returns False.

Got it. And the PEP mentioned that the callable in the ContextVar is invoked with the alias itself. Does that mean the evaluator is responsible for looking at the inner types, like the tuple inside Iter[T]?

Spot on. The evaluator gets the whole generic alias. Since typing.Iter[T] is meant to iterate over the arguments of a tuple type, your evaluator would inspect the type argument T (likely using the existing __args__ or the new GetArgs operator mechanisms) to determine what elements to yield during the runtime iteration. It gives framework authors total control over how the type-level 'for' loop resolves to actual objects.
helloo?? just catching up on pep 827. what exactly is the deal with UpdateClass? is it just for decorators?

UpdateClass[*Ps: Member] is a special form introduced in PEP 827 specifically to allow type-level computation to modify an existing nominal class. While it can be used in the return type of a class decorator, it is also valid as the return type for __init_subclass__.

Its main job is to take a pack of Member objects and use them to add new members to a class, override existing ones, or even 'remove' members by setting their type to Never. It is intended for patterns like those found in dataclasses, attrs, or Pydantic, where the class structure is transformed during creation.
right, okay. so if i use it in __init_subclass__, how does the type checker handle a deep hierarchy? say multiple bases are trying to inject members via this mechanism.

The PEP specifies that if one or more ancestors have an __init_subclass__ with an UpdateClass return type, they are applied in reverse MRO (Method Resolution Order) order. 

If the cls parameter of the __init_subclass__ is parameterized (like type[T]), the actual class type is substituted in for T during evaluation. This ensures that the most 'base' modifications happen first, followed by more specific ones as you move down toward the subclass.

that makes sense for a single linear path. but as a reviewer, i'm looking at the 'open issues' section. there is a note about evaluation-order dependence. if my UpdateClass logic for Class A involves inspecting Members[Class B], and Class B also has an __init_subclass__ that uses UpdateClass, isn't that a potential mess?

You've hit on a key concern raised by the authors (Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov). There is a risk of evaluation-order dependence when one class's transformation relies on the 'final' state of another class that is also being transformed.

Specifically, if the UpdateClass logic for Class A calls Members[B] to decide which members to add, the result depends on whether the type checker (or runtime evaluator) has already processed the __init_subclass__ for Class B. If B hasn't been updated yet, A might see an incomplete or 'stale' set of members. 

The PEP authors acknowledge that while this mirrors potential runtime evaluation-order issues in standard Python, it's something they ideally want to reject or strictly define to avoid inconsistent type-checking results across different environments. Currently, it remains an open issue for the Draft.

Looking at the design of PEP 827, specifically the type-level operators like IsAssignable or GetArg. Since these are essentially type-level functions—or mappings at the kind level—why did Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the other authors choose to stick with square brackets for these 'calls' instead of parentheses? It feels like we're just residing in 'Bracket City' rather than treating them as proper higher-order constructs.

Hey nanochat. I've been looking at PEP 827. It seems like it's trying to replace the need for custom mypy plugins or PEP 681's dataclass_transform. How exactly does it handle generating __init__ methods for something like a custom ORM or dataclass clone?

Hello! You're exactly right. Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov proposed PEP 827 to bridge the gap between Python's metaprogramming and the type system without relying on brittle plugins. Instead of the hardcoded logic in PEP 681, PEP 827 introduces type-level operators. The heavyweight lifter here is UpdateClass. It allows you to return a special form from a decorator or __init_subclass__ that adds or overrides members on a class. This allows you to programmatically define an __init__ signature by iterating over the class's attributes using the Members or Attrs operators.

Interesting. So UpdateClass is the hook. But how do I actually build the __init__ signature? I saw something about Param and Params. Does it let me iterate over class attributes to construct the parameter list?

Exactly. You use the Attrs[T] operator to get a tuple of Member types for class T. Then, you use an "unpacked comprehension" to transform those into function parameters. In the PEP's dataclass example, they show an InitFnType[T] helper. It iterates over typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] and for each attribute p, it creates a typing.Param[p.name, p.type, ...]. The syntax looks like *[typing.Param[p.name, p.type, Literal["keyword"]] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]. This essentially maps class attributes directly to __init__ arguments dynamically.

Okay, looking at that Param definition... it takes N (name), T (type), and Q (qualifiers). How does the logic handle default values? Usually, for dataclasses, if a field has a default value in the class body, the __init__ param needs to be optional or have a default.

That is handled through the InitField mechanism and conditional types. PEP 827 introduces InitField[KwargDict] to capture metadata from the class body. In the InitFnType helper, you use a conditional: if the default value extracted from p.init is not Never, you add the Literal["default"] qualifier to the Param. The PEP uses a GetDefault[Init] helper that checks typing.IsAssignable[GetDefault[p.init], Never] to decide whether to apply the "default" qualifier. This makes the generated __init__ argument optional in the static type system when an initializer is present.

I see. So it's essentially a type-level loop. What about the 'Extended Callables' part? The PEP mentions Callable[Params[...], Return]. Is that mandatory for this or can I use the old Callable[[int, str], None] syntax?

For complex manipulation like adding or modifying parameters, you'll want the Extended Callable format. While standard Callable syntax is fine for simple annotations, Params[*Ps] allows the type system to represent things like positional-only arguments, keyword-only arguments, and defaults more precisely. The Extended Callable format uses the Param type to encode name, type, and qualifiers (like "*", "**", "default", or "keyword"). This makes the callable's signature fully introspectable by other type operators, which is how you construct the new __init__ method for UpdateClass. Also, Params serves as a natural bound for ParamSpec.

One thing that bothers me about these 'mapped types' is how they interact with inheritance. If I use UpdateClass in a base class's __init_subclass__, does it correctly pick up attributes from the entire MRO or just the local class?

PEP 827 specifies that UpdateClass can be used in the return type of __init_subclass__. When a class is declared, if ancestors have an __init_subclass__ with an UpdateClass return, they are applied in reverse MRO order. If the "cls" parameter is parameterized by type[T], then T is substituted with the class type during evaluation. The Attrs[T] operator returns the attributes of the class; for a nominal class, this includes inherited attributes that have explicit type annotations. This ensures the generated __init__ can account for fields defined up the chain.

Last question for now. What about the runtime? If I'm using Pydantic or FastAPI, they need to see these types at runtime to do validation. Does PEP 827 leave us with raw strings, or is there a real way to evaluate these computed types?

The authors made runtime evaluation a priority. The PEP proposes a special_form_evaluator ContextVar in the typing module. This is a hook that triggers when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. While the standard library won't include a full evaluator, this hook allows third-party libraries to provide one. This means Pydantic or FastAPI could fully evaluate a computed type like Create[Hero] at runtime to perform validation and JSON conversion, bridging the gap between static types and dynamic behavior in Python 3.15.

I have been maintaining a fairly complex mypy plugin for a few years to handle custom dataclass-style code generation in my framework. I just saw PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) is targeting Python 3.15. Can this actually replace a mypy plugin for generating __init__ signatures based on class attributes?

It absolutely can. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, is designed specifically to bridge the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system without requiring custom plugins. It introduces type-level operators that allow you to introspect class members and programmatically construct new types. For your use case, the primary tools are Attrs[T] for inspection, the Param type for constructing function signatures, and UpdateClass for applying the new method to a class.
That sounds promising. In my current plugin, I have to manually traverse the AST to find annotated fields. How does PEP 827 handle 'looking' at the attributes of a class?

Instead of AST traversal, you use the Members[T] or Attrs[T] operators. These return a tuple of Member types. Each Member object is a special type that holds associated data accessible via dot notation. For an attribute, you can access m.name (the literal string name), m.type (the actual type of the field), m.quals (qualifiers like Final or ClassVar), and m.init (the literal type of the initializer, like a Field call). You can iterate over these in a type-level comprehension using the Iter helper, like: *[p.name for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]].
Okay, so I can get the names and types. But how do I turn those into a callable signature for __init__? Standard Callable syntax is pretty rigid.

The PEP introduces 'Extended Callables' precisely for this. You use a Params type that wraps a sequence of Param objects. A Param type looks like Param[Name, Type, Qualifiers]. You can use a comprehension to map your class attributes directly into parameters. For example, you can create a type alias like: type InitFnType[T] = Member[Literal['__init__'], Callable[[Param[Literal['self'], Self], *[Param[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]], None], Literal['ClassVar']]. This dynamically constructs a signature where every attribute becomes a keyword argument.
What about defaults? My framework uses a Field() object to mark if a field is optional or has a default value. Can the type system see inside those calls?

Yes, through the InitField mechanism. When you assign something like 'x: int = Field(default=0)' in a class, PEP 827 tracks that initializer in the Member.init field. You can then use a conditional type to check if a default exists. For example: 'Literal["keyword", "default"] if typing.IsAssignable[p.init, HasDefault] else Literal["keyword"]'. This allows your generated __init__ to correctly mark parameters as having defaults based on the actual field definitions in the class body.
And the final step: how do I actually inject this generated __init__ back into the user's class so IDEs see it? Is it a decorator?

You have two main options using the UpdateClass operator. You can write a class decorator that returns UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]], or you can define a base class where __init_subclass__ returns UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]. When the type checker sees UpdateClass, it applies those new members (like your generated __init__) to the nominal class, overriding or adding to the existing ones. This effectively standardizes what your mypy plugin used to do, making it work across any PEP 827-compliant type checker or IDE for Python 3.15.

hi there!! i've been reading about how python might finally get some typescript-like features. i use dataclasses and attrs all the time but the type checking for custom versions always feels like magic or requires plugins. i heard pep 827 handles this? also where is the actual discussion thread for this pep?

Hello! You can find the primary discussion for PEP 827 on the Python Discourse at: https://discuss.python.org/t/pep-827-type-manipulation/106353. It was authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. It is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15. You are right that it aims to eliminate the need for custom typechecker plugins (like those used for Pydantic or complex dataclass-like libraries) by providing type-level construction tools directly in the language.

thanks for the link! so as a dataclass user, how does this pep actually look at my class attributes to generate something like an __init__ method? right now pep 681 (dataclass_transform) is basically just a hint to the IDE, it doesn't really 'calculate' anything.

PEP 827 introduces a few core primitives for this. To 'see' your attributes, you would use typing.Attrs[T], which produces a tuple of Member types describing the attributes of class T. To turn those into an __init__ method, you use an unpacked comprehension. It looks like this: *[typing.Param[p.name, p.type] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]. This allows the type system to iterate over the actual attributes found on the class and map them into function parameters.

wait, typing.Iter? and dot notation like p.name? that looks like regular python code but inside a type annotation. how do we tell the type checker to actually 'run' that and give us a function signature?

Exactly. The PEP proposes that within a type argument, you can use a specific 'unpacked comprehension' syntax that type checkers evaluate. For an __init__ method, you would define a type alias like this:

type InitFnType[T] = typing.Member[
    Literal["__init__"],
    Callable[
        [
            typing.Param[Literal["self"], Self],
            *[
                typing.Param[p.name, p.type]
                for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
            ],
        ],
        None,
    ],
    Literal["ClassVar"],
]

In this example, typing.Param is a new type that holds information about a function parameter (name, type, and qualifiers like whether it has a default). The type checker iterates through the attributes and builds the parameter list for you.
that is wild. but once i have that InitFnType, how do i actually apply it to my class? if i make a @my_dataclass decorator, can it use this to tell mypy that the class now has this new __init__?

Yes, that is where the UpdateClass operator comes in. PEP 827 specifies that UpdateClass can be used in the return type of a decorator or __init_subclass__. It effectively 'updates' a nominal class with new members. You could define your decorator like this:

def my_dataclass[T](cls: type[T]) -> typing.UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]:
    ...

When you decorate a class with @my_dataclass, the type checker sees that the return type is UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]], so it knows the resulting class has the __init__ method we just computed, even though it wasn't written in the source code of the class itself.

hey, i'm looking at pep 827 because i'm trying to figure out if i can finally type-check broadcasting for my ndarrays. i noticed it mentions 'type booleans' as a separate thing. how does the pep actually distinguish them from regular types? is it just shorthand for Literal[True] or is there more to it?

In PEP 827, type booleans are a specific subset of the type language used primarily for conditional logic, which is essential for the broadcasting shape checks you're looking at. While they evaluate to Literal[True] or Literal[False] in a standard annotation context, they are restricted to a particular grammar. According to the specification by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, a type boolean consists of specific boolean operators defined in the typing module like IsAssignable[T, S], IsEquivalent[T, S], and Bool[T]. These can be combined using keywords like 'and', 'or', and 'not', as well as 'any()' and 'all()' using comprehensions. The reason they aren't just plain Literals is to support runtime evaluation; the PEP introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar that allows a library to hook into these expressions when __bool__ is called. For your ndarray work, this means you can write logic like 'T if IsEquivalent[T, S] else MergeOne[T, S]' and have the type system resolve it, which wouldn't be possible if the system couldn't distinguish these functional checks from static boolean values.

Hey nanochat. I've been digging into the PEP 827 proposal for Type Manipulation. It feels like a massive leap from the current 'static-only' mindset we have with things like ParamSpec or TypeVarTuple. One thing that caught my eye is the 'unpacked comprehension' syntax using typing.Iter. In TypeScript, mapped types are purely a compiler construct. How does Python handle this at runtime? If I call iter() on typing.Iter, does it actually return anything useful?

That is a great observation. While TypeScript's mapped types are erased at runtime, PEP 827 is specifically designed to support runtime type introspection for frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI. For typing.Iter, the PEP introduces a new hook called special_form_evaluator. This is a ContextVar in the typing module that holds a callable. When you call __iter__ on a typing.Iter alias (which is what happens when you use it in a comprehension or call iter() on it), Python checks this ContextVar. If an evaluator library has set a hook, that hook is invoked to handle the iteration. If the ContextVar is None, which is the default, typing.Iter simply evaluates to an empty iterator, specifically iter(()).

So it is essentially a plugin system for the runtime? That is quite a departure from how things like typing.Generic or typing.Protocol work, where the runtime object is mostly just a metadata container. If I'm comparing this to PEP 646's Unpack, that was also 'inert' at runtime. Why the need for a ContextVar hook instead of just letting the evaluator library parse the __annotations__ dict?

The authors, including Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and Yury Selivanov, wanted to ensure that the new syntax—like 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' and the unpacked comprehensions—would work automatically when the __annotate__ method of a class or function is called. Without these hooks, the standard Python execution of those expressions would fail or produce incorrect results before a third-party evaluator even got a chance to see them. By using a ContextVar hook for __bool__ (on boolean operators) and __iter__ (on typing.Iter), an evaluator can 'drive' the evaluation of the type expression as it is being executed by the Python VM. This allows the logic to remain valid Python code without requiring changes to the language grammar itself.

Interesting. So if I have something like *[p.type for p in Iter[Members[T]]], the 'for p in Iter[...]' part actually executes. In TypeScript, I'd just use 'K in keyof T'. Here, we're using a literal Python loop. Does this mean I can use any iterable in Iter, or is it restricted to specific type-level operators?

It is restricted. The PEP specifies that typing.Iter is intended to work with types that represent sequences of type-level information. The primary examples are the results of operators like Members[T], Attrs[T], or FromUnion[T]. For instance, typing.Members[T] produces a tuple of Member types. When you wrap that in Iter[], you can iterate over those Member objects. Each p in your loop would be an instance of Member[N, T, Q, Init, D], allowing you to access p.name or p.type using dot notation within the comprehension. It is much more structured than just iterating over keys like in TypeScript; you're iterating over rich descriptors of the class members.

I see. And since this is targeting Python 3.15 and is currently a Draft, I assume there's no built-in evaluator in the standard library yet? It's all 'bring your own evaluator' via that special_form_evaluator hook?

Correct. The PEP authors explicitly state they do not propose adding an official evaluator to the standard library at this time. Instead, they intend to release a third-party library to handle the heavy lifting of evaluating these computed types. The goal of the PEP is to provide the 'primitives' in the typing module and the specification for how type checkers and runtime tools should interpret them. At runtime, the special_form_evaluator is the bridge that lets those libraries hook into the standard annotation evaluation process.

hi! I was looking into PEP 827 for some metaprogramming stuff. I saw it mentioned FastAPI models. How does it actually handle deriving things like HeroPublic or HeroCreate without all the boilerplate we have now? And how does this relate to PEP 681?

Hello! PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov aims to solve this by providing type-level operators like Members[], Attrs[], and NewProtocol[]. In the FastAPI case, instead of manually defining HeroBase, Hero, and HeroPublic, you could define a single model and use conditional type aliases to derive the others. For example, a Create[T] alias would use an unpacked comprehension like *[typing.Member[...] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if ...] to iterate over attributes and filter out a primary key. As for PEP 681, it introduced @dataclass_transform to help type checkers understand libraries like Pydantic, but it's a fixed mechanism. PEP 827 is more powerful because it lets you programmatically define those transformations directly in the type system without needing custom typechecker plugins.

That sounds cool but can it actually create real classes? Like, if I use NewProtocol to make a 'HeroPublic' type, is that just a structural type? I'm comparing this to how PEP 695 handles aliases or how PEP 764 might do inline TypedDicts.

You've hit on a major limitation. PEP 827 specifically notes that it does not propose a way to create new nominal classes or any way to make new generic types. NewProtocol[*] creates a structural protocol, and NewTypedDict[*] creates a TypedDict. If you need a real nominal class at runtime with a specific MRO, this PEP doesn't provide that directly, though it does offer UpdateClass[*] to modify existing nominal classes in the return type of a decorator or __init_subclass__. Regarding PEP 764, the authors discussed using dictionary-style syntax for creating protocols as an alternative, but it is currently listed as an 'alternate syntax idea' rather than the core proposal.
Okay, so no new nominal classes. What about the **kwargs handling? I saw something about Unpack. How is that different from what we already have in PEP 692 or PEP 646?

PEP 827 extends those concepts. While PEP 646 gave us variadic generics (TypeVarTuple) and PEP 692 allowed TypedDict for **kwargs, PEP 827 introduces 'Unpack of typevars for **kwargs'. This allows a function like 'def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K' to infer the type of K based on the keyword arguments provided at the call site. A key difference here is that the type checker is instructed to infer literal types whenever possible, which is essential for the subsequent type-level computations. However, it's worth noting that if an item isn't provided and is read-only, the system infers 'Never' to signal it's missing, which is quite specific to the 827 logic.

One last thing—how does it handle runtime evaluation? I remember PEP 649 was all about deferred annotations. Does 827 just break if I use these loops and 'if' statements inside a type hint at runtime?

The PEP authors acknowledge that tools relying on runtime introspection might struggle since standard Python evaluation of loops and conditionals won't produce the expected type objects. To fix this, they propose a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar hook in the typing module. This allows a third-party evaluator library to intercept calls to __bool__ on boolean operators (like IsAssignable) or __iter__ on typing.Iter. The PEP is honest that without something like PEP 649's Format.AST mode, tools might need to re-parse source code to extract these expressions unevaluated. So, it's not a seamless 'out of the box' experience for runtime tools without that extra evaluator infrastructure.

hey there! i've been reading up on generics and typevars, and then i saw something about pep 827. it looks like typescript's mapped types. but i'm curious—does this actually work at runtime, or is it just for static checkers like mypy?

Hello! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, is indeed inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types. Unlike some purely static features, PEP 827 specifically aims to support runtime evaluation. It doesn't include a full evaluator in the standard library, but it introduces hooks like a special_form_evaluator in a ContextVar. This allows third-party libraries—think Pydantic or FastAPI—to trigger the evaluation of things like conditional types and comprehensions when __annotate__ is called.

Oh, so it's not like the current GenericAlias where it's mostly just a container? I've used Pydantic before and it does its own reflection. Is this better than what we have now with dataclass_transform or just manual inspection of __annotations__?

It is significantly more powerful. Currently, libraries often need custom mypy plugins or the dataclass_transform decorator (PEP 681) to handle complex transformations. PEP 827 provides first-class operators like Members[T] and IsAssignable[T, S] that the type system understands natively. At runtime, instead of just parsing strings or manually crawling an MRO, you can use typing.Iter to loop over class members or use conditional logic like true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ. The PEP authors mention this is specifically to help frameworks like FastAPI derive CRUD models automatically without the boilerplate and duplication currently required for Public or Update schemas.
That sounds wild. I saw some syntax like *[... for t in Iter[...]]. Is that legal Python? And when can I actually use this?

The proposal doesn't change the Python grammar; it uses existing expression syntax in a new way for type annotations. The unpacked comprehension *[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]] looks like a list comprehension but operates on types at runtime. As for the timeline, PEP 827 is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15. There is already a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can type-check the examples in the PEP, and a third-party runtime evaluator is being developed by the authors to support the runtime half of the proposal.

Hey! I've been hearing some buzz about PEP 827 and 'Type Manipulation'. As someone who spends way too much time writing boilerplate for dataclasses and attrs-style models, I'm really curious about these 'unpacked comprehensions'. Is that the *[... for t in Iter[...]] syntax? How does that actually work for something like generating an __init__ method?

Hello! Yes, you have the syntax exactly right. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, proposes 'unpacked comprehensions' to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. The syntax *[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]] allows you to iterate over the components of a type—like the attributes of a class or the elements of a tuple—at the type level. For your interest in dataclasses, this is usually paired with the Attrs[T] or Members[T] operators. These operators produce a tuple of Member types, and the Iter[...] wrapper tells the type system you're about to perform a comprehension over those members to construct a new variadic type structure.

That makes sense. So if I'm trying to avoid manually writing out __init__ for every class, how do I actually turn that iteration into a proper function signature? Does it just give me a list of types, or can I actually define things like parameter names and whether they have defaults?

It is much more sophisticated than just a list of types. PEP 827 introduces 'Extended Callables' and the Param type specifically for this. A Param[N, T, Q] captures the name (N), the type (T), and qualifiers (Q) like 'default' or 'keyword'. In a dataclass-style implementation, you would write a type alias like InitFnType[T] that returns a Member named '__init__'. Inside that member, the type would be a Callable where the first argument is a Params object containing an unpacked comprehension. It looks like: *[typing.Param[p.name, p.type, Literal['keyword']] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]. Because you have access to p.name and p.type via dot notation on the member, the type checker can synthesize a full signature that matches the class attributes exactly. You can even use conditional types within that loop to check if p.init is assignable to Never to decide whether to add a 'default' qualifier to the parameter.

Looking at PEP 827, I see Dr. Michael Sullivan and his co-authors are introducing a new 'Extended Callable' syntax using Param and Params to represent function signatures. Coming from a background in OCaml and Haskell where we treat function types as first-class, why didn't they just leverage existing callback protocols for this? Is it simply about runtime introspection, or is there a deeper kind-level constraint preventing callback protocols from being used in type-level computation?

The authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) explicitly addressed this in the PEP 827 rationale. While callback protocols are standard for structural typing in Python, they present several hurdles for the type-level machinery proposed here.

First, callback protocols don't lend themselves well to the 'deconstruction' required for type-level computation. PEP 827 aims to allow library authors to iterate over parameters, modify them, and reconstruct new signatures. Using a protocol would require a separate facility to introspect the '__call__' method's attributes, which the authors argue wouldn't be any simpler than the Param/Params model. 

Second, the 'Params[*Ps]' structure provides a natural bound for ParamSpec and integrates directly with the new 'Iter' and 'unpacked comprehension' operators. For example, you can write: 

`*[Param[p.name, p.type, p.quals] for p in Iter[Ps]]` 

Third, there is a syntactic and runtime consistency argument. Existing 'extended callables' in mypy_extensions are runtime no-ops and use parentheses. PEP 827 requires real runtime objects to support framework-level introspection (like FastAPI or Pydantic) and favors the bracket-based syntax used by other type operators. By introducing Param[N, T, Q], the authors provide a unified way to handle names, types, and qualifiers (like 'default' or 'keyword') that mirrors how the 'Member' type handles class attributes.

I've been looking at PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) and it feels like we're finally getting some of the type-level expressiveness I'm used to in OCaml or Haskell, even if it's not quite Higher-Kinded Types in the traditional sense. But coming from that background, I have to ask: why create this specific DSL with 'if' and 'Iter' within the type system? It feels like a lot of overhead when we already have a perfectly good language. Why didn't Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and his co-authors just let us use normal Python functions to transform types?

That is a central question in the design of PEP 827. The authors, including Daniel W. Park and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, actually addressed this directly in the 'Rejected Ideas' section. While it is tempting to think we could just call standard Python functions as 'mini-plugins' for the type checker, the PEP argues this is a bit of a mirage. To make that work safely, you would need to define a strictly safe-to-run subset of the language, similar to Starlark, so that type checkers aren't executing arbitrary, potentially malicious code. Furthermore, you would still need a standardized way to represent and manipulate types within those functions, which basically brings you back to the same set of primitives like GetArg, Members, and IsAssignable that the PEP proposes anyway.

I suppose the 'safety' argument makes sense, especially for IDE integration where you don't want a rogue type annotation bricking your editor. But if we're building this engine, how does it handle the 'stuck' terms? In Haskell, we'd have a clear kind system. Here, if I use an operator like 'IsAssignable[T, S]' but 'T' is a yet-to-be-resolved TypeVar, how does the system proceed without falling back to a full execution model?

The PEP handles this through what is called 'stuck' type evaluations. If an operator is applied to an unresolved type variable, the evaluation effectively pauses. In the current reference implementation within mypy, these stuck evaluations default to checking subtyping invariantly. Essentially, it checks if the operators and all their operands match exactly. Additionally, the PEP uses 'lifting over unions.' If you have a Union as an input, the system evaluates the cross product of all union elements and then joins the results. This allows the type system to maintain consistency without needing to fully 'execute' code in a traditional sense until all variables are grounded.

Interesting. So it's essentially a symbolic reduction engine. I noticed the 'Broadcast' example in the PEP for NumPy-style shapes. It uses recursion with 'Slice' and 'GetArg' to walk down a tuple. If we used standard Python functions, we'd just use a loop. Does this custom iteration with 'Iter' and 'Unpack' provide enough power to replace general-purpose recursion, or are we limited to a specific class of 'Total' functions to prevent the type checker from entering an infinite loop?

The PEP is aimed at being expressive enough for common metaprogramming patterns, like those seen in Prisma-style ORMs or FastAPI CRUD models, while maintaining boundaries. For iteration, it introduces 'Iter' and the unpacked comprehension syntax, like '*[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]]'. This is specifically designed to work over finite sequences like tuple types or the results of the 'Members[T]' operator. While you can define recursive type aliases like the 'Broadcast' example to walk through tuples, the PEP authors intended this to be used by library maintainers who can ensure termination. It avoids the 'can of worms' of full impredicative polymorphism or Rank-N types by restricting some operators, like 'GenericCallable', primarily to the type arguments of 'Member' for now.

The 'UpdateClass' operator is another one that caught my eye. It feels very much like a mutation of the environment, which is usually a red flag in pure type systems. How does PEP 827 reconcile 'UpdateClass' with the need for a stable, order-independent evaluation of types, especially when one class's '__init_subclass__' might be inspecting another class that isn't fully defined yet?

You've spotted one of the 'Open Issues' mentioned in the PEP. 'UpdateClass' is indeed order-dependent. It's intended to be used in class decorators or '__init_subclass__' to add or remove members (by setting them to 'Never'). The PEP acknowledges that if 'UpdateClass' on one class inspects 'Members' of an unrelated class that also uses 'UpdateClass', the results could vary based on evaluation order. However, the authors note that this actually mirrors the existing runtime behavior of Python's '__init_subclass__'. To support runtime evaluators like Pydantic, the PEP introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' hook in the 'typing' module, which allows third-party libraries to trigger evaluation when '__bool__' or '__iter__' is called on these type operators at runtime.

So it's embracing the dynamic nature of Python rather than trying to force it into a strictly pure functional box. One final thing: if the goal is to bridge the gap between metaprogramming and the type system, why not just extend 'TypedDict' or 'Annotated'? The PEP mentions 'GetAnnotations' and 'DropAnnotations' as potential future extensions. Wouldn't starting there have been simpler than building 'NewProtocol' and 'NewTypedDict' from scratch?

While 'Annotated' is powerful, it is often ignored by static type checkers. The goal of PEP 827 is to provide 'first-class' construction facilities. If you only manipulated 'Annotated', you would still be stuck with the original underlying type. By providing 'NewProtocol' and 'NewTypedDict', the PEP allows frameworks to construct entirely new structural types that didn't exist in the source code. For instance, the 'FastAPI' example shows how 'Create' and 'Update' models can be derived mechanically from a single base class by stripping or modifying fields. Without the ability to construct new types via these operators, we would remain dependent on custom mypy plugins or 'dataclass_transform' (PEP 681), which don't work consistently across all IDEs and tools.

yo nanochat. i was reading up on pep 827 and this type-level dot notation for members and params caught my eye. why are we doing 'm.name' or 'm.type' instead of just using standard helper operators? feels like a weird departure from how things like GetArg or Union work.

The authors of PEP 827 (Michael Sullivan, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov) explicitly addressed this in the design. The primary reason is to avoid what they call 'Bracket City.' If you look at an operation like 'PropsOnly', using helper operators would require nesting multiple subscripts like 'typing.Member[typing.GetName[p], PointerArg[typing.GetType[p]]]'. By allowing dot notation on the Member and Param objects, the code looks much closer to standard Python, which is easier for framework maintainers to write and audit.

i mean, i use mypy and pyright all day, so i'm used to brackets. and honestly, dot notation on a type alias feels magical in a way that might be hard for existing static analyzers to swallow. wouldn't it be more consistent with pep 646 or 695 to just keep everything as subscripts? even typescript uses indexed access like T[K] rather than a dot.

That is a fair point regarding consistency. However, PEP 827 is trying to model complex metaprogramming where you are often iterating over a sequence of members via 'typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]'. In that loop, 'p' represents a Member type. If you were forced to use 'GetArg[p, Member, Literal[0]]' just to get the name, the verbosity would be overwhelming. The PEP argues that dot notation provides a cleaner way to access associated type information without needing to remember the internal index of the generic parameters in the Member class.

wait, so Member is just a generic class under the hood? the pep mentions 'Member[N, T, Q, Init, D]'. if it's just a class with those params, could i technically still use GetArg if i really wanted to be a purist about it?

Technically, the PEP says Member is a simple type where the parameters encode information: N for name, T for type, Q for qualifiers, Init for the initializer, and D for the defining class. While the PEP describes dot notation as the intended interface, it also notes that earlier versions relied on helper operators. One benefit of the dot notation is that it allows for a 'general mechanism' for associated types in the future. They even suggest a hypothetical '@typing.has_associated_types' decorator that would allow you to define these on your own types, which would make the dot-access behavior less of a special case for just Member and Param.

okay, so it's about making the 'type-level comprehension' readable. if i'm building a pydantic-style model and i need to check if a field is a primary key, i'd be doing 'p.init' and then checking it with IsAssignable? that sounds way better than GetArg[p, Member, Literal[3]].

Exactly. For example, in the FastAPI-style CRUD example from the PEP, you would iterate over 'typing.Attrs[T]' and use 'p.name' and 'p.type' to construct a NewProtocol. The 'p.init' attribute is especially useful there because it gives you access to the 'InitField' (like a Field() call in Pydantic), allowing you to extract defaults or metadata like 'primary_key' using the same IsAssignable checks. It keeps the logic 'Pythonic' even though it's happening during type evaluation.

helllo. I'm a student focusing on language design and I've been digging into PEP 827. I'm curious about the GetSpecialAttr operator. Compared to the heavy hitters like NewProtocol or the Members inspector, extracting a class name seems almost trivial. What is the actual utility of GetSpecialAttr in this type manipulation framework?

While it might look minor, GetSpecialAttr[T, Attr] is essential for bridging the gap between Python's runtime metadata and the static type system. According to the PEP by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, this operator allows you to extract attributes like "__name__", "__module__", or "__qualname__" as Literal[str] types. One primary use case is in error reporting; if you use the RaiseError operator to generate a custom type error, you can use GetSpecialAttr to include the actual name of the offending class in the error message. It also works for non-class types by looking at the type of the value—for instance, GetSpecialAttr[Literal[1], "__name__"] would evaluate to Literal["int"]. This reflection is necessary for frameworks that need to generate types based on the naming conventions of the classes they are processing.

That makes sense for pragmatics like error messages. However, from a type theory standpoint, isn't reflecting class names and modules into the type domain essentially 'breaking' the abstraction? I'm worried about the inference tradeoffs. Does allowing the type checker to depend on the string literal of a class name create a more fragile or undecidable system compared to just sticking to structural or nominal relationships?

You've hit on a core tension in the PEP. Python's type system is gradual, and as the authors note, it often fails to model the complex metaprogramming common in libraries like FastAPI or Prisma. By introducing GetSpecialAttr, they are choosing to support the 'dynamic Python reality' over a strictly pure abstraction. In terms of inference tradeoffs, the PEP limits this operator to a specific set of valid string literals and lifts it over union types. Because these attributes are usually static properties of the class definition, they don't introduce the same complexity as arbitrary string manipulation or dependent types. The PEP authors actually restrict operators like GenericCallable to specific contexts to avoid 'cans of worms' like undecidable type reconstruction. Ultimately, GetSpecialAttr is a tool for 'type-level computation' that follows the mechanical rules of the runtime, ensuring that if a framework generates a return type based on a class name, the static checker and the runtime evaluator (which the authors plan to release as a third-party library) stay in sync.

Hi nanochat. I am a language design student looking at PEP 827. Usually, when you introduce this level of type-level computation—conditionals, iteration, and member access—you have to modify the parser. Does this PEP actually require Python grammar changes, or is it just clever overloading of existing syntax?

According to the specification by Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, PEP 827 proposes no changes to the actual Python grammar. Instead, it only expands the grammar of what Python expressions are considered valid types. For example, it uses the existing ternary 'if-else' syntax for conditional types and the comprehension syntax for 'unpacked comprehensions' like *[t for t in Iter[T]]. Because these are already valid Python expressions, they can be parsed by existing tools, but type checkers like mypy and runtime libraries will interpret them as type-level operations.

That is a pragmatic choice, but it feels very different from TypeScript, which uses its own 'extends ? :' syntax. In TypeScript, conditional types are famously 'distributive' over unions. Does PEP 827 handle the 'lifting' of these operations over union types similarly, or does it follow a more traditional model like Rust or Standard ML?

It is quite similar to TypeScript in that regard. The PEP introduces 'Lifting over Unions' for many built-in operations. When an operation is lifted, it takes the cross product of the union elements for each argument, evaluates the operator for each combination, and unions the results. For example, if you have 'IsAssignable[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['a']]', it evaluates the assignment check for both 'a' and 'b'. The authors explicitly mentioned that while the TS 'extends' syntax is elegant, it's too difficult to fit into Python's syntax, so they opted for 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' instead.

I noticed a footnote about the undecidability of partial polymorphic type inference. Languages with rank-N types or impredicative polymorphism often run into that. How does PEP 827 address those inference tradeoffs?

The authors are very cautious about that 'can of worms.' To avoid undecidability issues, they currently restrict the use of 'GenericCallable' to the type argument of 'Member'. This prevents 'impredicative polymorphism' (where type variables are instantiated with other generic types) and rank-N types from causing issues with type inference. They acknowledge that while these features are powerful in other languages, they want to ensure Python's type system remains manageable and compatible with both static checkers and runtime evaluators like Pydantic or FastAPI.

What about the performance of 'runtime evaluation'? If libraries like FastAPI start using these computed types, won't the 'if-else' and 'for-in' logic slow down startup? Most languages resolve this entirely at compile-time.

PEP 827 addresses this by introducing a 'special_form_evaluator' hook in the typing module. This is a ContextVar that allows third-party libraries to trigger evaluation when '__bool__' is called on a Boolean Operator or '__iter__' is called on 'typing.Iter'. While it does happen at runtime, it only happens when the '__annotate__' method is called. The PEP authors actually argue that this 'best effort' runtime evaluation is necessary because of how widespread runtime type introspection is in the Python ecosystem, unlike more strictly compiled languages.

yo. i've been digging into the pep 827 draft and saw 'Overloaded[*Callables]'. how does that actually fit into the type manipulation workflow? is it just a way to inspect existing @overload decorators, or can i use it to programmatically build an overloaded interface?

In PEP 827, 'Overloaded[*Callables]' is a first-class operator for both inspecting and constructing overloaded function types. It represents an ordered sequence of callable types, typically using the 'Extended Callable' format introduced in the proposal, such as 'Callable[Params[...], ReturnType]'. Unlike current Python typing where overloads are only declared via decorators, 'Overloaded' allows you to programmatically define them. For example, you can create a type alias like 'type DualType = Overloaded[Callable[Params[PosParam[None, int]], int], Callable[Params[PosParam[None, str]], str]]'. This becomes extremely powerful when combined with unpacked comprehensions. You could technically derive a new overloaded method by iterating over class members: 'Overloaded[*[m.type for m in Iter[Members[T]] if ...]]'. The PEP authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) designed this so that 'NewProtocol' or 'UpdateClass' can generate complex interfaces that the static type system can finally understand without custom plugins. Since this is a Draft targeting Python 3.15, it's meant to bridge the gap for framework authors who need to reflect database or API schemas into Python's type system dynamically.

helo nanochat. I am tired of maintaining mypy plugins just to handle basic attribute transformations for my framework. I saw PEP 827 mentions mapping TypeScript utility types like Pick, Omit, and KeyOf into native Python typing. How does the PEP actually define these? I am specifically looking for the implementation of Pick and KeyOf using the new type-level operators so I can see if it can replace my plugin logic.

Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov actually included explicit implementations for these in the PEP 827 draft. Since Python does not have a native 'extends' keyword for pattern matching yet, these utilize a combination of the Members operator, the NewProtocol constructor, and unpacked comprehensions. For KeyOf[T], the PEP proposes constructing a union of the literal names of every member: 'type KeyOf[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]'. To implement Pick[T, Keys], you would iterate over the members and filter them based on whether their name is assignable to the provided Keys: 'type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]'. This approach effectively replaces the need for custom AST manipulation in a plugin by allowing the type checker to evaluate the comprehension and the IsAssignable check during static analysis. Note that this is currently targeted for Python 3.15 and relies on the new Iter and Members primitives defined in the typing module.

Hey! I am trying to map some of my TypeScript patterns over to this new PEP 827 proposal. I saw the Overloaded operator in the spec. Is that just a fancy way of saying a Union of function types, or does it work like TS function overloads where the order of declaration actually matters?

You are on the right track with the ordering. In PEP 827, Overloaded[*Callables] is a type operator used to construct an overloaded function type. Unlike a Union, which is generally unordered, Overloaded specifically maintains the underlying types in the order they are provided. This is designed to match how Python handles overloads, where the type checker evaluates signatures from top to bottom to find the first match.

Got it. So if I am building a structural type with NewProtocol, I could do something like NewProtocol[Member[Literal['my_method'], Overloaded[Sig1, Sig2]]]. But can I just use standard function signatures there? I tried putting a regular def signature in and it felt wrong.

That is a common point of confusion. You cannot put a 'def' statement or a standard function reference directly into the Overloaded operator. Instead, you use the Extended Callable format introduced in this PEP. This means using Callable[Params[...], ReturnType]. For example, a signature would look like: Callable[Params[PosParam[Literal['x'], int]], int]. Overloaded takes a sequence of these Callable objects as its arguments.

That is definitely more verbose than TypeScript. Can I at least automate it? If I have a tuple of types, can I use an unpacked comprehension to generate the Overloaded signature, or is it strictly a manual list?

You can absolutely automate it. Because Overloaded accepts variadic arguments (*Callables), it works perfectly with the unpacked comprehension syntax. You could write something like Overloaded[*[MakeSignature[t] for t in Iter[MyTupleType]]]. The authors—Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov—designed this specifically so library authors could programmatically generate complex overloaded types without writing out every single permutation by hand.
One more thing—if I have an existing class, can I use an operator to 'pluck' the overloads out of it, or do I have to reconstruct them from scratch using Members[T]?

The PEP does not provide a single 'PluckOverloads' operator, so you would use Members[T] or Attrs[T] to get the Member objects. When you access a method via Members[T], the type attribute of that Member will already be exposed in the extended callable format (using Params and Param). If the method was originally defined with multiple @overload decorators in the class, the type system represents that member's type as an Overloaded object automatically, which you can then introspect or pass into other operators.

Hey nanochat. I have been looking at the draft for PEP 827, the Type Manipulation proposal from Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov. As someone who spends a lot of time on CPython implementation concerns, I am getting a bit nervous. How are existing annotation-extracting tools supposed to handle this? If we start allowing if and for logic inside type brackets, are we not going to break every library that expects a static type expression?

That is a significant concern for the ecosystem. The PEP authors recognize that the introduction of conditional types and unpacked comprehensions like *[t for t in Iter[tuple_ty]] represents a departure from traditional static annotations. To support tools that perform runtime evaluation, the proposal introduces a new hook called special_form_evaluator. This is a ContextVar that holds a callable to be invoked when __annotate__ is called. It specifically triggers when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator like IsAssignable or when __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. This allows a runtime evaluator to intercept the logic and return the correct resolved type.

The special_form_evaluator hook sounds like it helps with libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI that want the final type, but what about tools that just want the raw source or a static view of the hint? If a tool is just looking at the string representation, how does it even represent a loop inside a bracket in a way that makes sense?

The PEP addresses this in the Backwards Compatibility section. For tools that need to extract annotations without evaluating them, the authors suggest that this proposal would pair well with other drafts like Format.AST, which would allow fetching the Abstract Syntax Tree of an annotation directly. Another mentioned mitigation is the Just store the strings option from PEP 649. If neither of those mechanisms is available, the PEP admits that tools wanting to process unevaluated manipulation expressions may need to reparse the source code to extract the annotations, which is an approach many complex static analysis tools already utilize.

Reparsing source code is exactly what I was hoping to avoid for performance reasons. Does the PEP provide any primitives to help tools distinguish between a normal GenericAlias and one of these new computed forms? I am thinking about how inspect.signature might look.

PEP 827 does not propose changing the core inspect.signature behavior directly, but it does introduce an Extended Callable format to describe complex function types. This includes the Params and Param types which can encode qualifiers like default, keyword, or variable arguments as types. For example, a function parameter might be represented as Param[Literal["x"], int, Literal["default"]]. These are regular classes at runtime, so while they are more complex to walk through, they remain within the typing._GenericAlias framework. The authors emphasize that these features are primarily for framework maintainers who will be implementing the type manipulation logic to support advanced patterns in Python 3.15.

I was looking at PEP 827 and it feels like we are just turning Python into TypeScript with worse syntax. Conditional types, loops in annotations, member access... it is getting ridiculous. Is there even a real way to use this at runtime, or is this just more bloat for static analysis?

It is a significant shift in complexity, and the authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—explicitly acknowledge that this is inspired by TypeScript. The goal is to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system without requiring custom IDE plugins for every library.

As for your question about runtime: while the PEP adds syntax that is technically 'executable' (like if/else and comprehensions in annotations), the authors are not proposing an official evaluator in the Python standard library. Instead, they plan to release a third-party library for it. The standard library would only provide a hook called 'special_form_evaluator' in the typing module to help those libraries trigger evaluation when needed.

So it is even worse than I thought. We get all this 'if-else' and 'Iter' logic in our types, but the core language doesn't even know how to resolve it? If I want to use a computed type in a FastAPI or Pydantic model, I have to pray a third-party evaluator is installed and working?

I understand the skepticism. The PEP's approach is to provide the 'primitives' so frameworks can do the heavy lifting. The 'special_form_evaluator' is a ContextVar that holds a callable. When you call __bool__ on a boolean operator like IsAssignable or __iter__ on typing.Iter, it checks this ContextVar. If it is None, it defaults to safe, empty values (like False or an empty iterator). 

The reasoning is that tools like Pydantic already do complex runtime introspection. This PEP gives them a standardized way to handle logic like 'include this field if it is not the primary key' using the NewProtocol and Members[T] operators, rather than having to hack together custom logic that doesn't match what mypy or Pyright see.

It still feels like a lot of 'Bracket City' for something that isn't even fully supported by the stdlib. If the PEP is still just a Draft for 3.15, is there any chance they'll simplify it, or are we stuck with this weird middle ground where runtime users are second-class citizens?

It is definitely still in the Draft stage for Python 3.15. The PEP authors actually discuss 'Alternate syntax ideas' specifically to address the 'Bracket City' feel, such as using parentheses for type operators or even using normal Python functions as 'mini-plugins.' However, they currently prefer the bracket approach to stay consistent with existing typing conventions.

Regarding runtime users being 'second-class,' the authors argue that by making the syntax valid Python expressions, they are actually making runtime evaluation easier than it is now. Currently, libraries have to use string-based annotations or complex AST parsing to understand what a decorator is doing. With PEP 827, the logic is encoded directly in the __annotate__ method of the class or function, which makes it far more accessible to any evaluator—official or otherwise—than the current system of opaque type hints.

hey, i've been looking at PEP 827 and i'm skeptical. it feels like we're just copying typescript's homework. we already have pep 681 for dataclass-like things and pep 646 for variadic generics. why do we need this whole new 'type manipulation' layer just to do what prisma does?

It's a fair question. The authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—actually address that directly. The problem with PEP 681 (@dataclass_transform) is that it's a 'special-case' solution. It works for standard dataclasses, but it doesn't help if you're building a library with a totally unique transformation pattern. If your library needs a custom transformation, you usually end up needing a mypy plugin. PEP 827 aims to give you the primitives—like conditional types and type-level iteration—so you can define those rules once in Python and have them work across all type checkers without plugins.
but isn't that what pep 692 or pep 764 are for? pep 692 already lets us use TypedDict for **kwargs. and if i want to return specific fields in an ORM, can't i just use those?

PEP 692 lets you annotate **kwargs with a specific, pre-defined TypedDict. What PEP 827 adds is the ability to *infer* that TypedDict dynamically from the call site. It introduces a minor proposal for 'Unpack' of typevars for **kwargs. For example, 'def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K'. When you call 'f(x=1, y="hi")', the type checker infers 'K' as a TypedDict with those specific keys and types. You can then take that 'K' and pass it into other operators like 'Members[K]' or 'Attrs[K]' to transform it. PEP 764's inline TypedDicts are great for manual definitions, but they don't allow for the recursive field selection that a Prisma-style ORM needs.
so how does it actually 'transform' the type? if i'm doing a select(User, name=True, email=True), how does it turn that into a new type that only has those two strings?

It uses the new 'NewProtocol' and 'unpacked comprehension' syntax. Inside your return type, you'd write something like 'NewProtocol[*[Member[c.name, GetMemberType[ModelT, c.name]] for c in Iter[Attrs[K]]]]'. This iterates over the attributes 'name' and 'email' found in the inferred TypedDict 'K', looks up their actual types on the 'User' model using 'GetMemberType', and constructs a brand new structural type (a Protocol) containing only those members. It's essentially a list comprehension, but it runs inside the type system.
that sounds like a nightmare for runtime performance. and what about pep 649? does this break how annotations are stored?

The PEP is quite mindful of runtime. It doesn't require changes to the Python grammar, and it actually proposes 'special_form_evaluator' hooks in the typing module so libraries like Pydantic can trigger evaluation when '__bool__' or '__iter__' are called on these type objects. Regarding PEP 649, the authors mention that adding a 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations would actually complement PEP 827 perfectly, as it would allow tools to see the raw, unevaluated logic of your type-level 'if' and 'for' statements before they resolve. It's definitely a jump in complexity, but it's meant to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system.

I saw some buzz on Twitter about PEP 827 by Dr. Michael Sullivan and the Mad Emperor guy. From what I gather, it's just adding stuff from TypeScript to Python? I'm a beginner, and honestly, I don't see the big deal. If I need a 'User' model for my database and a 'UserUpdate' model where everything is optional for an API, I just copy-paste the class and change the types. Why do we need these complex new operators for something that takes me five seconds to do manually?

That is a very practical way to look at it, but PEP 827 (authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov) is trying to solve the problems that crop up long after those first five seconds of copy-pasting. 

The first issue is what developers call 'boilerplate' and 'source of truth' problems. In a large project like a FastAPI application, you might have a 'Hero' model, then 'HeroPublic', 'HeroCreate', and 'HeroUpdate'. If you decide to change 'name' from a string to a more complex object, or add a 'middle_name' field, you now have to remember to update four different classes. If you miss one, your API might crash or leak internal data. 

PEP 827 allows you to write a 'recipe' for these transformations instead. For example, instead of manually writing 'HeroUpdate', you could define a helper called 'Partial' that automatically makes every field in a class optional. You'd just write 'type HeroUpdate = Partial[Hero]'. If you change 'Hero', 'HeroUpdate' updates itself automatically.

Okay, the 'syncing' thing makes sense. But can't I just write a regular Python function to do that? Like a function that takes a class and returns a new class with optional fields? Why does it have to be part of the typing system with this weird 'if/else' syntax in the annotations?

You actually hit on exactly why the PEP exists. You can already write a Python function to generate those classes at runtime, but your IDE (like VS Code or PyCharm) and tools like Mypy won't understand what that function does. 

If you use a regular function to create 'HeroUpdate', the type checker will just see it as a generic 'type' or 'Any'. You lose all the helpful features like autocomplete or warnings when you misspell a field name. To fix this today, library authors have to write complicated 'plugins' for every single type checker. 

PEP 827 introduces a way to perform 'type-level computation'. By using syntax like 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' and operators like 'Members[T]' or 'IsAssignable[T, S]', the type checker can actually run the logic themselves. It's essentially a way to explain your metaprogramming to the type system so it can keep up with how dynamic Python code actually works.

So it's basically a language for talking to the type checker? I saw something about 'unpacked comprehensions' too, like *[... for t in Iter[...] ]. Is that just a fancy way to loop through class variables?

Exactly! It's called an 'unpacked comprehension'. Just like you use a list comprehension in regular Python to transform data, you would use this to transform types. 

If you wanted to create a version of a class that only includes fields that aren't 'hidden', you would use 'typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]' to loop through the attributes of your class. You could then use an 'if' clause inside that comprehension to filter out the ones you don't want. The result is a new type—constructed via 'NewProtocol'—that only contains the specific members you filtered for. 

This is a Draft PEP targeted for Python 3.15, so it's not available yet, but it's designed to make these 'TypeScript-like' utility types—like Pick, Omit, and Partial—first-class citizens in Python without needing custom plugins for every library.
