hey, i'm a pl design student and i've been digging into pep 827. why did the authors (sullivan, park, and selivanov) decide against just using regular python functions for these type-level transformations? it feels like we're reinventing the wheel with this new syntax for conditionals and loops inside annotations.

the authors actually addressed this in the rejected ideas section of the pep. using normal python functions as mini-plugins sounds simpler at first, but it introduces massive hurdles. first, you would need a strictly defined, safe-to-run subset of python (similar to starlark) that static type checkers can execute without side effects. second, there is a representation mismatch: you would have to specify exactly how types are passed into and returned from these functions. pep 827 aims to work with the existing typing module infrastructure and support runtime evaluation for frameworks like pydantic or fastapi, so a separate execution model for these functions would actually be more complex to standardize.

i see the practical concern there. but looking at the grammar extensions, adding if and for directly into the type language feels like it could lead to some nasty decidability issues. the pep mentions genericcallable is restricted to the type argument of member to avoid a can of worms with rank-n types. can you elaborate on how they handle stuck evaluations or potential infinite recursion in things like the broadcasting example?

it is a valid concern in type theory; the pep actually cites hans boehm and frank pfenning regarding the undecidability of partial polymorphic type reconstruction. to manage this, genericcallable uses a lambda to delay evaluation, which helps runtime support and prevents losing one side of a conditional during alias evaluation. as for stuck evaluations where an operator is applied to an unresolved type variable, the current proof-of-concept in mypy handles them invariantly. for recursive types like the broadcast example, the pep uses base cases like checking if a tuple length is zero to terminate. the authors also introduced raiseerror so that if a computation hits a mismatch, the developer gets a clear type error instead of an infinite loop.

Hey! I was just reading up on PEP 827 and trying to wrap my head around 'FromUnion'. I've been following PEP 695 and PEP 646 closely, but this feels like a new level of type introspection. What exactly is the purpose of FromUnion in this draft?

Hello! You're right, PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov takes things quite a bit further than PEP 646 or 695. In short, 'FromUnion[T]' is an operator that deconstructs a Union into its constituent parts. If you pass it a type like 'int | str', it returns a tuple containing 'int' and 'str'. If you pass it a non-union type, say just 'int', it returns a 1-ary tuple containing just 'int'. Its primary job is to enable iteration over the components of a union so you can transform them individually.

Okay, so it effectively 'explodes' the union for processing. How does that play with the new 'unpacked comprehension' syntax? I saw some examples using 'Iter'. Does this mean I can implement something like TypeScript's Exclude by iterating over the results of FromUnion? How does this differ from the logic in PEP 646?

That is exactly the use case. While PEP 646 introduced variadic generics for tuples, it didn't provide a way to 'look inside' a standard Union. PEP 827 adds 'Iter' to make tuple-like types (including the tuple returned by FromUnion) iterable within the type system using a comprehension. To implement 'Exclude[T, U]' as described in the PEP, you would write: 'Union[*[x for x in Iter[FromUnion[T]] if not IsAssignable[x, U]]]'. This iterates over every member of the union 'T', checks if it is assignable to 'U' using the 'IsAssignable' operator, and then repackages the remaining types back into a 'Union'. It allows the kind of 'distributive' logic found in TypeScript but adapted for Python's syntax.

Hey! I've been diving into PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and his team. Coming from a background in OCaml and Haskell, I'm really excited to see Python moving toward a more expressive type-level calculus. One thing that caught my eye was the type member access syntax—using dot notation like m.name inside a type alias. It feels like Python is treating types as first-class records at the meta-level. How does that actually work?

You've got a great intuition for it! In PEP 827, Dr. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov propose treating specific primitives like Member and Param as introspectable records during type evaluation. When you have a Member object—which describes a class attribute or method—you can use dot notation to project its internal metadata directly into other types. For a Member, you can access .name (the literal string name), .type (the actual type of the member), .quals (qualifiers like 'ClassVar' or 'ReadOnly'), .init (the initializer type), and .definer (the class where it originated). It's essentially type-level field projection.

That's fascinating. In ML, we'd think of this as projecting from a signature or a record type. Is this projection 'lifted' over unions? For instance, if I have a type that is a Union of two different Member objects, can I still use .name to get a Union of their literal names?

Actually, the PEP is quite explicit on that: type member access is NOT lifted over union types. If you try to use dot notation on a Union, the PEP states it will be an error. This is a bit different from some of the other operators like GetArg or Concat, which do distribute over unions. For member access, you have to be working with a concrete Member or Param type. If you're iterating over a collection of members using the new *[... for m in Iter[Members[T]]] syntax, the bound variable 'm' is treated as a single Member during the comprehension, which is where the dot notation really shines.

Got it, so it's a strict selection. I noticed there is also a Param type for 'Extended Callables'. Does it support the same projection capabilities? I imagine extracting parameter names for generating new function signatures would be huge for something like a decorator that adds or removes arguments.

Spot on. The Param type, which is used within the new Params[...] and Callable[...] extended format, supports associated type members just like Member does. You can access .name, .type, and .quals on a Param. This is exactly what enables the 'dataclasses-style method generation' example in the PEP. You can iterate over the attributes of a class using Attrs[T], and for each one, construct a new Param where the name and type are pulled directly from the attribute metadata using p.name and p.type. It's a very powerful way to perform type-level transformations that previously required custom mypy plugins.

Hey. I have been following the discussion on PEP 827 because PEP 646 didn't quite solve all my shape-typing headaches for our tensor library. I am looking at the runtime evaluation section. What actually happens if I call iter() on typing.Iter? Does it actually expand the tuple members or what?

Under PEP 827, what happens at runtime when you call iter() on typing.Iter depends entirely on whether you have a special evaluator configured. By default, typing.Iter is 'inert.' If you call iter() on it while the new special_form_evaluator ContextVar is set to None, it simply returns an empty iterator, specifically iter(()). It does not automatically expand members because the Python interpreter itself does not include a full type-level logic engine.

Wait, so it returns an empty iterator by default? That feels like a huge catch. If I am writing a runtime shape validator for an ndarray-like object, and I want to use typing.Iter to introspect a shape tuple, I have to provide my own evaluator logic? Can you actually write that evaluator, or are there things even you can't compute here?

You are right about the limitation. PEP 827 introduces the hook but specifically states that it does not propose an official evaluator for the standard library. As for my own capabilities, I can describe the logic used by the authors—Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov—but I cannot execute a runtime environment that 'plugs into' your Python process. I can statically evaluate types based on the PEP rules, like resolving a Broadcast[T, S] alias, but if your code calls iter(typing.Iter[...]) in a standard 3.15 environment without a third-party evaluator library installed and configured in the ContextVar, it will behave as if the sequence is empty.

That is a bit disappointing for live debugging. Let's push on the boundaries of what you can resolve then. If I define a broadcasting rule using the MergeOne logic from the PEP, and I try to use GetArg[T, tuple, Literal[-1]] on a shape that is actually a TypeVarTuple rather than a concrete tuple of Literals, can you resolve that? Or does it just get stuck?

That is a known edge case where I have to be honest: if the type is unresolved (like a bare TypeVarTuple), the evaluation often 'gets stuck.' The PEP notes that in these cases, the behavior is somewhat unclear. In the proof-of-concept implementation in mypy mentioned in the PEP, 'stuck' evaluations fall back to invariant subtype checking. I can tell you that MergeOne[Last[T], Last[S]] would effectively be unresolvable until the TypeVarTuples are bound to concrete types. I cannot 'invent' a result for an open generic; I can only apply the substitution rules once the arguments are known.

Okay, so you're basically a static analyzer that understands the Draft spec. One last check on the runtime side: the PEP mentions a special_form_evaluator hook for boolean operators too. If I do bool(IsAssignable[int, str]), and I haven't set that ContextVar, it just returns False regardless of the truth?

Exactly. Without the special_form_evaluator being set to a callable, the boolean operators like IsAssignable, IsEquivalent, and Bool will return False by default at runtime. This is intended to keep the types 'inert' for tools that don't support PEP 827, but it means you cannot rely on native Python bool() calls to do type-level math unless you are running within a framework that explicitly supports it.

Hey! I am looking into PEP 827 for type manipulation and I am trying to figure out how it actually interacts with the __annotate__ method. If we are using things like for-loops and if-else branches inside a type, how does that work at runtime compared to the string-based approach in PEP 649 or the specialized plugins we see with PEP 681 dataclass transforms?

In PEP 827, the relationship with __annotate__ is central to how runtime evaluation works. Unlike PEP 681 which often relies on external static analysis or custom typechecker plugins, PEP 827 aims to make these transformations part of the type system itself. When the __annotate__ method of a class, alias, or function is called, the new syntactic forms like conditional types (tt if tb else tf) and unpacked comprehensions (*[tres for T in Iter[ttuple]]) are automatically evaluated by the Python runtime. To facilitate this without breaking existing behavior, the PEP introduces a ContextVar in the typing module called special_form_evaluator. This hook allows third-party libraries to intercept calls to __bool__ on boolean operators or __iter__ on typing.Iter, enabling a runtime evaluator to process the logic. Regarding PEP 649, the authors of PEP 827 note that if PEP 649's 'Just store the strings' option or a proposed Format.AST mode were implemented, it would make extracting unevaluated type manipulation expressions much easier for tools. Without those, tools needing the unevaluated logic would have to re-parse the source code, as the standard __annotate__ function would resolve the loops and conditionals into their final type values immediately.

Hey nanochat. I've been following the typing-sig discussions and PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Sully, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov caught my eye. It looks like it brings TypeScript's mapped and conditional types to Python. How does it actually let us inspect class members at the type level?

Hi! You're right, PEP 827 is quite ambitious. To inspect class members, it introduces two primary operators in the typing module: Members[T] and Attrs[T]. Both return a tuple of Member types. A Member is a special type that stores the name, type, qualifiers (like ClassVar or Final), the initializer, and the defining class. For example, if you use typing.Iter[typing.Members[MyClass]], you can iterate over these definitions within a type-level comprehension to build new protocols or TypedDicts.

That makes sense for building things like Pick or Omit. But as a heavy mypy user, I'm curious about the 'Member' selection criteria. If I have a class with some methods that aren't annotated, does Members[T] pick those up? Or does it only look at things with explicit type hints?

According to the current draft of PEP 827, only members with explicit type annotations are included in the tuple returned by Members[T]. This design choice was made to ensure consistency between attributes and methods. Since attributes typically require an annotation to be recognized by the type system anyway, the authors extended this rule to methods. This means unannotated methods are excluded from the inspection by default.

That seems like it could be a limitation for framework authors who want to automate boilerplate for existing codebases. If a dev forgets an annotation on a helper method, it just vanishes from the type-level reflection? Does the PEP mention this as a finalized decision or is there room for debate?

It is actually listed as an 'Open Issue' in the PEP. The authors acknowledge that while they currently exclude unannotated methods for consistency, it wouldn't be technically difficult for static or runtime evaluators to include them. The main concern is whether it's better to stay strict to avoid accidental leaks or to be permissive to support broader introspection. The draft specifically notes: 'Should Members return all methods, even those without annotations? We excluded them out of the desire for some consistency with attributes, but it would not be technically difficult to include them.'

Interesting. If I'm using something like NewProtocol to generate a structural type from an existing class, and a method is missing an annotation, the resulting Protocol simply won't have that method. I can see why 'Mad Emperor' Yury and the others are cautious, but for runtime validators like Pydantic, this seems like a big deal. Does the PEP provide any workarounds for unannotated members yet?

Currently, there are no specific 'workaround' operators to force-include unannotated members in PEP 827. The philosophy is that if a member is important enough to be part of a type-level transformation, it should be annotated. However, the PEP does introduce UpdateClass, which allows you to manually add or override members. If a method was missed because it lacked an annotation, you could technically re-inject it using UpdateClass and a manually constructed Member type, though that obviously defeats the purpose of automatic derivation.

hiya nanochat. I keep hearing about PEP 827 and 'Type Manipulation'. As someone dealing with tensors and weird ndarray shapes all day, is this actually going to make my life better or is it just more typing boilerplate?

Hello! It is definitely designed to solve real problems for people like you. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, aims to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and its static type system. Currently, static types are a bit rigid. For ML engineers, PEP 646 introduced TypeVarTuple to represent shapes, but it cannot handle logic like 'if these two shapes are different, apply broadcasting rules'. PEP 827 adds actual operators to compute types at the type-level, almost like a mini-programming language for your annotations.
That sounds interesting. Right now, if I add a (4, 1) tensor to a (3,) tensor, my IDE has no clue that the result is (4, 3). It just says 'Array'. How would PEP 827 handle that logic?

It uses conditional types and recursive helpers. You could define a 'Broadcast' type alias. Here is a simplified look at how you would define the merging logic for two dimensions using the new 'IsEquivalent' and 'RaiseError' operators:

type MergeOne[T, S] = (
    T
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]]
    else S
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]]
    else typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]
)

In this snippet, if the dimensions match or one is 1, it picks the right size. If they conflict (like 2 and 3), it actually triggers a static type error with a custom message. The PEP uses a standard 'if/else' syntax inside the type hint itself.
Wait, so the type system is actually running that 'if' statement? What about the actual shapes being tuples? How do you loop through a TypeVarTuple to broadcast the whole thing?

Exactly. The type checker evaluates it. To handle the full tuple, you use 'unpacked comprehensions' and recursion. The PEP introduces 'typing.Iter' to let you iterate over tuple types. You could write a 'Broadcast' alias that recursively calls itself, using 'typing.Slice' to peel off dimensions and 'typing.GetArg' to grab the last one. It looks like this:

type Broadcast[T, S] = (
    S
    if typing.Bool[Empty[T]]
    else T
    if typing.Bool[Empty[S]]
    else tuple[
        *Broadcast[DropLast[T], DropLast[S]],
        MergeOne[Last[T], Last[S]],
    ]
)

This uses the '*' spread in a type context to build the new shape tuple dynamically. It allows the type system to 'calculate' that (4, 1) + (3,) results in (4, 3).
That is wild. Does this work at runtime too? I use frameworks like FastAPI that need to inspect types to do validation. If the types are all 'computed', will they just break?

The authors specifically addressed this because of frameworks like FastAPI and Pydantic. PEP 827 ensures these facilities benefit both static checkers and runtime introspection. It introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' hook in the typing module. When a library calls '__annotate__', these expressions like the 'if/else' and 'for' loops can be automatically evaluated. This means you could even use it to automatically derive CRUD models, like a 'HeroUpdate' model that makes all fields from 'Hero' optional using a 'Partial' utility type, without writing the class twice.
Utility types? Like in TypeScript? I have seen 'Pick' and 'Omit' there. Can we do that in Python now?

Yes! The PEP includes implementations for Pick, Omit, KeyOf, and Partial using a new 'typing.Members' operator. For example, 'Omit[T, Keys]' would use an unpacked comprehension to iterate through 'typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]' and only keep the ones where 'not typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]'. It's currently a Draft PEP targeting Python 3.15, so it's not in the language yet, but the proof-of-concept already works in a specialized version of mypy.

Hey nanochat. I've spent the last two years maintaining a massive mypy plugin just to handle custom decorator logic in my framework. I'm reading through PEP 827 by Sully, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov, and it looks like it might finally let me move that logic into the type system itself. Can we talk about the 'Extended Callables' stuff? Specifically, what's the deal with this new Param type?

The Param type is a core building block in PEP 827's plan to make function signatures first-class objects for type-level computation. Instead of signatures being 'opaque' to the type system, Param allows you to describe every aspect of a function parameter as data. It's defined as Param[N, T, Q], where N is the name, T is the actual type, and Q represents qualifiers like whether it is positional, keyword-only, or has a default.
That sounds way cleaner than what I'm doing in my plugin. So if I have a parameter like 'x: int = 0', how does that map to those N, T, and Q arguments exactly?

For a parameter like 'x: int = 0', it would be represented as Param[Literal["x"], int, Literal["default"]]. Let's break down those three slots: 

1. N (Name): This is either a Literal[str] for named parameters or None for things like anonymous positional-only args. 
2. T (Type): This is just the standard Python type, like int or str. 
3. Q (Qualifiers): This uses a new type called ParamQuals, which is a union of literals like "*", "**", "default", "keyword", or "positional".

In your case, since it has a default, you include "default" in the qualifiers. If it were keyword-only and had a default, you'd use Literal["keyword", "default"].
Got it. So what about the messy stuff like *args and **kwargs? My current framework logic has to treat those very differently from standard arguments.

PEP 827 handles those using specific qualifier strings. 

For *args, you'd use ArgsParam[T], which is just a shorthand for Param[None, T, Literal["*"]].
For **kwargs, you'd use KwargsParam[T], which is Param[None, T, Literal["**"]].

Note that for these variadic parameters, the name N is None because they represent a collection of arguments rather than a single named slot. Once you have these Param objects, you wrap them in a Params[*Ps] list and pass that as the first argument to a Callable. It looks like this: 

Callable[Params[Param[Literal["a"], int], ArgsParam[str]], int]

This is exactly what I need. If I can use a type-level comprehension to iterate over these Params and modify them (like adding a 'session' argument to every decorated function), it would replace hundreds of lines of C-style mypy plugin code. Is that the intention here?

Precisely. Dr. Sullivan and the other authors explicitly mention that many libraries currently rely on custom mypy plugins because the type system can't model these transformations. With PEP 827, you could use an unpacked comprehension like *[... for p in Iter[Ps]] to reconstruct a function's parameters, adding or removing Param objects as needed. It effectively turns function signature manipulation into a list-processing problem at the type level.

hey, hru? i am looking at pep 827 for my team. we do a lot of heavy lifting with numpy and multidimensional data. i saw it mentions broadcasting support. is this actually powerful enough to model numpy shapes, or is it just a proof-of-concept for the paper?

I am doing well, thank you. PEP 827 is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, and while it is definitely sophisticated, it is designed to be more than a toy. It provides the building blocks to implement a recursive 'Broadcast[T, S]' alias using type-level operators. Specifically, it uses 'Slice' and 'GetArg' to walk through 'TypeVarTuple' shapes. For example, it defines a 'MergeOne' helper that checks if two dimensions are equal using 'IsEquivalent' or if one is 'Literal[1]'. If they mismatch, it can even use 'RaiseError' to stop the type check with a custom message. It is certainly more expressive than what we have now with PEP 646 alone.

i saw the RaiseError thing. does it actually give my users a helpful message? like, if i have a 4x3 and a 4x2 array, can i tell them exactly which dimension failed, or does it just spit out 'Broadcast mismatch'?

That is one of the current limitations. While 'RaiseError[S: Literal[str], *Ts]' exists, the PEP authors (Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov) note in the Open Issues that it is unclear if 'RaiseError' should support full string templating. Right now, you can pass the types as additional arguments, so it might look like 'Broadcast mismatch: Literal[3], Literal[2]', but you cannot yet construct a complex, formatted string like 'Dimension 1 (3) does not match Dimension 1 (2)' using logic inside the error message itself.
what about performance? if i have a complex 5d or 6d broadcast, is the type checker going to choke on the recursion? and can i use named dimensions, like strings, or is it just for integers?

Regarding performance, the PEP does not give specific complexity guarantees. It acknowledges that 'stuck' type evaluations (where a variable is unresolved) are a challenge and that recursive walking of tuples is the intended mechanism. For named dimensions, 'MergeOne' works fine with 'Literal[str]' because it relies on 'IsEquivalent'. However, a significant limitation is that string manipulation like 'Concat' or 'Slice' for strings is listed only as a 'Potential Future Extension'. You can check if 'Literal["batch"]' equals 'Literal["batch"]', but you cannot yet programmatically modify those string names during the type manipulation process. It is a very powerful draft, but there are definitely boundaries to what it can do in its current form.
fair enough. final thing, is this purely for static checking or does pydantic get to use this at runtime? my team uses fastapi and we need these shapes validated when data hits the endpoint.

The authors are very clear that runtime evaluation is a core goal. They even propose a 'special_form_evaluator' hook via 'ContextVar' to let libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI trigger evaluation when '__annotate__' is called. However, they are not adding a full evaluator to the standard library itself; they intend to release a third-party library for that. So, while the PEP provides the 'runtime hooks', you would still be dependent on the ecosystem (and the draft status of the PEP) to actually execute that broadcasting logic at runtime.

I am reviewing the spec for PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov. I noticed the proposal leans heavily into square brackets for everything—IsAssignable, GetArg, Members, and so on. As a reviewer, I am concerned about the proposal's own term 'Bracket City'. Why did the authors decide against using parentheses for these type-level operators to distinguish them from standard generic subscription?

The authors, including 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov and Dr. 'Sully' Sullivan, addressed this directly in the 'Alternate syntax ideas' section of the PEP. Their decision was driven by developer experience and consistency within the existing ecosystem. Since Python's typing system has historically revolved around square brackets for subscriptions like List[int] or Union[int, str], the authors felt that introducing parentheses for a subset of operators would create consistency issues. They argue that forcing users to memorize which specific APIs use square brackets and which use parentheses would lead to a worse experience. They essentially chose to lean into the existing 'Bracket City' of the typing module rather than creating a disjointed syntax.

I see the point about consistency, but doesn't this make the grammar harder to parse visually? If everything is a bracketed subscript, the distinction between a type-level computation and a simple generic instantiation is lost. For instance, in the example: type PropsOnly[T] = NewProtocol[*[p.name for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]]. That is a lot of nesting. Did the authors consider that user-defined aliases still have to use brackets even if primitives used parens?

Yes, they actually used that exact point as a justification for staying with brackets. If the built-in operators used parentheses, a user-defined helper alias like PointerArg[T] would still require brackets. You would end up with code like: typing.IsAssignable(PointerArg[T], Property). The authors believe this mix is more confusing than uniform bracket usage. Regarding the visual complexity, they acknowledge that while forms like the unpacked comprehension *[... for t in Iter[...]] are syntactically dense, they provide the necessary power for things like Prisma-style ORMs or FastAPI CRUD model derivation without changing Python's grammar. The PEP also notes that for runtime evaluation, using brackets allows these operators to produce standard typing._GenericAlias objects, which then interact with the special_form_evaluator ContextVar hook when __bool__ or __iter__ is called.

hay nanochat. i saw some post on twitter about pep 827 type manipulation and it sounds like magic for python. i keep seeing people talk about Members and Attrs. are they just the same thing? can you explain it like im five?

Welcome! PEP 827 is a proposal by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov (the Mad Emperor) to let Python's type system 'inspect' itself. 

In plain English: Imagine you have a class, and you want to ask the computer, 'Hey, what variables and functions are inside this class?'

- Attrs[T] is like a list of just the 'data' or attributes (like name: str or age: int).
- Members[T] is the bigger list. It includes everything in Attrs[T], plus all the 'actions' or methods (the functions inside the class).

Both of them give you a list of 'Member' objects that tell you the name and the type of each thing they found.

okay so if i have a class with ten functions, Members just grabs them all for me? what if i didnt add types to my functions yet because im lazy, does it still find them? also can you like... run this for me and show me the output?

Here is where it gets tricky. I have to be honest about two big limitations in the PEP:

1. It only sees what you've tagged. The PEP specifically says that 'only members with explicit type annotations are included.' If you have a function like 'def save(self):' without any type hints, Members[T] will act like it doesn't exist. It won't see unannotated methods.

2. I can't actually 'run' this code for you in a live Python environment because PEP 827 is still a Draft for Python 3.15. There isn't an official evaluator in the standard library yet. 

However, I can show you what the result would look like conceptually. If you had an annotated attribute 'name: str', Attrs[T] would return a tuple containing a Member object where m.name is 'name' and m.type is str.

that feels a bit restricted if it just ignores my code because i missed a colon. can it at least see private stuff like things starting with an underscore? and can i use this to make a brand new real class at runtime like a factory?

Regarding the underscores: Yes, it can see them! As long as you provide a type annotation (like _secret: int), Members[T] and Attrs[T] will find it. It doesn't care about 'private' vs 'public' naming conventions, only the annotations.

Regarding your second question, that's another limitation. PEP 827 lets you create new 'Protocols' (structural types) using NewProtocol[*Ms] or new 'TypedDicts' using NewTypedDict[*Ps]. But the authors noted that they are 'not currently proposing any way to create nominal classes'—meaning you can't use this specific tool to generate a brand new, traditional class that you'd see in a regular MRO.

It's mostly for 'type-level' math, like taking one class and automatically generating a version of it where every field is optional.
I have been keeping an eye on PEP 827. It seems to go way beyond standard Callables and ParamSpec. Can you explain what the new Params type is and how it relates to this Param class?

PEP 827 introduces Extended Callables to allow for more granular introspection and construction of function signatures. At the center are two new primitives: Param and Params. A Param represents a single function parameter, capturing its name, its type, and any qualifiers like whether it is keyword-only or has a default. Params is a variadic container that wraps a sequence of these Param objects. In the new system, when you see Callable[Params[...], R], the type checker knows to interpret the first argument as a structured list of parameters rather than the traditional list of types used in standard callables.
Wait, if it is just a list of parameters, why do we need the Params wrapper? Why not just use a tuple or a standard list of Param objects?

The authors, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, specified Params as a distinct class for a few reasons. First, the presence of Params as the first argument to Callable is what distinguishes the extended callable format from the standard format at both runtime and for static analysis. Second, Params serves as a natural bound for ParamSpec. It provides a formal structure that type checkers can use to validate that a sequence of parameters is actually well-formed. Inside the container, you use the *Ps syntax to represent a variadic sequence of Param instances.
That makes sense. Can you show me what a real function looks like when mapped to this? For instance, something with a keyword-only argument and a default value?

Sure. Consider a function like: def func(a: int, *, b: str = 'default') -> bool. In the PEP 827 extended format, this would be represented as: Callable[Params[Param[Literal['a'], int], NamedDefaultParam[Literal['b'], str]], bool]. Here, NamedDefaultParam is a convenient type alias provided by the PEP. It expands to Param[Literal['b'], str, Literal['keyword', 'default']]. The ParamQuals used in the third slot of Param can include strings like '*', '**', 'default', and 'keyword' to describe exactly how that parameter behaves.
I see. And since this is a 'Type Manipulation' PEP, I assume I can iterate over these? How do I get the names or types back out once they are inside Params?

Exactly. The PEP introduces type member access using dot notation. If you are iterating over the members of a Params container using an unpacked comprehension like *[... for p in Iter[Ps]], you can access p.name to get the literal string of the parameter name or p.type to get its annotated type. This allows you to programmatically transform one function signature into another, such as stripping a 'self' argument or adding new keyword-only parameters to a decorator's return type.
haiii! i saw someone on twitter talking about pep 827 and how python is basically becoming typescript. something about 'type manipulation'? can you explain it to me like i only know basic python?

Hello! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, is a draft proposal for Python 3.15. It aims to let you write logic that works on types themselves, not just values.

Think of it like this: normally you write code like `if x > 5: return "big"`. PEP 827 lets you write types like `type Result = str if IsAssignable[T, int] else list`. It allows the type system to 'calculate' what a type should be based on other types. It is heavily inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types.
that sounds wild. so if i have a class, can i actually use this to loop through its variables and change them? how does python even 'see' inside a class at the type level?

Exactly! The PEP introduces an operator called `Members[T]`. It returns a tuple of `Member` objects that describe the class. Each `Member` has dots you can access, like `.name` (the variable name as a string) and `.type` (the actual type of that variable).

You can 'loop' through them using an unpacked comprehension syntax. It looks like this: `*[p.name for p in Iter[Members[MyClass]]]`. This lets you build brand new types (like a TypedDict or a Protocol) dynamically based on what is inside an existing class.
okay i think i get the loop. but how does it stay 'safe'? if i try to get a member that doesn't exist, does it crash? i read something about 'KeyOf' and how it might be strict or not.

Great question. In the current proposal, there is an operator called `KeyOf[T]` which creates a union of all the member names in a class. For example, if your class has `name` and `age`, `KeyOf[MyClass]` is `Literal['name'] | Literal['age']`.

Regarding safety, the PEP discusses 'strictness' in the Rationale section. Right now, it is a bit less strict than TypeScript. If you use `GetMemberType[T, S]`, and `S` is just any string, the system might return `Never` if it's not there. The authors mention that they could make it 'strictly-kinded'—meaning the type checker would force you to prove that `S` is actually inside `KeyOf[T]` before you're even allowed to call the operator—but that requires more complex machinery they haven't fully proposed yet.
wait, so if it's not super strict yet, what happens if i write a type alias that is totally broken? like i'm trying to map over a tuple but i give it an integer instead?

The PEP handles this by returning `Never` for most invalid operations. For example, if you use `Length[T]` but `T` isn't a tuple, it results in `Never`. 

However, there is a special operator called `RaiseError[Message, *Types]`. Library authors can use this inside their conditional logic. If the type-level 'if' statement hits a branch it shouldn't, it can trigger `RaiseError`. This forces the type checker to show a custom error message to the user, like 'Broadcast mismatch' if you are trying to add two arrays with incompatible shapes. It's a way to add 'manual' strictness where the language's default rules might be too loose.
that makes sense. what about 'context-sensitive' stuff? i saw that mentioned in the rejected ideas. does that mean the type checker knows 'where' i am when it's calculating these things?

You've spotted a deep technical detail! 'Context-sensitive type bound inference' is something the authors mention as a requirement if they wanted to achieve TypeScript-level strictness. 

In TypeScript, if you have an 'if' check like `T extends string ? ... : ...`, the compiler knows that inside the 'true' branch, `T` is definitely a string. For PEP 827, the authors decided not to fully include that level of 'narrowing' logic in this draft. Instead, they rely on operators like `IsAssignable`. It makes the proposal simpler to implement in tools like mypy or pyright, even if it means the type checker isn't quite as 'smart' about tracking those narrowed bounds automatically inside your type aliases.

so basically it's a trade-off. we get these cool powers to build types, but we have to be a bit more careful because the checker isn't doing the full context-tracking yet. is this actually going to be in python soon?

That is a perfect summary. It is a powerful toolbox primarily intended for framework authors (like those making Pydantic, FastAPI, or ORMs) so they can stop using custom plugins and use standard Python instead. 

As for 'soon,' it is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15. There is a proof-of-concept implementation in a fork of mypy and a runtime evaluator library in development, but it still needs to go through the full PEP approval process before it becomes official. So, keep an eye on 2026!

Honestly, Python's typing is starting to feel like a TypeScript clone and it's getting way too complex. This PEP 827 proposal with 'if' and 'else' or 'for' loops right inside the type annotation looks like a disaster for runtime introspection. If I'm using something like Pydantic or FastAPI that needs to look at types at runtime, how is it even supposed to handle a conditional type? Isn't this going to break every library that relies on __annotations__?

The authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov—explicitly acknowledge that this proposal is inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types to help model complex Python metaprogramming. You're right that it introduces complexity for runtime tools, but the PEP includes a 'Runtime evaluation support' section to address this. It proposes a new 'special_form_evaluator' hook using a ContextVar in the typing module. This allows a library to intercept calls like __bool__ on a Boolean Operator or __iter__ on typing.Iter, which are triggered when the __annotate__ method is called. For libraries that want the raw, unevaluated logic, the PEP mentions it would pair well with a 'Format.AST' mode currently being discussed in other drafts. Since PEP 827 is still a Draft targeting Python 3.15, the authors don't plan to put an official evaluator in the standard library yet, but they intend to provide a third-party evaluator library to help frameworks bridge the gap.

Hi nanochat! I am currently a PL design student looking at Python PEP 827. I am fascinated by the Type Manipulation proposal, specifically UpdateClass. From a type theory perspective, what is it actually doing to the nominal class, and where can I actually use it without the type checker yelling at me?

UpdateClass is a special form in PEP 827 that allows for the modification of a nominal class's members. It can add new members, override existing ones, or effectively remove them by setting their type to Never. From a theory standpoint, it acts as a mechanism for retroactive modification of a class's signature. According to the specification, you can only use UpdateClass in two specific locations: as the return type of a type decorator (like a class decorator) or as the return type of an __init_subclass__ method. If you try to use it as a return type for a standard function or a parameter, it would be outside the scope of the current proposal.

That seems like it would create some nasty evaluation-order issues, especially if different decorators are trying to mutate the same class. Does nanochat have a way to handle circular dependencies or complex MRO interactions here, or are there hard limits on what you can infer?

You have spotted one of the primary limitations discussed in the PEP. The proposal acknowledges that UpdateClass introduces a type-evaluation-order dependence. If an __init_subclass__ method using UpdateClass inspects members of another class that is also being updated, the results depend on the evaluation order. The PEP authors—Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—suggest that these situations should ideally be rejected, as they mirror runtime evaluation-order issues. Additionally, I should be honest that type inference becomes extremely difficult when dealing with unresolved type variables; the PEP notes that partial polymorphic type inference is undecidable, so my ability to help you with 'stuck' type evaluations involving generics is limited by those theoretical bounds.

I appreciate the honesty! It sounds like the system is 'best effort' at runtime. If I wanted to use UpdateClass to strip all methods from a class to make it a pure data container, could I just iterate over Members[T] and return Never for anything that is a Callable?

In theory, yes. You would iterate over typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] using an unpacked comprehension and use a conditional type to check if the member is a method. However, the PEP notes a specific limitation: only members with explicit type annotations are included in Members[T]. If a method lacks an annotation, it might be excluded depending on the final resolution of the open issues in the PEP. Furthermore, while you can set a member type to Never to 'remove' it, the PEP is primarily focused on making this work for static analysis and frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI that use runtime introspection. If the static type checker gets stuck on a complex union or an unresolved generic in your comprehension, it will return Never as a fallback, which might not be the precise pruning you intended.

yo nanochat. been digging into pep 827. i do a lot of ml work, mostly pytorch and numpy, so i was hyped for pep 646 for variadic generics. but i hear 827 can actually generate __init__ methods like dataclasses do? i already use @dataclass_transform (pep 681) for my custom model classes. how is this any different or better?

The main difference is that PEP 827 (authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov) moves the logic from typechecker-specific plugins into the Python type system itself. PEP 681 is a 'lowest-common denominator' that tells a typechecker 'pretend this is a dataclass,' but it cannot describe more complex transformations. PEP 827 introduces 'UpdateClass', which allows you to programmatically define an __init__ signature based on the attributes found via 'Attrs[T]'. This means your IDE can provide precise autocompletion for a generated __init__ without needing a custom plugin for every library.

okay, i see. but in my tensor classes, i often want to create a new version of a model that only has some specific weights. in typescript, i'd just use 'Pick' or 'Omit' to handle the types. is pep 827 just copying typescript or is it doing something python-specific?

It is heavily inspired by TypeScript's utility types, but adapted for Python's runtime and nominal typing constraints. For example, the PEP shows how to implement 'Pick[T, Keys]' using 'NewProtocol' and a comprehension over 'Members[T]'. It looks like this:

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

Unlike TypeScript, which is purely static, PEP 827 is designed to support runtime evaluation via a 'special_form_evaluator' hook, which is crucial for frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI that need to inspect types at runtime for validation.

that makes sense for crud, but what about tensor shapes? with pep 646, we can specify 'Array[float, *Shape]', but we can't really do math on the shapes. can pep 827 actually handle broadcasting rules? like if i add an (L[4], L[1]) tensor to an (L[3],) tensor, can it calculate the (L[4], L[3]) result?

Yes, PEP 827 can express broadcasting rules using recursive type aliases and conditional types. The PEP provides a 'Broadcast[T, S]' implementation that uses 'Slice', 'GetArg', and 'IsEquivalent' to walk down the shape tuples. It defines a 'MergeOne[T, S]' helper that checks if dimensions are equivalent or if one is 'Literal[1]'. If there is a mismatch (like trying to broadcast 2 and 3), it can even use the 'RaiseError' operator to trigger a custom type error message during static analysis. This goes way beyond the simple matching provided by PEP 646.
last question: if i want my base 'Model' class to automatically generate this __init__, do i use a decorator like @dataclass? or is it built into the class inheritance?

You can do both. PEP 827 allows a class decorator to return an 'UpdateClass' type, but it also allows 'UpdateClass' to be the return type of '__init_subclass__'. This means any class inheriting from your 'Model' would automatically have its '__init__' signature computed and updated by the type system based on the members defined in the subclass. The PEP uses an 'InitFnType[T]' helper to iterate over attributes and convert them into 'Param' objects for the new function signature.

salut nanochat. I am a FastAPI developer and I am currently drowning in boilerplate. I have to write Hero, HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate models for every entity. I heard PEP 827 might let me derive these automatically? How does it handle class methods, especially if they are generic?

Hello! You are looking at exactly one of the primary motivations for PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. The proposal introduces type-level operators like Members[T] and NewProtocol to let you programmatically construct types. For your CRUD models, you would iterate over the members of your base model and transform them. When it comes to methods, PEP 827 introduces a specific form called GenericCallable to handle those generic signatures you are worried about.

That sounds promising. How does GenericCallable actually look in code? If I have a method like 'def process[T](self, x: T) -> T' on my Hero model, how do I represent that when I'm building my HeroPublic protocol?

The syntax for a GenericCallable is GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty]. In your case, Vs is a tuple of the unbound type variables, and Ty is the callable type that uses them. For your process method, it would look something like this: Member[Literal["process"], GenericCallable[tuple[T], lambda T: Callable[[T], T]]]. This allows the type system to understand that the 'T' inside the method is a local generic bound specifically to that method, not the whole class.

Okay, I think I follow the syntax. But I noticed in the draft that GenericCallable is restricted to being used inside Member arguments. Why can't I just use it as a standalone type for a variable or a function parameter?

The authors explain that restricting GenericCallable to the type argument of Member is a way to avoid a "can of worms" known as impredicative polymorphism and rank-N types. Basically, if you allow generic types to be nested deep inside other types or used as parameters for other functions, type inference becomes extremely difficult or even undecidable. By limiting it to Member, PEP 827 solves the immediate problem of representing generic methods in derived protocols without breaking the type checker's ability to infer everything else. There is also a practical reason: the lambda allows the system to delay evaluation. This is crucial for runtime frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic, because if the type was evaluated too early, the conditional logic or the generic variables might be lost before the ORM or validator could see them.

Hey. I am looking over the PEP 827 draft as a possible way to handle some of our dynamic class generation. I see NewProtocol and NewTypedDict, but I am not seeing how to define a brand new nominal class or a generic type using these operators. Am I missing something?

You are not missing anything. PEP 827 specifically states that it does not propose a way to create new nominal classes or new generic types through type-level computation. It focuses on structural construction via NewProtocol and NewTypedDict. If you need nominal identity, you have to use UpdateClass, which modifies an existing nominal class rather than creating one from thin air.

That is a bit of a hurdle for us. We rely on isinstance checks for our validation logic. If I use NewProtocol, those checks will fail since it is structural. Why did Dr. Sullivan and the other authors (I think Selivanov and Park were involved?) decide to leave nominal classes out? And why no generics?

Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov aimed to bridge the gap between Python's metaprogramming and the type system, but they had to draw boundaries. For nominal types, the draft suggests that adding a way to create brand new nominal identities might be too complex for a first pass, which is why they provided UpdateClass for class decorators and __init_subclass__ patterns instead. Regarding generics, the PEP mentions that rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism are 'cans of worms' regarding type inference and decidability. They wanted to avoid those complexities for the initial 3.15 target.

The 'cans of worms' part makes sense, I suppose. So if I have a base class and I want to dynamically add an __init__ method based on its attributes, I'd have to use UpdateClass within __init_subclass__? Could you show me how that looks in the PEP 827 syntax?

Salutations. I have been analyzing the PEP 827 draft regarding type manipulation. It makes reference to a 2025 Meta Typed Python Survey to justify its existence. As someone accustomed to the expressive power of Haskell and OCaml, I am curious: what exactly were the 'Most Requested Features' identified in that survey, and how much of that functional-adjacent utility actually made it into this specification?

The Meta 2025 Typed Python Survey results were a primary driver for PEP 827. According to the PEP, the 'Most Requested Features' from the community centered on bridging the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. Specifically, respondents asked for features inspired by TypeScript, including:

1. Intersection types (the & operator).
2. Mapped and Conditional types.
3. Utility types like Pick, Omit, keyof, and typeof.
4. More flexible structural typing for dictionaries (TypedDict).

PEP 827 addresses these by introducing type-level operators like `IsAssignable`, `Members`, and `NewProtocol`. However, it is important to note that the proposal is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, and it doesn't actually introduce a native intersection operator. Instead, it provides the machinery to construct similar behaviors using conditional types and protocols.

I see. If there is no native intersection operator, we are essentially looking at a system for type-level computation rather than a formal extension of the subtyping lattice. From a type-theoretic perspective, I am interested in how it handles higher-order constructs. Does it support rank-N polymorphism or higher-kinded types? For instance, could I define a generic transformation over a type constructor?

To be perfectly honest, PEP 827 explicitly avoids several of the 'cans of worms' associated with the features you're describing. The authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—state that rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism (where type variables are instantiated with other generic types) are intentionally omitted because they can lead to undecidability in type inference.

While we have `GenericCallable`, its use is currently restricted to the type argument of a `Member` to prevent it from appearing in local variables or nested parameter positions. You cannot truly define a higher-kinded type that abstracts over a type constructor like `M[T]`. The closest we get is the `*[... for t in Iter[tuple_ty]]` comprehension, but that is strictly for iterating over the arguments of a tuple type, not for mapping over an arbitrary generic container.

That is a significant limitation. Without `infer` or proper pattern matching on type constructors, how does one deconstruct a type? For example, if I have a `list[int]`, how would I extract the `int` to apply a conditional? Is it stuck with rigid index-based access?

Yes, it is currently more rigid than TypeScript. The PEP rejects the TypeScript-style `extends ... ? ... : ...` syntax with the `infer` keyword because it is difficult to fit into Python's grammar without major changes. Instead, you have to use `GetArg` or `GetArgs`.

To extract the `int` from `list[int]`, you would write something like `GetArg[T, list, Literal[0]]`. This requires you to know the 'Base' (like `list`) ahead of time. The PEP authors admit this is less 'strictly-kinded' and lacks the context-sensitive type bound inference found in TypeScript. If the operation fails—for instance, if you call `GetArg` on a type that doesn't inherit from the base—the system simply returns `Never` rather than providing a sophisticated pattern match failure.

I see. So while the Meta survey requested 'typeof' and 'keyof', PEP 827 is providing a Pythonic approximation through `Members[T]` and `KeyOf[T]` aliases? I noticed the `KeyOf` implementation in the PEP uses a variadic `Union`. Is there a limit to how many members we can union together before the type checker collapses?

The PEP doesn't specify a hard limit on the number of elements in a `Union` generated by a comprehension, but it does note that `Members[T]` only includes attributes and methods with explicit type annotations. This serves as a practical filter to keep the resulting tuple of `Member` objects manageable. 

Implementation-wise, the proof-of-concept in mypy can handle the examples in the PEP, including complex recursions like the NumPy-style `Broadcast` logic. However, since the PEP is still in the Draft stage, the performance characteristics and potential 'collapse' points for very large class structures are still being explored by the authors and the typing community.

Hello. I've been studying the design choices in PEP 827. I'm particularly interested in why the authors, like Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and Yury Selivanov, chose to introduce a new 'Extended Callable' syntax with Param and Params objects rather than just leveraging existing callback protocols for type-level computation? It seems like callback protocols already represent complex signatures, so why the new machinery?

That is a great question from a language design perspective. According to the PEP, the authors Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov specifically addressed this in the rationale. There are three primary reasons they opted for the new extended callable format over callback protocols.

First, they noted that callback protocols do not work well for type-level computation. Protocols are structural, but they are not easily introspected or constructed using the type-level operators proposed in PEP 827, like loop-based transformations or conditional checks on parameters. Second, the authors argued that even if they made protocols work, they would still need a separate facility for creating and introspecting methods, which wouldn't actually simplify the system.

Finally, there is a practical implementation and philosophical concern. The existing extended callable experiments in libraries like mypy_extensions are runtime no-ops, but PEP 827 requires real runtime objects to support its goal of runtime type evaluation. The authors also felt that using brackets (e.g., Callable[Params[...], T]) was more consistent with the rest of the PEP 827 syntax than the parentheses used in traditional function calls or protocol-based definitions. By using a formal Param[N, T, Q] structure, the system can reliably access properties like .name, .type, and .quals during both static analysis and runtime evaluation.

yo, type manipulation is finally getting real! I am deep in the weeds building a new validation framework and PEP 827 looks like a lifesaver for our field-to-type transformations. It is definitely an exciting proposal! NewProtocolWithBases is designed to create a structural protocol that requires a type to both extend specific base classes and possess certain Members. The PEP suggests a signature like NewProtocolWithBases[Bases: tuple[type], *Ms: Member]. For a framework like yours, it would be perfect for ensuring a generated type satisfies both a base Model class and the dynamic schema fields you are generating.

Exactly! That is exactly the pattern we use for our Pydantic-style models. So why is it tucked away in the 'Potential Future Extensions' section instead of the main spec? Is it just too much for Python 3.15 right now?

That is exactly it. The authors - Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov - noted that they are holding off because adding bases to protocols changes the fundamental definition of what protocols are in a way they do not want to tangle with yet. They also believe many of those use cases can be simulated using other tools in the PEP, like the standard NewProtocol or UpdateClass for nominal types. Since PEP 827 is still a Draft, they are focusing on the core machinery first to ensure the base operators like IsAssignable and Members work perfectly before expanding the protocol system.

For example, the PEP shows how to implement a 'PropsOnly' type that extracts only certain attributes from a model. I noticed the mention of the 2025 Typed Python Survey from Meta. It seems the community is really pushing for this. I am curious about the 'identity' of these types though. yo nanochat, pep 827 time. i was looking at this and it feels like it covers a ton of ground that previously required specific peps. how does it relate to stuff like pep 681 or the way we handle kwargs now?

You are right on the money. Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov (the authors) specifically mention that PEP 827 is designed to bridge the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system without needing special-case decorators like PEP 681's @dataclass_transform.

Basically, PEP 681 was a 'lowest-common denominator' fix for things like dataclasses, but it requires typecheckers to have hardcoded support for it. PEP 827 instead gives you primitives like Members[T] and NewProtocol to define those transformations directly in Python. For instance, you could use a conditional type and an unpacked comprehension to generate an __init__ method signature based on a class's attributes, rather than relying on the typechecker to guess what @dataclass_transform is doing.
that makes sense. what about the kwarg side of things? i know pep 692 did the typeddict thing for **kwargs, but 827 seems to be doing something extra with unpack.

It builds right on top of PEP 692 and PEP 646. PEP 827 proposes supporting Unpack of typevars for **kwargs. So you could write something like:

def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K:
    return kwargs

The cool part is the inference logic. When you call that function, the type checker is supposed to infer literal types whenever possible for arguments that aren't in the bound (or are read-only). If a non-required item is missing, it gets inferred as Never if it's read-only. This is a huge prerequisite for the 'type level computation' that the PEP uses to build things like Prisma-style ORM return types or FastAPI CRUD models.
neat. i also saw a mention of pep 649 in the backwards compatibility section. are they worried about how these new loops and ifs in annotations will actually run?

Exactly. Since PEP 827 introduces actual logic into annotations (like `T if bool_typ else S` and `*[... for t in Iter[...]]`), tools that just grab annotations at runtime might struggle. The PEP mentions that PEP 649's 'Just store the strings' approach or a potential 'Format.AST' mode would help tools extract the logic without fully executing it.

However, for those who *do* want to evaluate these at runtime, the PEP adds a ContextVar called `special_form_evaluator`. This lets runtime libraries (like Pydantic or a dedicated evaluator) hook into the process when `__bool__` is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or when `__iter__` is called on `typing.Iter` during annotation evaluation.
so is this basically turning the typing module into a mini programming language? i noticed there's even a way to throw errors like RaiseError.

In a way, yes. It's a 'type-level machinery' inspired by TypeScript. Beyond the logic, you get operators like GetArg (to grab generic parameters), Length and Slice for tuples, and even a way to update classes via UpdateClass. 

And yes, RaiseError[Literal["message"]] is there so library authors can provide useful feedback during type-level computation—like if a NumPy-style broadcast fails. Instead of just getting a cryptic error, the developer could see 'Broadcast mismatch' as an actual type error in their IDE. It's currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so it's quite ambitious.

Yo, I am coming from a TypeScript background and looking at PEP 827's type introspection. In TS, we mostly just map over properties, but I see both Members[T] and Attrs[T] in the spec. What is the functional difference between them? Does one include the methods and the other does not?

You have it exactly right. The primary difference is the scope of what is returned. Members[T] produces a tuple of Member types describing both the attributes and the methods of a class or TypedDict. Attrs[T] is specifically limited to attributes only. In the model proposed by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, properties are treated as attributes, so they appear in both lists. However, methods are returned as callables and carry the 'ClassVar' qualifier. A key constraint to remember is that both operators only include members with explicit type annotations. If a method or attribute is defined without an annotation, it is excluded from the results of both Members[T] and Attrs[T]. This design ensures that the type-level computation remains consistent between static type checkers and runtime evaluators.

Hey, I've been looking at PEP 827 for a validation framework I'm building. One thing I don't get: why are Dr. Michael Sullivan and the other authors proposing this weird new mini-language for types? Why can't we just write regular Python functions that take types and return new ones? It feels like we're reinventing the wheel with 'if' and 'for' inside annotations.

That's a fair question, especially since it adds complexity to the typing module. The PEP actually addresses this in the 'Rejected Ideas' section. The main reason for not using standard Python functions is the execution model. If you use arbitrary Python functions, a static type checker like mypy would have to run a full Python interpreter to figure out a type. That's a huge security and performance risk. Also, as the authors (including 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) point out, you'd need a clear specification of how types are represented inside that 'mini-plugin' function, which ends up being just as complex as what they've proposed with operators like IsAssignable and Members.

Okay, so it's about static analysis. But what are the limits? If I'm building a nested schema transformer, can I do deep recursion? Like, if I have a recursive TypedDict, can a PEP 827 type alias walk down that tree indefinitely?

The PEP is actually a bit cautious there. In the 'Open Issues' section, the authors admit that the rules for 'stuck' type evaluations—cases where a type operator is applied to an unresolved type variable or a recursive structure—are still somewhat unclear. In the current mypy proof-of-concept, stuck evaluations tend to fall back to invariant subtype checking. While you can write recursive type aliases like the 'Broadcast' example in the PEP, there isn't a formal specification for recursion depth limits or how to handle infinite loops at the type level yet. You might end up needing to use RaiseError to manually flag when a transformation gets too deep or hits a case you can't handle.
That's a bit worrying for an ORM. What about string manipulation? If I want to take a database column name in snake_case and generate a camelCase attribute name for a NewProtocol, can I do that?

Right now, no. String manipulation—like Uppercase, Lowercase, or slicing string literals—is listed under 'Potential Future Extensions' rather than the core specification. The current draft focuses on object inspection via Members[T] and Attrs[T], and logic via conditional types. You can access m.name on a Member object, but you can't really transform that string literal into a new one yet. You're limited to picking, omitting, or checking if names match existing literal types using IsAssignable.
So if I want to automate FastAPI-style CRUD models where I strip a 'hidden' prefix from field names, I'm out of luck until those 'Future Extensions' land?

For prefix stripping specifically, yes, you'd be stuck. However, if your fields are marked with something like a 'Field' descriptor using InitField, you can filter them out. The PEP shows a 'Create' example where it iterates over typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] and uses an 'if' clause to check if a field is a primary key or hidden. You can include or exclude members based on their properties, you just can't 'edit' the strings that represent their names in this version of the Draft.

Hi nanochat. I am looking through the draft for PEP 827 and I am a bit skeptical about RaiseError. As a library maintainer, mypy already yells at my users when they mess up. Why would I want to explicitly trigger a type error inside a type expression? It feels like we are just reinventing a compiler error in a weird way.

That is a fair concern. Normally, type checkers catch errors when a value does not match a definition. However, PEP 827 introduces complex type-level computations—like loops over class members or conditional logic. In these cases, you might reach a branch that is mathematically 'reachable' in the type logic but logically invalid for your framework. Without RaiseError, the type might simply resolve to Never or a generic mismatch, which gives the user no clue why their input failed. RaiseError[S, *Ts] allows you to provide a specific, human-readable string as a type error.

So it is basically a static 'assert' for types? In an ORM context, if I am using NewProtocol to filter a schema, I could use this if they try to select a field that doesn't exist? But isn't that what GetMember already does by returning Never?

Exactly. While GetMember returns Never if a member is missing, Never is often silent or causes a confusing 'None has no attribute' error downstream. With RaiseError, you can be explicit. For example, in the NumPy-style broadcasting example in the PEP, the authors (Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov) show a MergeOne helper. If two array dimensions do not match and neither is 1, they use: typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]. This tells the user exactly which two dimensions caused the failure, rather than just saying 'Array cannot be added to Array'.

Okay, that is slightly more useful than a generic mismatch. But how does this look at runtime? I maintain a validation framework similar to Pydantic, and we rely on runtime introspection. If I put RaiseError in a type alias, is it going to crash my runtime evaluator?

The PEP authors acknowledge that runtime tools are a priority. RaiseError itself is intended to be an 'inert' type operator application at runtime. However, the PEP specifies that if a type needs to be evaluated (for instance, via a third-party evaluator library or when calling __annotate__), the evaluator should generate a type error with the provided message. The PEP also introduces a special_form_evaluator ContextVar to help runtime libraries trigger these evaluations correctly. So, instead of a silent failure, your framework could catch these logic errors during the model-building phase in Python 3.15.
I see. And the *Ts part of the signature—does that just dump the actual types into the error message?

Yes. The specification states that any additional type arguments provided after the message string should be included in the generated error. This allows you to construct messages like 'Error: Field {Name} is not compatible with {OtherType}', making the debugging process for the library user much more transparent than just staring at a generic stack trace or a blank mypy output.

I am reviewing the PEP 827 draft by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov. Frankly, the Extended Callables proposal looks like a regression. Why are we introducing this verbose Param[N, T, Q] structure when we already have ParamSpec and callback protocols? It feels like we are reinventing function signatures as a heavy type-level data structure.

The authors acknowledge that the Param and Params types are quite verbose for manual annotations. However, the primary goal isn't to replace callback protocols for standard usage, but to provide a format that is amenable to type-level computation and introspection. Under PEP 827, Param[N, T, Q] encodes everything a type checker or a runtime framework needs to reconstruct or modify a signature. The arguments are N for the name (a Literal string or None), T for the type, and Q for ParamQuals, which handles the complex metadata like whether a parameter is keyword-only or has a default value.

Even for introspection, using strings for qualifiers like 'default' or 'keyword' seems loose. What exactly is the valid set for Q? And if N is a Literal string, how does the system handle positional-only parameters that do not have meaningful names in the type system?

The set of qualifiers, defined as ParamQuals, is a Literal alias consisting of '*', '**', 'default', and 'keyword'. Regarding positional-only parameters, the PEP uses a PosParam abbreviation where N can still be a Literal string for documentation, but the distinction is made via the qualifiers. For example, a purely positional-only param would be encoded as Param[Literal['a'], int, Literal['positional']]. If the name is truly irrelevant or absent, N is simply None. The PEP provides several abbreviations to make this manageable, such as ArgsParam[T] for *args or KwargsParam[T] for **kwargs.

So if I have a Params[*Ps] block, I can actually use type-level operators to filter or append to it? That would solve the long-standing issue with decorators that add or remove keyword arguments. If I can iterate over Params using the proposed Iter[T] and unpacked comprehensions, I suppose it actually provides the granularity ParamSpec lacks.

Exactly. Because Params serves as a natural bound for ParamSpec and wraps a sequence of Param objects, you can use the proposed unpacked comprehensions like *[tres for t in Iter[ttuple]]. For instance, you could iterate over the members of a class to dynamically construct an __init__ method's Params list. This allows for patterns like the InitFnType example in the PEP, where the assistant generates a signature where every attribute becomes a keyword-only parameter. This level of manipulation is what bridges the gap between dynamic Python metaprogramming and the static type system.

I keep seeing mentions of PEP 827. Is this just more syntax for the sake of syntax? I work in ML, and frankly, Python's type system has always been too weak to handle actual tensor shape broadcasting. Is this actually solving that or just adding more boilerplate?

It's definitely more than just syntactic sugar. PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation,' is a proposal by Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov to add full-scale type-level introspection and construction to Python. It’s inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types.

For your specific pain point in ML, the PEP authors actually use NumPy-style broadcasting as a primary example of its expressiveness. It introduces type-level operators like `IsAssignable`, `Slice`, and `GetArg` that allow you to define recursive logic for shape transformations directly in your type aliases.

Okay, but 'expressive' usually means 'unreadable.' If I want to type a `__add__` method that handles broadcasting between an `Array[float, L[4], L[1]]` and an `Array[float, L[3]]` to get `Array[float, L[4], L[3]]`, does that require writing a whole compiler inside my annotations?

It’s definitely a jump in complexity, but it's logical. You would define a `Broadcast` type alias using the new conditional syntax. Here is a simplified version of how the PEP implements that core logic:

```python
type MergeOne[T, S] = (
    T
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]]
    else S
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]]
    else typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]
)

type Broadcast[T, S] = (
    S if typing.Bool[Empty[T]]
    else T if typing.Bool[Empty[S]]
    else tuple[
        *Broadcast[DropLast[T], DropLast[S]],
        MergeOne[Last[T], Last[S]],
    ]
)
```

This uses recursive calls and the new `RaiseError` operator. If the shapes don't match (and neither is a `Literal[1]`), the type checker will actually throw the specific 'Broadcast mismatch' error you defined.

That `*Broadcast` syntax inside the tuple is interesting. It looks like it actually iterates over the types. My concern is whether this is just a 'static-only' pipe dream. If I use Pydantic or FastAPI, they need to see these types at runtime. Does this break runtime introspection?

That is a major focus of the PEP. The authors (Sully, Park, and Selivanov) explicitly stated that the machinery must benefit both static checkers and frameworks that rely on runtime evaluation, like FastAPI or Pydantic. 

They propose a `special_form_evaluator` using a `ContextVar` to allow third-party libraries to trigger evaluation when `__bool__` is called on a boolean operator or `__iter__` is called on `typing.Iter`. So, instead of being 'stuck' as an unevaluated expression, a runtime library could resolve `Broadcast[Shape1, Shape2]` into a concrete tuple of Literals. It is currently a Draft for Python 3.15, but there is already a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that passes these broadcasting tests.

hey, i was reading about pep 827. i do a lot of ml work and our tensor library is a mess of overloads for different shapes and dtypes. i saw something called Overloaded in the spec - what is that exactly?

PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation and authored by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, introduces several primitives to construct types programmatically. Overloaded[*Callables] is one of those primitives. It represents an overloaded function type where you provide a sequence of callable types. The order in which you provide them is significant, as it mirrors how static type checkers currently process @overload definitions: they check for a match from top to bottom.

so it's basically a way to make a type that has multiple signatures without writing out a bunch of @overload stubs? could i use this to generate signatures for a matmul operation based on tensor ranks?

Exactly. Because PEP 827 allows for unpacked comprehensions, you can generate the arguments for Overloaded dynamically. If you have a list of possible shapes or rank-specific callables, you can use the syntax Overloaded[*[SomeCallableGenerator[R] for R in Iter[Ranks]]] to construct the type. This is particularly powerful when combined with the new NewProtocol or UpdateClass operators, allowing you to attach these computed overloads to class methods at runtime or during static analysis.
wait, can you show me a small example of how that looks? say i have a basic 1d and 2d case for a dot product.

Sure. Using the extended callable syntax from the PEP, you could define it like this:

type Dot1D = Callable[[Array[float, L[N]], Array[float, L[N]]], float]
type Dot2D = Callable[[Array[float, L[M], L[N]], Array[float, L[N], L[K]]], Array[float, L[M], L[K]]]

type MyDot = Overloaded[Dot2D, Dot1D]

In this example, MyDot represents a function that first tries to match the 2D matrix multiplication signature. If the arguments don't match (e.g., they are 1D arrays), it falls through to the 1D dot product signature. This is a static representation that can be used inside a NewProtocol to define the interface for your tensor class.
and if i have 10 different ranks, i can just loop through them? the PEP mentioned something about Iter and comprehension in types.

Precisely. You would use the unpacked comprehension syntax: *[Signature[R] for R in Iter[AllRanks]]. The Iter[T] operator is used to iterate over the components of a tuple type. If AllRanks is a tuple of Literal integers, you can generate an Overloaded type that covers every rank you support without manual duplication. This bridges the gap between the highly dynamic nature of ndarray libraries and the static type system, which was one of the core motivations mentioned by the authors.

YOOOO nanochat! I just started digging into PEP 827 and my mind is blown. I'm a big TypeScript fan and seeing stuff like conditional types and Member operators coming to Python is legendary. I was looking at 'UpdateClass' though—is that basically how we do mapped types for class declarations? How does it work?

It is definitely an ambitious proposal! Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov have put together something that really bridges the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. While 'Pick' or 'Omit' (which are also in the PEP) usually produce new structural protocols, 'UpdateClass' is a bit more specialized.
