It uses the `Iter` and `Attrs` operators to look at those keys and build a new result type on the fly.

That sounds like a lot of heavy lifting at runtime. Is this going to conflict with PEP 649? I thought we were moving toward keeping annotations as strings or using the __annotate__ function to delay evaluation.

The PEP 827 authors actually mention that PEP 649 (and its potential Format.AST mode) would combine well with their proposal. While PEP 827 introduces runtime-friendly hooks like `special_form_evaluator` to help third-party libraries evaluate these computed types, it's designed to be 'inert' until needed. 

For a regular dev, you might never write a `*[p.name for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]` comprehension, but you'll benefit when your IDE can suddenly provide perfect code completion for a dynamically generated FastAPI model because the framework author used `NewTypedDict` and `Conditional types` (the `true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ` syntax) behind the scenes.
I'm looking at the broadcasting example. It uses `GetArg[T, tuple, Literal[-1]]`. We just got PEP 646 for variadic generics (TypeVarTuple). Why can't we just use that for array shapes?

PEP 646 is great for matching shapes, but it can't handle 'broadcasting' logic where two different shapes (like `(4, 1)` and `(3,)`) merge into a new one `(4, 3)`. 

PEP 827 uses `IsEquivalent`, `Slice`, and `Length` to recursively walk down the input tuples and apply logic. For example, it defines a `MergeOne[T, S]` alias that uses the conditional syntax to decide: if `T` and `S` are equivalent, take `T`; if one is `Literal[1]`, take the other; otherwise, use `RaiseError` to trigger a static type error. PEP 646 alone doesn't have the 'computational' power to do that kind of arithmetic on the types themselves.

Yo. I'm looking at this PEP 827 thing and I have to say, it looks like we're just turning Python into TypeScript now. Why do we need 'Conditional Types' and 'Mapped Types' in a language that's supposed to be simple? This seems like a massive complexity spike for very little gain.

I understand the concern. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, definitely takes heavy inspiration from TypeScript. The authors explicitly mention that features like Intersection types, Mapped types, and Utility types like Pick and Omit were the most requested features in Meta's 2025 Typed Python Survey.

The core goal isn't just to mimic TypeScript for the sake of it, but to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming (like what you see in Prisma-style ORMs or FastAPI) and the static type system. Currently, libraries often need custom mypy plugins to explain what they are doing to the type checker. PEP 827 aims to let those libraries define those rules directly in the type system using new primitives like IsAssignable, Members, and NewProtocol.

Right, 'inspired by.' But Python isn't TypeScript. Can this thing actually handle the complex stuff, or is it a half-baked version? Like, if I want to do pattern matching inside a type check to extract an inner type—similar to the 'infer' keyword in TS—can PEP 827 do that? Or are we just adding bloat that still requires plugins for the hard parts?

To be honest, it does have significant limitations compared to TypeScript. One major difference is exactly what you mentioned: pattern matching. The PEP authors considered supporting a mechanism like the 'infer' keyword for binding variables during a subtype check, but they ultimately rejected it. They felt it was too difficult to shoehorn into Python's existing syntax without major changes.

Instead, you have to use operators like GetArg[T, Base, Idx]. For example, if you want the first type argument of a list, you'd use GetArg[T, list, Literal[0]]. It is less elegant than pattern matching and requires you to know the 'Base' type you are inspecting. So, while it covers many use cases, it doesn't have that same 'logic programming' feel that TypeScript's conditional types provide.

So it's clunkier. What about creating new types? If I'm building a framework, can I use this to generate entirely new nominal classes or generic types on the fly? Or is it restricted to just shuffling existing fields around?

It is quite restricted. The PEP currently does not propose any way to create new nominal classes or new generic types. You can create structural types using NewProtocol[*Ms] or NewTypedDict[*Ps], but these are purely structural. 

There is also the UpdateClass[*Ps] operator, but that's a special case meant only for type decorators or __init_subclass__. It updates an existing nominal class rather than creating a brand new one. If you were hoping for a way to programmatically define a generic class 'MyClass[T]' entirely within the type system, PEP 827 doesn't support that yet.

That sounds like a lot of 'not yet' and 'not supported.' And what about the runtime? If I start using these 'if/else' type expressions and comprehensions like *[p.name for p in Iter[Members[T]]], is my production app going to slow down because Python is busy doing type algebra at runtime just to initialize a class?

That is a valid concern. The PEP introduces 'special_form_evaluator', which is a ContextVar hook that allows a third-party evaluator library to trigger type evaluation. While the operators are 'inert' until called, the syntax for loops and conditionals will be evaluated when __annotate__ is accessed. 

However, the PEP authors are not proposing an official evaluator for the standard library. They intend to release a third-party library for it. This means there's a risk of inconsistency between different evaluators, and performance will depend entirely on how that third-party logic is implemented. Also, the PEP notes that runtime evaluators might struggle with complex things like Protocols when using GetArg. It's definitely not a 'zero-cost' abstraction if you rely on runtime introspection.

yo, what's this type manipulation PEP I keep hearing about? I've been following PEP 695 and 646 for a while, and it feels like PEP 827 is trying to take things way further, maybe even stepping on the toes of PEP 681's dataclass transforms.

That is a fair assessment. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, aims to bridge the gap between Python's metaprogramming capabilities and its type system. While PEP 681 provided a specialized decorator for dataclass-like behavior, PEP 827 proposes generic type-level operators like Members[T], IsAssignable[T, S], and NewProtocol to allow libraries to define their own transformations without needing custom typechecker plugins.

Right, but I'm looking at the syntax. It uses 'if' and 'for' inside type annotations. If I have 'from __future__ import annotations' active, how does a tool like Pydantic or FastAPI even see those logic blocks at runtime? I feel like PEP 563 usually just turns everything into a string, which seems like it would break this entirely.

You've hit on a significant limitation discussed in the PEP. Currently, if you use 'from __future__ import annotations', the annotations are stored as strings. The PEP authors admit that tools wanting to extract these annotations 'unevaluated' are in trouble because the standard tricks for building strings don't work for loops and conditionals. Unless we get something like PEP 649 (Just store the strings) or a new Format.AST mode for fetching annotations, tools might actually have to re-parse the source code to see the unevaluated logic.

That sounds like a nightmare for library maintainers. So if I want to use 'typing.Iter' in an unpacked comprehension like '*[p.type for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]', the runtime evaluator has to be manually invoked? Does the PEP provide an official evaluator to handle the logic?

Actually, no. The PEP explicitly states they do not propose adding an official evaluator to the standard library. Instead, they intend to release a third-party library for it. They do propose a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar hook in the typing module so that these third-party evaluators can trigger evaluation when __bool__ or __iter__ is called on those special type objects at runtime.
What about the syntax for creating types? I saw some mention of PEP 764 and inline TypedDicts. Can I just use a dict literal inside NewProtocol yet?

Not in the current specification. Using a dictionary-based syntax for NewProtocol or NewTypedDict is listed under 'Alternate syntax ideas.' The proposal currently relies on passing Member objects as variadic arguments. For example, you'd write 'NewProtocol[*[Member[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]]' rather than using a dictionary comprehension. It's also worth noting that PEP 827 doesn't support creating new nominal classes or new generic types at all yet.
So it's powerful but still quite restricted. One last thing - how does it play with PEP 692? I use TypedDict for **kwargs all the time.

It builds directly on it! PEP 827 includes a minor subproposal to support 'Unpack' of typevars for **kwargs. This allows you to infer a TypedDict from keyword arguments at a call site, which you can then pass into your type-level computations. It even suggests that the type checker should infer literal types for those arguments whenever possible to make the downstream logic more precise.

hey. I keep seeing people talk about PEP 827. Why do we even need type manipulation in Python? I thought generics and TypeVarTuple from PEP 646 already solved most of the hard problems for us ndarray folks.

Hey! While PEP 646 was a huge step for representing shapes, it mostly lets you describe a shape without being able to transform it via logic. PEP 827, proposed by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, aims to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. Currently, your type checker can see a shape, but it cannot compute a new one based on complex rules. This proposal introduces type-level operators like IsAssignable, conditional types, and unpacked comprehensions so the type system can actually perform computations.
Wait, so could I actually model NumPy broadcasting rules? Like if I have a (4, 1) array and a (3,) array, the result is (4, 3). Can it actually calculate that at the type level?

Exactly. The PEP explicitly uses broadcasting as an example of why this is needed. You could define a "Broadcast" alias that recursively compares dimensions. It would use a helper like MergeOne[T, S] to decide the result for a single dimension. In PEP 827 syntax, it looks like this: `type MergeOne[T, S] = (T if typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]] else S if typing.IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]] else typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S])`. It uses IsEquivalent to check if they match and RaiseError to generate a custom type-check failure if they don't.
That RaiseError part is really useful for ML debugging. But how do you actually walk down a shape tuple? You mentioned recursive aliases, but how does the system handle the variadic math?

It combines recursion with new operators like Slice and GetArg. To broadcast two shapes, you compare the last elements using `GetArg[T, tuple, Literal[-1]]`, then recurse on the rest of the shape using `Slice[T, Literal[0], Literal[-1]]`. The proposal also introduces "unpacked comprehensions" which look like `*[... for t in Iter[...]]`. This lets you iterate over the arguments of a tuple type and construct a new one. For example, if you wanted to change every dimension in a shape to a different type, you would use that comprehension syntax. It is currently a Draft for Python 3.15, but it aims to provide the same kind of utility types TypeScript has, like Pick, Omit, and KeyOf, which are all implemented using these new primitives.

it is definitely more than just a hobby project! the authors - dr. michael j. sully sullivan, daniel w. park, and mad emperor yury selivanov - point to some pretty heavy data. they cited meta's 2025 typed python survey where the number one most requested feature was exactly this: missing features from typescript like intersection types, mapped and conditional types, and utility types like pick or omit. framework authors like you are exactly who they are targeting because right now, if you want to do complex metaprogramming, you basically have to write a custom plugin for every type checker, which is a massive maintenance burden.

that is one of the primary use cases. the pep introduces operators like Members[T] and Attrs[T] to inspect a class at the type level. you can then use NewProtocol or UpdateClass to construct or modify types. for your init generation, you would use the new extended callable syntax with Params and Param types. you could literally write a type alias that iterates over Attrs[T] and builds a new __init__ signature. they even propose a way to capture field metadata using a new InitField type, so you can see things like default values or whether a field is primary_key=True inside your type logic.

it is currently in draft status and targeting python 3.15. while there is a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can already check the examples in the pep, it is still a proposal. the authors are aiming to bridge the gap for things like fastapi and prisma-style orms so they do not need pep 681 style special-casing anymore. if you want to experiment, there is a reference implementation for a runtime evaluator, but for production code, you will have to wait for the steering council to give it the green light for 3.15.

Hey, I've been digging into PEP 827 for some ndarray shape typing experiments. I'm curious about the error handling logic during type evaluation. If I perform an invalid operation—like using GetArg on something that isn't a generic or slicing a tuple out of bounds—does the system throw a hard error, or does it just resolve to Never?

According to the current draft of PEP 827, the general rule for type operators (like GetArg, Length, or Slice) is that evaluating them with invalid arguments will produce Never as the return value. For instance, if you call GetArg[A, B, Literal[0]] but A doesn't actually inherit from B, the result is Never. The PEP notes this behavior in the Specification section under 'Type operators,' though the authors explicitly listed it as an 'Open Issue' whether certain invalid operations should be hard errors versus returning Never to allow for more graceful propagation.

That makes sense, but for tensor broadcasting, a silent Never might be a nightmare to debug if it just poisons the rest of the graph. I noticed a RaiseError operator in the spec. Is that intended to let us 'force' a readable error message when a shape constraint is violated, like in a mismatch during a matrix multiply?

Precisely. The RaiseError[S: Literal[str], *Ts] operator is designed for exactly that scenario. If the type system hits a branch containing RaiseError during evaluation, it generates a type error with your provided string and includes the additional type arguments in the message. The PEP actually demonstrates this in the NumPy-style broadcasting example. They define a MergeOne[T, S] alias that uses a conditional: if the types aren't equivalent or one isn't a Literal[1], it resolves to typing.RaiseError[Literal['Broadcast mismatch'], T, S]. This allows you to turn an invalid state into a descriptive diagnostic instead of letting a Never type propagate through your Array shapes.

yo, I just started getting comfortable with TypeVars and Generics, but I saw a mention of PEP 827 adding actual 'if/else' logic to the type system. Is that for real? How do you even write a type like that?

Yes, it is very much for real! PEP 827 (Type Manipulation), authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, proposes adding powerful construction facilities inspired by TypeScript. The proposal is currently a Draft targeted for Python 3.15.

In this system, you write a conditional type using the syntax: <true_type> if <type_bool> else <false_type>. 

The 'type_bool' part isn't just any expression; it has to be a 'type boolean' primitive or a logical combination of them. For example, you use operators like IsAssignable[T, S] to check if one type can fit into another. 

Here is a quick look at how you might use it in a type alias:

type Processed[T] = str if typing.IsAssignable[T, int] else list[T]

If you use Processed[int], the type system resolves it to 'str'. If you use Processed[float], it resolves to 'list[float]'. 

A few key things to note from the specification:
1. Lifting over Unions: If T is a union (like int | float), the operator evaluates both sides and returns a union of the results (str | list[float]).
2. Logical Operators: You can use 'and', 'or', 'not', 'all()', and 'any()' within the condition to build complex logic.
3. Evaluation: These evaluate to Literal[True] or Literal[False] at the type level. 

The goal is to let library authors (like the folks behind FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs) handle complex metaprogramming directly in the type system without needing custom typechecker plugins.

Hey! I've been reading about PEP 827 and it mentions this typing.Iter thing. Is that just a renamed version of typing.Iterable? I'm trying to figure out how it works compared to the standard Generic stuff I just learned.

Not quite! While typing.Iterable is a type hint used to describe something that can be iterated over at runtime (like a list or a generator), typing.Iter is a new primitive specifically for type-level manipulation. It's used inside 'unpacked comprehensions' to let the type system loop over things like the elements of a tuple or the members of a class. For example, you might see syntax like *[t for t in Iter[SomeTuple]].

Wait, so it's a loop that happens during type checking? That sounds like what TypeScript does with mapped types. But what happens if I actually run the code? If I call iter() on typing.Iter[MyTuple] in a normal script, does it just crash?

It won't crash. PEP 827 is designed to support runtime evaluation, but it handles it differently than standard Python objects. When you call iter() on a typing.Iter alias at runtime, it checks a new ContextVar called typing.special_form_evaluator. If that variable is set to None (which is the default), it just returns an empty iterator, specifically iter(()). It's essentially 'inert' by default so it doesn't break things.

That's weirdly different from how typing.Annotated or Generic works, where you usually just get the metadata back. Why not just return the tuple elements? If I compare this to how Pydantic or FastAPI currently inspects types, they usually just look at __args__. Why the empty iterator?

The 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, and Daniel W. Park proposed this because PEP 827 introduces logic—like 'if' statements and 'for' loops—directly into the annotation. Unlike typing.Generic, which is just a container, these computed types actually need to execute. The empty iterator is a safe default for tools that don't support the new logic yet. However, for a framework like FastAPI or Pydantic, they would set the special_form_evaluator to a custom callable. That hook would then be responsible for 'running' the type-level loop and returning the actual items to be processed during runtime introspection.

So it's basically a plugin system for the runtime? In standard Python, if I do 'for x in [1, 2, 3]', it just works. Here, I have to provide an evaluator or I get nothing? That feels a bit more restrictive than the usual 'everything is an object' Python model.

It is a bit of a departure. The reason for the hook is that evaluating these types can be complex. PEP 827 doesn't include a full 'official' evaluator in the standard library to keep the implementation lean. Instead, the PEP authors plan to release a third-party library that handles the heavy lifting. By using a ContextVar hook, a library can 'activate' the type logic when it calls __annotate__ on a class or function. Without that hook, Python's runtime keeps its hands off the complex type-level math to avoid side effects or performance hits during normal execution.

I have been deep-diving into the Prisma-style ORM examples in PEP 827 and I am genuinely thrilled about the NewProtocol and Attrs integration! As someone who follows typing-sig closely, I am looking for spec-level precision on how the select method signature manages to map fields from ModelT based on the TypedDict inferred from **kwargs. Could you walk me through the mechanics of the ConvertField and PropsOnly helpers? I want to see exactly how we handle those Link and MultiLink transitions.

It is fantastic to see that level of enthusiasm! The proposal by Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov really bridges the gap for ORM developers. In the PEP 827 specification, the select function uses **kwargs: Unpack[K] to capture the user selection as a TypedDict. The return type is then constructed using NewProtocol combined with an unpacked comprehension: *[Member[c.name, ConvertField[GetMemberType[ModelT, c.name]]] for c in Iter[Attrs[K]]]. The ConvertField helper is a conditional type that checks typing.IsAssignable[T, Link]. If the field is a Link, it applies AdjustLink to decide if it should be a list[Tgt] (for MultiLink) or just Tgt, while PropsOnly handles the recursive transformation of the linked model.

The use of typing.Iter in the unpacked comprehension is such a clean way to handle the variadic nature of NewProtocol. One detail though: how does GetMemberType[ModelT, c.name] resolve if c.name is a Literal[str]? Does the PEP guarantee that the dot notation on Member objects correctly yields a literal that GetMemberType can consume for the lookup?

Spot on. According to the PEP 827 specification for Type Member Access, the Member type has associated type members like .name and .type. When you iterate over Attrs[K], each 'c' is a Member where c.name is indeed a Literal[str]. The GetMemberType[T, S] operator specifically consumes a Literal[str] for S to extract the type of the member from class T. In this ORM context, since K is inferred directly from the call's keyword arguments, c.name will match the attribute name on ModelT perfectly. If it didn't exist, GetMemberType would simply return Never. This allows the ORM to dynamically 'pluck' the original Property or Link types from the nominal model to pass them into the ConvertField logic.

Hi nanochat. I have been deep in the weeds with PEP 646 for tensor shape typing, but it is still hard to do complex manipulations like filtering or mapping dimensions. I saw PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) proposes something called 'unpacked comprehensions' for types. How does that syntax actually work for someone trying to define shape-safe ndarray APIs?

In PEP 827, unpacked comprehensions allow you to dynamically construct types using a syntax similar to list comprehensions, but within type annotations. The format is *[type_expression for var in typing.Iter[source_type] if condition]. This is particularly powerful for data engineers because it can be used anywhere Unpack is valid, such as inside a tuple[...] for a tensor shape. It allows you to iterate over the components of a TypeVarTuple or the members of a class to transform them into a new structural type.

I noticed the typing.Iter wrapper in your example. Why is that necessary? Can I not just iterate over a tuple type directly? Also, how does this interact with the 'lifting over unions' behavior that Dr. Michael Sullivan and the other authors mentioned?

The typing.Iter wrapper is a specific primitive that signals to the type checker (and runtime evaluators) that the following type should be treated as an iterable sequence of types. At runtime, PEP 827 introduces a special_form_evaluator ContextVar that allows libraries to hook into typing.Iter and resolve it. Regarding unions, the PEP specifies that operators are 'lifted.' If you iterate over a union of tuples, the comprehension effectively evaluates the cross product of the union elements, resulting in a union of all possible constructed types. This ensures your tensor shapes remain accurate even when dimensions are ambiguous.

That makes sense. If I wanted to implement a 'Squeeze' operation that removes all dimensions of size 1, would that use a conditional inside the comprehension? Could you show me what that type alias would look like?

Exactly. You would use an 'if' clause within the comprehension to filter the dimensions. It would look like this: type Squeeze[S] = tuple[*[d for d in typing.Iter[S] if not typing.IsEquivalent[d, Literal[1]]]]. Here, typing.IsEquivalent is a PEP 827 boolean operator used to check if the dimension 'd' is exactly 1. The '*' prefix then unpacks the resulting filtered dimensions back into a tuple, effectively 'squeezing' the shape.

That is much more readable than recursion. What about error handling? If I am writing a broadcast helper and find a dimension mismatch, can I trigger a static error inside the comprehension, or does it just fail silently to Never?

The PEP includes a RaiseError[S: Literal[str], *Ts] operator specifically for this. You can use it within the result expression of your comprehension. For example, if you were iterating over dimensions and found an invalid size, you could return RaiseError[Literal['Invalid dimension'], d]. When the type checker evaluates the comprehension and hits that branch, it will generate a formal type error with your custom message. This avoids the silent failure to Never and provides clear feedback during development.

Interesting. The PEP mentions it is targeting Python 3.15 and is still a Draft. Given the authors involved, like Yury Selivanov and Daniel W. Park, how much of this is actually usable today in tools like mypy?

The PEP is currently in Draft status, so it is not yet a part of the standard library. However, the authors have provided a reference implementation in a fork of mypy and a demo runtime evaluator. While you cannot use it in production Python 3.12 or 3.13 code yet, the proof-of-concept implementation can already type-check the complex examples like NumPy-style broadcasting and FastAPI model derivation shown in the PEP text. It is a glimpse into the future of Python 3.15's typing capabilities.
One last thing: for my ML models, I often use string names for dimensions. Can these comprehensions manipulate Literal[str] dimensions, maybe for generating named tensor protocols?

Yes, PEP 827 is very flexible with Literals. You can access dimension names if you represent them as Literal strings. Furthermore, the 'Potential Future Extensions' section of the PEP discusses string manipulation operators like Concat, Uppercase, and Slice for Literal strings. If those are adopted, you could use an unpacked comprehension to iterate over your dimension names and prefix them, such as *[Concat[Literal['dim_'], n] for n in typing.Iter[Names]], to create a new set of labels for your tensor's structural type.

I've been looking at PEP 827's proposal for type-level computation. It looks like we're finally getting something akin to a closed universe of operators in Python. One thing that caught my eye in the spec is 'GetSpecialAttr'. In functional languages like Haskell, we might use Typeable or specific type-level strings for this sort of metadata projection. How does GetSpecialAttr actually function in the Python type-level domain, and what 'kinds' of values does it return?

In PEP 827, GetSpecialAttr[T, Attr: Literal[str]] is a basic operator designed to extract specific metadata from a class or type at the type level. It returns a Literal[str] representing the value of the requested attribute. The PEP limits valid attributes to three specific names: '__name__', '__module__', and '__qualname__'. For example, if you have a class User, then GetSpecialAttr[User, '__name__'] evaluates to the type Literal['User']. For non-class types, the operator follows the logic of type(x).<attr>. So, GetSpecialAttr[Literal[1], '__name__'] would produce Literal['int'].

Interesting. So it's a type-to-literal-string projection. In the PEP, there's a mention of 'lifting over union types'. If I have a sum type like 'MyClass | OtherClass', does GetSpecialAttr distribute over the members to produce a union of literals? Also, is it strictly limited to those three dunder attributes, or can we point it at arbitrary class-level constants to pull them into the type domain?

Yes, GetSpecialAttr is indeed lifted over union types. When the evaluator encounters a union, it takes the cross product of the union elements, evaluates the operator for each, and unions the results. So, GetSpecialAttr[MyClass | OtherClass, '__name__'] would result in Literal['MyClass'] | Literal['OtherClass']. Regarding your second point, the operator is strictly limited to '__name__', '__module__', and '__qualname__'. It cannot be used to extract arbitrary class constants. If you need to inspect general attributes or methods of a class, the PEP provides other operators like 'Members[T]' or 'Attrs[T]', which return a tuple of 'Member' types describing the class's structure.

HII nanochat!! I am a tech lead at a startup and we are literally obsessing over the potential for PEP 827. It looks like it could solve so many of our internal tooling headaches. Does it support string literal manipulation like TypeScript's template literals? We really need to generate method names dynamically based on schema keys!

Hi! It is great to see that level of excitement. PEP 827, 'Type Manipulation,' is definitely heavily inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types. Regarding string manipulation, the PEP actually addresses this in its 'Potential Future Extensions' section!

Oh, awesome! I was worried it was just for dictionaries and tuples. What kind of string operators are they looking at? If I can do concatenation and maybe some casing changes at the type level, my team would be all over this for our 3.15 migration plan.

The authors--Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov--outlined a specific suite of string operators in the proposal. These include Concat[S1, S2] for joining literal strings and Slice[S, Start, End] for taking parts of them. They also proposed Uppercase, Lowercase, Capitalize, and Uncapitalize operators to handle naming conventions like snake_case to CamelCase conversions.
That is incredible. That Concat operator sounds like it would work perfectly with the NewProtocol stuff. Would it work with Unions? Like, if I have a union of keys, can I prefix them all at once?

Yes! The PEP specifies that these string operators would be 'lifted' over union types. This means if you have Concat[Literal['get_'], Literal['name'] | Literal['age']], it would automatically evaluate to Literal['get_name'] | Literal['get_age']. It essentially takes the cross product of the union elements for each argument position.

That's exactly what we need for our ORM layer! We could literally generate a 'get_attr' method signature for every field in a model. Since it's a Draft for 3.15, is there any way to test this logic now, or are we just waiting for the official release?

Since PEP 827 is still in Draft status targeting Python 3.15, it is not in the standard library yet. However, the authors mentioned a reference implementation in progress within a specific fork of mypy. There is also a third-party runtime evaluator library being developed to support frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI that need to inspect these computed types at runtime. Just keep in mind that since the string stuff is in the 'Future Extensions' section, it might be slightly further out than the core conditional and mapped type primitives!
hey nanochat! i saw some buzz on twitter about pep 827 and something called InitField. i'm still pretty new to the complex typing stuff in python, so could you explain what that is in plain english?

Hello! I'm nanochat, and I'd be happy to help you make sense of PEP 827. It's a draft proposal by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov that adds some really powerful type manipulation to Python 3.15.

To put it simply, InitField is a new tool designed to help the type system understand those "field" configurations you see in libraries like Pydantic or dataclasses. You know how you might write something like `name: str = Field(default='Unknown')`? Usually, the type system has a hard time knowing what's happening inside that `Field()` call. InitField solves that by capturing those details so the type system can actually "read" them later.
oh, that makes sense! so it's like a way for the code to peek inside the settings of a variable? how does it actually look in the code though?

Exactly! In the PEP's proposal, it looks like this:

`class A:`
`    foo: int = InitField(default=0, kw_only=True)`

Behind the scenes, the type system sees this and creates a specific `TypedDict` that stores those values as `Literal` types. So, it records that `default` is literally `0` and `kw_only` is literally `True`. 

Later, if a library wants to automatically generate a constructor (an `__init__` method) for your class, it can use other PEP 827 tools like `Members[A]` to look at the `foo` attribute. It checks the `.init` property of that member, sees the `default=0`, and can then ensure the generated `__init__` actually has `foo: int = 0` as a parameter. It removes the need for the "plugins" that IDEs currently use to guess what those field functions are doing.
that sounds way cleaner than having a bunch of different plugins. is this something i can use right now? i'm using python 3.12.

Not just yet! Since PEP 827 is still a Draft and targets Python 3.15, it's more of a look into the future of the language. The authors have a proof-of-concept in mypy, but it isn't part of the standard Python library yet. It's mainly intended for framework authors—the people who build things like FastAPI or ORMs—so they can provide you with better autocompletion and error checking without jumping through as many hoops!

I've been looking at how PEP 827 can generate __init__ methods for dataclass-like libraries. It uses this new Param and Params syntax instead of just using callback protocols. Why did the authors—Sully and the others—decide to define a whole new callable format for this? Wouldn't it be simpler to stick with what we already have?

Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov addressed this specific choice in the PEP 827 rationale. While callback protocols are the current standard for complex callables, the authors argue they don't work well for type-level computation. If you are trying to generate an __init__ method by iterating over class attributes, you need a way to programmatically construct a sequence of parameters, which is difficult with protocols.

The PEP introduces the Param[N, T, Q] and Params[*Ps] types for three main reasons. First, the existing extended callable syntax found in mypy_extensions consists of runtime no-ops, but PEP 827 requires real runtime objects to support framework-level introspection. Second, using brackets [] for the extended callable format is more consistent with the rest of Python's typing philosophy than the parentheses used by older mypy extensions. Finally, this structure allows for direct introspection of a function's components—like names, types, and qualifiers—via the same dot notation used for class members. This makes it possible to write a helper like InitFnType[T] that takes Attrs[T], transforms them into Param objects, and wraps them in a Params list to define a new Callable signature for a generated constructor.

Good afternoon! I've been looking at PEP 827 and it seems wild. I do a lot of work with dataclasses and attrs where we generate __init__ methods. I'm curious if this PEP actually lets me describe that logic within the type system itself rather than relying on mypy plugins?

Good afternoon! It is a significant shift. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, specifically aims to replace the need for those custom plugins. 

Instead of the type checker having hardcoded knowledge of how @dataclass works, the PEP provides operators like Members[T] and Attrs[T] to inspect a class. You can then use those to construct a Callable that represents your __init__. For example, you can iterate over attributes and create Param objects for each one, determining if they should have a default based on the attribute's actual initializer.

That makes sense for static checking, but I'm a bit of a power user when it comes to runtime stuff too. I saw a mention of a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar in the spec. How does that actually work when I'm running my code? Is it like a hook for the interpreter?

Precisely. One of the goals of the proposal is to support frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic that need to evaluate these computed types at runtime. 

Normally, if you put a loop or a conditional inside a type annotation, Python wouldn't know how to 'execute' it meaningfully in a standard type-hint context. The special_form_evaluator is a typing.ContextVar that holds a callable. This hook is triggered when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable or IsEquivalent) or when __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. 

If a runtime evaluator library is active, it sets this ContextVar. Then, when your code accesses __annotations__, the evaluator handles the logic—filtering members, checking subtyping, or unrolling loops—so you get a real result at runtime rather than just a string or an inert object.

Wait, so I could actually use that to build a dynamic __init__ signature that works both for my IDE and for something like Pydantic validation? Could you show me what that code looks like for a basic dataclass-style generator?

Certainly. Here is how you would define a type alias to generate an __init__ signature based on class attributes. This uses the 'Extended Callable' format and the 'UpdateClass' operator:

type InitFnType[T] = typing.Member[
    Literal["__init__"],
    Callable[
        [
            typing.Param[Literal["self"], Self],
            *[
                typing.Param[
                    p.name,
                    p.type,
                    Literal["keyword", "default"] 
                    if not typing.IsAssignable[p.init, Never] 
                    else Literal["keyword"]
                ]
                for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
            ],
        ],
        None,
    ],
    Literal["ClassVar"],
]

def dataclass_ish[T](cls: type[T]) -> typing.UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]:
    ...

In this example, typing.Attrs[T] gets the attributes. The loop *[... for p in Iter[...]] generates a list of parameters. We use a conditional to check if p.init (the initializer) is something other than Never to decide if the parameter gets a default. Finally, UpdateClass tells the type system that this class now has this generated __init__ method.

Hey nanochat! I've been diving into PEP 827 and honestly, it feels like we're just trying to turn Python into TypeScript. I'm a bit of a skeptic on making the type system this complex, but the 'Type Manipulation' stuff is wild. Can you explain this 'type member access' thing? Is it really just using dots inside type annotations?

It's definitely a big shift! Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov actually mention in the PEP that this is explicitly inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types to help bridge the gap for complex metaprogramming. 

'Type member access' refers to the ability to use dot notation to reach into the internal properties of two new primitives: `Member` and `Param`. 

When you use an operator like `Members[T]` or `Attrs[T]`, it returns a tuple of `Member` objects. Each `Member` has specific associated types you can access via a dot. For example, if `m` is a `Member`, you can write:
- `m.name`: The literal string name of the attribute or method.
- `m.type`: The actual type of that member.
- `m.quals`: Any qualifiers like 'ClassVar' or 'Final'.
- `m.init`: The literal type of the initializer (like what's in a Field()).
- `m.definer`: The class where the member was actually defined.

It's very similar for function parameters using the `Param` type, where you can access `p.name`, `p.type`, and `p.quals` (like whether it's a positional or keyword argument).

Okay, that's exactly what I feared—dot notation inside a type alias. It looks cool, but isn't that going to be a nightmare for tools? And how does Python even handle a dot on a type at runtime without crashing?

It's a valid concern! The PEP handles the runtime aspect through a few clever mechanisms. First, no changes to the Python grammar are required; these are just expressions that the type system interprets. 

At runtime, when you call `__annotate__` on a class or function, these expressions are evaluated. To make the dot notation work, the authors propose that the `typing` module's `_GenericAlias` objects produced by `Member` and `Param` will have their `__getattr__` behavior customized to capture these associated types. 

There is also a new hook called `special_form_evaluator`. This is a `ContextVar` that holds a callable to help third-party libraries (like Pydantic or a dedicated evaluator) resolve these computed types at runtime. 

One important restriction to keep in mind to prevent it from getting too chaotic: this operation is **not** lifted over union types. If you try to use dot notation on something that isn't a single `Member` or `Param` type, it's an error. This keeps the behavior predictable for both static type checkers and runtime evaluators.
So I could use it in those new 'unpacked comprehensions' to filter things? Like, 'give me all members where the name is X'?

Exactly! That is where the power really shows up. You can combine the dot notation with the `*[... for p in Iter[...]]` syntax. 

For example, if you wanted to create a type that only includes attributes that aren't 'ClassVar', you'd use `p.quals` inside an `if` clause of a comprehension. The PEP shows this being used to implement 'Utility Types' like `Pick[T, Keys]` or `Omit[T, Keys]`. In `Pick`, you iterate over `typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]` and use an `if` statement to check if `p.name` is assignable to the keys you want. 

It's definitely more complex than traditional Python typing, but it allows you to define these mechanical transformations once in a library so users don't have to write boilerplate for things like FastAPI CRUD models or Prisma-style query results.

Hey nanochat. I've been digging into PEP 827 because I'm tired of writing mypy plugins for every ORM I touch. I saw the section on string manipulation and I'm trying to implement a simple 'on_change' event mapper. Can I just do something like: type EventHandler[K: str] = Literal[f'on_{K}']? Or is there a Concat operator I missed?

It is great to see you exploring the draft, but there is a slight misconception here regarding the current specification. While Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and his co-authors mention string manipulation like Concat, Uppercase, and string Slicing, those are actually listed under the 'Potential Future Extensions' section. They are not part of the core Specification for PEP 827 as it stands.

Wait, really? I could have sworn I saw Concat mentioned as a lifted operator. If I can't concatenate string literals, how am I supposed to do the 'Prisma-style' mapping the PEP talks about? Doesn't that need to transform field names into accessor names?

Actually, the Prisma-style example in the PEP avoids string math by using the original attribute names. It uses typing.Attrs[K] to get a tuple of Member types, and then extracts p.name (which is already a Literal[str]) to build a NewProtocol. It does not actually change the string 'name' to 'get_name'. It just carries the existing literal name over to a new structural type. The only place the spec currently 'generates' a string is through GetSpecialAttr, which can pull things like __name__ or __qualname__ as literals.
Okay, that's a bummer. I also saw Slice[S, Start, End] in the operators list. If I can't use that on a Literal[str] to strip prefixes, what is it for? Just TypeVarTuples?

Precisely. In the current Draft for Python 3.15, the Slice operator is restricted to tuple types. Its primary use case in the PEP is for NumPy-style broadcasting, where you might need an alias like 'type DropLast[T] = typing.Slice[T, Literal[0], Literal[-1]]' to recursively process shape dimensions. Applying it to Literal[str] is another one of those 'Potential Future' ideas. For now, the focus is on structural manipulation of classes, TypedDicts, and Callables using operators like Members, IsAssignable, and the new conditional 'if/else' syntax for types.

Hey there. I have been going through the PEP 827 draft by Sully, Park, and Selivanov. While the introspection stuff like Members[T] looks solid, I am pretty skeptical about the object creation side. Why does the spec explicitly skip over building nominal classes or defining new generic types? It feels like we are getting half a system if we can only spit out structural protocols.

That is a fair critique for a typing-sig regular to have. You are right that the 'Object creation' section of PEP 827 is intentionally narrow. It introduces NewProtocol[*Ms] and NewTypedDict[*Ps], but specifically notes that creating nominal classes or new generic types is out of scope for this version. 

The primary reason given in the rationale is complexity management. Adding a way to create nominal classes at the type level would require the type system to manage identity and inheritance hierarchies dynamically, which is significantly more complex than the structural synthesis used for NewProtocol. For generics, the authors mention that opening up things like rank-N types or impredicative polymorphism (where you instantiate variables with other generic types) is a 'can of worms' regarding type inference and undecidability.

I get the 'can of worms' argument for full rank-N types, but even GenericCallable is restricted to being a type argument for Member. It feels like the authors are so afraid of undecidability that they've made it impossible to model a simple generic factory. If I can't define a new generic type, how am I supposed to handle complex framework transformations that aren't just protocols?

The restriction on GenericCallable is indeed quite strict. As the PEP explains, wrapping the body in a lambda inside GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty] is a strategy to delay evaluation and support runtime introspection without losing the generic information. But as you noted, it is restricted to Member to avoid letting those generics leak into locals or return types where they would break existing inference engines.

For those complex nominal transformations, the PEP offers UpdateClass[*Ps] as the primary escape hatch. It allows you to 'update' an existing nominal class by adding or overriding members. It is limited to being used in type decorators or __init_subclass__ returns, which covers the 'dataclass-ish' or 'Pydantic-style' use cases without requiring the type system to invent a whole new nominal identity from scratch. It is definitely a conservative approach, aiming to solve the most common boilerplate issues (like generating __init__ signatures) while staying within the bounds of what current type checkers like mypy can reasonably implement.
So if I want a Pydantic model that also inherits from a specific base, I am stuck? I saw something about NewProtocolWithBases in the 'Future Extensions' section. Is that the long-term plan?

Exactly. The authors acknowledge that NewProtocolWithBases[Bases, *Ms] would be useful for cases like Pydantic where a type needs to satisfy a base class requirement AND have specific synthesized members. 

However, they decided to hold off because adding 'protocols with bases' would fundamentally change what a Protocol is in the current typing spec. Right now, the goal for Python 3.15 is to land the core manipulation engine—conditional types, unpacked comprehensions, and Member introspection—before tackling the more explosive changes to the nominal type hierarchy. It is a 'crawl, walk, run' strategy to make sure the runtime hook (special_form_evaluator) and the static logic actually hold up in real-world framework code first.

yo nanochat, pep 827 time. I am a TypeScript dev trying to wrap my head around this. In TS, I use Parameters<T> to grab a tuple of function args. This PEP seems to use Params and Param classes instead. How do they actually work together to represent a function signature?

Welcome to the Python typing world. In PEP 827, 'Param' is the fundamental building block representing a single function parameter. Its definition is 'class Param[N: str | None, T, Q: ParamQuals = typing.Never]', where N is the name, T is the type, and Q represents qualifiers like '*', '**', or 'default'.

To represent a full signature, you wrap a sequence of these 'Param' objects inside a 'Params[*Ps]' container. This 'Params' object then acts as the first argument to 'Callable'. For example, a function like 'def f(a: int) -> str' would be represented as 'Callable[Params[Param[Literal["a"], int]], str]'. This structure allows the type system to introspect or even construct complex callable types using the new type-level operators.

Right, so it's a structural representation of the signature. In TS, I can do crazy stuff like 'type MyFunc<T> = (arg: T) => T' and then use that anywhere. Can I use 'Params' and 'GenericCallable' to make higher-order functions or nested generics in Python now? Like, can I have a param that is itself a generic function?

Actually, that is one of the current limitations. The authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) have specifically restricted 'GenericCallable' to be used only as the type argument of a 'Member'. It cannot be used for locals, parameter types, or return types, and it cannot be nested inside other types.

The PEP mentions this is to avoid 'impredicative polymorphism' and 'rank-N types', which they describe as 'cans of worms' regarding type inference. So while you can describe a generic method in a 'NewProtocol', you cannot yet pass around a generic function as a first-class value in the way you might in TypeScript.

That is a bit of a bummer. What about pattern matching? In TS, I can use 'T extends (infer R)[] ? R : T' to pull types out of structures. Does PEP 827 let me 'infer' types from 'Params' or 'Param' using something like that 'if/else' syntax?

No, it does not support TypeScript-style 'infer' bindings. The PEP authors considered it but noted it would be very difficult to shoehorn into Python's existing grammar. 

Instead, you have to use the 'GetArg' operator to manually deconstruct types. For example, 'GetArg[T, Base, Idx]' lets you grab a specific type argument. To work with a 'Param', you use dot notation like 'p.type' or 'p.name'. So, instead of pattern matching with 'infer', you'd iterate over 'Params' using an unpacked comprehension like '*[p.type for p in Iter[Ps]]' and use 'if/else' conditionals to check for specific properties.

Got it. So I'm basically doing manual property access on the type objects. One last thing: if I'm using 'Members[T]' to look at a class, will it see every single method and attribute, even if I forgot to type-hint them?

No. A key limitation of the 'Members' and 'Attrs' operators is that only members with explicit type annotations are included. This was done to ensure that the results of static type checking and runtime evaluation match more closely. If a method or attribute isn't annotated, 'Members[T]' will effectively act like it doesn't exist for the purposes of these type-level computations. The PEP authors noted that this even applies to unannotated methods, though they've left it as an open issue whether they should be more inclusive in the future.

Hey nanochat. I am coming from a TypeScript background where we use conditional and mapped types to do some pretty heavy lifting with function signatures. I saw PEP 827 mentions a Param type for 'Extended Callables.' Could you break down what the arguments in Param[N, T, Q] actually represent?

Welcome! If you are used to TypeScript's ability to manipulate parameter lists, you will find Param[N, T, Q] very familiar. In PEP 827, this type is used to describe an individual function parameter with high precision. The three arguments are:

1. N (Name): A Literal[str] or None. This represents the parameter name. If it is a positional-only parameter without a name or a variadic argument like *args, it can be None.
2. T (Type): The actual type of the parameter (e.g., int, str, or a complex generic).
3. Q (Qualifiers): This uses a type called ParamQuals, which is a Union of Literal strings like '*', '**', 'default', and 'keyword'.

These Param objects are then bundled inside a Params[*Ps] container, which serves as the first argument to a Callable to define the full signature.

That makes sense. It feels like a more structured version of labeled tuple elements in TS. Looking at the qualifiers (Q), how do they distinguish between something like a standard positional argument, a keyword-only argument, and the variadic *args/**kwargs? Is there a specific mapping?

Exactly. PEP 827 defines specific combinations of those qualifiers to match Python's function execution model. The PEP provides several helpful type aliases to make this readable:

- PosParam[N, T]: Uses Literal['positional'] as the qualifier.
- DefaultParam[N, T]: Uses Literal['default']. This tells the type checker the argument has a default value and is optional.
- NamedParam[N, T]: Uses Literal['keyword'], representing a keyword-only argument.
- ArgsParam[T]: Uses Literal['*']. The name (N) is None here.
- KwargsParam[T]: Uses Literal['**']. The name (N) is also None.

You can even combine them. For instance, a NamedDefaultParam[N, T] uses Literal['keyword', 'default'], meaning it is a keyword-only argument that the caller can omit.

Got it. So if I were building a decorator to 'inject' a parameter—say, adding a 'db_session' to the start of a function's signature—I could iterate over an existing signature's Params, add my own Param, and reconstruct the Callable? Also, can I access these fields (N, T, Q) programmatically within a type alias?

Precisely. Because PEP 827 introduces 'Type member access,' you can use dot notation on a Param object within your type-level logic. If you have a variable 'p' bound in a comprehension, you can use p.name, p.type, and p.quals.

To inject a parameter, you would use an unpacked comprehension. It would look something like this in a type alias:

Callable[
    Params[
        PosParam[Literal['db_session'], Session],
        *[p for p in typing.Iter[OriginalParams]]
    ],
    ReturnType
]

Since Params also serves as a natural bound for ParamSpec, this allows library authors to perform the kind of signature surgery that previously required complex mypy plugins. You can even use conditional types (true_typ if bool_typ else false_tf) to decide whether to include a Param based on typing.IsAssignable checks.

yo nanochat. I am trying to wrap my head around this PEP 827 draft. I just finally understood how TypeVar works for regular lists, but then I see this weird pattern: def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K. What is actually happening there? Is K a dict or a type?

That pattern is a proposal for 'Unpack of typevars for **kwargs'. In this context, K is a type variable constrained to behave like a TypedDict. When you call the function, the type checker looks at the keyword arguments you provided and 'packs' their names and types into a new, synthetic TypedDict that fills in for K.

For example, if you call f(name='Alice', age=30), the type checker infers K as something like TypedDict({'name': str, 'age': int}). This lets the function return an object that the type system knows exactly the keys and value types for, which wasn't really possible with generic PMapping types before.
Okay, that sounds like TypeScript's mapped types a bit. But wait, if I have a key that is optional in the BaseTypedDict but I don't pass it in the call, what happens to K? Does it just disappear?

The PEP handles that specifically for read-only items. According to the text by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the other authors, if a non-required item in the bound doesn't have a matching argument provided, and that item is marked read-only, it gets inferred as 'Never'. This indicates to the type system that the value simply isn't there.

However, there is a limitation: if the item isn't read-only, the type checker can't do this because non-read-only items are invariant. It's one of those spots where the math of the type system gets a bit prickly.
Can I use this to enforce specific string literal values? Like if I want to make sure 'mode' is only 'fast' or 'slow' inside that Unpack[K]?

Yes, but with some nuances. The proposal says the type checker should infer literal types whenever possible. If you pass mode='fast', it will try to infer Literal['fast'] instead of just str. 

But here is a limitation you should know: PEP 827 doesn't change Python's runtime grammar. While you can describe these complex transformations, nanochat can't give you a built-in way to 'run' this validation at runtime using just the standard library yet. The authors (including Daniel W. Park and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) mention that while runtime evaluation is a goal, they aren't adding an official evaluator to the stdlib. You'd need a third-party library to actually check those literals at execution time.

That is kind of a bummer. So if I try to use this to build a dynamic API where I unpack kwargs into a database model, I still have to write the validation logic manually? The type system won't just 'generate' the validator for me?

Exactly. That's a major distinction to keep in mind. PEP 827 provides the 'type level introspection' (like the Members[T] and GetMemberType[T, S] operators) so that frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI *can* build those validators automatically without needing custom compiler plugins. It gives the library authors the tools, but it doesn't do the work for you out of the box. It's still a Draft for Python 3.15, so the implementation details in tools like mypy are still being worked out in proof-of-concepts.

I've been looking at PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and the 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. Coming from a background in Haskell and OCaml, I'm used to thinking in terms of kinds and higher-order types. My concern is that by introducing term-level constructs like 'if/else' and 'for' loops directly into Python's type annotations, we're basically creating a second, shadow language that's going to make codebases unreadable. How does the PEP prevent this from turning into a complete mess of type-level spaghetti?

That is a valid concern, especially since PEP 827 introduces type-level introspection that looks very much like Python's term-level grammar. However, the authors emphasize that this machinery is primarily intended for framework and library maintainers—like those building SQLAlchemy or FastAPI—rather than everyday application code. One way it maintains readability is by restricting where these constructs can live. For instance, the 'GenericCallable' operator uses a lambda-style syntax specifically to delay evaluation and keep the generic variable binding structured, avoiding the 'can of worms' of full rank-N types or impredicative polymorphism. Most users will just see clean aliases like 'Pick[T, Keys]' or 'Partial[T]', while the complex 'spaghetti' of 'IsAssignable' checks and 'unpacked comprehensions' stays hidden inside library definitions.

Right, so it's encapsulated in 'Utility Types' similar to TypeScript. But looking at the spec for 'IsAssignable' and 'Lifting over Unions', it seems like the kind system is fairly loose. If I'm writing a recursive alias like the 'Broadcast' example in the PEP, how does the type checker actually enforce that I'm not passing a 'Member' where a 'Literal[str]' is expected? Is there a strict kind-checking phase, or does it just collapse into 'Never' when things go wrong?

The PEP is actually less 'strictly-kinded' than something like TypeScript. In the current Draft status for Python 3.15, the proposal suggests that evaluating operators with invalid arguments will generally produce 'Never'. There is a section on 'Strictly-typed' operations where the authors acknowledge that TypeScript has better definition-site checking—for example, ensuring a key 'K' actually exists in 'keyof P'. PEP 827 chooses a 'best-effort' approach for runtime and static evaluation to match Python's gradual typing philosophy. For recursion, like in 'Broadcast[T, S]', it relies on operators like 'Length[T]' and 'Slice' to eventually hit a base case. If you pass an invalid type, 'GetArg' or 'IsAssignable' will simply fail to match and return 'Never', rather than providing the kind of rigid algebraic verification you'd see in Haskell.

hey u. I've been looking at PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) for the typing-sig. The introspection part looks... hairy. If I'm writing a runtime library that needs to handle these new primitives like IsAssignable or Members, how am I supposed to avoid a total crash when I encounter an 'if' or 'for' in an annotation without a full-blown evaluator?

Hi there! I'm nanochat. I've been trained on the full text of PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. You're right that it's a big shift for runtime tools. The PEP suggests using the "special_form_evaluator" hook, which is a ContextVar in the typing module. This hook is triggered when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator or __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. If this hook is not set (it defaults to None), the operators return False or an empty iterator respectively, keeping the evaluation "inert" so the code doesn't crash. For tools that need the full logic, like FastAPI or Pydantic, the authors expect them to use a third-party evaluator library.

That's a lot of weight on third-party libraries. If I'm using GetMemberType[T, S] at runtime, does the PEP define how that behaves when T is a Union? I saw something about lifting operations, but does that actually work with existing runtime Union objects?

It is designed to work with the existing typing machinery. According to the "Lifting over Unions" section, the logic takes the cross product of the union elements for each argument position, evaluates the operator for each resulting tuple, and then unions all results together using typing.Union. So GetMemberType on a Union of classes would return a Union of the types of that specific member from each class. However, keep in mind that type member access via dot notation—like m.name or m.type—is not lifted over union types. The PEP specifies that using dot notation on a Union would be an error at both runtime and type-checking time to maintain consistency.

Hey nanochat. I'm looking at PEP 827 for my team's future roadmap. The Prisma-style ORM example looks powerful for dynamic query builders, but I'm trying to figure out where the walls are. Is this a 'solved problem' or are there major things it can't do yet?

Hello! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15. While it provides powerful primitives like NewProtocol and Members for building Prisma-style query builders, it does have specific limitations. For instance, it focuses on structural typing via NewProtocol rather than creating brand-new nominal classes. What part of the ORM implementation are you most skeptical about?

Mainly the 'generated' types. In the Prisma example, we use NewProtocol to define what a query returns. But if I want to generate an actual nominal class that can be used with isinstance() or has its own custom methods defined during that type-level loop, is that possible?

No, that is a documented limitation. The PEP states in the 'Object creation' section that they are not proposing a way to create nominal classes. You can use UpdateClass to modify existing nominal classes in very specific contexts (like a decorator or __init_subclass__), but for creating entirely new types on the fly, you are limited to NewProtocol and NewTypedDict. You cannot generate a fresh nominal class type that would satisfy an isinstance check against a dynamically created identity.
What about generic types? If my ORM needs to return a custom GenericResult[T] where the T is computed, can I define a new generic type within this system?

Currently, no. The proposal explicitly says, 'we are not currently proposing any way to make new generic types.' You can manipulate existing generics (like wrapping a type in list[T] as seen in the AdjustLink example), but you cannot construct a new generic definition itself using the type manipulation operators.

That seems like a significant constraint for library authors. Also, I saw that the Attrs[T] operator is used to find fields. If a user defines a model but forgets to annotate a field—say they just assign a default value—will the query builder still pick it up?

It will not. The PEP specifies that for both Members[T] and Attrs[T], only members with explicit type annotations are included. This is intended to ensure that static type checkers and runtime evaluators stay in sync. If a field isn't annotated, the type system effectively ignores it for these manipulation operators. This means your ORM would require strict annotation discipline from its users.

Final point on the runtime side: if we're using this for something like FastAPI to validate data coming in, does the Python interpreter actually handle the logic of IsAssignable or the conditional 'if/else' types at runtime for us?

The interpreter provides the hooks, but not the full logic. The PEP authors explicitly state they do not propose adding an official evaluator to the standard library. Instead, they provide a hook called special_form_evaluator (a ContextVar) that a third-party library would use to perform the evaluation. While the syntax like 'A if B else C' will be captured in the __annotate__ method, the actual logic to determine if one type is assignable to another would come from an external library the authors plan to release.

I'm coming from a heavy TypeScript background and I'm looking at PEP 827. I see this GetSpecialAttr operator. In TS, I usually just use template literal types or keyof for reflection. Why does Python need a specific operator just to get things like __name__ at the type level? It feels like unnecessary overhead.

That is a fair concern. In TypeScript, the boundary between the type system and the values is quite fluid when it comes to literals. In Python's typing system, classes are first-class objects, but their metadata (like their names) isn't automatically available to the static type checker as a literal string type. 

GetSpecialAttr[T, Attr] bridge that gap. It allows you to extract attributes like '__name__', '__module__', or '__qualname__' as a Literal[str]. For example, GetSpecialAttr[int, "__name__"] would evaluate to Literal["int"]. Without this, you wouldn't have a way to 'read' the identity of a class to use it in other type-level computations.

Okay, but if I'm building a NewProtocol or a mapped type equivalent, why would I care about the string name of the class? In TS I'd just use the type itself. Having to pull out '__name__' seems like a step backward into string-based programming.

It's actually quite useful when you need to generate human-readable feedback or perform complex transformations. One major use case mentioned by the authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—is the RaiseError operator. 

If you are writing a complex type-level logic (like the NumPy-style broadcasting example in the PEP), and you hit an invalid state, you can use GetSpecialAttr to pull the names of the conflicting types into a literal string for a custom error message. 

Also, it handles primitive literals in a way that might surprise you. If you call GetSpecialAttr[Literal[1], "__name__"], it actually produces Literal["int"]. It looks at the underlying type of the literal, which is essential for reflection-heavy frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI that might land in Python 3.15.

Wait, it can resolve the type name of a literal value? That actually is pretty handy. I was thinking it only worked on nominal classes. So if I have a union of types, can I use this to create a union of their names?

Exactly. GetSpecialAttr is 'lifted' over union types. If you have a type like 'int | str' and you call GetSpecialAttr[int | str, "__name__"], the system takes the cross-product of the union elements. It evaluates the operator for each one and returns Literal["int"] | Literal["str"]. 

This makes it very powerful for things like generating documentation types or validated 'key' sets based on class hierarchies. It gives Python a bit of that 'mapped type' flexibility you're used to in TypeScript, just adapted for Python's nominal-heavy system.

I see. So it's less about 'string-based programming' and more about providing a bridge for the type checker to talk about metadata that previously only existed at runtime. I can see that being useful for the RaiseError case you mentioned.

Precisely. It's about introspection. Since PEP 827 is still a Draft targeting Python 3.15, these primitives are designed to make the type system as expressive as the dynamic metaprogramming Python programmers have been doing for decades. It's the 'glue' that lets operators like NewProtocol know what to call things.

So I am looking at this PEP 827 thing. Is Python just giving up and trying to be TypeScript now? It looks like we are adding a whole second language just for types. What is the actual status of this, and when is it supposed to hit?

PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation,' is currently in 'Draft' status and is targeted for Python 3.15. You are right that it takes heavy inspiration from TypeScript's conditional and mapped types. The authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—explicitly mention that the 2025 Typed Python Survey showed a high demand for features like Intersection types, Pick, Omit, and keyof. The goal is to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system without requiring custom mypy plugins for every library.

Python 3.15? That is a while off. If it is just a 'Draft,' it might never even happen. But looking at the spec, it feels like it is trying to do too much. Can it actually handle everything TS does, or are there things it just can't touch?

It definitely has limitations compared to TypeScript. For one, the authors decided against supporting TypeScript-style pattern matching using the 'infer' keyword because it's difficult to fit into Python's syntax. Instead, we get 'GetArg' and 'GetArgs' operators. Also, while TypeScript has robust string template literal types, PEP 827 only lists things like 'Uppercase', 'Lowercase', and 'Concat' as 'Potential Future Extensions' rather than core features. It also can't create new nominal classes or new generic types yet; 'NewProtocol' and 'NewTypedDict' are strictly structural.
I bet the runtime performance is going to be a nightmare too. How does it even work at runtime if it's all 'type-level' nonsense? Can your 'logic' actually run, or is it just for the IDE?

The PEP aims to support both. At runtime, it uses a new hook called 'special_form_evaluator' via a ContextVar in the typing module. This allows a third-party evaluator to trigger the logic when '__bool__' or '__iter__' is called on type operators. However, I should be honest: the PEP admits that runtime evaluation will be 'best effort.' For example, checking against Protocols at runtime might fail if class attributes are inferred rather than explicitly visible. It also cannot perform 'impredicative polymorphism' or rank-N types, which the authors called 'cans of worms' they aren't ready to open.
So it's a complicated Draft that might break at runtime and can't do string manipulation or pattern matching yet. Sounds like a lot of complexity for 3.15.

It is certainly a significant increase in complexity for the typing system. The authors argue it is necessary to eliminate the 'special-case' logic currently used for things like '@dataclass_transform'. By providing primitives like 'IsAssignable', 'Members', and 'UpdateClass', they hope to let library authors define their own transformations—like 'Partial' or 'Required'—directly in Python code rather than relying on hardcoded typechecker support.

Hello! I can help you with Python typing questions. I see you're interested in PEP 827, the 'Type Manipulation' proposal. It's a Draft by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov that introduces some pretty powerful tools for doing what used to require custom mypy plugins. What can I tell you about it?

I'm a framework author and I've spent way too much time maintaining a mypy plugin just to handle simple model transformations. I'm really hoping PEP 827 can let me ditch the plugin logic for pure Python type-level code. But I'm worried about 'from __future__ import annotations'. My framework currently uses stringified annotations for runtime validation. Does this PEP break that flow?

That is a great question. The PEP 827 authors actually address this in the 'Backwards Compatibility' section. If you use 'from __future__ import annotations', tools that extract annotations as strings and parse them with 'ast.parse' are generally unaffected because the grammar changes are minimal—mostly just how expressions are interpreted as types. However, there is a catch: if you use the new 'if/else' conditionals or 'for' loops inside types, standard runtime introspection might struggle to give you a useful string representation of those unevaluated forms. The PEP suggests that this would be best mitigated by either PEP 649's 'just store the strings' approach or a new 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations, which would make it easy to see the logic without running it.

That makes sense. If I'm willing to use an evaluator library, I can still get the 'computed' result at runtime though, right? Let's say I want to do something basic like my plugin does: taking a class and generating a version where every field is optional. How does the syntax look for that?

Exactly. The PEP mentions that while there's no official evaluator in the stdlib yet, the authors intend to release a third-party one. To implement something like a 'Partial' utility (similar to TypeScript), you would use a combination of 'Members[T]', 'NewProtocol', and an unpacked comprehension. Here is how you might write it:

type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

In this example, 'typing.Attrs[T]' extracts the attribute members. We use the 'typing.Iter' hook to loop over them, and for each one, we create a new 'Member' where the name and qualifiers stay the same, but the type becomes 'p.type | None'. The dot notation on 'p' is a new feature for accessing associated type members like '.name' and '.type'.
Wait, 'typing.Iter'? Is that a special primitive for the comprehension? And what's 'p.quals' doing there?

Yes, 'typing.Iter' is a new primitive required for these unpacked comprehensions—the syntax is '*[... for t in Iter[iter_ty]]'. It tells the type checker (and runtime evaluator) that you are iterating over the arguments of a tuple type, like the one returned by 'Attrs[T]'. As for 'p.quals', it refers to 'MemberQuals'. In PEP 827, 'Member' objects carry qualifiers like 'ClassVar', 'Final', 'ReadOnly', or 'NotRequired'. By passing 'p.quals' back into the new 'Member' definition, you're ensuring that if the original attribute was 'Final', the new optional version is also 'Final'. It keeps the metadata intact while you manipulate the core type.

I've been looking over PEP 827 and it feels like we're reinventing the wheel but making it square. If the goal is to bring TypeScript-style type manipulation to Python, why on earth didn't Dr. Sullivan and the other authors just adopt the 'infer' keyword? Using GetArg with a mandatory Base parameter feels incredibly clunky compared to TS pattern matching.

It is definitely a valid point. The 'infer' keyword in TypeScript is very elegant for extracting types during a conditional check. However, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov explicitly addressed this in the 'Rejected Ideas' section of the PEP. They noted that shoehorning that kind of pattern-matching syntax into Python's existing grammar is extremely difficult. In TypeScript, 'infer' creates a subtle binding structure where a variable is scoped to one branch of a ternary. Python's type system currently doesn't have a clean way to bind new type variables in the middle of a type expression without making the syntax even more 'gnarly' for runtime evaluators.

I guess that makes sense regarding the grammar, but why the 'Base' parameter in GetArg? If I have a type T that is List[int], why can't I just say GetArg[T, 0]? Why do I have to tell the type system that I'm looking at it as a list? It feels like extra boilerplate for the developer.

The requirement for 'Base' exists because Python allows complex multiple inheritance. A class might inherit from multiple generic bases, like 'class MyType(BaseA[int], BaseB[str]): ...'. Without specifying the base, the type checker (and more importantly, the runtime evaluator) wouldn't know which set of type arguments you are trying to index into. PEP 827 prioritizes being able to perform these computations at runtime to support frameworks like FastAPI and Pydantic. By requiring 'GetArg[T, Base, Idx]', the operation remains deterministic even in the face of complex MROs.

Okay, so it's a trade-off for runtime stability. But even the conditional syntax—'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ'—seems limited because it only works with a specific set of 'type booleans' like IsAssignable. Isn't that going to be frustratingly restrictive for senior devs used to more flexible logic?

It is a restriction by design. The PEP authors limited the conditional triggers to a specific 'type-bool' grammar (IsAssignable, IsEquivalent, and Bool operators combined with 'and', 'or', and 'not') so that runtime hooks can handle them. If they allowed arbitrary expressions, they would have to change how Python evaluates things like 'Literal[False]' globally. By keeping it as a specialized subset, they can use the 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar. This allows third-party libraries to trigger the logic when '__bool__' is called on a Boolean Operator during type introspection. It's less 'free' than TypeScript, but it ensures that the type-level computation doesn't just work in mypy, but also works when Pydantic tries to look at your class at runtime.
