I was looking at PEP 827 because I'm sick of writing the same model three times for FastAPI (Hero, HeroCreate, HeroUpdate). I saw this thing called special_form_evaluator. Is that the function I call to generate the new classes? Like, I just pass my base class to it and it spits out the Pydantic model?

Not quite. There is a bit of a misconception there. The special_form_evaluator introduced in PEP 827 is not a high-level function for generating classes; it is a low-level ContextVar used for runtime introspection plumbing.

To actually derive your models, you would use type-level operators like NewProtocol or NewTypedDict combined with comprehensions. For example, your HeroCreate might look like this in the new syntax:

type HeroCreate[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]]]
    ]
]

The special_form_evaluator is what allows a third-party library to actually execute that 'for' loop and 'if' check at runtime when FastAPI or Pydantic looks at the annotations.
Wait, so if it's just a 'ContextVar', why do the authors (I think it was Sullivan and Selivanov?) say it defaults to None? If I don't set it, does my HeroCreate type just break?

Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov designed it to 'fail safe'. If special_form_evaluator is None (the default), the type system stays inert at runtime. Specifically, if Python tries to evaluate a boolean operator like IsAssignable inside a conditional type, it returns False. If it tries to iterate over typing.Iter, it returns an empty iterator.

This prevents the runtime from crashing when it encounters these complex new structures. It only 'activates' when an evaluator library sets that ContextVar to a specific callable. That callable intercepts the __bool__ calls on boolean operators and __iter__ calls on typing.Iter to perform the actual type-level logic you've defined.
So as a user, I probably won't ever touch special_form_evaluator directly? I'll just use the NewProtocol and Iter stuff, and FastAPI will handle the ContextVar part?

Exactly. You would write the 'type' aliases using the operators provided by the PEP, and the framework maintainers would use the special_form_evaluator hook to make sure those aliases resolve to real data structures at runtime. 

It is basically the bridge between the static declaration (which type checkers like mypy understand) and the dynamic requirements of frameworks like FastAPI that need to know exactly which fields exist to validate JSON. The PEP authors noted they don't plan to put a full evaluator in the standard library itself, but this hook makes it possible for the community to build them.

Why are we turning Python into TypeScript? I'm looking at PEP 827 and it looks like a mess of brackets. Do we really need 'conditional types' and 'mapped types' in a language that was supposed to be simple?

The authors of PEP 827—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—actually acknowledge that TypeScript is a major influence here. The reason for the complexity is a massive gap in how Python works. Right now, Python libraries do incredible things with dynamic metaprogramming (like SQLAlchemy or FastAPI), but the type system is too rigid to follow along. We end up needing custom IDE plugins for every single library just to get autocomplete. This PEP tries to bridge that gap so the type system can finally keep up with the language's actual expressiveness.

But we already have things like @dataclass_transform in PEP 681. That seems much cleaner than this weird syntax like '*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]'. Why can't we just stick to specific decorators instead of turning Python into a logic programming language?

That is a fair comparison, but the PEP argues that @dataclass_transform is a 'lowest-common denominator' approach. It only covers one very specific pattern. If you want to do something slightly different, like how the Prisma ORM generates types based on a selection dictionary, PEP 681 can't help you. You'd be back to writing a mypy plugin. PEP 827 introduces generic operators like Members[T], GetArg[T, Base, Idx], and NewProtocol so that library authors can define these transformations themselves directly in Python code, without needing the core team to add a new decorator for every use case.

It still feels like overkill. If I see 'T if bool_typ else false_typ' inside a type alias, I'm going to lose it. TypeScript uses the 'extends' keyword which is much more natural for subtyping. Why did they go with 'if/else' and 'IsAssignable' instead?

The PEP actually discusses why they didn't copy TypeScript's 'extends' syntax. They felt that 'extends' combined with the 'infer' keyword was too difficult to shoehorn into Python's grammar without making it even more confusing. By using 'if/else' and 'IsAssignable[T, S]', they stay within the existing Python expression grammar. It might look heavy on the brackets, but it allows for things like 'typing.Bool[Empty[T]]' to drive logic. It's definitely a shift, but it's targeted at 3.15 to give framework maintainers the power to build tools like 'HeroPublic' or 'HeroUpdate' models in FastAPI automatically without duplicating every class definition.

Hey nanochat! I've been seeing some posts on Twitter about PEP 827 and it mentions something called 'type booleans.' I'm still a bit of a beginner with types in Python, but are these just like regular True and False? How does Python tell them apart from other types?

Hello! It is great that you are diving into PEP 827. To keep it simple, type booleans are not the same as the regular True or False values you use in your logic. Instead, they are a special set of instructions that only live inside the type system.

PEP 827, written by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, introduces these to let the type checker make decisions. A type boolean is usually a result of a 'Boolean Operator' like IsAssignable[T, S] or IsEquivalent[T, S]. When a type checker looks at these in an annotation, they turn into Literal[True] or Literal[False].

Oh, so they are like 'logic for types'? That makes sense. But if they just turn into Literal[True], why does the PEP call them out as a separate thing? Can I just use a regular string or int as the condition in those new 'if' types I saw?

That is exactly what they are! The reason they are distinguished is mainly for the grammar of the language. PEP 827 restricts what can go into the 'if' part of a conditional type. You cannot just put a regular type like 'int' or 'str' there; it has to be a 'type-bool'.

Technically, a type boolean is built from specific operators like IsAssignable, IsEquivalent, and Bool[T], which can be combined using 'and', 'or', 'not', or even 'all()' and 'any()'. The PEP authors did this so that at runtime, these operators can produce special type values without messing up how regular Literals work. So while a type boolean evaluates to Literal[True] or Literal[False], it has to start as one of those approved boolean operators to be valid in a condition.
Got it. So if I wanted to check if something is a string, I'd use IsAssignable? And I could use 'and' to check two things at once? That sounds like I'm writing a tiny program inside my type hints.

You hit the nail on the head. You are basically doing 'type-level computation.' For example, you could write something like 'int if IsAssignable[T, str] else list[T]'. 

Behind the scenes, the type checker sees 'IsAssignable[T, str]', realizes it is a type boolean, and checks if T is actually a string. If it is, the whole thing becomes 'int'. If not, it becomes 'list[T]'. It is a powerful way to bridge the gap between dynamic Python code and static type checking, which is exactly what 'Sully', Daniel, and Yury intended with this draft for Python 3.15.

hey nanochat, I am digging into PEP 827. I am a tech lead evaluating whether our internal framework can leverage these new 'extended callables'. Can you break down the relationship between Params and Param for me?

Hello! PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) introduces a more granular way to define and introspect callables. Currently, standard Python callables are quite opaque to the type system. The proposal by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov introduces two key structures: Param and Params.

Param is a type that describes a single function parameter. It takes three arguments: its name (N), its type (T), and its qualifiers (Q). 

Params is a variadic container that wraps a sequence of these Param types. When you use Params as the first argument to a Callable, like Callable[Params[...], ReturnType], it signals to the type checker and runtime evaluators that you are using the extended callable format rather than the standard list-of-types format.
Got it. So Params is essentially the bundle. But functions are complex. How does Param actually encode things like keyword-only arguments or *args and **kwargs? The PEP mentions ParamQuals.

Exactly. The complexity is handled by the third argument in Param, which is a union of literals defined as ParamQuals. The valid literal values are "*", "**", "default", "keyword", and "positional".

To make it readable, the PEP provides several type abbreviations:

- PosParam[N, T]: A positional-only argument.
- DefaultParam[N, T]: A parameter with a default value.
- NamedParam[N, T]: A keyword-only parameter.
- ArgsParam[T]: Represents *args (qualifier "*").
- KwargsParam[T]: Represents **kwargs (qualifier "**").

For example, if you have def f(*args: int), that's represented inside Params as Param[None, int, Literal["*"]].

Okay, that's precise. As a lead, I'm worried about the 'metadata' aspect. Does this replace ParamSpec, or does it play nicely with it? And how would my team actually 'inspect' these at runtime if we're building something like a dynamic API validator?

It is designed to play nicely. In fact, Params serves as a natural bound for ParamSpec. You can use it to constrain what a ParamSpec can capture.

For runtime introspection, PEP 827 is quite powerful. Unlike current annotations which can be hard to parse, these extended callables are designed to be evaluated. The PEP introduces a typing.special_form_evaluator hook (a ContextVar) that allows framework authors to trigger evaluation when __bool__ or __iter__ is called on these type objects.

You can actually iterate over the parameters of a callable using the new Iter operator: *[p for p in typing.Iter[MyParams]]. This means your framework could programmatically walk through a function's signature, check qualifiers like 'default', and generate validation logic or JSON schemas entirely within the type system.

Wait, the Iter thing is interesting. If I have a Params bundle, can I manipulate it? Like, can I take an existing function's Params and append a new parameter to the end before passing it to a new Callable?

Yes, that is a core goal of the proposal. Since Params takes variadic arguments (*Ps), you can use unpacked comprehensions to rebuild signatures. 

If you have a function's parameters captured in a type P, you could construct a new signature like:

Params[*[p for p in typing.Iter[P]], NamedParam[Literal['new_arg'], int]]

This is much more flexible than PEP 612's Concatenate, which only allows adding positional arguments to the front. With PEP 827, you can filter, modify, or extend the parameter list anywhere because you are treating the signature as a list of Param objects that you can iterate over and reconstruct.

That is a significant jump in expressiveness. Last question for now: since this is targeting Python 3.15 and is currently a Draft, what's the risk for a team like mine? If we start designing around this, are there parts of the Param spec that are still 'open issues'?

There are definitely open issues. The authors are still debating whether Param should mirror inspect.Signature more closely (using Enums like POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD) or keep the current string literal qualifiers. There is also an open question about whether Members and Params should return unannotated arguments or if they should remain strictly focused on explicitly typed ones.

Since it is a Draft, the syntax for 'GenericCallable'—which allows callables to have their own bound type variables—is also restricted to specific contexts to avoid 'cans of worms' like undecidable type inference. It is a powerful tool, but as a tech lead, you should treat it as a preview of where Python 3.15 is headed rather than a settled API.

hey nanochat, I have been reading about PEP 827 and this thing called special_form_evaluator. I know my way around TypeVars and Generics, but this proposal seems to go way beyond that. What is the high-level idea behind this ContextVar?

Hello! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, is a Draft proposal for Python 3.15 that introduces Type Manipulation. The special_form_evaluator is a ContextVar added to the typing module to bridge the gap between static type definitions and runtime execution. It allows third-party libraries to provide a custom evaluator that resolves the new computed type expressions—like conditional types (if/else) and type-level loops—whenever they are accessed at runtime via the __annotate__ method.
Okay, so it is a hook for runtime tools. But why do we need a ContextVar for that? I thought type hints were mostly just static metadata that tools like mypy look at.

In PEP 827, type annotations are no longer just static metadata; they can contain logic. For instance, you can write a type like 'T if IsAssignable[S, T] else None'. When a runtime framework like Pydantic or FastAPI tries to use that type, it needs to 'calculate' which branch of the 'if' to take. Since Python evaluates these expressions when you fetch annotations, it needs a mechanism to determine the truthiness of a type check like IsAssignable. The special_form_evaluator ContextVar provides that logic. Without it, these boolean operators would simply return False by default, and Iter would return an empty iterator.
That makes sense. If I were writing a framework, what kind of object is actually passed to this evaluator, and how do I implement it?

The special_form_evaluator expects a callable. When __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable or IsEquivalent) or when __iter__ is called on typing.Iter, the typing module invokes this callable with a typing._GenericAlias as its argument. Your evaluator must then process that alias—inspecting its arguments and the types involved—and return a value that can be converted to a bool or an iterator. This allows your library to 'run' the type-level logic using its own internal logic for subtyping and member inspection.
Wait, so if I have a complex conditional type, Python just calls this hook in the middle of the 'if' statement? Is that how it works?

Exactly. Because PEP 827 uses the existing Python 'if/else' syntax for conditional types, the interpreter naturally calls the __bool__ method on the condition. For example, in 'TrueType if IsAssignable[T, S] else FalseType', Python calls bool(IsAssignable[T, S]). The typing module's implementation of that operator checks the special_form_evaluator ContextVar. If it finds your hook, it lets your hook decide the result. This avoids changing Python's core grammar while still allowing types to behave like mini-programs during runtime evaluation.
I see. The PEP mentions this is useful for the FastAPI CRUD example. How does the evaluator handle things like the Members or Attrs operators?

Operators like Members[T] and Attrs[T] return tuples of Member objects. In a runtime scenario, your evaluator would use the information in the ContextVar to populate those Member objects with the actual data from the class T—its names, types, and qualifiers like Final or ClassVar. This is crucial for things like the 'Create' type alias in the FastAPI example, which needs to iterate over a class's members and filter out things like primary keys. By setting the special_form_evaluator, the framework can ensure that the loop '*[... for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]' actually finds the real attributes defined on your model.
Got it. So it basically turns 'typing' into a programmable interface for type transformations. Since it is just a Draft for 3.15, are people already using a reference implementation for this?

The PEP authors have provided a demo of a runtime evaluator and a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can already type-check the examples in the proposal, including the Prisma-style ORM and NumPy broadcasting cases. However, since it is a Draft, it is primarily intended for framework maintainers to experiment with. As Dr. Sullivan and his co-authors note, the goal is to provide a standardized way to do the kind of complex metaprogramming that previously required brittle type-checker plugins. If it lands in 3.15, it will likely change how high-level Python libraries handle data validation and code generation forever.

Hi nanochat, quick typing question. I'm a heavy user of dataclasses and attrs, and I've been looking at PEP 827. I'm trying to see if I can use it to auto-generate __init__ signatures at the type level, but specifically, can I read metadata I put inside Annotated? Like if I have Annotated[int, 'field_info'], can the type system see that string?

Hello! PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov is designed exactly for that kind of metaprogramming. For generating __init__ signatures, it introduces a logic where you can iterate over Attrs[T] and use UpdateClass to modify a class's members. However, when it comes to Annotated, there is a significant limitation: the current draft does not include built-in operators for it in the core specification.
Wait, really? I thought I saw something about GetAnnotations or something similar. Is that not part of the plan?

You have a good eye—GetAnnotations[T] and DropAnnotations[T] are indeed mentioned in the PEP text, but they are categorized under "Potential Future Extensions." The authors acknowledge that libraries like FastAPI use Annotated heavily, but for the initial Draft targeting Python 3.15, those operators aren't part of the primary specification. This means, as it stands, you can't officially use the type system to pull out that 'field_info' string from an Annotated wrapper.

That's a bit of a bummer for the ORM stuff I'm working on. If I can't use Annotated, how am I supposed to handle field-specific metadata? Like, if a field is a primary key or has a specific database alias?

The PEP suggests using a new type called InitField[KwargDict] for that. Instead of relying on Annotated, you would use an assignment like 'foo: int = InitField(primary_key=True)'. When you inspect the class using Members[T] or Attrs[T], each Member has an .init attribute. This .init attribute holds the literal types of the arguments passed to the field constructor. So while you can't reach into Annotated yet, you can reach into the initializer of the attribute to find those metadata flags.

Okay, so I'd have to switch my metadata from the type hint to the actual assignment. Can I at least use those results to build the __init__ method? Like, can I make a parameter optional if the InitField has a default?

Yes, absolutely. The PEP shows an example of an InitFnType[T] alias. It iterates over Attrs[T] and uses a conditional type: it checks if the field's default value is IsAssignable to Never. If it has a default, you can construct a Param using the 'default' and 'keyword' qualifiers. You then wrap all these Params into a Callable and use UpdateClass to inject that __init__ method back into the class or a NewProtocol.

Hey. I am an engineer working on IDE completions and I have been dreading PEP 827. Adding if and for loops directly into type annotations feels like a massive grammar change for 3.15. Are we looking at a whole new set of tokens or a PEG rewrite? This is going to break our go-to-definition logic if I cannot parse the new syntax.

I can clear that up for you. According to the PEP 827 text, there are actually no changes to the Python grammar being proposed. The authors, Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov, specifically designed this to reuse existing Python expression syntax.

Wait, how can it not be a grammar change? I saw things like *[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]] in the abstract. That is not how types work today. If I have a type alias and I put a for-loop inside the brackets, the current parser is going to throw a fit, right?

Actually, Python's grammar already allows for various expressions inside subscript brackets. PEP 827 just broadens the annotation syntax that static type checkers are expected to support. For example, an unpacked comprehension like *[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]] is treated as a valid type expression, but it uses the existing grammar for unpacking and comprehensions. The hard part for your IDE will not be the parsing, but the semantic evaluation of these forms at the type level.

I see. So my parser stays the same, but my type-evaluator needs a promotion. What about the dot notation? I saw m.name and m.type used for Member access. Is the IDE supposed to treat those like normal attribute lookups on a class?

That is a great question. The PEP defines this as 'Type member access.' It is not a standard runtime attribute lookup on a class instance. Instead, the Member and Param types have 'associated' type members accessible via dot notation. For example, if you have a Member object m, m.name gives you the name as a literal string type, and m.type gives you the type of that member. Since this is not lifted over union types, using it on anything other than a Member or Param type will be an error. Your IDE would need to recognize these specific types to provide the right completions.

That makes sense. It sounds like the logic for go-to-definition is going to get complex when dealing with things like IsAssignable[T, S] or conditional types. If I have true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ, I have to figure out if bool_typ is Literal[True] just to know where to send the user when they click a type.

Exactly. The complexity moves from the parser to the type inference engine. Since PEP 827 is still a Draft targeting Python 3.15, you have some time, but you are correct that you will need to evaluate 'Type booleans' to resolve those conditionals. The PEP even suggests using a special_form_evaluator hook at runtime, which might be something your IDE tooling can leverage or mimic to stay in sync with how Python 3.15 will evaluate these annotations.

hey nanochat! i am working on some tensor libraries and getting shape mismatch errors at runtime is the bane of my existence. i saw some chatter about pep 827 having new tricks for this. what is the deal with it and why is it obsessed with literal types for keyword arguments?

hello! pep 827, authored by dr. michael j. sully sullivan, daniel w. park, and yury selivanov, is a massive proposal for type manipulation. for someone doing ml engineering, the most relevant part is how it enables type-level computation. the obsession with literal types in **kwargs comes down to one thing: you cannot do math or logic on the type int. if a function takes a keyword argument like axis=1 or dim=4, a standard type checker just sees int. pep 827 proposes that when you use its new unpack syntax for **kwargs, the type system should infer literal types whenever possible so that other type operators can actually inspect those values.
okay so if i have a function like select_dim(dim=0), the type checker actually knows it is the literal 0? how does that work in the signature?

exactly. the pep introduces a minor proposal to support unpack of typevars for **kwargs. you would write something like: def f[k: basetypeddict](**kwargs: unpack[k]) -> k. if you then call f(dim=0), the type inferred for k becomes something like typeddict({'dim': literal[0]}). this is a huge step up from previous peps because it bridges the gap between the values you pass at runtime and the logic the type checker can run during static analysis.
i see. but why go through all that trouble for a typeddict? how does that help me with something like numpy broadcasting where the dimensions have to match or be one?

that is where the literals become the engine for logic. since pep 827 adds conditional types like true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ and operators like isequivalent, you can write actual broadcasting rules. for example, the pep shows a mergeone[t, s] helper. if t is literal[1], it returns s. if s is literal[1], it returns t. if they are equivalent, it returns t. but if they are different literals like literal[4] and literal[3], it can trigger a raiseerror[literal['broadcast mismatch'], t, s]. without literal inference for those keyword arguments or shape dimensions, the type checker would just see int and int and assume everything is fine, even if a crash is coming.
wait, so i could actually get a static type error for a broadcast mismatch? that would save me hours. is this stuff usable yet?

it is currently a draft targeted for python 3.15. the authors have a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy and a runtime evaluator library in progress. it is definitely the most ambitious change to python typing since peps like 646. for your use case, it means you could eventually define a broadcast[t, s] type alias that recursively walks through your shape tuples and verifies every dimension using these literal-aware operators.
hey there nanochat! i saw something about pep 827 on twitter today and it mentioned a Param type. i am still pretty new to types in python, so could you explain what that is in plain english?

Hello! PEP 827 is a draft proposal for Python 3.15 that introduces new ways to manipulate types. The Param type is a new tool used to describe exactly what a single argument in a function looks like. Currently, Python's type system has some limits when trying to describe very complex functions. Param helps fix this by breaking down a function parameter into three specific pieces of information: its name, its data type, and special properties called qualifiers (like whether it is a keyword argument or has a default value). It is essentially a way for the type system to 'read' the details of a function's signature so it can do advanced calculations with them.

thanks! that makes sense. so if i have a function like def greet(name: str = 'friend'), how would that look using this Param thing? and what do those letters N, T, and Q i saw in the PEP actually stand for in the code?

In the technical spec, Param is defined as Param[N, T, Q]. Those letters are placeholders for the three pieces of information I mentioned: N stands for Name (as a literal string), T stands for Type (the actual data type), and Q stands for Qualifiers (special labels). For your greet(name: str = 'friend') example, the parameter would look like this: Param[Literal['name'], str, Literal['keyword', 'default']]. Because 'name' is the name, 'str' is the type, and it has a default value and can be used as a keyword, those are the qualifiers. The PEP also provides shorter nicknames, so you could just call it a NamedDefaultParam[Literal['name'], str] to save some typing.

Hi nanochat. I'm currently maintaining a complex mypy plugin for my validation framework that extracts metadata from Annotated types to generate runtime schemas. It's a maintenance nightmare. I've been reading PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. Does this proposal offer a way to get rid of my plugin by letting the type system handle Annotated metadata directly?

Hello! It sounds like you're exactly the kind of framework author the authors had in mind. PEP 827 is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, and while its core focus is on operators like IsAssignable and Members, it specifically addresses your needs in the "Potential Future Extensions" section. It proposes two operators: GetAnnotations[T] and DropAnnotations[T]. These are designed to allow the type system to 'see' and manipulate the metadata that is normally opaque to static checkers.

That's exactly what I need. If I have something like Annotated[int, "primary_key", 5], what would GetAnnotations actually return? Is it a tuple I can iterate over, or something else? The PEP mentioned something about Literal types.

According to the proposal, GetAnnotations[T] would fetch the annotations and return them as a Literal type. For your example of Annotated[int, "primary_key", 5], the evaluator would produce Literal["primary_key", 5]. If you call it on a type that isn't annotated, like GetAnnotations[int], it returns Never. This allows you to use the metadata in conditional types—for instance, checking if a specific string literal is present in the annotations before applying a transformation.

Okay, that makes sense. My plugin also does some heavy lifting with generating structural types. Could I use GetAnnotations inside a NewProtocol or NewTypedDict construction? For example, could I iterate over a class's members and only include those that have a specific 'Public' annotation?

Yes, that is a primary use case. You would combine it with the Members[T] operator and an unpacked comprehension. It would look something like this: NewProtocol[*[m for m in Iter[Members[T]] if IsAssignable[GetAnnotations[m.type], Literal["Public"]]]]. You use the dot notation (m.type) to access the type of each Member object, pass that to GetAnnotations, and then use IsAssignable to filter the comprehension. This would allow you to programmatically build a new structural type based on metadata annotations without any custom plugin logic.

yo. been diving into PEP 827. it looks like a half-step towards a proper kind system for python. coming from haskell, the way it handles type-level computation feels like it is trying to emulate row polymorphism or maybe some sort of record mapping. how does it actually implement things like typescript's Pick or Omit? is it just a catomorphism over a record type?

In PEP 827, the authors (Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov) achieve something similar to record mapping using 'unpacked comprehensions' over class members. Instead of a recursive catomorphism, you use a syntax that looks like a list comprehension but operates on types. For example, 'Pick' is implemented by iterating over the 'Members' of a class or TypedDict and filtering them based on a set of literal keys. The syntax uses 'NewProtocol' to construct a new structural type from those filtered members. Here is the implementation from the PEP: 

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

right, so you are using 'IsAssignable' as a predicate. in ocaml or haskell, i would expect a more rigid kind-level check. here, it seems like you are relying on the fact that 'p.name' is a Literal[str] and checking assignability against the 'Keys' union. what about sum types? if i have a union, how do i perform operations like 'Exclude' or 'Extract'? does it have a way to deconstruct the union into a type-level list?

It uses an operator called 'FromUnion[T]' to deconstruct a union into a tuple of its constituent elements. To implement 'Exclude<T, U>', you iterate over that tuple and use 'not IsAssignable[x, U]' to filter. The results are then re-packed into a union using the variadic 'Union' operator. Because many operators in PEP 827 are 'lifted' over unions, it behaves quite naturally. The PEP implementation for 'Exclude' looks like this:

type Exclude[T, U] = Union[
    *[
        x
        for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]
    ]
]
and what about accessing the labels themselves? 'KeyOf' is the classic example. can i just map over the record and extract the string literals?

Exactly. You use 'Members[T]' to get a tuple of 'Member' objects, and then access the '.name' attribute of each member using dot notation. That '.name' is a literal string type. For 'KeyOf[T]', the implementation is:

type KeyOf[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]

This dot-notation access is a core feature of the proposal. It allows you to grab '.name', '.type', '.quals', or even '.init' (the type of the attribute initializer) directly from the member object during the iteration.

it is interesting that they chose dot-notation for associated types. feels very object-oriented for a type system. what happens if i pass a junk type to one of these? is it strictly-kinded where the compiler catches the mismatch at the alias definition, or is it more like C++ templates where it only blows up on instantiation?

It is closer to the 'blow up on instantiation' (or rather, evaluation) model. The PEP explicitly mentions that it is less 'strictly-typed' or 'strictly-kinded' than TypeScript. If you try to evaluate an operator with invalid arguments, it generally results in 'Never' as the return type rather than a hard kind-error at the definition site. The authors did discuss making it more strict—for instance, requiring 'K' to be 'KeyOf[T]'—but they decided that would require too much additional machinery for this initial proposal. It targets Python 3.15 and is currently in Draft status.

I've been reviewing the draft for PEP 827 by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov. Honestly, the FastAPI example feels like a solution in search of a problem. We already have the 'HeroCreate', 'HeroUpdate', and 'HeroPublic' pattern. It's explicit. Why should we complicate the typing grammar with 'NewProtocol' and 'Iter' just to avoid writing a few boilerplate classes?

The core of the motivation in PEP 827 is the mismatch between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. While manual duplication is explicit, the authors argue it's fragile at scale. In the 'Automatically deriving FastAPI CRUD models' section, they point out that these transformations are mechanical: 'Create' usually includes all fields except the primary key, and 'Update' makes everything optional. Currently, if you use a runtime function to generate these models, your static type checker is left in the dark unless there's a custom plugin involved.

But we have PEP 681 for dataclass-like transformations. Isn't that enough for most of these CRUD use cases? Adding conditional types like 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' and 'IsAssignable' into the type language seems like a huge leap just for some boilerplate reduction.

Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov actually address PEP 681 specifically. They view it as a 'lowest-common denominator' stopgap. The problem with '@dataclass_transform' is that it's a hardcoded special case. If a library does something slightly different—like the Prisma-style query builder example in the PEP—PEP 681 can't describe it. This forces library authors to write mypy plugins, which don't work in IDEs like PyCharm or VS Code without extra effort. PEP 827 aims to provide the primitives, like 'Members[T]' and 'NewTypedDict', so the type system itself is expressive enough to handle these patterns without external plugins.

Okay, but looking at the spec for 'Create[T]', the implementation is dense. Using '*[typing.Member[...] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if ...]' is much harder to read than a standard class definition. How is this better for the average developer?

The PEP acknowledges that the syntax is verbose and primarily targeted at framework and library maintainers rather than end-users. The idea is that a FastAPI user would just write 'type HeroCreate = Create[Hero]'. The complexity is hidden inside the framework's implementation of the 'Create' alias. This allows for 'runtime evaluation of the type annotations' that stays in sync with static analysis. For example, using 'GetMemberType[ModelT, c.name]', the return type of a database select can be dynamically computed but still provide full IDE code completion, which is currently impossible with standard Python typing.

I see. So the 'Member' type carrying 'Init' and 'Quals' information allows us to introspect things like whether a field was defined with 'Field(primary_key=True)'. Is that handled via the 'InitField' mechanism?

Exactly. PEP 827 introduces 'InitField[KwargDict]' to capture those arguments. When you write 'foo: int = Field(primary_key=True)', the type system can infer 'InitField[TypedDict(..., {"primary_key": Literal[True]})]'. This is then accessible via 'p.init' on a 'Member' object. By using the 'IsAssignable' operator against 'p.init', the 'Create[T]' helper can programmatically filter out the primary key. It bridges the gap where we currently have to choose between 'static safety' and 'DRY' (Don't Repeat Yourself) code.

I am currently maintaining a complex mypy plugin for an RPC framework that generates method signatures at runtime. I saw PEP 827 and it looks like a godsend for moving this logic into the type system. I have been playing with the Param type to define function parameters, but I am getting some errors. Can you explain how it actually works? I thought I could just use it like `def foo(x: Param['val', int]): ...` but that seems wrong.

You are correct that using it directly in a function signature like that is not supported. PEP 827 introduces the Param type as part of the Extended Callables proposal, but it is meant for type-level construction and introspection, not as a replacement for standard Python parameter syntax. Its primary purpose is to be used inside a Params wrapper, which then sits inside a Callable. The goal is to let you programmatically build or deconstruct function types using type operators.

That makes sense. So it is more of a building block for the type system to look at. I am looking at the Param signature in the PEP. It seems to take three arguments: N, T, and Q. I assumed N was the name and T was the type. Is Q for the return value? I tried putting the return type there and mypy complained about a literal mismatch.

That is a common misconception. In the signature Param[N, T, Q], the Q argument actually stands for Qualifiers, not the return type. The return type of the function is still handled by the second argument of the outer Callable form. Q uses a special type called ParamQuals, which is a Literal union of strings that describe the behavior of the parameter, such as whether it is a keyword-only argument or a variadic pack.
Ah, okay. So if I wanted to represent a keyword-only argument named 'request' that is an int, how exactly would I structure that? And what are the valid values for that Q field?

For a keyword-only parameter named 'request' of type int, you would write: Param[Literal['request'], int, Literal['keyword']]. The valid values for Q, defined as ParamQuals, are '*', '**', 'default', and 'keyword'. Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the other authors also provided convenience abbreviations in the PEP. For example, you could use NamedParam[Literal['request'], int] instead of writing out the full Param form. If it had a default value, you would use NamedDefaultParam, which maps to Literal['keyword', 'default'] in the Q position.
Wait, what about positional arguments? I do not see 'positional' in your list for ParamQuals. And how does the system know if I'm doing a *args or **kwargs?

Actually, positional-only is represented by using Literal['positional']. For standard positional-or-keyword arguments (the default in Python), you actually leave the qualifier as Never or use a blank list, as seen in the PosParam abbreviation. To handle *args or **kwargs, you use the '*' or '**' literals respectively. Crucially, for variadic packs, the name (N) must be Literal[None]. For example, ArgsParam[int] expands to Param[Literal[None], int, Literal['*']]. All of these Param entries must be wrapped in a Params[...] block before being passed to a Callable to distinguish it from the standard, simpler Callable syntax.

I'm maintaining a Pydantic-style validation library and I've been following the Type Manipulation proposal (PEP 827). One of the things that caught my eye for reducing our reliance on mypy plugins is 'UpdateClass'. Can you explain what it actually does and where it can be used?

According to PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, UpdateClass is a special form designed to update an existing nominal class with new members. It allows you to add members, override existing ones, or even 'remove' them by setting their type to Never. Unlike standard generic aliases, it has a very specific set of allowed locations: it can only be used in the return type of a type decorator (like @dataclass_ish) or as the return type of an __init_subclass__ method.
That's exactly what I need for our model-to-schema transformations. How do I actually define what's being updated? Is there a specific syntax for the members I want to inject into the class?

Yes, UpdateClass takes a variadic number of Member types: UpdateClass[*Ps: Member]. Each Member object encodes several pieces of information: the name (as a Literal[str]), the type, qualifiers like 'ClassVar' or 'Final', and even the initializer type (InitField). For example, if you wanted to add a computed __init__ method, you would pass a Member where the name is Literal['__init__'] and the type is an extended Callable. This allows the type system to 'see' the transformation that previously required a hardcoded plugin.

The __init_subclass__ part is really interesting because that's how we handle inheritance in our library. If I have a base Model class that uses UpdateClass in its __init_subclass__, how does the type checker handle it when someone inherits from it? Does it respect the MRO?

It does. PEP 827 specifies that when a class is declared, if one or more ancestors have an __init_subclass__ with an UpdateClass return type, those updates are applied in reverse MRO order. Additionally, if the 'cls' parameter in __init_subclass__ is parameterized by type[T], the actual class type is substituted for T during the evaluation. This ensures that the computed members are correctly bound to the specific subclass being defined.
Are there any hidden pitfalls with using it this way? I'm worried about complex inheritance hierarchies where classes might depend on each other's types.

The PEP does flag a specific concern: evaluation-order dependence. If an UpdateClass return type inspects the Members of an unrelated class that also has its own __init_subclass__ update, the final result might depend on the order in which the type checker (or runtime evaluator) processes them. This effectively mirrors the potential runtime complexity of Python's dynamic class construction. Also, remember that PEP 827 is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so while it solves the 'plugin problem,' it isn't available in production environments yet.

Hi nanochat, I have been reading through the PEP 827 draft (Type Manipulation) and it is pretty wild. I was looking at the UpdateClass operator. How does it fit in with things like PEP 681 dataclass_transform or the Unpack stuff from PEP 692?

Hello! I can certainly help clarify that. PEP 827's UpdateClass is essentially a more powerful, programmatic way to achieve what PEP 681 (dataclass_transform) tried to standardize. While PEP 681 provides a hint to type checkers that a class behaves like a dataclass, it often requires custom plugins for complex logic. 

UpdateClass[*Ps: Member] is a special form that actually modifies a nominal class's members (attributes or methods). It can override existing ones or even 'remove' them by setting their type to Never. Regarding PEP 692, PEP 827 actually builds on that by proposing that we support Unpack of typevars for **kwargs. This allows you to infer a TypedDict from keyword arguments and then use that information within type-level computations to drive what UpdateClass eventually produces.

That makes sense. So instead of a hardcoded decorator, I am basically writing a function that returns a modified version of the class? Where exactly can I use UpdateClass? I am curious if I can just use it in a standard type alias like I might with PEP 695 generics.

Actually, UpdateClass is more restricted than a standard type alias. According to the PEP, it can only be used in two specific places: as the return type of a type decorator or as the return type of an __init_subclass__ method. 

For example, if you wanted to implement a custom dataclass-like decorator that generates an __init__ method based on class attributes, you would define your decorator to return UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]. If you try to use it for local variables or general return types, it would be an error. It is designed specifically for the 'class-shaping' phase of metaprogramming.

Got it. I also noticed some mentions of PEP 646 and PEP 764 in the 'Rejected' or 'Prerequisites' sections. How does it handle those? I am trying to see if this replaces the need for some of the variadic stuff.

It actually complements PEP 646 (Variadic Generics) quite heavily. PEP 827 uses the TypeVarTuple logic from 646 to handle shapes in things like NumPy-style broadcasting examples. It introduces 'unpacked comprehensions' like *[p.name for p in Iter[Members[T]]] which look a lot like the unpacking syntax introduced in 646 but applied to type-level loops.

As for PEP 764 (inline TypedDicts), the authors mentioned it as a potential inspiration for a more concise syntax for creating protocols or dictionaries, though they currently stick to using NewProtocol and NewTypedDict operators for clarity. It is definitely aiming to be the 'connective tissue' that lets you manipulate all these newer structures programmatically.

One last thing, since I saw Yury Selivanov and Michael Sullivan are on this. Is there a runtime cost? PEP 649 was all about when annotations get evaluated. Does this make things slower at runtime if I have huge loops in my type signatures?

The PEP authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) are very aware of runtime evaluation. One of the core goals of PEP 827 is to support tools like Pydantic or FastAPI that rely on runtime introspection. 

To manage performance and complexity, they propose a special_form_evaluator ContextVar. This allows third-party libraries to provide their own optimized evaluators. The PEP also notes that this proposal works best with something like the 'Format.AST' mode (from the PEP 9999/AST format drafts) because it allows tools to fetch the unevaluated structures without executing a full Python loop every time someone looks at __annotations__.

Hi! I am the tech lead for a platform team and I am evaluating PEP 827 for our next infrastructure migration. I heard this landed in the Python 3.14 alpha and I am trying to find the official discussion thread to see the status of the new ampersand intersection operator. Do you have that link?

Hello! It is wise to look ahead at these features, but I should clarify a few details from the PEP text to ensure your evaluation is accurate. First, PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) is currently a Draft and is targeting Python 3.15, not 3.14. Regarding the intersection operator, while the authors—Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—acknowledge that the 2025 Typed Python Survey showed high demand for an intersection operator (the & symbol), this specific PEP focuses on building the underlying type manipulation machinery like conditional types and the Members operator rather than defining a standalone & operator. You can find the official discussion thread here: https://discuss.python.org/t/pep-827-type-manipulation/106353

Thanks for the correction. I must have misread the version. I also saw some examples using 'if' and 'else' logic directly in the type aliases. My team is worried about the performance impact of changing the Python grammar just for annotations. How are they handling the parser changes?

That is a common misconception! PEP 827 actually proposes no changes to the Python grammar. The syntax for conditional types, such as 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ', utilizes the existing ternary expression syntax that Python's parser already understands. The PEP simply broadens the 'grammar' of what expressions are considered valid within a type annotation context for static type checkers and runtime evaluators. It introduces hooks like 'special_form_evaluator' to allow libraries to process these expressions at runtime without requiring a new parser.
That makes more sense. So if we wanted to implement something like TypeScript's Pick utility for our internal API models, does the PEP provide a way to do that using these new operators?

Yes, it does. In the 'TypeScript-style Utility Types' section of the PEP, the authors demonstrate how to implement Pick[T, Keys] using the NewProtocol operator combined with an unpacked comprehension. The implementation looks like this: 'type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]'. This uses 'Members[T]' to get the attributes of a class and 'IsAssignable' to check the member's name against the provided keys, all within a type-level loop.

helo nanochat. I have been reading through PEP 827 by Dr. Michael Sullivan, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov. It feels like a massive leap toward TypeScript. I am curious how it handles those classic utility types like Pick, Omit, and Partial. We already have PEP 681 for dataclass transforms and PEP 646 for variadic generics, so why do we need this more complex machinery?

Hello! You are right that PEP 827 is heavily inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types. While PEP 681 (dataclass_transform) solved a specific problem for library authors, it was essentially a hard-coded hack for type checkers. PEP 827 provides a generic framework so you don't need a custom plugin for every library. 

For example, Pick[T, Keys] in PEP 827 uses the NewProtocol operator and an unpacked comprehension. It looks like this:

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

This uses Members[T] to introspect the type and IsAssignable to filter the property names. It's much more flexible than the limited subset PEP 681 supports.

That syntax is wild. The Iter and Members operators feel very different from PEP 695's clean generics. How does this work for Unions? In TS, Exclude and Extract are huge. If I have a Union, can PEP 827 actually deconstruct it? I'm comparing this to PEP 764's draft for inline TypedDicts and it seems way more verbose.

It is definitely more verbose, but that's because it's designed to be a programmable engine for types rather than just a shorthand. For Union processing, PEP 827 introduces FromUnion[T], which turns a Union into a tuple of its members. 

Here is how you would implement Exclude[T, U] to remove union members assignable to U:

type Exclude[T, U] = Union[
    *[
        x
        for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]
    ]
]

Regarding the comparison to PEP 764 (Inline TypedDicts), the authors actually discussed using dictionary-style syntax for NewProtocol as a "Rejected Idea." They ultimately stuck with brackets to maintain consistency with existing Python typing, even though it results in what they call 'Bracket City'.

Bracket City indeed. What about Partial? In Python, we usually just use Optional or type | None, but TypedDicts have that whole NotRequired thing from PEP 692. Does PEP 827 handle the difference between a nullable field and a missing key?

Yes, it distinguishes between them using MemberQuals. A standard Partial[T] would just make the types nullable, but for TypedDicts, you can use PartialTD to apply the NotRequired qualifier:

type PartialTD[T] = typing.NewTypedDict[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals | Literal["NotRequired"]]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

This iterates over Attrs[T] (which are attributes, excluding methods) and pipes the existing qualifiers into a union with Literal["NotRequired"]. It's a key reason why the PEP introduces the Member and Param objects with dot-notation access like p.name and p.type.

I see. And the runtime side? If I'm a library author using Pydantic or FastAPI, I need to know if this actually evaluates when I call __annotations__. PEP 649 (deferred evaluation) and PEP 646 were supposed to help with runtime introspection. Does PEP 827 play nice with those?

The authors (Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov) specifically prioritized runtime evaluation because frameworks like FastAPI depend on it. They propose a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar hook. When a runtime library calls __bool__ on a boolean operator (like IsAssignable) or __iter__ on typing.Iter, this hook allows a third-party evaluator to resolve the logic. 

They also mention that PEP 649's Format.AST would be a perfect companion, as it would allow tools to fetch the unevaluated type manipulation logic (the 'if/else' and 'for' loops) directly as an AST before processing it. Without that, evaluating these types at runtime would requires the hooks to trigger the evaluation when __annotate__ is called.

hi! been reading up on pep 827. coming from a functional background with ocaml and haskell, i am curious how this stacks up against typescript's conditional types. is python finally getting proper type-level computation or is it more of a limited utility set?

Hello! PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation, is a significant shift for Python's type system. The authors—Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—explicitly mention TypeScript as a primary inspiration. It introduces type-level operators and a conditional syntax: `true_type if bool_type else false_type`. While it might not feel as naturally higher-order as OCaml, it allows for computing new types based on the properties of others. It is currently a Draft PEP targeted for Python 3.15.

the syntax looks very pythonic. in ts, we use the 'extends' keyword which allows for pattern matching via 'infer'. does pep 827 support anything like that, or am i stuck with predefined operators for checking assignability?

The PEP actually addresses this in the Rejected Ideas section. The authors decided against a TypeScript-style 'infer' keyword because the binding structure is difficult to fit into Python's grammar without major changes. Instead, you use boolean operators like `IsAssignable[T, S]` to branch, and deconstruction operators like `GetArg[T, Base, Idx]` to extract specific type parameters. For example, `GetArg[list[int], list, Literal[0]]` would evaluate to `int`. It is more manual than pattern matching but serves a similar purpose.

interesting. how does it handle mapped types? in haskell i might use a type family, and in ts i can map over keys of an interface. can i transform a record—or a TypedDict—by iterating over its fields?

Yes, PEP 827 handles that via 'unpacked comprehensions'. You can write expressions like `*[f(m) for m in Iter[Members[T]]]` inside a `NewProtocol` or `NewTypedDict`. The `Members[T]` operator produces a tuple of `Member` types, and you can access metadata like `m.name`, `m.type`, or `m.quals` using dot notation. This allows you to implement things like `Pick[T, Keys]` or `Partial[T]` by iterating and filtering with an `if` clause inside the comprehension. It's essentially a type-level list comprehension for constructing structural types.

I've been looking at the draft for PEP 827. Specifically, I'm skeptical about IsAssignable. We already have structural typing and nominal subtyping rules. Why do we need a new primitive that returns a Literal boolean? Is it just a rehash of what type checkers already do internally?

In PEP 827, IsAssignable[T, S] is designed as a core engine for type-level computation. While type checkers do perform subtyping logic internally, Python currently has no way to branch based on that logic within the type system itself. This operator returns Literal[True] if T is a consistent subtype of S and Literal[False] otherwise. This allows for conditional types like 'X if IsAssignable[T, int] else Y', which lets us model complex transformations like those found in Prisma-style ORMs or FastAPI's CRUD models without custom plugins.

The 'consistent subtype' part worries me. Does it follow the gradual typing rules (handling Any) or is it a strict subtyping check? And how does it handle Unions? If I pass a Union to T, does it fail or narrow?

It specifically follows the consistency relation defined in the typing specification, meaning it treats Any according to gradual typing rules. Regarding Unions, PEP 827 specifies that IsAssignable is 'lifted' over Union types. If T or S are Unions, the operator evaluates the cross product of the union elements. For example, IsAssignable[int | str, int] would evaluate both branches. The PEP's logic for lifting is: results = [eval_operator(*xs) for xs in itertools.product(*args_union_els)]. If any results exist, they are Unioned together.

That cross product sounds like it could explode in complexity for nested types. Also, if I'm checking IsAssignable[Any, int], is that Literal[True]? Because technically Any is consistent with int. That seems like it could lead to very permissive and potentially buggy type-level branching.

You are correct that because it follows consistency, IsAssignable[Any, int] would be Literal[True]. The authors, Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, acknowledge that this mirrors the existing behavior of Python's gradual type system. To handle cases where you want stricter checks, you might combine it with other operators. However, the primary goal is to bridge the gap for frameworks that already use dynamic metaprogramming. If the evaluation gets 'stuck' (for example, on an unresolved TypeVar), the current reference implementation in mypy falls back to invariant checking to avoid unsoundness.

I suppose if it is limited to the typing module and only evaluated during type checking or via the proposed __annotate__ hook, it might be manageable. It's certainly better than the current state of @dataclass_transform where every tool has to hardcode support. I'll need to look closer at the 'Bool[T]' helper mentioned in the spec too.

Yes, Bool[T] is another primitive defined in the PEP. It returns Literal[True] if T is assignable to Literal[True] and not Never. This is useful for wrapping 'helper aliases' that perform their own logic and return a boolean literal. The proposal also includes a special_form_evaluator ContextVar to allow third-party libraries to hook into these evaluations at runtime, which addresses the concerns about Pydantic and FastAPI needing to use these computed types for actual validation.
Hi! I have been getting into generics and TypeVars lately. Someone mentioned PEP 827 'Type Manipulation' to me—is that basically just more advanced TypeVars or something different?

It is quite a bit more powerful than standard TypeVars! While PEP 695 gave us cleaner syntax for generics, PEP 827 (authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) proposes actual type-level computation. Think of it as moving from just 'labeling' types to 'calculating' them.

In standard Python typing, a TypeVar is mostly a placeholder. In PEP 827, 'Type Manipulation' means you can use logic like conditionals and loops to construct new types based on existing ones. It's heavily inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types. For example, you could write a type that automatically changes its structure based on whether an input is a string or an integer, or a type that programmatically strips fields from a class to create a 'Public' version of a database model. It is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15.
Wait, you can use 'if' and 'else' inside a type hint? That sounds wild. How does the type checker know which branch to take? Does it check the actual variable values at runtime?

Not exactly variable values—it checks the 'types' themselves. The PEP introduces 'Type booleans' and conditional syntax that look like this: `TrueType if BooleanExpression else FalseType`. 

The magic happens with operators like `IsAssignable[T, S]`. The type checker evaluates this at static-analysis time. For instance, you could define a helper like this:

`type Convert[T] = int if typing.IsAssignable[T, str] else list[T]`

If you use `Convert[str]`, the type checker evaluates the `IsAssignable` check, sees it is true, and treats the result as `int`. If you pass `Convert[float]`, it resolves to `list[float]`. 

It even allows for 'unpacked comprehensions' using a new `Iter` primitive, like `*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]`. This lets you loop over the attributes of a class (its `Members`) to build a new `TypedDict` or `Protocol` on the fly. It is basically a way to do the kind of complex metaprogramming usually reserved for internal CPython logic or messy mypy plugins, but directly in your Python code.

Hey nanochat, I am a tech lead evaluating the Draft for PEP 827. I am trying to understand the prerequisites for my team. What exactly is BaseTypedDict and why is it being introduced as a new primitive instead of just using standard TypedDict classes?

In PEP 827, Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov introduce BaseTypedDict as a specific tool for type-level introspection. It is defined simply as: `class BaseTypedDict(typing.TypedDict): pass`. Its primary role is to serve as a bound for type variables when using the Unpack operator with **kwargs. By defining a function like `def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K:`, you enable the type checker to infer a specific TypedDict based on the literal arguments passed at the call site. This is a prerequisite for more advanced type manipulation because it allows the system to 'capture' the structure of keyword arguments so they can be processed by other operators like Members or NewProtocol later on.

Got it, so it's a capture mechanism. I'm a bit concerned about the inference logic though. The PEP mentions that for non-provided arguments, the type might be inferred as 'Never'. How does that work in practice? I don't want my devs seeing confusing 'Never' errors if they omit an optional key.

The PEP handles this by inferring 'Never' only for read-only items that are missing from the provided arguments. This effectively marks the item as 'not provided' within the synthesized TypedDict. This inference behavior is intentional to support type-level computation; if an item is not read-only, it is treated as invariant and won't be inferred as Never. Additionally, the type checker will attempt to infer Literal types whenever possible for these arguments. The goal isn't to create errors for your team, but to allow library authors to write logic like `true_typ if IsAssignable[T, Literal[True]] else false_typ` to programmatically decide return types based on whether a specific keyword was passed or not.

Hi there. I am a tech lead evaluating some of the upcoming typing features. I have been reading the draft for PEP 827, Type Manipulation. It looks powerful for our ORM and validation logic, but I noticed a limitation. Can I use it to generate brand new nominal classes or generic types at the type level?

Hello! It is wise to look ahead to Python 3.15. To answer your question directly: no, PEP 827 does not currently support the creation of new nominal classes or brand-new generic types. According to the specification, the creation of types is limited to 'NewProtocol' and 'NewTypedDict'.

That seems like a significant constraint. If I use NewProtocol, isn't that a new type? I am trying to understand the boundary here. Why can I make a Protocol but not a regular class? We use a lot of 'isinstance' checks in our legacy code.

The distinction lies in structural versus nominal typing. 'NewProtocol[*Ms]' creates a structural type. It is essentially a set of requirements for members (attributes and methods). Any object that has those members satisfies the protocol. However, it does not have a unique identity in Python's Method Resolution Order (MRO). You cannot use 'isinstance' on it at runtime unless it is a runtime-checkable protocol, and even then, it checks for structure, not the specific 'name' of the class. The authors, including Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and Yury Selivanov, opted to avoid nominal class creation because the proposal focuses on manipulating existing type information rather than inventing new runtime class hierarchies.

Okay, that makes sense for the class side. But what about the generic types? I saw an implementation of 'Pick[T, Keys]' in the PEP examples. If I can write 'type Pick[T, Keys] = ...', isn't that a generic type?

That is a generic type alias, which uses standard Python 3.12+ (PEP 695) syntax. The 'generic' limitation in PEP 827 refers to the fact that you cannot use the type operators to *programmatically* construct a new generic type constructor. For example, you cannot create a type manipulation that results in a 'MyGeneric[X]' where 'X' is a brand new type variable generated by the logic. The PEP states that topics like impredicative polymorphism and rank-N types are 'cans of worms' that the authors chose not to open to ensure the system remains decidable and manageable for type checkers.
I noticed 'UpdateClass' in the spec though. Does that let me modify nominal classes at least? If I can't build one from scratch, can I at least change one that exists?

Yes, 'UpdateClass[*Ps: Member]' is the exception. It allows a decorator or '__init_subclass__' to return a specialized form that updates a nominal class with new or overridden members. For example, you could use it to add a computed '__init__' method based on the class's attributes. You are still working with a class defined in the source code, but you are 'patching' its type signature. It is a targeted way to support patterns like dataclasses or Pydantic models without needing custom type-checker plugins.

I see. So for a team like mine, we should think of PEP 827 as a way to transform and map existing types into structural interfaces, rather than a way to build a completely dynamic nominal hierarchy. That is a helpful distinction for our architecture planning. Thanks for clarifying.

Exactly. It bridges the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system by allowing you to describe those transformations (like 'Pick', 'Omit', or 'Partial') using the 'if/else' and 'for/in Iter[...]' syntax. While it stops short of letting you invent new nominal identities, it provides the tools to make those identities much more expressive. If you decide to experiment, keep in mind it is currently a Draft targeting 3.15.

Hi nanochat. I've been looking over the PEP 827 draft by Sully, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov. I maintain a small validation library similar to Pydantic, and I'm really curious about the runtime implementation of these new type-level comprehensions. Specifically, if I have a type like `*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]`, what actually happens at runtime when `iter()` is called on that `typing.Iter` object?

That is a great question. In PEP 827, most of these type manipulation constructs are designed to be 'inert' by default so they don't break existing tools, but they do include specific hooks for runtime evaluation. When you call `iter()` on a `typing.Iter` object at runtime, it triggers the `__iter__` method on the underlying `typing._GenericAlias` object. By default, if you haven't configured anything special, it simply returns an iterator for an empty tuple, effectively `iter(())`.

An empty tuple? That wouldn't be very useful for my validation logic. I need to actually see the attributes of `T` to generate my schemas. The PEP mentions something about a `special_form_evaluator` hook. How does a library maintainer like me actually use that to make `typing.Iter` return real data during an inspection pass?

Exactly. To make it functional, you use a new hook introduced in the `typing` module called `special_form_evaluator`. This is a `ContextVar` that holds a callable. When you are performing your runtime type evaluation, you set this `ContextVar` to your own evaluator function. When `__iter__` is called on `typing.Iter` (or when `__bool__` is called on a Boolean Operator like `IsAssignable`), Python looks at this `ContextVar`. It calls your evaluator, passing in the `typing._GenericAlias` as an argument. Your evaluator then returns a value that Python calls `iter()` on. This allows your library to 'inject' the actual list of members or attributes into the comprehension at runtime.

So it's context-local? That's clever. So if I'm walking a class's `__annotate__` method, I'd wrap that call in a context where my evaluator is active. What if I don't want to write a full type-checker? Does the PEP provide a standard evaluator?

The PEP authors (Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov) explicitly state they do not propose adding an official evaluator to the Python standard library. However, they intend to release a third-party evaluator library alongside the implementation. For a framework like yours, you would likely use that library or implement a 'best-effort' version. It's worth noting that if the `special_form_evaluator` is set to `None` (the default), boolean operators return `False` and `Iter` returns that empty iterator we discussed. This ensures that the code doesn't crash if someone accesses annotations in a standard environment, even if the results aren't 'correct' for the logic.

I am reviewing the implementation details for the Prisma-style ORM use case in PEP 827. Can you explain the precise mechanism for how the select method's return type is derived from the keyword arguments? I am specifically interested in how the draft uses Unpack of typevars for **kwargs in conjunction with the NewProtocol operator.

The PEP 827 approach for Prisma-style ORMs relies on two primary mechanisms to achieve dynamic return types. First, it uses a prerequisite feature: 'Unpack of typevars for **kwargs'. In a signature like 'def select[ModelT, K: BaseTypedDict](typ: type[ModelT], **kwargs: Unpack[K])', the type checker infers a TypedDict 'K' that contains the literal keys provided at the call site. For instance, 'db.select(User, name=True)' results in 'K' being inferred as 'TypedDict({'name': Literal[True]})'. Second, the return type uses 'NewProtocol' to construct a structural type on the fly. This is defined as: 'NewProtocol[*[Member[c.name, ConvertField[GetMemberType[ModelT, c.name]]] for c in Iter[Attrs[K]]]]'. Here is the breakdown: 1. 'Attrs[K]' extracts the attributes of the inferred selection TypedDict. 2. 'Iter' allows the unpacked comprehension to loop over these attributes. 3. 'GetMemberType[ModelT, c.name]' looks up the actual type of that attribute (e.g., 'Property[str]') from the original 'User' model. 4. 'ConvertField' is a conditional type alias that strips the 'Property' or 'Link' wrapper. This results in a new, anonymous structural protocol containing only the fields requested in the kwargs, with their original types correctly resolved.

Hi! I am a student focusing on PL design. I have been reading up on PEP 827 and noticed a section on 'Extended Callables'. Why does Python need a new way to represent function signatures if we already have Callable and ParamSpec?

Welcome! That is a great question from a design perspective. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, introduces Extended Callables because existing tools like Callable and ParamSpec are essentially 'black boxes' for type-level computation. While ParamSpec allows for forwarding arguments, it does not allow you to easily reach inside to add, remove, or modify a single parameter. The Extended Callable format treats a function signature as a sequence of discrete objects. It introduces a 'Param' type: 'Param[N, T, Q]', where N is the name, T is the type, and Q represents qualifiers like 'default' or 'keyword'. These are then wrapped in a 'Params' container. This allows the type system to treat a function's arguments like a tuple that can be iterated over or transformed using the new type-level operators proposed in the PEP.

Interesting. So it is essentially turning a signature into a data structure for the type checker? How does it handle the complexities of Python's argument system, like keyword-only arguments or varargs?

Exactly. It models the signature as a sequence. To handle Python's specific semantics, the PEP defines 'ParamQuals' which can include literal strings like '*', '**', 'default', and 'keyword'. For example, a keyword-only parameter with a default value would be represented as 'NamedDefaultParam[Literal['x'], int]', which is an alias for 'Param[Literal['x'], int, Literal['keyword', 'default']]'. Variadic arguments use 'ArgsParam[T]' for *args and 'KwargsParam[T]' for **kwargs. This granularity is crucial for the type-level operators. If you want to create a decorator that adds a specific keyword argument to a function, you can now construct a new 'Params' list by prepending a 'Param' object to the existing sequence of parameters extracted from a function.

I am curious about the inference tradeoffs. If we can programmatically construct these callables, does it get into undecidable territory like Rank-N types? I saw 'GenericCallable' mentioned in the PEP and wondered if that opens a can of worms.

You hit on a major design concern discussed in the Rationale. The authors acknowledge that Rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism can lead to undecidability. To mitigate this, PEP 827 restricts 'GenericCallable' primarily to be used inside the 'Member' type for class members. A 'GenericCallable' uses a lambda to delay evaluation: 'GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty]'. Here, 'Vs' is a tuple of type variables. By wrapping the type in a lambda, the system avoids 'stuck' evaluations during runtime or static analysis. The PEP targets Python 3.15 and is currently in Draft status, focusing on enabling framework-level metaprogramming (like generating an __init__ for a dataclass) without making the entire type system undecidable.

Hey! I am a PL design student currently obsessed with type theory, and I just found PEP 827. This looks like a massive shift for Python's type system! What is the current status of this proposal, and which version is it targeting? I'm hoping it's soon!

It is definitely one of the more ambitious typing proposals in recent memory! To answer your first question: PEP 827 is currently in Draft status. It was created in February 2026 and is officially targeting Python 3.15. The authors behind it are Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. It's still in the early stages, so while the ideas are exciting, we have a bit of a wait before it becomes a standard part of the language.

Python 3.15, okay! I'll mark my calendar. As a student, I'm really curious about the inference tradeoffs here. Adding things like IsAssignable[T, S] and conditional types (T if bool_typ else S) feels like it could make the type-checking process significantly more complex or even undecidable. Did the authors address that?

They actually did! The authors acknowledge that things like impredicative polymorphism and rank-N types are 'cans of worms' when it comes to undecidability. To mitigate this, they've initially restricted the use of the new GenericCallable operator specifically to the type argument of Member. This prevents those generic types from appearing in deep, nested positions like local variables or return types where inference becomes a nightmare. They also mention that for 'stuck' type evaluations (where a type variable isn't resolved yet), the current proof-of-concept in mypy implements subtype checking fully invariantly to keep things predictable.

That is a very pragmatic way to handle it. I also noticed they are proposing 'unpacked comprehensions' like *[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]]. That feels like such a cool way to bridge term-level logic and type-level construction. Does that require a total overhaul of the Python grammar?

Surprisingly, no! The proposal is specifically designed to avoid changes to the Python grammar itself. Instead, it broadens the annotation syntax that static type checkers are expected to understand. It leverages existing Python expressions—like if/else and for-in loops—but gives them new semantics within the context of a type annotation. It's a clever way to add a 'type-level language' without breaking the core parser.

Hey nanochat! I have been wrestling with a mypy plugin for my custom ORM for months and it is a total nightmare to maintain. I just saw PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) and it looks like it might solve my problems with things like Pick and Attrs. But here is the deal: my framework does heavy validation at runtime. Does this PEP actually support runtime evaluation, or is it just another static-only thing?

I totally hear you on the plugin maintenance struggle. Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov specifically mention in the PEP that one of their core goals is to bridge the gap between static type checking and runtime frameworks. They explicitly state that PEP 827 machinery is intended to benefit both static type checkers and libraries that rely on runtime introspection, like Pydantic or FastAPI.

That is good to hear, but how does it actually work? If I have a conditional type like 'T if IsAssignable[T, str] else int', does that just sit there as an unevaluated object when I look at __annotations__?

It is pretty clever. The PEP introduces a hook called 'special_form_evaluator', which is a ContextVar in the typing module. This hook holds a callable that gets triggered when someone calls __bool__ on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or __iter__ on a typing.Iter. This allows an evaluator library to intercept the evaluation of the 'if' or 'for' logic inside your annotations and resolve it to a concrete type value.
Wait, so I have to write my own evaluator?

Not necessarily! While the PEP does not propose putting a full evaluator into the Python standard library immediately, the authors (Sully, Park, and Selivanov) plan to release a third-party evaluator library alongside the implementation. This library would handle resolving the logic like 'Attrs[T]' or 'IsAssignable[T, S]' at runtime so you don't have to roll your own logic for the entire type system.

Okay, that is a huge relief. I was worried I would be trading a mypy plugin for a custom runtime parser. If I can use things like 'NewProtocol' and 'Iter' to generate my CRUD models on the fly and have them actually mean something at runtime, I can finally delete half my codebase. Is this landing soon?
