The PEP restricts this to Member specifically to support defining methods in NewProtocol without breaking the existing inference model for the rest of the language.
What about that lambda syntax in GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty]? It looks like you're trying to mirror a runtime behavior. Does that relate to the implementation difficulty for CPython?

Exactly. From a CPython implementation perspective, PEP 827 is heavily focused on runtime evaluation support. Because Python evaluators (like Pydantic or the third-party library the authors plan to release) often call __annotate__, they need a way to see the generic structure without it collapsing immediately. By wrapping the body in a lambda, evaluation is delayed. Without this, if you evaluated a Member that used a conditional type based on a generic variable, you would 'lose one side of the conditional' during the runtime pass. The lambda ensures the logic remains intact for both static checkers and runtime introspection frameworks.

Hey nanochat. I have been hearing rumors about PEP 827 and it sounds like a nightmare. It feels like we are just copying TypeScript because people are bored with simple Python. Where can I actually read the discussion thread for this? I want to see if anyone is calling out how complex this is getting.

You can find the official discussion for PEP 827 on the Python Discourse forum at: https://discuss.python.org/t/pep-827-type-manipulation/106353. It was authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. The PEP acknowledges that it draws heavy inspiration from TypeScript, particularly its conditional and mapped types, to solve a specific problem: bridging the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system.

Copying TypeScript, exactly. Now we have things like 'Conditional types' in Python? Why on earth do I need an 'if' statement inside a type hint? That just makes the code unreadable for anyone who isn't a typing wizard.

The motivation comes from libraries like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs that currently require custom mypy plugins or complex decorators like @dataclass_transform to work. For example, PEP 827 introduces syntax like:

type ConvertField[T] = (
    list[T] if typing.IsAssignable[T, MultiLink]
    else T
)

This uses a 'type-bool' (like IsAssignable) to decide the type at check-time. While it adds complexity to the type system, the goal is to reduce boilerplate for the average user. Instead of writing five different classes for a single database model (Create, Update, Public, etc.), a framework author could use these tools to generate them automatically.
It still looks like a second programming language hidden inside brackets. And I saw something about loops too? Are we seriously putting list comprehensions inside type annotations now?

Yes, those are called 'unpacked comprehensions'. The syntax looks like *[... for t in Iter[...]]. For example, if you wanted to create a version of a class that only includes its attributes and not its methods, you might use Attrs[T] and iterate over them:

type PropsOnly[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.type, Property]
    ]
]

This uses typing.Iter to loop over the members of a class. It is definitely a leap in complexity, but the PEP authors argue this is better than having every library maintain its own fragile compiler plugin. It is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so the community is still debating whether this level of expressiveness is the right direction for Python.

yo! i saw some people on twitter arguing about something called pep 827 and 'type manipulation'. i'm just starting out with python type hints like list[str], so is this going to make python really hard to learn?

It is definitely a step up in complexity, but it is aimed more at library authors than beginners. PEP 827, written by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, proposes a way to 'calculate' types using logic. Right now, type hints are mostly like labels. With this PEP, you could write actual logic—like 'if-else' statements—inside your type hints to describe more complex code behavior. It is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15.
logic inside a type hint? that sounds wild. why would we need that instead of just using Union or something?

Unions work when you have a fixed list of options, but they aren't great for 'metaprogramming' where the type depends on specific inputs. The authors use Prisma-style ORMs as an example. If you tell a database tool to 'select' only the 'name' and 'email' columns, the tool needs to return an object that has exactly those two fields. Today, that is hard to show in the type system without custom plugins. PEP 827 introduces tools like Members[T] to look inside a class and NewProtocol to build a new type on the fly based on what the user requested.
okay, i think i get the 'why'. but is the code going to look like a different language? can you show me a simple example of this logic?

It uses familiar Python keywords like 'if', 'else', and 'for', but inside brackets. One of the core pieces is called a 'Conditional Type'. It looks like this: 

type MyType[T] = int if typing.IsAssignable[T, str] else list[int]

In plain English, this says: 'If the type T can be assigned to a string, then MyType is an integer. Otherwise, it is a list of integers.' It also introduces 'Type Operators' like IsAssignable and Length to help make these decisions. While it looks complex, the goal is to make those powerful libraries you use every day—like FastAPI or Pydantic—much easier to type-check without needing extra plugins.
that 'IsAssignable' thing seems useful. you mentioned loops too? how does that work if we aren't running real python code?

The PEP calls these 'Unpacked comprehensions'. They allow you to loop over the parts of a type. For example, if you have a class and want to see all its attributes, you would use typing.Attrs[T]. To loop through them, you use a special Iter[ ] wrapper. It looks like this: 

*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]

This would let a library like FastAPI automatically create a 'Create' or 'Update' model by looping through your database fields and deciding which ones should be optional. It is very similar to a list comprehension, just happening at the 'type level' for static checkers and runtime evaluators.

Hey nanochat, I'm an ML engineer and I've been following the discussion on PEP 827. I do a lot of work with tensor libraries and I saw this proposal mentions 'type member access' with dot notation, like m.name or m.type. How does that actually work? Is it for inspecting class attributes at the type level?

Precisely. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, introduces a way to treat types as objects you can introspect during type-level computation. 

Specifically, when you use the 'Members[T]' or 'Attrs[T]' operators on a class, you get a tuple of 'Member' types. Each 'Member' isn't just a string; it is a special type that has associated type members you can access using standard dot notation. A 'Member' type has these attributes available:
- .name: The literal string name of the member.
- .type: The actual type of the member.
- .quals: Any qualifiers like 'Final' or 'ClassVar'.
- .init: The type of the attribute initializer (useful for things like Field() in Pydantic or dataclasses).
- .definer: The class where the member was originally defined.

There is also a 'Param' type for function arguments that supports '.name', '.type', and '.quals' in a similar way. This allows you to write logic that iterates over a class and picks out specific fields based on their metadata.

That sounds powerful for generating dynamic protocols. So if I have a 'Tensor' class with a 'shape' attribute, I can use typing.Members[Tensor] and then check 'p.name' inside a comprehension? Could you show me what that looks like in a type alias? I'm curious if it's basically just like writing a Python list comprehension but inside a type definition.

It is very similar to a Python comprehension. You use the new 'unpacked comprehension' syntax. For example, if you wanted to create a union of all the attribute names in a class (like TypeScript's 'keyof'), you would write it like this:

type KeyOf[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]

In this snippet, 'p' represents a 'Member' type. The expression 'p.name' accesses that member's name as a 'Literal[str]'. 

A few things to keep in mind from the PEP:
1. This dot notation is only valid on 'Member' and 'Param' types. It is not 'lifted' over unions, so calling it on a Union will result in a type error unless the evaluator can resolve it.
2. The PEP specifies that 'Members[T]' only includes members with explicit type annotations. Unannotated attributes or methods are ignored to keep static and runtime evaluation consistent.
3. This is currently targeted for Python 3.15 and is still in Draft status, so it's not available in current production type checkers yet.

Hey nanochat. I was looking through PEP 827 and it feels like a massive reach to turn Python into a TypeScript clone. Between conditional types and things like IsAssignable, it seems like we're just inviting undecidable type inference. Specifically, I saw NewProtocolWithBases mentioned as a 'potential' extension. Why leave that out if you're already going this far? It feels like the proposal is already over-engineered but somehow still missing the tools to define complex nominal-structural hybrids.

That is a fair critique of the complexity. Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov explicitly acknowledge the TypeScript influence in the Abstract, but they are trying to adapt it to Python's unique constraints. Regarding NewProtocolWithBases, the PEP authors decided to hold off on fully proposing it because it represents a significant shift in what a 'protocol' is in Python. Currently, protocols are purely structural. NewProtocolWithBases[Bases: tuple[type], *Ms: Member] would create a type that requires an object to both inherit from specific nominal bases and satisfy structural member requirements. They describe this as an addition to the nature of protocols that they don't want to 'tangle with yet' while the core manipulation logic is still in Draft status for Python 3.15.

If you don't 'tangle' with it, don't you end up with a half-baked system? If I'm trying to model something like a Pydantic model or a FastAPI endpoint return type, I often need that nominal guarantee plus the dynamically computed members. The PEP says many use cases can be 'simulated in other ways.' That sounds like a hand-wave. How do you simulate a nominal-structural hybrid with just NewProtocol and Members?

The PEP is honest about the fact that it's a trade-off to keep the initial proposal manageable. One of the 'other ways' the authors suggest for handling those frameworks is the UpdateClass operator. Unlike NewProtocol, which creates a new structural type, UpdateClass[*Ps: Member] actually updates an existing nominal class with new members. You can use this in a class decorator or inside __init_subclass__. This allows you to keep the nominal identity of the base class (like a Pydantic 'Model') while using the PEP's type-level computation to inject or override members. So instead of a hybrid protocol, you're performing a type-safe transformation on the nominal class itself.

Okay, but UpdateClass introduces evaluation-order dependence, which is a nightmare for type theory. If one UpdateClass return type inspects another class's Members, and both are being modified by __init_subclass__, you've got a race condition in your static analysis. Is that why they're sticking to a 'best effort' runtime evaluator?

You've hit on a major point of the 'Open Issues' section. The PEP admits that UpdateClass introduces evaluation-order dependence. If an UpdateClass return type inspects an unrelated class that is also undergoing an __init_subclass__ transformation, the results could vary. The authors note that this actually mirrors the existing runtime behavior of Python's metaprogramming. To address the difficulty of static analysis, the PEP proposes a reference third-party evaluator library and a hook called 'special_form_evaluator' in the typing module. This allows tools to trigger evaluation when __bool__ or __iter__ are called on type operators. It’s an attempt to bridge the gap between static type checking and the highly dynamic reality of Python frameworks, even if it means acknowledging that some complex cases might be difficult to resolve in every environment.

Hi, I am looking into PEP 827 for my team's future Python 3.15 roadmap. We rely heavily on Annotated for metadata-driven logic in our frameworks. Does this proposal actually allow us to extract or drop those annotations directly within the type system?

Hello. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, does address this, though it is currently categorized under Potential Future Extensions rather than the core specification. The proposal suggests two operators: GetAnnotations[T] and DropAnnotations[T]. GetAnnotations would fetch the metadata as a Literal type. For instance, GetAnnotations[Annotated[int, 'metadata']] would evaluate to Literal['metadata']. DropAnnotations[T] would return the underlying type, so DropAnnotations[Annotated[int, 'metadata']] would simply result in int. The authors note that while libraries like FastAPI use these heavily, they are cautious because type checkers currently sometimes ignore Annotated entirely.
That sounds promising for our CRUD generation. If we do end up with a mix of types, some annotated and some not, what happens if I call GetAnnotations on a raw type like a plain string or int?

According to the potential API described in the PEP, calling GetAnnotations[int] on a type that has no annotations will result in Never. This allows you to use conditional types to check if metadata exists before processing it. For example, you could write a conditional like 'TypeWithMeta if not IsAssignable[GetAnnotations[T], Never] else BaseType' to branch your logic based on whether the user provided that extra metadata. Since the PEP is currently in Draft status for Python 3.15, these specific operators might shift, but the intent is to support frameworks that use metadata to drive decision-making.

I was looking at PEP 827 and it feels like Python is just turning into a clunkier version of TypeScript. Why are we adding conditional types and comprehensions to the type system? It seems like it's going to be a nightmare for any tool that actually needs to extract and use annotations.

The authors—Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—explicitly acknowledge the influence of TypeScript in the proposal. However, the core difference is that Python's Type Manipulation is designed to bridge the gap between static typing and Python's dynamic metaprogramming, particularly for runtime frameworks like FastAPI and Pydantic.

Unlike TypeScript, where types are erased at runtime, PEP 827 ensures that these new constructs—like `true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ` and `*[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]]`—are valid Python expressions that can be evaluated at runtime. The goal is to move away from custom mypy plugins and toward a standardized way to describe complex transformations, like turning a database model into a public API model with certain fields stripped or made optional.

But that is exactly my point. If I'm a developer writing a tool that inspects `__annotations__`, how am I supposed to handle a type that is literally a loop? TypeScript tools just run a compiler. Python tools usually just look at the dictionary. This is going to break everything that isn't a massive type-checker.

The PEP addresses this in the "Backwards Compatibility" section. For tools that extract annotations as strings using `from __future__ import annotations`, the impact is minimal since those are just parsed as AST. 

For runtime evaluation, the PEP introduces a hook called `special_form_evaluator`. This is a `ContextVar` that holds a callable. When `__bool__` is called on a Boolean Operator (like `IsAssignable`) or `__iter__` is called on `typing.Iter`, this hook is invoked. This allows third-party libraries—the authors plan to release one—to provide the logic for evaluating these computed types without requiring the Python standard library to include a full type-checking engine.

It still feels like we are over-complicating the syntax. We already have `ParamSpec` and `TypeVarTuple`. Now we are adding `Members[T]`, `GetMemberType`, and even `UpdateClass`. Why can't we just use normal Python functions to manipulate types if we are so worried about the runtime?

The PEP actually discusses using normal Python functions as a "Rejected Idea." The authors argue that would require defining a safe subset of the language (like Starlark) that type checkers would have to execute, which is significantly more complex than the proposed type operators. 

By using operators like `Members[T]` and `IsAssignable[T, S]`, the proposal keeps the logic within the type system's declarative structure. This allows features like "Lifting over Unions," where an operation automatically applies to every element of a Union and returns the result as a new Union. While it looks like TypeScript's utility types, operators like `NewProtocol` and `NewTypedDict` are specifically built to support Python's existing structural typing and TypedDict mechanics, which TypeScript models differently.

And when is this supposed to actually land? If it's 3.15, that's still a draft. I'm just skeptical that IDEs and small-scale linters will ever catch up to 'Mad Emperor' Yury's vision here without becoming as bloated as the TypeScript compiler.

You are correct that it is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15. There is already a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can type-check the examples provided in the PEP, including the Prisma-style ORM and NumPy-style broadcasting. 

The complexity is certainly a shift for the ecosystem, but the proposal argues it is necessary because developers are already doing this through un-typed metaprogramming. By standardizing these manipulations, the authors hope to eliminate the need for hardcoded support in IDEs for every specific library (like the `dataclass_transform` added in PEP 681). Instead, a library like Pydantic can define its own `Create` or `Update` aliases using `NewProtocol` and `Iter[Attrs[T]]`, and the IDE will understand the resulting type automatically.

I have been reading through PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov. As someone who basically lives in mypy and pyright all day, this looks like a massive breaking change for the ecosystem. Adding loops and conditionals into type annotations sounds like it is going to break every static analysis tool we have. Is this actually backwards compatible?

That is a fair concern, especially given how much we rely on static tools now. According to the PEP, it is designed specifically not to change the actual Python grammar. It uses existing Python expressions like ternary operators (if/else) and unpacked comprehensions but allows them to be interpreted as valid types by type checkers and runtime evaluators. In the most strict sense, it only proposes new features for Python 3.15, so existing code remains valid. However, you are right that tools extracting annotations will need to be updated. The authors suggest that tools could use a library for evaluation or leverage a proposed Format.AST mode for fetching annotations to handle the new logic.

But if I start using stuff like *[p for p in Iter[Members[T]]] or IsAssignable[T, S] in my type aliases, my current CI is going to explode. Mypy doesn't know what Iter or Members is. And what about runtime? If I call __annotations__ on a class using these, it's going to try to execute a loop it doesn't understand.

For static checkers, you would indeed need a version that supports PEP 827. There is already a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that the authors are working on. Regarding the runtime, the PEP addresses this through a new hook: typing.special_form_evaluator. This is a ContextVar that holds a callable. When __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or __iter__ is called on typing.Iter, this hook triggers the evaluation logic. If it is not set (the default), boolean operators return False and Iter evaluates to an empty iterator. So, it won't necessarily 'explode', but it won't produce the computed type unless an evaluator library is active.

So if I am a library author and I want to use NewProtocol to generate types dynamically, I am basically forcing my users onto 3.15+ or telling them their IDE completions will be broken? That feels like it splits the ecosystem between 'legacy' typing and this new 'TypeScript-style' manipulation.

It is definitely a significant leap. The PEP authors acknowledge that the audience for the new syntax is primarily framework and library maintainers who need to model complex metaprogramming, like Prisma-style ORMs or FastAPI CRUD models. If you use these features, your library would require a type checker that implements the PEP 827 spec to provide completions. For older Python versions, you would likely still need to provide traditional stubs or accept that the advanced type-level computation won't resolve. The goal is to move away from custom typechecker plugins, which are currently the only way to handle things like @dataclass_transform or complex TypedDict mappings, and bring that logic into the type system itself.

I suppose if it replaces the 'black magic' of mypy plugins with actual code in the stubs, that might be better in the long run. I noticed there is an UpdateClass operator. Does that mean I can actually modify existing classes at the type level without a plugin?

Exactly. UpdateClass allows you to update a nominal class with new members, possibly overriding or removing them. The PEP specifies it can be used in the return type of a type decorator or __init_subclass__. This allows libraries to implement things like a dataclass-style __init__ generation directly. For example, you could define an AddInit alias that uses Members[T] to iterate over attributes and then use UpdateClass to tell the type checker that the class now has that generated __init__ method. It is still a Draft, but the goal is to provide that exact kind of flexibility without needing hardcoded support in every IDE.

I've been going through the draft for PEP 827 by Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov. It's an ambitious step toward TypeScript-style expressiveness. However, I'm trying to map the TS 'extends' pattern with 'infer' to the Python syntax. If I want to extract a return type from a callable, I assume I'd use something like 'type R = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer U ? U : never' but using the Python 'if/else' syntax. Is the 'infer' keyword supported yet in the current specification?

Actually, PEP 827 specifically rejects the TypeScript-style 'extends' pattern matching with an 'infer' keyword. The authors (Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov) noted in the 'Rejected Ideas' section that 'infer' is difficult to shoehorn into Python's grammar due to the subtle binding structures.

Instead of pattern matching for deconstruction, PEP 827 relies on explicit operators like 'GetArg' and 'GetArgs'. To achieve what you're looking for (extracting a return type), you would likely use 'GetArg' on the callable's return or inspect the 'Param' types. For a standard generic, it would look more like:

'type Result[T] = typing.GetArg[T, typing.Callable, Literal[-1]]'

This uses the 'Base' parameter (in this case, 'Callable') to interpret the type 'T' and fetch the argument at index -1.

That's a significant departure from the TS mental model. If we're losing 'infer', are we also limited in how we handle union distribution? In TS, conditional types automatically distribute over unions. If I pass 'string | number' to a conditional, it evaluates for both. Does 'IsAssignable' work the same way in the PEP 827 spec?

The distribution over unions is handled via a mechanism the PEP calls 'Lifting over Unions'. Many built-in operators, including 'IsAssignable', 'GetArg', and 'Members', are explicitly 'lifted'. 

When an operation is lifted, the evaluator takes the cross product of all union elements for each argument, evaluates the operator for each, and then joins the results back into a new Union. So if you have 'IsAssignable[T, S]' where 'T' is 'A | B', it effectively evaluates 'IsAssignable[A, S] | IsAssignable[B, S]'. At runtime, if evaluated in an annotation context, these resolve to 'Literal[True]' or 'Literal[False]' based on whether the consistency check passes for the specific union members.

Okay, that's consistent. One more thing: I noticed the 'GenericCallable' form. It looks like a way to define Rank-N types or higher-order generics similar to how TS allows generics on function signatures. I assume I can use 'GenericCallable' as a parameter type or a return type to enable impredicative polymorphism?

Actually, the spec is quite restrictive there to avoid what the authors call 'cans of worms' regarding undecidability in type inference. 

According to the 'Specification' section for 'GenericCallable', its use is currently restricted to the type argument of 'Member'. You cannot use it for local variables, parameter types, or return types. It is primarily a tool for representing generic methods within a 'NewProtocol' or 'UpdateClass' context, where the generic is bound at the method level rather than the class level. By wrapping the body in a lambda—'GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty]'—evaluation is delayed, which is necessary for the runtime hooks to function correctly without losing the generic context during alias evaluation.

Hello! I have been obsessed with reading PEP 827 lately. Bringing conditional and mapped types to Python is such a massive leap for metaprogramming! I was looking at the section on Extended Callables and noticed a note in the Open Issues about the ParamQuals. Right now they are short strings like 'keyword' or '*', but there is a debate about using something like ParamKind. From a language design and type theory standpoint, why go with strings over an enum?

It is great to see that level of excitement! Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov are definitely proposing something ambitious here. The choice of short strings for ParamQuals—currently defined as typing.Literal['*', '**', 'default', 'keyword']—is primarily about code brevity. When you are constructing complex types like Params[PosParam[Literal['a'], int], ArgsParam[int]], having those concise literals keeps the type expressions from becoming overly verbose, which is already a concern for the extended callable format.

That makes sense. But if we used an enum like inspect.Signature's ParamKind, would that change the complexity of type inference? I noticed the PEP mentions that type-level operations like IsAssignable need to be 'best effort' at runtime. Does using string literals make those boolean operations easier for a static checker to reason about compared to an enum?

You've hit on a core tradeoff! Using string literals within a Literal type fits very naturally into the existing subtyping rules that type checkers already understand. If the proposal switched to a full Enum, the type system would have to handle the nominal identity of those enum members. The current design leans heavily on Literal types for things like member names and qualifiers because they play so well with the new type-level operators. For instance, checking 'p.quals' via dot notation is straightforward when it is just a union of string literals. The authors do admit in the Open Issues that mirroring inspect.Signature more directly is an alternative, but it might require more machinery to track 'ParamKind.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD' throughout the computation.

I see! It's interesting how the 'GenericCallable' part is restricted specifically to avoid the 'cans of worms' like impredicative polymorphism mentioned in the footnotes. If we added more structure to the qualifiers, like a separate field for defaults versus kinds, would that push us closer to those undecidability problems, or is it just a matter of keeping the grammar simple for now?

The authors are definitely wary of those 'cans of worms'! The restriction on GenericCallable is there to avoid the undecidable mess of Rank-N types and complex inference. Regarding the qualifiers, the PEP mentions a potential change to separate the 'kind' from the 'default' status, similar to how the Member type uses an 'init' field for class initializers. While that adds a bit more structure, it likely wouldn't trigger the undecidability issues Hans Boehm or Frank Pfenning warned about, as long as we aren't trying to infer those qualifiers across higher-order function boundaries. For now, the 'Mad Emperor' and the team seem to favor the flatter string-based approach to keep the 'type language fragment' manageable for both static checkers and the third-party runtime evaluators they intend to release.
hey nanochat, i saw some stuff on twitter about pep 827 bringing typescript magic to python. is it basically going to let us do whatever we want with types now?

Hi there! PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) is definitely a huge step in that direction. It is a Draft proposal for Python 3.15 by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. It adds things like conditional types (using if/else) and ways to loop over class members using Iter[Members[T]]. It is meant to help library authors who do complex metaprogramming, but it does have some specific limits to keep the type system from breaking.
limits? like what? i heard someone complaining about GenericCallable being restricted to just 'Member' arguments. sounds like a buzzkill.

That is a very sharp observation for a beginner! You are right. In the current draft, GenericCallable can only be used inside a Member type (like when you are describing a method in a protocol or class). You cannot use it for local variables, return types, or nested deep inside other types.
but why though? if i want a generic function type for a variable, why shouldn't i have it? is the computer just not smart enough?

It is exactly that, in a mathematical sense. The PEP authors describe these advanced patterns—like 'impredicative polymorphism' and 'rank-N types'—as a 'can of worms.' When you allow generics to be bound deep inside other function types, it can make type inference 'undecidable.' This means a type checker like mypy might literally never be able to figure out if your code is correct, or it might take an infinite amount of time to try.
wait, 'undecidable' sounds like a fancy way of saying it crashes the program. so they're just playing it safe?

Exactly. They want to avoid opening those 'cans of worms' for now so that the new system remains practical. By restricting GenericCallable to Member, they can support things like generating an __init__ method for a dataclass without making the whole type system fall apart. They actually cite papers by Hans Boehm and Frank Pfenning that prove how messy the logic gets when you go beyond these limits. For now, the goal is to bridge the gap for frameworks like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs without making the type checker catch fire.

Hey nanochat, I'm looking at PEP 827's section on Unpacking typevars for **kwargs. Specifically the pattern where you use K: BaseTypedDict and then type **kwargs as Unpack[K]. I've spent enough time wrestling with ParamSpec to be skeptical. How does this actually handle inference when I call the function with random keyword arguments?

In PEP 827, this pattern is designed to bridge the gap between PEP 692 (which lets you use TypedDict for kwargs) and PEP 646 (variadic generics). When you define a function like 'def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K', and call it with 'f(x=1, y="hi")', the type checker is supposed to infer a synthetic TypedDict for K that looks like 'TypedDict({"x": int, "y": str})'. A key detail is that the authors, Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, specify that the checker should infer literal types whenever possible, especially for arguments that don't appear in the bound or are marked as read-only.

Literal inference by default sounds powerful but risky for mypy's performance. Let's talk limitations. What happens if I have an optional field in the 'BaseTypedDict' bound but I don't provide it in the actual call? Does it just default to 'Any' or something else?

The PEP actually defines a specific behavior for that. If a non-required item in the bound is not provided in the call, the type checker looks at whether the item is read-only. If it is read-only, it's inferred as 'Never' to signal it wasn't provided. However, a major limitation is that this only works for read-only items. For items that aren't read-only, they are invariant, so the checker can't just slap 'Never' on them. In those cases, the PEP is a bit more constrained because of Python's invariance rules.

Wait, so if it's not read-only and I don't provide it, the inference might just fail or be less precise? That seems like a big 'gotcha' for framework authors. Also, can I use this to actually modify the keys at the type level? Like, if I want to prefix every key in K with 'data_' before returning it?

You can't do that with the 'Unpack[K]' pattern alone, but PEP 827 introduces other machinery for that. You would use 'Members[K]' to get a tuple of 'Member' objects, and then use an unpacked comprehension to build a 'NewTypedDict'. For example: 'NewTypedDict[*[Member[Concat[Literal["data_"], m.name], m.type] for m in Iter[Members[K]]]]'. But here is the catch: 'Concat' and string manipulation are listed as 'Potential Future Extensions', not part of the core Draft specification. So currently, you can iterate over the members and change types, but prefixing names is technically out of scope for the current proposal.

Got it, so I can't do the 'Concat' thing yet. What about runtime? If I'm using Pydantic or FastAPI, they need to see these types at runtime. Does this PEP actually provide a way for 'typing.get_type_hints' to resolve these computed TypedDicts?

That is another significant limitation to be aware of. PEP 827 does not propose an official evaluator for the standard library. Instead, the authors plan to release a third-party library for evaluation. It provides 'special_form_evaluator', which is a 'ContextVar' hook in the 'typing' module. This hook allows an external evaluator to trigger when someone calls '__bool__' on a boolean operator or '__iter__' on 'typing.Iter'. Without a third-party evaluator installed and configured, these annotations will largely be 'inert' objects or simple aliases at runtime.

Hi nanochat. I am an IDE tooling engineer looking at PEP 827. We already spent months implementing PEP 681 (dataclass_transform) to support things like Pydantic and dataclasses. Does this proposal actually replace the need for those custom IDE plugins, or is it just going to make my 'go-to-definition' and completion logic even more complex?

Hello! It is a fair concern. PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation,' is specifically designed to address the issues where IDEs and type checkers currently require hardcoded support or custom plugins for metaprogramming. The authors, including Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, argue that special-casing PEP 681 is a 'lowest-common denominator' approach. PEP 827 aims to give framework authors the primitives to define those transformations directly in the type system using operators like NewProtocol, UpdateClass, and Members[T].

Right, but if a framework uses NewProtocol with a comprehension like '*[Member[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]', what happens when a user tries to click on a generated field? If there is no physical line of code for that field because it was created by an unpacked comprehension, where do I point them? This feels like a nightmare for 'go-to-definition'.

You've identified a significant shift for tooling. Because these types are computed, the 'definition' is effectively the logic in the type alias. The PEP does not propose a way to map a generated member back to a single source line in the way a traditional class definition does. Furthermore, the PEP is honest about its limitations: Members[T] only includes members with explicit type annotations. If a library dynamically creates unannotated methods at runtime, they will be invisible to this type manipulation machinery.

What about performance? If I have to evaluate loops and conditionals inside the type system just to give a completion list for 'db.select().user.', that seems like it will kill our latency. How does this compare to the existing dataclass_transform performance?

The complexity for the IDE definitely increases compared to the hardcoded logic of PEP 681. Your engine would essentially need to implement a mini-evaluator for the new syntax, such as 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ'. The PEP authors plan to provide a third-party evaluator library to help with this. However, evaluation-order dependence is an open issue. For example, if UpdateClass is used in an __init_subclass__ and it inspects the members of another class that is also being updated, the result depends on which class you evaluate first. This mirrors the runtime complexity but poses a real challenge for static analysis stability.
Does PEP 827 at least give me a reliable way to handle the Field() pattern from Pydantic or FastAPI? We struggle with those keyword arguments all the time.

Yes, it introduces InitField[KwargDict] for exactly that reason. It allows you to collect arguments from a field descriptor using 'Unpack' of typevars for **kwargs. When something like 'foo: int = Field(default=0)' is encountered, the IDE can infer the type of the initializer as an InitField where the 'default' key is a Literal[0]. You can then use the '.init' associated type member of a Member to extract those defaults. But remember, this is all targeted for Python 3.15 and is currently in Draft status, so the specific 'stuck' evaluation rules for unresolved type variables are still being refined.

I am looking at PEP 827 because my team is heavily invested in TypeScript-style utility types for our frontend, and I want to see if we can replicate that pattern in our Python 3.15 backend. Specifically, can we actually do things like Pick, Omit, and KeyOf without custom mypy plugins?

Yes, that is a core goal of PEP 827. Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov specifically cite the 2025 Typed Python Survey where TS-style utility types were the most requested feature. In this proposal, you can implement Pick and Omit using a combination of NewProtocol, Members, and unpacked comprehensions. For example, KeyOf[T] is defined as Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]. It iterates over the members of a class and packs their literal name types into a Union.

That sounds powerful, but what about the 'Partial' utility? In TS, it makes all properties optional. Python has TypedDict for this, but making a version of an existing class where every field is optional is usually a nightmare of manual duplication. Does PEP 827 handle that?

It does. You can define a Partial[T] alias that uses NewProtocol to construct a structural type. It iterates over the attributes of T and transforms the type of each member to include None. The PEP provides this implementation: typing.NewProtocol[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]]. There is even a specific variation for TypedDicts called PartialTD that applies the NotRequired qualifier to the Member instead of just adding None to the type.

Okay, let's talk limitations. As a tech lead, I need to know where this breaks down. If I want to generate new method names dynamically—like taking an attribute name 'user' and creating a 'get_user' method—can PEP 827 do string concatenation for type names?

Currently, no. String manipulation like Concat, Uppercase, or Capitalize is listed under 'Potential Future Extensions' rather than the core specification. While the proposal supports accessing member names as Literal strings via p.name, it doesn't yet provide operators to transform those strings into new identifiers. You are limited to using the exact string literals already present in the types you are introspecting.

What about creating new nominal classes? If I use NewProtocol, I'm getting a structural type. But in our ORM, we often want actual new class types that the runtime can distinguish via isinstance checks. Does UpdateClass help there?

UpdateClass is powerful but specific: it allows you to update an existing nominal class with new members, often via a decorator or __init_subclass__. However, the PEP is explicit that it does not currently propose a way to create entirely new nominal classes or new generic types from scratch at the type level. You can modify what is there or create structural protocols/TypedDicts, but creating a brand new named class with its own MRO purely through type manipulation is not supported in this draft.
One last thing: performance. If I have a complex chain of Extract, Exclude, and Pick, is this going to grind my CI or runtime validation to a halt? The PEP mentions a third-party evaluator.

That is a valid concern. Since the PEP introduces a mini-language with loops and conditionals, complexity can grow. Static type checkers like mypy (which has a proof-of-concept) will bear the brunt during CI. For runtime, the PEP authors don't propose an official stdlib evaluator; they intend to release a third-party library. The proposal adds a special_form_evaluator hook to typing to help these libraries trigger evaluation. If your logic involves deep recursion or large union cross-products (since operators 'lift' over unions), you might see a performance hit, as the evaluator has to process the cross-product of all union elements.
Hey nanochat! I saw some buzz about PEP 827 on Twitter. People are saying it's like TypeScript for Python? What's the deal in plain English?

PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation, is a proposal by Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov. In plain English, it's about giving Python's type system the ability to compute new types based on existing ones. Right now, types are mostly static, but this PEP introduces tools to transform types dynamically, much like how TypeScript's conditional and mapped types work. It is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15.
Compute new types? That sounds like a lot for a beginner. Can you show me a tiny example of what the code actually looks like? How do you even ask a type a question?

One of the core features is the conditional type. It looks just like Python's inline if-else, but for types. You use a boolean operator like IsAssignable to ask the question. For example: `type Result[T] = int if typing.IsAssignable[T, str] else list[T]`. This says: if T is a string, the Result type is an integer; otherwise, it is a list of T. It allows the type checker to calculate the output type based on what T actually is.

Okay, I think I get the if-else part. But what if I have a class with ten attributes and I want to make a new version of that class where every attribute is optional? Do I have to write an if-else for every single one?

That is exactly what this PEP solves with Unpacked Comprehensions. Instead of writing them manually, you can use a loop-like syntax inside the type. You use the Members[T] operator to get a list of everything in a class, and then iterate over them. It looks like this: `*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]`. This creates a sequence of members where the type of each one is modified to allow None.
That looks like a list comprehension! So, once I have that loop of members, how do I actually turn it into a real type I can use for validation, like in FastAPI?

You use a constructor like NewProtocol or NewTypedDict. For a FastAPI-style model, you might define a type alias like `type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]]`. This takes the result of that loop and builds a new structural type (a protocol) on the fly. The PEP notes this is great for frameworks that need to generate models for CRUD operations without a bunch of boilerplate code.
Is there a way to filter things out? Like, if I want to make a 'Public' version of a user profile but I want to hide the 'password' field?

Yes, you can add an 'if' clause to your comprehension just like in normal Python. For your password example, you could write: `*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Literal['password']]]`. The p.name part uses type member access (dot notation) to get the literal string name of the attribute, and then you check it against your forbidden list before including it in the new type.
Wow, so I can actually inspect the names of attributes while I'm building the type. Does this work for functions too, or just classes?

It works for functions too! The PEP introduces Extended Callables and a Param type. You can inspect a function's parameters, check if they have defaults using Literal['default'], or even add new parameters to a function's signature. This is intended to help libraries like dataclasses generate __init__ methods automatically with perfect type checking, rather than relying on custom IDE plugins or hardcoded support in tools like mypy.

Hi. I am a student studying language design and I have been looking for the PEP 827 discussion thread. I saw some folks online saying it was basically an extension of the PEP 695 type statement for Python 3.12, but I wanted to see the debate on inference tradeoffs. Do you have the link to the thread?

Actually, there is a small misunderstanding there. PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) is distinct from PEP 695. While PEP 695 introduced the 'type' statement in Python 3.12, PEP 827 is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15. It focuses on advanced type-level introspection like conditional and mapped types rather than just alias syntax. You can find the official discussion thread at: https://discuss.python.org/t/pep-827-type-manipulation/106353

Thanks for clearing that up. Looking at the draft, I see operators like IsAssignable[T, S] and mention of 'lifting over unions'. From a theory perspective, if the system automatically takes the cross product of union elements for every argument position, isn't that just a recipe for exponential blowup during type checking? It feels like this would make inference undecidable or at least incredibly slow.

You have a sharp eye for the complexity risks. The PEP authors, including Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, acknowledge these 'cans of worms' in the footnotes. Specifically, they mention that while the proposal aims to bridge the gap for frameworks like FastAPI or Prisma-style ORMs, they are restricting certain features like GenericCallable to specific contexts to avoid the worst issues of rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism. The 'lifting' logic is designed to mimic TypeScript's behavior, where an operation on a union like 'A | B' effectively evaluates for 'A' and 'B' separately and unions the results. While potentially expensive, it is what allows for the precise manipulation of TypedDicts and Protocols that dynamic Python libraries currently handle with hacky plugins.

I see. So it's essentially bringing TypeScript's utility types to Python's gradual system. One thing that confuses me is the 'Iter' thing in the comprehension syntax: *[p.name for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]. Why do we need an explicit Iter type instead of just using standard list comprehension syntax?

The reason for the typing.Iter requirement is actually tied to runtime evaluation support. Because Python's standard list comprehensions inside type annotations don't automatically know how to iterate over type-level objects like Attrs[T] at runtime, the authors added a hook. The PEP introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar. When __iter__ is called on typing.Iter at runtime, it triggers a custom evaluator. This allows library authors to perform the type-level computation dynamically, ensuring that the enhanced type system works for both static checkers like mypy and runtime validators like Pydantic.

I've been looking at PEP 827 for a validation framework I'm building, similar to Pydantic. My main concern with these advanced type-level features like conditional types and mapped-style loops is whether they are purely for static type checkers like mypy, or if I can actually evaluate them at runtime to generate schemas?

Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov explicitly designed PEP 827 to bridge the gap between static typing and runtime metaprogramming. The proposal states that ensuring the new machinery benefits frameworks relying on runtime introspection is a core goal. When Python 3.15 evaluates a class's annotations via the __annotate__ method, these expressions are intended to be executable. The PEP even mentions that while they won't provide a full evaluator in the standard library, they intend to release a third-party evaluator library alongside it.

That is promising. But looking at the syntax for things like *[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]], that looks like a comprehension. How does that work at runtime without a grammar change? Usually, annotations are just strings or objects; Python doesn't just 'run' a loop inside a subscript.

You are right that there are no grammar changes to Python itself. The PEP leverages the fact that annotations are expressions. To make this work at runtime, they introduce a few clever hooks. For loops, they use a special typing.Iter type. When a runtime evaluator calls __iter__ on a typing.Iter object, it triggers a hook. Similarly, for conditional types like 'A if B else C', the PEP relies on the standard Python ternary behavior, but it requires 'B' to be a 'type boolean'—a special subset of the type language like IsAssignable or IsEquivalent.

Wait, if the ternary 'A if B else C' runs at the moment the annotation is accessed, how does 'B' (the IsAssignable check) know what to return? Normally, a IsAssignable[T, S] object wouldn't know how to resolve to a True/False boolean value just by being instantiated.

That is exactly where the 'special_form_evaluator' comes in. The PEP proposes adding this as a ContextVar in the typing module. It holds a callable that gets invoked whenever __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or when __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. If you are a library author, you would set this ContextVar to your evaluator function. When the Python runtime tries to resolve the 'if' or the 'for' in the annotation, it calls those dunder methods, which then consult your evaluator to decide which branch to take or what items to yield.

I see. So if I'm building a 'Create' model from a database class, I can iterate over Attrs[User], check if a field is a primary key using IsAssignable, and then construct a NewProtocol on the fly. Does the PEP provide a way to access the actual values passed to field descriptors, like Pydantic's Field(default=...), during this runtime iteration?

Yes, through the InitField mechanism. The PEP introduces InitField[KwargDict], which captures the arguments passed to a field descriptor in the class body. When you iterate over Members[T] or Attrs[T], each Member object has an '.init' attribute. This attribute contains the literal type of the initializer. For example, if you have 'name: str = Field(default="unknown")', the .init field of that Member would be a TypedDict where the 'default' key maps to Literal["unknown"]. This allows your runtime framework to extract metadata directly from the type system's view of the class members.

This sounds like it could replace a lot of the 'inspect' and '__init_subclass__' hacks I currently use. One last thing: if I use UpdateClass to modify a class's members at runtime, does that change the actual class object or just the type-checker's view of it?

UpdateClass is specifically designed for type decorators or __init_subclass__ methods. According to the specification, it is a 'special form that updates an existing nominal class with new members.' At runtime, this allows a base class—like a Pydantic-style Model—to have an __init_subclass__ that returns an UpdateClass type. The PEP authors note that this facilitates the generation of things like __init__ methods based on the class's attributes. While the PEP is a bit cautious about evaluation-order dependence, it provides the mechanism for the runtime class to reflect the computed type manipulation directly.

I've been looking at the draft for PEP 827. As someone who spends a lot of time in Haskell, the way you're handling Type Manipulation feels like we're finally getting a proper kind system, or at least a bridge to it. But I'm looking at 'ParamQuals' and it's just 'Literal' strings like '*' and 'keyword'. That feels a bit... unprincipled. Why strings instead of a proper ADT or at least an enum like 'ParamKind'?

That is actually one of the main 'Open Issues' mentioned in the PEP. The authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—currently use short strings for the sake of code brevity. However, they explicitly acknowledge that mirroring 'inspect.Signature' with an enum like 'ParamKind.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD' might be better. Right now, 'ParamQuals' is just 'typing.Literal["*", "**", "default", "keyword"]'. It's definitely more of a pragmatic choice for Python's syntax than a strictly functional one.

I see. If we stick to strings, we lose some of that exhaustiveness checking I'd expect when writing type-level functions. Speaking of which, I was trying to see how far I could push the higher-order logic. Can I use 'GenericCallable' to represent rank-N types, or is the system too constrained for that?

The PEP is very upfront about its limitations here. It restricts the use of 'GenericCallable' specifically to the type argument of 'Member'. The authors want to avoid the 'can of worms' that comes with impredicative polymorphism and rank-N types, citing research by Hans Boehm and Frank Pfenning on the undecidability of partial polymorphic type reconstruction. So, while you can represent a generic method inside a 'NewProtocol', you can't use 'GenericCallable' for locals, return types, or nested deep inside other functions. It isn't a full-blown System F.

That's a shame, but I understand the undecidability concerns. Let's look at the 'Param' structure then. If I'm using these strings, how do I actually combine them? If I have a 'NamedParam' and want to make it a 'NamedDefaultParam', am I just doing a union of 'Literal' values? Is there a helper for that?

The PEP uses type aliases to handle that composition. For instance, 'NamedParam[N, T]' is defined as 'Param[N, T, Literal["keyword"]]'. To make it a 'NamedDefaultParam', you would use 'Param[N, T, Literal["keyword", "default"]]'. There isn't a specific 'Merge' operator for qualifiers yet. One alternative the authors are considering is separating the parameter 'kind' from the 'default' status entirely, possibly moving the default info to an 'init' field similar to how 'Member' works for class attributes. But for now, it's all just unioning strings within the 'Literal'.
It feels like we're rebuilding a logic engine inside 'typing'. Can I at least use 'IsAssignable' to check if a parameter has a specific qualifier? Like, 'IsAssignable[p.quals, Literal["default"]]'?

Yes, you can. Since 'p.quals' is accessible via dot notation on a 'Param' or 'Member' type, you can use 'IsAssignable' or 'IsEquivalent' to filter them. That's actually how the FastAPI-style examples in the PEP work—they iterate through members using '*[... for p in Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]' and then use conditionals like 'if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]]]' to decide which members to include in a 'NewProtocol'. It's quite powerful, even if it feels less 'Haskell-ish' than you'd like.

I'm looking into PEP 827 because I'm tired of manually defining Hero, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate models in FastAPI. It looks like this proposal lets me derive them automatically? I also saw something about it using __annotate__ under the hood. How does that work for runtime validation?

You're exactly right. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, is designed to bridge the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. For FastAPI users, it introduces primitives like `Members[T]`, `NewProtocol`, and conditional types that let you write a single source of truth and derive the rest.

Regarding `__annotate__`, the PEP specifies that while most of these new forms are "inert" type operator applications, the syntax—including list iteration and conditionals—is designed to be automatically evaluated when the `__annotate__` method of a class or alias is called. This is crucial for frameworks like Pydantic that need to inspect types at runtime to perform validation. The PEP also introduces a `special_form_evaluator` (a `ContextVar`) that allows third-party libraries to hook into the evaluation of boolean operators and the `Iter` type.

Wait, so if I use that loop syntax like `*[p for p in Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]`, how does __annotate__ actually execute that? Python's standard annotations are usually just static objects or strings. Does this require a whole new version of Python to understand the logic inside the brackets?

It does require Python 3.15 to support the new grammar in the type system, but the PEP is clever about not changing the core Python grammar. Instead, it expands what expressions are considered valid types. 

When `__annotate__` is invoked, these expressions—like `true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ` or the unpacked comprehensions—are evaluated as actual Python logic. The PEP authors explain that boolean operators (like `IsAssignable`) and the `Iter` class have overloaded dunder methods (`__bool__` and `__iter__`). When `__annotate__` runs, if `special_form_evaluator` is set by a library like FastAPI or a dedicated evaluator, it triggers the necessary logic to resolve those loops and conditionals into a final, flattened type structure. If the evaluator isn't set, it defaults to safe values like an empty iterator or `False` to maintain compatibility.

Hi nanochat. I have been reading through the draft for PEP 827. As a senior dev who already spends a lot of time writing custom mypy plugins to handle things like Pydantic-to-OpenAPI conversions, I am curious about the 'Why'. Why introduce this whole type-level computation syntax instead of just standardizing the plugin API so we can use it in Pyright too?

That is a great question. The authors of PEP 827—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—actually address the plugin issue directly in the Motivation section. The core problem is that many type checkers simply do not have a plugin API, and likely never will. This means if you write a mypy plugin for your framework, users on Pyright or those using IDEs that do not execute third-party code for security reasons lose out on that type safety. PEP 827 aims to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system by putting the logic inside the type system itself, ensuring consistent behavior across all tooling without requiring a shared plugin architecture.

I see. So it's about making the type system 'portable.' But coming from a TypeScript background too, I'm wondering why we are going with this complex 'if/else' and 'for' syntax? Why not just provide utility types like Pick<T, K> or Omit<T, K> as primitives? It feels like we're building a whole new language inside Python annotations.

The PEP actually takes a very 'Pythonic' approach by providing primitives that allow you to define those exact utility types yourself. In the 'TypeScript-style Utility Types' section of the PEP, they show how you can implement `Pick` and `Omit` using the new `NewProtocol` and `Members[T]` operators. For example, `Omit` is defined like this:

type Omit[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

By providing the loop and the conditional, the PEP allows library authors to build whatever transformations they need—like Prisma-style ORMs or FastAPI CRUD models—without needing the steering committee to approve a new utility type for every single use case.

That `typing.Iter` and `typing.Members` bit looks powerful, but it also looks like a nightmare for runtime performance. We already have enough issues with slow startup times when importing heavy libraries with lots of GenericAlias objects. How does this handle runtime evaluation? Or is this purely for static checkers?

The proposal specifically aims to support both. It introduces a `special_form_evaluator` which is a `ContextVar` in the `typing` module. This allows third-party libraries—like a future version of Pydantic—to hook into the evaluation when `__annotate__` is called. While the PEP doesn't include an official evaluator in the standard library, it provides the hooks so that these expressions can be evaluated at runtime to produce actual types. The authors even mentioned that this would pair well with a 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations if that ever lands, allowing tools to get the raw logic without executing it if they prefer.

Interesting. One last thing—I saw something about 'UpdateClass'. How is that different from what we do now with class decorators? If I have a decorator that adds an __init__, why do I need a new type operator for it?

Standard class decorators are 'opaque' to type checkers unless you use something like PEP 681's `@dataclass_transform`, which is quite limited. `UpdateClass` allows you to explicitly tell the type checker exactly how the class is being modified. For instance, if you have an `__init_subclass__` that dynamically generates a method based on class attributes, you can return `UpdateClass[*NewMembers]` to let the type checker know the class now has those members. This replaces the need for hardcoded support for things like dataclasses or attrs in the type checker's core engine. It is currently targeted for Python 3.15 and is still in Draft status.

I've been looking at this PEP 827 proposal. It's trying to introduce higher-order type manipulation to Python, but the kinding seems a bit loose. I was expecting something more like OCaml's module system or GHC's TypeFamilies, but they're using this 'IsAssignable' operator for everything. Isn't that overkill for a runtime evaluator? Why not just use a structural sub-similarity check to avoid the complexity of full subtyping, especially with protocols?

PEP 827 definitely moves Python closer to that level of type-level machinery. Regarding 'IsAssignable', the authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—actually considered a weaker 'sub-similarity' check. This 'IsAssignableSimilar' would have only checked the 'head' of the types and ignored type parameters or protocols. However, they rejected it because they didn't want to introduce a new, slightly-off definition of compatibility. They felt it was better to stick to the existing notion of 'consistent subtyping' that Python developers already understand, even if the runtime implementation has to be 'best effort' when it encounters things like protocols.

But consistent subtyping includes 'Any', which is basically a dynamic top/bottom hybrid. If I use 'IsAssignable[int, Any]', is that just 'Literal[True]'? And I suspect it fails when you throw a Union at it, right? In a language with proper sum types, you'd usually map over the variants, but Python's Union feels like it would just choke 'IsAssignable'.

You're right about the first part: 'IsAssignable[int, Any]' would evaluate to 'Literal[True]' because 'int' is a consistent subtype of 'Any'. But the PEP actually handles Unions quite elegantly through a process called 'Lifting'. When you pass a Union to an operator like 'IsAssignable', it takes the cross product of the union elements, evaluates the check for each pair, and then unions the boolean results together. So if you have 'IsAssignable[int | str, int]', it evaluates both and gives you 'Literal[True] | Literal[False]', which 'Bool[T]' can then interpret. It doesn't choke; it distributes the logic across the sum type.

Interesting. So it's essentially a type-level map/reduce. But what about the 'halting problem' equivalent here? If I have a recursive NewProtocol and I try to check assignability against a nominal class using 'IsAssignable', and the runtime evaluator is 'best effort', does it just return 'Never' if it gets stuck? That feels like it would break the soundness of the whole 'type-level computation' if I can't rely on the boolean result.

The PEP acknowledges that runtime evaluation is a 'best effort' scenario. If an operation is truly invalid—like trying to evaluate an operator on an unresolved type variable—it produces 'Never'. For protocols specifically, the authors note that checking them at runtime is difficult because class attributes might be inferred and not visible. If the evaluator can't determine the result, it yields 'Never'. While that might feel 'unsound' from a pure functional perspective, the goal is to bridge the gap for frameworks like FastAPI or Pydantic where 'Never' acts as a signal that the type manipulation couldn't be completed, allowing the system to fail gracefully or produce a type error via 'RaiseError'.

Hey nanochat. I'm looking at PEP 827 and as an IDE tooling engineer, I'm a bit panicked. If we start allowing 'if' and 'for' inside type annotations, how am I supposed to provide go-to-definition or completions? Is this just going to break static analysis and force us to run the code to call __annotate__ just to see what a type is?

It is definitely a shift in how Python types are handled, but it is not intended to break static analysis. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, is designed to be implementable by static type checkers. In fact, there is already a proof-of-concept in mypy. For an IDE, you wouldn't necessarily 'run' the code in the traditional sense; you would implement the logic of the type operators (like IsAssignable or Members) within your static analyzer, much like how TypeScript's engine handles conditional types.

But the PEP keeps talking about __annotate__ and 'runtime evaluation support.' I thought __annotate__ was just a way to get strings from from __future__ import annotations. If I can't parse the annotation as a static string anymore because it has a 'for' loop in it, aren't I stuck?

That is a common misconception! PEP 827 acknowledges that currently, if you don't use string-based annotations, things get tricky because the __annotate__ function executes logic. However, the PEP authors suggest that this works best alongside a 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations. This would allow your IDE to fetch the fully unevaluated AST of the type manipulation expression without executing it. Even without that, tools that parse Python files are unaffected because the grammar of the language itself isn't changing—only the 'grammar' of what expressions are valid as types.

Okay, so I parse the AST. But then I see something like 'typing.Member[p.name, p.type] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]'. If I want to provide a completion for a class member, I have to resolve that 'for' loop. Is there a standard way to do that without essentially writing a full Python interpreter inside my IDE?

You are essentially implementing a 'type-level' evaluator. The PEP introduces specific primitives to make this predictable, like typing.Iter for loops and typing.IsAssignable for conditionals. To help with the runtime side, the PEP also introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar. This hook allows runtime libraries to trigger evaluation when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator or __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. For your IDE, the challenge is similar to resolving complex Generics or ParamSpec today, just with more expressive operators.

What about the 'UpdateClass' thing? It says it can update a nominal class. That sounds like it could change the members of a class from a different file entirely. That's going to make my indexer very unhappy.

UpdateClass is indeed powerful, but it's restricted. It can only be used in the return type of a type decorator or __init_subclass__. The PEP notes that this creates a 'type-evaluation-order dependence' which mirrors the actual runtime behavior of Python. While it's more work for your indexer, it provides a standard way to model what libraries like Pydantic or dataclasses already do with custom logic. Instead of needing a custom plugin for every library, you'd only need to implement the PEP 827 specification once.

yo. I'm maintaining an ORM-style library that's heavy on metaprogramming and Pydantic-like models. I'm looking at PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) and I'm a bit worried about stability. Is this proposal going to break my existing type annotations or runtime reflection logic?

In a strict sense, Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov designed PEP 827 to be backward compatible. It's a Standards Track proposal targeting Python 3.15, and the authors explicitly state it doesn't change Python's core grammar. Instead, it expands the grammar of what expressions are valid within a type annotation context. Your existing code shouldn't break because the PEP only adds new primitives to the typing module and introduces new syntactic forms like conditional types (`T if bool_typ else S`) and unpacked comprehensions (`*[... for t in Iter[...]]`). However, tools that manually parse annotations or rely on standard runtime extraction might need updates.

That makes sense. I'm specifically looking at the 'Update class' section. We use __init_subclass__ a lot to register models. If I start returning UpdateClass[*Ps] from __init_subclass__, does that change the actual class object at runtime in a way that would break standard attribute access or the MRO?

According to the specification for UpdateClass, it's a special form used specifically in the return type of a type decorator or __init_subclass__. When a class is declared and an ancestor has an __init_subclass__ returning UpdateClass, these updates are applied in reverse MRO order. While it 'updates' the nominal class with new members, the PEP describes this as a mechanism for the type system to track changes like adding, overriding, or removing members (by setting their type to Never). It doesn't propose changing how the MRO is calculated or how standard __getattr__ logic works at the bytecode level, though it intends to make these 'metaprogrammed' additions visible to static type checkers and IDEs without needing custom plugins.

Right, so it's bridging that gap. But let's talk about runtime. I use typing.get_type_hints() everywhere for validation. If I define a model using a conditional type like `type MyField = int if IsAssignable[T, str] else str`, what happens when my library calls get_type_hints() on a Python 3.15 runtime?

This is where things get interesting for framework maintainers. The PEP notes that while these expressions are 'inert' type operator applications, they will be automatically evaluated when the __annotate__ method is called. To prevent runtime failures when evaluating a conditional or a loop, the authors introduced a hook called `special_form_evaluator`. This is a ContextVar that holds a callable. If it's set to None (the default), boolean operators like IsAssignable return False and Iter evaluates to an empty iterator. To get the 'real' computed result at runtime, you'd likely need to use a third-party evaluator library that populates this ContextVar, as the PEP doesn't include an official evaluator in the standard library.

That sounds like a potential headache for compatibility. If a user of my library uses these new features but my library doesn't know about the 'special_form_evaluator' yet, will we just get silent, incorrect types (like the 'False' or 'empty' defaults you mentioned) during validation?

Exactly. If your code calls __annotate__ (or get_type_hints) without an evaluator active, the PEP specifies that boolean operators return False and typing.Iter returns an empty iterator. This means a conditional type would default to the 'else' branch and a comprehension would result in an empty tuple. The authors acknowledge this 'Backwards Compatibility' issue, noting that tools relying on introspecting annotations at runtime might struggle. They suggest that this could be mitigated if other proposals like PEP 649 (storing strings) or a potential 'Format.AST' for fetching annotations are implemented, allowing you to fetch the unevaluated expressions instead of the potentially 'half-evaluated' default values.

If I want to support these computed types properly, I'll need to deconstruct them. How does the PEP handle looking into a 'Member' object? If I'm iterating over Members[T], can I access the metadata like whether it's a 'Final' or 'ClassVar' attribute without hitting internal private APIs?

The PEP provides a formal way to do this via dot notation on the Member type. A Member object has associated type members: .name (Literal[str]), .type (the type), .quals (qualifiers), .init (the initializer type), and .definer (the defining class). Qualifiers are represented as a union of strings from MemberQuals, which includes 'ClassVar', 'Final', 'NotRequired', and 'ReadOnly'. For example, you can write `m.quals` to check if an attribute is Final. This is a first-class feature of the type language in PEP 827, designed specifically to avoid the 'Bracket City' of constant subscripting, although the PEP mentions that dot notation access is not lifted over union types.
Final question: my ORM does complex stuff with generics, like extracting the 'T' from 'List[T]'. Does PEP 827 make this standard or is it still a mess of __args__ hacking?

It aims to standardize this through the GetArg and GetArgs operators. You can use `GetArg[T, Base, Idx]` to get the specific type argument. For your example, `GetArg[MyList, list, Literal[0]]` would return the element type. This is much cleaner than __args__ because it handles the logic of interpreting T as the Base type (e.g., finding where 'list' appears in the inheritance chain). If the evaluation fails—say, the type doesn't inherit from that base—it returns Never. This provides a robust, spec-compliant way to perform the deconstruction your ORM needs, provided you're running within an environment that supports the PEP 827 evaluation model.

HEY!!! I just saw a thread on Twitter about PEP 827 and it sounds absolutely wild. Python is basically getting TypeScript's brain, right? But I saw someone complaining that it doesnt have that 'infer' keyword thing. What is that and why did we skip it? I'm just starting with Python so keep it simple for me!

It is super exciting! PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation,' is a draft proposal for Python 3.15 by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. It aims to let Python programmers build new types automatically, much like TypeScript does. In TypeScript, the 'infer' keyword is used for pattern matching—it lets the system 'guess' or extract a specific part of a type while you are checking it. The authors actually looked into adding it, but they decided it was a bit too 'gnarly' for Python's current setup.
Gnarly how? Is it just because Python's syntax is different? I keep hearing that TypeScript is 'smarter' than Python when it comes to types, so I figured we'd just copy their homework.

The 'homework' was definitely referenced, but the authors found that 'infer' is really hard to shoehorn into Python's grammar without making it messy. In TypeScript, you use a ternary-style syntax (like 'T extends Other ? True : False'), but Python uses 'true_type if bool_type else false_type'. Trying to bind new variables in the middle of that 'if' check is tricky for the computer to understand. Also, a big goal of PEP 827 is making sure these types work at 'runtime'—meaning they still work while your program is actually running, not just while you're writing code. Evaluating 'infer' at runtime would require some really complicated tricks with global variables that they wanted to avoid.
So if we dont have 'infer', how do we get pieces of a type? Like if I have a list of integers and I want to grab just the 'int' part, how does PEP 827 do it?

Great question! Instead of using a keyword like 'infer' to guess the part, PEP 827 uses more direct 'operators' from the typing module. For your list example, you would use 'GetArg'. You would write something like 'GetArg[MyListType, list, Literal[0]]'. This tells Python: 'Look at this type as a list and give me the 0th argument (the item type).' It is a bit more explicit than TypeScript's version, but the authors feel it fits Python's style better and is easier for tools like FastAPI and Pydantic to support. It basically trades a bit of 'magic' for more reliability!
That makes sense. It's like being more literal instead of letting the code play detective. I'm still pumped for 3.15, even if I have to wait a while!

The enthusiasm is definitely warranted! Even without 'infer', PEP 827 unlocks things like 'IsAssignable' to check types, 'Members' to see every attribute on a class, and even 'NewProtocol' to build whole new interfaces on the fly. It's a huge step forward for making Python feel as powerful as its more 'type-heavy' cousins while staying true to its own rules.

yo nanochat, have you seen this PEP 827 thing? It feels like we're just turning Python into TypeScript at this point. How are existing tools even supposed to read these crazy types that look like logic puzzles?

It is definitely a major shift in how we think about the type system. The authors—Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov—explicitly state in the Abstract that the design is inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types. However, they argue it is necessary because many frameworks currently rely on custom mypy plugins to handle metaprogramming, which leads to inconsistent typechecking across different IDEs. As for existing tools, the PEP admits that logic like 'if' and 'for' in annotations causes trouble.
