It suggests that tools might need to use a proposed 'Format.AST' mode to fetch unevaluated annotations or rely on a third-party evaluator library that the authors intend to release for runtime support.

Logic in brackets was already bad enough. Now we have actual loops like *[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]. That's not a type, that's a mini-program. In TypeScript, they have a dedicated syntax with 'extends' and 'infer'. Why is Python doing this halfway house with Python keywords like 'if' and 'for' inside type brackets?

The PEP authors actually addressed the TypeScript 'extends' syntax in their Rejected Ideas. They felt it was too difficult to shoehorn into Python's grammar without major changes. By using existing keywords like 'if', 'else', and 'for', they can broaden the annotation syntax without changing Python's core grammar. While TypeScript uses 'infer' to bind variables, PEP 827 uses operators like IsAssignable and GetArg. The big difference is that Python's version is designed to work with runtime evaluation. There is even a new hook called 'special_form_evaluator' in the typing module to help evaluator libraries trigger boolean checks and iterations when a class's __annotate__ method is called.

It still sounds like a nightmare for anyone writing a simple reflection tool. If I just want to know the fields of a class, I used to just check __annotations__. Now I have to run an entire type-evaluator just to get a list of names? How does this not break every existing tool that expects types to be simple objects?

You are right that the simplicity of just reading a dictionary is being challenged. In the Backwards Compatibility section, the authors acknowledge that tools wanting to evaluate these 'computed' types will need an evaluator. If a tool doesn't have one, it will just see the raw GenericAlias objects. To help with this, they introduce primitives like Members[T] and Attrs[T] to standardize how we inspect attributes and methods. The idea is that instead of every library like Pydantic or FastAPI inventing its own way to parse class bodies, they can all use these standard operators. It is more complex for the tool authors, but the goal is to reduce the amount of boilerplate users have to write, like having to manually define HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate classes in FastAPI.

Hi nanochat. I am studying the runtime evaluation mechanics in PEP 827. Can you explain the purpose of the special_form_evaluator ContextVar? It seems like a very specific design choice for bridging the gap between static types and Python's eager execution.

You are exactly right to spot that as a bridge. Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov proposed the special_form_evaluator ContextVar to allow third-party libraries to intercept type-level logic at runtime. Because PEP 827 introduces syntax like 'true_type if bool_type else false_type' and unpacked comprehensions using 'Iter[T]', these expressions are evaluated by the Python interpreter when a function or class's __annotate__ method is called. Without a hook, a standard Python runtime wouldn't know how to resolve whether IsAssignable[int, str] should be True or False; it would simply see a generic alias object. This ContextVar holds a callable that is triggered whenever __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsEquivalent or IsAssignable) or when __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. If the ContextVar is set, the evaluator can perform the actual type-system logic and return a result that Python's native control flow can use. If it is None, these operators default to False or empty iterators to avoid crashing. It is a pragmatic tradeoff to support tools like Pydantic or FastAPI that need to inspect these complex computed types at runtime without requiring a massive change to the Python interpreter's core execution loop.

I am looking at PEP 827. Adding ternary logic and comprehensions to the type system seems like a nightmare for the interpreter. How is this supposed to work at runtime without breaking __annotations__ or bloating the runtime?

That is a valid concern, especially given how CPython handles annotations. Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov address this in the "Runtime evaluation support" section. They aren't actually proposing a full evaluator for the standard library yet. Instead, they introduce a hook called typing.special_form_evaluator, which is a ContextVar. When the __annotate__ method of a class or function is called, it can trigger this hook for logic like typing.Iter or boolean operations. If the hook is not set, it defaults to inert behavior where boolean operators return False and Iter returns an empty iterator.

A ContextVar hook feels like we are asking for trouble with state management in the middle of a type evaluation. And what about the syntax? If I have a type like 'T if IsAssignable[T, int] else str', does the interpreter actually execute that 'if'?

Yes, it does. Because the PEP proposes no changes to the Python grammar, these use standard Python expressions. When the __annotate__ method is called, the expression is evaluated. For a conditional type like 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ', the result depends on whether bool_typ is equivalent to Literal[True]. To make this work at runtime, the boolean operator (like IsAssignable) needs to return a value that behaves correctly when __bool__ is called. The special_form_evaluator hook allows an evaluator library to intercept that __bool__ call and provide the correct truth value based on the type system's rules.
So we are relying on third-party libraries to make sense of the core type system at runtime? That sounds like it could lead to fragmented behavior between static checkers and the runtime.

The authors acknowledge that risk, but they argue it is better than the status quo. Currently, frameworks like FastAPI and Pydantic use custom logic or mypy plugins to handle complex transformations, which leads to a complete mismatch between static analysis and runtime reality. PEP 827 provides a structured language for these transformations. For tools that do not want to execute the code, the PEP mentions that a 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations would be a perfect complement, allowing tools to inspect the unevaluated logic as an abstract syntax tree rather than running it.

I've been reading through PEP 827 and I'm skeptical about this Overloaded operator. As a library maintainer, I already struggle with getting standard @overload signatures to behave in IDEs. Adding a way to programmatically construct them with Overloaded[*Callables] feels like it's just going to break every type checker in existence. Why was this even added?

The skepticism is fair. Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov proposed Overloaded specifically because static @overload declarations are insufficient for the kind of metaprogramming Python frameworks often do. While @overload works for code you write by hand, it doesn't help when you are using something like UpdateClass or NewProtocol to generate a type dynamically. If you're building a framework that generates a class with a method that behaves differently based on input types, you need a way to represent those multiple signatures as a single type object.
So it is just a container for multiple Callable types? If I'm using NewProtocol to define a generated interface, how do I actually use it? Does it just take a list of standard Callables?

Exactly. It takes a variadic number of callable types in order of precedence. In the context of PEP 827, these would ideally be the new extended callables. For example, if you were building an ORM select method that could return either a single object or a list, you might define it inside a Member like this: typing.Member[Literal['get'], Overloaded[Callable[Params[Param[Literal['id'], int]], Model], Callable[Params[Param[Literal['filter'], dict]], list[Model]]]] . The type checker would then treat that member as having multiple valid signatures, just like a standard overloaded function.

That looks incredibly verbose. And what about runtime? My library relies on Pydantic-style introspection to validate data at the boundaries. If I start using Overloaded, I'm worried I'll have to implement a full type-system evaluator just to figure out which signature to use for validation.

The PEP authors acknowledge that runtime evaluation is a major hurdle. They aren't adding a full evaluator to the standard library yet, but they are proposing a special_form_evaluator hook. This is a ContextVar that allows third-party libraries (like the one the authors plan to release) to intercept calls to things like typing.Iter or boolean operators. For Overloaded, the runtime object would just be a standard generic alias that stores the list of callables. You would still need logic to iterate through them and match the arguments, but PEP 827 provides the Extended Callables machinery (Params and Param) specifically to make that introspection easier than parsing raw strings or complex callback protocols.
It still feels like a lot of 'magic' for a Draft PEP. Is there any evidence that existing type checkers like mypy can even handle this?

Actually, the PEP mentions there is already a proof-of-concept implementation in mypy that can type check all the examples in the document, including the complex broadcasting and ORM ones. It uses a logic where 'stuck' type evaluations (like an Overloaded type waiting for a type variable to resolve) are handled invariantly. While it's targeting Python 3.15 and is still in Draft, the goal is to standardize what libraries are currently doing with fragile mypy plugins so that IDE support becomes more consistent, not less.

Hey! I am a big FastAPI user and I am constantly writing redundant Pydantic models for Create and Update schemas. I was reading about PEP 827 and the idea of 'lifting over unions' caught my eye. Why is that specific feature so important for deriving these models? Wouldn't a transformation just work automatically on a Union?

In PEP 827, 'lifting' is a core mechanism that ensures type operators behave correctly when they encounter a Union. Without it, transformations would often fail or produce 'Never'.

When an operation is lifted, the type system takes the cross product of the union elements for each argument, evaluates the operator for each tuple in that product, and then unions all those results back together. The PEP Authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) explain the logic using a Python-like implementation where results are collected via itertools.product and wrapped in a Union.

For someone building FastAPI models, this is critical for polymorphism. If you have a type like 'User | Admin' and you apply a helper like 'PartialTD' (which makes all fields NotRequired), lifting ensures the result is 'PartialTD[User] | PartialTD[Admin]'. Without lifting, the 'Attrs' or 'Members' operators would look at the Union itself, find no explicit annotations on the Union object, and likely return an empty set or an error. By distributing the operation over the members, PEP 827 allows your CRUD derivations to remain stable even when your input types are complex unions.

Looking at PEP 827, I'm trying to determine the expressiveness of its type-level algebra. Coming from a background where I'm used to Dependent Types or at least GHC's TypeLits, I'm curious about the 'kind' of string manipulation available. Specifically, does this proposal allow for the sort of template literal types we see in TypeScript for constructing or deconstructing string literal types?

In the current specification of PEP 827 (authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov), string literal manipulation is categorized under 'Potential Future Extensions' rather than the core specification. The PEP acknowledges the utility of TypeScript-style template literals for generating method names or transforming case, but it doesn't include them in the primary draft for Python 3.15.

However, the authors do outline a specific set of proposed operators for future inclusion: 
- Concat[S1, S2]: To concatenate two string literal types.
- Slice[S, Start, End]: To extract substrings.
- Case-folding operators: Uppercase, Lowercase, Capitalize, and Uncapitalize.

Currently, the core of the proposal focuses on object inspection via Members[T], Attrs[T], and conditional logic using the 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' syntax.

Interesting. If those string operators like Concat move from the 'Future Extensions' into the core, how do they interact with Union types? In OCaml or Haskell, I'd think of this as a distributive property. If I pass a Union of literals to Concat, does it produce a single Union of all permutations, or is it a type error?

The PEP specifies a behavior called 'Lifting over Unions' for these types of operators. If an operation like Concat is lifted, it takes the cross product of the union elements for each argument position. 

The PEP provides an explicit example for Concat:
Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['c'] | Literal['d']] results in Literal['ac'] | Literal['ad'] | Literal['bc'] | Literal['bd'].

At runtime, the evaluator logic described in the PEP would involve getting the union elements of each argument, evaluating the operator for each tuple in the cross product, and then joining the results back into a new Union. This distributive behavior ensures that the type-level computation remains consistent with the gradual typing model and avoids manual iteration over every possible combination in your type aliases.

yo nanocha. I've been looking over the Sullivan/Park/Selivanov draft for PEP 827. I'm trying to wrap my head around the structural type derivation. Specifically, if I want to implement a Pick utility to create a subset of a class, I'm assuming I just pass a TypedDict of names to NewProtocol, right? And Attrs[T] just gives me a list of strings?

Actually, it's a bit more structured than that. According to the PEP 827 specification, Attrs[T] doesn't return strings; it produces a tuple of Member types. Each Member object encapsulates the name, type, qualifiers (like ClassVar or Final), and even the initializer. As for NewProtocol, it doesn't take a TypedDict directly. It's defined as NewProtocol[*Ms: Member], meaning it expects a variadic sequence of Member objects. To derive a subset, you'd typically use an unpacked comprehension like *[m for m in Iter[Attrs[T]] if ...] to filter those Member objects and pass them into NewProtocol.

Wait, so I have to use typing.Iter? I can't just do a normal list comprehension inside the brackets? And if Attrs returns Member objects, how am I supposed to check the name of the attribute? I assume I'd need some GetName operator to pull the string out for my filter condition?

The PEP requires typing.Iter for the comprehension to be valid in a type context, as it allows the evaluator to trigger the proper hooks. Regarding the name access, you don't actually need a separate GetName operator. PEP 827 introduces type member access via dot notation for Member and Param types. So, if 'p' is your loop variable representing a Member, you can access its name as a literal string type using p.name. A Pick implementation would look like this: type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]. This uses IsAssignable to check if the member's literal name is part of the 'Keys' union you provided.

yo nanochat. been looking at this pep 827 thing. im deep in fastapi and pydantic and writing six versions of the same Hero model is killing me. can i just use that UpdateClass thing in a base model to fix this?

Hello! PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov would allow exactly that. You could define a Model base class where __init_subclass__ returns UpdateClass[*DerivedMembers]. This lets you inject or modify members, such as stripping primary keys for a Create model or adding a generated __init__ method, automatically during inheritance.
sounds too good to be true. what is the catch? if i have a bunch of models inheriting from each other or referring to each other, does it get messy?

There is a significant catch. The PEP authors admit there is a risk of evaluation-order dependence. According to the Open Issues section, if your UpdateClass logic for ClassA needs to inspect the Members of ClassB, and ClassB is also being modified by its own UpdateClass logic, the final type depends on which one the type checker evaluates first. The PEP notes this mirrors a potential runtime evaluation-order dependence, but it is problematic for static analysis.
so if my User model depends on Post and both are being dynamically updated by this type machinery, my IDE might just give up or show random types? that sounds like a dealbreaker for a framework.

It is definitely a known limitation. Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov state that ideally this kind of case would be rejected by the type checker to avoid inconsistency. However, they also mention that exactly what the evaluation rules should be when an operator is applied to an unresolved or stuck type is still somewhat unclear. Since the PEP is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, these recursive dependency issues are still being debated.
can i at least use it to generate generic methods that work for any model?

You can, but there are more constraints. The PEP introduces GenericCallable for representing methods with their own type variables, but the authors restricted its use specifically to the type argument of Member. They did this to avoid opening the can of worms that is rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism, which can make type inference undecidable.

Hey nanochat! I've been doing a lot of tensor work with NumPy and PyTorch lately, and shape errors are killing me. I saw some talk about PEP 827 bringing TypeScript-style type manipulation to Python. Does this mean we're finally getting 'full' TypeScript parity for things like broadcasting?

Hello! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, is definitely a huge step toward the kind of expressiveness you see in TypeScript. It introduces 'Conditional Types' and 'Type Operators' specifically to handle things like shape broadcasting that standard generics can't touch. However, it isn't a 1:1 clone of TypeScript. There are a few core TS features it intentionally leaves out or handles differently.

Interesting. I use TypeScript for my web frontends, so I'm used to stuff like 'Pick', 'Omit', and the 'infer' keyword for pattern matching. Does PEP 827 include that 'infer' logic? That would be amazing for pulling dimension sizes out of a shape tuple.

Actually, the 'infer' keyword is one of the things PEP 827 doesn't have right now. In TypeScript, you'd use 'T extends [infer El] ? El : never'. The authors considered this but found it hard to shoehorn into Python's syntax. Instead, PEP 827 uses explicit operators like 'GetArg' and 'Slice'. For example, if you wanted the last dimension of a shape, you'd use 'GetArg[T, tuple, Literal[-1]]'. It achieves the same goal but through a more functional operator style rather than pattern matching.
Okay, so no 'infer'. What about string manipulation? Sometimes I want to generate layer names or keys dynamically in my ML models. Does it have those template literal types?

Not in the current draft. String manipulation—like 'Uppercase', 'Concat', or slicing string literals—is listed under 'Potential Future Extensions'. For now, the focus is on object and tuple manipulation. You can use 'Members[T]' or 'Attrs[T]' to inspect class attributes, but you can't yet transform the string 'get_' + 'shape' into a 'get_shape' method name within the type system itself.

That's a bit of a bummer for my naming conventions, but I get it. You mentioned broadcasting earlier. How would I actually implement a 'Broadcast' helper for two ndarrays using this? Is it as clean as the TS version?

It's a bit more verbose because Python uses 'if/else' syntax and square brackets. In PEP 827, you'd define a 'Broadcast' alias that recursively checks dimensions. You'd use 'IsEquivalent' to see if two dimensions match or if one is 'Literal[1]'. If they don't match, you can use the 'RaiseError' operator to produce a custom type-level error message like 'Broadcast mismatch'. Here is how the core logic might look: 'type MergeOne[T, S] = (T if IsEquivalent[T, S] or IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]] else S if IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]] else RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S])'. You then use a comprehension like '*[... for t in Iter[...]]' to process the tuples.
Wow, 'RaiseError' actually lets me put the specific mismatch in the IDE? That's actually better than some of the hacky stuff I do in TS. Is this landing soon?

It is currently a 'Draft' targeting Python 3.15. The authors have a proof-of-concept in mypy, and since it aims to support runtime tools like Pydantic, it includes a 'special_form_evaluator' hook for runtime introspection. It's powerful, but since it's still in the proposal stage, the exact syntax for things like 'GenericCallable' or 'UpdateClass' might still shift.

Hey nanochat! I have been digging through the draft for PEP 827 because I do a lot of work with dataclass-like libraries. I noticed this thing called BaseTypedDict being introduced. Why do we need a specific BaseTypedDict instead of just using the regular TypedDict we already have?

In PEP 827, BaseTypedDict is introduced primarily to support a new mechanism for inferring type variables from keyword arguments. The authors, including Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, define it simply as:

class BaseTypedDict(typing.TypedDict):
    pass

Its main purpose is to serve as a bound for a type variable K. This allows you to write function signatures like 'def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K'. When you call that function, the type checker uses the keyword arguments to synthesize a specific TypedDict on the fly. This is a crucial prerequisite for the rest of the PEP because it provides a way to capture the names and types of dynamic arguments so they can be processed by type-level operators.

That makes sense for capturing inputs. But as someone interested in attrs/dataclasses, I'm more curious about the output. If I'm trying to generate an __init__ method for a class at the type level, how does capturing those kwargs via BaseTypedDict actually help me construct a new signature?

The synergy comes from combining that inference with the 'Extended Callables' and 'Object Inspection' features. Once you have a class, you use typing.Attrs[T] to get a tuple of Member types. For an __init__ method, you would use a type alias like InitFnType[T] to map those members into a sequence of Param objects.

For example, the PEP shows how you can iterate over those attributes using a comprehension: '*[typing.Param[p.name, p.type, ... ] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]'. Because the type system can now 'see' the names and types as literals, you can programmatically decide if a parameter should be keyword-only or have a default value based on the original class definition.

Okay, let's go deeper. In many libraries, we have a 'Field' object that holds metadata like 'default' or 'primary_key'. How does PEP 827 handle those? Specifically, if I have 'x: int = Field(default=0)', can the type system actually extract that '0' literal to decide if the generated __init__ param gets a 'default' qualifier?

Yes, that is exactly what the InitField[KwargDict] type is for. When you instantiate an InitField (or a subclass like a Pydantic Field) in a class body, the type checker is instructed to infer literal types for the arguments passed to it. 

This metadata is stored in the '.init' attribute of the Member type. You can then use a conditional type and the IsAssignable operator to check for these defaults. The PEP provides a helper example called GetDefault[Init] that looks like this:

type GetDefault[Init] = (
    GetFieldItem[Init, Literal["default"]]
    if typing.IsAssignable[Init, Field]
    else Init
)

In your __init__ generator, you would check 'if typing.IsAssignable[GetDefault[p.init], Never]'. If it is NOT Never, you know there is a default value, so you can apply the Literal["keyword", "default"] qualifier to that Param. This allows the static type checker to correctly identify which arguments are optional in the synthesized __init__ method.

I've been reading through PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. Coming from a background in OCaml and Haskell, this feels like a very ad-hoc way to implement higher-order type functions. It seems like it's adding a whole new grammar to Python just to support things like conditional types and iteration. Isn't this far too complicated for a language that's supposed to be 'simple'?

It is a significant leap in expressiveness for Python's type system, but there is an important distinction to make regarding the grammar. PEP 827 actually proposes zero changes to the Python grammar itself. Instead, it reuses existing Python expression syntax—like ternary operators (if/else) and comprehensions—within type annotations. The 'complexity' is handled by the type checkers and runtime evaluators interpreting these expressions as type-level operations.

The authors acknowledge that this is complex, but they argue it's necessary to bridge the gap between Python's highly dynamic metaprogramming (like what you see in SQLAlchemy or Pydantic) and the static type system. Currently, many libraries rely on custom mypy plugins or special-case hacks like PEP 681's @dataclass_transform because the type system can't model their behavior. PEP 827 provides a standard way to do this using primitives like IsAssignable, GetArg, and Members.

Okay, so no grammar change, but the logic still feels 'stuck' compared to a real kind system. For example, without TypeScript's 'infer' keyword for pattern matching, how am I supposed to actually deconstruct a type? If I want to pull 'T' out of 'list[T]', it feels like I'm just guessing with these GetArg operators.

You've touched on a specific design trade-off mentioned in the PEP. The authors did consider TypeScript-style 'infer' for pattern matching, but they found it too difficult to shoehorn into Python's existing syntax without major grammar changes. 

Instead, they introduced the GetArg[T, Base, Idx] operator. To get the 'T' out of a 'list[T]', you would use:

type ListArg[T] = typing.GetArg[T, list, Literal[0]]

While it might feel less elegant than pattern matching, it provides a deterministic way to deconstruct types based on their base class and argument index. It's 'less strictly-kinded' than TypeScript—meaning the type alias definition site isn't checked as strictly—but it provides the functional power needed for things like NumPy-style broadcasting or Prisma-style query result mapping.

I'm still skeptical about the runtime aspect. One of Python's strengths is runtime introspection. If we start doing complex type-level computation with *[p for p in Iter[Members[T]]], won't libraries like Pydantic just break because they can't evaluate these 'unpacked comprehensions' at runtime?

Actually, supporting runtime evaluation is a core goal of the PEP. This is why the proposal includes specific runtime hooks like 'special_form_evaluator', which is a ContextVar. When a library calls __annotate__ on a class or function, these operators can be evaluated dynamically. 

The PEP specifically targets use cases like FastAPI and Pydantic. For instance, the authors show how you could define a 'Create' model by iterating over the Members of a base class and filtering out primary keys:

type Create[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]]]
    ]
]

By providing a standard way to evaluate these at runtime, framework authors won't have to write fragile string-parsing code or custom plugins. It is a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so the community is still evaluating if the utility for framework authors outweighs the cognitive load for the average user.

Looking at the Object Creation section in PEP 827, I noticed it explicitly stops short of allowing the construction of new nominal classes or generic types. As someone concerned with CPython's runtime stability and the complexity of the type-hinting internals, I am curious why the authors decided to limit this to structural types like NewProtocol and NewTypedDict. Is it just a matter of keeping the 3.15 scope manageable?

You are correct that PEP 827 excludes nominal and generic type creation for now. According to the text by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, the omission of generic types is primarily to avoid 'cans of worms' like impredicative polymorphism and rank-N types, where generics are bound in deep nested positions. These features can make type inference undecidable, and the authors want to avoid that complexity in this initial proposal.

Regarding nominal classes, while the PEP includes them in the 'Potential Future Extensions' (specifically a NewProtocolWithBases form), they aren't in the current draft. The authors focus on structural types like NewProtocol because they are easier to model as collections of Member types without the heavy overhead of defining new nominal identities and their associated inheritance hierarchies during type-level computation.

That is a fair point on the 'undecidable' front. However, I see the UpdateClass operator is included. If we are avoiding the mess of creating nominal classes, why allow an operator that modifies existing ones? I am specifically worried about the evaluation-order dependence if __init_subclass__ uses UpdateClass to inspect Members of another class that is also being modified. Does the PEP address how we handle those race conditions in the type evaluator?

The authors acknowledge that UpdateClass is a sensitive addition. It is limited to the return types of type decorators or __init_subclass__ calls. In the 'Open Issues' section, the PEP explicitly identifies the 'type-evaluation-order dependence' you mentioned. If an UpdateClass return type inspects the Members of an unrelated class that also has an active __init_subclass__ modification, the results are order-dependent.

The PEP's current stance is that this mirrors existing runtime behavior in Python, where dynamic class modification already faces similar evaluation-order issues. While they suggest that ideally such cases would be rejected, they haven't proposed a formal mechanism for that yet. The goal for UpdateClass is to enable patterns like dataclass_ish method generation (e.g., automatically adding an __init__ method) without needing the full machinery required to create a brand new nominal class from scratch.

hello. i am a tech lead evaluating whether pep 827 is worth the headache for our backend team. specifically looking at these conditional types. adding 'if' and 'else' inside a type alias seems like a recipe for unmaintainable 'type-level' spaghetti code. why did dr. michael j. sully sullivan and his co-authors think this was a good idea?

it is a fair concern. dr. michael j. 'sully' sullivan, daniel w. park, and 'mad emperor' yury selivanov proposed pep 827 to bridge the gap between python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. currently, many frameworks like fastapi or prisma-style orms require custom mypy plugins because the standard type system cannot model their transformations. the 'if/else' syntax allows you to express these logic-driven types directly in python without external plugins. for example, you can define a type that changes based on whether a parameter is assignable to a specific class: 'type Convert[T] = T if IsAssignable[T, str] else list[T]'. it aims to provide the expressiveness of typescript's conditional types but adapted for python's semantics.

that sounds like it could get messy fast when unions are involved. if i have a union of five types and pass it to a conditional alias, does the type checker just give up? also, i am worried about the learning curve for the average dev on my team who just wants to write standard business logic.

pep 827 handles unions through a process called 'lifting'. when an operator or conditional is applied to a union, it takes the cross product of the union elements, evaluates the logic for each, and then unions the results together. regarding the learning curve, the pep authors expect that most 'standard' developers will just consume these types from libraries. the complexity of writing things like '*[p for p in Iter[Attrs[T]] if not IsAssignable[p.type, Hidden]]' is intended for framework maintainers. for your team, it might actually mean less boilerplate because they can use a single model and have 'HeroPublic' or 'HeroUpdate' derived automatically rather than manually maintaining four nearly identical classes.

okay, but what happens when someone makes a mistake in one of these aliases? debugging a normal python function is hard enough. if a 'type if boolean else type' logic fails, does the ide just show a cryptic error? and what about runtime? we rely on pydantic for validation.

to help with debugging, the pep introduces 'RaiseError[S, *Ts]', which allows a library author to trigger a specific type error with a custom message if a condition is not met. as for runtime, pep 827 is specifically designed to support tools like pydantic. it introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' hook using a contextvar in the typing module. this allows runtime libraries to trigger the evaluation of these 'if' and 'for' blocks when accessing annotations. while it is still in draft for python 3.15, the goal is to ensure that static checkers and runtime validators see the exact same computed type, which is a major improvement over the 'magic' strings and plugins we use today.

Hey nanochat, I'm a PL design student looking at PEP 827. I was reading about NewProtocolWithBases in the future extensions section. It looks like a classic 'nominal-meets-structural' hybrid. Why did Dr. Michael Sullivan and the other authors decide to defer it instead of including it in the initial draft?

You've got a good eye for the type theory there. In PEP 827, NewProtocolWithBases is defined as a form taking a tuple of base types and a variadic pack of Member objects: NewProtocolWithBases[Bases: tuple[type], *Ms: Member]. The idea is that a type would only satisfy this if it both extends all those specific nominal bases and possesses the required structural members.

As for why it's deferred, the authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—explicitly stated they are holding off because 'protocol-with-bases' would be a significant addition to the fundamental definition of what a Protocol is in Python. They didn't want to tangle with the core semantics of protocols yet, especially since they believe many of the target use cases, like generating Pydantic-style models, can be simulated through other operators already in the proposal.

That makes sense from a scope-creep perspective, but I'm curious about those 'simulations.' If I wanted to model something that looks like it needs a nominal base plus new members, is UpdateClass the preferred alternative for now? I noticed the PEP mentions some gnarly evaluation-order dependencies with that one.

Exactly. UpdateClass[*Ps: Member] is the primary tool for modifying nominal classes in this PEP. It's intended to be used in type decorators or __init_subclass__ to return a 'patch' for the class. However, as you noted, it's listed under Open Issues because it introduces evaluation-order dependence. 

If one class's UpdateClass logic inspects the Members of another class that also has a pending UpdateClass, the resulting type could change depending on which one the type checker processes first. This mirrors a real runtime problem in Python's dynamic meta-programming. For cases where you don't need a strict nominal inheritance check, the PEP suggests just using NewProtocol to build a purely structural interface from the members of a class using the Members[T] or Attrs[T] operators. It's a trade-off: NewProtocol is safer and more 'pure' in a structural sense, while UpdateClass handles the nominal requirements but brings along those evaluation-order headaches.

Hi nanochat. I've been looking at the PEP 827 spec for type manipulation. Coming from a background in OCaml and Haskell, I'm curious about the 'kind' system being used here. Specifically, when a type operator is applied to arguments that don't satisfy its bounds, why does the PEP often choose to return Never (the bottom type) rather than raising a hard type error? For instance, GetArg returns Never if it fails, but then we have a dedicated RaiseError operator.

That is a perceptive observation. In the current Draft of PEP 827, the general philosophy for the built-in operators is to favor returning Never when a type-level operation 'fails' or receives invalid arguments. For example, GetArg[T, Base, Idx] returns Never if the index is out of bounds or if T doesn't inherit from Base. Similarly, GetMember and GetMemberType return Never if the requested member name doesn't exist on the class. This effectively makes many of these type-level functions total by using Never as a failure sentinel.

However, the PEP authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) have actually listed this as an Open Issue. They are still debating which invalid operations should be errors versus which should return Never. The RaiseError[S, *Ts] operator is provided specifically so that framework authors can provide meaningful diagnostics. For instance, in the NumPy-style broadcasting example, the PEP uses a conditional to check if types match and calls RaiseError with a 'Broadcast mismatch' message if they don't. Without RaiseError, a mismatch would just propagate a Never, which can be difficult for a user to debug in a complex type-level computation.

There is also a section on 'strict-kinded' operations where the authors acknowledge that TypeScript has better typechecking at the alias definition site. Python's current approach in PEP 827 is more 'best-effort,' especially since it needs to support runtime evaluation where the evaluator might not have full access to the static type graph (like Protocol definitions).

Hey nanochat! I just spent the morning reading through PEP 827 and I am honestly hyped. I lead a team that manages a massive internal ORM, and we have been struggling with custom mypy plugins for years. Can you walk me through how the conditional 'if/else' type syntax works? Is it basically Python's ternary operator but for types?

It is great to see that energy! You are spot on—the syntax proposed by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov looks exactly like Python's value-level ternary expression: `true_type if boolean_type else false_type`. The 'boolean_type' part must be a 'type boolean,' which is a special category of type expressions that evaluate to `Literal[True]` or `Literal[False]` at check-time. This is huge for framework authors because it allows the type system to make decisions based on the properties of the types passed into your generics.
That is exactly what we need. So if I want to check if a type is a string to decide on a return type, how do I actually perform the check? Do I just use 'is' or something?

Since PEP 827 avoids changing Python's core grammar, you use specific type operators from the `typing` module to generate those booleans. The most common one you would use is `IsAssignable[T, S]`. For example, if you wanted a type that returns `int` if `T` is a string, and `list[int]` otherwise, you would write: `int if typing.IsAssignable[T, str] else list[int]`. There are other operators too, like `IsEquivalent[T, S]` and `Bool[T]`, which help you build logic without needing complex plugins.
This is going to save us so much time. One concern though: our users often pass in Unions. If someone passes 'str | int' into that logic, does it just fail or do I have to handle it manually?

The PEP has a built-in feature called 'Lifting over Unions' that handles this beautifully! When a type operator—including the conditional check—encounters a Union, it automatically takes the cross product of all union elements. It evaluates the logic for each piece of the Union individually and then unions all the results back together. So for your team, it means you can write a single rule and it will 'just work' for complex union inputs without extra boilerplate. It is targeting Python 3.15, so while it is still a Draft, it is definitely a feature to keep an eye on for your future architecture!

Hey nanochat! I was scanning through some of the newer type-level proposals and stumbled on PEP 827. Coming from a background where I think in terms of higher-order types and kinds, I'm curious if this is basically turning the Python annotation space into a real meta-language. What is the current status of this thing and which version is it targeting?

It is definitely moving in that direction! PEP 827, titled Type Manipulation, is currently in Draft status. It is targeting Python 3.15. The authors—Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov—explicitly mention that while Python has a fairly conventional static type system, they want to bridge the gap for complex metaprogramming using things like conditional and mapped types inspired by TypeScript.

Python 3.15 is a bit of a wait, but the primitives look interesting. I saw some talk about strictly-kinded checking in the rationale section. Is it giving us actual type-level branching and iteration? Like, can I write a type-level function that filters members of a record?

Yes, exactly. The PEP introduces type-level conditionals and unpacked comprehensions. For example, you can write a type like `true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ`. The iteration happens via the Iter operator and syntax like `*[some_type for t in Iter[some_tuple]]`. You can use the Members[T] or Attrs[T] operators to get a list of members, then filter them using an 'if' clause in the comprehension to build a NewProtocol or NewTypedDict. It effectively lets you perform structural transformations at the type level.

That sounds very powerful, almost like a restricted form of type-level programming in Haskell. Who are the brains behind this? I noticed the name Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov, which is a fantastic title.

The proposal is a joint effort by Dr. Michael J. Sully Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and indeed, Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov. They've designed it to handle things like Prisma-style ORM return types and automatically deriving FastAPI CRUD models, where the output type is a dynamically computed subset of a database model. Since it's still a Draft, they are also looking at potential future extensions like string manipulation (Uppercase, Lowercase, Concat) to allow generating method names dynamically based on attribute names.

Yoooo! I just stumbled onto PEP 827 by Sully, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov. This Type Manipulation stuff looks like magic for anyone who uses dataclasses or attrs. Are you telling me we can finally generate __init__ signatures at the type level without writing custom mypy plugins?

It is definitely a huge shift! Under PEP 827, you can use the NewProtocol and UpdateClass operators to programmatically construct those signatures. Instead of the 'lowest-common denominator' support we got with PEP 681 (dataclass_transform), a library author can actually iterate over a class's members using typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] and then build a Callable type for __init__ that exactly matches the fields, defaults and all.

That is so sick. I'm tired of my IDE telling me 'Unexpected argument' when I'm doing complex metaprogramming. But honestly, I'm a bit worried—if we start putting 'if' statements and 'for' loops inside our type annotations, aren't the error messages going to become totally unreadable? Like, if I pass a string where an int should be in a generated __init__, will it just give me a 100-line trace of the type-level computation failing?

That is a very valid concern when you're doing 'Type-Level Programming.' However, the authors actually included a specific operator to help with exactly that: RaiseError[S, *Ts]. If a type-level computation hits a state that shouldn't happen—like a broadcasting mismatch in NumPy-style arrays or a bad field configuration in a dataclass—you can return RaiseError with a custom literal string message. The type checker then displays that specific message as the error.

Oh, so I can actually control the error message? That's way better than just getting a generic 'Type X is not assignable to Type Y' when the logic gets deep. How would that look in an __init__ generator? Could I tell the user specifically which field is blowing up?

Exactly! You could use a conditional type inside your loop. For example, if a field is marked as both 'Required' and 'Default' in a way that doesn't make sense, your type alias could resolve to something like typing.RaiseError[Literal['Field cannot be both required and have a default'], p.name]. It makes the 'type program' much more debuggable for the end user, even if the logic under the hood is complex. It's still in Draft for Python 3.15, but it's designed specifically to make these framework-level 'magic' behaviors transparent to the type system.

yo nanochat! i was reading about this pep 827 thing. i am a huge fan of dataclasses and attrs, and i heard it might make generating __init__ methods easier at the type level. is that true or did i hallucinate that?

You definitely didn't hallucinate it! I'm nanochat, and I've been looking into PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov. It's specifically designed to handle cases like dataclasses without needing custom typechecker plugins. In the PEP, they show an example of an `InitFnType[T]` alias. It uses `typing.Attrs[T]` to grab all the attributes of a class and then uses a type-level comprehension to build a `Callable` with `Param` objects for each field. You can even use `UpdateClass` in a decorator or `__init_subclass__` to tell the type system: 'Hey, this class now has this specific __init__ signature.'

that sounds like literal magic. but honestly, if i start putting loops and 'if' statements inside my type hints, won't that slow down mypy or pyright like crazy? i'm already waiting long enough for my ci to pass.

That is a very reasonable worry. Adding things like `*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]` and conditional types (`T if bool_typ else S`) definitely adds a new layer of computation for the typechecker. However, the authors argue that we are already doing this work today, just in a fragmented way. Right now, many libraries use PEP 681 (`@dataclass_transform`) or custom plugins to hack these features in. Those plugins are essentially mini-programs anyway. PEP 827 standardizes these 'mechanical' operations. Since it's currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, there is a proof-of-concept in mypy that the authors say can typecheck all the examples in the proposal. It might be more intensive than simple static hints, but the goal is to make it consistent across all tools so you don't need a different plugin for every IDE.
i guess that makes sense if it's replacing plugins. can it handle defaults? like if my dataclass field has a default value, will the generated __init__ know it's optional?

It can! The PEP introduces a `Member` type that tracks an `init` field. If you use something like `InitField` (or a subclass like Pydantic's `Field`), the `Member` object captures that initializer info. In the PEP's `InitFnType` example, they use a helper called `GetDefault` and a conditional: `Literal["keyword"] if typing.IsAssignable[GetDefault[p.init], Never] else Literal["keyword", "default"]`. This allows the type system to decide if a parameter in the generated `__init__` should have a default or not based on whether the original class attribute had one.

Hi nanochat. I'm a TypeScript dev moving into Python and I'm looking at PEP 827. In TS, I'd use mapped types like `{ [K in keyof T]?: T[K] }` to create an 'Update' model for FastAPI. Can I just use that syntax in Python 3.14?

Welcome to Python! To clear up a few points first: PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, not 3.14. Also, it doesn't use the TypeScript brace-style mapped type syntax. Instead, it introduces 'unpacked comprehensions' and new operators in the typing module. To transform a type, you'd use something like `typing.NewProtocol[*[... for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]]`. It looks more like a Python list comprehension inside the type brackets.

Okay, 3.15 then. If I wanted to build a 'Public' model that strips out hidden fields, I'd probably use something like `T extends { hidden: true } ? never : T` in a conditional type, right? Does Python use the 'extends' keyword for that now?

Actually, PEP 827 doesn't use 'extends'. It adapts Python's ternary syntax for conditional types: `true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ`. For your 'Public' model, you wouldn't check the type itself with extends; you'd iterate over the members of the class using `typing.Members[T]` and check each member's qualifiers or attributes. For example, you can use `typing.IsAssignable[T, S]` to see if a type is consistent with another, which is the equivalent of the 'extends' check in TypeScript.
That's a bit different. How would I actually implement the FastAPI 'Create' model to skip the primary key? FastAPI uses `Field(primary_key=True)` usually. Can the type system even see that?

Yes, that is a core part of the proposal. PEP 827 introduces `InitField[KwargDict]` to capture arguments passed to field descriptors like `Field()`. These are exposed via the `.init` attribute of a `Member` type. For a FastAPI 'Create' model, the implementation looks like this: `type Create[T] = typing.NewProtocol[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals, GetDefault[p.init]] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]]]]]`. This loop skips any attribute where the initializer has `primary_key=True`.
So `p.name` and `p.type` are just dot-access on the member? That's actually cleaner than I expected. Does this work for making everything optional for an 'Update' model too, like TS's Partial?

Exactly. The PEP provides an implementation for a `Partial[T]` utility type that handles this. It creates a `NewProtocol` where each member's type is transformed to `p.type | None`. For TypedDicts specifically, you could use `typing.NewTypedDict` and modify the qualifiers to include `Literal["NotRequired"]`. This gives you the same power as TypeScript's Utility Types but tailored to Python's structural and nominal typing rules.

I am so incredibly hyped for PEP 827! This feels like the missing link for Python metaprogramming. I've been looking at how it compares to things like PEP 681's dataclass_transform and PEP 646's variadics, and it seems way more flexible. I noticed something in the spec though: why is GenericCallable restricted only to Member type arguments? Is that just to keep it simple, or is there a bigger reason?

It is great to see that enthusiasm! PEP 827 is definitely aiming to move past the 'special-case' solutions like PEP 681 by providing generic primitives. Regarding GenericCallable, the authors (Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov) are being very intentional about that restriction. The primary reason is to avoid 'cans of worms' like impredicative polymorphism and rank-N types. If you could use GenericCallable anywhere—like in parameter types or return types—you end up with situations where type inference becomes potentially undecidable. By limiting it to the type argument of Member, they can represent generic methods for a NewProtocol without opening up the complexity of full rank-N type systems.

That makes total sense, undecidability is a scary cliff to jump off. I also saw the syntax uses a lambda: GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty]. Is that because of how PEP 827 handles runtime evaluation? I'm curious if this interacts with the stuff discussed in the PEP 649 draft for Format.AST.

Exactly! The lambda is a clever trick for runtime support. When you evaluate a Member on a class with generic functions, or a NewProtocol alias, evaluating the body immediately would cause you to lose one side of a conditional type or struggle with unbound variables. The lambda delays that evaluation. And you're spot on about the interaction with PEP 649; the PEP 827 authors mention that an AST-based format for fetching annotations would be a perfect companion to this proposal. It would make it much easier for tools to extract these complex type-level computations without the hurdles of runtime function execution.
This is going to make Prisma-style ORMs and FastAPI-style models so much cleaner to type. I can't wait for 3.15!

It really is a game changer for frameworks. Being able to use things like Attrs[T] and NewTypedDict to programmatically derive HeroCreate or HeroUpdate models—like the example in the PEP—will eliminate massive amounts of boilerplate. It's currently in Draft status for Python 3.15, so there's still plenty of discussion to be had, but the proof-of-concept in mypy is already showing it can handle those complex recursive 'Broadcast' and 'Pick' operations!

Hey nanochat. I was looking through PEP 827 and saw this 'UpdateClass' thing for modifying nominal classes. I'm a senior dev used to mypy/pyright, but this seems like a massive shift. How does it actually look in code, specifically with __init_subclass__?
Interesting. So if I have a complex hierarchy where multiple parents are trying to use UpdateClass to inject methods, how does the type checker decide the final shape of the child class?

The PEP defines a specific order for this. If a class has multiple ancestors with an '__init_subclass__' that returns an 'UpdateClass' type, the transformations are applied in reverse MRO (Method Resolution Order). 

This means the type checker starts with the 'most base' class and works its way down to the 'most derived' class. Each step can override or modify the members provided by the previous one. If 'cls' is parameterized by 'type[T]', the checker substitutes the specific class type into 'T' during this evaluation.
That seems logical, but I caught a mention in the 'Open Issues' about evaluation-order dependence. If we follow the MRO, shouldn't it be deterministic? Why is that listed as a problem?

The MRO handles the vertical hierarchy, but the 'evaluation-order dependence' issue refers to horizontal dependencies between unrelated classes. 

Imagine Class A's 'UpdateClass' logic uses the 'Members[B]' operator to inspect Class B. If Class B also has an '__init_subclass__' that returns an 'UpdateClass', the results for Class A depend on whether the type checker has already 'finished' updating Class B. 

If the checker processes A before B is updated, A sees B's original members. If it processes B first, A sees the modified members. Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov (the 'Mad Emperor') noted that while this mirrors potential runtime dependencies, it's a 'can of worms' for static analysis. Ideally, the PEP suggests these cases should be rejected to ensure consistent type checking across different environments.

hello, trying to wrap my head around a new PEP. I'm a big user of dataclasses and attrs, and I keep hearing that PEP 827 might change how we handle those generated methods at the type level. What's the main gap it is trying to bridge?

The main gap is that Python's type system currently struggles to model metaprogramming. In libraries like dataclasses or attrs, methods like __init__ are generated dynamically at runtime, but the type system can't 'calculate' those signatures on the fly. To fix this, you usually need custom IDE plugins or special decorators like @dataclass_transform (PEP 681).

PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation', proposes adding TypeScript-style facilities like conditional and mapped types. This allows the type system itself to inspect a class's attributes and construct a new type (like a specific __init__ method) using logic defined directly in Python's typing module.

That sounds interesting. I also saw something about Prisma-style ORMs in the draft. How does this help with database queries? In my experience, those are always the hardest to type correctly without manual boilerplate.

The Prisma example in the PEP is a great showcase. Imagine a db.select() call where you pass arguments like name=True and email=True. Currently, the return type would likely just be 'User' or 'Any', even if you only fetched two fields. 

With PEP 827, the ORM can use 'Unpack' on a TypedDict for the keyword arguments and then use a type-level comprehension to build a result type. It uses 'NewProtocol' to create a structural type on the fly that only contains the fields you actually selected. It can even recursively transform fields—like turning a database 'Link' into a 'list' of nested objects—using conditional logic like: 

list[Tgt] if typing.IsAssignable[LinkTy, MultiLink] else Tgt

Wait, so I can actually write logic inside the type hints? Can you show me how I'd use this to generate a constructor for a class based on its attributes? That's what I do with dataclasses all the time.

Exactly. You would use the 'Members' or 'Attrs' operators to inspect the class. Here is a simplified version of the 'InitFnType' logic from the PEP:

type InitFnType[T] = typing.Member[
    Literal['__init__'],
    Callable[
        [
            typing.Param[Literal['self'], Self],
            *[
                typing.Param[
                    p.name,
                    p.type,
                    Literal['keyword', 'default'] if typing.IsAssignable[p.init, object] else Literal['keyword']
                ]
                for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
            ]
        ],
        None
    ],
    Literal['ClassVar']
]

This uses an 'unpacked comprehension' (*[... for p in Iter[...]]) to iterate over every attribute (p) of class T. It builds a 'Param' for each one, determining if it should have a default based on the 'init' field of the member. Then you use 'UpdateClass' in a decorator or __init_subclass__ to apply that new __init__ to the actual class.
That is a lot of new syntax! I see 'IsAssignable' and 'Iter'. Is this actually coming to Python soon? And who is behind this proposal?

It is definitely a significant jump in complexity. The authors are Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. 

As for the timeline, PEP 827 is currently in 'Draft' status and is targeting Python 3.15. Because it introduces new type-level operators and the ability to perform evaluation at runtime (via a proposed 'special_form_evaluator' hook), there's a lot of work to be done. But if it lands, it would mean framework authors could provide perfectly typed APIs without needing any typechecker plugins.

Hey nanochat. I'm looking at PEP 827 for our Python 3.15 roadmap. Honestly, this looks like a maintenance nightmare. If we start putting 'if/else' and 'for' loops inside type hints, won't that just break every runtime introspection tool we have? How does it even work with the __annotate__ method?

That is a very reasonable concern for a tech lead. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, acknowledges that these new syntactic forms--conditional types and unpacked comprehensions--are 'automatically evaluated' when the __annotate__ method of a class, alias, or function is called. Instead of being purely static strings, these expressions become part of the execution flow during annotation lookups.

That is exactly what worries me. If __annotate__ is now executing loops over class members via typing.Iter, what is the performance hit? And what happens if the environment isn't ready to handle these complex operators? I saw something about a ContextVar hook in the spec.

The PEP introduces a hook called 'special_form_evaluator'. This is a ContextVar that holds a callable. When __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or __iter__ is called on typing.Iter, this hook is invoked. If the hook is None, which is the default, the boolean operators return False and Iter evaluates to an empty iterator. This allows the system to remain 'inert' unless a specific evaluator library--which the authors plan to release as a third-party tool--is active. As for performance, the goal is to replace complex manual boilerplate or slow mypy plugins with these mechanical, type-level operations.

So if I'm building a tool that needs to see the types but *doesn't* want to execute them, am I just out of luck? I don't want to have to run a full evaluator just to check a field name. Is there a way to get the raw, unevaluated logic?

The authors suggest two main paths for that. First, if 'from __future__ import annotations' is used, the annotations stay as strings and can be parsed with 'ast.parse'. Second, they point to a separate PEP draft for a 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations, which would allow tools to get the AST directly without triggering the __annotate__ function's execution. They actually considered using simpler operator forms like 'Cond[tb, tt, tf]' to avoid this runtime complexity, but they rejected that idea because the syntax felt 'a lot worse' for human readers. They are betting that the Pythonic 'if/else' and 'for' syntax is worth the extra lifting for tooling maintainers.

Hello! I am nanochat. I'm definitely well-versed in PEP 827 (Type Manipulation). It is a pretty ambitious proposal for Python 3.15, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. It brings a lot of TypeScript-style power to Python, like conditional types and mapped types. What specifically caught your eye in the draft?

the whole 'GenericCallable' thing. i was looking at how it's used to construct methods in a NewProtocol. it uses a lambda, which is kind of wild for a type annotation. but i noticed the PEP says it's strictly limited to being a type argument for 'Member'. why the restriction? shouldn't a generic callable be usable anywhere?

That is a very sharp observation for a language design student! You're right—GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty] is currently gated. The authors decided to restrict it to the 'Member' operator (used inside NewProtocol or NewTypedDict) to avoid some classic type theory nightmares.
nightmares as in inference problems? i'm guessing something about higher-rank types?

Exactly. The PEP refers to this as a 'can of worms.' If you allow GenericCallable to appear anywhere—like as a parameter to another function or nested deep inside other types—you run into 'impredicative polymorphism' and 'rank-N types.' In many cases, performing type inference in those systems becomes undecidable. By limiting it to 'Member' definitions, the type checker only has to deal with these generics when they are bound to a specific method of a protocol, rather than trying to infer them in arbitrary local variables or return types. It's a pragmatic trade-off to keep the type system manageable while still allowing frameworks to programmatically 'generate' methods that are generic.
makes sense. so the lambda is basically just a way to delay evaluation so the generic variables don't get 'lost' during the introspection of the class members?

Spot on. Without the lambda, if you evaluated 'Members[T]' on a class with generic methods, the evaluation might lose the relationship between the type variables and the method body, or try to evaluate a conditional type before the variable is actually bound. The lambda wraps the body so that 'typing' can inspect the structure of the generic without collapsing it immediately. It's a clever way to bridge the gap between static analysis and runtime evaluation, which is a big goal for Sully, Daniel, and Yury in this PEP.

I'm looking at PEP 827 because I'm sick of writing four different versions of every Pydantic model for FastAPI (Hero, HeroCreate, HeroUpdate, etc). I heard this PEP brings TypeScript's Utility types to Python so I can just do something like 'type HeroCreate = Omit[Hero, Literal['id']]'. Is that how it works?

That is exactly the kind of use case the authors, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, intended to solve. PEP 827 is heavily inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types, specifically to bridge the gap between Python's metaprogramming and its type system. While it does not add 'Omit' as a hardcoded keyword in the grammar, it provides the primitives like 'NewProtocol', 'Members', and 'Iter' so that you (or the FastAPI framework) can define a 'Pick' or 'Omit' alias yourself. The PEP even includes a specific example of how to implement those TypeScript-style utility types using these new operators.

Wait, I have to build the operators myself? I thought it was just adding the '&' operator for intersections like in TS. If it's just a draft for Python 3.15, maybe I can just use a mypy plugin for now?

Actually, the PEP authors explicitly mention that the goal is to avoid custom plugins. They note that plugins are hard to maintain across different IDEs and tools. Instead, PEP 827 adds type-level operators like 'IsAssignable' and 'NewProtocol' to the 'typing' module. Regarding the '&' operator, while the proposal mentions it was a top request in the '2025 Typed Python Survey', this specific PEP focuses on providing the programmatic machinery to construct types. You wouldn't just use '&'; you would use a syntax like 'NewProtocol[*[p for p in Iter[Members[T]] if ...]]' to filter or modify class members dynamically.
So if I use 'NewProtocol', it generates a real class for Pydantic to validate at runtime? I need it to actually strip extra fields from the JSON input.

There is a slight distinction there. PEP 827 is clear that 'NewProtocol' creates a structural protocol, not a new nominal class. It explicitly states that they are not yet proposing a way to create nominal classes or new generic types. However, the PEP includes a section on 'Runtime evaluation support'. It introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' hook using a ContextVar. This allows third-party libraries like Pydantic to evaluate these computed types at runtime and use them for validation and data stripping, even though they aren't standard 'class' objects.

Hi nanochat. I am coming from a heavy TypeScript background and I am finding Python's typing system incredibly rigid. I heard about PEP 827, but I am skeptical. Can it actually handle something like TypeScript's Pick or Omit utility types, or is it just more syntactic sugar for the same old limited system?

It is definitely more than just sugar. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, aims to bring precisely that kind of TypeScript-style power to Python. It introduces type-level introspection and construction via operators like Members, IsAssignable, and NewProtocol. For example, you can implement Pick by iterating over a class's members and checking their names against a union of keys. It looks like this: type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]. This uses an unpacked comprehension to build a new structural protocol on the fly.

Wait, that syntax is actually kind of wild. 'typing.Iter' and 'p.name'? So you're telling me I can treat types as objects with properties? And what about 'keyof'? In TS, 'keyof T' is bread and butter. How do you get a union of all attribute names as string literals in Python without manually typing them out?

Exactly. The PEP introduces 'Type member access' via dot notation for Member and Param types. To implement KeyOf[T], you use the Union operator combined with a comprehension over the members. Here is how the PEP suggests implementing it: type KeyOf[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]. The p.name expression returns the name of the attribute as a Literal[str]. It is quite powerful because it lets you keep your types in sync with your class definitions automatically.

Okay, that addresses the mapping part, but what about 'Partial'? In TS, I just do Partial<User> and suddenly every field is optional. Python has TypedDict and Classes which are quite different. Making a nominal class 'partial' sounds impossible because of how inheritance works in Python.

You are right that Python's nominal system is a hurdle, which is why PEP 827 leans into 'NewProtocol' and 'NewTypedDict' for construction. To create a Partial utility, you would iterate over the attributes and wrap their types in a Union with None. For a protocol-based version, you could write: type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]]. This creates a new structural type where every attribute found in T is now allowed to be None. If you are working specifically with TypedDicts, there is even support for manipulating qualifiers like 'NotRequired' by doing p.quals | Literal['NotRequired'].

I'm starting to see the vision, but I'm still worried about runtime. One thing that makes Python typing frustrating is that Pydantic or FastAPI often need to see these types at runtime to do validation. Does PEP 827 just disappear after the type checker runs, or can I actually use these computed types to drive logic?

That is a core motivation of the PEP. The authors specifically mention that libraries like FastAPI and Pydantic currently rely on custom plugins or redundant class definitions (like HeroCreate vs HeroUpdate). PEP 827 includes 'Runtime evaluation support' via a special_form_evaluator hook in the typing module. This allows a third-party library to evaluate these conditionals and comprehensions at runtime when __annotate__ is called. So, you could define a Create[T] alias that strips primary keys and use it as a real return type in a FastAPI route. Just keep in mind this is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so it is not in the standard library yet.

I was looking at this PEP 827 thing. It feels like we are just turning Python into a worse version of TypeScript. Do we really need stuff like IsAssignable? It seems way too complex for a language that's supposed to be readable.

I understand the skepticism. The proposal by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov is a major shift. The goal of PEP 827 is to allow the type system to model the kind of complex metaprogramming that is already common in Python libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI. The authors chose IsAssignable specifically to provide a 'consistent subtype' check that works across both static type checkers and runtime frameworks.

But IsAssignable sounds like an implementation nightmare. Why didn't they just go with something simpler, like a basic check that doesn't try to be so smart? I bet it can't even handle protocols properly at runtime.

You've pinpointed a specific limitation mentioned in the PEP. The authors actually considered a weaker primitive called 'IsAssignableSimilar' which would have done a simple 'head' check of types without looking at type parameters or protocols. However, they rejected it because they didn't want to introduce a new notion that is 'similar to but not the same as' subtyping, which they felt would be misleading for developers.
So they went with the complex version that might not even work? If I use NewProtocol to generate types dynamically, can this system actually prove those types are safe?

The PEP is honest about the limits here. It admits that full Python typing assignability checking is not fully implementable at runtime. For example, checking against protocols often fails at runtime because class attributes might be inferred and have no visible presence. Consequently, any runtime evaluator for PEP 827 will have to be 'best effort.' If the evaluator encounters a situation it can't resolve—like a type operator applied to an unresolved type variable—the evaluation may become 'stuck' and the behavior is still being refined in the reference implementation.
That sounds like a recipe for inconsistent behavior between my IDE and my actual production code. Is there any part of this that isn't just a best-effort guess?

The core syntactic features, like conditional types using 'if/else' and unpacked comprehensions using 'Iter', are meant to be predictable. However, the PEP authors acknowledge that tools wanting to extract unevaluated annotations will face challenges. Unless something like the 'Format.AST' mode is adopted, tools might have to reparse source code to understand these complex type manipulations. It is still a Draft for Python 3.15, so these concerns about consistency between static and runtime environments are part of the ongoing discussion.

Hey nanochat! I have been reading through the PEP 827 draft by Dr. Michael Sullivan and the team. It is basically the Type-Level Programming update I have been waiting for. I am really interested in the introspection operators. What is the specific utility of GetSpecialAttr? Is it intended as a way to lift nominal class metadata into the type-level string domain for further computation?

It is exactly that! Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov included GetSpecialAttr to bridge that gap. It lets you extract constant metadata from a class--specifically __name__, __module__, and __qualname__--and returns it as a Literal[str]. This is huge because it allows your type-level logic to be aware of the 'identity' of the types it is processing, which is perfect for things like auto-generating specific error messages or class-related strings via type manipulation.

That is brilliant. Coming from a background where I think about kinds and higher-order type transformations, having those strings reified in Python is a game changer. I noticed the PEP mentions it works for non-class types too? Like, what happens if I apply it to a literal or a primitive? Does it break the kind-checking?

The PEP defines a really consistent behavior there to avoid breaking things. For non-class types, the rule is that if an object x has type T, GetSpecialAttr[T, Attr] should produce the value you would get from type(x).<attr>. For example, if you ran GetSpecialAttr[Literal[1], '__name__'], the result would evaluate to Literal['int']. It ensures that even when you are working with singleton types or literals, you can still reflect back to the underlying implementation type's metadata for your computations.

Hey there. I've been struggling with tensor shapes in PyTorch and NumPy for years. I saw PEP 827 mentioned as a way to finally get broadcasting logic into the type system. But I'm confused about how it actually works at runtime. Is it just another way to look at __annotations__? I'm used to how Pydantic or FastAPI parses types, but people are talking about this new __annotate__ method instead.

The relationship between PEP 827 and __annotate__ is actually quite central to how these new 'Type Manipulation' facilities work. Unlike standard annotations that just sit there as static metadata, PEP 827 introduces logic—like conditionals (if/else) and loops (Iter)—that needs to be executed. The PEP authors, including Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and Yury Selivanov, specify that these expressions will be automatically evaluated when the __annotate__ method of a class, alias, or function is called. It is a step beyond Pydantic's current model because the type system itself becomes 'computational' rather than just a collection of labels.

So if I write a recursive 'Broadcast' helper for my ndarray shapes, it's not just a string representation? In TypeScript, I can do recursive conditional types to match dimensions. Is PEP 827 just copying that, or is it doing something different because Python is a runtime-heavy language?

It is heavily inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types, but it's adapted for Python's architecture. While TypeScript's types disappear at runtime, PEP 827 ensures that a framework can actually run the logic. For example, if you define a Broadcast[T, S] alias using Slice, Length, and GetArg, the __annotate__ method will perform that recursion. To make this work at runtime without breaking existing code, the PEP introduces a ContextVar called special_form_evaluator. This hook allows a runtime evaluator to intercept things like typing.Iter or boolean operators (like IsAssignable) and return actual values. This is much more dynamic than PEP 563's stringized annotations.

That sounds powerful, but also risky. If I compare it to the current way we handle shapes—like using TypeVarTuple from PEP 646—how does 827 handle mismatches? If I try to broadcast a (4, 2) array with a (3,) array, does __annotate__ just crash, or do I get a nice error like I would in a C++ template or a TS build?

It provides a specific operator for that called RaiseError. In the NumPy-style broadcasting example provided in the PEP, the authors use a helper called MergeOne. If the types don't match (for instance, if they aren't equivalent and neither is a Literal[1]), it resolves to typing.RaiseError[Literal['Broadcast mismatch'], T, S]. When a type checker or a runtime evaluator hits that RaiseError type during the evaluation of __annotate__, it triggers a formal type error with your custom message. It's a way to bake the validation logic directly into the type definition itself, rather than relying on external runtime checks.

I'm evaluating PEP 827 for our team's Python 3.15 roadmap. We currently use a lot of hand-rolled TypedDicts for our API responses to ensure we don't leak internal database fields. I saw Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the other authors mention a pattern using NewProtocol and Attrs to derive subset types automatically - sort of like TypeScript's Pick. Could you walk me through how that actually looks in code?

Certainly. To implement a Pick[T, Keys] utility using the proposed PEP 827 machinery, you combine the Attrs[T] operator with NewProtocol. Attrs[T] produces a tuple of Member types for every annotated attribute in the class T. You then use an unpacked comprehension to filter them based on the provided keys. The implementation looks like this: `type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]`. This creates a new structural protocol containing only the members whose names match the Keys literal.

That p variable in the comprehension - how much metadata does it actually carry? I noticed the PEP mentions dot notation like p.type or p.quals.
