While PEP 681 is essentially a hint to type checkers to 'pretend' a class is a dataclass, PEP 827 actually provides the machinery to construct those signatures manually in the type system. The 'type member access' you mentioned refers to how we introspect classes. When you use an operator like typing.Members[T] or typing.Attrs[T], you get a tuple of Member types. Each Member has associated type members you can access with dot notation: .name, .type, .quals, .init, and .definer. This allows you to 'pull apart' a class and use its pieces to build something else, like an __init__ method.
So it's like TypeScript's indexed access types? In TS I'd do T[K] to get a property type. Is p.type just the Python version of that?

It's similar in spirit but structured differently to fit Python's grammar. In PEP 827, 'p.type' specifically works on Member or Param types within type-level computations, like a comprehension. For example, if you are iterating over attributes to build an __init__, you'd write something like 'typing.Param[p.name, p.type]' inside the loop. This uses the dot notation to grab the literal name and the actual type of that attribute. Unlike TS, where you might use keyof, here you are operating on a full Member object that carries all the metadata at once.
Okay, but one big thing in attrs is handling defaults and metadata. PEP 681 is pretty rigid there. Can dot notation see if a field has a default value?

It can! The Member type has an '.init' property specifically for this. PEP 827 introduces a type called InitField[KwargDict] that captures arguments from things like Field() or attr.ib() calls. When you access 'p.init', you get a TypedDict of those arguments. You can then use a conditional type to check it. For instance, the PEP shows a GetDefault[Init] helper that checks if 'default' exists in that KwargDict. You'd use 'p.init' with another operator like GetFieldItem to decide if your generated __init__ parameter should have a default or be required.

That is way more powerful than just tagging a class with a decorator. So I could theoretically write a generic 'AddInit[T]' that works for any class, regardless of whether it's a 'real' dataclass or not?

Exactly. You could define a type alias that iterates over typing.Attrs[T], uses p.name and p.type to build a list of Params, and then wraps them in a Callable. You'd use UpdateClass to actually apply that new __init__ to the class. It moves the 'magic' out of the type checker's internal C++ or Python code and into your own type-level logic. Just keep in mind this is still a Draft proposal targeted for Python 3.15, so it's not available in standard toolchains yet.

Hey nanochat. I am looking into PEP 827 for my team, specifically how it handles runtime introspection. We do a lot of dynamic validation similar to Pydantic. What is this 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar I see mentioned in the runtime support section?

The special_form_evaluator is a ContextVar introduced in the typing module to facilitate runtime evaluation of the new type-level operators. Because PEP 827 introduces logic like loops (*[... for t in Iter[...]]) and conditionals (T if B else S) directly into annotations, standard runtime tools need a way to actually execute that logic. This ContextVar holds a callable that is invoked whenever __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator or __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the other authors intended this to allow third-party libraries to provide a custom evaluator that can resolve these complex types at runtime.
Interesting. So if my code accesses __annotations__, it triggers these dunder methods? How does a library like FastAPI actually hook into this without making the global state a mess?

Actually, PEP 827 relies on the __annotate__ method (introduced in PEP 649/749) to trigger evaluation. When __annotate__ is called, it executes the code that defines the type. If that code includes something like 'IsAssignable[T, S]', it calls bool() on that object. If special_form_evaluator is set in the current context, the typing module uses that hook to decide whether that specific IsAssignable check should return True or False. By using a ContextVar, frameworks can safely set an evaluator locally within a specific scope—like during the initialization of a Pydantic model—without affecting the rest of the application's global state.
That makes sense. But what if I don't set an evaluator? Does everything just crash at runtime when it hits a type-level 'for' loop or 'if' statement?

The PEP provides a safe fallback. If special_form_evaluator is set to None (which is the default), the Boolean operators like IsAssignable or IsEquivalent will always return False when __bool__ is called. For loops using typing.Iter, the __iter__ call will return an empty iterator: iter(()). This 'inert' behavior ensures that basic runtime tools won't crash, though they obviously won't get the 'correct' computed result unless they provide an actual evaluator. The authors, including 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov and Daniel W. Park, noted that they don't plan to put a full evaluator in the standard library initially, but will release a third-party one for this purpose.

If the default is to return False or an empty list, that sounds like it could lead to some very confusing silent failures in validation. Is there a way to get the 'raw' unevaluated type if we want to do our own parsing instead of running the evaluator?

You are right to be concerned about silent failures. To address this, PEP 827 mentions that it would combine well with a proposed 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations. This would allow tools to fetch the fully unevaluated AST of the annotation expression instead of running the __annotate__ function. Without that, you'd be stuck with the 'inert' results or you would have to re-parse the source code manually. The goal of special_form_evaluator is specifically for tools that *want* to run the logic but need a hook to resolve things like 'IsAssignable' which require a sophisticated type-checking engine to answer accurately at runtime.

Okay, let's talk about the 'Iter' hook specifically. If I have a comprehension like '*[p.type for p in Iter[Members[T]]]', how does the evaluator handle that? Does it just pass the whole 'Members[T]' object to my hook?

Exactly. When the runtime encounters 'Iter[Members[T]]', it calls __iter__() on that object. That call checks the special_form_evaluator ContextVar. If a callable is present, it is invoked with the typing._GenericAlias representing that 'Iter' application. Your evaluator function then looks at the argument (Members[T]), performs the introspection (perhaps by looking at the class __annotations__ or using the new 'Members' operator logic), and returns an iterator of Member objects. Each 'p' in your comprehension then becomes a Member object, allowing you to access 'p.type' or 'p.name' via the dot notation the PEP introduces for these specific types.

It sounds powerful but complex to implement. If we adopt this for 3.15, we are basically committing to either writing our own type-system evaluator or depending on the one the authors release. Is there any performance hit if we leave the ContextVar as None?

The performance hit of leaving it as None is negligible. It is essentially a single ContextVar.get() and an 'if None' check inside the dunder methods of the typing objects. The real overhead only comes when you actually set an evaluator and begin performing complex type-level computations at runtime, like recursively broadcasting NumPy-style shapes or filtering large class schemas. For a tech lead, the trade-off is between the boilerplate reduction (like generating CRUD models automatically) and the added dependency of a runtime type evaluator. As the PEP is currently a Draft, these implementation details might still be refined before the 3.15 release.

hello nanochat :) I am evaluating PEP 827 for our team's tech roadmap. We have hit some walls with custom mypy plugins for our internal libraries. Why does the PEP suggest that plugins are not a sufficient long-term answer for Python typing?

Hello! It is a great question for a tech lead. According to PEP 827 (authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov), the core issue is that many type checkers and IDEs do not provide a plugin API. This creates a fragmentation problem: you might get perfect type checking in your CI suite using mypy, but your developers' IDEs or other tools will not understand the transformations. The PEP aims to bridge the gap between Python's complex metaprogramming and the type system by adding primitives like conditional and mapped types directly into the language, ensuring consistency across all tooling without needing custom plugins.

That makes sense. We use @dataclass_transform today, which helped, but it feels like a hack. How would PEP 827 handle things like automatically generating an __init__ signature without a special decorator?

The PEP describes PEP 681 (dataclass_transform) as a special-case solution for a common problem. PEP 827 replaces this with generic operators. For example, you could use 'Members[T]' to inspect a class, and 'UpdateClass' to modify its interface. To generate an '__init__', you would use an extended callable format inside a type alias. You could define an 'InitFnType[T]' that iterates over attributes using a comprehension like '*[Param[p.name, p.type] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]'. This allows the framework to define the method's type signature using standard Python type syntax, which any PEP 827-compliant tool can then resolve statically.

Our stack relies heavily on Pydantic for runtime validation. If we use these new operators for static typing, can we still use them at runtime to drive our validation logic, or is this only for static analysis?

Actually, supporting runtime evaluation is a primary goal of PEP 827. The authors specifically use FastAPI and Pydantic as a motivation. Currently, you can write functions that generate Pydantic models at runtime, but you cannot typecheck those functions. PEP 827 allows you to write a type like 'type HeroCreate = Create[Hero]', where 'Create' is a computed type that strips primary keys. To make this work at runtime, the PEP proposes a 'special_form_evaluator' context variable in the typing module. This hook allows runtime libraries to intercept boolean checks on operators like 'IsAssignable' or iterations on 'Iter', enabling frameworks to evaluate these complex type transformations during execution just as the static checker does.

Howdy! I have been reading up on generics and I stumbled across a mention of PEP 827 and something about unpacking type variables into kwargs. I think the example was f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]). Can you explain what that actually does?

Certainly! This is a prerequisite feature proposed in PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) by Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov. Essentially, it allows a function to 'capture' the structure of its keyword arguments into a TypeVar that is bound to a TypedDict. When you call a function using this pattern, the type checker looks at the specific keys and value types you passed and 'shoves' them into a dynamically synthesized TypedDict for K. A key part of the proposal is that the type checker will infer Literal types whenever possible for those arguments, allowing for very precise type-level computation later on.

That sounds powerful for capturing structure. But what if I have a key in my BaseTypedDict that I do not provide in the call? And more importantly, once I have K, can I use it to define a brand new class or a generic type on the fly?

Regarding missing arguments: the PEP specifies that for each non-required item in the bound that is not provided, if the item is marked as read-only, it will be inferred as Never. As for your second question, that is where we hit a limitation. PEP 827 does not currently propose any way to create new nominal classes or any way to make new generic types via these operators. You can construct new structural types using NewProtocol or NewTypedDict, but you cannot define a new 'class MyNewClass' template dynamically within the type system.

So I am stuck with structural types? That is a bit of a bummer. What about runtime stuff? If I use GetArg on K to inspect what the dev passed in, does that work even if I am checking against a Protocol?

To be honest, that is another area where the PEP is realistic about its limits. The authors note that runtime evaluators of type annotations are likely to struggle when using Protocols as the 'Base' for the GetArg operator. While static checkers like mypy (which has a proof-of-concept for this) might handle it, a runtime evaluator might fail to resolve the type arguments of a Protocol because class attributes are not always visible or inferred in the same way at execution time. The proposal aims for 'best effort' runtime support, but it is not a perfect mirror of the static type system's capabilities.

yo nanochat, I've been looking at PEP 827. I just finally got comfortable with callback protocols for typing functions, but now Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the others are proposing this whole new 'Extended Callable' thing with Param and Params. Why not just stick with protocols?

That is a fair question. Callback protocols are excellent for standard static analysis, but PEP 827 aims for something more dynamic: type-level computation. The authors (Dr. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) argue that protocols don't work well when you need to programmatically construct or deconstruct types. If you want to use operators like Members[T] or create a new signature based on an existing class, you need a way to treat the function parameters as a sequence of data that a type-level loop can inspect. A protocol is essentially an opaque interface, whereas the new Params[Ps] structure lets the type system 'see' every individual parameter as a Param object.
So it's more about 'building' the type? Can I use this new syntax to do stuff that's totally impossible right now? I'm trying to see where your limits are with this proposal.

Exactly. One major use case is adding or removing specific keyword arguments dynamically, which is very hard with just ParamSpec. For example, using the proposed Unpack of typevars for **kwargs, you could have a function that accepts exactly the keys defined in a dynamically computed TypedDict. As for my limits, keep in mind this is still a Draft PEP targeting Python 3.15. While I can explain the spec, I cannot 'run' this code in a standard 3.12 or 3.13 interpreter yet. Also, the PEP itself has restrictions; for instance, GenericCallable is currently restricted to being a type argument of Member to avoid 'opening a can of worms' with rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism.
Wait, so even if this lands, I can't just throw a GenericCallable anywhere? That feels like a big limitation.

It is a deliberate one. The authors want to support generic methods in NewProtocol—like a .process[T](x: T) -> T method—but they are wary of allowing these complex generic structures in local variables or return types where they might break type inference. The PEP explicitly mentions that full rank-N types are a 'can of worms' regarding undecidability. So, while I can help you design a NewProtocol with a generic method using a lambda-based GenericCallable, I have to be honest: the current proposal doesn't let you use those for everyday variable annotations.

aloha. I've been diving into PEP 827. Coming from a background where we have proper higher-kinded types and type-level functions like in Haskell, this 'Type Manipulation' proposal feels like a bridge toward that. Where does it stand right now and when is it supposed to land?

Hello! PEP 827 is currently a Draft and is targeting Python 3.15. It is authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. While it does not introduce a formal system of kinds in the way you might see in Haskell or OCaml, it adds powerful type-level operators like IsAssignable, Members, and NewProtocol to allow for construction and introspection that matches Python's dynamic patterns.

I see. The 'GenericCallable' with the lambda syntax `lambda <vs>: Ty` looks suspiciously like a type-level abstraction. In OCaml, I'd just use a functor or a polymorphic record. Is this trying to achieve rank-N polymorphism, or is it more restricted?

The PEP actually explicitly avoids the "can of worms" that is rank-N types or impredicative polymorphism. The `GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty]` is currently restricted to being a type argument for Member. The lambda is primarily used to delay evaluation so that runtime tools can handle generic methods without losing the type variables immediately. It is specifically designed to solve boilerplate in frameworks like FastAPI or Prisma rather than becoming a general-purpose logic engine for the type system.

Fair enough. One more thing: the conditional types using if/else within the annotation syntax. That feels like it is bordering on dependent types. Is the type checker actually going to evaluate these boolean operators like IsEquivalent during the check?

It functions as a type-level conditional. The syntax `<type> if <type-bool> else <type>` resolves to one of the two types based on whether the boolean operator evaluates to Literal[True]. While this introduces a form of type-level logic, the PEP authors ground it in the consistency model of Python's gradual typing rather than a full dependent type theory. They even propose a special_form_evaluator ContextVar hook to allow runtime libraries to trigger these evaluations when the __annotate__ method is called.

Hey, looking at PEP 827. As a core dev, I am worried about the impact on the runtime. Are we essentially breaking how annotations work? This looks like it is pulling in the TypeScript model where types are just erased, but in Python, we actually care about things like get_type_hints and runtime introspection.

The authors (Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov) actually explicitly address this concern in the PEP. Unlike the TypeScript model where types are strictly for the compiler, PEP 827 is designed to support runtime evaluation. It does not change the Python grammar itself, only the grammar of what expressions are valid as types. For runtime tools, the PEP introduces a hook called special_form_evaluator which is a ContextVar. This allows libraries like Pydantic or FastAPI to trigger evaluation of things like Iter or boolean operators when __bool__ or __iter__ is called during annotation processing. So, it aims to be more 'runtime-aware' than TypeScript's erasure model.

Right, but what about existing PEPs like 649? If we have complex logic like 'tt if tb else tf' or unpacked comprehensions inside the annotations, how does that not cause a performance hit or break tools that parse strings? It seems much more fragile than the static hints we have now.

PEP 827 notes that if PEP 649's 'Just store the strings' approach or a Format.AST mode for fetching annotations is adopted, it would mitigate the extraction issues. For tools that currently rely on from __future__ import annotations, those strings are still just strings and won't break. The real complexity is at the evaluation stage. The PEP acknowledges that while current string-based tricks for building annotations won't work for loops and conditionals, the __annotate__ function will handle the execution. It's a shift from the static nature of PEP 484, but it's grounded in the fact that many frameworks already generate types dynamically at runtime; they just can't typecheck them properly right now.

Okay, but look at UpdateClass. We already have dataclass_transform from PEP 681 to handle class transformations. Why introduce a new mechanism that potentially messes with the MRO or evaluation order? Is this not just duplicating effort but making it harder for the interpreter?

The difference between PEP 681 and PEP 827's UpdateClass is expressiveness. PEP 681 is a 'lowest-common denominator' hint for type checkers to recognize a pattern, but it doesn't allow a library to actually compute a signature. UpdateClass allows a decorator or __init_subclass__ to return a set of Members computed from the class's own attributes. For example, you could use InitFnType[T] to programmatically generate an __init__ signature. Regarding the MRO, the PEP specifies that UpdateClass only updates nominal classes and is applied in reverse MRO order during declaration. While it introduces evaluation-order dependence, the authors argue this actually mirrors existing runtime behavior for dynamic class modification.

So I saw people on Twitter complaining about PEP 827. It looks like a bunch of math and weird brackets. Are we really just trying to turn Python into TypeScript? It seems like a lot of complexity for no reason.

I understand the skepticism. PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation,' is definitely a big shift. It was written by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. The core idea isn't just to copy TypeScript for the sake of it, but to help the type system keep up with how 'dynamic' Python libraries already are. Right now, frameworks like FastAPI or ORMs like Prisma have to do a lot of magic or use custom plugins to explain their types to IDEs. This PEP aims to give them standard tools like conditional types and member introspection so they don't need those hacks.
If it's 'inspired by TypeScript,' what is actually missing? If I have to deal with this complexity, does it at least do everything TypeScript does?

Actually, no, it doesn't do everything. The authors were quite selective about what fits into Python. For example, TypeScript has a powerful 'infer' keyword for pattern matching within types. PEP 827 explicitly rejects adding that for now because it is very hard to fit into Python's current grammar. It also doesn't support 'template literal types' yet—the ones in TS that let you manipulate strings at the type level—though those are listed as potential future extensions. Finally, it restricts 'GenericCallable' usage to avoid 'cans of worms' like Rank-N types, which can make type checking undecidable.
Wait, so it's arguably 'TypeScript Lite' but with more brackets? That sounds like the worst of both worlds. Why would a regular dev like me care if it can't even do the cool string stuff yet?

It might feel like 'lite' in some ways, but it has a unique Python advantage: runtime evaluation. TypeScript types vanish when you run the code, but PEP 827 is designed so that libraries like Pydantic can actually look at these types while your program is running to validate data. It introduces things like 'IsAssignable' and 'Members[T]' which allow a library to 'see' what's inside a class. So while you might not write these complex types every day, the libraries you use will get much better at showing you errors in your IDE without needing weird plugins.
I guess if it makes FastAPI less 'magical' and more predictable, that's okay. When is this supposed to actually happen?

It is currently a Draft PEP targeting Python 3.15. It is still being discussed, so the syntax—like the 'if/else' in types or the '*[... for t in Iter[...]]' comprehensions—could still change. The goal is to make it feel natural to Python programmers by using familiar keywords like 'if', 'else', and 'for', even if seeing them inside a type annotation takes some getting used to.

Hey there. I'm a tech lead evaluating the Python 3.15 roadmap for my team. We come from a heavy TypeScript background where we use conditional types and 'never' to handle impossible states. I'm looking at PEP 827's RaiseError operator. Is this just a fancy way to say 'type is Never', or is it doing something more like a static_assert?

It is closer to a static_assert for the type system. While 'Never' represents a bottom type that is valid in the sense that it identifies an empty set of values, RaiseError[S: Literal[str], *Ts] is an explicit instruction to the type checker. If the checker needs to evaluate that specific branch to determine a type, it must halt and generate a type error using the message provided in S.

In TS, we usually just let a conditional branch resolve to 'never'. If a dev tries to use it, the compiler yells 'Property X does not exist on type never'. How does RaiseError improve on that? It seems like extra syntax for the same result.

The improvement is in the diagnostic quality. In the TS model, as you noted, the error message is often secondary and cryptic. In PEP 827, authors Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov designed RaiseError to take an explicit string literal. For example, in the NumPy-style broadcasting example in the PEP, the MergeOne helper uses it like this: 'else typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]'. This means instead of a dev seeing 'Union[Literal[2], Literal[3]] is not compatible', they get a specific 'Broadcast mismatch' error that actually includes the offending types T and S in the output.
Wait, so it can actually interpolate the types into the error message? You mentioned T and S being included. Is that a template string thing or just how the checker handles the variadic arguments?

The PEP specifies that 'Any additional type arguments should be included in the message.' It doesn't propose full string templating (though that is mentioned as a potential future extension), but it does require that the type checker surface those extra arguments (*Ts) alongside the primary string S when it reports the error. It makes the 'impossible' state self-documenting.

That's definitely better than what we deal with in current Python or TS. But my concern is runtime. We use Pydantic and FastAPI which rely on evaluating annotations at runtime to build schemas. If I put a RaiseError in a type alias, is my production server going to crash when it tries to import the module and inspects the types?

The PEP is careful about runtime evaluation. Most of these operators are 'inert' typing generic alias objects. However, the proposal introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar. This allows a third-party library (which the authors plan to release) to handle the logic. In a standard runtime environment without a specialized evaluator, RaiseError is just another object in the __annotations__ dict. It only 'triggers' if a tool—either a static checker or a dedicated runtime type-evaluator—actually attempts to resolve the logic to find the 'result' type.

So if I'm building a complex ORM with these tools, I could essentially create custom compiler errors for my users. Does RaiseError lift over unions like IsAssignable does? Like, if I have a Union where only one branch hits a RaiseError, does the whole thing fail?

RaiseError is not listed among the operators that are 'lifted' over unions in the specification. Lifting applies to operators like Concat or GetArg where you want to distribute the operation across union elements. RaiseError is a terminal operation. If your type-level logic (like a conditional 'if/else' type) evaluates to a RaiseError branch, the evaluation fails. It's meant to catch invalid states that shouldn't be part of a union to begin with.

I was looking at this PEP 827 proposal by Michael Sullivan, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov. It feels like we are just turning Python into TypeScript at this point with all these type-level operators. Specifically, why do we need both Members[T] and Attrs[T]? It seems like unnecessary complexity just to do what getattr and dir already do.

The distinction between Members[T] and Attrs[T] in PEP 827 is intended to simplify filtering when you are performing type-level transformations. While Members[T] produces a tuple of all annotated members including both attributes and methods, Attrs[T] specifically excludes methods. This is useful for frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI where you often only want to iterate over the data fields of a class to generate a schema or a database query, without having to manually filter out the functions defined in the class body.

Right, but Python is dynamic. If I have a method or an attribute that is not annotated, does it just disappear? If these operators ignore unannotated code, then the type system's view of the 'members' is just a lie compared to what actually exists at runtime.

You are correct that it is a subset. According to the PEP, only members with explicit type annotations are included in the results of Members[T] and Attrs[T]. This design choice by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan and the other authors was made to ensure that static type checking and runtime evaluation coincide more closely. It prevents the type system from having to guess the types of unannotated members, which would lead to 'Any' everywhere and defeat the purpose of precise type manipulation.

That still sounds like a lot of overhead. If I use Attrs[T], what am I actually getting back? Is it just a tuple of strings for the names, or are we introducing some massive new object that the type checker has to track for every single field?

It is more than just strings. Both operators return a tuple of Member types. A Member is a specialized type that encodes five pieces of information: the name (as a Literal[str]), the type of the member, a union of qualifiers (like 'ClassVar' or 'Final'), the initializer type (Init), and the defining class. For example, if you have 'x: int = 5', the Member object for 'x' would store that 'x' is an int and that it was initialized with a Literal[5]. This allows you to reconstruct things like __init__ methods or validation logic entirely within the type system.
Wait, you mentioned methods are in Members[T] but not Attrs[T]. How does the PEP represent a method? If a method has decorators or complex signatures, does the Member type just fall apart?

The proposal handles methods by treating them as functions carrying a 'ClassVar' qualifier. To represent the complex signatures you are worried about, PEP 827 introduces 'Extended Callables'. Instead of the standard Callable syntax, these use a Params type that wraps a sequence of Param objects. Each Param tracks the name, type, and kind (like positional, keyword, or variadic). This allows the type system to 'see' the internal structure of a method's arguments so it can do things like add or remove parameters when you wrap a function with a decorator.

This is exactly what I mean by 'TypeScript-like'. We are building a second, shadow version of the Python runtime inside the type system. If I am using Members[T] to look at a method, how does it handle something like 'self'? Does it just treat it as a normal argument or does it understand the bound instance?

It treats it as part of the signature but provides tools to manage it. For instance, the PEP shows an example of an 'InitFnType' where 'self' is explicitly handled as a Param[Literal['self'], Self]. Because Members[T] returns callables that are introspectable, you can use operators like Slice or GetArg to strip 'self' if you are trying to generate a signature for a factory function. The authors, including 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, essentially want to give library authors the same power that mypy plugin writers have, but without requiring custom C or Python code for the type checker itself.

If I have a class that inherits from three different bases, does Members[T] give me everything in the MRO or just what is defined on the class? And does it handle conflicts? In TypeScript, you just get an intersection, but Python's MRO is much more rigid.

Members[T] includes inherited members, but each Member object has a '.definer' attribute that identifies which class in the MRO originally defined that member. As for conflicts, the PEP introduces 'UpdateClass', which is used in type decorators or __init_subclass__ to modify a class's members. If you provide a new Member with the same name, it overrides the old one. If you want to remove a member, you can set its type to 'Never'. This mirrors how Python handles class dictionaries at runtime, just moved into the static type evaluation phase.

hey nanochat! I am moving over from TypeScript and was checking out PEP 827. It looks like it brings mapped and conditional types to Python. How do I actually modify a class's members? In TS I'd just define a new type, but here it seems I can actually update the class itself?

Welcome to the Python typing world! You're spot on. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, introduces a feature called UpdateClass specifically for this. It allows you to 'update' a nominal class with new members, possibly overriding old ones or even removing them by setting their type to Never. Unlike a standard TypeScript type alias which creates a new structural shape, UpdateClass is designed to be used in the return type of a class decorator or the __init_subclass__ method to change how the class is perceived by the type checker.
That makes sense. If I use __init_subclass__ to add methods, how does Python handle the inheritance chain? Like, if I have a base class and then a subclass, when does the type update happen?

Great question. The PEP specifies that when a class is declared, if its ancestors have an __init_subclass__ with an UpdateClass return type, those updates are applied in reverse MRO (Method Resolution Order). If the 'cls' parameter in __init_subclass__ is parameterized by type[T], the actual class type is substituted in for T. This lets you programmatically generate method signatures, like an __init__ for a dataclass, based on the attributes defined in the subclass.
Okay, so it's order-dependent. What if I have a complex hierarchy where multiple bases are trying to run UpdateClass, and they need to inspect each other's members? Does that get messy?

You've hit on one of the 'Open Issues' mentioned in the PEP. There is a concern about type-evaluation-order dependence. If an UpdateClass return type for one __init_subclass__ inspects the Members of an unrelated class that is also undergoing an update, the result might depend on the order in which the type checker evaluates them. The PEP authors note that this actually mirrors potential runtime evaluation-order issues in standard Python. While it's currently a Draft for Python 3.15, the proposal acknowledges that these cases are tricky and ideally such circular or order-sensitive dependencies should be rejected to keep the type system predictable.

Hi nanochat. I am currently evaluating PEP 827 as a potential standard for our team once Python 3.15 lands. One thing I am worried about is how the new conditional and comprehension syntax in types interacts with 'from __future__ import annotations'. We rely on that heavily for performance and avoiding circular imports.

That is a valid concern for any tech lead. According to the PEP 827 text by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, the proposal is designed with this in mind. Specifically, the authors note that if 'from __future__ import annotations' is used, these new type-level expressions like 'T if bool_typ else S' or the unpacked comprehensions are stored as strings. This actually benefits tools that want to perform static analysis because they can simply use 'ast.parse' on the string to inspect the logic without needing to execute it.

Okay, that covers the static side. But what about runtime frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI that actually need to resolve these types? If the annotation is just a string because of the future import, does the PEP provide a way to trigger that evaluation properly even with the complex logic like 'Iter' and 'Members'?

The PEP acknowledges that runtime evaluation is more complex with this new syntax. It specifies that these types are evaluated when the '__annotate__' method of a class, alias, or function is called. However, the authors admit that current tricks for turning code into strings do not work well for loops and conditionals. To mitigate this, they point toward two potential external solutions: PEP 649's 'Just store the strings' option or a proposed 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations. For the evaluation itself, the PEP introduces a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar in the typing module. This hook allows a runtime library to intercept calls to boolean operators or 'typing.Iter' to perform the necessary computation at runtime.

hey nanochat, got a sec for python typing? I am finally getting the hang of Generics and TypeVar, but I just saw some stuff about PEP 827. It looks a lot like TypeScript. Is it basically bringing TS conditional types to Python?

Precisely. Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Mad Emperor Yury Selivanov specifically mention in the PEP 827 abstract that the design is largely inspired by TypeScript conditional and mapped types. However, they had to adapt the idea to Python's specific semantics. Instead of the TS 'T extends U ? X : Y' syntax, Python 3.15 would use a more familiar ternary form: 'X if IsAssignable[T, U] else Y'. It is still a draft, but the goal is to bridge the gap between Python's heavy use of metaprogramming and the static type system.
Interesting. One thing I love in TS is the 'infer' keyword to grab a type out of a generic. Like if I have a list of strings, I can 'infer' the string part. Does PEP 827 have an 'infer' equivalent?

Actually, that is one of the explicit limitations. The authors looked at TS-style pattern matching with 'infer' but found it too difficult to shoehorn into Python syntax. Instead of 'infer', PEP 827 provides the 'GetArg' operator. If you want to extract the element type of a list, you would write something like this:

type ListArg[T] = typing.GetArg[T, list, Literal[0]]

It requires you to be explicit about which base class ('list') and which index ('Literal[0]') you are targeting. It is less 'magical' than TS pattern matching, but it achieves the same result within Python's existing grammar.
That seems a bit more verbose, but I get it. Can I use this to create totally new classes on the fly? Like if I want to combine two classes into a third one dynamically?

There is a significant restriction there: PEP 827 does not allow you to create new 'nominal' classes (actual 'class' objects with their own identity) or new generic types via these operators. You are limited to creating structural types. You can use 'NewProtocol[*Ms]' to create a new Protocol or 'NewTypedDict[*Ps]' for a TypedDict, but they do not have a real runtime class name in the way a standard class does. The only way to affect a nominal class is through the 'UpdateClass' operator, which is restricted specifically to the return type of a decorator or '__init_subclass__'.
Okay, so I can't just spawn a 'User' class out of nowhere. What about the loops? I saw something about '*[... for t in Iter[...]]'. Is that just a list comprehension inside a type?

It looks like one, but it is technically an 'unpacked comprehension'. It only works where 'Unpack' (from PEP 646) is already allowed. You must use 'typing.Iter' to wrap the type you are iterating over, which is usually a tuple of types. For example, if you wanted to create a protocol containing all attributes of a class T, you would do:

type AttrsOnly[T] = NewProtocol[*[p for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]]

One big limitation to keep in mind: 'Members' and 'Attrs' only see things with explicit type annotations. If you have a method or attribute without a type hint, PEP 827 operators will generally ignore it. It is also important to remember that while this works for static checkers like mypy, the standard library won't include a full runtime evaluator; you would need a third-party library for that.

Hi nanochat! I am currently studying language design and looking into PEP 827. I am specifically interested in the proposal for unpacking typevars for **kwargs, like the f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]) -> K pattern. Could you walk me through the basic mechanics of how K is inferred when someone actually calls that function?

In PEP 827, this pattern builds on PEP 692 and PEP 646. When you define a function like f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K]), the type checker treats K as a TypeVar bound to a TypedDict. When the function is called, the checker looks at the provided keyword arguments. For example, if you call f(x=10, y='hello'), the checker infers K to be a TypedDict with the structure TypedDict({'x': int, 'y': str}). It essentially synthesizes a structural record type on the fly based on the call site arguments, which allows the return type to perfectly match the input shape.

That makes sense. I noticed the PEP mentions a specific rule about inferring Literal types whenever possible during this process. From a type theory standpoint, why is that Literal-heavy inference so critical for the rest of the 'Type Manipulation' proposal? And how does the checker handle optional fields in the bound that aren't provided in the call?

Literal types are the 'fuel' for type-level computation in PEP 827. Operators like GetMemberType or conditional types (true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ) rely on Literal strings for member names or Literal[True/False] for logic. If the checker inferred 'str' instead of 'Literal["x"]', you could not look up specific members. Regarding missing arguments: if an item is non-required in the bound and is marked as read-only, the checker infers its type as 'Never' if it is missing from the call. This acts as a type-level signal that the field was not provided, which you can then branch on using a conditional type.

The use of Never as a 'missing' sentinel is fascinating. It basically enables a form of exhaustiveness checking at the type level. If I wanted to use this to build a decorator that adds or removes a specific keyword parameter, how would this interact with the proposed Extended Callables? Does the Unpack[K] inference integrate directly into the Params[Ps] structure?

Yes, it integrates via the KwargsParam[T] and the Params sequence. PEP 827 introduces a way to represent function signatures using Params[*Ps], where each element is a Param[Name, Type, Qualifiers]. If you have a typevar K inferred from **kwargs, you can deconstruct it using the Members[K] operator to get a tuple of Member types. You can then iterate over those members using an unpacked comprehension like *[Param[m.name, m.type, ...] for m in Iter[Members[K]]] to construct a new Callable. This allows you to programmatically modify the signature—adding a parameter is just prepending a Param to the Params list, and removing one is an 'if' clause in the comprehension that filters out a specific m.name.

Hey! I'm moving over from TypeScript and exploring PEP 827. I'm used to mapped types and utility types like Pick and Omit, but I'm trying to wrap my head around 'InitField'. Is that Python's version of a constructor transformer?

Welcome to the Python typing world! PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, is definitely a big leap toward that TypeScript-style expressiveness. To answer your question, InitField is more of a metadata bridge than a direct transformer. In Python, libraries like Pydantic or dataclasses use field descriptors like 'name: str = Field(default="unknown")'. Historically, the type system couldn't see the 'default="unknown"' part inside that function call. InitField is a special class introduced to capture those keyword arguments at the type level so other manipulation operators can use them.
Ah, okay. So it's like a container for the 'runtime' config of a property? In TS, I'd just look at the property signature. How does the syntax look for capturing those args? Does it use a TypeVar?

Exactly. It uses a generic TypedDict. The PEP defines it as: 'class InitField[KwargDict: BaseTypedDict]: ...'. When you define an attribute in a class, like 'age: int = InitField(default=0, kw_only=True)', the type system infers a specific TypedDict for 'KwargDict'. Because of the new 'Unpack' rules for typevars in '**kwargs', the type checker infers literal types where possible. So 'default' becomes 'Literal[0]' and 'kw_only' becomes 'Literal[True]'. This information is then exposed via the '.init' attribute of a 'Member' object when you introspect the class.
That's wild. So if I'm using the 'Members[T]' or 'Attrs[T]' operators to iterate over a class, each 'Member' has a '.init' field containing that TypedDict? Can I use a conditional type to check that?

You hit the nail on the head. You can use 'p.init' within a comprehension. For example, the PEP shows how to implement 'Create[T]' for FastAPI. You iterate over 'typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]' and use an 'if' clause to filter out members where 'primary_key=True' is set in the 'InitField'. You'd use 'IsAssignable' to check the metadata, like 'if not typing.IsAssignable(Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]])'. This allows you to programmatically derive a 'NewProtocol' or 'NewTypedDict' based on how those fields were initialized in the original class definition.

Hey nanochat. I've been spending way too much time manually writing out HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate models for my FastAPI projects. I saw PEP 827 mentioned something about 'Type Manipulation' and a specific operator called GetMemberType. Could I use that to avoid all this duplication?

Hello! Yes, PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) is specifically designed to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov proposed several operators for this. 'GetMemberType[T, S]' is one of the core object inspection primitives. It allows you to extract the type of a specific member (attribute or method) named 'S' (which must be a 'Literal[str]') from a class or TypedDict 'T'. If the member doesn't exist, it returns 'Never'. For a FastAPI user, this is a building block for programmatically defining the shape of your models based on a single source of truth class.

That sounds useful for single fields, but my 'HeroUpdate' model needs every field from 'Hero' to be optional. Just pulling a type with GetMemberType doesn't seem to automate the whole class transformation. Is there a way to map over all attributes at once?

Exactly. For a full transformation like 'HeroUpdate', you would combine 'GetMemberType' with other operators like 'Attrs[T]' and 'NewProtocol'. 'Attrs[T]' produces a tuple of 'Member' types for every annotated attribute in class 'T'. You can then use the 'unpacked comprehension' syntax introduced in the PEP to iterate over these. For example, a 'Partial[T]' utility would look like: 'type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]]'. Here, 'p.name' and 'p.type' use dot notation to access associated types of the 'Member' object, allowing you to reconstruct a new type where everything is optional.

Okay, that's powerful. But what about logic? In FastAPI, my 'HeroCreate' model shouldn't include the 'id' field if it's a primary key. Can I actually filter the members while I'm building that 'NewProtocol'?

Yes, the PEP allows 'if' clauses inside the unpacked comprehension. To filter out a primary key, you would inspect the 'Member' qualifiers or the initializer. In the FastAPI example provided by the authors, they use 'typing.IsAssignable' to check metadata. If you use a 'Field' descriptor, the PEP's 'InitField' mechanism captures those arguments (like 'primary_key=True') as literal types in the '.init' attribute of the 'Member'. Your comprehension would look like: '*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]]]]'. This effectively strips any field tagged as a primary key during the type construction.
Wait, 'GetFieldItem'? I don't see that in the standard list of operators. Is that how we're supposed to peek inside the 'Field(...)' call?

Good catch. 'GetFieldItem' is a helper alias used in the PEP's FastAPI implementation example, rather than a built-in primitive operator. It leverages 'InitField[KwargDict]', a new type introduced in PEP 827. When you define a class attribute like 'id: int = Field(primary_key=True)', 'Field' would be a subtype of 'InitField'. The type system then infers a 'TypedDict' for those keyword arguments. You use 'IsAssignable' and 'GetArg' (or similar logic) to inspect that 'KwargDict'. This allows the static type checker to see that 'primary_key' is 'Literal[True]' at compile time.

This feels like it's bringing TypeScript's 'Mapped Types' to Python. If I build these 'Partial' or 'Create' helpers, can I use them as return types for my ORM's select methods? Like, if I only select 'name' and 'age', can the return type be dynamically narrowed?

Precisely. The 'Prisma-style ORM' example in the PEP demonstrates this. You would define a method using 'Unpack' of typevars for '**kwargs' (e.g., 'def select[ModelT, K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K])'). The type checker infers 'K' as a 'TypedDict' of the keys you passed. Then, the return type uses a 'NewProtocol' that iterates over 'Attrs[K]' and uses 'GetMemberType[ModelT, p.name]' to fetch the actual types from the original model. This results in a return type that only contains the properties you explicitly requested, with full IDE autocomplete for those specific fields.

One last thing—since FastAPI relies on Pydantic for runtime validation, does this PEP actually work at runtime, or is it just for static checkers like mypy? If 'HeroPublic' is a computed type, can Pydantic still use it to validate JSON?

That is a core goal of the proposal. The authors (Sullivan, Park, and Selivanov) explicitly state that these facilities must benefit both static checkers and runtime frameworks. While the PEP doesn't include a built-in evaluator in the standard library, it introduces 'special_form_evaluator' via a 'ContextVar' in the 'typing' module. This hook allows third-party libraries (like the one the authors are planning) to trigger evaluation of 'if' and 'for' logic in annotations. When Pydantic calls '__annotate__', the evaluator resolves the 'NewProtocol' into a concrete structure that can be used for runtime validation and JSON conversion, just like a manually defined class.

I've been going through PEP 827 and looking at the type manipulation operators. I'm a heavy mypy user, and I noticed the proposal uses IsAssignable[T, S] as a core primitive. Why did Dr. Michael Sullivan and the other authors go with full assignability checking instead of a weaker sub-similarity check? My concern is that checking protocols at runtime is going to be incredibly heavy compared to just checking the 'head' of the type.

That is a valid concern that the authors actually addressed in the Rejected Ideas section of the PEP. They considered a weaker predicate called IsAssignableSimilar, which would have performed a simpler check of the 'head' of the types without looking at type parameters or protocols. However, Sully, Park, and Selivanov decided against it because they didn't want to introduce a new notion that is similar to but not quite the same as subtyping. They felt a weaker operator would either need a long, confusing name or a misleadingly short one. By using IsAssignable, they stay aligned with the existing 'consistent subtyping' logic used in Python's gradual type system.

But TypeScript's 'extends' operator in conditional types feels much more natural for structural matching. If I'm writing a conditional type like 'T if IsAssignable[T, list] else list[T]', I'm basically asking if T fits the shape. Why tie it so strictly to assignability, which carries all that gradual typing baggage, especially if the runtime evaluator is just going to be 'best effort' anyway?

The PEP acknowledges that a runtime evaluator will inevitably be 'best effort' because some things, like inferred class attributes in typeshed, aren't visible at runtime. However, the choice of IsAssignable over a weaker check is about consistency between the static and runtime worlds. If the operator used 'sub-similarity,' you might get a 'True' at runtime but a type error from mypy, or vice versa. Regarding the TypeScript comparison, the authors did look at the 'T extends U ? X : Y' syntax, but they found it difficult to shoehorn into Python's grammar without major changes. They preferred using the existing Python ternary 'A if B else C' and the IsAssignable operator to maintain the 'Pythonic' feel while achieving similar goals to TypeScript's conditional types.

Speaking of those conditionals, how does the lifting over unions work here? If I have a union like 'int | str' and I check 'IsAssignable[T, int]', does it just collapse to False, or does it distribute like it does in TS?

It lifts. According to the specification, many built-in operators, including IsAssignable, are 'lifted' over Union types. When an operator is lifted, the system takes the cross product of the union elements for each argument position, evaluates the operator for each combination, and then unions the results together. For example, if you have 'IsAssignable[Literal["a"] | Literal["b"], Literal["a"]]', the evaluator treats it as the union of 'IsAssignable[Literal["a"], Literal["a"]]' and 'IsAssignable[Literal["b"], Literal["a"]]'. This results in 'Literal[True] | Literal[False]', which the Bool[T] operator or a conditional type would then treat as a 'True' equivalent if the union contains Literal[True].

Okay, so it does the cross product. That makes sense for precision, but again, it feels like a lot of work for a 3.15 runtime. If I'm using something like Members[T] inside a comprehension for a NewProtocol, and T is a massive Union, isn't that going to explode? Why not allow the 'mini-mypy-plugin' approach where we just write a Python function to transform the types?

The authors rejected the 'mini-plugin' or Python function approach for a few reasons. First, it would require defining a safe-to-run subset of Python (like Starlark) that typecheckers could execute securely, which is a massive undertaking. Second, you'd still need a standardized way to represent and manipulate types within those functions, which would end up looking a lot like the operators proposed in PEP 827 anyway. By using the declarative operator approach with defined lifting rules, they provide a system that is easier for both static checkers and runtime libraries to implement consistently without needing to embed a full Python interpreter into the typing loop.

I was looking over the draft for PEP 827, specifically the Extended Callables section. For the ParamQuals, you've got them as Literals of short strings like '*' and 'keyword'. As a core dev, I'm thinking about the introspection side of this—is there a reason the authors didn't go with something like an enum, similar to the Parameter.kind in the inspect module? Strings feel a bit loose for the type system.

The authors, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, actually address this in the Open Issues section of the PEP. The current use of short strings in 'ParamQuals'—specifically '*', '**', 'default', and 'keyword'—was chosen primarily for code brevity to keep the syntax less verbose when constructing complex callable types manually.

However, they are explicitly seeking feedback on whether mirroring 'inspect.Signature' with an enum, perhaps named 'ParamKind.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD' or similar, would be more robust. The PEP acknowledges that while short strings are easier to type, an enum would provide a more direct mapping to existing Python introspection tools.

Brevity is nice for the user writing the annotations, sure. But if we're generating these dynamically via the NewProtocol or UpdateClass machinery, isn't it going to be a pain to map those strings back to actual function behaviors at runtime? Also, how does the PEP handle the distinction between 'default' and the actual value if we're just using these literals in a Param type?

That is a valid implementation concern. Under the current Draft, 'Param[N, T, Q]' uses the 'Q' slot for a union of 'ParamQuals' like 'Literal["keyword", "default"]'. If a parameter has a default, the 'default' string is simply present in the qualifier union.

To your point about runtime mapping, the PEP mentions a 'related potential change' in the Open Issues. It suggests fully separating the 'kind' from the 'default' status. In this alternative model, 'Param' would gain an 'init' field similar to the 'Member' type used for class attributes. 'Member' already has a dedicated 'Init' slot that stores the literal type of the initializer. If this change is adopted, 'Param' would use an enum for the kind and the 'init' slot for the default value, which would likely simplify the work for runtime evaluators and CPython's internal handling of these computed callables.

Hey. I have been reading through PEP 827, the Type Manipulation proposal by Sully, Park, and Yury Selivanov. Honestly? It looks like a nightmare for the runtime. I use Pydantic and FastAPI daily. If people start throwing 'if/else' and 'for' loops inside type annotations, won't 'get_type_hints()' just break for every existing tool?

You are right to be cautious. PEP 827 introduces significant complexity by allowing expressions like 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' and unpacked comprehensions like '*[ty for t in Iter[iter_ty]]'. Since these aren't just static strings or simple types, a standard 'get_type_hints()' call might indeed struggle to make sense of them.

The PEP authors acknowledge that tools relying on runtime introspection are in a tricky spot. Currently, the proposal suggests two main mitigations. First, there is a new 'special_form_evaluator' hook (a ContextVar) in 'typing'. This allows an evaluator library to intercept calls to '__bool__' on boolean operators or '__iter__' on 'typing.Iter' to produce real values at runtime. Second, it suggests that something like 'Format.AST' (from another draft PEP) would be a perfect companion to allow tools to fetch the unevaluated tree instead of the executed result.

So you are saying every library author—Samuel Colvin for Pydantic, Tiangolo for FastAPI—now has to write a full-blown type-level interpreter just to figure out what a field is? That sounds like a massive regression in DX for the ecosystem maintainers.

The PEP authors actually plan to release a third-party evaluator library specifically so every framework doesn't have to reinvent the wheel. The idea is that these frameworks would call into a shared evaluator that understands the PEP 827 primitives.

It is definitely a shift. Right now, if you have a class member with an annotation, you expect a simple object. Under PEP 827, 'Members[T]' returns a tuple of 'Member' objects. These 'Member' objects have dot-notation access, like 'm.name' or 'm.type'. To handle this at runtime, the 'typing._GenericAlias' objects produced by these operators would need to be 'best effort.' The PEP admits that full Python assignability checking isn't 100% possible at runtime—especially with Protocols—so the runtime evaluation will be more of a 'best effort' compared to what mypy or pyright can do statically.

I guess that is a bit more honest than pretending it will work perfectly. I am still skeptical about the 'UpdateClass' stuff. If that is used in '__init_subclass__', and it inspects other classes that also have 'UpdateClass' logic, won't we hit evaluation-order issues?

You hit on a key 'Open Issue' in the PEP. 'UpdateClass' does introduce evaluation-order dependence. If a return type for '__init_subclass__' inspects another class's members, and that class is also undergoing a transformation, the results could vary depending on which one the type checker (or runtime) hits first.

The authors note that this mirrors a potential runtime evaluation-order dependence that already exists in Python's dynamic nature, but they suggest that these complex cases should ideally be rejected or handled carefully. While it is a 'can of worms' (to use the PEP's own language regarding GenericCallables), the goal is to bridge the gap for things like 'dataclass_transform' (PEP 681) so we don't need hardcoded typechecker plugins for every new framework.

It is a lot to take in, but if it means I can stop writing custom mypy plugins just to get basic CRUD models to type-check, maybe it is worth the headache. At least Sully and the team are being up-front about how 'stuck' type evaluations will work.

Exactly. The proof-of-concept in mypy already handles most of these examples by checking operators and operands invariantly when things get 'stuck' due to unresolved type variables. It's a Draft for 3.15, so there is still plenty of time for the community to weigh in on whether 'Bracket City'—as they jokingly call it in the PEP—is the right direction for Python.

hai! I'm a PL design student looking into the type-level introspection proposed in PEP 827. I'm specifically curious about how it handles function signatures. How does the proposal distinguish between positional-only and keyword-only arguments when we're manipulating types?

Hello! PEP 827 introduces an 'Extended Callable' format to handle exactly that. It uses a new type called Param[N, T, Q], where N is the name, T is the type, and Q represents ParamQuals. For positional-only vs. keyword-only, the distinction lives in the Q parameter, which is a Literal of 'positional', 'keyword', or 'default'. To make it easier, the PEP authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—provided aliases. For example, PosParam[N, T] is a Param where the qualifier is Literal['positional'], while NamedParam[N, T] uses Literal['keyword']. If an argument is positional-or-keyword, the qualifier is simply Never.
That covers standard arguments, but what about the variadic stuff? Like, how do you model *args: int or **kwargs: str within this Params sequence? Does it just use another qualifier?

Exactly. The ParamQuals set includes '*' for variable positional arguments and '**' for variable keyword arguments. The PEP defines ArgsParam[T] as Param[Literal[None], T, Literal['*']] and KwargsParam[T] as Param[Literal[None], T, Literal['**']]. These are then wrapped inside a Params[*Ps] type, which serves as the first argument to a Callable. This allows a type-level representation that reflects the full complexity of Python's function grammar, including the fact that *args and **kwargs don't have individual names in the same sense as standard parameters.

From a type theory perspective, why introduce this verbose 'Extended Callable' format instead of just sticking to the standard Callable[[int, str], bool] or using Protocols? It seems like a lot of extra machinery.

The standard Callable syntax is too rigid for the type-level manipulation this PEP targets. You can't easily iterate over the parameters of a standard Callable to add, remove, or modify a single keyword argument. By deconstructing the function signature into a sequence of Param objects inside Params[*Ps], the system can use the proposed type-level operators like 'Iter' and 'unpacked comprehensions'. For instance, you could write a type alias that takes a function's Params and filters out any Param that has the 'default' qualifier. Protocols could model this at the structural level, but they don't provide the constructive primitives needed to programmatically transform one signature into another at check-time.

I see. I also noticed the 'GenericCallable' form in the spec. If I'm building a NewProtocol and I want a method to be generic—like map[U](self, f: Callable[[T], U]) -> list[U]—how is that bound? The PEP mentions a lambda syntax for this.

GenericCallable[Vs, lambda <vs>: Ty] is used to represent methods where the generic is bound at the method level rather than the class level. 'Vs' is a tuple of the unbound type variables, and the lambda 'freezes' the evaluation of the return type 'Ty' (which is usually a Callable). The PEP authors restricted GenericCallable to being a type argument for 'Member' specifically to avoid the complexities of rank-N types and impredicative polymorphism, which they describe as 'cans of worms'. By using a lambda, the system delays evaluation, ensuring that when you introspect 'Members[T]', you don't lose the generic structure to a premature IsAssignable check.

The proposal for Unpack[K] in **kwargs where K is a TypedDict seems like it would be a nightmare for the solver. How does the PEP manage the trade-off between that flexible inference and the risk of undecidable type reconstruction?

The PEP acknowledges this by citing the work of Hans Boehm and Frank Pfenning on the undecidability of partial polymorphic type reconstruction. To keep it manageable, the solver follows specific rules: it must infer 'literal types' when possible. If you call a function f(**kwargs: Unpack[K]), the checker looks at the literal keys provided. For non-required items in the bound that aren't provided in the call, if the item is read-only, it's inferred as 'Never' to signal its absence. This constraint ensures that the 'TypedDict' inferred for K is concrete and based on the actual call-site literals, rather than forcing the solver to search an infinite space of possible dictionary shapes.

Last thing—if I'm using UpdateClass to modify a class's __init__ based on its members, what happens with inheritance? If multiple ancestors have an __init_subclass__ with an UpdateClass return, which one wins?

PEP 827 specifies that if multiple ancestors provide an UpdateClass return type via __init_subclass__, they are applied in 'reverse MRO order'. This means the most base class's transformation is applied first, and more derived transformations follow. The PEP admits this introduces a type-evaluation-order dependence, similar to how runtime execution order works. If a transformation depends on inspecting 'Members' of another class that is also undergoing an 'UpdateClass' transformation, the results depend on the order of evaluation. The proposal suggests that ideally such circular cases would be rejected, but notes that this mirrors existing dynamic behavior in Python.

yo nanochat, pep 827 time. I am an IDE tooling engineer and I'm looking at the 'Extended Callables' section. Honestly, why are we adding Param and Params when callback protocols already exist? It feels like this is just going to break go-to-definition and completions for every library that adopts it.

I understand the concern from a tooling perspective. Adding a new way to represent callables means your type resolver has to handle more edge cases. However, the authors—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—specifically addressed why callback protocols don't suffice for the type-level manipulation proposed in PEP 827.

But why? If I have a Protocol with a __call__ method, I can already see the signature. Why do I need a specific Params[Param[Literal['x'], int]] syntax? It just looks like reinventing the wheel with extra brackets.

The fundamental issue is that callback protocols are difficult to 'construct' or 'modify' programmatically within the type system. PEP 827 introduces type-level operators that need to iterate over, filter, or transform function parameters—much like how the proposal allows you to iterate over class members using Members[T]. For example, if you wanted to generate a new __init__ method based on class attributes, as seen in the 'dataclasses-style method generation' example, you need a format that is amenable to unpacked comprehensions like: *[Param[p.name, p.type, ...] for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]. You can't really perform that kind of logic by reflecting on a standard Protocol's __call__ method during a type-level computation.
So I'm supposed to tell my users that 'go-to-definition' on a parameter now points to a Literal string inside a type alias? That's a nightmare for UX.

It is a shift, but it's consistent with how PEP 827 treats class metadata. The Param type uses Literal['name'] so that the name itself is part of the type's identity, which is what allows operators like GetMember to function. The PEP authors also noted that previous 'extended callable' attempts, like the ones in mypy_extensions, were no-ops at runtime. PEP 827 requires real runtime objects to support frameworks like FastAPI that perform validation. By providing a sequence-like Params[...], the type system can treat callables as first-class sequences of data that can be sliced, filtered, or joined just like tuples.

Okay, so if I see Callable[Params[...], R], I treat it as the extended format. Does this replace the standard Callable[[int], str] syntax? Because having two ways to define the same function is exactly what leads to bugs in my completion engine.

It doesn't replace the standard syntax. The PEP explicitly states that the presence of Params as the first argument is what distinguishes the extended format from the standard format. For simple, static signatures, users will likely stick to the standard syntax. The extended format is primarily for 'constructing and introspecting' types. For your IDE, you can think of Params as a natural bound for ParamSpec, providing a bridge between the existing generics and these new computed signatures.

yo, I've been digging into PEP 827. It feels like a massive leap for metaprogramming compared to PEP 681's dataclass_transform. I'm trying to wrap my head around the 'Extended Callables' part. What is the Param type exactly, and what do those three type arguments encode?

Hello! PEP 827 introduces the Param type as a building block for its 'Extended Callables' proposal. The goal is to provide a way to construct and introspect callable types using type-level computation. The Param type is defined as Param[N, T, Q], where each argument serves a specific role: 1. N (Name): This represents the parameter name, typically as a Literal[str] or None. 2. T (Type): This is the actual type of the parameter (e.g., int, str). 3. Q (Qualifiers): This uses a new ParamQuals type, which is a Literal containing values like '*', '**', 'default', or 'keyword' to describe the parameter's behavior. This structure allows the type system to represent complex function signatures that were previously hard to model, like those with mixed positional and keyword-only arguments.

That makes sense, but how do *args and **kwargs look in that system? I see N can be None. Also, how does this interact with PEP 646 variadics or PEP 612's ParamSpec? I noticed the proposal mentions a 'Params' type too.

For variadic arguments, PEP 827 provides specific type abbreviations. An ArgsParam[T] is just a Param[None, T, Literal['*']], and a KwargsParam[T] is a Param[None, T, Literal['**']]. The name N is set to None because these are not named parameters in the standard sense. As for ParamSpec and variadics, the PEP introduces a Params[*Ps] type that wraps a sequence of these Param objects. This Params type actually serves as the first argument to a Callable to signal it is using the extended format. The authors, including Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, noted that Params also acts as a natural bound for ParamSpec. While PEP 612 improved decorator typing, PEP 827 goes further by allowing you to add, remove, or modify parameters through type-level operators like unpacked comprehensions, which PEP 612 and 646 couldn't fully handle on their own.

I've been looking into PEP 827's type-level primitives. It feels like we're finally getting something approaching proper kind-level manipulation in Python. I noticed the proposal defines IsAssignable[T, S] as the core predicate for consistency checks. Why go with full assignability instead of the 'sub-similarity' check mentioned in the rejected ideas? In a language with Python's dynamic semantics, full assignability seems like it's asking a lot of a runtime evaluator.

That is a fair observation. The authors--Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov--actually debated this within the proposal. A 'sub-similarity' check would have been a weaker, easier-to-implement predicate that only looks at the 'head' of a type without recursing into type parameters or handling complex structural protocols. However, the PEP argues that introducing a brand-new concept that is 'almost like subtyping but not quite' would be too confusing for users. Instead, they opted for IsAssignable, even if runtime evaluators have to operate on a 'best effort' basis. If a check can't be fully resolved (like against some protocols), the evaluator has to handle it gracefully, but the goal is to keep the mental model aligned with Python's existing gradual typing spec.

The 'best effort' approach for runtime is understandable, though it makes the semantics feel a bit less principled than, say, OCaml's module system. If IsAssignable is the core engine, how does it interact with Union types? I saw some notes on 'lifting' operations. Does IsAssignable[T1 | T2, S] automatically distribute, or is there a specific logic for how it handles the cross product of members?

It does indeed lift. According to the specification, many operators--including IsAssignable--are lifted over Union types by taking the cross product of the union elements. For IsAssignable[T, S], the evaluator takes the union of the results for every combination of the elements within the arguments. In Python logic, the PEP describes this using itertools.product across all union elements. If you evaluate IsAssignable against unions, the result will be a union of the resulting literal types (Literal[True] | Literal[False]). This lifting ensures that type-level computation doesn't break down when a branch resolves to a polymorphic or choice-based type, allowing helpers like IsEquivalent[T, S] to work by simply checking assignability in both directions.

yo nanochat. i was looking at this pep 827 thing by dr. michael sullivan and 'mad emperor' yury selivanov. it feels like they are just trying to turn python into typescript. why do we need all these conditional types and weird bracket syntax when we could just write a mypy plugin for complex stuff?

I hear you. Dr. Sullivan, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov definitely took a lot of inspiration from TypeScript because that's what developers asked for in the 2025 Typed Python Survey. The reason they aren't sticking with mypy plugins is consistency. Right now, if you write a custom plugin for mypy, it won't work in Pyright, or in your IDE's auto-complete, or in other linting tools. PEP 827 wants to bake these 'Type Manipulation' facilities directly into the language so every tool handles them the same way.

but look at the syntax. stuff like '*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if typing.IsAssignable[p.type, Property]]'. that is barely python anymore. it's just adding massive complexity for what, orms and fastapi?

It is a lot to take in. The PEP authors admit it's a 'fairly conventional' static system trying to keep up with very dynamic metaprogramming. The goal is to reduce boilerplate. Take the FastAPI example in the PEP: instead of manually defining HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate classes that all look almost identical, you could just use a helper like 'type HeroCreate = Create[Hero]'. It uses those operators like NewProtocol and Attrs to derive the types automatically at runtime and statically. It's complex for the library authors, but the idea is that it makes things much simpler for the people actually using those libraries.
fair enough, but is this actually going to happen? and what about runtime? i use pydantic which needs to know types when the code is running, not just in my editor.

The PEP is currently a Draft targeting Python 3.15, so it's not a done deal yet. Regarding runtime, that's actually one of its big selling points. Unlike some other typing PEPs, this one includes 'special_form_evaluator' hooks. When you call __annotate__ on a class, these new structures like the conditional 'if/else' and the 'Iter' comprehensions are designed to be evaluatable by third-party libraries. So, a tool like Pydantic could use a runtime evaluator to understand exactly what 'Pick[User, Literal["name", "email"]]' means while your app is running.

Hello! I am a student studying programming language design and I have been looking into PEP 827, specifically the Type Manipulation proposal. One thing that caught my eye is the runtime support. Is there an official evaluator being added to the Python standard library to handle these new operators?

Hello! That is a great area of focus. According to the text of PEP 827, the authors (Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov) do not actually propose adding an official evaluator to the Python standard library. Instead, they intend to release a third-party evaluator library. The PEP is currently in Draft status targeting Python 3.15. While the standard library won't have the evaluator itself, it will include a new hook called `typing.special_form_evaluator`, which is a `ContextVar` used to trigger evaluation when dunder methods like `__bool__` are called on boolean operators or `__iter__` on `typing.Iter`.

That is an interesting design choice. From a type theory perspective, why would they leave the evaluator out of the stdlib? It seems like having third-party evaluators could lead to inconsistent behavior across different frameworks like Pydantic or FastAPI. How does the `special_form_evaluator` hook actually bridge that gap?

The PEP authors justify this by noting that most extensions are "inert" operator applications. The `special_form_evaluator` hook allows a third-party library to intercept evaluation. When `__bool__` is called on a Boolean Operator (like `IsAssignable`) or `__iter__` is called on `typing.Iter`, Python checks this `ContextVar`. If it holds a callable, that callable is invoked with the generic alias. This allows an external library to compute the result (like `Literal[True]`) before the standard `bool()` or `iter()` conversion happens. The goal is to support runtime-heavy frameworks like FastAPI without bloating the core `typing` module with a complex inference engine.

I see. I am also curious about the 'lifting over unions' mechanic mentioned in the specification. If an operator like `Concat` is applied to two unions, it takes a cross product. In a complex type system, doesn't this create a potential for exponential state explosion during runtime evaluation? How does PEP 827 balance that against the need for expressiveness?

You are right to spot that risk. The PEP specifies that for any operator lifted over unions, the evaluator must take the cross product of the union elements, evaluate the operator for each tuple, and then union the results. For example, `Concat[Literal['a'] | Literal['b'], Literal['c'] | Literal['d']]` results in four union members. To manage complexity and avoid the "cans of worms" like undecidability in rank-N types, the PEP restricts certain powerful features. For instance, `GenericCallable` is restricted to being a type argument of `Member` only.
