As the PEP notes, this is useful for invoking helpers that return a boolean literal type while keeping the runtime evaluation predictable. Without this syntactic boundary, tools that fetch annotations at runtime—like Pydantic—would struggle to determine when to trigger the evaluator versus when to treat a value as a static literal. The goal was to provide powerful introspection while remaining 'inert' by default, only activating via the special_form_evaluator when an evaluator library is explicitly opted-in.
yo nanochat, checking out the PEP 827 draft. I am trying to reduce boilerplate in my ORM. How do those conditional types with the if/else syntax work? Is it just like Python's ternary?

Precisely. PEP 827 introduces conditional types using the syntax: `<true_type> if <bool_type> else <false_type>`. It works similarly to Python's value-level ternary operator but operates entirely within the type system. If the `bool_type` evaluates to `Literal[True]`, the expression resolves to the first type; if it is `Literal[False]`, it resolves to the second. The 'bool_type' must be what the PEP calls a 'type boolean' - a specific subset of expressions like `IsAssignable`, `IsEquivalent`, or `Bool`, which can be combined with `and`, `or`, and `not`.
That makes sense. So if I have a generic T and I want to return list[T] only if T is a 'Link' type from my ORM, how would that actually look in code? Do I just put Link in the middle?

You cannot just put the class 'Link' in the middle. You need to use a boolean operator to produce a type-level boolean. Your example would look like this: `list[T] if IsAssignable[T, Link] else T`. Here, `IsAssignable` checks if T is a consistent subtype of Link. If you pass a Union like `int | Link`, the PEP specifies that the operation is 'lifted' over the union. The type checker evaluates the condition for each part of the union independently and returns the union of the results, so you'd get `int | list[Link]` automatically.

Hi nanochat. I am looking at PEP 827 as a potential way to clean up some of our internal ORM typing. Right now we use a custom mypy plugin to handle field transformations, but I am worried this proposal just shifts that complexity into the type system with things like Members[T] and Conditional types. From a tech lead's perspective, is there a real win here, or are we just trading one flavor of maintenance burden for another?

That is a valid concern. The authors -- Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov -- explicitly address this in the Motivation section of the PEP. The primary 'win' is not necessarily a reduction in the raw logic required to describe a transformation, but rather portability and consistency across the ecosystem.

Currently, if you write a mypy plugin, that logic is invisible to developers using Pyright, Pyre, or various IDE intellisense engines that do not share a plugin API. This often leads to the 'red squiggles' problem where the code runs fine but the editor is confused. By using standardized operators like `IsAssignable`, `GetArg`, and `NewProtocol`, your ORM's logic lives directly in the source code as valid Python. This means any tool following the PEP 827 spec will offer identical type checking and autocompletion.

Furthermore, PEP 827 supports runtime evaluation through the `special_form_evaluator` hook. This allows frameworks like FastAPI to use these same computed types for validation and JSON conversion at runtime, something static plugins cannot handle. While your implementation of a helper like `type PropsOnly[T]` might still be complex, it becomes a standard, reusable part of your codebase that works across all CI and development environments.

Hey! Longtime TypeScript dev here just diving into the Python ecosystem. I just saw the PEP 827 draft and I am genuinely hyped. Conditional types and these 'unpacked comprehensions' look like they're bringing that TS-level expressiveness I've been missing. I was looking through the Object Inspection section and I'm a bit confused on the split between Members[T] and Attrs[T]. In TS we usually just use keyof T, so what's the distinction here?

Welcome to the Python typing world! It is a very exciting time, especially with Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov proposing these TS-inspired features for Python 3.15. To answer your question: Members[T] and Attrs[T] are the primary ways to deconstruct a class or TypedDict. The fundamental difference is scope. Members[T] produces a tuple of Member types for every annotated part of the class, including both data attributes and methods. Attrs[T] is more specialized; it only returns the data attributes, effectively filtering out the methods. If you are building a utility type that needs to map over a class but ignore its functions—like a Prisma-style query builder or a Pydantic-like model—Attrs[T] is the tool you want.

That is awesome! So if I have a class with a bunch of helper methods and I use Attrs[T], they just vanish from the tuple? That's way cleaner than manual filtering. Does this catch every property though? Like, if I just have a variable in the class body without a type hint, does Attrs[T] still pick it up?

That is an important detail! According to PEP 827, only members with explicit type annotations are included in either Members[T] or Attrs[T]. If you have a variable or a method in your class body that isn't annotated, the type system will ignore it for these operations. This is intended to keep static type checking and runtime evaluation consistent. Another cool detail for a TS dev: while Attrs[T] filters for data, if you do use Members[T], methods are returned as callables carrying the 'ClassVar' qualifier. Also, properties are treated as attributes, so they will show up in Attrs[T] as well.

I love that it's strict about annotations. It makes the 'mapped type' equivalents like Partial[T] so much more predictable. One last thing—if I'm iterating over these with the new syntax, can I get the names easily? In TS it's just 'K in keyof T'.

Exactly! You can use the dot notation introduced in the PEP. Since both Members[T] and Attrs[T] return a tuple of Member objects, you can access m.name to get the literal string type of the name. For example, you could write a KeyOf[T] helper using Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]] to get that exact TS behavior you're used to!

I'm looking at PEP 827 for a validation framework I maintain. It looks heavily inspired by TypeScript, which is great, but I'm trying to figure out what's missing. For example, in TS I use the 'infer' keyword inside conditional types to pull out nested types constantly. Does 827 have that?

You've hit on one of the biggest departures from TypeScript. PEP 827 specifically rejects the TS 'infer' keyword and the 'extends' pattern matching syntax. The authors, including Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and Yury Selivanov, found it difficult to shoehorn into Python's grammar without major changes. Instead of 'infer', you use the 'GetArg' operator. For example, if you want the first type argument of a generic, you'd write 'typing.GetArg[T, Base, Literal[0]]'. It requires you to be more explicit about the base class you are deconstructing.

That seems a bit more verbose than just 'T extends Array<infer U>'. What about string manipulation? In my ORM, I often want to take a database column name and map it to a 'get_column' method. In TS, I can use Template Literal Types to concatenate strings. Is that in here?

Currently, no. String manipulation like 'Concat', 'Uppercase', or 'Capitalize' is listed under 'Potential Future Extensions' but is not part of the core specification for Python 3.15. The PEP authors noted that while TS supports this, they are holding off to keep the initial draft manageable. Right now, you can extract a member's name using 'm.name' (which returns a 'Literal[str]'), but you can't transform that string into a new name at the type level yet.

Bummer. One more comparison: TypeScript has 'Mapped Types' where you can iterate over keys and change them. 827 has 'unpacked comprehensions'—is that the equivalent? And can I create actual classes with it, or just these 'structural' types?

The unpacked comprehension syntax '*[... for t in Iter[...]]' is indeed the equivalent of Mapped Types. It's designed to work with 'NewProtocol' and 'NewTypedDict' to construct new structural types. However, a major limitation compared to standard Python metaprogramming is that PEP 827 does not allow you to create new 'nominal' classes (actual 'class' objects) or new generic types. You can use 'UpdateClass' to modify existing nominal classes in very specific contexts like a type decorator or '__init_subclass__', but you can't just generate a brand new 'class MyGeneratedModel' from scratch in the type system.
Interesting. So it's more about transforming the 'shape' of types. What about the strictness? In TS, if I define a utility type, it checks the constraints immediately. Does Python do that?

Actually, PEP 827 is 'less strictly-typed' at the definition site than TypeScript. The PEP mentions that TS has 'strict kinding' where it validates that a key 'K' exists on type 'T' within the alias definition itself. Python's proposal is more permissive; if an operation is invalid, it usually just results in 'Never' at evaluation time rather than a hard error during the alias definition. The 'RaiseError' operator is provided if you want to manually trigger a type error with a custom message when a specific condition is met.

yo nanochat! i saw some hype on twitter about pep 827. it sounds like it lets you manipulate types in python. but i am confused... why do we need a new pep for this? can't i just write a regular python function that takes a class and returns a new one? like a decorator or something?

That is a very common question! You are right that at runtime, you can already write functions to create new classes or modify objects. However, PEP 827, authored by Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, is solving a different problem: making those changes visible to static type checkers like mypy or the ones in your IDE.
i thought mypy just read my code. if i write a function that changes a type, why doesn't it just follow along? why do we need this weird new syntax like T if bool_typ else S?

The issue is that type checkers generally don't 'run' your Python code. They analyze it without executing it. If you write a complex function that uses loops and logic to build a class, the type checker gets lost because it doesn't know what that function will actually do until it runs. PEP 827 introduces a specific 'type-level' language that type checkers are built to understand. For example, it lets you use conditional logic directly in a type annotation, like: `type MyType[T] = int if IsAssignable[T, str] else list[T]`. This tells the type checker exactly how to calculate the result without needing to execute arbitrary Python logic.
oh, so it's like a mini-language just for the type checker? but wait, i heard this works at runtime too for things like pydantic. if it is just for the type checker, how does pydantic use it?

That is one of the coolest parts of PEP 827. Even though it is designed for type checkers, the PEP authors included 'runtime evaluation support.' This means tools like FastAPI or Pydantic can call a special hook to evaluate these types while the program is running. It bridges the gap. Without PEP 827, you either have a dynamic runtime function that the type checker doesn't understand, or a static type that is too rigid for dynamic frameworks. This PEP provides one syntax that works for both.
got it. so instead of me writing a function that returns a type, i use these new operators like Members[T] or NewProtocol[*Ms]. is it available now? i want to try it in my 3.12 project.

Not quite yet! PEP 827 is currently a Draft and is targeting Python 3.15. There is a proof-of-concept in mypy and a demo runtime evaluator, but it is not part of the standard Python language just yet. It is definitely something to keep an eye on for the future of Python typing though!
hey nanochat, i saw something about PEP 827 on my feed. it was talking about 'literals' and 'kwargs' and i am totally lost. is this just for the super experts or can a beginner like me get the gist?

Hey there! It sounds fancy, but the core idea is pretty cool. I am nanochat, and I am here to help you navigate Python's typing world. PEP 827 is a Draft proposal for Python 3.15 by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov. The whole 'Literal' thing with kwargs is about making Python's type checker much more observant. Normally, when you use **kwargs, the type system just sees a generic dictionary of strings. But with PEP 827, if you pass an argument like mode='fast', the type checker tries to remember the exact value 'fast' as a 'Literal' type instead of just saying 'it is a string.'
Why does it need to be that specific? Isn't just knowing it is a string enough for most things?

For a lot of code, just 'str' is fine. But PEP 827 is about 'Type Manipulation.' It allows library authors to write logic that runs inside the type system itself. If the type checker knows you passed exactly mode='fast', it can use new tools like 'conditional types' (which look like 'TypeA if Condition else TypeB') to change what the function returns. The PEP specifically says type checkers should infer literal types for **kwargs because it allows those arguments to be used in type-level computations. It essentially turns your function calls into a precise 'TypedDict' that can be sliced, diced, and inspected by the type checker.
Ohhh, so it is like giving the type checker a magnifying glass instead of just blurry glasses?

That is a great way to put it! By inferring Literal types for those keyword arguments, the type system can do things like 'Unpack' a type variable into your kwargs. This is huge for libraries like FastAPI or ORMs. Instead of needing a custom plugin to understand your code, the library author can use PEP 827 operators like 'Members' or 'GetMemberType' to look at what you passed and give you perfect autocomplete and error checking based on those literal values. It makes the type system as flexible as the Python code we actually write.

Hello. I am evaluating the current draft of PEP 827 to see if our team should plan around these type manipulation features for our 3.15 migration. I noticed the RaiseError operator in the specification. Is that intended to act like a runtime exception for the type system, and how does a developer actually use it?

In PEP 827, RaiseError[S, *Ts] is a specialized type operator designed to provide clear, actionable feedback when a type-level computation fails. It does not raise a Python RuntimeError; instead, if a static type checker or a runtime type evaluator needs to resolve an expression that hits a RaiseError branch, it must generate a type error using the string provided in the first argument.

You would typically use it within conditional types to handle invalid states. For example, in the NumPy-style broadcasting example within the PEP, it is used to catch shape mismatches:

type MergeOne[T, S] = (
    T
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]]
    else S
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]]
    else typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]
)

In this case, if the type checker determines that T and S are incompatible for broadcasting, it triggers a diagnostic message that includes the string "Broadcast mismatch" along with the specific types T and S that caused the failure. This prevents the type system from silently falling back to 'Any' or 'Never' when a logic error occurs in your type-level programming.

Hey nanochat! I've been looking over the PEP 827 draft by Sully, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov. The mapped and conditional types look like a total game changer for someone who spends all day in mypy. I'm trying to wrap my head around InitField though. What's the specific problem that is trying to solve?

It is great to meet a fellow typing enthusiast. PEP 827 is definitely ambitious. InitField is basically the bridge between the dynamic world of field descriptors (like what you see in Pydantic, dataclasses, or attrs) and the static type system. Currently, if you want a type checker to understand that a field has a default value or is 'primary_key=True', you usually need a custom mypy plugin or you have to rely on PEP 681's dataclass_transform, which is pretty limited. InitField solves this by capturing those 'Field' arguments into the type system itself.
Wait, so instead of the type checker just seeing 'Field(...)', it actually parses the arguments into a type? How does it store that without changing the grammar?

Exactly. When you define a field like 'foo: int = InitField(default=0, primary_key=True)', the PEP proposes that the type checker infers a specific type for that initializer. It uses a TypedDict to hold those arguments. Specifically, it maps them to Literal types whenever possible. So the initializer for 'foo' becomes something like InitField[TypedDict({'default': Literal[0], 'primary_key': Literal[True]})]. This information is then exposed through the '.init' attribute of the new Member type when you inspect a class using typing.Members[T].

That is clever. So if I'm building a framework, I can actually iterate over those members and check if 'primary_key' is set to True at the type level to generate, say, a 'Create' model that excludes IDs?

Spot on. The PEP actually shows a 'FastAPI-style' example where you define a 'Create' alias. It uses an unpacked comprehension like '*[typing.Member[p.name, p.type] for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal["primary_key"]]]]'. This lets you programmatically strip out fields based on their InitField metadata during type-level construction. It essentially replaces the need for hardcoded logic in the type checker with user-defined type manipulation.

Interesting. It feels like this makes things like @dataclass_transform obsolete because we can just define the __init__ signature ourselves using those 'Extended Callables' mentioned in the PEP. Is that the goal?

That is a big part of it! Dr. Michael Sullivan and the other authors explicitly mention that patterns like dataclass-style method generation are widespread and currently require special-casing. By using InitField to capture field info and the new 'Params' and 'Param' types to construct a 'Callable', you can write a helper like 'InitFnType[T]' that generates a perfectly typed __init__ method based on your class attributes. It moves the 'magic' out of the type checker's core and into the library code where it belongs.

Hi nanochat. I am going through the PEP 827 draft. I use ParamSpec and callback protocols quite a bit in mypy, but this new Extended Callable format looks like a different beast. How does it actually represent things like positional-only vs keyword-only parameters?

Hello. PEP 827 introduces a more granular way to introspect and construct callables than standard callback protocols. It uses a new Param type and a Params wrapper to describe function signatures. For positional-only vs. keyword-only, it relies on the third type argument of Param, which is a union of Literal strings called ParamQuals. Specifically, if a parameter is strictly positional, it uses the 'positional' qualifier. If it is keyword-only, it uses the 'keyword' qualifier. Parameters that can be both typically omit these specific qualifiers or use a combination like 'positional' and 'keyword' depending on the exact semantics.

Interesting. In mypy_extensions, we have things like Arg(), DefaultArg(), and NamedArg(). Does PEP 827 just replicate that or is it more flexible? For instance, how do I know if a parameter has a default value?

It is more flexible because it treats these characteristics as composable qualifiers rather than distinct class types. To indicate a default value, PEP 827 adds 'default' to the ParamQuals union. For example, a keyword-only parameter with a default would be represented as Param[Literal['my_param'], int, Literal['keyword', 'default']]. The PEP authors, including Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and Yury Selivanov, provide type abbreviations to make this less verbose, such as NamedDefaultParam[N, T] for that exact case. This allows you to use type-level operators to check for the presence of 'default' in the quals attribute.

And what about the variadic stuff? ParamSpec usually bundles *args and **kwargs together so you can pass them to another function. If I am writing a type-level transformation, can I pull out the type of just the **kwargs?

Yes, unlike ParamSpec which is largely opaque, the Extended Callable format allows you to iterate over the parameters in the Params list. For variadics, PEP 827 uses the '*' and '**' qualifiers. An ArgsParam[T] is defined as Param[None, T, Literal['*']], and a KwargsParam[T] is Param[None, T, Literal['**']]. If you have a Callable[Params[*Ps], R], you can use an unpacked comprehension like *[p for p in Iter[Ps] if IsAssignable[p.quals, Literal['**']]] to isolate the keyword variadic. This allows the type system to perform the kind of surgical manipulations that were previously only possible with custom mypy plugins.

That seems powerful, but how do I actually use this with a real function? If I have a function defined with the slash and asterisk syntax, like def f(a, /, b, *, c=1), how is that represented in this Params object?

Under PEP 827, that signature would be mapped to a Params sequence. The 'a' parameter becomes a PosParam[Literal['a'], type_of_a] because it is before the slash. The 'b' parameter is a standard Param[Literal['b'], type_of_b] since it can be either. The 'c' parameter becomes a NamedDefaultParam[Literal['c'], int]. You would wrap these in a Params object as the first argument to a Callable. The PEP also introduces dot notation for these types, so if you have a variable 'p' representing a parameter, you can access p.name, p.type, and p.quals directly in your type-level logic.

HEYOOOO! I've been diving into the draft for PEP 827. Coming from a background in OCaml and Haskell, seeing Python move toward higher-order type manipulation is wild. I saw the 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov is on there with Sully and Daniel Park—quite the power trio. But honestly, isn't this going to make typechecking incredibly slow? We're talking about type-level comprehensions and conditional branching here.

It's definitely a massive leap in complexity! Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov are essentially trying to bridge the gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and the static type system. You're right to worry about performance; the PEP introduces some heavy machinery like 'Lifting over Unions.' When you apply an operator to a Union, it calculates the cross product of all elements. If you have nested unions being processed by something like 'IsAssignable' or 'NewProtocol,' the number of evaluations could certainly grow. However, the proposal aims to replace the current mess of custom mypy plugins with a standardized logic that typecheckers can optimize.

The 'Lifting' part sounds exactly like where my IDE might start smoking. If I'm using 'Iter[FromUnion[T]]' to map over a big union and then constructing a 'NewProtocol' based on 'Members[T]', isn't that essentially type-level recursion? How does the PEP handle infinite loops or just deep recursion in things like that 'Broadcast' example for NumPy shapes?

The PEP is honest about that! In the NumPy-style broadcasting example, it uses a recursive type alias 'Broadcast[T, S]' that calls itself on 'DropLast[T]' and 'DropLast[S]'. To prevent the typechecker from spinning forever, it suggests that evaluations might get 'stuck' on unresolved type variables. In the reference implementation section, they mention that the mypy proof-of-concept handles stuck evaluations by checking operators and operands invariantly. Also, if things go truly sideways, there's the 'RaiseError[S, *Ts]' operator, which allows a library author to manually trigger a type error with a custom message if the computation hits an invalid state.

That 'RaiseError' is a nice safety valve. I'm also curious about 'UpdateClass'. The PEP says it can update a nominal class with new members via '__init_subclass__'. If I have a deep MRO and multiple classes are returning 'UpdateClass', doesn't the order of evaluation become a nightmare? Is it deterministic?

The authors specified that if ancestors have an '__init_subclass__' returning an 'UpdateClass', they are applied in reverse MRO order. The PEP does acknowledge an 'Open Issue' regarding evaluation-order dependence, especially if one 'UpdateClass' inspects 'Members' of an unrelated class that is also being modified. They admit this mirrors the potential complexity of runtime evaluation, but since PEP 827 targets Python 3.15 and is still in Draft, they're looking for feedback on whether to restrict those side effects. It's a bold attempt to give Python the kind of structural flexibility you see in TypeScript's 'Pick' and 'Omit', but within the constraints of Python's nominal system.

Hi nanochat. I am looking into PEP 827 because I am tired of maintaining five different Pydantic models for the same database entity in FastAPI. I saw a mention of something called special_form_evaluator. Is that a decorator I should use on my HeroUpdate types to make the conditional logic run?

Actually, special_form_evaluator is not a decorator. According to PEP 827, it is a ContextVar introduced in the typing module to facilitate runtime evaluation of the new type-level operators. While the proposal allows you to write logic like 'T if IsAssignable[T, int] else S' or loops using Iter, Python does not automatically execute that logic in the way you might expect for normal code. The special_form_evaluator is a hook for library authors to provide a custom evaluator that handles these operations at runtime.

That is confusing. I thought the whole point of using 'if' and 'for' in the type annotations was that Python 3.15 would just understand them as standard syntax. If I define 'type HeroUpdate = Update[Hero]' using the logic from the PEP, why do I need a ContextVar for it to work?

You are right that no changes to the Python grammar are required; the syntax is valid Python. However, the 'if' and 'for' used in these type-level expressions rely on specific dunder methods being called on typing objects. For example, when a conditional type is evaluated at runtime, it triggers a __bool__ call on a Boolean Operator like IsAssignable. By default, PEP 827 specifies that if special_form_evaluator is set to None, these boolean operators return False and typing.Iter evaluates to an empty iterator. If you want your Update[Hero] alias to actually compute a new TypedDict or Protocol at runtime for Pydantic validation, a runtime evaluator library must be active and registered through that ContextVar.

Oh, so if I just try to use it in a standard FastAPI app without some extra library setting that ContextVar, my HeroUpdate will just be empty or resolve to the 'else' branch every time? That seems like a lot of overhead for just trying to deduplicate some fields.

Yes, without an evaluator, the boolean checks would default to False. The PEP authors, including Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and Yury Selivanov, noted that while the type system can handle these statically, runtime evaluation is a separate challenge. They are not proposing a full evaluator for the standard library yet. Instead, they intend to release a third-party library that users or framework maintainers like the FastAPI team would use. This library would set the special_form_evaluator ContextVar so that when your code accesses __annotate__, the hook is triggered to correctly process the Member and Attrs logic you defined for your CRUD models.
Okay, so if I were building a tool to do this, what does my evaluator actually receive when the hook is called?

The callable stored in special_form_evaluator is invoked with a single argument: a typing._GenericAlias. This happens specifically when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable, IsEquivalent, or Bool) or when __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. Your evaluator then processes that alias and returns a value that Python can then call bool() or iter() on. This mechanism ensures that the complex type manipulation remains 'inert' until something specifically asks to evaluate the annotation at runtime.

hey nanochat! I have been maintaining a pretty beefy mypy plugin for my framework's data-layer, and I am honestly tired of it. I heard PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) basically brings TypeScript's utility types to Python. Can we walk through how things like Pick or Omit would look? I'd love to replace my plugin logic with actual type-level code if this lands in 3.15.

Hello! I am nanochat. I'm here to help you navigate Python's evolving type system. You are right to be excited—PEP 827 is specifically designed to bridge the gap between complex metaprogramming and the static type system, which currently often requires those custom plugins you are talking about. According to the draft by Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov, we can implement those utility types using new primitives like Members, Iter, and NewProtocol. For example, Pick[T, Keys] would look like this:

type Pick[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        p
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]
    ]
]

This uses an unpacked comprehension to iterate over the members of T and only includes those where the member name is assignable to your set of Keys.
That is so much better than what I'm doing in my plugin. What about KeyOf? I often need to generate a union of all field names in a class for my select() methods.

KeyOf is very straightforward with the new syntax. You iterate over the members and just extract the .name property, which is a literal string type. Then you wrap it in the new variadic Union:

type KeyOf[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]

The PEP introduces dot notation for Member types, so p.name and p.type allow you to reach inside each member definition during the comprehension.
Wait, does it handle unions too? Like if I have a union and I want to Exclude certain types from it?

Yes, PEP 827 handles union processing through a specific operator called FromUnion. To implement Exclude, you would do this:

type Exclude[T, U] = Union[
    *[
        x
        for x in typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]]
        if not typing.IsAssignable[x, U]
    ]
]

FromUnion[T] returns a tuple of the union's elements. The comprehension then filters out any element x that is assignable to U. This same 'lifting' logic is actually built into many of the operators, but for manual filtering, FromUnion is your friend.
This is going to save me so much maintenance. One last one: Partial. I use that constantly for update payloads where everything becomes optional.

For a standard class, Partial[T] would use NewProtocol and iterate over the attributes (Attrs[T]), making each type a union with None:

type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

If you are working with TypedDicts specifically, the PEP suggests a version called PartialTD that uses the 'NotRequired' qualifier instead:

type PartialTD[T] = typing.NewTypedDict[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type, p.quals | Literal['NotRequired']]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]

This really shows off how you can manipulate the qualifiers (p.quals) and the types (p.type) simultaneously.

hey nanochat, got a sec for python typing? I've been reading PEP 827 by Dr. Michael Sullivan and the others. I'm a big dataclasses user, and I'm trying to figure out if this could actually replace things like PEP 681 for generating __init__ methods automatically.

It definitely aims to! The authors (Sully, Daniel Park, and Yury Selivanov) specifically highlighted dataclass-style method generation as a motivator. Instead of relying on typechecker plugins or the hardcoded logic of PEP 681, PEP 827 provides primitives like 'Members' and 'NewProtocol' so you can construct that __init__ signature yourself in the type system. You'd basically iterate over a class's attributes and build a 'Callable' with matching 'Param' objects.

Right, I saw the 'InitFnType' example in the PEP. But it looks... very wordy? A lot of 'typing.GetArg' and 'typing.Member'. In TypeScript, if I want to pull a type out of a generic, I just use the 'infer' keyword in a conditional. It's so much cleaner. Why didn't they just bring 'infer' over to Python?

That's actually addressed in the 'Rejected Ideas' section of the PEP. The authors admitted that the TypeScript 'extends ... infer' pattern is elegant, but they found it extremely difficult to shoehorn into Python's current syntax. The main issue is the binding structure: 'infer' creates a new type variable on the fly. Doing that at runtime in Python would require some 'gnarly' tricks with custom globals environments to catch those unbound arguments. Instead, they went with explicit operators like 'GetArg[T, Base, Index]' to extract type arguments without needing new binding grammar.

I guess that makes sense since they want to avoid changing the Python grammar. But even the 'if/else' syntax for conditional types feels a bit off compared to TS. It's more like a ternary, right? Like 'T if IsAssignable[T, int] else S'.

Exactly. It follows Python's existing expression-level ternary syntax ('x if condition else y'). This keeps it familiar to Python devs and avoids the '?' and ':' syntax from TypeScript. While it might feel like more typing (the keyboard kind), it allows the type system to perform logic like 'GetDefault' for your dataclass fields by checking if an 'InitField' has a default value or is a 'Never' type. You're basically writing a small program that the type checker (and eventually a runtime evaluator) executes to determine what the class looks like.
And then I'd use 'UpdateClass' to actually 'attach' that generated __init__ to my model? It reminds me of how 'attrs' works where the class is transformed after definition.

Spot on. 'UpdateClass' is the special form that lets a decorator or an '__init_subclass__' method return a set of new members to override or add to the class. It's much more flexible than the current 'dataclass_transform' because you can programmatically decide which fields are keyword-only or optional based on the 'MemberQuals' (like 'ReadOnly' or 'NotRequired'). It's basically a bridge between the dynamic nature of libraries like Pydantic or attrs and the static world of mypy.

hey nanochat, i've been diving into python typing lately and just got comfortable with TypeVars and generics. but i saw something about PEP 827 and 'UpdateClass'. it sounds pretty wild. what does it actually do?

It is definitely a powerful addition! PEP 827, titled 'Type Manipulation', introduces UpdateClass[*Ps: Member] as a way to programmatically modify a nominal class's definition within the type system. Think of it as a tool for 'patching' a class with new members, overriding existing ones, or even 'removing' them by setting their type to Never. It is designed to solve the problem where libraries like dataclasses or Pydantic perform metaprogramming that static type checkers usually can't follow without custom plugins.
that sounds like it could be used everywhere, but i'm guessing there are limits. where am i allowed to actually use UpdateClass in my code?

You're right to guess there are limits. According to the PEP, UpdateClass is a 'special form' that can only be used in two specific places: as the return type of a type decorator (like a class decorator) or as the return type of an __init_subclass__ method. You can't just use it as a return type for a standard function or a variable annotation. It is specifically tied to the moments when a class is being defined or transformed.
makes sense. so if i use it in __init_subclass__ in a base class, what happens when i inherit from it? does the type system automatically update the child class?

Exactly. When a class is declared, the type system looks at its ancestors. If any of them have an __init_subclass__ with an UpdateClass return type, those updates are applied. If there are multiple updates from different levels of the hierarchy, PEP 827 specifies they are applied in reverse MRO (Method Resolution Order). Also, if the 'cls' parameter in your __init_subclass__ is parameterized by type[T], the actual class type is substituted in for T during that evaluation. This allows the type system to accurately reflect things like a dynamically generated __init__ method for every subclass.
neat. so i could basically write a base class that automatically 'types' a generated constructor for all its kids without needing a mypy plugin?

That is one of the primary goals! The PEP actually provides an example of a 'dataclass_ish' decorator and a 'Model' base class using UpdateClass for exactly that purpose. It uses other PEP 827 features, like Members[T] and the new Param type, to inspect the class attributes and construct a new __init__ signature. It turns what used to be 'magic' known only to the runtime into something the static type checker can fully understand and verify.

Hey! I've been spending a lot of time with PEP 646 for tensor shapes, but it's pretty limited for logic like broadcasting. I heard PEP 827 brings in some TypeScript-inspired magic for type manipulation. How does it actually handle boolean logic? In TS we have 'extends', but I'm curious how PEP 827 knows what is a logic check versus just a regular type?

You're right that PEP 827 is heavily inspired by TypeScript, but it adapts the concepts to Python's existing grammar. To distinguish between a regular type and a logic check, it introduces a specific subset of the type language called 'type booleans'.

Unlike regular types (like 'int' or 'list'), type booleans are built using specific boolean operators defined in the typing module, such as IsAssignable[T, S] or IsEquivalent[T, S]. When these are evaluated in a type annotation context, they resolve to Literal[True] or Literal[False]. The conditional type syntax itself--'true_type if bool_type else false_type'--requires that 'bool_type' be one of these syntactically valid type booleans.

So it's more structured than just 'if T == S'. If I'm trying to write a MergeOne helper for NumPy-style broadcasting, and I need to check if two dimensions are equivalent or if one is a Literal[1], how does the system treat those? Is there a risk of it getting confused with regular boolean values?

It avoids confusion by restricting what can appear in the 'if' part of a conditional. You wouldn't just write 'if Literal[True]'. Instead, you'd use the operators. For your broadcasting case, the PEP actually provides a MergeOne example. It looks like this:

'type MergeOne[T, S] = (
    T
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]]
    else S
    if typing.IsEquivalent[T, Literal[1]]
    else typing.RaiseError[Literal["Broadcast mismatch"], T, S]
)'

In this snippet, 'typing.IsEquivalent[T, S] or typing.IsEquivalent[S, Literal[1]]' is the type boolean. The system knows it's a logic check because 'or', 'and', and 'not' are specifically permitted to compose these type operators.

That 'or' and 'and' syntax feels very natural for a Python dev. But what if I have a helper alias that already returns a Literal[True]? Can I just drop that alias into the 'if' clause, or does the parser reject it because it's not a 'type boolean' operator?

That's a sharp observation. If you have a helper alias that resolves to a boolean literal, you can't use it directly in the 'if' clause because it doesn't meet the syntactic requirement of being a 'type boolean'. 

To bridge that gap, PEP 827 provides the 'Bool[T]' operator. It returns Literal[True] if T is assignable to Literal[True]. So if your helper 'IsEven[T]' returns Literal[True/False], you would write '... if typing.Bool[IsEven[T]] else ...'. This ensures the runtime evaluation hooks, which the authors (Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov) designed to support frameworks like Pydantic, can trigger correctly via the 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar.
I see. And since I'm dealing with variadic shapes, can I do 'all()' or 'any()' checks across a whole TypeVarTuple? TypeScript has some recursive ways to do this, but it can get messy.

Yes, PEP 827 actually supports 'all()' and 'any()' as part of the type boolean grammar. They take a comprehension of type booleans. For a variadic shape, you'd use the 'Iter' form to loop through the tuple elements. 

For example, you could write 'all(typing.IsAssignable[t, int] for t in typing.Iter[ShapeVar])' within your conditional. It's essentially a list comprehension for types that the static checker (and eventually a runtime library) can flatten and evaluate. Just keep in mind this is still a Draft PEP targeting Python 3.15, so the syntax isn't in standard tools yet.

yo nanocha, have you seen the draft for PEP 827? Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan and the team are basically trying to bring TypeScript's mapping and conditional magic to Python. As someone usually neck-deep in CPython internals, the 'why' is obvious for library authors, but why do we need this specifically in the type system rather than just sticking with mypy plugins?

It is an exciting proposal! The core issue PEP 827 identifies is the massive mismatch between Python's dynamic metaprogramming capabilities and its relatively conventional static type system. Currently, libraries like FastAPI and various ORMs have to rely on custom typechecker plugins or special-case decorators like @dataclass_transform (PEP 681). The problem is that many IDEs and typecheckers don't support a plugin API, so you lose that 'consistency' across the ecosystem. By moving this into the typing module, we get a standardized way to model these transformations that works everywhere without hardcoded support.

Right, and the Meta 2025 Typed Python Survey showed people are screaming for Intersection types and Utility types like Pick or Omit. I love that they aren't changing the Python grammar for this. But from an implementation standpoint, how are we supposed to handle logic like 'tt if tb else tf' or the unpacked comprehensions at runtime without it being a total mess?

That is one of the clever bits! Since they aren't changing the grammar, these expressions are already valid Python. To make them work at runtime, they're introducing a 'special_form_evaluator' ContextVar in the typing module. This hook is invoked when __bool__ is called on a Boolean Operator (like IsAssignable) or when __iter__ is called on typing.Iter. It allows a third-party evaluator library to intercept the evaluation of the __annotate__ method. It keeps the core interpreter light while giving framework authors the power to resolve complex types like 'NewProtocol[*[p for p in Iter[Members[T]] if ...]]' dynamically.

The 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov is on the author list too, so I'm not surprised there is a ContextVar involved! I'm particularly hyped about UpdateClass. Being able to return that from __init_subclass__ to actually modify the class members or generate an __init__ signature statically? That's the holy grail for Pydantic-style models.

Absolutely. The PEP shows how you can define an 'InitFnType[T]' that iterates over a class's Attrs[T] and constructs a 'Member' representing a new __init__ method. Because PEP 827 introduces 'Extended Callables' with the 'Param' type, we can finally describe function signatures with specific names, defaults, and qualifiers (like keyword-only) entirely within the type system. It essentially lets you write a 'type-level' version of the logic usually hidden in a library's private C implementation or a complex metaclass.
hru

I am doing well! I am here to help you understand the details of PEP 827, the Type Manipulation proposal by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov. Are you looking into the technical implementation or the general motivation?

I have been reading the draft. Coming from a Haskell background, the syntax for these type-level functions like IsAssignable[T, S] feels a bit strange. Why did the authors choose square brackets for operators instead of parentheses? In my mind, these are clearly higher-order functions being applied to type arguments.

The choice of square brackets was a deliberate decision to maintain consistency with the existing Python typing ecosystem. The authors considered using parentheses in the "Rejected Ideas" section but concluded that mixing them would lead to a poor developer experience. Since Python's existing generics and type aliases already use brackets for parameterization, forcing users to remember whether a specific API is a "type operator" or a "type alias" would be confusing. For instance, a user-defined alias like PointerArg[T] must use brackets, so making a built-in like GetArg use parens would create an inconsistent API.

That is a very Pythonic constraint. However, it feels like the system is quite limited in terms of what it can actually construct. I see NewProtocol and NewTypedDict, but I do not see any way to define a new nominal class or a generic type constructor at the type level. Does the PEP provide a way to handle higher-kinded types or is this strictly a first-order system?

You are correct about those limitations. PEP 827 does not propose any mechanism for creating nominal classes or making new generic types. The authors explicitly state they want to avoid the "cans of worms" associated with impredicative polymorphism and rank-N types, as these can make type inference undecidable. Even the GenericCallable operator is restricted to the type argument of a Member to prevent it from leaking into locals or return types. It is not a complete type-level programming language; it is a specialized set of primitives for deconstructing and reconstructing existing types.

So if I wanted to implement a recursive type transformation that depends on pattern matching, like TypeScript's 'infer' keyword, I am essentially out of luck? The PEP seems to rely on these ternary if/else structures instead.

Exactly. The authors considered adding a mechanism like TypeScript's 'infer' for pattern matching but rejected it because it is difficult to shoehorn into Python's grammar without major syntactic changes. Instead, you are limited to the Python-style ternary syntax: true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ. While this is more verbose and lacks the elegant binding of 'infer', it works within the existing grammar. For errors, you have to rely on the RaiseError[S, *Ts] operator to manually trigger a type-level failure message if your conditional logic reaches an invalid branch.

hey nanochat! I keep seeing talk about PEP 827. I am a huge FastAPI fan but I am sick of defining Hero, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate models over and over. Does this PEP actually give us TypeScript-style utility types like Pick or Omit to stop the duplication?

It does exactly that. PEP 827, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, proposes a type manipulation system inspired by TypeScript but tailored for Python. It introduces operators like 'Members', 'NewProtocol', and 'IsAssignable' that allow you to programmatically derive types. For example, 'Omit' in PEP 827 looks like this: 'type Omit[T, Keys] = typing.NewProtocol[*[p for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]] if not typing.IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]'. This lets you strip fields from a base model dynamically.

That is exactly what I need. But what about Pydantic Field metadata? If I have 'id: int = Field(primary_key=True)', can I use these new types to specifically find that primary_key flag and exclude it for my 'Create' model?

Yes, the PEP handles this through a feature called 'InitField'. When you use something like Pydantic's 'Field', the PEP allows the type system to capture those arguments into the 'init' attribute of a 'Member' type. You can then use 'GetFieldItem' or a similar helper to inspect that metadata. The PEP actually includes an example of a 'Create[T]' helper that filters out attributes by checking if 'primary_key' is set to 'Literal[True]' within the initializer metadata.
This sounds almost too good. What are the limits? Can I just use this right now to generate new classes at runtime for my database?

There are some important limitations to keep in mind. First, PEP 827 is still a Draft targeting Python 3.15. In terms of capability, the PEP explicitly states it does not currently propose a way to create new 'nominal' classes (like a standard 'class MyModel') or new 'generic' types. It primarily creates structural types via 'NewProtocol' or 'NewTypedDict'. While it introduces 'UpdateClass' to modify existing classes through decorators or '__init_subclass__', it is not a general-purpose 'class factory'. Also, the PEP does not include an official evaluator in the standard library; the authors intend to release a third-party library for the actual runtime evaluation of these computed types.
I see. Does it handle functions too? Like if I want to generate a union of all the keys in my model for a query parameter?

It does! You can implement TypeScript's 'KeyOf' using the 'Members' operator and a variadic union. It looks like: 'type KeyOf[T] = Union[*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Members[T]]]]'. This would result in a union of literal string types representing your model's field names. However, another limitation is that 'Members' currently only includes attributes or methods with explicit type annotations; unannotated members are excluded to keep things consistent with how attributes are handled.

I honestly feel like Python typing is jumping the shark. PEP 827 is basically copying TypeScript's homework with these conditional and mapped types. Adding loops and if-statements inside a type hint? It feels like we are losing the plot. And how is this even supposed to work at runtime with __annotate__ without breaking everything?

It is understandable to be skeptical. The authors, Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and "Mad Emperor" Yury Selivanov, actually state in the abstract that this design is inspired by TypeScript. The goal is to bridge the gap between Python's complex metaprogramming and its static type system so we can stop relying on custom mypy plugins for every library. Regarding __annotate__, PEP 827 specifies that syntax like list iteration, conditionals, and attribute access will be automatically evaluated when the __annotate__ method of a class or function is called. To prevent this from being a mess at runtime, they introduced typing.special_form_evaluator. It is a ContextVar that holds a hook for a third-party evaluator library. If that hook is not set, it defaults to None, meaning the boolean operators just return False and Iter returns an empty iterator, keeping it relatively inert for simple runtime access.

So it relies on a ContextVar just to make a type hint work? That sounds incredibly fragile for the core language. What happens if I am a tool developer who just wants to see the raw annotation without executing some complex type-level logic? Does this mean I have to ship a full type checker just to read a class definition?

That is a fair point regarding tool complexity. For tools that need unevaluated annotations, the PEP notes this would work best with a Format.AST mode for fetching annotations, which is being discussed in other draft PEPs. Otherwise, you would indeed see the result of the evaluation if you call __annotate__. However, the authors emphasize that this machinery is intended for framework and library maintainers rather than every developer. By using these primitives like IsAssignable, Members, and NewProtocol, maintainers can define logic once in the typing system so IDEs and type checkers can understand their dynamic APIs without special-casing every single library in the ecosystem. It is complex, but it is a structured complexity meant to replace the 'black box' of current runtime-only metaprogramming.

Hey. I've been hitting a wall with mypy trying to type some of our more dynamic ORM code lately. I saw Dr. Michael J. Sullivan and Yury Selivanov (the 'Mad Emperor' lol) are pushing PEP 827. What exactly is the 'gap' they are trying to bridge here? Is it just more @dataclass_transform stuff?

PEP 827 addresses the fundamental mismatch between Python's dynamic metaprogramming capabilities and its relatively conventional static type system. Currently, many libraries (like Pydantic, FastAPI, or Prisma-style ORMs) perform complex transformations on classes at runtime. To make these work with static analysis, we currently rely on custom mypy plugins or special-case decorators like @dataclass_transform (PEP 681).

The 'gap' is that these plugins aren't portable across IDEs or other type checkers. PEP 827 proposes moving that logic directly into the type system using type-level introspection (like Members[T]) and construction (like NewProtocol) so the type system can 'keep up' with the code without needing hardcoded compiler support.

Right, plugins are a nightmare for consistency. I see they mention the 2025 Typed Python Survey where people were asking for TypeScript features. Does PEP 827 actually bring keyof or conditional types like T extends U ? X : Y into Python?

Yes, it brings very similar mechanics. It introduces conditional types using Python's existing ternary syntax: 'true_type if bool_type else false_type'. The 'bool_type' isn't a standard boolean, but a type boolean using operators like IsAssignable[T, S] or IsEquivalent[T, S].

For 'keyof', the PEP defines KeyOf[T], which evaluates to a union of literal string types for every member of T. This enables 'Utility Types' like Pick[T, Keys] or Omit[T, Keys] which are implemented by iterating over Members[T] and checking if the member name is assignable to the requested keys.

Interesting. How does it handle the runtime side? Most of these libraries, like FastAPI, actually need to look at annotations at runtime for validation. If we start using complex conditionals and loops in the type hints, doesn't that break get_type_hints() or pydantic's internal logic?

The authors (Sully, Park, and Selivanov) explicitly prioritized runtime evaluation. They propose a 'special_form_evaluator' hook via a ContextVar in the typing module. This allows third-party libraries to trigger the evaluation of these computed types when __bool__ is called on a boolean operator or __iter__ is called on typing.Iter.

While the PEP authors plan to release a third-party evaluator library rather than putting one in the stdlib immediately, the infrastructure is there to ensure that tools like FastAPI can actually resolve 'Public[Hero]' into a concrete class-like structure at runtime. It essentially turns annotations into a programmable DSL.

Okay, let's look at the implementation of something like 'PropsOnly'. If I want to strip everything but scalar properties from a class, how do I actually iterate over the members? I saw *[... for t in Iter[...]] in the spec. How does that look for attribute inspection?

To implement something like PropsOnly[T], you'd use typing.Attrs[T] to get a tuple of Member types. Then you use an 'unpacked comprehension' to build a NewProtocol. It looks like this:

type PropsOnly[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, PointerArg[p.type]]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
        if typing.IsAssignable[p.type, Property]
    ]
]

The Member type has dot-notation access to p.name, p.type, p.quals, and p.init. The Iter[T] form is necessary to signal to the runtime evaluator that it should actually expand the comprehension over the elements of the tuple type.

That dot notation on p.name is wild for a type-level object. Is that a grammar change? And what about **kwargs? Usually, if I'm doing a generic select(**kwargs), mypy has no idea what the keys are unless I use Unpack[TypedDict], but that's static. Can PEP 827 infer a TypedDict from the call site?

There are no changes to the Python grammar itself; the PEP only expands the grammar of what expressions are considered valid types. The dot notation is handled by the type checker and the runtime evaluator for Member and Param types.

For **kwargs, PEP 827 includes a sub-proposal for 'Unpack of typevars for **kwargs'. If you write 'def f[K: BaseTypedDict](**kwargs: Unpack[K])', the type checker will infer K as a TypedDict based on the keys and values provided in the call. It specifically aims to infer Literal types for those arguments when possible. This lets you pass that inferred TypedDict into your type-level operators to dynamically compute a return type based on exactly which keyword arguments the user provided.

One last thing -- decorators. ParamSpec solved some issues, but it's still hard to surgically edit a function signature, like adding a specific keyword-only arg or a 'self' param. Does this PEP provide a way to rebuild Callables argument-by-argument?

Precisely. The PEP introduces 'Extended Callables' using Params[*Ps] and Param[Name, Type, Quals] objects. This makes function signatures just as introspectable and constructible as class members. You can deconstruct a Callable into its Params, iterate over them, add or remove specific Param objects (using 'keyword' or 'positional' qualifiers), and wrap them back into a new Callable.

The 'UpdateClass' operator then allows you to take these computed signatures and apply them to a class, for example, to dynamically generate an __init__ method for a dataclass-like object based on its annotated attributes. It effectively makes the function signature part of the data you can manipulate with the new type-level operators.

I've been looking at PEP 827 for tensor shape broadcasting. In NumPy or PyTorch, we often have to hack around with custom plugins or TypeVarTuple for shape checking. This 'Type Manipulation' looks like it could finally handle things like Broadcast[A, B] using recursion. But looking at the 'Extended Callables' part--why are the qualifiers like 'keyword' or 'default' just strings? Coming from a background with strict reflection APIs, or even just looking at Python's own inspect module, shouldn't these be Enums like ParamKind?

You have highlighted one of the specific 'Open Issues' in the current PEP 827 draft. Currently, Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov propose using ParamQuals defined as Literal strings like '*', '**', 'default', or 'keyword'. The primary reason cited in the PEP for this is 'code brevity.' Since type-level computation already involves heavy nesting with square brackets, using short strings keeps the signatures relatively readable compared to long Enum names.

Brevity is fine, but compare it to how we handle shape logic. If I'm building a generic Array class where I need to introspect a method to see if it's a classmethod or has a default, strings feel fragile. In C++ or even TypeScript's utility types, there's a bit more structure. If I'm using GetMember to pull a method out of a class, I want to know exactly what I'm getting. Is there a technical reason not to use an Enum like the one in inspect.Signature?

The PEP authors actually discuss this trade-off. One alternative mentioned in the Open Issues is to mirror inspect.Signature directly by using an enum with names like ParamKind.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD. However, there is a technical advantage to the current Literal approach: it fits naturally into the 'lifting over unions' logic. If you have a Param whose qualifier is Literal['keyword'] | Literal['default'], the type operators like IsAssignable can resolve those relationships without extra machinery to handle Enum-to-Literal mapping. Another point of debate is whether to separate the 'kind' of parameter from the presence of a 'default'. Currently, Param combines them into one qualifier union, but they could move to a system where defaults are tracked in a separate field, similar to how the Member type uses an 'init' field for class attributes.

Wait, so if I use Members[T] to look at my Tensor class, I get back a tuple of Member types, right? And those members have a .quals attribute. If I'm trying to filter for just ClassVars or Final attributes, I'm just checking if Literal['ClassVar'] is assignable to p.quals? That seems consistent with the rest of the PEP's logic.

Exactly. Whether it is MemberQuals (for classes) or ParamQuals (for callables), the system relies on the same boolean type operators. You would use typing.IsAssignable[p.quals, Literal['ClassVar']] to perform that check. This consistency is the main argument for staying with strings: if everything from member names to parameter types is handled via Literals and the IsAssignable operator, the 'language' of type manipulation stays small. While the authors admit that mirroring ParamKind might be better for those familiar with Python internals, the draft is currently optimized for this unified operator-based approach.

Greetings. I have been digging into the draft for PEP 827 lately. As someone who keeps an eye on CPython implementation and performance, I am particularly interested in the sections regarding the manipulation of Annotated types. Do you think the proposed GetAnnotations and DropAnnotations mechanisms are feasible without creating too much overhead in the internal type machinery?

Hello! It is great to discuss PEP 827 with a core developer perspective. As a Draft for Python 3.15, this proposal by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov aims to significantly expand what the type system can do. Regarding your question on Annotated, the authors actually categorize GetAnnotations and DropAnnotations under 'Potential Future Extensions' rather than the core specification. The idea is that GetAnnotations[T] would return a Literal representing the metadata, such as GetAnnotations[Annotated[int, 'static']] becoming Literal['static'], while DropAnnotations[T] would return the base type, like int. From a CPython standpoint, the PEP suggests that much of this complexity would be handled by a third-party evaluator library, though it does propose a special_form_evaluator ContextVar to hook into runtime evaluation for logic like Iter and boolean operators.

That ContextVar approach for runtime hooks is a clever way to avoid baking every operator directly into the interpreter core. However, I worry about the 'Lifting over Unions' logic. If we start extracting annotations from a Union of five different Annotated types, the cross-product evaluation sounds like it could get expensive during runtime introspection. Does the PEP provide any guardrails for that?

You raise a valid concern regarding the lifting logic. PEP 827 specifies that operators like GetArg, Members, and the proposed string manipulation helpers are lifted over unions by taking the cross product of all union elements. For GetAnnotations, the PEP is currently a bit light on specific guardrails, primarily because it is a future extension. However, it does note that if an operation is performed on a type that does not support it, such as calling GetAnnotations on a plain int, it should return Never. The authors, including 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, are aiming to support frameworks like FastAPI and Pydantic which already do heavy runtime introspection. By providing these as type-level operators, the hope is that static type checkers can resolve these computations during CI, potentially reducing the need for expensive runtime logic in the long run.

Interesting. So if GetAnnotations returns Never for non-annotated types, we can use the conditional 'if' syntax to handle defaults. Something like: T_Metadata if IsAssignable[GetAnnotations[T], Literal] else Literal['default']. Does the syntax actually allow nesting like that?

Yes, exactly! The grammar specification in PEP 827 allows for conditional types using the 'true_typ if bool_typ else false_typ' syntax. You could certainly nest an operator inside the condition. As long as the expression evaluates to a type boolean—meaning a boolean operator like IsAssignable or IsEquivalent, or a combination using 'and', 'or', or 'not'—it is valid. The authors designed this specifically so that library maintainers can build complex 'Utility Types' similar to what TypeScript offers, like Pick or Omit, which are explicitly demonstrated in the PEP implementation examples.

hello! I've been getting more comfortable with generics and TypeVar lately, but I just stumbled across PEP 827. It looks like it adds actual logic to types? I'm curious about how it plays with 'from __future__ import annotations' though. Doesn't that PEP turn everything into a string? Would that break this new type manipulation stuff?

Hello! It is great that you are diving into the deeper parts of the type system. You are spot on: PEP 827 (Type Manipulation) is a major leap from standard generics. It introduces conditional types like 'A if IsAssignable[T, int] else B' and even loops using unpacked comprehensions.

Regarding PEP 563 (from __future__ import annotations), you've identified a key friction point. Since that future import turns annotations into strings, it can make runtime evaluation tricky. The authors of PEP 827—Dr. Michael J. Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and Yury Selivanov—explicitly mention this in the 'Backwards Compatibility' section. They note that while string annotations don't break the syntax, tools that need to evaluate them at runtime (like Pydantic or FastAPI) would have a harder time processing strings that contain loops and conditionals compared to standard types.

That makes sense. I usually use those runtime tools for validation, so if they can't 'see' the logic because it's just a string, it seems like a bit of a step back. Does the PEP offer a way around that? Also, I saw 'typing.Iter' in one of the examples—is that how you loop over class attributes now?

The PEP suggests a few ways to handle the runtime evaluation issue. One idea is a potential 'Format.AST' mode for fetching annotations, which would provide a tree structure instead of just a flat string. They also plan to release a third-party evaluator library to help tools navigate these complex types. 

As for the looping, you are seeing the 'unpacked comprehension' syntax. To loop over a class's attributes, you would use 'typing.Attrs[T]' to get a tuple of 'Member' objects, and then wrap that in 'typing.Iter' to iterate. For example:

*[p.name for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]] if not IsAssignable[p.name, Literal['secret']]]

This would create a list of attribute names while filtering out any named 'secret'. It is definitely more powerful than what we can do with current TypeVars!

Wow, okay, that is a lot of power. It feels very 'TypeScript' in a way. Is this something I can use today, or is it still a long way off? I'm still trying to grasp when I'd use 'IsAssignable' instead of just a normal check.

It is very much inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types! However, it is currently a Draft proposal targeting Python 3.15, so it is not yet part of the language. 

You would use 'IsAssignable[T, S]' within these type expressions to perform a 'consistent subtype' check. For instance, if you're writing a helper alias that needs to know if a type matches a specific interface before deciding what type to return. It allows the type system to make decisions statically that previously required custom mypy plugins or complex runtime hacks. It is still in the early stages, but it aims to bridge that gap between Python's dynamic metaprogramming and its static type system.
hey nanochat, i've been looking at this pep 827 draft. honestly, it looks like a total carbon copy of typescript's conditional types. are we just turning python into typescript at this point?

You aren't far off on the inspiration! The authors of PEP 827—Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov—explicitly mention that this design is inspired by TypeScript's conditional and mapped types. They even cite a 2025 Typed Python Survey where 'Missing Features From TypeScript' was the top request. However, the implementation is adapted for Python's unique constraints, especially since Python lacks the 'extends' keyword for types and relies more on runtime introspection.
okay, so if i want to do a basic check like TS's 'T extends string ? true : false', how does that look in python? do we use the ternary operator?

In PEP 827, you use a conditional type syntax that looks like standard Python ternary expressions, but inside an annotation. Instead of 'extends', you use a new operator called 'IsAssignable'. It would look like this:

`Literal[True] if typing.IsAssignable[T, str] else Literal[False]`

The grammar specifically supports '<type> if <type-bool> else <type>'. The 'IsAssignable' operator checks if T is a consistent subtype of str. It's built to work with Python's gradual typing model rather than TS's structural 'extends' logic.
what about mapped types? in TS i can just loop over keys. how do i iterate over a class or a typeddict to change its fields, like making them all optional?

For that, PEP 827 introduces 'unpacked comprehensions' and the 'Members' or 'Attrs' operators. To make a type partial (like TypeScript's Partial<T>), you'd use 'NewProtocol' combined with a loop over the attributes. 

Here is how the PEP implements 'Partial[T]':

```python
type Partial[T] = typing.NewProtocol[
    *[
        typing.Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals]
        for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]
    ]
]
```

Notice the 'typing.Iter' and 'typing.Attrs[T]'. This lets you deconstruct a class or TypedDict into 'Member' objects, then reconstruct a new structural type. One big difference from TS is that Python's 'Members' only includes items with explicit type annotations.
that 'typing.Iter' and the dot notation like 'p.name' feels very different from standard typevar stuff. is this actually going to work at runtime or is it just for mypy/pyright?

A major goal of PEP 827 is supporting runtime evaluation. Unlike TypeScript, where types are erased, frameworks like FastAPI and Pydantic need to see these transformations at runtime to do validation. The PEP proposes a 'special_form_evaluator' hook in the typing module. This allows third-party libraries to trigger the evaluation of these 'if' and 'for' blocks when you call '__annotate__' on a class. So while it's targeting Python 3.15 and is still a Draft, the intention is definitely for it to benefit both static checkers and runtime frameworks.

I am currently neck-deep in FastAPI boilerplate, specifically creating separate Pydantic models like HeroPublic, HeroCreate, and HeroUpdate just to toggle optionality or hide fields like a primary key. I heard PEP 827 might fix this. Where is the official discussion thread for this PEP, and how exactly does it propose to handle this 'derived model' problem?

You can find the active discussion for PEP 827 at https://discuss.python.org/t/pep-827-type-manipulation/106353. The PEP, authored by Dr. Michael J. 'Sully' Sullivan, Daniel W. Park, and 'Mad Emperor' Yury Selivanov, specifically addresses the FastAPI/Pydantic duplication problem. It proposes using 'type-level manipulation' to derive those variants programmatically. Instead of defining five classes, you would define one base model and then use operators like Public[Hero] or Create[Hero] to generate the other types. This is inspired by TypeScript's utility types but adapted for Python's runtime introspection needs.

That sounds like exactly what I need. But how does it actually 'filter' the fields? In TypeScript, you have Pick and Omit. Does Python get those, and what is the syntax for iterating over a class's attributes to decide which ones make it into the new model?

PEP 827 introduces several primitives to enable this. To iterate over attributes, you use a new 'unpacked comprehension' syntax: `*[... for p in Iter[Attrs[T]]]`. The Attrs[T] operator returns a tuple of Member types representing the fields of class T. For example, to implement a version of Omit[T, Keys], you would use: `type Omit[T, Keys] = NewProtocol[*[p for p in Iter[Members[T]] if not IsAssignable[p.name, Keys]]]`. Here, p.name is a dot-notation access to the Member's metadata, and IsAssignable is a type-level boolean operator.

Wait, p.name is dot-notation? And NewProtocol is the constructor? This looks very powerful. Let's look at the FastAPI Hero example again. How does it handle things like stripping the 'primary_key' or making fields optional for an 'Update' model? I often use Field(primary_key=True) in SQLModel.

The PEP handles this through the InitField[KwargDict] type and the Member object. When you define a field like `id: int = Field(primary_key=True)`, PEP 827 captures those arguments into the 'init' attribute of the Member. To strip the primary key, your comprehension would include a filter like `if not IsAssignable[Literal[True], GetFieldItem[p.init, Literal['primary_key']]]`. For the 'Update' model, you would wrap the type in a Union with None: `Member[p.name, p.type | None, p.quals]`. This allows the evaluator to generate a new structural type (via NewProtocol) where every attribute from the original class is now optional.

I noticed the PEP mentions 'IsAssignable' and 'GetFieldItem'. How does the type checker know what is inside that Field() call? Is that what InitField is for? I'm curious about the specific mechanics of how p.init gets populated with my primary_key=True flag.

Exactly. The PEP proposes that when InitField (or a subclass like Pydantic's Field) is instantiated in a class body, the type checker infers a specific TypedDict for the keyword arguments. This KwargDict is stored in the Member.init field. So, if you have `Field(primary_key=True)`, p.init becomes something like `TypedDict({'primary_key': Literal[True]})`. The GetFieldItem helper then extracts that literal value. This allows the type system to 'read' your runtime field configurations during static analysis, effectively bridging the gap between metaprogramming and static typing.

If this is all happening at the type level, does it break Pydantic's runtime validation? Pydantic needs to actually see these fields to parse JSON. Does PEP 827 provide a way for Pydantic to 'see' these computed types at runtime without a custom plugin?

That is a core goal of the proposal. PEP 827 is designed to support runtime evaluation. It introduces a `special_form_evaluator` hook via ContextVar. When a library like Pydantic calls `__annotate__` on a class using these computed types, the evaluator can trigger. The syntax, including the `if` and `for` expressions in annotations, is structured so that it remains 'inert' until evaluated. The PEP authors even mention they intend to release a third-party evaluator library so that frameworks can resolve these complex types into standard objects that look like traditional classes or TypedDicts at runtime.

hey nanochat. I spend a lot of time with attrs and dataclasses. I saw PEP 827 by Dr. Michael J. "Sully" Sullivan and others mentions replacing the need for @dataclass_transform from PEP 681. How does it actually handle generating an __init__ method without a custom mypy plugin?

Hello! PEP 827 proposes bridging the gap between dynamic metaprogramming and the type system by adding type-level operators. Currently, tools like @dataclass_transform are 'lowest-common denominator' solutions that often require hardcoded support in type checkers. Under PEP 827, a library can use a type alias like InitFnType[T] to programmatically construct a method signature by iterating over a class's attributes using the new typing.Attrs[T] and typing.Member primitives. This allows the type system to 'calculate' the __init__ signature instead of relying on a plugin to guess it.

That sounds much cleaner. I was looking at the InitFnType[T] example in the PEP. How does it actually loop through the attributes? And how does it know which ones should be optional in the generated __init__?

It uses an unpacked comprehension syntax: *[... for p in typing.Iter[typing.Attrs[T]]]. Inside that loop, 'p' is a Member type. To handle defaults, it looks at p.init, which captures the literal type of the attribute's initializer. The PEP uses a conditional type: if typing.IsAssignable[GetDefault[p.init], typing.Never] else Literal["keyword", "default"]. If there is a default value, it adds the 'default' qualifier to the Param, making it optional in the signature. This logic can even handle complex field descriptors if they are subtypes of the new InitField[KwargDict] class.
Wait, what's the difference between typing.Attrs and typing.Members? And if I have this alias, how do I apply it to a class? I usually just use a decorator like @my_dataclass.

typing.Attrs[T] only returns data attributes, while typing.Members[T] includes both attributes and methods. To apply your generated InitFnType[T] to a class via a decorator, you use the UpdateClass operator. You would define your decorator to return typing.UpdateClass[InitFnType[T]]. When the type checker sees this, it 'updates' the nominal class T with the new __init__ member. This can also be used in __init_subclass__ to support Pydantic-style base classes. It basically allows the framework to tell the type checker exactly how the class structure was modified at runtime.

Hey, I am working on IDE indexing support for the Python 3.15 draft features, specifically PEP 827. I am looking at FromUnion right now. How does it actually represent the union elements once extracted? Is it returning a list or some special internal object?

According to PEP 827, FromUnion[T] returns a tuple containing all of the union elements. If the type T is not a union, it returns a 1-ary tuple containing T itself. In the context of the new type-level manipulation grammar, this is primarily intended to be used with typing.Iter inside unpacked comprehensions. For example, to implement an Exclude utility type, you would iterate over typing.Iter[typing.FromUnion[T]] to inspect each individual member of the union.

Got it, so it is a tuple. My concern for go-to-definition is how this handles nested unions, like A | (B | C). Does FromUnion preserve that structure or flatten it? Also, from a tooling perspective, if I am iterating over this to build a NewProtocol, can I still track the original source locations of those types?

The PEP does not explicitly define a custom flattening behavior for FromUnion, so it likely follows the standard behavior of Python's Union which is to flatten nested unions automatically. Regarding your tooling concern, FromUnion simply provides the types as elements of a tuple for further processing by operators like IsAssignable. If you are using these elements to construct a NewProtocol via a comprehension, you are essentially mapping over those tuple elements to create Member types.
